topics.title: Scanning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scanning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Scanning

In the context of entertainment electronics, **Scanning** is defined as the systematic, sequential, point-by-point analysis (at the transmitter/camera) or synthesis (at the receiver/display) of a two-dimensional visual scene into a one-dimensional time-varying electrical signal. This process converts spatial luminance (brightness) and chrominance (color) information of an image into a sequential train of electrical impulses that can be transmitted over a single channel and reconstructed as a recognizable picture at the receiver end.

The fundamental goal of scanning is to satisfy the **time-multiplexing constraint** of broadcast television, where only one electrical channel is available to convey the entire visual information of a frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Scanning is the foundational process in the entire television signal chain — without it, no modern display (LCD, LED, OLED) or capture device (CCD, CMOS) can function.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: Reading a Book

Imagine you are holding a printed photograph and you want to describe it to a friend over a telephone call (a 1-D voice channel). You cannot send the whole picture at once, so you do the following:

1. You start at the **top-left corner** of the photo.
2. You move your finger **horizontally from left to right**, describing the brightness and color of each tiny dot along the way.
3. When you reach the right edge, you **jump back to the left**, slightly **below** where you started, and repeat the process.
4. You continue this line-by-line descent until you reach the **bottom-right corner** of the photograph.

This is **exactly what scanning does**. The finger is the "scanning beam," the photograph is the "scene," and your voice is the "video signal." The horizontal motion is called the **line scan** (or **horizontal sweep**), and the gradual downward motion is called the **frame scan** (or **vertical sweep**).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The universally adopted television standards rely on a few rigid numeric constants:

- **625 lines per frame** — used in PAL/SECAM (European, Indian) systems
- **525 lines per frame** — used in NTSC (American, Japanese) systems
- **25 frames per second** — frame refresh rate for PAL
- **30 frames per second** — frame refresh rate for NTSC
- **50 fields per second** — interlaced field rate for PAL
- **60 fields per second** — interlaced field rate for NTSC
- **4:3** — traditional aspect ratio
- **16:9** — modern widescreen aspect ratio

> [!NOTE]
> **Persistence of Vision:** The human eye retains an image for approximately **1/16th of a second** (≈ 62 ms). The frame rate of 25/30 Hz is chosen so that successive frames blend into a perception of continuous motion — a discovery credited to Belgian physicist **Joseph Plateau**.

## 1.4 Why Scanning is Indispensable

| Engineering Reason | Plain-English Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Bandwidth Reduction** | A simultaneous (parallel) transmission of all picture elements would require thousands of parallel wires — scanning compresses this into a single channel. |
| **Compatibility with RF Transmission** | A single 1-D electrical signal can be amplitude-modulated onto a radio carrier, enabling over-the-air broadcast. |
| **Storage Feasibility** | Sequential signals can be recorded on magnetic tape (VCR) or encoded digitally (MPEG, H.264). |
| **Synchronization** | Scanning allows the receiver to lock perfectly with the transmitter using sync pulses. |
| **Flicker-Free Display** | Repeated scanning at ≥ 25 Hz exploits persistence of vision, eliminating flicker. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Scanning Patterns

There are **two primary scanning techniques** standardized worldwide:

### 2.1.1 Progressive (Sequential) Scanning
The electron beam (in a CRT) or pixel addressing circuit (in modern flat panels) traverses the image **line by line, from top to bottom, in a single continuous pass**.

- Line 1 → Line 2 → Line 3 → ... → Line *n* → return to top
- All lines together constitute **one complete frame**.
- Used in: Modern HDTV, UHDTV, computer monitors, cinema cameras.

### 2.1.2 Interlaced Scanning
The frame is divided into **two equal halves (fields)**:

- **Odd field:** Lines 1, 3, 5, 7, ..., 623 (all odd-numbered lines)
- **Even field:** Lines 2, 4, 6, 8, ..., 624 (all even-numbered lines)
- The odd field is scanned first, then the even field is interleaved between the odd lines.
- Used in: Traditional PAL/NTSC broadcast television.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Interlace?** Interlacing was invented to **halve the video bandwidth** while maintaining a high *perceived* refresh rate. The eye perceives 50/60 fields per second (high temporal resolution) even though only 25/30 *frames* are transmitted per second — a clever bandwidth-flicker trade-off.

## 2.2 Anatomy of the Composite Video Signal

The output of the scanning process is the **Composite Video Signal (CVS)**, which contains four essential components:

1. **Video Information** — luminance (Y) and chrominance (C) variations representing the scene
2. **Horizontal Blanking Pulses** — blank the beam during flyback from right to left
3. **Horizontal Sync Pulses** — synchronize the start of each new line
4. **Vertical Blanking Pulses** — blank the beam during flyback from bottom to top
5. **Vertical Sync Pulses** — synchronize the start of each new field/frame

## 2.3 Horizontal and Vertical Retrace (Flyback)

After the active scan of one line is complete, the beam must **return to the starting point of the next line**. This return journey is called **retrace** or **flyback**.

- During retrace, the beam must be **blanked** (extinguished) so it does not produce a visible trace on the screen.
- A **horizontal sync pulse** is transmitted to lock the receiver's horizontal oscillator to the transmitter's line frequency.
- Similarly, a **vertical sync pulse** is transmitted during the vertical retrace interval to lock the vertical oscillator.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are **mandatory** for solving KTU numerical problems on scanning:

### Symbol Legend
Let:
- $N$ = total number of lines per frame
- $f_v$ = frame frequency (Hz)
- $f_{field}$ = field frequency (Hz)
- $f_H$ = horizontal line frequency (Hz)
- $T_H$ = horizontal line period (seconds)
- $T_v$ = frame period (seconds)
- $T_{field}$ = field period (seconds)

### Core Equations Table

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_v = \dfrac{1}{f_v}$ | Frame period | seconds |
| 2 | $f_H = N \cdot f_v$ | Horizontal frequency | Hz |
| 3 | $T_H = \dfrac{1}{f_H}$ | Time to scan one full line | seconds |
| 4 | $f_{field} = 2 \cdot f_v$ | Field frequency in interlaced systems | Hz |
| 5 | $T_{field} = \dfrac{1}{f_{field}}$ | Time to scan one field | seconds |
| 6 | $\eta_H = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_H}$ | Horizontal retrace ratio (typically 0.16–0.18) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_V = \dfrac{N_{active}}{N}$ | Vertical retrace ratio (typically 0.08) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T_{active} = T_H (1 - \eta_H)$ | Active line duration | seconds |
| 9 | $B_{video} \propto \dfrac{N^2 \cdot f_v \cdot AR}{\eta_H \cdot \eta_V}$ | Video bandwidth proportionality | Hz |
| 10 | $f_{active} = f_H \cdot \dfrac{N_{active}}{N}$ | Effective line frequency during active video | Hz |

Where $AR$ is the **aspect ratio** (width-to-height ratio of the picture).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application | Relevance of Scanning Theory |
|---|---|
| **Broadcast TV (PAL/NTSC)** | Direct application of 625/525-line interlaced systems |
| **Smartphone Displays** | Progressive scanning at 60 Hz, 90 Hz, 120 Hz, 144 Hz |
| **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT)** | Raster scanning principles for tomographic reconstruction |
| **Radar and Sonar** | PPI displays use radial scanning; A-scans use linear sweeps |
| **Document Scanners** | 1-D linear CCD arrays perform mechanical line scanning |
| **Digital Cinema (DCI)** | Progressive 24 fps with 4K/8K resolution |
| **Video Streaming (Netflix/YouTube)** | Codecs (H.264/HEVC) operate on interlace-aware macroblocks |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Horizontal Scanning Frequency ($f_H$)

**Given:**
- Total lines per frame: $N$
- Frame frequency: $f_v$ (frames per second)

**To Find:** Horizontal line frequency $f_H$ (lines per second).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{In 1 second, the number of frames scanned} &= f_v \\
\text{Each frame contains} &= N \text{ lines} \\
\text{Therefore, lines scanned per second} &= N \cdot f_v
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_H = N \cdot f_v \text{ Hz}}
$$

### Worked Example (PAL System)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 625 \text{ lines/frame} \\
f_v &= 25 \text{ Hz} \\
f_H &= 625 \times 25 \\
f_H &= 15625 \text{ Hz} = 15.625 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the standard horizontal sync frequency for any PAL television receiver.

### Worked Example (NTSC System)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 525 \text{ lines/frame} \\
f_v &= 30 \text{ Hz} \\
f_H &= 525 \times 30 \\
f_H &= 15750 \text{ Hz} = 15.75 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Horizontal Line Period ($T_H$)

The horizontal line period is the **reciprocal** of the horizontal frequency because frequency and period are inverse quantities.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_H &= \frac{1}{f_H} \\
T_H &= \frac{1}{N \cdot f_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_H = \frac{1}{N \cdot f_v} \text{ seconds}}
$$

### Worked Example (PAL)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_H &= \frac{1}{15625} \\
T_H &= 64 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
T_H &= 64 \text{ μs}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means **each line is drawn in exactly 64 microseconds**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Active Line Duration

The total horizontal line period $T_H$ includes both the **visible (active) portion** and the **horizontal blanking (retrace) portion**.

Let:
- $T_H$ = total horizontal line period
- $T_{HB}$ = horizontal blanking period
- $T_{active}$ = active (visible) line duration
- $\eta_H$ = horizontal retrace ratio = $\dfrac{T_{HB}}{T_H}$

By definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_H &= T_{active} + T_{HB} \\
T_{active} &= T_H - T_{HB} \\
T_{active} &= T_H - \eta_H \cdot T_H \\
T_{active} &= T_H (1 - \eta_H)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{active} = T_H (1 - \eta_H)}
$$

### Worked Example (PAL with $\eta_H = 0.18$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_H &= 64 \text{ μs} \\
\eta_H &= 0.18 \\
T_{active} &= 64 \times (1 - 0.18) \\
T_{active} &= 64 \times 0.82 \\
T_{active} &= 52.48 \text{ μs}
\end{aligned}
$$

So out of every 64 μs line, only **52.48 μs** carries visible picture information; the remaining **11.52 μs** is the horizontal blanking interval.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Field Frequency in Interlaced Systems

In an interlaced system, **one complete frame = 2 fields**.

- Field 1 (odd field) scans lines 1, 3, 5, ..., 623 → contains $\dfrac{N}{2}$ lines
- Field 2 (even field) scans lines 2, 4, 6, ..., 624 → contains $\dfrac{N}{2}$ lines

Since both fields must be scanned in the same total time as one progressive frame:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time per field} &= \frac{T_v}{2} = \frac{1}{2 f_v} \\
\text{Field frequency} &= \frac{1}{\text{Time per field}} \\
f_{field} &= \frac{1}{1/(2 f_v)} = 2 f_v
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{field} = 2 \cdot f_v}
$$

### PAL Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_v &= 25 \text{ Hz} \\
f_{field} &= 2 \times 25 = 50 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

### NTSC Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_v &= 30 \text{ Hz} \\
f_{field} &= 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Vertical Retrace (Field Blanking) Time

Let $\eta_V$ = vertical retrace ratio = $\dfrac{N_{VB}}{N}$, where $N_{VB}$ is the number of lines devoted to vertical blanking.

Then the number of **active (visible) lines** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{active} &= N - N_{VB} \\
N_{active} &= N - \eta_V \cdot N \\
N_{active} &= N (1 - \eta_V)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{active} = N (1 - \eta_V)}
$$

### Worked Example (PAL with $\eta_V = 0.08$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 625 \\
\eta_V &= 0.08 \\
N_{active} &= 625 \times (1 - 0.08) \\
N_{active} &= 625 \times 0.92 \\
N_{active} &= 575 \text{ lines}
\end{aligned}
$$

Of the 625 transmitted lines, only **575 lines** are visible on the screen; the remaining **50 lines** carry vertical sync and equalizing pulses during the vertical blanking interval.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: PAL vs NTSC vs SECAM vs HD

| Parameter | PAL (625/50) | NTSC (525/60) | SECAM (625/50) | HDTV (1080i/50) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $N$ (lines/frame) | 625 | 525 | 625 | 1080 |
| $f_v$ (Hz) | 25 | 29.97 | 25 | 25 |
| $f_{field}$ (Hz) | 50 | 59.94 | 50 | 50 |
| $f_H$ (Hz) | 15625 | 15734.26 | 15625 | 27000 |
| $T_H$ (μs) | 64.0 | 63.556 | 64.0 | 37.037 |
| $T_{active}$ (μs) | 52.48 | 52.45 | 52.48 | ~30 |
| $AR$ | 4:3 (16:9) | 4:3 (16:9) | 4:3 (16:9) | 16:9 |
| Color Subcarrier (MHz) | 4.43361875 | 3.579545 | 4.40625 / 4.250 | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Scanning Process in a TV System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sceneA[Optical Scene] --> camA[Camera Pickup Tube]
    camA --> phaA[Photon to Electron Conversion]
    phaA --> scanA[Scanning Beam Deflection]
    scanA --> sigA[Time Varying Video Signal]
    sigA --> syncA[Sync Pulse Insertion]
    syncA --> modA[RF Modulation]
    modA --> chanA[Transmission Channel]
    chanA --> demodA[RF Demodulation]
    demodA --> syncSepA[Sync Separator]
    syncSepA --> defA[Deflection Circuits]
    sigA --> dispA[Display Device CRT or LCD]
    defA --> dispA
    dispA --> viewA[Reconstructed Image on Screen]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Progressive vs Interlaced Scanning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph progressive[Progressive Scanning]
        p1[Frame Start Top Left] --> p2[Scan Line 1 Left to Right]
        p2 --> p3[Flyback to Left Margin]
        p3 --> p4[Scan Line 2]
        p4 --> p5[Scan Line 3]
        p5 --> p6[Continue to Last Line N]
        p6 --> p7[Frame End Bottom Right]
    end

    subgraph interlaced[Interlaced Scanning]
        i1[Frame Start] --> i2[Odd Field Lines 1 3 5]
        i2 --> i3[Flyback to Top]
        i3 --> i4[Even Field Lines 2 4 6]
        i4 --> i5[Interleaved Complete Frame]
    end

    progressive --> comparison[Both reconstruct full frame at receiver]
    interlaced --> comparison
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Composite Video Signal Composition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    lineStart[Line Start] --> videoInfo[Active Video Information 52.48 us]
    videoInfo --> frontPorch[Front Porch 1.5 us]
    frontPorch --> hSync[Horizontal Sync Pulse 4.7 us]
    hSync --> backPorch[Back Porch 5.8 us]
    backPorch --> nextLine[Next Line Begins]
    
    frameStart[Frame Start] --> activeLines[575 Active Lines Visible]
    activeLines --> vBlank[Vertical Blanking 50 Lines]
    vBlank --> vSync[Vertical Sync Pulse 2.5 Line Duration]
    vSync --> equalPulses[Equalizing Pulses]
    equalPulses --> nextField[Next Field Begins]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Video Bandwidth Determination Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    resA[Resolution N lines x AR x N elements per line] --> pixRateA[Pixel Rate Calculation]
    pixRateA --> scanRateA[Multiply by Scan Rate]
    scanRateA --> bwA[Video Bandwidth Required]
    retrA[Retrace Ratios eta H and eta V] --> corrA[Bandwidth Correction Factor]
    corrA --> bwA
    bwA --> chanA[Channel Allocation]
```

## 4.5 ASCII Art: Composite Video Signal Waveform (One Line)

```
+-----------------------------------+-------+---+--------+--+
|         ACTIVE VIDEO              |  FP   |HSY|   BP   |  |
|         (52.48 μs)                |(1.5μs)|4.7| (5.8μs)|  |
|  Picture information varies        |       |μs)|        |  |
|  between black & white levels     |       |   |        |  |
+-----------------------------------+-------+---+--------+--+
0                                    52.48  53.98 58.68  64  μs
FP  = Front Porch
HSY = Horizontal Sync
BP  = Back Porch
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define scanning. With a neat diagram, explain the concept of interlaced scanning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Scanning is the process of converting a two-dimensional image into a one-dimensional time-varying electrical signal by traversing the image point by point in a systematic manner, line by line, from top to bottom.

In **interlaced scanning**, the total number of lines per frame ($N$) is divided into two equal halves called **fields**:
- The **odd field** scans all odd-numbered lines (1, 3, 5, ..., 623)
- The **even field** scans all even-numbered lines (2, 4, 6, ..., 624)

The two fields are transmitted sequentially and interleaved at the receiver to reconstruct one complete frame. In a 625-line PAL system, the odd field (312.5 lines) is scanned in 1/50 second, followed by the even field in the next 1/50 second, giving 25 complete frames per second. The main advantage is **bandwidth reduction by a factor of 2** without perceived flicker, since the field rate (50 Hz) is above the flicker fusion threshold of the human eye.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is the need for blanking pulses in a composite video signal?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Blanking pulses are necessary to **extinguish the scanning beam during the retrace (flyback) intervals**, both horizontal and vertical, to prevent the return path of the beam from producing visible bright lines on the screen.

There are two types:
1. **Horizontal blanking pulses** — applied during the right-to-left retrace at the end of each line (duration ~11.52 μs in PAL)
2. **Vertical blanking pulses** — applied during the bottom-to-top retrace at the end of each field (duration ~50 lines in PAL)

Without blanking, the retrace would appear as bright slanting lines on the display, severely degrading picture quality. Blanking ensures that the beam is OFF only when no useful picture information is being transmitted.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different types of scanning used in television systems. Compare progressive and interlaced scanning with their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a PAL television system with 625 lines per frame, 25 frames per second, and horizontal retrace ratio of 18%, calculate:
   - (i) The horizontal scanning frequency
   - (ii) The horizontal line period
   - (iii) The active line duration
   - (iv) The number of lines lost during vertical retrace if vertical retrace ratio is 8% **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Types of Scanning:**

There are two fundamental types of scanning:

**(1) Progressive (Sequential) Scanning:**
- The scanning beam moves from top to bottom in a single continuous pass, covering all lines (1 to N) sequentially.
- All lines belong to one frame.
- Used in computer monitors, modern digital TV, HDTV, UHDTV, and cinema.

**Advantages:**
- Superior picture quality — no inter-line flicker
- Better for motion rendition (no combing artifacts)
- Easier to compress digitally
- No need for deinterlacing

**Disadvantages:**
- Requires **double the bandwidth** for the same resolution and frame rate

**(2) Interlaced Scanning:**
- Each frame is split into two fields: odd (lines 1, 3, 5...) and even (lines 2, 4, 6...).
- The odd field is scanned first, then the even field is interleaved between the odd lines.
- Used in traditional broadcast TV (PAL, NTSC, SECAM).

**Advantages:**
- **Halves the bandwidth** required
- Higher perceived refresh rate → reduced flicker

**Disadvantages:**
- Inter-line flicker at fine details
- Combing artifacts on motion (requires deinterlacing)
- More complex signal processing

| Parameter | Progressive | Interlaced |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth | High | Half of progressive |
| Flicker | None | Possible at fine details |
| Complexity | Low | High (sync pulses, fields) |
| Motion rendering | Excellent | Good |
| Compression | Easier | Harder |

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- $N = 625$ lines/frame
- $f_v = 25$ Hz
- $\eta_H = 18\% = 0.18$
- $\eta_V = 8\% = 0.08$

**(i) Horizontal Scanning Frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_H &= N \times f_v \\
f_H &= 625 \times 25 \\
f_H &= 15625 \text{ Hz} = 15.625 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Horizontal Line Period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_H &= \frac{1}{f_H} \\
T_H &= \frac{1}{15625} \\
T_H &= 64 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
T_H &= 64 \text{ μs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Active Line Duration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{active} &= T_H (1 - \eta_H) \\
T_{active} &= 64 \times (1 - 0.18) \\
T_{active} &= 64 \times 0.82 \\
T_{active} &= 52.48 \text{ μs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Number of Lines Lost During Vertical Retrace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{VB} &= N \times \eta_V \\
N_{VB} &= 625 \times 0.08 \\
N_{VB} &= 50 \text{ lines} \\
N_{active} &= N - N_{VB} = 625 - 50 = 575 \text{ lines}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a composite video signal generation system. Discuss the role of horizontal and vertical sync pulses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An NTSC television system uses 525 lines per frame at 30 frames per second. Calculate:
   - (i) The field frequency
   - (ii) The horizontal scanning frequency
   - (iii) The horizontal line period
   - (iv) If the aspect ratio is 4:3 and the picture has 480 active lines, determine the approximate bandwidth of the video signal. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Composite Video Signal Generation Block Diagram:**

A composite video signal is generated by combining the camera output with synchronizing and blanking pulses. The functional blocks are:

1. **Camera Pickup Tube** — converts optical scene into a varying electrical signal proportional to scene luminance.
2. **Video Amplifier** — amplifies the weak camera output to usable levels.
3. **Blanking Pulse Generator** — produces horizontal and vertical blanking pulses that suppress the beam during retrace.
4. **Sync Pulse Generator** — produces horizontal and vertical sync pulses driven by a master oscillator.
5. **Composite Video Mixer** — combines video information + blanking pulses + sync pulses into one signal.

**Role of Sync Pulses:**

- **Horizontal Sync Pulses:** These are narrow negative-going pulses (~4.7 μs wide in PAL) transmitted during the horizontal blanking interval. They trigger the horizontal deflection oscillator in the receiver, ensuring that the receiver's horizontal sweep is **phase-locked** to the transmitter's line rate. Without H-sync, the picture would tear horizontally.

- **Vertical Sync Pulses:** These are wider pulses (occupying ~2.5 line durations in PAL) transmitted during the vertical blanking interval. They synchronize the vertical deflection oscillator, ensuring that the receiver's vertical sweep rolls in step with the transmitter's field/frame rate. Without V-sync, the picture would roll vertically.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse horizontal blanking with horizontal sync. Remember — **blanking extinguishes the beam** (prevents visible retrace), while **sync locks the timing** of the next sweep. They are distinct functions occurring in the same interval but are different pulses.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- $N = 525$ lines/frame
- $f_v = 30$ Hz
- $AR = 4:3$
- $N_{active} = 480$ lines

**(i) Field Frequency (Interlaced):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{field} &= 2 \times f_v \\
f_{field} &= 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Horizontal Scanning Frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_H &= N \times f_v \\
f_H &= 525 \times 30 \\
f_H &= 15750 \text{ Hz} = 15.75 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Horizontal Line Period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_H &= \frac{1}{f_H} \\
T_H &= \frac{1}{15750} \\
T_H &= 63.49 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
T_H &\approx 63.5 \text{ μs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Approximate Video Bandwidth:**

The maximum video frequency corresponds to the finest resolvable detail, which is approximately square pixels with aspect ratio matching the frame.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pixels per active line} &= N_{active} \times AR = 480 \times \frac{4}{3} = 640 \\
\text{Pixels per frame} &= 640 \times 480 = 307200 \\
\text{Pixels per second} &= 307200 \times 30 = 9.216 \times 10^{6} \\
\text{Bandwidth (approx.)} &= \frac{\text{Pixels per second}}{2} \\
B &= \frac{9.216 \times 10^{6}}{2} \\
B &\approx 4.58 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the standard NTSC video bandwidth of approximately **4.2 MHz**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Field and Frame Rates** — In interlaced systems, $f_{field} = 2 f_v$. Students often use the wrong multiplier.
> 2. **Forgetting to Multiply Lines by AR** — Aspect ratio directly affects bandwidth calculations. A 4:3 frame has 33% more horizontal pixels than vertical pixels.
> 3. **Mixing Up Sync and Blanking** — These are separate functions; sync = timing, blanking = beam-off.
> 4. **Incorrect Units** — Always state horizontal frequency in **Hz or kHz**, period in **μs or ms**.
> 5. **Not Drawing Field Diagrams** — In 7-mark questions on interlacing, a labeled diagram of odd/even field interleaving is mandatory; missing it loses 2-3 marks.
> 6. **Assuming All Lines Are Visible** — Remember the vertical blanking lines (50 lines in PAL) are not displayed.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Scanning Module**
>
> - **Scanning** converts 2-D image → 1-D electrical signal (transmitter) and vice-versa (receiver).
> - **Two types:** Progressive (full frame in one pass) and Interlaced (frame split into 2 fields).
> - **Interlace advantage:** Halves bandwidth, doubles perceived refresh rate.
> - **PAL standards:** 625 lines, 25 fps, 50 fields/s, 15.625 kHz horizontal.
> - **NTSC standards:** 525 lines, 30 fps (29.97), 60 fields/s, 15.75 kHz horizontal.
> - **Sync pulses** lock the receiver sweep to the transmitter (H-sync = line, V-sync = field/frame).
> - **Blanking pulses** extinguish the beam during retrace (H-blanking ~18%, V-blanking ~8%).
> - **Key formula:** $f_H = N \cdot f_v$
> - **Key formula:** $T_H = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot f_v}$
> - **Key formula:** $f_{field} = 2 f_v$ (interlaced only)
> - **Key formula:** $T_{active} = T_H (1 - \eta_H)$
> - **Key formula:** $N_{active} = N (1 - \eta_V)$
> - **Persistence of vision** threshold: ~16 Hz; broadcast uses 25/30 Hz to stay well above this.
> - **Horizontal retrace** occurs at end of every line (right edge → left edge).
> - **Vertical retrace** occurs at end of every field (bottom → top).
> - **Composite Video Signal (CVS)** = Video + Blanking + Horizontal Sync + Vertical Sync.
> - **PAL = 575 active lines**, **NTSC ≈ 484 active lines** out of the total.
> - Modern displays use **progressive scan** (LCD/LED/OLED at 60/120/144 Hz); interlaced is legacy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Horizontal and Vertical Synchronization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Horizontal and Vertical Synchronization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Horizontal Synchronization (H-Sync)** and **Vertical Synchronization (V-Sync)** are the timing-control pulses embedded in a composite video signal that lock the receiver's scanning electron beam (or pixel readout clock in LCD/LED panels) to the transmitter's camera scan. They ensure the picture is reproduced **geometrically stable**, with every line starting at the same horizontal position and every new frame beginning at the same vertical position on the display.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standard 2-Mark Format):**
> *"Synchronization is the process of locking the time-base of the receiver to that of the transmitter so that the scanning beam in the picture tube retraces the same path as the scanning beam in the camera tube. Horizontal sync controls line retrace; vertical sync controls field/frame retrace."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you and your friend are both writing the same paragraph on two separate whiteboards, line by line:
- **Horizontal Sync** is the "go back to the start of the *next line*" signal — like the bell that tells you to lift your pen and move it to the left margin of the line below.
- **Vertical Sync** is the "go back to the top of the *next page*" signal — like the louder bell that tells you to scroll your paper all the way up and start a fresh page.

If you don't hear these bells, your writing drifts to the right, lines overlap, and the page becomes a mess. That is exactly what happens to a TV without sync — the picture tears, rolls, or goes blank.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Horizontal rate ≈ 15.625 kHz to 33 kHz** (thousands of times per second).
> - **Vertical rate ≈ 50 Hz to 60 Hz** (dozens of times per second).
> - One vertical sync contains many horizontal syncs — typically **625 lines (PAL)** or **525 lines (NTSC)**.

## 1.3 Why Synchronization is Non-Negotiable

In entertainment electronics, **a single Hz of drift in the sync oscillator causes the entire picture to roll vertically or tear horizontally**. This is why all standards (NTSC, PAL, SECAM, HDTV, 4K) define sync timing to the microsecond.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Composite Video Signal Anatomy

A standard composite video waveform (per ITU-R BT.1701 / BT.470) is built from these time-interleaved components:

1. **Active Video** — the visible picture information (luminance + chroma + color burst).
2. **Horizontal Blanking Interval (HBI)** — a short dark period where the beam flies back from right to left.
3. **Horizontal Sync Pulse** — a narrow negative-going pulse inside the HBI that *triggers* the line retrace.
4. **Vertical Blanking Interval (VBI)** — a longer dark period where the beam flies from bottom to top.
5. **Vertical Sync Pulse** — a wider, serrated pulse train inside the VBI that *triggers* the field/frame retrace.
6. **Front Porch** — the brief gap between the end of active video and the start of the sync pulse.
7. **Back Porch** — the brief gap between the end of the sync pulse and the start of active video.
8. **Color Burst** — a 9-cycle reference of the color subcarrier placed on the back porch (NTSC/PAL only).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### A. Horizontal Sync (Line-Lock)
- At the end of every scanned line, the transmitter drops the video signal to "blacker than black" (≈ 0 IRE units, where **IRE = Institute of Radio Engineers, 1 IRE = 7.143 mV**).
- After a short **front porch** (≈ 1.5 µs), a **horizontal sync pulse** (≈ 4.7 µs wide, -40 IRE) is transmitted.
- The receiver's sync separator strips this pulse and feeds it to a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** controlling the line oscillator.
- During the sync pulse, the beam is *blanked* (electron gun cut off) and deflected back to the left.
- Once the pulse ends, the **back porch** (≈ 5.8 µs) and the **color burst** appear, then the next active line begins.

### B. Vertical Sync (Field/Frame-Lock)
- At the end of every field (interlaced) or frame (progressive), a long **vertical sync pulse train** replaces the normal H-sync pulses.
- For PAL/B, this serration contains **5 equalizing pulses** before and after the main V-sync pulse, ensuring odd and even fields interlace correctly.
- The V-sync pulse (≈ 2.5 lines wide, i.e. ≈ 160 µs for PAL) drives the vertical oscillator that returns the beam to the top.
- A **vertical blanking interval** of ~25 lines ensures the beam is invisible during the long flyback.

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Block

| Block | Engineering Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| Front Porch | Prevents the sync separator from latching on picture edges | Once per line |
| Sync Pulse | Hard, unambiguous trigger for retrace | H-rate / V-rate |
| Back Porch | Re-establishes DC reference & houses color burst | Once per line |
| Color Burst | Phase reference for the 4.43 MHz (PAL) / 3.58 MHz (NTSC) chroma subcarrier | Once per line |
| Equalizing Pulses | Maintain H-sync PLL lock *during* the vertical flyback | 2.5 line periods |
| Serrated V-Sync | Allows horizontal PLL to keep locking line-by-line inside the vertical interval | 5 line periods |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Line Frequency (NTSC M) | $f_H = \frac{4.5 \text{ MHz}}{286} \approx 15.734$ | kHz |
| 2 | Line Frequency (PAL B/G) | $f_H = \frac{4.43361875 \text{ MHz}}{284} \approx 15.625$ | kHz |
| 3 | Line Period (PAL) | $T_H = \frac{1}{f_H} = 64$ | µs |
| 4 | Field Rate (NTSC) | $f_V = 59.94$ | Hz |
| 5 | Field Rate (PAL) | $f_V = 50$ | Hz |
| 6 | Frames per Second | $F = \frac{f_V}{(\text{interlace factor})}$ | fps |
| 7 | Aspect Ratio | $AR = \frac{W}{H}$ (4:3, 16:9) | unitless |
| 8 | Sync-to-Line Ratio (H) | $\frac{T_{sync}}{T_H} \approx 0.075$ | unitless |
| 9 | Blanking Factor (H) | $\frac{T_{blank}}{T_H} \approx 0.18$ (PAL) | unitless |
| 10 | Vertical Blanking | $N_{VB} = 25$ (PAL) or $21$ (NTSC) lines | lines |
| 11 | Equalizing Pulse Width | $t_{eq} = 2.35$ | µs |
| 12 | Serration Pulse Width | $t_{serr} = 4.7$ | µs |
| 13 | IRE Unit Definition | $1 \text{ IRE} = \frac{1}{140} V_{pp} \approx 7.143$ | mV |
| 14 | HDTV 1080i (SMPTE 274M) | $f_H = 33.75$, $f_V = 60$ | kHz / Hz |
| 15 | Frame Frequency General | $f_{frame} = \frac{f_H}{N_{lines / frame}}$ | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Every modern digital display (LCD, OLED, MicroLED) still uses H-Sync and V-Sync as the fundamental timing reference. HDMI, DisplayPort, DVI, and VGA all carry H-Sync and V-Sync (or their embedded equivalent) — without them, the T-CON (timing controller) chip cannot align pixels row-by-row.

## 2.5 The 2:1 Interlace Trick (Why It Exists)

In interlaced systems, the vertical sync of **field 1 (odd lines)** is offset by half a line period ($\frac{T_H}{2}$) from **field 2 (even lines)**. This is achieved by the 5 pre-equalizing + 5 post-equalizing pulses sandwiching the serrated V-sync block.

$$t_{\text{offset}} = \frac{T_H}{2} = \frac{64 \,\mu s}{2} = 32 \,\mu s \quad \text{(PAL)}$$

This half-line offset is the *physical reason* old CRT TVs doubled the perceived vertical resolution without doubling the bandwidth.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Horizontal Line Frequency from Color Subcarrier

In **PAL-B/G**, the color subcarrier frequency $f_{SC}$ and horizontal line frequency $f_H$ are mathematically locked to prevent subcarrier harmonics from interfering with the baseband luminance (cross-color artifacts).

**Given Standards:**
$$f_{SC} = 4.43361875 \text{ MHz}$$
$$f_V = 50 \text{ Hz (nominal)}$$
$$\text{Interlace factor} = 2$$
$$N_{lines/frame} = 625$$

**Step 1:** Compute the number of lines per full frame (including vertical blanking).
$$N_{lines/frame} = 625$$

**Step 2:** Compute lines per second (all lines, active + blanked).
$$N_{lines/sec} = N_{lines/frame} \times f_{frame}$$

**Step 3:** Since 2:1 interlace is used, frames per second equals field rate / 2.
$$f_{frame} = \frac{f_V}{2} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the line-rate equation.
$$N_{lines/sec} = 625 \times 25 = 15{,}625 \text{ lines/sec}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to kHz.
$$f_H = 15{,}625 \text{ Hz} = 15.625 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 6:** Verify against the subcarrier lock formula.
$$f_H = \frac{f_{SC}}{284} = \frac{4{,}433{,}618.75}{284} = 15{,}625 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Logic Note:** The integer 284 comes from $\frac{4 \times 625}{25} \times 2 + 1 = 1137$... actually the canonical PAL relation is $f_{SC} = \frac{1135}{4} \times f_H + f_{V}/2$, simplified to $f_H = f_{SC}/284$ for 625/50 systems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sync Pulse Width from Sync-to-Line Ratio

**Given (PAL B/G):**
- $T_H = 64 \,\mu s$ (line period)
- Sync-to-line ratio $k = 0.075$

**Step 1:** Express the ratio definition.
$$k = \frac{T_{sync}}{T_H}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for sync pulse width.
$$T_{sync} = k \times T_H$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numeric values.
$$T_{sync} = 0.075 \times 64 \,\mu s$$

**Step 4:** Compute the product.
$$T_{sync} = 4.8 \,\mu s \approx 4.7 \,\mu s \text{ (standard rounded)}$$

**Step 5:** Cross-check — the back porch + front porch + active video must equal $T_H - T_{sync}$.
$$T_{back} + T_{front} + T_{active} = 64 - 4.7 = 59.3 \,\mu s$$

This matches the PAL spec where $T_{front} = 1.5 \,\mu s$, $T_{back} = 5.8 \,\mu s$ (incl. color burst), and $T_{active} \approx 52 \,\mu s$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Vertical Frequency Stability Bound

The receiver's vertical oscillator must stay locked within $\pm 2$ Hz of the transmitted 50 Hz to avoid visible roll. The PLL capture range is governed by:

$$\Delta f_{capture} = \pm \sqrt{f_V \times f_{loop, natural}}$$

**Given:** $f_V = 50$ Hz, $f_{loop,natural} = 0.5$ Hz

**Step 1:** Compute the product.
$$f_V \times f_{loop} = 50 \times 0.5 = 25$$

**Step 2:** Take the square root.
$$\Delta f = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3:** Apply as bounds.
$$f_{V,locked} \in \left[45 \text{ Hz}, \, 55 \text{ Hz}\right]$$

This is why a worn-out electrolytic capacitor in the vertical oscillator (drift > 5 Hz) makes a TV "roll" — the PLL can no longer hold lock.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sync Parameter Comparison Across Standards

| Parameter | NTSC M | PAL B/G | SECAM | HDTV 1080i/60 | HDTV 720p/60 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lines / Frame | 525 | 625 | 625 | 1125 | 750 |
| $f_V$ (Hz) | 59.94 | 50 | 50 | 60 | 60 |
| $f_H$ (kHz) | 15.734 | 15.625 | 15.625 | 33.75 | 45.00 |
| $T_H$ (µs) | 63.556 | 64.000 | 64.000 | 29.630 | 22.222 |
| $T_{sync}$ (µs) | 4.7 | 4.7 | 4.7 | 0.595 | 0.444 |
| H-Blank (µs) | 10.9 | 12.05 | 12.05 | 5.30 | 4.50 |
| V-Blank (lines) | 21 | 25 | 25 | 22.5* | 22.5* |
| Interlace | 2:1 | 2:1 | 2:1 | 2:1 | 1:1 (progressive) |
| Color Burst | Yes (3.58 MHz) | Yes (4.43 MHz) | No (FM chroma) | No (digital) | No (digital) |

*In HDTV, "V-Blank" is part of the digital horizontal total — measured in lines of digital blanking, not analog retrace time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Composite Video Sync Processing Chain

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Camera Sensor Output] --> B[Video Amplifier]
    B --> C[Sync Tip Clamp and DC Restore]
    C --> D[Add Front Porch 1.5 us]
    D --> E[Insert H Sync Pulse 4.7 us]
    E --> F[Insert Back Porch 5.8 us]
    F --> G[Inject Color Burst 9 cycles]
    G --> H[Composite Video Baseband Signal CVBS]
    H --> I[Receiver Sync Separator]
    I --> J[Horizontal PLL]
    I --> K[Vertical Integrator]
    J --> L[H Deflection Driver]
    K --> M[V Deflection Driver]
    L --> N[CRT Yoke or LCD TCON]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Stable Raster on Screen]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Timing Diagram — One Horizontal Line (PAL)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Active Video Line 52 us] --> P2[Front Porch 1.5 us]
    P2 --> P3[H Sync Pulse 4.7 us]
    P3 --> P4[Back Porch 5.8 us]
    P4 --> P5[Active Video Line 52 us Next]
```

> The total of these segments = $52 + 1.5 + 4.7 + 5.8 + \text{overlap} = 64 \,\mu s = T_H$ for PAL.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Vertical Field Structure (PAL, 2:1 Interlace)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Field 1 Odd Lines 312.5] --> B[Pre Equalizing 5 pulses]
    B --> C[Serrated V Sync 5 lines]
    C --> D[Post Equalizing 5 pulses]
    D --> E[Active Odd Lines 287.5]
    E --> F[Field 2 Even Lines 312.5]
    F --> G[Pre Equalizing 5 pulses]
    G --> H[Serrated V Sync 5 lines]
    H --> I[Post Equalizing 5 pulses]
    I --> J[Active Even Lines 287.5]
    J --> A
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Sync Separator (Receiver Side)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Composite Video In] --> B[Sync Tip Clamp]
    B --> C[Low Pass Filter Cutoff 1 MHz]
    C --> D[Schmitt Trigger 50 percent slicing]
    D --> E[Horizontal PLL VCO 15.625 kHz]
    D --> F[Vertical Integrator RC tau 30 us]
    F --> G[Vertical Oscillator 50 Hz]
    E --> H[H Drive to Deflection Yoke]
    G --> I[V Drive to Deflection Yoke]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a low-pass filter before sync slicing?** The 4.43 MHz color burst must be removed; otherwise its high-frequency content would falsely trigger the sync separator (called **false sync locking**).

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Modern Digital Replacement (HDMI/DisplayPort)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Digital Video Processor] --> B[TMDS Encoder HDMI]
    A --> C[8b10b Encoder DisplayPort]
    B --> D[H Sync Embedded in Data Island Period]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Receiver TCON Timing Controller]
    E --> F[Gate Driver Row Select]
    E --> G[Source Driver Column Data]
    F --> H[LCD or OLED Panel]
    G --> H
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define horizontal and vertical synchronization. State the standard horizontal and vertical frequencies for the PAL-B system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 mark) **H-Sync:** Timing pulses transmitted at the end of each line to return the scanning beam to the left of the screen.
- (1 mark) **V-Sync:** Timing pulses transmitted at the end of each field to return the beam to the top of the screen.
- (1 mark) **PAL-B values:** $f_H = 15.625 \text{ kHz}$, $f_V = 50 \text{ Hz}$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the function of the front porch and back porch in a composite video signal?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1.5 marks) **Front Porch:** Provides a guard interval between the active video and the H-sync pulse, ensuring the sync separator sees a clean DC step and preventing premature triggering on picture edges.
- (1.5 marks) **Back Porch:** Provides a recovery interval after the sync pulse and houses the **color burst** (8–10 cycles of subcarrier used as the chroma phase reference).

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the function of the **sync separator circuit** in a television receiver. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A PAL-B television system uses a color subcarrier of $4.43361875$ MHz and has 625 lines per frame operating at 50 fields/sec in 2:1 interlace. Calculate:
   (i) Horizontal line frequency
   (ii) Horizontal line period
   (iii) Width of the horizontal sync pulse if the sync-to-line ratio is 0.075. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Definition:**
A sync separator is a circuit that extracts the horizontal and vertical sync pulses from the composite video signal and routes them to the deflection oscillators.

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Block Diagram:**
Refer to Section 4.4 (Sync Separator Block Diagram). Key stages:
1. Sync tip clamp — restores DC level.
2. Low-pass filter — removes chroma subcarrier.
3. Schmitt trigger — slices the sync at 50% amplitude.
4. H-PLL & V-Integrator — separate H and V sync.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Working Principle:**
- The composite video enters the clamp, which holds the sync tip at a fixed DC potential.
- The LPF removes the 4.43 MHz color burst.
- The Schmitt trigger squares up the sync pulses.
- H-sync (4.7 µs) feeds directly to a PLL locking a 15.625 kHz VCO.
- V-sync (serrated, 2.5 lines wide) is integrated by an RC network ($\tau = 30 \,\mu s$) to produce a slow-rising ramp that triggers the 50 Hz vertical oscillator.

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Outputs:**
- Clean H-drive pulses → H-deflection output stage → yoke.
- Clean V-drive pulses → V-deflection output stage → yoke.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — Compute $f_{frame}$:**
$$f_{frame} = \frac{f_V}{2} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Compute $f_H$:**
$$f_H = N_{lines/frame} \times f_{frame} = 625 \times 25 = 15{,}625 \text{ Hz} = 15.625 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark) — Compute $T_H$:**
$$T_H = \frac{1}{f_H} = \frac{1}{15625} = 64 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 64 \,\mu s$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Compute sync pulse width:**
$$T_{sync} = 0.075 \times 64 \,\mu s = 4.8 \,\mu s \approx 4.7 \,\mu s$$

**Final Answer:** (i) $f_H = 15.625 \text{ kHz}$, (ii) $T_H = 64 \,\mu s$, (iii) $T_{sync} = 4.7 \,\mu s$.

---

### Question B (Alternative 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Draw and explain the **composite video waveform** of one horizontal line in the PAL-B system, labeling the front porch, sync pulse, back porch, and color burst. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain **2:1 interlaced scanning** with reference to the equalizing and serrated pulses in the vertical blanking interval of a PAL signal. Why is interlacing used? (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Overall Structure:**
One PAL line period = $T_H = 64 \,\mu s$, containing active video + H-blanking interval.

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Components in Order:**
1. Active video line — $\approx 52 \,\mu s$, contains luminance + chroma modulation.
2. Front porch — $1.5 \,\mu s$, 0 IRE (black level).
3. H-sync pulse — $4.7 \,\mu s$, $-40$ IRE (sync tip).
4. Back porch — $5.8 \,\mu s$, 0 IRE.
5. Color burst — 9 cycles of 4.43361875 MHz subcarrier on back porch.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Voltage Levels (IRE):**
- Active video: 0 IRE (black) to 100 IRE (white).
- Sync tip: $-40$ IRE.
- Total signal: $1 V_{pp}$ across $140$ IRE (i.e., $1$ IRE $= 7.143$ mV).

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Function of Each Block:**
- Front porch: Prevents sync separator false triggering.
- H-sync: Triggers line retrace.
- Back porch: DC re-establishment + houses color burst.
- Color burst: Phase reference for hue demodulation.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — Definition:**
2:1 interlace = every frame is split into **two fields**. Field 1 scans all odd lines (1, 3, 5, ..., 623), field 2 scans all even lines (2, 4, 6, ..., 624). Field 2 is offset by **half a line period** (32 µs in PAL) from Field 1.

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Role of Equalizing Pulses:**
- 5 pre-equalizing + 5 post-equalizing pulses (each $2.35 \,\mu s$ wide at $2\times$ the line rate) flank the serrated V-sync block.
- They keep the H-PLL locked *during* the vertical flyback and establish the half-line offset between odd and even fields.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Serrated V-Sync:**
- The V-sync block (5 line periods wide) is **serrated** — i.e., it contains narrow gaps at the H-rate, so the H-PLL still sees a pulse every 64 µs and stays locked.

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Why Interlace?**
- Doubles the perceived vertical resolution **without** doubling the video bandwidth.
- Prevents large-area flicker at 50/60 Hz refresh by presenting the eye with half the lines twice as often.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose Marks Here:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always write Hz, kHz, µs. Bare numbers lose 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Confusing frame rate vs field rate** — NTSC field rate is 59.94 Hz, frame rate is 29.97 Hz. Mixing them up costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Missing color burst in waveform** — Omitting the burst in the waveform question in Q.B(a) loses 1 full mark.
> 4. **Not stating the 2:1 interlace half-line offset** — Saying only "alternating odd and even" without the 32 µs offset is incomplete.
> 5. **Confusing front porch vs back porch** — Front porch is *before* sync; back porch is *after*. Common error, costs 0.5 mark.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **H-Sync** = line retrace, **V-Sync** = field/frame retrace.
- **PAL-B/G** uses $f_H = 15.625$ kHz, $f_V = 50$ Hz, $N_{lines} = 625$, $T_H = 64 \,\mu s$.
- **NTSC-M** uses $f_H = 15.734$ kHz, $f_V = 59.94$ Hz, $N_{lines} = 525$.
- **H-Sync pulse width** $\approx 4.7 \,\mu s$ in both standards.
- **Sync-to-line ratio** = $0.075$ → $T_{sync} = 0.075 \times T_H$.
- **IRE unit** = $\frac{1}{140} V_{pp} \approx 7.143$ mV; sync tip is at $-40$ IRE.
- **Front porch** = pre-sync guard band ($1.5 \,\mu s$).
- **Back porch** = post-sync DC recovery + houses color burst ($5.8 \,\mu s$).
- **Color burst** = 9 cycles of subcarrier ($4.43$ MHz PAL, $3.58$ MHz NTSC).
- **Vertical blanking** = 25 lines (PAL) / 21 lines (NTSC).
- **Equalizing pulses** = $2.35 \,\mu s$ wide, 2× line rate, ensure interlace half-line offset.
- **Serrated V-sync** = 5 line periods, contains H-rate gaps to keep H-PLL locked.
- **HDTV (1080i)** uses $f_H = 33.75$ kHz, $f_V = 60$ Hz, $N = 1125$, no color burst (digital).
- **PLL capture range** for V-oscillator typically $\pm 5$ Hz around 50 Hz.
- **Sync separator** order: Clamp → LPF (cut $\geq 1$ MHz) → Schmitt trigger (50% slice) → H-PLL + V-integrator.
- **Why interlace** = double vertical resolution without doubling bandwidth; reduce 50/60 Hz flicker.
- **Modern displays** (LCD/OLED) still use H-Sync and V-Sync — now embedded inside HDMI/DP TMDS data islands rather than as analog pulses.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Color Information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Color Information

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Color Information** is the structured representation, encoding, transmission, and reproduction of the visible portion of the electromagnetic spectrum (≈ **380 nm to 750 nm** wavelength) using standardized mathematical models that map physical light stimuli into perceptually meaningful parameters (luminance, hue, saturation, chrominance) suitable for storage, broadcast, and display in entertainment systems.

In the context of **Entertainment Electronics**, color information is the engineering discipline that bridges the gap between *physically measured spectral power distributions* and *human visual perception*, enabling faithful reproduction of scenes on televisions, cinema screens, mobile devices, and gaming consoles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Three-Judge Painting Competition:**
> Imagine a contest where every color painting is judged by exactly **three judges**: a *Red judge*, a *Green judge*, and a *Blue judge*. Each judge gives a score from 0 to 255 based on how strongly their color appears in the painting. A "yellow" painting receives a high score from the Red and Green judges but a low score from the Blue judge. The three scores together (R, G, B) *uniquely describe* the painting. This is exactly how the **human eye** perceives color — through three types of cone photoreceptor cells (L, M, S cones), and how displays recreate it — using three sub-pixels per pixel (Red, Green, Blue emitters).

### 1.3 Key Physical Parameters and Standards

| Parameter | Value / Range | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| Visible Spectrum | **380 nm – 750 nm** | Full range of human-visible light |
| Peak Luminous Efficiency | **683 lm/W** at 555 nm | Photometric constant (K_m) |
| Standard Illuminant D65 | **6504 K** (x=0.3127, y=0.3290) | Reference white for sRGB/HDTV |
| Standard Illuminant A | **2856 K** (Tungsten) | Reference for indoor lighting |
| Reference White (NTSC) | Illuminant C (6774 K) | Legacy broadcast standard |
| Luminance range (SDR) | **0.1 – 100 cd/m²** | Standard Dynamic Range |
| Color Gamut (sRGB) | 35.9% of CIE 1931 | Standard web/HD color space |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wavelength Bands of Primary Colors (for display primaries):**
> - **Red (R):** 620 – 750 nm
> - **Green (G):** 495 – 570 nm
> - **Blue (B):** 450 – 495 nm

### 1.4 The Human Visual System — Trichromatic Foundation

The human retina contains three types of cone cells, each sensitive to a different portion of the visible spectrum:

- **L-cones (Long wavelength):** Peak sensitivity ≈ **564 nm** (Red region)
- **M-cones (Medium wavelength):** Peak sensitivity ≈ **534 nm** (Green region)
- **S-cones (Short wavelength):** Peak sensitivity ≈ **420 nm** (Blue region)

Any color stimulus is therefore encoded by the brain as **three independent signals** — this is the physiological basis for the entire field of colorimetry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Trichromatic Theory and Grassmann's Laws

> [!NOTE]
> **Trichromatic Theory (Young-Helmholtz, 1802):** Any color can be matched by a linear combination of three suitably chosen primary stimuli. This is the mathematical foundation of all color models used in entertainment electronics.

**Grassmann's Laws (1853)** formalize color mixing for additive systems:

1. **Additivity Law:** If color $C_1 = a_1R + b_1G + c_1B$ and $C_2 = a_2R + b_2G + c_2B$, then
$$C_1 + C_2 = (a_1+a_2)R + (b_1+b_2)G + (c_1+c_2)B$$

2. **Scalar Multiplication:** $k \cdot C = (ka)R + (kb)G + (kc)B$

3. **Associativity & Linearity:** Color matching is a linear vector operation in tristimulus space.

### 2.2 CIE 1931 XYZ Color Space

The CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage) defined an imaginary, device-independent reference space:

$$\begin{aligned}
X &= k \int_{380}^{750} S(\lambda)\,\bar{x}(\lambda)\,d\lambda \\
Y &= k \int_{380}^{750} S(\lambda)\,\bar{y}(\lambda)\,d\lambda \\
Z &= k \int_{380}^{750} S(\lambda)\,\bar{z}(\lambda)\,d\lambda
\end{aligned}$$

where $S(\lambda)$ is the spectral power distribution of the source and $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$ are the **CIE 1931 Standard Observer Color Matching Functions**.

The normalization constant $k$ is chosen such that $Y = 100$ for a perfect diffuse white reflector.

**Chromaticity Coordinates (projecting to 2D):**

$$x = \frac{X}{X+Y+Z}, \quad y = \frac{Y}{X+Y+Z}, \quad z = \frac{Z}{X+Y+Z}$$

and crucially $x + y + z = 1$, so only two coordinates ($x, y$) are independent — this defines the famous **CIE 1931 Chromaticity Diagram** (horseshoe shape).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — these formulas appear every KTU exam cycle.**

| Color Model | Equations (R, G, B normalized [0,1]) | Used In |
|------------|--------------------------------------|---------|
| **Luminance (Luma Y')** | $Y' = 0.299R' + 0.587G' + 0.114B'$ | All analog/digital TV |
| **NTSC (YIQ)** | $\begin{aligned} Y &= 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B \\ I &= 0.596R - 0.274G - 0.322B \\ Q &= 0.211R - 0.523G + 0.312B \end{aligned}$ | NTSC (US, Japan) |
| **PAL (YUV)** | $\begin{aligned} Y &= 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B \\ U &= 0.493(B-Y) \\ V &= 0.877(R-Y) \end{aligned}$ | PAL (Europe, India) |
| **Digital (YCbCr, ITU-R BT.601)** | $\begin{aligned} Y &= 16 + 65.481R' + 128.553G' + 24.966B' \\ C_b &= 128 - 37.797R' - 74.203G' + 112.0B' \\ C_r &= 128 + 112.0R' - 93.786G' - 18.214B' \end{aligned}$ | SDTV, DVD, MPEG |
| **CMY (Subtractive)** | $C = 1 - R, \quad M = 1 - G, \quad Y = 1 - B$ | Printing |
| **CMYK** | $K = \min(C,M,Y)$, $C \leftarrow C-K$, $M \leftarrow M-K$, $Y \leftarrow Y-K$ | 4-color printing |
| **HSV → RGB** | $\begin{aligned} H' &= 6H \ (\text{sector}) \\ C &= V \cdot S \\ X &= C(1 - \vert H' \bmod 2 - 1\vert) \\ (R,G,B) &= (V-C) + \text{sector}(C,X,0) \end{aligned}$ | Image editing |
| **Color Temperature (CCT)** | Planckian locus approximation; mired shift $\Delta T = 10^6/T_2 - 10^6/T_1$ | White balance |
| **Color Difference (CIE76)** | $\Delta E^*_{ab} = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$ | Calibration |
| **Gamma Correction** | $V_{out} = V_{in}^{\gamma}$, typical $\gamma = 2.2$ | Display encoding |
| **Rec. 709 Luma (HD)** | $Y' = 0.2126R' + 0.7152G' + 0.0722B'$ | HDTV |
| **Rec. 2020 Gamut** | 75.8% of CIE 1931; primaries at 630, 532, 467 nm | UHDTV / 4K / 8K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical distinction:** The luminance formulas use **gamma-corrected** values (denoted $R', G', B'$, i.e., $R' = R^{1/\gamma}$). The IQ and UV chroma formulas sometimes use linear (un-gamma-corrected) RGB. Always check whether the question gives you "RGB" (linear) or "R'G'B'" (gamma-corrected).

### 2.4 Why Luminance-Chrominance Separation Matters in Entertainment

The key engineering insight is that **the human eye is far more sensitive to brightness (luminance Y) than to color (chrominance I, Q or U, V)**. Specifically:

- Spatial resolution of luminance perception: up to **~25 cycles/degree**
- Spatial resolution of chrominance perception: only **~10 cycles/degree** for red-green, **~6 cycles/degree** for yellow-blue

This asymmetry is exploited in **NTSC, PAL, and SECAM** television systems: the chroma signals are allocated a **narrower bandwidth** (~1.3 MHz) than the luma signal (~4.2 MHz), saving spectrum while preserving perceived quality.

### 2.5 Additive vs. Subtractive Color Mixing

| Property | Additive (RGB) | Subtractive (CMY) |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|
| Primary colors | Red, Green, Blue | Cyan, Magenta, Yellow |
| Combines via | Adding light | Subtracting/filtering light |
| White produced by | R + G + B = White | No combination (needs K) |
| Black produced by | No light (0,0,0) | C + M + Y ≈ Black (use K) |
| Used in | Displays (TV, monitor, phone) | Print, paint, photography |
| Color space type | Linear in light intensity | Multiplicative (logarithmic) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: RGB → YUV Conversion (PAL System)

**Starting point:** We want to express color in terms of one luminance component (Y) and two chrominance components (U, V), where U and V each contain zero mean and carry only the color difference information.

**Step 1: Definition of Luminance (based on subjective brightness sensitivity of human eye):**

$$Y = 0.299\,R + 0.587\,G + 0.114\,B$$

This weighting is the result of NTSC/PAL perceptual studies: green dominates human brightness perception (0.587), then red (0.299), then blue (0.114). These coefficients sum to **1.000**, ensuring that white (R=G=B=1) maps to Y=1.

**Step 2: Definition of Chrominance difference signals:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(R - Y) &= R - (0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B) \\
(R - Y) &= 0.701R - 0.587G - 0.114B
\end{aligned}$$

Similarly:
$$(B - Y) = -0.299R - 0.587G + 0.886B$$

**Step 3: Scale the chroma signals for full range utilization:**

To use the full available bandwidth of the chroma channels, scale the differences so that pure red and pure blue map to the limits of the U, V range:

$$U = 0.493\,(B - Y) \quad \text{(scale factor 0.493 chosen so that pure blue gives U = 0.436)}$$

$$V = 0.877\,(R - Y) \quad \text{(scale factor 0.877 chosen so that pure red gives V = 0.600)}$$

**Step 4: Final expanded form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y &= 0.299\,R + 0.587\,G + 0.114\,B \\
U &= -0.147\,R - 0.289\,G + 0.436\,B \\
V &= 0.615\,R - 0.515\,G - 0.100\,B
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** For pure red (R=1, G=0, B=0): $Y=0.299$, $U=-0.147$, $V=0.615$. The chroma values describe the color "red" relative to the luminance, confirming correct decomposition.

### 3.2 Derivation: YUV → RGB Inverse Conversion

**Step 1:** We have three unknowns R, G, B and three knowns Y, U, V. Solve the linear system.

**Step 2:** From the definitions of U and V:
$$\begin{aligned}
B - Y &= U / 0.493 = 2.028\,U \\
R - Y &= V / 0.877 = 1.140\,V
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the luminance equation. We know $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$, and we have expressions for R and B in terms of Y, U, V:

$$R = Y + 1.140\,V, \quad B = Y + 2.028\,U$$

Therefore:
$$Y = 0.299(Y + 1.140V) + 0.587G + 0.114(Y + 2.028U)$$

$$Y = 0.299Y + 0.341V + 0.587G + 0.114Y + 0.231U$$

$$Y = (0.299 + 0.114)Y + 0.587G + 0.341V + 0.231U$$

$$Y = 0.413Y + 0.587G + 0.341V + 0.231U$$

**Step 4:** Solve for G:

$$0.587G = Y - 0.413Y - 0.341V - 0.231U = 0.587Y - 0.341V - 0.231U$$

$$G = Y - 0.581V - 0.394U$$

**Step 5: Final inverse equations (rounded for the PAL system):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= Y + 1.140\,V \\
G &= Y - 0.395\,U - 0.581\,V \\
B &= Y + 2.032\,U
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This inverse system is what the **TV receiver** implements to reconstruct RGB signals for the display from the received YUV composite signal.

### 3.3 Derivation: CIE 1931 Chromaticity Coordinates

**Given:** Tristimulus values $X = 41.24$, $Y = 35.76$, $Z = 18.14$ for a certain color sample (from a spectrophotometer reading under Illuminant D65).

**Step 1: Calculate the sum $X + Y + Z$:**
$$X + Y + Z = 41.24 + 35.76 + 18.14 = 95.14$$

**Step 2: Calculate the chromaticity coordinates:**

$$x = \frac{X}{X+Y+Z} = \frac{41.24}{95.14} = 0.4335$$

$$y = \frac{Y}{X+Y+Z} = \frac{35.76}{95.14} = 0.3759$$

**Step 3: Verify that $z$ is consistent:**
$$z = \frac{Z}{X+Y+Z} = \frac{18.14}{95.14} = 0.1907$$

**Step 4: Check the constraint:**
$$x + y + z = 0.4335 + 0.3759 + 0.1907 = 1.0001 \approx 1.000 \ \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> The point $(0.4335, 0.3759)$ lies on the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram, where it can be identified as a saturated reddish-orange. Pure white (D65) is at $(0.3127, 0.3290)$.

### 3.4 Derivation: HSV → RGB Conversion

**Given:** Hue $H = 210°$, Saturation $S = 0.75$, Value $V = 0.80$ (a sky-blue color).

**Step 1: Determine the chroma:**
$$C = V \cdot S = 0.80 \times 0.75 = 0.60$$

**Step 2: Determine the sector of the hue hexagon:**
$$H' = \frac{H}{60°} = \frac{210°}{60°} = 3.5$$

This places us in **sector 3** (between Blue and Magenta), with the intermediate value $H' = 3.5$.

**Step 3: Compute the intermediate X value:**
$$X = C \cdot \left(1 - \left\vert (H' \bmod 2) - 1 \right\vert \right)$$
$$X = 0.60 \cdot \left(1 - \left\vert (3.5 \bmod 2) - 1 \right\vert \right) = 0.60 \cdot \left(1 - \left\vert 1.5 - 1 \right\vert \right) = 0.60 \cdot 0.5 = 0.30$$

**Step 4: Compute the brightness offset (m):**
$$m = V - C = 0.80 - 0.60 = 0.20$$

**Step 5: Apply sector 3 mapping (sector indices: 0=RG, 1=GB, 2=GB², 3=BR, 4=BR², 5=RB):**
For sector 3, the (R', G', B') tuple is $(0, X, C) = (0, 0.30, 0.60)$.

**Step 6: Add the offset to all three channels:**
$$\begin{aligned}
R &= 0 + m = 0.20 \\
G &= 0.30 + m = 0.50 \\
B &= 0.60 + m = 0.80
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:** RGB = (51, 128, 204) in 8-bit — a medium sky-blue. ✓

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common Color Model Trade-offs in Entertainment Systems

| Use Case | Best Model | Reason | Bit Depth |
|----------|-----------|--------|-----------|
| **Camera sensor (RAW)** | RGB (Bayer filter) | Direct physical capture | 12–16 bit per channel |
| **Studio editing** | HSV / Lab | Intuitive control of hue/saturation | 8–16 bit |
| **Broadcast analog TV** | YIQ (NTSC) / YUV (PAL) | Backward-compatible with B/W, bandwidth saving | Analog |
| **Digital SDTV (DVD)** | YCbCr (BT.601) | Digital, subsampling 4:2:0, 4:2:2 | 8 bit |
| **Digital HDTV (Blu-ray)** | YCbCr (BT.709) | Wider gamut, HD resolution | 8/10 bit |
| **4K / 8K UHDTV** | YCbCr (BT.2020) | Wide color gamut, HDR | 10/12 bit |
| **Web display (sRGB)** | RGB (gamma 2.2) | Standard monitor input | 8 bit |
| **Cinema projection (DCI-P3)** | XYZ / linear RGB | Wide gamut, reference quality | 12–16 bit |
| **Print / magazine** | CMYK | Subtractive, ink-based | 8 bit per channel |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Color Information Flow in a Television Transmission System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sceneA["Scene with RGB Light"] --> camA["Camera RGB Sensors"]
    camA --> matA["RGB to YUV Matrix"]
    matA --> filt1["Luma LPF 4.2 MHz"]
    matA --> filt2["Chroma BPF 1.3 MHz"]
    filt1 --> muxA["Frequency Multiplexer Composite Video"]
    filt2 --> muxA
    muxA --> chanA["Channel Transmitter"]
    chanA --> chanB["Channel Receiver"]
    chanB --> demuxA["Y C Demultiplexer"]
    demuxA --> inv1["Inverse YUV to RGB Matrix"]
    inv1 --> dispA["Display RGB Pixels"]
    whiteA["Reference White D65 6504K"] --> matA
    whiteA --> inv1
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: CIE 1931 Chromaticity Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Light Source SPD S lambda"] --> nodeB["Multiply by Observer CMF x_bar"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Multiply by Observer CMF y_bar"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Multiply by Observer CMF z_bar"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Integrate from 380 to 750 nm"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Integrate from 380 to 750 nm"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Integrate from 380 to 750 nm"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Normalize k factor to Y equals 100"]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["X value"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["Y value"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["Z value"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["Compute x equals X over X plus Y plus Z"]
    nodeJ --> nodeM["Compute y equals Y over X plus Y plus Z"]
    nodeL --> nodeN["Plot point on Chromaticity Diagram"]
    nodeM --> nodeN
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: YCbCr 4:2:0 Chroma Subsampling for Digital Video

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pixA["Pixel 0 0 R0 G0 B0"] --> blockA["Block 2x2 Pixels"]
    pixB["Pixel 1 0 R1 G1 B1"] --> blockA
    pixC["Pixel 0 1 R2 G2 B2"] --> blockA
    pixD["Pixel 1 1 R3 G3 B3"] --> blockA
    blockA --> yconv["Convert all 4 to Y"]
    blockA --> cconv["Compute single Cb Cr average"]
    yconv --> ystore["Y0 Y1 Y2 Y3 stored"]
    cstore["Cb Cr stored once for block"] --> cstore
    cconv --> cstore
    ystore --> outA["Total bits: 4xY plus 1xCb plus 1xCr"]
    cstore --> outA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (nodeA, pixA, etc.), labels are uppercase raw text without bold/italic markdown, and reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are never used as node names.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

State the three **Grassmann's laws** of additive color mixing. Why are they important in television color encoding?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three Grassmann's laws are:
1. **Additivity Law:** The color match of a mixture of two color stimuli is the sum of their individual tristimulus vectors: $C_1 + C_2 = (a_1+a_2)R + (b_1+b_2)G + (c_1+c_2)B$.
2. **Scalar Law:** Scaling the luminance of a stimulus by $k$ scales all three tristimulus values by $k$.
3. **Linear Independence / Associativity:** The three primaries are linearly independent, and color matching is associative across observers.

**Importance in TV encoding (1 mark):** These laws justify treating color as a 3-dimensional linear vector space, which enables the linear **RGB ↔ YUV matrix transformations** used in NTSC and PAL broadcast systems. Without linearity, the cheap 3×3 matrix encoder at the transmitter and the 3×3 decoder at the receiver would not work.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

What is the **CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram**? How is it used to specify a color using only two parameters instead of three?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram (1 mark) is a 2D plot of the normalized chromaticity coordinates $(x, y)$ derived from the CIE XYZ tristimulus values. Any visible color corresponds to a unique point inside or on the horseshoe-shaped boundary (the **spectrum locus**).

**Reduction from 3D to 2D (2 marks):** Since the three chromaticity coordinates satisfy the constraint $x + y + z = 1$, only two of them are independent. So $z$ is redundant: $z = 1 - x - y$. The luminance (Y) is preserved separately, giving the complete color description with 3 numbers: $Y$ (brightness), $x$ (redness), $y$ (greenness). This is the basis for specifying any color (gamut, white points, primaries) graphically.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2, Module 1, 7 marks]**

A video frame has uniform color with $R = 200/255$, $G = 100/255$, $B = 50/255$. Compute the **NTSC YIQ** components and the corresponding **CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates** (assume sRGB primaries, D65 white, with the standard matrix for sRGB→XYZ). Verify whether the color lies within the sRGB gamut.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Normalize to [0,1]:**
$$R = 0.7843, \quad G = 0.3922, \quad B = 0.1961$$

**Step 2 (3 marks) — Apply NTSC YIQ equations:**
$$\begin{aligned}
Y &= 0.299(0.7843) + 0.587(0.3922) + 0.114(0.1961) \\
  &= 0.2345 + 0.2302 + 0.0224 = 0.4871 \\
I &= 0.596(0.7843) - 0.274(0.3922) - 0.322(0.1961) \\
  &= 0.4674 - 0.1075 - 0.0631 = 0.2968 \\
Q &= 0.211(0.7843) - 0.523(0.3922) + 0.312(0.1961) \\
  &= 0.1655 - 0.2051 + 0.0612 = 0.0216
\end{aligned}$$

So **Y = 0.487, I = 0.297, Q = 0.022**. (The high I and near-zero Q indicates this color is in the orange-red region, dominated by red-green axis.)

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Round-trip verification (YIQ → RGB):**
$$\begin{aligned}
R' &= Y + 0.956I + 0.621Q = 0.487 + 0.2837 + 0.0134 = 0.784 \ \checkmark \\
G' &= Y - 0.272I - 0.647Q = 0.487 - 0.0807 - 0.0140 = 0.392 \ \checkmark \\
B' &= Y - 1.106I + 1.703Q = 0.487 - 0.3283 + 0.0368 = 0.196 \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Compute XYZ using sRGB→XYZ matrix (D65):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.4124 & 0.3576 & 0.1805 \\ 0.2126 & 0.7152 & 0.0722 \\ 0.0193 & 0.1192 & 0.9505 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.7843 \\ 0.3922 \\ 0.1961 \end{bmatrix} \\
X &= 0.4124(0.7843) + 0.3576(0.3922) + 0.1805(0.1961) = 0.3234 + 0.1403 + 0.0354 = 0.4991 \\
Y &= 0.2126(0.7843) + 0.7152(0.3922) + 0.0722(0.1961) = 0.1667 + 0.2805 + 0.0142 = 0.4614 \\
Z &= 0.0193(0.7843) + 0.1192(0.3922) + 0.9505(0.1961) = 0.0151 + 0.0467 + 0.1864 = 0.2482
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 (Chromaticity, 1 mark) — Compute (x, y):**
$$x = \frac{0.4991}{0.4991 + 0.4614 + 0.2482} = \frac{0.4991}{1.2087} = 0.4129$$
$$y = \frac{0.4614}{1.2087} = 0.3818$$

**Step 6 (Verification, 1 mark) — Gamut check:**
The sRGB primaries are at (R: 0.64, 0.33), (G: 0.30, 0.60), (B: 0.15, 0.06). The triangle formed by these contains the point (0.4129, 0.3818) — confirmed visually it lies in the orange region. So **the color is inside the sRGB gamut.** ✓

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2, Module 1, 7 marks]**

Explain the **YCbCr color model** used in digital video. Derive the **4:2:0 chroma subsampling scheme** and compute the storage savings over **4:4:4** for a 1920×1080 frame.

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — YCbCr definition (3 marks):**

YCbCr separates an image into:
- **Y** (Luma): Perceived brightness, $Y = 0.299R' + 0.587G' + 0.114B'$ (BT.601 weights).
- **Cb** (Blue chroma): Difference between blue and luma, $C_b = 0.564(B'-Y) + 0.5$ (the +0.5 offsets the signal to unsigned 8-bit range [16, 235] for Y and [16, 240] for chroma).
- **Cr** (Red chroma): Difference between red and luma, $C_r = 0.713(R'-Y) + 0.5$.

**Purpose:** Enables lossy compression (chroma has less visual acuity), separates brightness (used for sync, edge detection, motion estimation in codecs) from color (used purely for hue/saturation), and matches the analog YIQ/YUV heritage.

**Part 2 — 4:2:0 subsampling derivation (2 marks):**

| Scheme | Y samples / 2×2 block | Cb samples | Cr samples | Total / 4 pixels |
|--------|----------------------|------------|------------|------------------|
| 4:4:4 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 12 (no compression) |
| 4:2:2 | 4 | 2 | 2 | 8 (33% saving) |
| 4:2:0 | 4 | 1 | 1 | 6 (50% saving) |

In **4:2:0**, a 2×2 block of pixels shares a single (Cb, Cr) pair — typically the average of the four chroma values. All four pixels retain full luma (Y) at native resolution, but chroma is shared at quarter resolution.

**Part 3 — Storage calculation (2 marks):**

Assume 8 bits per sample. For a **1920 × 1080** frame:
- Total pixels = 1920 × 1080 = **2,073,600 pixels**

**4:4:4 storage:**
$$\text{Bytes} = 2{,}073{,}600 \times 3 = 6{,}220{,}800 \text{ bytes} = 6.22 \text{ MB/frame}$$

**4:2:0 storage:**
Average samples per pixel = $6/4 = 1.5$
$$\text{Bytes} = 2{,}073{,}600 \times 1.5 = 3{,}110{,}400 \text{ bytes} = 3.11 \text{ MB/frame}$$

**Savings:**
$$\Delta = 6.22 - 3.11 = 3.11 \text{ MB/frame} \quad (\text{50% reduction})$$

For 30 fps video over 1 hour: $3.11 \text{ MB} \times 30 \times 3600 = 335{,}880 \text{ MB} = 335.88 \text{ GB}$ saved per hour of video.

---

### 5.3 Alternative Choice — Q4(a) & Q4(b)

---

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 3, 7 marks]**

Compare the **additive RGB** and **subtractive CMY** color models. Why does the printing industry use CMYK instead of pure CMY? Show with the **RGB → CMY** conversion for a magenta ink (RGB = 255, 0, 255).

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Feature | RGB | CMY |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Type | Additive (light) | Subtractive (pigment) |
| Primaries | Red, Green, Blue | Cyan, Magenta, Yellow |
| White = | R+G+B | No combination (use K=0 paper) |
| Black = | (0,0,0) | C+M+Y (impure; use K) |
| Medium | Emissive (displays) | Reflective (paper, print) |
| Gamut | Larger | Smaller |

**Why CMYK (2 marks):**
1. Pure C+M+Y inks in practice produce a muddy dark brown, not true black, due to pigment impurities and incomplete absorption.
2. Using a separate **K (Key/Black) plate** is cheaper (less ink used) and gives sharp, true black text.
3. The K plate also adds detail and contrast in shadow regions.
4. It is more stable across different paper types and lighting conditions.

**Conversion for magenta (RGB = 255, 0, 255) (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 1 - R/255 = 1 - 1 = 0 \\
M &= 1 - G/255 = 1 - 0 = 1 \\
Y &= 1 - B/255 = 1 - 1 = 0
\end{aligned}$$
So CMY = (0, 1, 0) = pure magenta ink. The result is a single-channel magenta ink, exactly as expected.

For CMYK:
$$K = \min(0, 1, 0) = 0, \quad C' = 0-0 = 0, \quad M' = 1-0 = 1, \quad Y' = 0-0 = 0$$
So CMYK = (0, 1, 0, 0) — only the magenta plate is inked. ✓

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 3, 7 marks]**

Explain **gamma correction** in the context of color information for displays. Derive the relationship between the encoded signal $V'$ and the linear light output $V$, given that the display has a power-law response of exponent $\gamma$. Why is the standard gamma value chosen as 2.2?

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual explanation (3 marks):**

A CRT (Cathode Ray Tube) does not respond linearly to its input voltage. The intensity of light emitted from a phosphor spot is approximately proportional to the input voltage raised to a power: $I \propto V_{in}^{\gamma}$, with $\gamma_{CRT} \approx 2.2$ to 2.5. If we naively stored image data linearly ($V_{in} \propto L$, where $L$ is desired light), the image would appear far too bright in the midtones, with detail crushed in the shadows.

**Gamma correction** is the deliberate pre-distortion of the stored signal so that, after the display's natural gamma curve, the final light output is approximately linear with the original scene intensity. The chain is:

$$V_{stored} = V_{linear}^{1/\gamma}$$

$$V_{displayed} = V_{stored}^{\gamma_{display}} = (V_{linear}^{1/\gamma})^{\gamma} = V_{linear}$$

if $\gamma = \gamma_{display}$.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

Let the scene radiance be $L$ (linear, [0,1]). The camera/encoder applies gamma correction:
$$V' = L^{1/\gamma}$$

The display applies its own gamma (assumed equal to $\gamma$ for the system design):
$$L_{out} = (V')^{\gamma} = (L^{1/\gamma})^{\gamma} = L$$

Thus $L_{out} = L$ — the displayed light exactly matches the captured light. ✓

**Why gamma = 2.2 (2 marks):**
1. **CRT heritage:** Early displays naturally exhibited $\gamma \approx 2.2$; the standard was set to exploit this behavior.
2. **Perceptual uniformity:** Human perception of brightness is approximately logarithmic (Weber-Fechner law). A gamma of 2.2 spreads the code values more evenly across perceptually distinguishable brightness steps, reducing visible banding in dark regions where the eye is most sensitive.
3. **Noise masking:** Gamma pre-distortion allocates more bits/code values to dark regions where the eye is most sensitive, maximizing effective bit depth in the perceptual domain.
4. **Compatibility:** Standardized at 2.2 in sRGB, BT.709, and BT.2020 — every device in the chain (camera, encoder, decoder, display) must agree on this constant for color fidelity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing linear and gamma-corrected RGB:** The NTSC Y formula ($Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$) is for **linear** RGB. For digital video with gamma-corrected $R'G'B'$ (denoted with primes), the same coefficients apply, but the result is called **luma** $Y'$ (not luminance $Y$). Students often forget the prime distinction and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **CIE normalization:** A common error is to compute $x = X/Y$ instead of $x = X/(X+Y+Z)$. The denominator is the **sum**, not just Y.
> 3. **Chroma subsampling ratio:** 4:2:0 does **not** mean 4 Y, 2 Cb, 0 Cr. It means 4 luma samples horizontally, 2 chroma samples horizontally, 0 chroma samples vertically. The result is 6 samples per 4 pixels, not "no blue."
> 4. **YUV ≠ YCbCr:** YUV is the analog PAL signal; YCbCr is its digital scaled version with offsets (the +128 in 8-bit and +0.5 in floating point). They use the same matrix but different ranges.
> 5. **Chromaticity coordinates** must satisfy $x + y + z = 1$. If your answer gives $x + y + z \neq 1$, you have made an arithmetic mistake.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Color Information**

- [ ] **Visible spectrum:** 380–750 nm; peak sensitivity at 555 nm (683 lm/W).
- [ ] **Trichromatic theory:** Three cone types (L, M, S) → all colors are 3D vectors.
- [ ] **Grassmann's laws:** Additivity, scalar multiplication, linear independence — justify matrix encoding.
- [ ] **CIE 1931:** $X, Y, Z$ tristimulus; $x = X/(X+Y+Z)$, $y = Y/(X+Y+Z)$, $x+y+z = 1$.
- [ ] **Reference whites:** D65 (6504K, sRGB/HDTV), A (2856K, tungsten), C (NTSC legacy).
- [ ] **Luminance (BT.601):** $Y' = 0.299R' + 0.587G' + 0.114B'$ — memorize.
- [ ] **Luminance (BT.709/HD):** $Y' = 0.2126R' + 0.7152G' + 0.0722B'$ — different coefficients.
- [ ] **NTSC YIQ:** Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B; I = 0.596R − 0.274G − 0.322B; Q = 0.211R − 0.523G + 0.312B.
- [ ] **PAL YUV:** Y same; U = 0.493(B−Y); V = 0.877(R−Y). U is blue chroma, V is red chroma.
- [ ] **YCbCr (digital):** Adds +0.5 offset to chroma (or +128 in 8-bit) to keep values unsigned.
- [ ] **CMY subtractive:** C = 1−R, M = 1−G, Y = 1−B. CMYK adds K = min(C,M,Y) for pure black and ink savings.
- [ ] **HSV:** Hue (0–360°), Saturation (0–1), Value (0–1). Best for intuitive color editing.
