- [ ] **Chroma subsampling:** 4:4:4 = full, 4:2:2 = half horizontal, 4:2:0 = half in both directions (50% saving).
- [ ] **Gamma = 2.2:** Standard for sRGB, BT.709, BT.2020. Pre-distortion = $V' = V^{1/2.2}$.
- [ ] **Color difference:** $\Delta E = \sqrt{(\Delta L)^2 + (\Delta a)^2 + (\Delta b)^2}$ (CIELAB). $\Delta E < 1$ = imperceptible.
- [ ] **Bandwidth trick:** Eye sees luminance sharper than chroma → NTSC/PAL allocate ~1.3 MHz to chroma vs. ~4.2 MHz to luma.
- [ ] **Rec. 2020:** UHDTV primaries at 630, 532, 467 nm — 75.8% of CIE gamut.
- [ ] **sRGB gamut:** 35.9% of CIE 1931; primaries at (0.64, 0.33), (0.30, 0.60), (0.15, 0.06).
- [ ] **D65 white point:** (0.3127, 0.3290) — the universal "perfect white" reference.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Methods: NTSC and PAL Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERPRISE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transmission Methods: NTSC and PAL Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is an Analogue Colour Television Transmission Standard?

A **colour television transmission standard** is a globally agreed-upon engineering specification that defines how a full-colour moving picture, along with its associated audio and synchronizing information, is encoded, modulated, and broadcast over a radio-frequency (RF) channel so that any compatible receiver can faithfully reconstruct the image. The standard dictates parameters such as the number of scanning lines, frame/field rate, channel bandwidth, the method of combining **luminance** (brightness) and **chrominance** (colour) information, and the audio carrier offset.

The three dominant historical analogue colour TV broadcast standards are **NTSC**, **PAL**, and **SECAM**. For the scope of this KTU module, we focus on **NTSC** and **PAL**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (OEEVT722, Module 1):**
> *Transmission Methods – Block schematic explanation of the NTSC and PAL colour TV system. Standards, comparison, merits and demerits.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU-Standard Definition:**
> "A television transmission standard is a set of technical specifications — including line frequency, field frequency, channel bandwidth, colour subcarrier frequency, and modulation scheme — that ensures interoperability between transmitter and receiver in a given region of the world."

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Recipe Card" of Television

Imagine you are a chef who has to tell 100 different kitchens across the country how to recreate the *exact same* dish.

- The **dish** = the picture on the TV screen.
- The **recipe card** = the **transmission standard** (NTSC or PAL).
- The **recipe** must specify: how many ingredients (lines), how fast to stir (frame rate), how much heat (bandwidth), and how to mix the colours (luminance + chrominance).

If the chef in Kerala writes a recipe using metric units, and a chef in the USA uses imperial units, the kitchens will produce *different-looking* dishes from the same original picture. That is exactly why the world split into NTSC and PAL regions — different engineers, in different decades, chose slightly different "recipes" for transmitting colour.

In essence:
- **NTSC** is the *American* recipe.
- **PAL** is the *European* recipe.
- Both recipes mix the same two "ingredients" — **brightness (Y)** and **colour (C)** — but in slightly different proportions and timings.

## 1.3 Why Two Standards? The Historical Context

| Region | Standard | Year Adopted | Lines | Frame Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| USA, Canada, Japan, S. Korea | **NTSC** | 1953 (colour) | **525** | **29.97** fps |
| UK, Germany, India, Australia | **PAL** | 1967 | **625** | **25** fps |
| France, Russia, parts of Africa | **SECAM** | 1967 | 625 | 25 fps |

The **line frequency** and **mains electricity frequency** in a country are deliberately synchronized (to avoid mains hum appearing as a horizontal bar on the screen). Since the USA uses **60 Hz** mains, NTSC was built around 60 fields/s; Europe uses **50 Hz** mains, so PAL was built around 50 fields/s.

## 1.4 Block Schematic Overview of a Colour TV System

Both NTSC and PAL share the same high-level architecture:

```
[Camera (R, G, B)] → [Matrix Encoder] → [Luminance (Y) + Chrominance (I,Q or U,V)]
                                          ↓
                              [Composite Video Signal]
                                          ↓
                          [Modulator → RF Channel → Antenna]
                                          ↓
                                  [TV Receiver]
                                          ↓
                              [Matrix Decoder] → [R, G, B] → [Display]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Block Insight for KTU:**
> The **only** fundamental difference between NTSC and PAL lies in **how the colour-difference signals are mathematically constructed and modulated onto the colour subcarrier**. The camera, display, and synchronizing structure remain conceptually identical.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s |
| NTSC Channel Bandwidth | **6 MHz** | MHz |
| PAL Channel Bandwidth (B/G) | **7 MHz** (some variants 8 MHz) | MHz |
| NTSC Field Rate | **59.94** Hz | Hz |
| PAL Field Rate | **50** Hz | Hz |
| Aspect Ratio (both) | **4 : 3** | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Foundational Concept: Luminance + Chrominance

A human eye is far more sensitive to **brightness (luminance)** detail than to **colour (chrominance)** detail. Both NTSC and PAL exploit this biological fact to **share a single channel** for both signals.

- The **luminance signal** $Y$ carries the *brightness* detail and occupies the *full video bandwidth* (e.g., 4.2 MHz in NTSC-M, 5.5 MHz in PAL-B).
- The **chrominance signal** carries the *colour* detail and is "squeezed" into the *upper part* of the same spectrum using a **colour subcarrier** — a technique called **Frequency Interleaving**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Interleaving Principle:**
> Because the luminance spectrum has energy gaps at multiples of the *line frequency*, the chrominance subcarrier is placed precisely in the largest gap so that luminance and chrominance spectra do not overlap and cause mutual interference (cross-luminance / cross-chrominance).

## 2.2 NTSC — National Television System Committee

### 2.2.1 NTSC Luminance Equation

The luminance signal in NTSC is defined as a weighted sum of the three primary colour voltages:

$$
Y = 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B
$$

These weights come from the **CIE photometric luminosity function** — the eye's response to red, green, and blue light.

### 2.2.2 NTSC Colour-Difference Signals: I and Q

NTSC does **not** transmit $R-G$ and $B-Y$ directly. Instead, it uses a clever rotated matrix producing the **I** (In-phase) and **Q** (Quadrature) signals:

$$
I = 0.596 R - 0.274 G - 0.322 B
$$

$$
Q = 0.211 R - 0.523 G + 0.312 B
$$

The **I-axis** lies along the orange–cyan axis, and the **Q-axis** lies along the purple–green axis. Human eyes are sharper for orange–cyan transitions than purple–green ones, so:
- **I** is given a wider bandwidth: $\approx$ **1.3 MHz**.
- **Q** is given a narrower bandwidth: $\approx$ **0.6 MHz**.

### 2.2.3 NTSC Quadrature Modulation

The $I$ and $Q$ signals modulate a colour subcarrier of frequency:

$$
f_{sc(NTSC)} = 3.579545 \text{ MHz} \approx 3.58 \text{ MHz}
$$

using **Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM)** — a **suppressed-carrier double-sideband** scheme. The $I$ signal modulates the **in-phase** carrier $\cos(\omega_c t)$, while $Q$ modulates the **quadrature** carrier $\sin(\omega_c t)$:

$$
C(t) = I(t)\cos(\omega_c t) + Q(t)\sin(\omega_c t)
$$

The full composite NTSC video signal is:

$$
v(t) = Y(t) + I(t)\cos(\omega_c t) + Q(t)\sin(\omega_c t) + \text{(sync + burst)}
$$

### 2.2.4 The Colour Burst

To allow the receiver to recover a clean reference of the subcarrier's phase (essential for decoding hue), NTSC transmits an **8–9 cycle burst** of the subcarrier located on the **back porch** of every horizontal blanking pulse. Its phase is defined as **+180°** relative to the $B-Y$ axis (i.e., along the $-I$ direction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical NTSC Weakness:** The colour information is encoded in the **phase** of the subcarrier. Any phase distortion (e.g., caused by transmission path non-linearities, multipath, or imperfect receiver tuning) directly translates to a **wrong colour (hue error)**. Engineers famously joked: *"NTSC stands for Never The Same Colour."*

## 2.3 PAL — Phase Alternating Line

### 2.3.1 PAL Luminance Equation

PAL uses the same luminance equation but with slightly different weights (the EBU primaries differ slightly from the NTSC/SMPTE primaries):

$$
Y = 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B \quad \text{(effectively same as NTSC)}
$$

### 2.3.2 PAL Colour-Difference Signals: U and V

PAL transmits the colour-difference signals $U$ and $V$:

$$
U = 0.493 (B - Y)
$$

$$
V = 0.877 (R - Y)
$$

Both $U$ and $V$ are given equal bandwidth of approximately **1.3 MHz** in PAL-B/G.

### 2.3.3 PAL Subcarrier Frequency

$$
f_{sc(PAL)} = 4.43361875 \text{ MHz} \approx 4.43 \text{ MHz}
$$

### 2.3.4 PAL's Signature Trick: Phase Alternation

This is the single most important conceptual point for KTU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Phase Alternation" in PAL:**
> On **even-numbered** scan lines, the $V$ signal modulates $\sin(\omega_c t)$ with its *normal* polarity.
> On **odd-numbered** scan lines, the $V$ signal is **inverted (alternated)** before modulating $\sin(\omega_c t)$.

Mathematically:

$$
C_{PAL}(t) = U(t)\cos(\omega_c t) \pm V(t)\sin(\omega_c t)
$$

where the $\pm$ sign alternates line by line, starting with $+$ on line 1.

### 2.3.5 Why Phase Alternation Works — Hue Error Cancellation

If a transmission error introduces a constant phase shift $\Delta\phi$ (hue error), it affects lines 1, 2, 3, 4... as $+V, -V, +V, -V, \ldots$ but the *true colour* should have been $V, V, V, V, \ldots$

By **averaging two adjacent lines** in the receiver (using a **1-H delay line**), the error cancels:

$$
\frac{+V_{\text{error}} + (-V_{\text{error}})}{2} = V_{\text{correct}}
$$

The luminance signal $Y$ is added (so the eye sees the *average* colour of the two lines, which is correct). Only the chrominance is averaged.

> [!NOTE]
> **This is why PAL was invented — to fix NTSC's phase-error problem.** The trade-off is half the vertical chrominance resolution, but the human eye barely notices this.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Constant | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NTSC Luminance | $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | Volts (normalized) |
| 2 | PAL Luminance | $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | Same form |
| 3 | NTSC I-signal | $I = 0.596R - 0.274G - 0.322B$ | Bandwidth 1.3 MHz |
| 4 | NTSC Q-signal | $Q = 0.211R - 0.523G + 0.312B$ | Bandwidth 0.6 MHz |
| 5 | PAL U-signal | $U = 0.493(B-Y)$ | Bandwidth 1.3 MHz |
| 6 | PAL V-signal | $V = 0.877(R-Y)$ | Bandwidth 1.3 MHz |
| 7 | NTSC Subcarrier | $f_{sc} = 3.579545$ MHz | Often 3.58 MHz |
| 8 | PAL Subcarrier | $f_{sc} = 4.43361875$ MHz | Often 4.43 MHz |
| 9 | NTSC Chroma | $C = I\cos(\omega_c t) + Q\sin(\omega_c t)$ | QAM |
| 10 | PAL Chroma | $C = U\cos(\omega_c t) \pm V\sin(\omega_c t)$ | $\pm$ alternates per line |
| 11 | Colour Burst | 8–9 cycles, back porch, reference phase | NTSC: 180°; PAL: $\pm 135°$ |
| 12 | NTSC Line Rate | $f_H = 15734.264$ Hz | 525 lines, 59.94 Hz field |
| 13 | PAL Line Rate | $f_H = 15625$ Hz | 625 lines, 50 Hz field |
| 14 | NTSC Field Rate | $f_V = 59.94$ Hz | 2:1 interlaced |
| 15 | PAL Field Rate | $f_V = 50$ Hz | 2:1 interlaced |
| 16 | Aspect Ratio | $4:3$ | Both standards |
| 17 | NTSC Bandwidth | **6 MHz** channel | 4.2 MHz video |
| 18 | PAL-B Bandwidth | **7 MHz** channel | 5.5 MHz video |
| 19 | PAL-D Bandwidth | **8 MHz** channel | 6 MHz video |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Broadcast engineering:** Choosing the correct standard determines antenna design, RF channel planning, and receiver manufacturing.
- **Video production:** Old NTSC/PAL footage must be standards-converted for modern digital displays (resampling 29.97 → 25 fps or vice versa).
- **Medical imaging & surveillance:** Legacy systems still use analogue composite video; understanding NTSC/PAL is essential for frame-grabber hardware.
- **Satellite TV (historical):** Early satellite channels used FM-modulated NTSC/PAL "bent-pipe" transponders.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the NTSC Chroma Signal as a Single Phasor

**Goal:** Show that the two orthogonal modulations $I\cos(\omega_c t)$ and $Q\sin(\omega_c t)$ can be combined into a single phasor with amplitude = saturation and phase = hue.

### Step 1: Combine the two modulations

$$
C_{NTSC}(t) = I(t)\cos(\omega_c t) + Q(t)\sin(\omega_c t)
$$

### Step 2: Use the identity $\ a\cos\theta + b\sin\theta = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}\cos(\theta - \phi)\ $ where $\ \tan\phi = b/a$

Applying this:

$$
C_{NTSC}(t) = \sqrt{I^2 + Q^2}\ \cos\!\left(\omega_c t - \phi\right)
$$

where

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{Q}{I}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Interpret the result

- **Saturation** (colour purity) is proportional to $\sqrt{I^2 + Q^2}$.
- **Hue** (the actual colour — red, green, blue, etc.) is encoded in the **phase angle** $\phi$ of the chroma subcarrier relative to the burst reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** This is the mathematical root of NTSC's famous weakness — *any* phase shift $\Delta\phi$ in the channel (multiplicative distortion, differential gain, multipath) is interpreted by the receiver as a **change in hue**, producing a colour error. PAL's line-alternation scheme mathematically eliminates this.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the PAL Hue-Error Cancellation

**Goal:** Prove mathematically that averaging the chroma of two adjacent PAL lines cancels a constant phase error.

### Step 1: Write the chroma on line $n$ (no error)

$$
C_n(t) = U\cos(\omega_c t) + (-1)^n V\sin(\omega_c t)
$$

(The factor $(-1)^n$ reflects the alternation: $+$ on line 1, $-$ on line 2, $+$ on line 3, ...)

### Step 2: Introduce a constant phase error $\Delta\phi$ in the channel

The channel multiplies the chroma by a complex phasor $e^{j\Delta\phi}$. In trigonometric form this becomes:

$$
C_n^{\text{received}}(t) = U\cos(\omega_c t + \Delta\phi) + (-1)^n V\sin(\omega_c t + \Delta\phi)
$$

### Step 3: Add line $n$ and line $n+1$ (and divide by 2)

$$
C_{\text{avg}}(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[C_n^{\text{received}} + C_{n+1}^{\text{received}}\right]
$$

Because the $V$-component has opposite sign on the two lines:

$$
C_{\text{avg}}(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[2U\cos(\omega_c t + \Delta\phi)\right] = U\cos(\omega_c t + \Delta\phi)
$$

For the $V$-component:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[+V\sin(\omega_c t + \Delta\phi) - V\sin(\omega_c t + \Delta\phi)\right] = 0 \ \text{ (still has error)}
$$

Wait — this cancels the *signal* itself. The receiver uses a **delay line and adder** trick: it **adds** the chroma of line $n$ to the chroma of line $n+1$ for the $U$ component, and **subtracts** for the $V$ component.

### Step 4: Correct receiver operation

- **For $U$:** Add line $n$ and line $n+1$ → phase errors are identical on both lines → they add constructively along with the signal. The phase error on $U$ is *not* cancelled (this is a known minor PAL artifact called **"Hanover bars"**), but $U$ carries only ~1/3 of the hue sensitivity.
- **For $V$:** Subtract line $n+1$ from line $n$. The error terms are subtracted too and cancel:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = V\sin(\omega_c t + \Delta\phi) - \bigl[-V\sin(\omega_c t + \Delta\phi)\bigr] = 2V\sin(\omega_c t)
$$

The $\Delta\phi$ phase error is **completely cancelled**, recovering the correct $V$ signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** PAL trades a factor-of-2 reduction in vertical chrominance resolution for complete immunity to differential phase errors — a brilliant engineering compromise.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Encoding a Pure Orange Pixel

A pixel has normalized values $R = 1.0$, $G = 0.65$, $B = 0.0$ (saturated orange).

### Step 1: Compute Luminance

$$
Y = 0.299(1.0) + 0.587(0.65) + 0.114(0.0)
$$

$$
Y = 0.299 + 0.3816 + 0.0 = 0.6806
$$

### Step 2: Compute NTSC I and Q

$$
I = 0.596(1.0) - 0.274(0.65) - 0.322(0.0) = 0.596 - 0.1781 = 0.4179
$$

$$
Q = 0.211(1.0) - 0.523(0.65) + 0.312(0.0) = 0.211 - 0.3400 = -0.1290
$$

### Step 3: Compute hue angle and saturation

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{Q}{I}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-0.1290}{0.4179}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.3087) = -17.13^{\circ}
$$

$$
S = \sqrt{I^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{(0.4179)^2 + (-0.1290)^2} = \sqrt{0.1746 + 0.0166} = 0.4373
$$

### Step 4: Compose the chroma waveform at one instant

For a chroma subcarrier phase reference of zero, at $t = 0$:

$$
C_{NTSC}(0) = I\cos(0) + Q\sin(0) = I = 0.4179
$$

The composite video at this pixel (excluding sync) is:

$$
v = Y + C = 0.6806 + 0.4179 = 1.0985 \ \text{(NTSC composite)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This number exceeds 1.0 because composite video swings between **0 and 1.33 V** at the sync tip. The receiver's clamping circuit restores DC levels.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — NTSC vs PAL

| # | Parameter | NTSC-M | PAL-B/G | PAL-D/K | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lines per frame | 525 | 625 | 625 | Mains freq (60 vs 50 Hz) |
| 2 | Field rate (Hz) | 59.94 | 50 | 50 | Matches mains |
| 3 | Frame rate (fps) | 29.97 | 25 | 25 | $\tfrac{1}{2}$ field rate (interlaced) |
| 4 | Line rate (Hz) | 15,734.26 | 15,625 | 15,625 | Lines × Field rate |
| 5 | Channel BW (MHz) | 6 | 7 | 8 | VHF/UHF allocation |
| 6 | Video BW (MHz) | 4.2 | 5.0 | 6.0 | Practical limit |
| 7 | Subcarrier (MHz) | 3.579545 | 4.433619 | 4.433619 | $\tfrac{1}{2}f_H \times \text{odd integer}$ |
| 8 | Chroma axes | $I$, $Q$ | $U$, $V$ | $U$, $V$ | Eye sensitivity |
| 9 | Chroma BW (MHz) | $I: 1.3$ / $Q: 0.6$ | Both 1.3 | Both 1.3 | NTSC exploits asymmetry |
| 10 | Phase alternation | **No** | **Yes** (line-by-line) | **Yes** | PAL's key innovation |
| 11 | Hue error | Sensitive | Immune | Immune | PAL averaging trick |
| 12 | Sat. errors | Sensitive | Sensitive | Sensitive | Both have same gain problem |
| 13 | Receiver complexity | Simpler | Slightly complex (delay line) | Same | PAL needs 1H delay |
| 14 | Vertical chroma res | Full | Halved (1H avg) | Halved | Trade-off |
| 15 | Aspect ratio | 4:3 | 4:3 | 4:3 | Same |
| 16 | Audio carrier offset | 4.5 MHz | 5.5 MHz | 6.5 MHz | Above video band |
| 17 | Sound modulation | FM | FM | FM | Same |
| 18 | Countries | USA, Japan, … | UK, India, … | China, … | Regional legacy |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — NTSC Encoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    camR[R Signal] --> matrix1[Matrix Encoder]
    camG[G Signal] --> matrix1
    camB[B Signal] --> matrix1
    matrix1 --> Ycalc[Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B]
    matrix1 --> Icalc[I = 0.596R - 0.274G - 0.322B]
    matrix1 --> Qcalc[Q = 0.211R - 0.523G + 0.312B]
    Ycalc --> adder1[Adder]
    Ycalc --> lowpass1[Low-pass Filter 4.2 MHz]
    lowpass1 --> adder1
    Icalc --> bpf1[Band-pass 1.3 MHz]
    Qcalc --> bpf2[Band-pass 0.6 MHz]
    bpf1 --> modcos[Multiplier with cos wc t]
    bpf2 --> modsin[Multiplier with sin wc t]
    modcos --> adder2[Chroma Adder]
    modsin --> adder2
    adder2 --> adder1
    adder1 --> compNTSC[Composite NTSC Video]
    syncgen[Sync + Burst Generator] --> adder1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — PAL Encoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    camR[R] --> matrix2[Matrix Encoder]
    camG[G] --> matrix2
    camB[B] --> matrix2
    matrix2 --> Ycalc2[Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B]
    matrix2 --> Ucalc[U = 0.493 B-Y]
    matrix2 --> Vcalc[V = 0.877 R-Y]
    Ycalc2 --> lowpass2[Low-pass 5 MHz]
    lowpass2 --> adder3[Adder]
    Ucalc --> bpfU[Band-pass 1.3 MHz]
    Vcalc --> swalt[PAL Switch Inverter line by line]
    swalt --> bpfV[Band-pass 1.3 MHz]
    bpfU --> modcos2[Multiplier cos wc t]
    bpfV --> modsin2[Multiplier sin wc t]
    modcos2 --> adder4[Chroma Adder]
    modsin2 --> adder4
    adder4 --> adder3
    adder3 --> compPAL[Composite PAL Video]
    palburst[Burst Generator plus minus 135 deg] --> adder3
    syncgen2[Sync Generator] --> adder3
    syncgen2 --> swalt
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — PAL Decoder with Hue-Error Cancellation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    compIn[Composite PAL Input] --> sepY[Luminance Trap + LPF]
    compIn --> chromaBPF[Chrominance BPF 4.43 MHz]
    chromaBPF --> burstGate[Burst Gate Sample Hold]
    burstGate --> refOsc[Reference Oscillator 4.43 MHz]
    refOsc --> cosDemod[Cosine Demodulator]
    refOsc --> sinDemod[Sine Demodulator]
    chromaBPF --> delay1H[1H Delay Line]
    chromaBPF --> cosDemod
    delay1H --> cosDemod
    chromaBPF --> sinDemod
    delay1H --> sinDemod
    cosDemod --> Uout[U Output error free]
    sinDemod --> Vout[V Output error free]
    sepY --> Yout[Y Output]
    Yout --> matrix3[Inverse Matrix RGB]
    Uout --> matrix3
    Vout --> matrix3
    matrix3 --> dispR[R out to Display]
    matrix3 --> dispG[G out to Display]
    matrix3 --> dispB[B out to Display]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart — Frequency Interleaving Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Ysp[Y Spectrum line spaced at fH] --> gap[Empty gaps at multiples of fH]
    gap --> placeCSC[Place chroma subcarrier in largest gap]
    placeCSC --> result[Composite spectrum luminance and chrominance do not overlap]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid State Diagram — PAL Switch Line-By-Line Alternation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Line1
    Line1 --> Line2: V positive polarity
    Line2 --> Line3: V negative polarity inverted
    Line3 --> Line4: V positive polarity
    Line4 --> Line5: V negative polarity inverted
    Line5 --> [*]
    note right of Line1: Subcarrier burst phase minus 135 deg
    note right of Line2: Subcarrier burst phase plus 135 deg
    note right of Line3: Subcarrier burst phase minus 135 deg
    note right of Line4: Subcarrier burst phase plus 135 deg
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams for KTU exams:** The examiner will often give you a *partial* block diagram and ask you to **label the blocks, the signals flowing in/out, and the modulating subcarrier frequency**. Memorize the three matrices (Y, I/Q, U/V) and the chroma adder structure.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, OEEVT722]`
**Define luminance and chrominance signals in a colour TV system. Why are colour-difference signals used in place of (R, G, B) for transmission?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Luminance (Y)** is the brightness component of the picture, representing the monochrome (black-and-white) information. It is formed as $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$. *(1 mark)*
- **Chrominance** is the colour information (hue + saturation), derived from the colour-difference signals $R-Y$, $B-Y$ (or their rotated forms $I,Q$ for NTSC; $U,V$ for PAL). *(1 mark)*
- **Why colour-ddifference instead of RGB?**
  (i) Compatibility — the $Y$ signal alone drives B/W receivers.
  (ii) Reduced bandwidth — human eye is less sensitive to colour detail, so chroma can be given narrower bandwidth.
  (iii) Lower crosstalk — the differences are near zero for grey/neutral colours, allowing them to be modulated onto a subcarrier without disturbing the luminance. *(1 mark)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, OEEVT722]`
**List any three differences between NTSC and PAL colour TV systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Colour-difference signals:** NTSC uses $I$ and $Q$; PAL uses $U$ and $V$. *(1 mark)*
2. **Phase alternation:** PAL inverts the $V$ (or $R-Y$) signal on alternate lines to cancel hue errors; NTSC does not. *(1 mark)*
3. **Lines per frame / field rate:** NTSC has 525 lines at 59.94 fields/s; PAL has 625 lines at 50 fields/s. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Choice Pattern (OSE-style):**
> Module 1 long questions typically give an internal choice between a question on NTSC and a question on PAL. The marks are split: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, OEEVT722]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **NTSC colour TV transmitter**. Derive the $Y$, $I$, and $Q$ signals. **— 7 Marks**

**(b)** Explain the **NTSC colour subcarrier modulation** and the role of the **colour burst**. Discuss why NTSC is sensitive to phase errors. **— 7 Marks**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the diagram shown in Section 4.1. Label the three camera signals $R, G, B$ entering a matrix encoder. The matrix produces three outputs: $Y$, $I$, $Q$. The $Y$ signal goes through a low-pass filter (4.2 MHz) to a summing amplifier. The $I$ signal is band-limited to 1.3 MHz and modulates $\cos(\omega_c t)$. The $Q$ signal is band-limited to 0.6 MHz and modulates $\sin(\omega_c t)$. The two modulated signals are added (QAM chroma adder) and then summed with $Y$, sync pulses, and the colour burst to form the composite NTSC video.

**Step 2 — Derive Y (2 marks):**

$$
Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B
$$

The coefficients are derived from the CIE photopic luminosity function, ensuring compatibility with B/W TV standards.

**Step 3 — Derive I and Q (2 marks):**

$$
I = 0.596R - 0.274G - 0.322B
$$

$$
Q = 0.211R - 0.523G + 0.312B
$$

The $I, Q$ axes are obtained by rotating the $(R-Y, B-Y)$ axes by **33°** in the colour vector diagram to exploit the human eye's reduced sensitivity to purple-green transitions (Q direction).

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Quadrature modulation (3 marks):** The $I$ signal modulates the in-phase carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ where $f_c = 3.579545$ MHz. The $Q$ signal modulates the quadrature carrier $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$. Both are added:

$$
C(t) = I\cos(\omega_c t) + Q\sin(\omega_c t) = \sqrt{I^2+Q^2}\cos(\omega_c t - \phi)
$$

**Step 2 — Colour burst (2 marks):** An 8–9 cycle burst of the subcarrier at 3.58 MHz is placed on the **back porch** of every horizontal blanking pulse. Its phase is fixed at 180° (i.e., along the $-I$ axis). The receiver uses this burst to phase-lock its local oscillator, providing the reference for synchronous demodulation of $I$ and $Q$.

**Step 3 — Phase sensitivity (2 marks):** In NTSC, hue is encoded in the **phase** $\phi$ of the chroma subcarrier. Any channel non-linearity, multipath reflection, or differential phase distortion shifts $\phi$ by an unwanted $\Delta\phi$. The receiver, decoding $\phi$ relative to the burst, will interpret the shift as a different colour — producing hue errors (e.g., skin tones turn green). This is the famous NTSC weakness.

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, OEEVT722]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the **PAL colour TV receiver**, emphasizing the role of the **1H delay line** in cancelling hue errors. **— 7 Marks**

**(b)** Derive the **PAL colour-difference signals** $U$ and $V$. Explain how the **PAL switch** alternates the phase of the chroma signal line by line. **— 7 Marks**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Receiver block diagram (3 marks):** Refer to Section 4.3. Composite PAL video enters the receiver. A **luminance channel** uses a low-pass filter (0–5 MHz) to extract $Y$. A **chrominance channel** uses a band-pass filter centred on 4.43 MHz to extract the chroma signal. The chroma passes through the 1H delay line in parallel with a direct path. The outputs of the delay line and the direct path are added (for $U$) and subtracted (for $V$) in two synchronous demodulators, which are driven by a 4.43 MHz local oscillator phase-locked to the colour burst.

**Step 2 — Role of 1H delay line (4 marks):** Because PAL alternates the $V$ polarity line by line, the chroma on line $n$ is $U\cos + V\sin$, and on line $n+1$ it is $U\cos - V\sin$. The delay line aligns line $n+1$ in time with line $n$. The synchronous detector for $U$ adds the two chromas: $2U\cos$ (the $V$ parts cancel because they have opposite signs). The synchronous detector for $V$ subtracts the two chromas: $2V\sin$ (the $U$ parts cancel because they are identical). Any constant phase error $\Delta\phi$ introduced by the channel is identical on both lines, so it is **cancelled** in the $V$ subtraction. This is the mathematical basis of PAL's hue-error immunity.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Derive U and V (3 marks):** The colour-difference signals are weighted versions of $(B-Y)$ and $(R-Y)$:

$$
U = 0.493(B - Y) = 0.493 B - 0.493(0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B)
$$

$$
U = -0.147R - 0.289G + 0.436B
$$

$$
V = 0.877(R - Y) = 0.877R - 0.877(0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B)
$$

$$
V = 0.615R - 0.515G - 0.100B
$$

The weights are chosen so that $U$ and $V$ swing between $\pm 0.7$ V for fully saturated colours.

**Step 2 — PAL switch (4 marks):** The PAL switch is an electronic switch driven by a half-line-rate square wave (derived from the burst phase alternation). On even lines, the $V$ signal is fed directly to the $\sin(\omega_c t)$ modulator; on odd lines, $V$ is inverted (sign-flipped) before modulation. Equivalently, the burst phase itself is switched between $+135°$ and $-135°$ line by line, which the receiver detects and uses to control its own PAL switch so that the demodulated $V$ signal is always restored to the correct polarity. This is the **"Phase Alternating Line"** that gives PAL its name.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $I,Q$ with $U,V$.** They are *different* matrices. Do not write $U$ where NTSC expects $I$.
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.493 and 0.877 scaling factors.** Writing $U = B-Y$ without the scale is a 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Saying "PAL has no phase error."** This is *wrong*. PAL has *residual* phase error called **Hanover bars** on $U$. Say "*V-component* phase error is cancelled" — be precise.
> 4. **Missing the colour burst.** It is a 1-mark item in almost every Part (a).
> 5. **Stating the line rate incorrectly.** NTSC: **15734.26 Hz**; PAL: **15625 Hz**. Do not say "15.7 kHz" loosely.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **NTSC** = *National Television System Committee*; 525 lines, 59.94 fields/s, 30 frames/s, 6 MHz channel, subcarrier **3.58 MHz**, uses **I, Q** axes.
- ⭐ **PAL** = *Phase Alternating Line*; 625 lines, 50 fields/s, 25 frames/s, 7–8 MHz channel, subcarrier **4.43 MHz**, uses **U, V** axes.
- ⭐ **Y equation** is the *same* in both: $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$.
- ⭐ **NTSC hue is phase-encoded** → any phase error → wrong colour.
- ⭐ **PAL inverts $V$ on alternate lines** → receiver averages to cancel phase errors.
- ⭐ **Colour burst** = reference subcarrier sample on back porch; NTSC: 180°; PAL: $\pm 135°$ alternating.
- ⭐ **Frequency interleaving** = subcarrier placed in a luminance spectrum gap at $f_c \approx (2n+1)f_H/2$.
- ⭐ **1H delay line** in PAL receiver = key to chroma averaging.
- ⭐ **QAM** = quadrature amplitude modulation = $I\cos + Q\sin$ (NTSC) or $U\cos \pm V\sin$ (PAL).
- ⭐ **I-signal BW = 1.3 MHz, Q-signal BW = 0.6 MHz** in NTSC; **U and V = 1.3 MHz** in PAL.
- ⭐ **Mains frequency** determined the field rate: 60 Hz mains → NTSC, 50 Hz mains → PAL.
- ⭐ **Compatibility** with B/W TV is preserved because $Y$ alone forms a complete monochrome picture.
- ⭐ **Demerits of NTSC:** hue errors, no automatic correction.
- ⭐ **Demerits of PAL:** reduced vertical chroma resolution, more complex receiver, Hanover bars.
- ⭐ **Merits of PAL over NTSC:** better colour fidelity, no manual tint control needed.
- ⭐ **Aspect ratio** = 4:3 in both; **sound** = FM in both, with different carrier offsets (4.5 / 5.5 / 6.5 MHz).
- ⭐ **Both standards are now obsolete** for broadcast (replaced by DVB, ATSC, ISDB) but legacy systems, archive footage, and some industrial cameras still use composite NTSC/PAL outputs (e.g., CVBS).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Media Streaming: Packetized Elementary Stream of Audio-Video Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Digital Media Streaming: Packetized Elementary Stream of Audio-Video Data # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the **MPEG-2 Systems Layer (ISO/IEC 13818-1)**, a **Packetized Elementary Stream (PES)** is a logical construct used to transform a continuous, raw **Elementary Stream (ES)** — such as a compressed audio bitstream, a compressed video bitstream, or private data — into discrete, variable-length packets that can be reliably transported, multiplexed, and synchronized across heterogeneous digital media networks and storage media. Each PES packet comprises a **PES Header** (containing stream identification, timing, and control information) followed by a **PES Payload** (a contiguous slice of the original elementary stream bytes). The PES layer sits precisely between the **Compression Layer (ES)** and the **Transport Multiplex Layer (MPEG-2 TS or MPEG-2 PS)**, acting as a universal "envelope" that decouples compression from transport. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 OEEVT722 Module 1 explicitly mandates understanding of *how* audio-video elementary streams are packetized for streaming, the PES packet structure, header fields, and the role of timestamps in A/V synchronization. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Parcel" Model Imagine you have a **very long movie file** (several gigabytes). You cannot mail this giant file in one piece — couriers have size limits. So you: 1. **Slice the movie** into smaller, manageable chunks (this is **packetization**). 2. **Stick a label on each chunk** saying: "This is part of the English audio track", "This chunk covers the time interval 00:10:23.400 to 00:10:23.500", and "Handle with care, reassemble in order". 3. **Box all the labelled chunks** into standardized mail sacks (these are the **Transport Stream packets**). The labelled chunk with its descriptive header **is exactly the PES packet**, and the entire labelling and slicing process is **PES packetization**. Without PES, the receiver would have no idea which bytes are audio, which are video, and in what order to play them back in real time. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Metrics & Constants Used in PES:** > - **PES Packet Length field:** **16 bits**, maximum 65,536 bytes per packet (in practice limited to 65,535 due to reserved zero). > - **PTS/DTS timestamp width:** **33 bits** (90 kHz clock domain) for video, with an optional +9-bit extension for 27 MHz domain. > - **Stream ID range:** **0xBD–0xFE** for private streams; **0xC0–0xDF** for audio; **0xE0–0xEF** for video. --- ## 1.3 Role of PES in the MPEG-2 Systems Stack PES is the **bridge layer** of the MPEG-2 architecture: | Layer | Function | Granularity | |---|---|---| | **Elementary Stream (ES)** | Raw compressed output of audio/video encoder | Continuous bitstream | | **Packetized Elementary Stream (PES)** | Variable-length packets with timing & ID headers | Packets of up to **64 KB** | | **Transport Stream (TS)** | Fixed 188-byte cells for broadcasting/IPTV | **188 bytes** | | **Program Stream (PS)** | Variable-length packs for DVD-style storage | **2048 bytes** (pack header) | > [!TIP] > **Why does PES exist separately from TS/PS?** Because PES is *format-independent of transport*. The same PES packets can be sliced into broadcast TS packets, stored as DVD PS packs, or recorded in a PVR file. PES decouples the "what is the content" question from the "how is it delivered" question. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Packetization Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic The transformation of raw A/V data into a stream-ready format follows a strict 5-stage pipeline: ### Step 1: Elementary Stream (ES) Generation - The **video encoder** (MPEG-2, H.264/AVC, HEVC) produces a continuous sequence of VCL (Video Coding Layer) NAL units — I-frames, P-frames, B-frames. - The **audio encoder** (MPEG-1 Layer II, AC-3, AAC) produces a continuous sequence of audio frames, each typically 24 ms long. - The output of each encoder is a single, byte-serial **Elementary Stream** belonging to a single PID-equivalent logical channel. ### Step 2: PES Encapsulation - The encoder (or a system multiplexer) **slices the ES into PES packets**. - Slicing typically occurs at **access unit boundaries** (e.g., between two video pictures, or between two audio frames) so that each PES packet aligns with a self-decodable unit. - A **PES Header is prepended** to every slice. ### Step 3: PES Header Insertion - The header contains: - **Packet Start Code Prefix** (24 bits) — fixed value `0x000001`. - **Stream ID** (8 bits) — identifies the stream type. - **PES Packet Length** (16 bits) — total bytes after this field. - **Optional PES Header fields** — flags, PES header data length, PTS, DTS, ESCR, ES rate, DSM trick mode, additional copy info, PES CRC, PES extension. - **PES Packet Data Bytes (Payload)** — the actual ES slice. ### Step 4: Multiplexing - Multiple PES streams (one video, two audio, subtitles, teletext) are **time-multiplexed** into a single Transport Stream (188-byte cells) or Program Stream (2048-byte packs). ### Step 5: Transmission / Storage - The TS or PS is transmitted via DVB, ATSC, ISDB, IP multicast (RTP), or stored on Blu-ray/DVD. ## 2.2 PES Packet Structure — Bit-Level Anatomy A PES packet has the following binary layout: ``` +-------------------+-------------------+ | Packet Start Code | 24 bits (0x000001) +-------------------+-------------------+ | Stream ID | 8 bits +-------------------+-------------------+ | PES Packet Length | 16 bits +-------------------+-------------------+ | '10' marker | 2 bits | PES Scrambling | 2 bits | PES Priority | 1 bit | Data Alignment | 1 bit | Copyright | 1 bit | Original/Copy | 1 bit +-------------------+-------------------+ | PTS / DTS Flags | 2 bits | ESCR Flag | 1 bit | ES Rate Flag | 1 bit | DSM Trick Mode | 1 bit | Additional Copy | 1 bit | PES CRC Flag | 1 bit | PES Extension | 1 bit +-------------------+-------------------+ | PES Header Data | 8 bits (N bytes follow) | Length | +-------------------+-------------------+ | [Optional Fields] | (PTS, DTS, ESCR, ...) +-------------------+-------------------+ | Stuffing Bytes | (variable) +-------------------+-------------------+ | PES Packet Data | (the actual ES bytes) | Bytes (Payload) | +-------------------+-------------------+ ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The `'10'` marker bits in the second byte are a **mandatory bit pattern** that distinguishes a PES packet from other MPEG-2 start codes (e.g., sequence header `0x000001B3`, picture header `0x00000100`, group of pictures `0x000001B8`). This is how a demultiplexer instantly recognizes "this is a PES packet, not an in-band ES header." ## 2.3 Stream ID Encoding — Decoding the 8-bit Field The Stream ID is the **single most important identifier** in PES. Standard assignments per ISO/IEC 13818-1: | Stream ID (hex) | Stream ID (binary) | Meaning | |---|---|---| | `0xBC` | `10111100` | Program Stream map | | `0xBD` | `10111101` | Private stream 1 (commonly AC-3 audio) | | `0xBE` | `10111110` | Padding stream | | `0xBF` | `10111111` | Private stream 2 | | `0xC0–0xDF` | `110xxxxx` | **Audio streams** (32 sub-streams) | | `0xE0–0xEF` | `1110xxxx` | **Video streams** (16 sub-streams) | | `0xF0–0xF7` | `11110xxx` | ECM/EMM (Entitlement Control/Management) | | `0xF8` | `11111000` | DSM (Digital Storage Media) | | `0xF9–0xFF` | reserved | — | > [!TIP] > **Quick Memory Trick for Students:** Video is `0xE0` (think "E" for Eye/Video), Audio is `0xC0` (think "C" for Concert/Audio), Private 1 is `0xBD` (AC-3 lives here in DVD). ## 2.4 PTS and DTS — The Synchronization Heart of PES ### Why Timestamps? A/V decoders need two critical timing markers: - **DTS (Decoding Time Stamp):** When the decoder should *begin decoding* this access unit. - **PTS (Presentation Time Stamp):** When the decoded unit should be *displayed/played* to the user. For **I and P frames**, `DTS == PTS` (no reordering needed). For **B frames**, `DTS < PTS` because B frames are decoded *before* the I/P frame they reference in display order, but displayed *after*. This is why B-frame video needs both PTS and DTS — without both, the decoder cannot maintain the correct display order. ### KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations | # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{PES\_StartCode} = 0\text{x}000001$ | 24-bit fixed prefix | — | | 2 | $L_{PES} = N_{payload} + N_{optHeader} + 3$ | PES Packet Length includes 3 bytes for Stream ID + Length fields themselves | bytes | | 3 | $L_{max} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65{,}535$ | Maximum PES packet payload-bound length | bytes | | 4 | $f_{PTS} = 90 \text{ kHz}$ | System clock frequency for PTS/DTS | Hz | | 5 | $T_{PTS} = \dfrac{N_{90k}}{90{,}000} = \dfrac{N_{27M}}{27{,}000{,}000}$ | Converting timestamp ticks to seconds | seconds | | 6 | $N_{90k} \in [0, 2^{33}-1]$ | Valid range of 33-bit PTS/DTS | ticks | | 7 | $\Delta t_{B} = \text{PTS} - \text{DTS}$ | Reorder delay imposed by B-frame buffering | ticks | | 8 | $\text{offset} = \text{base\_PCR} - \text{first\_PTS}$ | Initial offset aligning PTS to PCR | ticks | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Pitfall Avoidance:** Throughout this note, every vertical bar in equations (e.g., absolute value or "such that") is written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax. Always do the same in your own notes and answer sheets. ## 2.5 PTS/DTS Bit Encoding — The 5-Byte 33-bit Field The PTS/DTS field is **40 bits (5 bytes)** when both are present, structured as: - **'0010'** or **'0011' marker** (4 bits) — distinguishes PTS-only from PTS+DTS. - **PTS[32..30]** (3 bits) - **Marker bit '1'** (1 bit) — every 4th bit is a marker to maintain byte alignment and aid resynchronization after bit errors. - **PTS[29..15]** (15 bits) - **Marker bit '1'** (1 bit) - **PTS[14..0]** (15 bits) - **Marker bit '1'** (1 bit) The encoding formula is: $$ \text{EncodedPTS}_{5B} = \Big(4 \ll 36\Big) \;\Big\vert\; \big(\text{PTS}_{33b} \ll 3\big) \;\Big\vert\; \text{marker}_{4b} $$ A compact form for the marker-bit-injected 33-bit value is: $$ M_{33} = \big(\text{PTS}_{32..30}\big) \;\Vert\; 1 \;\Vert\; \big(\text{PTS}_{29..15}\big) \;\Vert\; 1 \;\Vert\; \big(\text{PTS}_{14..0}\big) \;\Vert\; 1 $$ ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility PES is the **backbone of every consumer digital entertainment device** manufactured in the last 25 years: - **DVB Set-Top Boxes** — decode PES from TS, render to HDMI. - **Blu-ray Players** — read PES from PS, demux to decoders. - **Streaming Set-Top Boxes (OTT/IPTV)** — convert RTP/MPEG-TS to PES internally. - **PVRs / DVRs** — record PES packets (often via TS) and seek through them. - **Professional Broadcast Equipment** — uses PES timestamps to maintain sub-frame A/V lip-sync across the entire studio-to-transmitter chain. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: PTS-to-Seconds Conversion **Objective:** Convert a 33-bit PTS counter value into a real-time playback duration in seconds. ### Given - A 33-bit PTS value $N_{90k}$ measured against a 90 kHz system clock. - The 90 kHz clock is derived from the 27 MHz PCR clock via a divide-by-300 prescaler. ### Step 1 — Identify the Unit Relationship By definition of the 90 kHz clock, **one tick** of the counter corresponds to: $$ T_{tick} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{90{,}000} \text{ seconds} $$ ### Step 2 — Express Total Time as a Multiple of Ticks If the PTS counter reads $N_{90k}$ ticks since the start of the stream, the elapsed real time is: $$ t_{elapsed} = N_{90k} \cdot T_{tick} $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify Substituting $T_{tick} = \frac{1}{90{,}000}$: $$ t_{elapsed} = N_{90k} \cdot \frac{1}{90{,}000} $$ $$ \boxed{\,t_{elapsed} \;=\; \frac{N_{90k}}{90{,}000} \text{ seconds}\,} $$ ### Step 4 — Convert to Hours:Minutes:Seconds (Practical Form) For human-readable display: $$ \begin{aligned} t_{elapsed} &= \frac{N_{90k}}{90{,}000} \text{ s} \\ &= \frac{N_{90k}}{90{,}000 \times 60} \text{ min} \\ &= \frac{N_{90k}}{90{,}000 \times 3600} \text{ h} \end{aligned} $$ ### Worked Example If $N_{90k} = 162{,}000{,}000$ (representing 30 minutes): $$ t = \frac{162{,}000{,}000}{90{,}000} = 1{,}800 \text{ s} = 30 \text{ minutes} \;\; \checkmark $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Bit-Stuffing of the 33-bit PTS into 5 Bytes **Objective:** Show how a 33-bit timestamp is squeezed into a 40-bit (5-byte) PES field while maintaining synchronization. ### Step 1 — Naive Packing (No Markers) A straightforward 33-bit-to-40-bit packing would waste 7 bits: $$ B_{naive} = \text{PTS}_{32..0} \;\Vert\; 0000000_{7b} $$ This is **illegal** in MPEG-2 because there is no resync mechanism inside the field. ### Step 2 — Insert Marker Bits Every 4th Bit MPEG-2 mandates a `'1'` marker bit after every 4 data bits within the timestamp field, in addition to a 4-bit prefix. The packing function is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Prefix} &= 0010_{2} \quad \text{(PTS only)} \quad \text{or} \quad 0011_{2} \quad \text{(PTS + DTS)} \\ \text{Segment}_1 &= \text{PTS}[32..30] \;\Vert\; 1 \quad (4 \text{ bits}) \\ \text{Segment}_2 &= \text{PTS}[29..15] \;\Vert\; 1 \quad (16 \text{ bits}) \\ \text{Segment}_3 &= \text{PTS}[14..0] \;\Vert\; 1 \quad (16 \text{ bits}) \\ \text{Total} &= \text{Prefix} \;\Vert\; \text{Segment}_1 \;\Vert\; \text{Segment}_2 \;\Vert\; \text{Segment}_3 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Total Bit-Count Verification $$ 4 + 4 + 16 + 16 = 40 \text{ bits} = 5 \text{ bytes} \;\; \checkmark $$ ### Step 4 — Why Markers Help Synchronization If a bit error corrupts byte 2 of the field, the demultiplexer can still: 1. Detect the error because the marker bits (which are *known* to be `1`) will fail to match. 2. Resync to the next 5-byte field by scanning for the next valid `'0010'` or `'0011'` prefix. 3. Lose at most 5 bytes (40 bits) of timestamp data — a tiny fraction of the 33-bit semantic payload. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The marker-bit pattern is a **Forward Error Correction (FEC) device at the bitstream level**, not a true FEC. It costs only 4 extra bits (10% overhead) to gain massive synchronization robustness — a classic engineering trade-off. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — PES vs TS vs PS | Property | PES | TS (Transport Stream) | PS (Program Stream) | |---|---|---|---| | **Primary Use** | Intermediate logical construct | Broadcasting, IPTV, errors | DVD, local storage | | **Packet Size** | Variable, up to 65,535 bytes | **Fixed 188 bytes** | Variable packs, header **2048 bytes** | | **Sync Byte** | 24-bit start code `0x000001` | 8-bit sync byte `0x47` | 32-bit pack start code `0x000001BA` | | **Error Robustness** | Low (no FEC at PES level) | High (188B cell + Reed-Solomon) | Low (assumed reliable storage) | | **Multiple Programs** | Not multiplexed (single program) | **Yes (multi-program)** | No (single program) | | **Timing Field** | PTS/DTS | PCR (Program Clock Reference) | SCR (System Clock Reference) | | **Application Layer** | Compression/Transport bridge | Physical transmission | File-based storage | --- ## 3.4 Derivation: PCR-to-PTS Offset Alignment **Objective:** Show how a receiver aligns an incoming PCR with the first PES PTS to lock the system clock. ### Given - $PCR_{base}$: First received PCR value (27 MHz domain). - $PTS_{first}$: First PES PTS in the same program (90 kHz domain). ### Step 1 — Convert PCR to 90 kHz Domain The PCR is in 27 MHz; to compare with PTS, divide by 300: $$ PCR_{90k} = \frac{PCR_{27M}}{300} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Offset The offset between the two clocks is: $$ \text{offset} = PCR_{base} - PTS_{first} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply Offset to All Subsequent PTS Values For any later PES packet with $PTS_{i}$: $$ PTS_{i,\text{aligned}} = PTS_{i} + \text{offset} $$ ### Step 4 — Validate At the receiver, the derived STC (System Time Clock) tracks $PCR_{90k}$. The decoder plays a PES packet when $STC \geq PTS_{i,\text{aligned}}$. > [!TIP] > This is **exactly how your DTH Set-Top Box keeps A/V lip-synced** to within ±5 ms — a remarkable engineering achievement considering the entire chain spans satellite → dish → LNB → STB → TV. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MPEG-2 Systems Layer The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete data path from raw audio/video sources through PES packetization to final transmission or storage: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Video Source
Camera or File] --> B[Video Encoder
MPEG-2 H264 HEVC] C[Audio Source
Microphone PCM AC3 AAC] --> D[Audio Encoder
MPEG Audio] B --> E[Video Elementary Stream ES
Continuous bytes] D --> F[Audio Elementary Stream ES
Continuous bytes] E --> G[PES Packetizer
Slices at access unit boundary] F --> G G --> H[Video PES Packets
Variable length up to 64KB] G --> I[Audio PES Packets
Variable length up to 64KB] H --> J[TS Multiplexer
Fixed 188 byte cells] I --> J J --> K[MPEG-2 Transport Stream
DVB ATSC IPTV] J --> L[Program Stream Mux
2048 byte packs] L --> M[DVD or Blu-ray Disc Storage] K --> N[Set Top Box Receiver
Demux and Decode] M --> N N --> O[HDMI Output
to Television] style A fill:#ffe6e6 style C fill:#e6f2ff style B fill:#fff4e6 style D fill:#fff4e6 style E fill:#e6ffe6 style F fill:#e6ffe6 style G fill:#f0e6ff style H fill:#f0e6ff style I fill:#f0e6ff style J fill:#ffe6f0 style K fill:#ffe6f0 style L fill:#ffe6f0 style M fill:#f9f9f9 style N fill:#e6f9ff style O fill:#f9e6ff ``` ## 4.2 PES Packet Header Field Map (Bit-Precise) The following Mermaid block renders a *byte-aligned field map* of the PES packet header, showing each field's exact bit offset and width. This serves as a structural schematic of the PES envelope: ```mermaid flowchart LR byte0[Byte 0: Packet Start Code MSB 0x00] --> byte1[Byte 1: Packet Start Code 0x00] byte1 --> byte2[Byte 2: Packet Start Code LSB 0x01] byte2 --> byte3[Byte 3: Stream ID
0xE0 video 0xC0 audio 0xBD private] byte3 --> byte4[Byte 4: PES Packet Length MSB] byte4 --> byte5[Byte 5: PES Packet Length LSB] byte5 --> byte6[Byte 6: Marker bits 10
Scrambling 2b Priority 1b
Alignment 1b Copyright 1b Original 1b] byte6 --> byte7[Byte 7: PTS DTS flags 2b
ESCR 1b ES Rate 1b
DSM 1b Add Copy 1b CRC 1b Ext 1b] byte7 --> byte8[Byte 8: PES Header Data Length N] byte8 --> optField[Optional Fields N bytes:
PTS 5B or PTS DTS 10B
ESCR 6B ES Rate 3B DSM trick mode etc] optField --> stuffing[Stuffing Bytes if N greater than used] stuffing --> payload[PES Packet Data Bytes
Actual Elementary Stream slice] style byte0 fill:#ffcccb style byte1 fill:#ffcccb style byte2 fill:#ffcccb style byte3 fill:#cce5ff style byte4 fill:#fff4cc style byte5 fill:#fff4cc style byte6 fill:#d4edda style byte7 fill:#d4edda style byte8 fill:#f0d4ff style optField fill:#e2d4ff style stuffing fill:#f0f0f0 style payload fill:#ffe6cc ``` ## 4.3 A/V Synchronization Timeline — PTS / DTS / PCR Interaction The following Mermaid sequence diagram shows the time-ordered interaction between the encoder, the PES packet stream, and the decoder. It visually demonstrates **why B-frames require both PTS and DTS** while I/P-frames require only PTS: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Enc as Video Encoder participant PES as PES Packetizer participant TS as TS Mux with PCR participant Dec as STB Decoder participant TV as TV Display Note over Enc: Display order I0 B1 B2 P3 B4 B5 P6 Note over Enc: Encode order I0 P3 B1 B2 P6 B4 B5 Enc->>PES: I-frame at DTS=0 PTS=0 PES->>TS: PES packet PTS=0 DTS=0 Enc->>PES: P-frame at DTS=3003 PTS=9009 PES->>TS: PES packet PTS=9009 DTS=3003 Enc->>PES: B-frame at DTS=5001 PTS=3003 PES->>TS: PES packet PTS=3003 DTS=5001 Enc->>PES: B-frame at DTS=6001 PTS=4002 PES->>TS: PES packet PTS=4002 DTS=6001 TS->>Dec: Stream of PES packets with PCR=90000 Dec->>Dec: Decode I when STC greater equal DTS 0 Dec->>Dec: Decode P when STC greater equal DTS 3003 Dec->>Dec: Decode B when STC greater equal DTS 5001 Dec->>TV: Present I at PTS 0 Dec->>TV: Present P at PTS 9009 after reordering Dec->>TV: Present B at PTS 3003 Note over TV: Final display order matches I0 B1 B2 P3 B4 B5 P6 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] **Define Packetized Elementary Stream (PES). List the major fields of a PES packet header.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Packetized Elementary Stream (PES)** is a logical grouping of bytes from a single elementary stream (audio, video, or private data) into variable-length packets, each prefixed with a header containing timing and identification information. It is defined in the MPEG-2 Systems standard (ISO/IEC 13818-1) and serves as the bridge between the compression layer and the transport/storage layer. **Major PES Header Fields (any 4 × 0.5 = 2 marks; definition = 1 mark):** 1. **Packet Start Code Prefix (24 bits)** — fixed `0x000001`, used for byte alignment and demux recognition. 2. **Stream ID (8 bits)** — identifies the stream type (e.g., `0xE0` for video, `0xC0` for audio). 3. **PES Packet Length (16 bits)** — number of bytes following this field. 4. **PES Header Flag Bits** — controls presence/absence of optional fields. 5. **PES Header Data Length (8 bits)** — number of bytes in the optional fields area. 6. **Optional Fields** — PTS, DTS, ESCR, ES Rate, DSM trick mode, PES CRC, PES extension. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model] **Differentiate between PTS and DTS in a PES packet. Why are both required for B-frame video?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **PTS (Presentation Time Stamp):** The instant, in 90 kHz clock ticks, at which the decoded access unit must be **presented (displayed)** to the viewer. (1 mark) - **DTS (Decoding Time Stamp):** The instant, in 90 kHz clock ticks, at which the decoder must **begin decoding** the access unit. (1 mark) **Why both are required for B-frames:** B-frames are bi-directionally predicted — they reference both a past and a future reference frame. To minimize buffer size, encoders **reorder** the bitstream so that the reference I/P frame is encoded and transmitted *before* the B-frames that depend on it. This means the **decoding order differs from the display order**. I and P frames have `PTS = DTS` (no reordering), but B-frames have `DTS < PTS`. Without both timestamps, the decoder cannot correctly reorder B-frames into their display positions, causing severe visual artifacts. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] **Question:** With a neat block diagram, explain the MPEG-2 Systems layer architecture showing the formation of PES packets from elementary streams. Describe the PES packet structure in detail with the function of each field. #### (a) MPEG-2 Systems Architecture and PES Formation [7 marks] **Model Solution:** The MPEG-2 Systems layer (ISO/IEC 13818-1) defines a hierarchical architecture consisting of five layers: 1. **Elementary Streams (ES):** The raw, compressed output of a single audio or video encoder. An ES is a continuous, unbounded byte stream containing coded pictures, access units, and slice headers. (1 mark) 2. **Packetized Elementary Stream (PES):** The ES is sliced into variable-length PES packets at access unit boundaries. Each PES packet has a header (timing, ID, length) followed by a payload of ES bytes. (1 mark) 3. **Program Stream (PS):** A family of PES packets from a single program (e.g., one video + one audio) is multiplexed into PS packs of 2048 bytes, intended for error-free media (DVD). (1 mark) 4. **Transport Stream (TS):** A family of PES packets from one or more programs is multiplexed into TS cells of fixed 188 bytes (4-byte header + 184-byte payload), intended for noisy/lossy media (DVB, ATSC, IPTV). (1 mark) 5. **Transmission / Storage:** The TS or PS is sent over the physical channel. **The PES formation process:** - **Step 1 — Slicing:** The video ES is sliced at picture boundaries (one I/P/B picture per PES packet is common). The audio ES is sliced at audio frame boundaries. - **Step 2 — Header Construction:** A 24-bit start code `0x000001`, an 8-bit stream ID, and a 16-bit length field are prepended. - **Step 3 — Optional Field Insertion:** PTS (5 bytes), DTS (5 bytes), ESCR, ES rate, etc., are inserted as per the flag bits. - **Step 4 — Payload Attachment:** The ES bytes are appended to form the complete PES packet. (2 marks for process) - **Block diagram** (drawn by student): Video ES → PES Packetizer → Video PES → Multiplexer; Audio ES → PES Packetizer → Audio PES → Multiplexer; Multiplexer → TS or PS. (1 mark for diagram) #### (b) PES Packet Structure and Field Functions [7 marks] **Model Solution:** | Field | Size (bits) | Function | |---|---|---| | Packet Start Code Prefix | 24 | Fixed `0x000001` — enables demux byte alignment. (0.5) | | Stream ID | 8 | Identifies stream type: `0xE0`–`0xEF` = video, `0xC0`–`0xDF` = audio, `0xBD` = private. (0.5) | | PES Packet Length | 16 | Total bytes of the PES packet *excluding* the start code prefix. Max = 65,535 bytes. (0.5) | | Marker `'10'` | 2 | Distinguishes PES header from in-band ES headers (sequence header, picture header, GOP header). (0.5) | | PES Scrambling Control | 2 | `00` = not scrambled; others indicate scrambling mode. (0.5) | | PES Priority | 1 | Indicates higher-priority payload (e.g., I-frames). (0.5) | | Data Alignment Indicator | 1 | `1` = PES payload starts at access unit boundary. (0.5) | | Copyright | 1 | `1` = material is copyrighted. (0.5) | | Original/Copy | 1 | `1` = original; `0` = copy. (0.5) | | PTS/DTS Flags | 2 | `10` = PTS only; `11` = PTS+DTS; `00` = neither; `01` = forbidden. (0.5) | | ESCR Flag | 1 | `1` = ESCR field present. (0.25) | | ES Rate Flag | 1 | `1` = ES rate field present. (0.25) | | DSM Trick Mode Flag | 1 | `1` = trick mode field present (for VCR-style fast forward). (0.25) | | Additional Copy Info Flag | 1 | `1` = additional copy info field present. (0.25) | | PES CRC Flag | 1 | `1` = 16-bit CRC follows. (0.25) | | PES Extension Flag | 1 | `1` = PES extension field present. (0.25) | | PES Header Data Length | 8 | Number of bytes in the optional-fields area that follows. (0.5) | | **Optional Fields (variable):** PTS, DTS, ESCR, ES Rate, DSM Trick Mode, Add. Copy Info, PES CRC, PES Extension | 0–255 bytes | Carries timing, bitrate, and integrity data. (0.5) | | **PES Packet Data Bytes (Payload)** | Variable | The actual elementary stream slice (e.g., one coded picture or one audio frame). (0.5) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the PES Packet Length with the *total* packet size. The field counts bytes *after itself* (i.e., from the flag bits onwards). Maximum payload is 65,535 − 3 − 3 (header overhead) ≈ **65,529 bytes**. Examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for not stating this distinction. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model] **Question:** Explain the role of timestamps (PTS, DTS, PCR) in MPEG-2 PES for audio-video synchronization. With a numerical example, show how PTS is encoded in a PES packet and converted to playback time. #### (a) Role of Timestamps in A/V Synchronization [7 marks] **Model Solution:** **The synchronization problem:** A digital TV stream contains video frames encoded out of display order (B-frames), audio frames of fixed duration, and possibly subtitles. The decoder must: 1. Decode the access units in the **correct order** (decode order). 2. Display them in the **correct order** (presentation order). 3. Maintain a single master clock shared by audio and video to prevent **lip-sync drift**. **The three timestamps:** - **DTS (Decoding Time Stamp) — 33 bits, 90 kHz:** Tells the decoder *when* to begin decoding an access unit. Present in the PES header when PTS-DTS flags = `11`. (1.5 marks) - **PTS (Presentation Time Stamp) — 33 bits, 90 kHz:** Tells the decoder *when* to display the decoded access unit. For I/P frames: `PTS = DTS`. For B frames: `PTS > DTS` (typically by the reorder delay). (1.5 marks) - **PCR (Program Clock Reference) — 42 bits, 27 MHz:** Embedded in the TS adaptation field (not directly in PES), serves as the **master clock reference** transmitted every ≤ 40 ms. The receiver reconstructs a 27 MHz System Time Clock (STC) locked to the PCR. All PTS/DTS values are interpreted as offsets from the PCR. (1.5 marks) **Synchronization mechanism:** 1. The receiver locks its STC to the PCRs (PLL-based clock recovery). 2. For each PES packet, the decoder compares the embedded PTS/DTS with the current STC. 3. When `STC ≥ DTS`, the decoder pulls the access unit into the decoder buffer. 4. When `STC ≥ PTS`, the decoder pulls the decoded picture into the display buffer. 5. Audio PTS governs audio DAC sample release; video PTS governs video display refresh. This closed-loop mechanism maintains sub-frame A/V synchronization across the entire distribution chain. (2.5 marks for mechanism + role) #### (b) Numerical Example: PTS Encoding and Conversion [7 marks] **Problem:** A PES video packet has a PTS counter value of `N₉₀ₖ = 270,000,000` ticks. Show the 5-byte encoded representation and compute the playback time. **Step 1 — Convert PTS to seconds** (1 mark): $$ t = \frac{270{,}000{,}000}{90{,}000} = 3{,}000 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ minutes} $$ **Step 2 — Convert PTS to 33-bit binary** (1 mark): $$ 270{,}000{,}000_{10} = 0001\ 0000\ 0010\ 1100\ 1000\ 0010\ 1010\ 0000_{2} $$ Padded to 33 bits (grouped for clarity): `0 0001 0000 0010 1100 1000 0010 1010 0000` **Step 3 — Extract the three PTS segments** (1 mark): - PTS[32..30] = `000` - PTS[29..15] = `1 0000 0010 1100 10` = 15 bits - PTS[14..0] = `00 0010 1010 0000` = 15 bits **Step 4 — Build the 5-byte encoded field with marker bits** (2 marks): - Prefix (PTS only): `0010` - Segment 1: `PTS[32..30] || '1'` = `000 1` - Segment 2: `PTS[29..15] || '1'` = `1 0000 0010 1100 100 1` (only first 15 of those 16 are data, then marker) - Data: `10000 00101100 100` → `1000000101100100` (16 bits total = 15 data + 1 marker) - Segment 3: `PTS[14..0] || '1'` = `00001010100000 1` (15 data + 1 marker) Concatenated 40-bit field (5 bytes): ``` Byte 1: 0010 0001 = 0x21 Byte 2: 0000 0010 = 0x02 Byte 3: 1100 1001 = 0xC9 Byte 4: 0000 1010 = 0x0A Byte 5: 1000 0001 = 0x81 ``` So the PTS field in the PES header is: `0x21 0x02 0xC9 0x0A 0x81` (1 mark) **Step 5 — Verify** (1 mark): Total bits = 4 + 4 + 16 + 16 = 40 ✓ Marker bits at positions 4, 12, 28 ✓ Playback time = 50 minutes = 3000 s ✓ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** When students compute PTS in binary, they often forget to **left-pad the 33-bit value with leading zeros**. If the PTS value is small, missing the leading zeros shifts all subsequent bits and the marker-bit pattern. Always write out the full 33-bit field before segmenting. A 2-mark deduction is typical for this error. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before Your Exam** - [ ] **PES = the logical bridge** between Elementary Stream (ES) and Transport/Program Stream (TS/PS). It is a *format-independent* packetization layer. - [ ] **PES packet = PES Header (≥ 9 bytes) + PES Payload (variable).** Header always starts with `0x000001` (24-bit start code). - [ ] **Stream ID rules:** `0xE0`–`0xEF` = video, `0xC0`–`0xDF` = audio, `0xBD` = private stream 1 (AC-3), `0xBF` = private stream 2. - [ ] **PES Packet Length** is a 16-bit field that gives the number of bytes *after itself*; max = 65,535. - [ ] **Marker bits `'10'`** in byte 6 distinguish a PES header from in-band ES headers — *memorize this for true/false questions*. - [ ] **PTS = Presentation Time Stamp** (when to display); **DTS = Decoding Time Stamp** (when to decode); both 33-bit at 90 kHz. - [ ] **PTS-only flag = `10`; PTS+DTS flag = `11`.** I/P frames need only PTS (`PTS = DTS`); B frames need both (`DTS < PTS`). - [ ] **PCR = Program Clock Reference**, 42-bit at 27 MHz, lives in the TS adaptation field (NOT directly in PES); PCR drives the receiver's System Time Clock (STC). - [ ] **PTS-to-seconds formula:** $t = \dfrac{N_{90k}}{90{,}000}$ seconds. Converting $N = 270{,}000{,}000$ gives $t = 3{,}000$ s = 50 min. - [ ] **Marker bits every 4th bit** in the 40-bit PTS field (5 bytes total) enable bit-error resynchronization. - [ ] **TS = 188-byte fixed cells, error-resilient, multi-program** (broadcast/IPTV). **PS = 2048-byte packs, error-free media, single program** (DVD). - [ ] **Same PES packets can be sliced into either TS or PS** — PES is transport-agnostic. This is the key architectural insight. - [ ] **PES slicing happens at access unit boundaries** (one coded picture per video PES packet, one audio frame per audio PES packet) to align with self-decodable units. - [ ] **Real-world applications:** DVB STBs, Blu-ray players, OTT/IPTV boxes, PVRs, professional broadcast chains — all rely on PES for A/V sync. --- *End of Notes — OEEVT722 Module 1 — Digital Media Streaming: Packetized Elementary Stream of Audio-Video Data*

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** MPEG Data Stream

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **MPEG Data Stream** refers to the standardized, time-multiplexed, bit-serial data structure defined by the **Moving Picture Experts Group (MPEG)** under **ISO/IEC 11172 (MPEG-1)** and **ISO/IEC 13818 (MPEG-2)** standards. It defines how compressed video, compressed audio, and system-level synchronization information are packaged, packetized, and transmitted over storage media or broadcast channels.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for **OEEVT722 – Entertainment Electronics**, the MPEG Data Stream encompasses three hierarchical abstractions:

- **Elementary Stream (ES)** – The raw compressed output of a single audio or video encoder.
- **Packetized Elementary Stream (PES)** – The ES segmented into variable-length packets carrying presentation and decoding time stamps.
- **Program Stream (PS) / Transport Stream (TS)** – The final multiplexed stream formed by interleaving multiple PES packets into a unified bitstream, designed for error-resilient transmission (TS) or software-oriented playback (PS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The MPEG data stream is the *backbone of digital TV, DVD, Blu-ray, DVB, ATSC, and IPTV*. Every student must internalize the relationship between **ES → PES → PS/TS**, as this is the most frequently tested hierarchy in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a **railway cargo station** (the MPEG multiplexer) where two trains arrive daily:

- **Train A** carries boxes of compressed video frames (I, P, B pictures).
- **Train B** carries boxes of compressed audio samples.

Each box needs a **shipping label** containing:
- Destination (decoder buffer)
- Delivery time (Presentation Time Stamp – PTS)
- Sorting priority (Decoding Time Stamp – DTS)

The cargo station repacks these boxes into **standardized shipping containers** (PES packets), tags them with **global positioning timestamps** (SCR/PCR), and loads them onto a single **super-freight train** – the final **MPEG Data Stream** (PS or TS). The receiving decoder unloads, sorts, and delivers each box to the correct playback buffer at the precise time required for synchronized audio-video reproduction.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

The following standard metrics govern MPEG stream engineering:

- **System Clock Frequency (SCF): 90 kHz** – the master clock for all time-stamping operations.
- **PCR/SCR Resolution: 27 MHz** (derived from 90 kHz × 300) for MPEG-2 transport streams.
- **Typical TS Packet Length: 188 bytes** (4-byte header + 184-byte payload) — a critical value standardized by **DVB** and **ATSC**.
- **PS Pack Length: 2048 bytes** (MPEG-1) or up to 64 KB (MPEG-2 program stream).
- **Constant Bit Rate (CBR)** or **Variable Bit Rate (VBR)** encoding modes.
- **End-to-End Delay: < 500 ms** for live broadcast applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pearl:** The number **188 bytes** for a TS packet is one of the most commonly asked values in KTU exams. Memorize: *Header = 4 bytes, Payload = 184 bytes, Sync byte = 0x47*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of the MPEG Data Stream

The MPEG system standard defines a strict **five-layer hierarchy**. Each layer encapsulates the layer beneath it, adding its own header and timing information.

### Layer 1 – Video/Audio Compression Layer
- The **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)**, **motion compensation**, and **psycho-acoustic modeling** produce the compressed bitstream.
- Output: raw **Elementary Stream (ES)**.

### Layer 2 – Elementary Stream Access Layer
- The ES is parsed and segmented into **access units** (a video access unit = one coded picture; an audio access unit = one audio frame).
- These access units are the smallest decodable entities.

### Layer 3 – PES Packetization Layer
- Each access unit is wrapped into a **PES Packet** consisting of:
  - **PES Header (variable length, ≥ 9 bytes)** containing:
    - Packet Start Code Prefix: `0x000001`
    - Stream ID (`0xE0–0xEF` for video, `0xC0–0xDF` for audio)
    - PES Packet Length (2 bytes)
    - Optional fields: **PTS**, **DTS**, **ESCR**, **ES rate**
  - **PES Payload** (variable length, up to 64 KB)

### Layer 4 – Multiplexing & Splicing Layer
- PES packets from multiple sources (video, audio, data) are **time-division multiplexed**.
- The result is packed into either:
  - **Program Stream (PS)** – for error-free media (DVD), long packs (~2 KB).
  - **Transport Stream (TS)** – for noisy channels (DVB-T, ATSC), fixed 188-byte packets.

### Layer 5 – Transmission Layer
- TS is modulated using **QPSK, 64-QAM, or COFDM** for broadcast.
- PS is stored on **optical media (DVD-Video, Blu-ray)**.

## 2.2 Critical Time Stamps and Clock References

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MPEG system layer exists primarily to **synchronize audio and video** at the decoder. This is achieved through four critical timing constructs.

| Construct | Full Form | Function | Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SCR** | System Clock Reference | Master clock of PS encoder | Once per pack |
| **PCR** | Program Clock Reference | Master clock of TS encoder | Every ≤ 100 ms |
| **PTS** | Presentation Time Stamp | When to display the access unit | Every access unit |
| **DTS** | Decoding Time Stamp | When to decode the access unit | Only when DTS ≠ PTS (B-frames) |

- **PTS / DTS** are coded on the **90 kHz system clock**.
- **SCR / PCR** are coded on the **27 MHz clock** to provide finer granularity.
- The relationship: $\text{PCR} = 27\,\text{MHz counter}$, $\text{PTS} = 90\,\text{kHz counter}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU ESE and internal assessments. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R_{\text{video}} = W \times H \times f \times b \times C$ | Uncompressed video bit rate (W,H = pixels, f = fps, b = bits/pixel, C = chroma factor) | bits/s |
| 2 | $R_{\text{compressed}} = R_{\text{video}} / \text{CR}$ | Compressed bit rate (CR = compression ratio) | bits/s |
| 3 | $\text{CR} = R_{\text{original}} / R_{\text{compressed}}$ | Compression Ratio (dimensionless) | unitless |
| 4 | $\Delta t_{\text{PTS}} = N / f_{\text{clock}}$ | Time-stamp interval per frame | seconds |
| 5 | $f_{\text{PCR}} = 27\,\text{MHz}$ | Transport stream clock | Hz |
| 6 | $f_{\text{PTS}} = 90\,\text{kHz}$ | Presentation clock | Hz |
| 7 | $L_{\text{TS}} = 188\,\text{B} = 1504\,\text{bits}$ | TS packet size | bytes / bits |
| 8 | $L_{\text{header}} = 4\,\text{B}$ | TS header size | bytes |
| 9 | $\text{PCR max interval} = 100\,\text{ms}$ | Maximum allowed gap between PCRs | ms |
| 10 | $B_{\text{decoder}} \geq R_{\text{peak}} \times T_{\text{jitter}}$ | Minimum VBV/CPB buffer size | bits |
| 11 | $T_{\text{access}} = 8 \times L_{\text{packet}} / R_{\text{bit}}$ | Time to transmit one TS packet | seconds |
| 12 | $\text{Efficiency} = L_{\text{payload}} / L_{\text{packet}}$ | Packet overhead efficiency | % |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The MPEG data stream architecture is the **DNA of every digital broadcasting and storage system** ever engineered. In practical terms:

- **DVB-T2 / ATSC 3.0** terrestrial broadcasting rely on the **TS structure** for resilience against multipath fading and impulse noise.
- **Blu-ray Disc (BD-ROM)** uses **PS / EVOB** multiplexing for sub-µs synchronization.
- **IPTV / OTT platforms (Netflix, Hotstar)** use the **MPEG-DASH** adaptation of TS for adaptive bitrate streaming over HTTP.
- **Video conferencing (H.264 over RTP)** is a direct descendant of the MPEG PES concept, with RTP timestamps replacing PTS.

A deep understanding of this stream structure is what allows engineers to design **set-top boxes, transcoders, and multimedia processors**.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Uncompressed Video Bit Rate

The most fundamental calculation in MPEG engineering is the **uncompressed video bit rate**, which sets the baseline against which all compression is measured.

### Starting Point
A single uncompressed frame in the **4:2:0 chroma sub-sampling** scheme contains:
- One **luma (Y) plane** of $W \times H$ pixels
- Two **chroma (Cb, Cr) planes** of $\frac{W}{2} \times \frac{H}{2}$ pixels each

### Step 1: Pixels per Luma Frame
A luma frame contains $W \times H$ pixels.

### Step 2: Pixels per Chroma Frame
Each chroma plane contains $\frac{W}{2} \times \frac{H}{2} = \frac{WH}{4}$ pixels.
There are two chroma planes, so the total chroma pixels per frame are:

$$
\text{Chroma pixels} = 2 \times \frac{WH}{4} = \frac{WH}{2}
$$

### Step 3: Total Pixels per Frame
Adding luma and chroma pixels:

$$
P_{\text{frame}} = WH + \frac{WH}{2} = \frac{3WH}{2}
$$

### Step 4: Bits per Frame
Each pixel is represented by $b$ bits (typically $b = 8$):

$$
B_{\text{frame}} = \frac{3WHb}{2}\,\text{bits}
$$

### Step 5: Multiply by Frame Rate
At a frame rate of $f$ frames per second, the **uncompressed video bit rate** is:

$$
R_{\text{video}} = \frac{3WHbf}{2}\,\text{bits/s}
$$

### Step 6: Worked Example
For **Standard Definition (SD) PAL** with $W = 720$, $H = 576$, $b = 8$, $f = 25$:

$$
R_{\text{video}} = \frac{3 \times 720 \times 576 \times 8 \times 25}{2}
$$

$$
R_{\text{video}} = \frac{3 \times 720 \times 576 \times 200}{2}
$$

$$
R_{\text{video}} = \frac{248{,}832{,}000}{2} = 124{,}416{,}000\,\text{bits/s}
$$

$$
R_{\text{video}} \approx 124.4\,\text{Mbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reality Check:** An uncompressed SD video stream consumes **~124 Mbps**. A standard DVD (MPEG-2) compresses this to **~4–9 Mbps** — a compression ratio of **15×–30×**. For HD (1920×1080×30), the uncompressed rate exceeds **1.4 Gbps**, which is why MPEG compression is non-negotiable for any consumer-grade delivery system.

## 3.2 Derivation of Compression Ratio

The **compression ratio (CR)** quantifies the effectiveness of an MPEG encoder.

### Step 1: Definition
Compression ratio is defined as the ratio of uncompressed data to compressed data:

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{R_{\text{uncompressed}}}{R_{\text{compressed}}}
$$

### Step 2: Solve for Compressed Rate
Rearranging:

$$
R_{\text{compressed}} = \frac{R_{\text{uncompressed}}}{\text{CR}}
$$

### Step 3: Worked Example
For the SD case above with a target DVD bit rate of $6\,\text{Mbps}$:

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{124.4\,\text{Mbps}}{6\,\text{Mbps}} \approx 20.7
$$

This corresponds to the typical DVD-Video compression ratio of **20:1 to 30:1** for MPEG-2.

## 3.3 Derivation of TS Packet Transmission Time

Knowing how long a single TS packet occupies the channel is essential for **buffer modeling** and **jitter analysis**.

### Step 1: Definition
The transmission time $T_{\text{packet}}$ is the packet size divided by the channel bit rate:

$$
T_{\text{packet}} = \frac{L_{\text{packet}}}{R_{\text{bit}}}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Known Values
For MPEG-2 TS, $L_{\text{packet}} = 188\,\text{bytes} = 188 \times 8\,\text{bits} = 1504\,\text{bits}$.

$$
T_{\text{packet}} = \frac{1504}{R_{\text{bit}}}
$$

### Step 3: Numerical Example
At a typical DVB-T bit rate of $R_{\text{bit}} = 24\,\text{Mbps}$:

$$
T_{\text{packet}} = \frac{1504}{24 \times 10^{6}} = 62.67\,\mu\text{s}
$$

This means **~15,950 TS packets per second** are transmitted.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of PS vs TS

| Parameter | Program Stream (PS) | Transport Stream (TS) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Use** | DVD, local storage | DVB, ATSC, IPTV |
| **Packet Size** | Variable (~2 KB) | Fixed 188 bytes |
| **Error Resilience** | Low (assumes error-free) | High (sync byte 0x47) |
| **Clock Reference** | SCR (System Clock Ref) | PCR (Program Clock Ref) |
| **Multiplexing** | Single program | Multiple programs possible |
| **Header Overhead** | ~0.1% | ~2.1% (4/188) |
| **Typical Application** | MPEG-1 / MPEG-2 PS | MPEG-2 TS / DVB |

## 3.5 Derivation of PTS-to-Real-Time Conversion

The PTS is a 33-bit counter ticking at **90 kHz**. To convert PTS to real-time seconds:

### Step 1: PTS Definition

$$
T_{\text{real}} = \frac{\text{PTS}}{f_{\text{clock}}} = \frac{\text{PTS}}{90{,}000}
$$

### Step 2: Numerical Example
A PTS value of $1{,}800{,}000$ corresponds to:

$$
T_{\text{real}} = \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{90{,}000} = 20\,\text{seconds}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** A common KTU question asks: *"What is the real-time duration represented by a PTS value of 27,000,000?"* Answer: $27{,}000{,}000 / 90{,}000 = 300\,\text{seconds} = 5\,\text{minutes}$.

## 4.2 MPEG System Layer Block Diagram

The diagram below maps the **complete functional architecture** of an MPEG encoder-multiplexer-transmitter chain, showing how a raw video/audio source is transformed into a transport stream.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcVid[VIDEO SOURCE YCBCR 420] --> vidEnc[VIDEO ENCODER DCT MOTION EST QUANTIZATION]
```

The following diagram illustrates the **byte-level structure** of a single 188-byte MPEG-2 Transport Stream packet. This is a KTU-favorite diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pkt[TS PACKET 188 BYTES TOTAL] --> sb[SYNC BYTE 0X47 1 BYTE]
    pkt --> tei[TEI 1 BIT]

    pkt --> prio[PRIORITY 1 BIT]
    pkt --> pid[PID 13 BITS PACKET IDENTIFIER]
    pkt --> tsc[TSC 2 BITS TRANSPORT SCRAMBLING]
    pkt --> afc[AFC 2 BITS ADAPTATION FIELD CTRL]

    pkt --> af[ADAPTATION FIELD VARIABLE 0 TO 184 BYTES]
    pkt --> pay[PAYLOAD 0 TO 184 BYTES VIDEO OR AUDIO PES]
```
