2. **Reed-Solomon Outer Encoder:** Encodes each 188-byte packet to a 204-byte packet using the systematic RS(204,188, t=8) code. The added 16 parity bytes allow correction of up to 8 byte errors per packet — excellent against burst errors.
3. **Outer Convolutional Interleaver:** Depth-12 Forney interleaver that redistributes burst errors so the Viterbi decoder sees random errors.
4. **Convolutional Inner Encoder:** Mother code of rate 1/2, constraint length K=7, generator polynomials G1=171(octal), G2=133(octal).
5. **Puncturer:** Selectively deletes coded bits to yield the operator-configured rate: 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, or 7/8.
6. **QPSK Baseband Mapper:** Maps pairs of bits to one of four Gray-coded constellation points: 00→0°, 01→90°, 11→180°, 10→270°.
7. **Square-Root Raised Cosine (SRRC) Filter:** Shapes the baseband I/Q signals with roll-off α = 0.35 to limit adjacent-channel interference.
8. **IF Modulator & RF Up-converter:** Mixes the baseband signal to a 70 MHz IF, then to the Ku-band uplink carrier (~14 GHz).
9. **HPA (Travelling Wave Tube Amplifier):** Boosts the signal to ~100–250 W for transmission to the satellite.

*(Marks: 1 for diagram quality, 1 per block explained × 5 key blocks, 1 for overall coherence)*

**(b) Explain the Reed-Solomon and convolutional coding schemes used in DVB-S with their parameters. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Reed-Solomon Outer Code — RS(204, 188, t = 8):**
- Operates in **GF(2⁸)** — 256-ary Galois Field.
- For each 188-byte MPEG-2 packet, it computes **16 parity bytes** appended to produce a 204-byte codeword.
- The minimum distance is $d_{min} = 2t + 1 = 17$.
- Corrects up to **t = 8 byte errors** per packet (powerful against burst errors from rain fade and interference).
- Native block FEC: corrects errors *after* the Viterbi decoder's residual errors.

**Convolutional Inner Code — K = 7, Rate 1/2 (mother code):**
- Constraint length **K = 7** (memory order m = 6, so 64 states in the trellis).
- Generator polynomials in octal: **G1 = 171, G2 = 133**.
- Viterbi soft-decision decoding with **3-bit (8-level) quantization** is standard.
- Provides the bulk of the **~5 dB coding gain** at QEF.

**Why Concatenation?** Viterbi's BER curve flattens ("error floor" at ~10⁻⁶) and cannot reach the 10⁻¹⁰ required by QEF alone. The outer RS code acts as a "cleanup" stage, taking the ~10⁻⁶ Viterbi output and pushing it to ~10⁻¹¹ — hence the name **concatenated coding** (Forney, 1966).

*(Marks: 2 for RS parameters, 2 for convolutional parameters, 2 for concatenation rationale, 1 for technical clarity)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Set B]**

**(a) Explain the key features of DVB-S2 and compare it with DVB-S. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**DVB-S2 Key Features:**
1. **Multiple Constellations:** QPSK, 8PSK, 16APSK, 32APSK — adapt constellation to link conditions.
2. **LDPC Inner Code:** Long block LDPC codes (64 800 bits) replace convolutional codes, operating within **0.7–1.2 dB of the Shannon limit**.
3. **BCH Outer Code:** Replaces Reed-Solomon; the BCH code cleans up the residual LDPC error floor.
4. **Flexible Input Formats:** Generic Stream (GS), MPEG-TS, and Continuous (CC) modes.
5. **Variable & Adaptive Coding-Modulation (VCM/ACM):** Different FEC rates/modulations per frame; ACM allows per-frame adaptation to rain fade.
6. **Reduced Roll-off:** α = 0.20 and 0.25 options (in addition to 0.35) — saving 15–25% bandwidth.
7. **Higher Net Bit Rate:** A 36 MHz transponder delivers **~75 Mbit/s** in DVB-S2 vs **~41 Mbit/s** in DVB-S.

**DVB-S vs DVB-S2 Comparison:**

| Aspect | DVB-S | DVB-S2 |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Modulation | QPSK only | QPSK, 8PSK, 16APSK, 32APSK |
| Inner FEC | Convolutional K=7 | LDPC |
| Outer FEC | Reed-Solomon | BCH |
| Max Spectral Efficiency | 1.30 bit/s/Hz | ~4.5 bit/s/Hz |
| Coding Gain vs. Shannon | ~5 dB | ~1 dB |
| Roll-off (α) | 0.35 only | 0.20, 0.25, 0.35 |
| Adaptive Coding | No | Yes (VCM/ACM) |
| Net Bit Rate (36 MHz) | ~41 Mbit/s | ~75 Mbit/s |

*(Marks: 3 for DVB-S2 features, 4 for comparison table and discussion)*

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the DVB-S receiver section. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The DVB-S receiver reverses every operation performed at the transmitter:

1. **Dish Antenna (60–90 cm):** Collects the 12 GHz Ku-band downlink signal.
2. **LNB (Low-Noise Block Down-Converter):** Amplifies with ~0.7 dB noise figure and down-converts to the **L-band (950–2150 MHz)** for coaxial cable distribution to the indoor unit.
3. **RF Tuner & AGC:** Selects the desired transponder, applies Automatic Gain Control to normalize the IF level.
4. **QPSK Demodulator (Costas Loop):** Recovers the carrier phase (since the carrier is suppressed in QPSK) and produces baseband I/Q.
5. **Matched SRRC Filter:** Minimizes ISI introduced by the satellite channel.
6. **Viterbi Soft-Decision Decoder:** Recovers the most likely convolutional code sequence using **8-level (3-bit) soft decisions** and outputs the 1/2, 2/3, … 7/8 punctured rate as selected by the operator.
7. **Deinterleaver:** Reverses the Forney interleaving.
8. **Reed-Solomon Decoder RS(204,188):** Corrects up to 8 byte errors per 204-byte codeword, achieving the QEF target of <1 error/hour.
9. **MPEG-2 Transport Stream Output:** Delivered to the Set-Top Box's MPEG-2 decoder, which decompresses video and audio for HDMI output.
10. **QEF Monitor:** Continuously checks for uncorrected errors; if errors exceed QEF, the STB may request the network operator to **reduce the symbol rate or switch to a more robust FEC rate**.

*(Marks: 1 for diagram, 5 for block explanations, 1 for the QEF feedback loop)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Confusing Roll-off Factors:** DVB-S uses **α = 0.35** *uniquely*. DVB-S2 introduces α = 0.20 and 0.25. Writing "α = 0.25 for DVB-S" costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the Interleaver:** Many students omit the convolutional interleaver between RS and convolutional coding. Without it, the Viterbi decoder's error correction is degraded by burst errors — the system fails QEF.
> 3. **Misstating the RS Code:** Writing "RS(255, 239)" (the DVB-C cable variant) instead of "RS(204, 188, t=8)" is a common error — DVB-S **shortens** the RS(255,239) by zero-padding.
> 4. **QEF Threshold:** Stating "BER < 10⁻⁴" is incorrect; the correct QEF threshold is **~10⁻¹⁰ to 10⁻¹¹** (one uncorrected error per hour).
> 5. **QPSK Bit-per-Symbol:** Forgetting that QPSK = 4 symbols, 2 bits per symbol, costs 1 mark in bit-rate calculations.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **DVB-S** = MPEG-2 TS + RS(204,188) + K=7 Convolutional + QPSK + α=0.35
- ✅ **Five FEC rates:** 1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8
- ✅ **Roll-off factor α = 0.35** uniquely; bandwidth factor = **1.35 × symbol rate**
- ✅ **Spectral efficiency formula:** $\eta = 2R_c / 1.35$ (max ≈ 1.30 bit/s/Hz at 7/8)
- ✅ **QEF = <1 uncorrected error per hour ≈ BER 10⁻¹⁰ to 10⁻¹¹**
- ✅ **Concatenated coding:** Outer RS (bursts) + Inner Viterbi (random) ≈ 5 dB coding gain
- ✅ **Constraint length K = 7, generator polynomials 171, 133 (octal)**
- ✅ **Interleaver depth = 12** (Forney/convolutional type)
- ✅ **DVB-S2 upgrades:** LDPC + BCH, QPSK/8PSK/16APSK/32APSK, ACM/VCM, α = 0.20/0.25
- ✅ **DVB-S2 net bit rate** on 36 MHz transponder ≈ **75 Mbit/s** (vs 41 Mbit/s in DVB-S)
- ✅ **LNB function:** 12 GHz → 950–2150 MHz L-band down-conversion with low noise figure (~0.7 dB)
- ✅ **Costas Loop** is the standard QPSK carrier recovery circuit
- ✅ **Viterbi decoding** uses 3-bit (8-level) soft decisions
- ✅ **Shannon limit context:** DVB-S sits ~5.5 dB from Shannon; DVB-S2 sits only ~0.7–1.2 dB from Shannon
- ✅ **Real-world DTH context:** Tata Play, Airtel Xstream, DD Direct+, Dish TV India — all DVB-S/S2 users
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DVB-S Modulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERPRISE ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** DVB-S Modulator # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) The **DVB-S Modulator** (Digital Video Broadcasting – Satellite Modulator) is the transmit-side signal processing subsystem defined by the **ETSI EN 300 421** standard. Its function is to convert a baseband digital television bitstream (an MPEG-2 Transport Stream) into a band-limited, error-protected, QPSK-modulated intermediate-frequency signal suitable for upconversion to the satellite downlink band (typically the **Ku-band (12 GHz)** or **C-band (4 GHz)**). In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the DVB-S modulator is the **"channel coder + baseband shaper + digital modulator"** assembly placed between the MPEG-2 multiplexer and the satellite upconverter (BUC – Block Up-Converter). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEEVT722 – Module 2):** > DVB-S forms the foundational digital satellite broadcast chain. You are expected to know the **functional blocks, FEC scheme, QPSK constellation, roll-off factor (0.35), and end-to-end bit-rate / bandwidth relationships**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Armored Courier" Imagine you have to send a stack of 1,000 highly confidential documents across a country where thieves (noise, interference, rain fade) will try to tamper with every page. | Courier Step | Real-World Action | DVB-S Modulator Equivalent | |---|---|---| | 1. Shuffle papers randomly | So thieves cannot spot patterns | **Energy Dispersal (M-sequence scrambler)** | | 2. Make 16 backup copies of each page | If a page is lost, reconstruct it | **Reed–Solomon outer FEC: RS(204,188,8)** | | 3. Interleave the pages | Burst loss (a whole chapter gone) is spread thin | **Convolutional Interleaver (I=12)** | | 4. Add clever error-correcting codes | Even partial damage is repaired | **Punctured Convolutional inner code (rate 1/2 to 7/8)** | | 5. Translate text into Morse code with 4 tones | Efficient long-distance transmission | **QPSK mapping** | | 6. Smooth the audio with a filter | Don't waste spectrum | **Square-Root Raised Cosine (SRRC), α = 0.35** | | 7. Hand to satellite courier | Final delivery to space | **Upconversion to RF (12 GHz Ku-band)** | So, the **DVB-S Modulator = Scrambler → RS Encoder → Interleaver → Convolutional Encoder → QPSK Mapper → SRRC Filter → IF output**. > [!NOTE] > **Why QPSK specifically for satellite?** Because the satellite channel is power-limited (the transponder has finite EIRP), QPSK's constant envelope and 2 bits/symbol efficiency make it the most robust choice against amplifier non-linearities and rain fade. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following parameters are **fixed by the DVB-S specification** and must be memorized verbatim for KTU board examinations: - **Roll-off factor (α) = 0.35** - **Convolutional code constraint length = 7 (G1 = 171_oct, G2 = 133_oct)** - **Outer code RS(204,188) with t = 8 symbol error correction** - **Interleaver depth I = 12 (Forney-style, I × M = 12 × 17 bytes)** - **Modulation: QPSK (Gray-coded)** - **IF center frequency: 70 MHz or 140 MHz (L-band 950–2150 MHz after upconversion)** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Block Architecture of a DVB-S Modulator The signal flow is **strictly serial** and **single-input / single-output**. Each block is mandatory by ETSI EN 300 421. ### Block 1 – MPEG-2 Transport Stream Adaptation & Sync Inversion - Input arrives as **188-byte packets** (1 sync byte + 187 payload bytes). - The sync byte `0x47` is **inverted to `0xB8`** every 8 packets for scrambling synchronization. ### Block 2 – Energy Dispersal (PRBS Scrambler) - A **Pseudo-Random Binary Sequence (PRBS)** of length $2^{15}-1$ is XORed with the bitstream. - **Generator polynomial**: $1 + X^{14} + X^{15}$ - **Purpose**: Breaks long runs of 0s or 1s that would otherwise create strong DC content and clock-recovery failure at the receiver. ### Block 3 – Reed–Solomon Outer Encoder: RS(204,188,8) - Operates on **GF(2⁸)** (Galois Field with 256 elements). - Adds **16 parity bytes** per packet → 188 → 204 bytes. - **Generator polynomial**: $g(x) = (x + \alpha^0)(x + \alpha^1)\cdots(x + \alpha^{15})$ - **t = 8**: can correct up to 8 byte errors per 204-byte codeword. ### Block 4 – Convolutional Interleaver (Forney / Ramsey Type III) - **Interleaving depth**: $I = 12$ - **Branch length offset**: $M = 17$ bytes - **Total memory**: $I \times M \times (I-1)/2 = 17 \times 12 \times 11/2 = 1122$ bytes - A byte-delay line shifts data across 12 branches with linearly increasing delays. ### Block 5 – Inner Punctured Convolutional Encoder - **Mother code rate**: 1/2 (output of Viterbi's classical code). - **Constraint length**: $K = 7$, generators $G_1 = 171_o$, $G_2 = 133_o$. - **Puncturing patterns** produce effective rates: - **1/2** (no puncturing) - **2/3** (every 4th bit of second output is dropped) - **3/4** - **5/6** - **7/8** - These trade **bandwidth efficiency** for **error resilience**. ### Block 6 – QPSK Baseband Mapper - Two consecutive bits → one QPSK symbol (00, 01, 10, 11). - **Gray coding** is used: adjacent constellation points differ by only one bit. - Symbol mapping rule: $$ s_k = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left[(2b_{2k}-1) + j(2b_{2k+1}-1)\right] $$ ### Block 7 – Square-Root Raised Cosine (SRRC) Pulse Shaping - Splits equally between TX and RX as **matched filters**. - **Roll-off factor**: $\alpha = 0.35$ (fixed for DVB-S). - 3-dB bandwidth: $\text{BW}_{\text{Nyquist}} = R_s/2$ - Total occupied bandwidth: $\text{BW}_{\text{sig}} = R_s(1+\alpha)$ ### Block 8 – IF Upconversion (70 MHz / 140 MHz / L-band 950–2150 MHz) - The shaped QPSK signal is mixed with a local oscillator to IF for subsequent BUC stage. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | QPSK Symbol Rate | $R_s = R_b / 2$ | symbols/s (baud) | | 2 | RS Coded Bit Rate | $R_b^{\text{(RS)}} = R_b \times (204/188)$ | bits/s | | 3 | Fully Coded Bit Rate | $R_c^{\text{(FEC)}} = R_b^{\text{(RS)}} / r$ | where $r$ = inner code rate | | 4 | Occupied RF Bandwidth | $\text{BW} = R_s (1+\alpha)$ | Hz, with $\alpha = 0.35$ | | 5 | Nyquist Minimum BW | $\text{BW}_{\min} = R_s/2$ | Hz (without roll-off) | | 6 | Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{2 \, r_{\text{net}}}{\,1+\alpha\,}$ | bit/s/Hz | | 7 | C/N to $E_b/N_0$ | $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} = \dfrac{C}{N} \cdot \dfrac{B_n}{R_b}$ | dimensionless | | 8 | Shannon Limit (AWGN) | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bits/s | | 9 | Reed–Solomon efficiency | $\eta_{\text{RS}} = 188/204 = 0.9216$ | ratio | | 10 | Overall system efficiency | $\eta_{\text{sys}} = \eta_{\text{RS}} \times r \times \eta_{\text{QPSK}}$ | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Trap:** In KTU valuation, marks are awarded only when the student mentions **α = 0.35** explicitly along with the bandwidth formula. Writing "BW = symbol rate" loses 1 mark. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering DVB-S modulators underpin: - **Direct-To-Home (DTH)** television (e.g., Tata Sky, Dish TV). - **Satellite news gathering (SNG)** trucks. - **IP-based satellite backhaul** for rural broadband. - **Military & maritime SATCOM** (used heavily because QPSK tolerates amplifier nonlinearity). Modern extensions (**DVB-S2** and **DVB-S2X**) inherit the same baseband pipeline but switch to **8PSK, 16APSK, 32APSK** and **LDPC** coding — questions on DVB-S therefore build directly into S2 questions in higher semesters. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section derives the **end-to-end bandwidth and efficiency** relationships from first principles — the most recurring 7-mark derivation in KTU Module 2. --- ## 3.1 Derivation: Bit-Rate to Symbol-Rate Relationship **Given:** - MPEG-2 TS source bit rate: $R_b$ (bits/s) - QPSK constellation: $M = 4$ → $\log_2 M = 2$ bits/symbol **Step 1 – QPSK symbol rate definition.** A QPSK symbol carries exactly 2 bits. Therefore, the symbol rate is half the input bit rate: $$ R_s = \frac{R_b}{2} \quad \text{[symbols/second]} $$ **Step 2 – Apply outer Reed–Solomon FEC.** RS(204, 188) appends 16 parity bytes per 188-byte packet. The bit rate after RS encoding rises by the factor $\frac{204}{188}$: $$ R_b^{\text{(RS)}} = R_b \times \frac{204}{188} = 1.0851 \cdot R_b $$ **Step 3 – Apply inner convolutional code of rate $r$.** The convolutional encoder (with puncturing) outputs $1/r$ bits for every input bit. Hence the **chipped bit rate** is: $$ R_c^{\text{(FEC)}} = \frac{R_b^{\text{(RS)}}}{r} = \frac{1.0851 \cdot R_b}{r} $$ **Step 4 – Re-derive symbol rate after full FEC chain.** Since QPSK is applied after FEC, the symbol rate seen at the modulator output is: $$ R_s = \frac{R_c^{\text{(FEC)}}}{2} = \frac{1.0851 \cdot R_b}{2r} $$ This is the most important DVB-S formula you will use in problems. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Occupied Bandwidth (Nyquist + Roll-off) **Step 1 – Ideal Nyquist bandwidth (brick-wall filter).** For a baseband signal transitioning at symbol rate $R_s$ with no excess bandwidth: $$ B_{\text{Nyquist}} = \frac{R_s}{2} \quad \text{(double-sided complex bandwidth)} $$ **Step 2 – Realistic bandwidth with raised cosine.** A SRRC filter of roll-off $\alpha$ widens the spectrum by the factor $(1+\alpha)$: $$ \text{BW}_{\text{occupied}} = R_s \times (1 + \alpha) $$ **Step 3 – Substitute DVB-S specific value $\alpha = 0.35$:** $$ \boxed{\text{BW}_{\text{DVB-S}} = R_s \times 1.35} $$ **Step 4 – Express fully in terms of source bit rate $R_b$:** $$ \text{BW}_{\text{DVB-S}} = \frac{1.0851 \cdot R_b}{2r} \times 1.35 $$ $$ \text{BW}_{\text{DVB-S}} = \frac{0.7325 \cdot R_b}{r} \quad \text{(approx.)} $$ --- ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style) > **Problem:** A DVB-S system transmits an MPEG-2 TS at **R_b = 6 Mbps** using an inner code rate **r = 3/4**. Calculate: > (a) QPSK symbol rate > (b) Occupied RF bandwidth > (c) Spectral efficiency ### Solution: **(a) Symbol rate:** $$ R_s = \frac{1.0851 \times 6 \times 10^6}{2 \times (3/4)} = \frac{6.5106 \times 10^6}{1.5} = 4.34 \times 10^6 \text{ symbols/s} $$ **(b) Occupied bandwidth:** $$ \text{BW} = R_s \times (1+\alpha) = 4.34 \times 10^6 \times 1.35 = 5.86 \text{ MHz} $$ **(c) Spectral efficiency:** $$ \eta = \frac{2 \, r_{\text{net}}}{1+\alpha} = \frac{2 \times (188/204) \times (3/4)}{1.35} $$ $$ \eta = \frac{2 \times 0.9216 \times 0.75}{1.35} = \frac{1.3824}{1.35} = 1.024 \text{ bit/s/Hz} $$ --- ## 3.4 $E_b/N_0$ vs $C/N$ Conversion Derivation This derivation is mandatory for satellite link-budget problems in KTU. **Step 1 – Definitions.** Carrier power $C$ delivers $\eta$ bits per second per Hz of bandwidth $B_n$: $$ R_b = \eta \cdot B_n $$ **Step 2 – Energy per bit:** $$ E_b = \frac{C}{R_b} $$ **Step 3 – Noise power spectral density:** $$ N_0 = \frac{N}{B_n} $$ **Step 4 – Combine:** $$ \frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{C}{R_b} \cdot \frac{B_n}{N} = \frac{C}{N} \cdot \frac{B_n}{R_b} $$ In dB form: $$ \boxed{\left[\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right]_{dB} = \left[\frac{C}{N}\right]_{dB} + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{B_n}{R_b}\right)} $$ For DVB-S with $r = 3/4$, RS efficiency 188/204, and $\alpha = 0.35$: $$ \frac{B_n}{R_b} = \frac{(1+\alpha)}{2 \, r_{\text{net}}} = \frac{1.35}{2 \times 0.9216 \times 0.75} = 0.976 $$ So $\frac{E_b}{N_0}$ is essentially equal to $\frac{C}{N}$ (in dB) for typical DVB-S parameters. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Block Diagram of a DVB-S Modulator The following Mermaid flow-diagram depicts every mandatory functional block, the data-width transformation, and the parameter set used in the KTU syllabus. ```mermaid flowchart LR in1["MPEG2 TS
188 BYTE PACKETS
Rb"] --> A1["SYNC INVERSION
0x47 to 0xB8"] A1 --> A2["ENERGY DISPERSAL
PRBS XOR
G1 plus X14 plus X15"] A2 --> A3["REED SOLOMON ENCODER
RS 204 188 8
GF 2 POWER 8"] A3 --> A4["CONVOLUTIONAL
INTERLEAVER
I equals 12
M equals 17"] A4 --> A5["INNER CONVOLUTIONAL
ENCODER
K equals 7
G1 171 G2 133"] A5 --> A6["PUNCTURING
MATRIX
RATE 1/2 2/3 3/4 5/6 7/8"] A6 --> A7["QPSK MAPPER
GRAY CODED
2 BITS PER SYMBOL"] A7 --> A8["SRRC FILTER
ALPHA equals 0.35
MATCHED AT RX"] A8 --> A9["IF UPCONVERSION
70 MHZ OR
LBAND 950 to 2150"] A9 --> out1["SATELLITE BUC
12 GHZ KUBAND
DOWNLINK"] style in1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB style A1 fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#F5862C style A2 fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#F5862C style A3 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853 style A4 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853 style A5 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853 style A6 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335 style A7 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335 style A8 fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6 style A9 fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6 style out1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB ``` ## 4.2 QPSK Constellation (Gray-Coded) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph quad_top_left["Q2: 10"] ptl["Imag axis +"] end subgraph quad_top_right["Q1: 00"] ptr["Real axis +"] end subgraph quad_bot_left["Q3: 11"] pbl["Imag axis -"] end subgraph quad_bot_right["Q4: 01"] pbr["Real axis -"] end ptr --- ptl ptr --- pbr ptl --- pbl pbr --- pbl style ptr fill:#B7E1CD,stroke:#34A853 style ptl fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335 style pbl fill:#B7E1CD,stroke:#34A853 style pbr fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335 ``` | Quadrant | Bit Pair (b0 b1) | I (Real) | Q (Imag) | |---|---|---|---| | Q1 (NE) | 00 | +1/√2 | +1/√2 | | Q2 (NW) | 01 | −1/√2 | +1/√2 | | Q3 (SW) | 11 | −1/√2 | −1/√2 | | Q4 (SE) | 10 | +1/√2 | −1/√2 | > Gray coding ensures adjacent symbols differ in **only one bit**, minimising the average Hamming distance per symbol error. ## 4.3 Puncturing Pattern Visualisation (Rate 3/4 Example) ```mermaid flowchart LR bitIn["INPUT BITS
A B C D E F"] --> conv["CONVOLUTIONAL
ENCODER K=7"] conv --> x1["X OUTPUT
A B C D E F"] conv --> x2["Y OUTPUT
A B C D E F"] x2 --> punch["PUNCTURING MATRIX
110 110
REMOVE POSITIONS
3 AND 6"] x1 --> merge punch --> merge["MULTIPLEXER
SERIAL OUTPUT"] merge --> outBit["OUTPUT BITS
A A B B C D E E F F"] style conv fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853 style punch fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335 style merge fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6 ``` For DVB-S at rate 3/4: out of every 6 bits input, 8 bits are output → effective rate = 6/8 = **3/4**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A – 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEEVT722]** **State the role of the Reed–Solomon outer encoder in the DVB-S modulator.** **Model Answer:** Reed–Solomon outer encoder operates on 188-byte MPEG-2 packets over GF(2⁸) and produces a 204-byte codeword (RS(204,188,8)). It can correct up to **t = 8 byte errors per packet**, thereby providing strong protection against random and burst errors introduced by the satellite channel. It is the **outer** layer of the concatenated FEC scheme in DVB-S. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, OEEVT722]** **Why is QPSK chosen as the modulation scheme in DVB-S?** **Model Answer:** QPSK is selected because: 1. It provides a **constant envelope** → robust against non-linear distortion in satellite HPAs (TWTAs). 2. Low **bit-error rate** at low SNR — ideal for the power-limited downlink. 3. Spectral efficiency of **2 bits/s/Hz** (with α = 0.35 → ~1.48 bit/s/Hz). 4. Simple coherent demodulation possible. **(3 marks)** --- ## PART B – 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEEVT722]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks of a DVB-S modulator and the role of each block. **(b) [7 Marks]** A DVB-S system carries an MPEG-2 transport stream at 8 Mbps using an inner code rate of **2/3**. Calculate: (i) The QPSK symbol rate. (ii) The occupied RF bandwidth (assume α = 0.35). (iii) The spectral efficiency. #### Model Solution **(a)** *[Draw block diagram from Section 4.1, then explain each block.]* | Block | Function | |---|---| | MPEG-2 TS Input | 188-byte packet stream | | Sync Inversion | Marks every 8th packet for descrambler sync | | PRBS Scrambler | Energy dispersal using $1 + X^{14} + X^{15}$ | | Reed–Solomon (204,188,8) | Adds 16 parity bytes, corrects ≤ 8 byte errors | | Convolutional Interleaver | Spreads burst errors, I = 12 | | Convolutional Encoder (K=7) | Inner FEC, mother rate 1/2 | | Puncturing | Achieves rates 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8 | | QPSK Mapper | 2 bits/symbol, Gray-coded | | SRRC Filter (α = 0.35) | Bandwidth limiting | | IF Upconversion | 70 MHz / L-band | **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) Symbol rate:** $$ R_s = \frac{R_b \times (204/188)}{2 \times r} = \frac{8 \times 10^6 \times 1.0851}{2 \times (2/3)} $$ $$ R_s = \frac{8.681 \times 10^6}{1.3333} = 6.51 \times 10^6 \text{ symbols/s} $$ **(ii) Occupied bandwidth:** $$ \text{BW} = R_s \times (1+\alpha) = 6.51 \times 10^6 \times 1.35 = 8.79 \text{ MHz} $$ **(iii) Spectral efficiency:** $$ \eta = \frac{R_b}{\text{BW}} = \frac{8}{8.79} = 0.910 \text{ bit/s/Hz} $$ Or using the formula: $$ \eta = \frac{2 \times r_{\text{net}}}{1+\alpha} = \frac{2 \times (188/204) \times (2/3)}{1.35} = \frac{1.228}{1.35} = 0.910 \text{ bit/s/Hz} $$ **(Total: 14 marks)** --- ### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, OEEVT722]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the Reed–Solomon encoding and convolutional interleaving operations performed in the DVB-S modulator. Why is concatenated coding used? **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between input MPEG-2 bit rate and the QPSK symbol rate for a DVB-S modulator using inner code rate $r$. Show that the occupied bandwidth is $\text{BW} = R_s(1+\alpha)$. #### Model Solution **(a)** Reed–Solomon Encoding: - RS(204,188,8) code defined over **GF(2⁸)** with primitive polynomial $p(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x^2 + 1$. - Generator polynomial: $g(x) = \prod_{i=0}^{15}(x + \alpha^i)$ - 188-byte packet → 204-byte codeword (16 parity bytes appended). - **t = 8 byte errors** can be corrected → code gain of ~5 dB at QEF. Convolutional Interleaving: - **Forney structure** with depth I = 12. - 12 branches with linearly increasing delays: $0, M, 2M, \ldots, 11M$ where $M = 17$ bytes. - **Total delay** = $I(I-1)M = 12 \times 11 \times 17 = 2244$ bytes. - At the receiver, a **deinterleaver** reverses the process, converting burst errors into spread-out single errors which the RS decoder can correct. **Why Concatenated Coding?** A single strong FEC would require huge decoder complexity. Concatenation combines: - **Outer RS (burst-correction)** with - **Inner convolutional (random-correction)** to achieve **QEF (Quasi-Error-Free, BER < 10⁻¹¹)** at very low $E_b/N_0$ (~5 dB). **(b) Derivation** *(Refer to Section 3.1 and 3.2 for full derivation.)* Starting from $R_s = \dfrac{R_b \times (204/188)}{2r} = \dfrac{1.0851 R_b}{2r}$ Applying the raised-cosine roll-off factor α: $$ \text{BW} = R_s(1 + \alpha) = \frac{1.0851 R_b (1+\alpha)}{2r} $$ For DVB-S with $\alpha = 0.35$: $$ \boxed{\text{BW} = \frac{1.0851 R_b}{2r} \times 1.35} $$ This proves the bandwidth relationship from first principles. **(14 marks total)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Avoid These Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting α = 0.35** in bandwidth problems. This costs 1 mark in nearly every KTU paper. > 2. **Confusing "inner code rate"** $r$ with the "effective rate". The QPSK mapper always uses the **post-puncturing** rate. > 3. **Mixing up "symbol rate"** $R_s$ with "chipped bit rate" $R_c$. Always show both in derivations. > 4. **Not specifying "Gray coding"** when drawing QPSK constellation — losing 1 mark. > 5. **Skipping the "Why concatenated coding?"** question — examiners expect at least 2 lines on the rationale. > 6. **Writing "188/204" instead of "204/188"** when computing coded bit rate — direction matters! --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **DVB-S = ETSI EN 300 421**, MPEG-2 based, QPSK modulated, satellite downlink standard. - ✅ **Modulator chain** = Scrambler → RS(204,188,8) → Interleaver (I=12, M=17) → Convolutional (K=7) → Puncturing → QPSK → SRRC (α=0.35) → IF upconversion. - ✅ **Roll-off factor α = 0.35** is the single most-tested numerical constant. - ✅ **Symbol rate formula**: $R_s = 1.0851 R_b / (2r)$ - ✅ **Occupied BW**: $\text{BW} = R_s(1+\alpha) = 1.35 R_s$ - ✅ **Outer code**: Reed–Solomon over GF(2⁸), $t = 8$ symbol errors corrected. - ✅ **Inner code**: Convolutional with generators $G_1 = 171_o$, $G_2 = 133_o$, K = 7. - ✅ **Punctured rates**: 1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8 — trade-off between robustness and throughput. - ✅ **QPSK**: 2 bits/symbol, Gray-coded, constant envelope. - ✅ **Spectral efficiency formula**: $\eta = \dfrac{2r_{\text{net}}}{1+\alpha}$ bit/s/Hz. - ✅ **Concatenated FEC** provides QEF (BER < 10⁻¹¹) at $E_b/N_0 \approx 4.5$ to 5.5 dB depending on rate. - ✅ **DVB-S2 upgrade**: replaces RS+CC with LDPC + BCH and adds 8PSK/16APSK/32APSK. - ✅ **Real-world use**: DTH TV (Tata Sky, Airtel), maritime SATCOM, satellite news gathering, rural broadband. > [!NOTE] > **Quick Mnemonic for the modulator pipeline:** > **"S-R-I-C-P-Q-F-U"** = **S**crambler → **R**S-Encoder → **I**nterleaver → **C**onvolutional Encoder → **P**uncturing → **Q**PSK → **F**ilter (SRRC) → **U**pconvert.

topics.title: DVB-S Set-Top Box
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DVB-S Set-Top Box

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **DVB-S Set-Top Box (STB)** is an embedded consumer-electronics receiver terminal designed to receive, demodulate, channel-decode, demultiplex, and decode MPEG-2 compressed audio-video bit-streams transmitted in conformance with the **ETSI EN 300 421** (Digital Video Broadcasting — Satellite) standard, and to present the reconstructed AV content to a standard television display.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The STB is the *end-equipment* counterpart of a DVB-S transmission chain. The 2024 scheme expects students to map every block inside the STB to the corresponding impairment it removes (noise, ISI, errors, scrambling, compression).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator Booth" Model

Imagine an international telephone call between two people who speak different languages:

| Stage | Telephone Analogy | DVB-S Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Speaker (Far end) | Talks in encoded language | Uplink encoder, MPEG-2 encoder at broadcaster |
| Noisy Channel | Crackling line, distance loss | Satellite link, rain fade, thermal noise |
| Translator Booth | Unscrambles language, removes noise | **DVB-S STB** |
| Listener | Understands the message | TV / display |

The STB is the "translator" that **inverts every stage** the broadcaster applied:
- Lifts the signal down from microwave frequencies → **Tuner**
- Pulls it out of noise & interference → **QPSK demod + FEC**
- Opens the package the broadcaster packed → **Transport demux**
- Decodes the compressed content → **MPEG-2 AV decoder**

> [!NOTE]
> The LNB (Low-Noise Block down-converter) is **NOT** technically inside the STB; it sits at the dish feed-horn. The STB begins at the L-band IF input (~950–2150 MHz).

### 1.3 Key Standardized Parameters (DVB-S / DVB-S2 baseline)

- **Carrier Frequency at STB input:** **L-band, 950 MHz – 2150 MHz** (after LNB)
- **Modulation:** **QPSK** (DVB-S); 8PSK/APSK (DVB-S2)
- **Outer FEC:** **Reed-Solomon (204, 188, t = 8)**
- **Inner FEC:** **Convolutional code** with puncturing; rates **1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8**
- **Roll-off factor (α):** **0.35**
- **Output formats:** Composite, S-Video, Component, HDMI (modern)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Signal-Flow Logic Inside the STB (Step-by-Step)

The DVB-S STB can be modeled as a **cascade of six canonical blocks**. Each block reverses one specific operation performed at the uplink.

1. **Tuner / Front-End (Channel Selection)**
   - Selects one transponder from the wideband L-band input.
   - Uses a PLL-synthesized local oscillator to mix down to a fixed **Zero-IF** or **Low-IF** (~36 MHz) for A/D conversion.
   - Why? Practical A/D converters and digital demodulators cannot work directly at GHz.

2. **A/D Conversion & QPSK Demodulation**
   - Samples the IF and applies matched filtering (root-raised cosine, α = 0.35).
   - Recovers the I and Q baseband streams, then makes hard/soft symbol decisions.
   - Why QPSK? It is the most **power-efficient** scheme for the noise-limited satellite channel (constant envelope, robust against non-linearity of TWTA).

3. **Inner FEC — Viterbi Decoder (Convolutional)**
   - Accepts soft decisions from the demodulator.
   - Decodes the punctured convolutional code (constraint length K = 7, rates 1/2 to 7/8).
   - Why? Corrects the random errors caused by AWGN before burst-error correction is attempted.

4. **Outer FEC — Reed-Solomon RS(204,188) Decoder**
   - The "188/204" overhead is **MPEG-2 Transport Stream framing**: 188 useful bytes are RS-encoded to 204 bytes (16 parity bytes).
   - Corrects up to **t = 8 byte errors per packet**.
   - Why? The Viterbi output still contains residual burst errors; RS is excellent against bursts.

5. **MPEG-2 Transport Stream (TS) Demultiplexer**
   - De-frames 188-byte packets (sync byte 0x47).
   - Filters by **PID (Packet Identifier)** to select the desired video, audio, and PMT/CAT.
   - Why? A single transponder carries many services; demux selects one.

6. **MPEG-2 Audio/Video Decoder + Conditional Access + Display Driver**
   - Decodes compressed AV (typically MPEG-2 MP@ML for SD, MP@HL for HD).
   - Conditional Access (CA) smart-card / Irdeto / Viaccess decrypts scrambled services.
   - Outputs CVBS, YPbPr, or HDMI to the TV.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two cascaded FECs?** The concatenated scheme (Viterbi + RS) gives a coding gain of **~6–7 dB** at a target BER of 2×10⁻⁴ after Viterbi, and a quasi-error-free output (BER < 10⁻¹¹) after RS — at very modest complexity compared to a single strong code.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bandwidth of a QPSK-DVB-S transponder | $BW = R_s\,(1 + \alpha) = 1.35\,R_s$ | Hz; $\alpha = 0.35$ roll-off |
| 2 | Useful bit rate (after both FECs) | $R_u = 2\,R_s \cdot \dfrac{188}{204} \cdot CR$ | bits/s; 2 = QPSK bits/symbol |
| 3 | Total transmitted bit rate | $R_{tx} = 2\,R_s \cdot CR$ | bits/s |
| 4 | Reed-Solomon code rate (outer) | $R_{RS} = \dfrac{188}{204} \approx 0.922$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Spectral efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{R_u}{BW} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 188 \cdot CR}{204 \cdot 1.35}$ | bit/s/Hz |
| 6 | Shannon capacity (reference) | $C = BW \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{C}{N}\right)$ | bit/s |
| 7 | Required $E_b/N_0$ (target BER 2e-4, Viterbi) | $(E_b/N_0)_{CR=3/4} \approx 5.5$ dB | dB; from DVB-S link budget |
| 8 | Carrier-to-Noise ratio | $\dfrac{C}{N} = \dfrac{E_b}{N_0} \cdot \dfrac{R_u}{BW}$ | dB |
| 9 | LNB noise figure contribution | $T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_{LNB}$ | Kelvin; system noise temp |
| 10 | Downlink free-space loss (approx.) | $FSL \approx 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$ | dB |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Direct-to-Home (DTH) operators** (Tata Sky, Airtel, Dish TV, Sun Direct) use DVB-S/DVB-S2 STBs at consumer premises.
- The same architecture underlies **VSAT terminals** for banking, maritime, and rural internet.
- Mastering this block diagram is a foundation for **DVB-T (terrestrial)** and **DVB-C (cable)** receivers, which differ mainly in the front-end and modulation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Symbol Rate to Useful Bit Rate

A standard DVB-S transponder (e.g., on INSAT/Intelsat) is configured as: **Symbol rate $R_s = 27.5$ Msym/s**, **Convolutional code rate $CR = 3/4$**, **QPSK modulation**.

**Step 1 — Bits per QPSK symbol**

QPSK has 4 constellation points; each symbol carries $\log_2(4) = 2$ bits.

$$
R_{tx} = 2 \cdot R_s
$$

**Step 2 — Apply convolutional (inner) code**

The punctured convolutional code of rate $CR$ reduces the net bit stream after the encoder by the factor $CR$ (number of info bits per transmitted bit).

$$
R_{tx,\text{after Viterbi}} = R_{tx} \cdot CR
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Reed-Solomon (outer) overhead**

For every 188 useful bytes, the RS encoder appends 16 parity bytes → 204 transmitted bytes per packet. The net useful fraction is:

$$
R_{RS} = \frac{188}{204}
$$

**Step 4 — Final useful bit rate**

$$
R_u = 2\,R_s \cdot CR \cdot \frac{188}{204}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical substitution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_u &= 2 \times 27.5 \times 10^6 \times \frac{3}{4} \times \frac{188}{204} \\
&= 55 \times 10^6 \times 0.75 \times 0.92157 \\
&= 55 \times 10^6 \times 0.69118 \\
&\approx 38.01 \ \text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **useful MPEG-2 payload bit rate** available to the broadcaster on a single 27.5 Msym/s DVB-S transponder at 3/4 FEC.

### 3.2 Derivation: Transponder Bandwidth Occupied

Using the standard roll-off:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= R_s \cdot (1 + \alpha) \\
   &= 27.5 \times 10^6 \times 1.35 \\
   &= 37.125 \ \text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

A standard C-band or Ku-band transponder is typically allotted **36 MHz** or **54 MHz**; the 27.5 Msym/s fits comfortably inside a 36 MHz slot (37.125 MHz is the Nyquist-occupied bandwidth — practical filtering trims edges).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SNR Requirements vs. FEC Rate

| Convolutional Rate $CR$ | Required $E_b/N_0$ for BER ≈ $2 \times 10^{-4}$ (after Viterbi) | Spectral Efficiency $\eta$ (bit/s/Hz) |
|---|---|---|
| 1/2 | ~ 4.5 dB | 0.69 |
| 2/3 | ~ 5.0 dB | 0.92 |
| 3/4 | ~ 5.5 dB | 1.04 |
| 5/6 | ~ 6.0 dB | 1.15 |
| 7/8 | ~ 6.4 dB | 1.21 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering trade-off:** A higher $CR$ means more payload bits per Hz (more TV channels per transponder) but requires a higher satellite EIRP or a larger dish at the receiver to maintain the link margin.

### 3.4 Link-Budget Quick Check (Conceptual)

A typical DVB-S downlink budget (Ku-band, 12 GHz, GEO satellite 36 000 km):

$$
\begin{aligned}
FSL_{dB} &\approx 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(12\,000) + 20\log_{10}(36\,000) \\
         &\approx 32.45 + 81.58 + 111.12 \\
         &\approx 205.15 \ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

The STB's required $C/N$ at the L-band input (after RS) is roughly **5–7 dB** (quasi-error-free threshold). Satellite EIRP + dish gain + LNB G/T must overcome FSL by this margin plus weather/rain-fade reserve (typically **4–6 dB** in tropical regions like Kerala).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete DVB-S Receiver (STB + LNB)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SATELLITE 36 000 km GEO Ku-Band 12 GHz] --> B[Parabolic Dish Antenna]
    B --> C[LNB Low Noise Block Down-Converter 12 GHz to 950 to 2150 MHz L-band]
    C --> D[Tuner + AGC + Channel Selection]
    D --> E[QPSK Demodulator I and Q Baseband]
    E --> F[Matched Filter Root Raised Cosine alpha 0.35]
    F --> G[Inner FEC Viterbi Decoder K=7 punctured]
    G --> H[Outer FEC Reed Solomon RS 204 188 t=8]
    H --> I[MPEG-2 Transport Stream Demux by PID]
    I --> J[Conditional Access Smart Card / Scrambling Removal]
    J --> K[MPEG-2 Video Decoder]
    J --> L[MPEG-2 Audio Decoder AC3 MPEG-1 Layer 2]
    K --> M[Video DAC and HDMI Encoder]
    L --> N[Audio DAC and SPDIF Output]
    M --> O[TELEVISION DISPLAY]
    N --> O
    P[Microcontroller User Interface IR Remote] --> I
    P --> J
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cascade Mapping: Uplink Distortion vs. STB Counter-Measure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph UPLINK_DISTORTION["UPLINK-SIDE DISTORTION (Cause)"]
        U1[AWGN Thermal Noise]
        U2[Rain Fade Attenuation]
        U3[ISI from Bandlimited Channel]
        U4[Random Bit Errors]
        U5[Burst Errors RS Packet Boundary]
        U6[MPEG-2 Compression and Service Multiplex]
    end
    subgraph STB_RECOVERY["DVB-S STB (Effect / Counter-measure)"]
        R1[Tuner selects one transponder]
        R2[LNB provides low noise amplification]
        R3[Matched filter RRC removes ISI]
        R4[QPSK demodulator symbol decisions]
        R5[Reed Solomon decoder corrects bursts up to 8 bytes]
        R6[Transport demux + MPEG-2 A/V decoder]
    end
    U1 --> R2
    U2 --> R1
    U3 --> R3
    U4 --> R4
    U5 --> R5
    U6 --> R6
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Smart-Card / CA Sub-system

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Encrypted MPEG-2 TS from RS Decoder] --> B[Transport Demux]
    B --> C{Service Scrambled?}
    C -- No --> D[Direct to A/V Decoder]
    C -- Yes --> E[CAT and ECM Packets to CA Module]
    E --> F[Smart Card / Embedded Security Chip]
    F --> G[Control Words CW]
    G --> H[DES / AES De-scrambler]
    H --> I[Decrypted TS to A/V Decoder]
    D --> I
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Conditional Access (CA) block is what differentiates a **free-to-air STB** from a **pay-TV STB**. The radio-frequency front-end, demodulator, and FEC blocks are identical in both.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, May 2024]**
*List the major functional blocks of a DVB-S set-top box in the order they appear from the antenna input to the TV output.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Tuner / Front-end** — selects one transponder from the L-band input (1 mark).
2. **QPSK Demodulator** with matched filter (root-raised cosine, α = 0.35) (½ mark).
3. **Inner FEC (Viterbi decoder)** for convolutional code + **Outer FEC (Reed-Solomon RS(204,188))** (½ mark).
4. **MPEG-2 Transport Stream Demultiplexer** — extracts video, audio, and SI by PID (½ mark).
5. **MPEG-2 A/V decoder** + **Conditional Access** module + DAC/HDMI driver (½ mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
*Why is QPSK the chosen modulation for DVB-S instead of 16-QAM or 64-QAM?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Satellite channel is **power-limited** (EIRP-constrained) and highly noisy; QPSK has the **lowest required $E_b/N_0$** for a given BER among the listed schemes (1 mark).
- QPSK is a **constant-envelope** modulation, immune to AM/AM and AM/PM distortion of the satellite's Travelling-Wave Tube Amplifier (TWTA) (1 mark).
- 16-QAM / 64-QAM require ~10–17 dB higher $C/N$ for the same BER, which is impractical with small domestic dishes and the high path loss at 12 GHz (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024, Module 2]** — *With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a DVB-S set-top box. Discuss the role of the tuner, QPSK demodulator, concatenated FEC, and MPEG-2 transport demultiplexer.*

**(a) Block Diagram and Front-End Description — 7 marks**

**Model Solution:**

A DVB-S STB receives a Ku-band (~12 GHz) downlink from a GEO satellite via a parabolic dish. The LNB at the feed-horn performs low-noise amplification and down-conversion to the **L-band (950–2150 MHz)**, which is sent over coaxial cable to the STB (½ mark).

**Tuner (1 mark):** A PLL-synthesized local oscillator mixes the L-band input to a fixed IF (~36 MHz). An AGC loop adjusts gain to keep signal level within the A/D input range. The tuner selects ONE transponder from the ~500 MHz-wide L-band spectrum.

**QPSK Demodulator (2 marks):** The IF is digitized by an A/D converter. Digital down-conversion produces I(t) and Q(t) baseband samples. These are passed through a **root-raised cosine filter (α = 0.35)** for matched filtering and ISI removal. Carrier and clock recovery loops track residual frequency/phase offsets. Symbol decisions are made using soft-decision metrics.

**Concatenated FEC (1.5 marks):**
- **Inner code:** Viterbi-decoded punctured convolutional code (K=7), correcting random errors.
- **Outer code:** Reed-Solomon **RS(204, 188, t=8)** correcting up to 8 byte errors per MPEG-2 packet, suppressing residual burst errors. After both stages, the output is "quasi-error-free" (BER < 10⁻¹¹).

**Transport Demultiplexer (1 mark):** Replaces sync bytes, validates the 188-byte packet structure (sync = 0x47), and filters packets by **Packet Identifier (PID)**. The PAT (PID 0) and PMT tables identify which PIDs carry the video, audio, PCR, and CA messages for the chosen service.

**CA / MPEG-2 A/V Decoder (1 mark):** If the service is scrambled, the demux feeds ECMs to the smart-card; decrypted control words de-scramble the PES packets. MPEG-2 video (MP@ML or MP@HL) and audio (Layer 2 / AC-3) are decoded, converted to analog/digital video (HDMI) and audio (SPDIF).

**(b) Why Concatenated Coding and Roll-off 0.35? — 7 marks**

**Model Solution:**

**Why concatenated coding? (4 marks)**

1. **Complementary error statistics:** A satellite link exhibits two error regimes — *random* errors from AWGN, and *burst* errors from impulse noise and channel non-linearities. A *single* code cannot correct both efficiently. Viterbi (inner) handles random errors at low complexity; Reed-Solomon (outer) is optimal against bursts (½ mark).

2. **Coding gain with low complexity:** A concatenated (½, 7/8) scheme achieves a total coding gain of **~6.5 dB** at the target BER of $2 \times 10^{-4}$ after Viterbi. Implementing a single code of equivalent power (e.g., a long convolutional or turbo code) at the same data rate is far more complex for the STB hardware of the 1990s-2000s era (1 mark).

3. **Near-Shannon performance:** The concatenated pair operates within ~1 dB of the Shannon limit for the AWGN channel — a remarkable result considering its simplicity (½ mark).

4. **MPEG-2 compatibility:** The RS(204, 188) framing aligns exactly with the 188-byte MPEG-2 Transport Stream packet, so no re-framing overhead is needed (1 mark).

5. **BER cliff at QEF threshold:** A characteristic "waterfall" curve — once $C/N$ exceeds the threshold, the post-RS BER drops from $10^{-4}$ to $<10^{-11}$ in less than 1 dB. This is called the **Quasi-Error-Free (QEF)** point and is the design target for the link budget (1 mark).

**Why roll-off factor α = 0.35? (3 marks)**

- The roll-off factor $\alpha$ of the root-raised cosine filter controls the **trade-off between spectral efficiency and ISI** (1 mark).
- $\alpha = 0$ (brick-wall) is ideal in theory but unrealizable — it would require an infinite-length filter (½ mark).
- $\alpha = 0.35$ was chosen in 1994 (DVB-S) as a compromise that achieves ~1.48 bit/s/Hz spectral efficiency while using a practical, finite-length FIR filter of ~30–50 taps (1 mark).
- Compared to $\alpha = 0.25$ (used in DVB-S2), the DVB-S value tolerates slightly more ISI but requires much simpler receiver equalization — appropriate for consumer hardware of the era (½ mark).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024, Module 2]** — *A DVB-S transponder transmits at a symbol rate of 30 Msym/s with 3/4-rate convolutional FEC and QPSK modulation. Calculate the useful bit rate, the occupied bandwidth, and the spectral efficiency. Explain how the convolutional code rate affects the broadcaster's choice of content (SD vs HD) per transponder.*

**(a) Numerical Computation — 7 marks**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $R_s = 30 \times 10^6$ sym/s, $CR = 3/4$, QPSK, $\alpha = 0.35$.

**Useful bit rate (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_u &= 2 \cdot R_s \cdot CR \cdot \frac{188}{204} \\
&= 2 \times 30 \times 10^6 \times \frac{3}{4} \times \frac{188}{204} \\
&= 60 \times 10^6 \times 0.75 \times 0.92157 \\
&= 60 \times 10^6 \times 0.69118 \\
&\approx 41.47 \ \text{Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Occupied bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW &= R_s \cdot (1 + \alpha) \\
&= 30 \times 10^6 \times 1.35 \\
&= 40.5 \ \text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Spectral efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{R_u}{BW} = \frac{41.47 \times 10^6}{40.5 \times 10^6} \\
&\approx 1.024 \ \text{bit/s/Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Or equivalently: $\eta = \dfrac{2 \cdot 188 \cdot CR}{204 \cdot 1.35} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 188 \cdot 0.75}{204 \cdot 1.35} \approx 1.024$)

**(b) Effect of CR on Content Choice — 7 marks**

**Model Solution:**

The convolutional code rate $CR$ is the single most flexible trade-off knob a DVB-S operator has (1 mark).

**At low CR (e.g., 1/2)** — *Robust, low-bitrate mode*:
- Useful bit rate drops: $R_u = 2 R_s \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.922 \approx 0.922\,R_s$.
- Required $E_b/N_0$ drops to ~4.5 dB → more noise-tolerant, smaller dish acceptable, better rain-fade resilience (2 marks).
- Suitable for: **rural coverage, heavy-rain regions (Kerala monsoon), and low-bandwidth services** (e.g., radio, SD MPEG-2 at 2–3 Mbps per channel) (1 mark).

**At high CR (e.g., 7/8)** — *High-bitrate, fragile mode*:
- Useful bit rate peaks: $R_u \approx 1.29\,R_s$, ~40% more than CR = 1/2.
- Required $E_b/N_0$ rises to ~6.4 dB → requires larger dish, higher satellite EIRP, smaller rain margin (1 mark).
- Suitable for: **HD MPEG-2 channels (15–20 Mbps each) packed densely** in C-/Ku-band transponders, common in European DTH (1 mark).

**Engineering trade-off curve (1 mark):**

| $CR$ | $R_u$ (Mbps at $R_s=30$ Msym/s) | Service Capacity (SD at 4 Mbps) | Service Capacity (HD at 15 Mbps) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1/2 | 27.65 | 6 SD | 1 HD |
| 3/4 | 41.47 | 10 SD | 2 HD |
| 7/8 | 48.36 | 12 SD | 3 HD |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Operators dynamically select $CR$ per transponder to balance **bitrate (revenue per MHz)** against **link margin (service availability %)**. DVB-S2 replaced this fixed-rate scheme with adaptive coding and modulation (ACM) and 8PSK/16APSK, allowing per-burst optimization.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Mark-Loss Hotspots**
> 1. **Confusing the LNB with the tuner** — the LNB is at the *dish*, not in the STB (loses 1 mark if mixed up).
> 2. **Forgetting the RS 188/204 factor** in the bit-rate formula — easy to write $R_u = 2R_s CR$ and lose 2–3 marks on the 14-mark numerical question.
> 3. **Writing "Viterbi corrects burst errors"** — the *opposite* is true. Viterbi corrects *random* errors; RS corrects *bursts*. This is a classic 1-mark trap.
> 4. **Mixing up DVB-S with DVB-T** — DVB-T uses **COFDM** with hundreds of carriers, not a single QPSK carrier. Don't transfer terminology across standards.
> 5. **Omitting the CA block** in the diagram — for pay-TV questions (Tata Sky, Airtel), the smart-card + descrambler block is mandatory for full marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DVB-S = Satellite.** Single-carrier QPSK, α = 0.35, RS(204,188) + convolutional FEC. → Always remember this triplet.
- **LNB ≠ STB.** LNB is at the dish; STB begins at the L-band input.
- **Bit-rate formula (memorize):** $R_u = 2 R_s \cdot CR \cdot \dfrac{188}{204}$ — the 188/204 is the RS overhead and is **non-negotiable**.
- **Bandwidth:** $BW = 1.35\,R_s$.
- **QPSK choice = constant envelope, power-efficient** for the noise-limited satellite link. (NOT bandwidth-efficient — that's why DVB-S2 introduced 8PSK/16APSK.)
- **Concatenated FEC = random-error code (Viterbi) + burst-error code (RS).** Viterbi is *inner*; RS is *outer*.
- **QEF threshold:** Post-RS BER < 10⁻¹¹ at the waterfall edge — this is what the link budget is designed for.
- **Transport Stream basics:** 188-byte packets, sync byte **0x47**, demux by **PID**, PAT/PMT for service discovery.
- **Conditional Access (CA):** Required for pay-DTH; uses ECMs and control words. Free-to-air STBs omit the smart-card block.
- **Outputs of a modern STB:** HDMI (digital), component YPbPr, composite CVBS (legacy), SPDIF/coaxial audio.
- **KTU "Must Mention" Terms for Full Marks:** tuner, AGC, QPSK, root-raised cosine, Viterbi, Reed-Solomon, MPEG-2 TS, PID, PAT/PMT, CA, smart card, HDMI.
- **One-line exam answer:** *"A DVB-S STB is a receiver that selects a QPSK-modulated, concatenated-FEC-protected MPEG-2 transport stream from the L-band downlink and decodes it into baseband audio-video for television display."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DVB-S2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DVB-S2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (ETSI EN 302 307)

**DVB-S2** (Digital Video Broadcasting — Satellite — Second Generation) is the second-generation ETSI-standardized (EN 302 307, ratified in 2005) digital satellite broadcasting system, designed to deliver SD, HDTV, UHDTV, 3D-TV, and broadband interactive services over a single satellite transponder. It supersedes the legacy DVB-S system by combining a near-Shannon-limit **LDPC (Low-Density Parity-Check)** forward error correction code concatenated with a **BCH (Bose–Chaudhuri–Hocquenghem)** outer code, together with a flexible set of constellations: **QPSK, 8PSK, 16APSK, and 32APSK**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** DVB-S2 is a high-weight topic. Examiners expect students to know (i) the functional block diagram, (ii) the FEC architecture (LDPC + BCH), (iii) the four modulation constellations and when each is preferred, (iv) ACM/VCM operation, and (v) the GSE protocol.

## Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Satellite Superhighway"

Imagine the original **DVB-S** as a **single-lane country road** built in 1995. Trucks (video streams) could travel, but only one at a time, slowly, and with frequent breakdowns (errors) in bad weather. Now think of **DVB-S2** as a **multi-lane, weather-adaptive smart superhighway** that:

- Uses different types of trucks for different terrains (QPSK = small robust truck, 32APSK = large fast truck)
- Has a state-of-the-art error-recovery crew (LDPC + BCH) that repairs damaged cargo on the fly
- Adapts lane use to traffic density and weather using **ACM (Adaptive Coding & Modulation)**
- Allows a single highway to simultaneously carry TV, radio, and internet data using **GSE (Generic Stream Encapsulation)**

This "smart highway" achieves **30 % to 70 % higher spectral efficiency** than DVB-S over the same transponder bandwidth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics of DVB-S2:**
> - **Spectral efficiency:** Up to **4.5 bit/s/Hz** (with 32APSK, R = 9/10, roll-off 0.05)
> - **Quasi-Error-Free (QEF) operating point:** BER $\leq 10^{-11}$ after FEC
> - **C/N range covered:** From $-2.4$ dB (QPSK 1/4) to $+16$ dB (32APSK 9/10)
> - **Standard reference:** ETSI EN 302 307-1 (DVBS2), EN 302 307-2 (S2 Extensions)

## Applications in Entertainment Electronics

| Application | Typical Mode | Why DVB-S2 is used |
|---|---|---|
| **DTH TV** (Direct-to-Home) | QPSK / 8PSK | Robust to rain fade, low-cost receivers |
| **HDTV broadcasting** | 8PSK / 16APSK | High throughput per transponder |
| **DSNG (Digital Satellite News Gathering)** | 8PSK / 16APSK | Higher bitrate for live uplink |
| **Broadband internet (VSAT)** | 16APSK / 32APSK with ACM | Per-terminal link adaptation |
| **Contribution feeds** | 16APSK / 32APSK | Studio-to-studio HD/4K transport |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DVB-S2 System Philosophy

The design of DVB-S2 is built on three foundational engineering goals, all of which the KTU examiner loves to test:

1. **Capacity Maximization** — Push the spectral efficiency as close as possible to the Shannon limit by using a powerful FEC (LDPC at near-capacity performance).
2. **Total Flexibility** — One standard covers everything from low-bitrate mobile reception to high-bitrate contribution links by selecting the right combination of (modulation, code rate).
3. **Total Compatibility** — The optional **backwards-compatible (DVB-S2 mode)** allows legacy DVB-S receivers to demodulate a subset of the DVB-S2 carriers during transition periods.

## 2.2 Operational Workflow (Six Functional Blocks)

The DVB-S2 transmitter is a strict sequential pipeline. Understanding each block is essential for KTU long-answer questions.

**Step 1 — Mode Adaptation (Input Stream Matching)**
The input can be one of three types:
- **Transport Stream (TS) packets** of 188 bytes (with optional 16-byte Reed-Solomon outer coding for ACM, called **"dummy frame" insertion**)
- **Generic Stream (GS)** — packets of fixed length from GSE
- **Generic Continuous Stream (GCS)** — a continuous bitstream

The interface is synchronized, null-packets are removed, and a CRC-8 is appended.

**Step 2 — Stream Adaptation**
Padding is applied to form a baseband frame of constant length. A baseband header (BBHEADER, 10 bytes) is inserted, which signals the input stream type, length, and synchronization bits.

**Step 3 — FEC Encoding (LDPC + BCH)**
The baseband frame (K bits info + parity = n bits total) is encoded:
- **BCH outer code:** corrects the "error floor" inherent to LDPC decoders
- **LDPC inner code:** the powerful near-Shannon code

The output FECFRAME is always **64 800 bits** (normal) or **16 200 bits** (short, for low-latency DSNG). The code rate R = K/n determines the protection level.

**Step 4 — Bit Interleaving (only for 8PSK, 16APSK, 32APSK)**
A block interleaver spreads the bits so that the constellation demapper sees independent bits on each dimension (a key reason DVB-S2 uses **APSK** instead of **QAM** for the higher constellations — APSK is more robust to non-linear amplifiers).

**Step 5 — Constellation Mapping**
Maps $m = \log_2(M)$ bits to one of $M$ complex symbols:
- $M=4$ (QPSK) — used in rain-fade regions
- $M=8$ (8PSK) — the workhorse of DTH
- $M=16$ (16APSK) — for moderate non-linearity
- $M=32$ (32APSK) — for quasi-linear satellites and high bitrates

**Step 6 — Physical Layer Framing + Baseband Filtering**
A **PLHEADER (90 symbols)** is prepended for synchronization (SOF + pilots + optional dummy). Then the stream is **square-root-raised-cosine (SRRC)** pulse-shaped with a roll-off factor $\alpha \in \{0.35,\ 0.25,\ 0.20\}$ (DVB-S2X allows 0.05 and 0.10).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shannon Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1+S/N)$ | bit/s | $B$ = bandwidth, $S/N$ = signal-to-noise power ratio |
| Symbol rate | $R_s = B / (1+\alpha)$ | Bd (Baud) | $\alpha$ = roll-off factor |
| Useful bit rate | $R_b = R_s \cdot m \cdot R_c$ | bit/s | $m = \log_2(M)$, $R_c$ = FEC code rate |
| Spectral efficiency | $\eta = R_b / B = m \cdot R_c / (1+\alpha)$ | bit/s/Hz | Compare with Shannon limit for given $E_b/N_0$ |
| $E_b/N_0$ to $C/N$ conversion | $C/N = (E_b/N_0) \cdot (R_b/B)$ | dB | $C/N$ (or $C/N_0$) is the quantity in link budgets |
| Free-space path loss | $L = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi d f / c)$ | dB | $d$ in metres, $f$ in Hz |
| Link budget C/N | $C/N = EIRP - L + G/T - k - B$ | dB | $k$ = Boltzmann const $(-228.6\ \text{dBW/K/Hz})$ |
| Shannon spectral efficiency | $\eta_{\max} = \log_2(1 + C/N)$ | bit/s/Hz | Practical DVB-S2 reaches within $\sim 0.7$ dB |
| $C/N_0$ from EIRP | $C/N_0 = EIRP - L + G/T - k$ | dB·Hz | Independent of bandwidth |
| DVB-S2 required $C/N_0$ | See table (QPSK 1/2 $\approx 84.6$ dB·Hz; 32APSK 9/10 $\approx 110$ dB·Hz) | dB·Hz | Defined for QEF BER = $10^{-11}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** The single most-tested calculation is the **useful bit rate** and the **spectral efficiency** from given $B,\ \alpha,\ M,\ R_c$. Memorize $R_b = B \cdot m \cdot R_c / (1+\alpha)$.

## 2.4 Why APSK and not QAM?

For satellite channels, the travelling-wave tube amplifier (TWTA) operates near saturation, which is **non-linear**. QAM constellations (e.g., 16-QAM, 32-QAM) require **linear** amplification — they suffer from severe AM/PM conversion distortion in a non-linear HPA.

APSK (Amplitude-Phase Shift Keying) places symbols on **concentric rings** (e.g., 32APSK = 4 + 12 + 16 points on 3 rings), so the amplitude levels are fewer. This gives APSK **inherent robustness against non-linearity**, which is why DVB-S2 uses 16APSK/32APSK for the higher throughputs.

## 2.5 ACM and VCM — Adaptive Operation

- **VCM (Variable Coding & Modulation):** Different carriers in the same satellite frame use different (modulation, code rate) pairs to serve different receivers (e.g., a small DTH dish in heavy rain vs. a large professional dish in clear sky).
- **ACM (Adaptive Coding & Modulation):** VCM applied **per-user, in real time, on the return channel**. The hub polls each VSAT's $C/N_0$ and assigns the most efficient (modulation, code rate) the link can sustain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** DVB-S2 ACM is the engine behind modern satellite broadband systems like **Hellas Sat, Hughes Jupiter, ViaSat-2, and SES O3b mPOWER** — it multiplies the satellite's total throughput by 2-3× compared to fixed-FEC DVB-S.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All algebraic steps are shown explicitly. No step is skipped. Multi-line equations use the `aligned` environment inside a single `$$` block.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Useful Bit Rate from DVB-S2 Parameters

We start from the Nyquist rate, which states that for a band-limited channel of bandwidth $B$ and a roll-off factor $\alpha$, the achievable symbol rate is:

$$
R_s = \frac{B}{1+\alpha}
$$

**Step 1:** Each constellation symbol carries $m$ bits, where $m = \log_2(M)$ and $M$ is the number of constellation points.

$$
\text{Bits per symbol} = m = \log_2 M
$$

**Step 2:** The FEC encoder (LDPC + BCH) replaces every $K$ information bits with $n = K/R_c$ transmitted bits, so the code rate is:

$$
R_c = \frac{K}{n}
$$

**Step 3:** Combining the symbol rate, the bits per symbol, and the code rate, the useful (information) bit rate is:

$$
R_b = R_s \cdot m \cdot R_c
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $R_s$ from Step 1:

$$
R_b = \frac{B}{1+\alpha} \cdot m \cdot R_c = \frac{B \cdot m \cdot R_c}{1+\alpha}
$$

**Step 5:** Normalizing by $B$ gives the **spectral efficiency** $\eta$:

$$
\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} = \frac{m \cdot R_c}{1+\alpha}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** $\eta$ tells you how many information bits are squeezed into each Hz of satellite bandwidth. For a 32APSK constellation with $m=5$, $R_c=9/10$ and $\alpha=0.20$:

$$
\eta = \frac{5 \cdot 0.90}{1.20} = \frac{4.50}{1.20} = 3.75\ \text{bit/s/Hz}
$$

This matches the order-of-magnitude performance quoted in ETSI EN 302 307.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Numerical Worked Example (Kerala Exam Standard)

**Problem:** A DVB-S2 carrier occupies **36 MHz** of transponder bandwidth with a roll-off factor $\alpha = 0.25$. The modulation is **8PSK** and the LDPC code rate is $R_c = 2/3$. Calculate (a) the symbol rate, (b) the useful bit rate, and (c) the spectral efficiency.

**Part (a) — Symbol rate:**

$$
R_s = \frac{B}{1+\alpha} = \frac{36 \times 10^{6}}{1.25} = 28.8 \times 10^{6}\ \text{symbols/s} = 28.8\ \text{MBaud}
$$

**Part (b) — Useful bit rate:**

For 8PSK, $m = \log_2(8) = 3$ bits per symbol.

$$
R_b = R_s \cdot m \cdot R_c = 28.8 \times 10^{6} \times 3 \times \frac{2}{3}
$$

$$
R_b = 28.8 \times 10^{6} \times 2 = 57.6 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bit/s} = 57.6\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**Part (c) — Spectral efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} = \frac{57.6 \times 10^{6}}{36 \times 10^{6}} = 1.6\ \text{bit/s/Hz}
$$

**Verification by the alternative formula:**

$$
\eta = \frac{m \cdot R_c}{1+\alpha} = \frac{3 \cdot (2/3)}{1.25} = \frac{2}{1.25} = 1.6\ \text{bit/s/Hz} \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Satellite Link Budget (EIRP Determination)

The full uplink/downlink C/N for a satellite transponder is given by:

$$
\frac{C}{N_0} = EIRP_{\text{station}} - L_{\text{path}} + \left(\frac{G}{T}\right)_{\text{station}} - k
$$

**Step 1 — Free-space path loss** (transmitting from Earth to geostationary satellite at $d = 38\,000$ km, $f = 12$ GHz Ku-band):

$$
L = 20\log_{10}(4\pi d f / c)
$$

$$
L = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi \times 38\times 10^{6} \times 12\times 10^{9} / 3\times 10^{8})
$$

$$
L = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi \times 1520 \times 10^{6} / 3 \times 10^{8})\ \text{(approx, dimensionless ratio)}
$$

For Ku-band GEO, the standard value is **$L \approx 205$ dB**.

**Step 2 — Required $EIRP$:** Suppose we need $C/N_0 = 95$ dB·Hz for a QPSK 2/3 link. With a $G/T = 20$ dB/K receive station:

$$
EIRP = C/N_0 + L - (G/T) + k
$$

$$
EIRP = 95 + 205 - 20 - 228.6 = 51.4\ \text{dBW}
$$

**Step 3 — Translate to dish power:** If the dish has gain $G_t = 45$ dBi, then the required transmitter power is:

$$
P_t = EIRP - G_t = 51.4 - 45 = 6.4\ \text{dBW} \approx 4.4\ \text{W}
$$

This is why home DVB-S2 reception is so easy to deploy — only a few watts from a transponder are needed to cover an entire continent.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Required C/N for DVB-S2 (QEF, $\alpha = 0.20$)

This is a **must-memorize** mini-table for the KTU exam. Values are taken from the ETSI specification at the **Quasi-Error-Free (QEF)** operating point of $BER \le 10^{-11}$ after LDPC decoding.

| Mode | $R_c$ | Spectral efficiency $\eta$ (bit/s/Hz) | Ideal $E_b/N_0$ (dB) | Required $C/N$ (dB) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| QPSK 1/4 | 1/4 | 0.36 | $-0.3$ | $-2.4$ |
| QPSK 1/2 | 1/2 | 0.72 | $1.0$ | $0.6$ |
| QPSK 2/3 | 2/3 | 0.96 | $2.1$ | $2.7$ |
| QPSK 3/4 | 3/4 | 1.08 | $2.7$ | $3.6$ |
| 8PSK 2/3 | 2/3 | 1.45 | $4.5$ | $5.5$ |
| 8PSK 3/4 | 3/4 | 1.62 | $5.4$ | $6.6$ |
| 16APSK 3/4 | 3/4 | 2.16 | $7.0$ | $8.3$ |
| 32APSK 3/4 | 3/4 | 2.71 | $9.6$ | $10.9$ |
| 32APSK 9/10 | 9/10 | 3.25 | $11.2$ | $12.7$ |

**Derivation of $C/N$ from $E_b/N_0$:** Starting from $E_b/N_0 = (C/N)\cdot(B/R_b) = (C/N) \cdot (1+\alpha)/(m R_c)$:

$$
\frac{C}{N} = \frac{E_b}{N_0} + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{m \cdot R_c}{1+\alpha}\right)
$$

For QPSK 1/2, $m=2,\ R_c=0.5,\ \alpha=0.20$:

$$
\frac{C}{N} = 1.0 + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2 \cdot 0.5}{1.2}\right) = 1.0 + 10 \log_{10}(0.833) = 1.0 - 0.79 = 0.21\ \text{dB}
$$

The actual DVB-S2 implementation margin (0.4 to 0.8 dB away from Shannon) gives the spec value of **0.6 dB**, as listed in the table.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DVB-S2 Transmitter — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source TS or GSE Stream] --> modeAd[Mode Adaptation]
    modeAd --> streamAd[Stream Adaptation]
    streamAd --> bch[BCH Outer Encoding]
    bch --> ldpc[LDPC Inner Encoding]
    ldpc --> interleaver[Bit Interleaver]
    interleaver --> mapper[Constellation Mapper]
    mapper --> phyFrame[Physical Layer Framing]
    phyFrame --> pulseShaper[SRRC Pulse Shaper]
    pulseShaper --> dac[DAC]
    dac --> upConv[RF Up Conversion]
    upConv --> hpa[High Power Amplifier]
    hpa --> ant[Satellite Dish Antenna]
    ant -.-> sat[Geostationary Satellite Transponder]
```

## 4.2 DVB-S2 Receiver — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lnb[LNB Down Conversion] --> agc[AGC and ADC]
    agc --> sync[Carrier and Timing Sync]
    sync --> matchedFilt[Matched SRRC Filter]
    matchedFilt --> deint[Bit De-interleaver]
    deint --> demap[Constellation De-mapper]
    demap --> ldpcDec[LDPC Decoder]
    ldpcDec --> bchDec[BCH Decoder]
    bchDec --> streamAdRx[Stream Adaptation RX]
    streamAdRx --> modeAdRx[Mode Adaptation RX]
    modeAdRx --> tsOut[Output TS or GSE Stream]
```

## 4.3 DVB-S2 Baseband Frame Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph bbFrame[DVB-S2 Baseband Frame 64 800 bits]
        bbHeader[BBHEADER 10 bytes]
        dataPayload[User Data 64 746 bits]
        padding[Padding to match FECFRAME]
        bchBlock[BCH Parity]
        ldpcBlock[LDPC Parity]
    end
    bbHeader --> dataPayload
    dataPayload --> padding
    padding --> bchBlock
    bchBlock --> ldpcBlock
```

## 4.4 Adaptive Coding and Modulation Decision Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hub[Hub Gateway Station] -->|Polls beacon or measures SINR| vsat1[VSAT Terminal A]
    hub -->|Polls beacon or measures SINR| vsat2[VSAT Terminal B]
    vsat1 -->|Reports EsNo| hub
    vsat2 -->|Reports EsNo| hub
    hub -->|Assigns MODCOD 8PSK 2 over 3| vsat1
    hub -->|Assigns MODCOD QPSK 1 over 2| vsat2
    vsat1 -->|High bitrate burst| sat[Satellite]
    vsat2 -->|Robust low bitrate burst| sat
    sat -->|DVB-S2 forward carriers| vsat1
    sat -->|DVB-S2 forward carriers| vsat2
```

## 4.5 DVB-S2 vs DVB-S — Side-by-Side Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph legacy[DVB-S Legacy]
        a1[Reed Solomon RS 188 over 204] --> a2[Convolutional Punctured Coding]
        a2 --> a3[QPSK only]
    end
    subgraph modern[DVB-S2 Modern]
        b1[BCH LDPC Concatenation] --> b2[Adaptive Bit Interleaving]
        b2 --> b3[QPSK 8PSK 16APSK 32APSK]
        b3 --> b4[ACM VCM GSE]
    end
    legacy -.deprecated.-> modern
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** When the question says "explain the DVB-S2 transmitter", draw these blocks **left to right** in the exact order: Mode Adaptation → Stream Adaptation → FEC (BCH + LDPC) → Bit Interleaver → Mapper → PL Framing → Pulse Shaping → RF up-conversion. Examiners award marks for **order**, not just for listing names.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 1]

**List any three differences between DVB-S and DVB-S2.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **FEC scheme:** DVB-S uses Reed–Solomon (188/204) + convolutional punctured coding (Viterbi decoded), giving an operational gap of $\sim 3$ dB to Shannon. DVB-S2 uses **BCH + LDPC**, achieving a gap of $\sim 0.7$ dB to Shannon.
2. **Modulation:** DVB-S supports only **QPSK**; DVB-S2 supports **QPSK, 8PSK, 16APSK, and 32APSK**, giving far higher spectral efficiency.
3. **Adaptivity:** DVB-S is a fixed MODCOD system; DVB-S2 supports **VCM (Variable Coding & Modulation)** and **ACM (Adaptive Coding & Modulation)** for per-link optimization.
4. **Input streams:** DVB-S accepts only MPEG-TS; DVB-S2 accepts TS, Generic Streams and **GSE (Generic Stream Encapsulation)** packets.
5. **Roll-off factor:** DVB-S uses 0.35; DVB-S2 also uses 0.35/0.25/0.20, with DVB-S2X going down to 0.05.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**What is the role of the LDPC + BCH concatenation in DVB-S2?**

**Model Answer:**

The **LDPC inner code** is a powerful code that operates within $\sim 0.7$ dB of the Shannon limit. However, at very low BER (e.g., $10^{-11}$), LDPC decoders exhibit a residual **"error floor"**. The **BCH outer code** is a small, high-rate, hard-decision block code whose sole job is to **push this error floor below the QEF (Quasi-Error-Free) threshold of one uncorrected error per hour of transmission**. The combination gives a system that is both **capacity-approaching** (LDPC) and **error-floor-free** (BCH).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Note:** In KTU ESE, the module question always offers two optional sub-questions. Both are shown below. **Answer ANY ONE** of the two 14-mark questions.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Modified]

**Q. (a)** Draw and explain the functional block diagram of a DVB-S2 transmitter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The DVB-S2 transmitter consists of the following sequential blocks (each is a distinct sub-part worth partial marks):

1. **Mode Adaptation (1 mark):** Accepts input — MPEG-TS packets, Generic Streams (GSE), or continuous bitstreams. Performs input stream synchronization, removal of null packets, and appends an 8-bit CRC. For ACM operation, dummy frames are inserted in place of packets whose transmission is momentarily suppressed.

2. **Stream Adaptation (1 mark):** Splits the bitstream into **FEC frames** of fixed length (64 800 bits for normal, 16 200 bits for short). A 10-byte **BBHEADER** is prepended carrying the input stream format, length, and sync bits. Padding bits are appended to ensure the input to FEC encoding is of constant length.

3. **FEC Encoding — BCH + LDPC (2 marks):** BCH outer code adds ~192 to 432 parity bits (depends on code rate) to correct the "error floor". LDPC inner code appends parity bits to form an **n = 64 800-bit FECFRAME**. The code rate $R_c$ is selectable among 11 values from 1/4 to 9/10.

4. **Bit Interleaving (1 mark):** Block interleaver that operates only for 8PSK, 16APSK, 32APSK. Spreads bit positions so each constellation dimension receives a stream with de-correlated bits, helping the LDPC decoder's iterative process.

5. **Constellation Mapping (1 mark):** Maps the bit stream to symbols of size $M = 2^m$, where $m \in \{2, 3, 4, 5\}$ giving QPSK, 8PSK, 16APSK, 32APSK. **APSK is preferred over QAM** for the larger $M$ because of its robustness to the **non-linearity** of satellite HPA.

6. **Physical Layer Framing (0.5 mark):** Prepends a **PLHEADER (90 symbols)** consisting of Start-of-Frame (SOF), pilot symbols, and an optional dummy section.

7. **Baseband Filtering + RF Up-conversion (0.5 mark):** Square-root-raised-cosine (SRRC) pulse shaping with roll-off $\alpha \in \{0.35, 0.25, 0.20\}$ minimizes inter-symbol interference. The baseband is then up-converted to the Ku/Ka/Ku-band RF carrier, amplified by the HPA, and radiated through the dish.

> KTU diagram should be drawn in the **left-to-right** direction (Section 4.1 of these notes).

**Q. (b)** Discuss the four modulation schemes of DVB-S2 and the principle behind choosing APSK for higher-order constellations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The four constellations are:

1. **QPSK (M=4, m=2 bits/symbol):** Two orthogonal BPSK signals; constant envelope. Most **robust** to noise and non-linearity. Used in poor link conditions, rain-fade regions, and small DTH terminals. **Spectral efficiency** at the highest code rate (9/10) is:

$$
\eta = \frac{2 \cdot 0.9}{1.2} = 1.50\ \text{bit/s/Hz}\quad (\text{with } \alpha=0.20)
$$

2. **8PSK (M=8, m=3 bits/symbol):** Phase-only modulation, constant envelope. Workhorse of DTH HDTV. The best trade-off between robustness and throughput:

$$
\eta = \frac{3 \cdot 0.9}{1.2} = 2.25\ \text{bit/s/Hz}\quad (\text{at } R_c=9/10)
$$

3. **16APSK (M=16, m=4 bits/symbol):** Two concentric rings — 4 inner points, 12 outer points. **Not constant envelope.** Requires better linearity from the HPA but offers much higher throughput:

$$
\eta = \frac{4 \cdot 0.9}{1.2} = 3.00\ \text{bit/s/Hz}
$$

4. **32APSK (M=32, m=5 bits/symbol):** Three concentric rings (4+12+16). Highest spectral efficiency of DVB-S2:

$$
\eta = \frac{5 \cdot 0.9}{1.2} = 3.75\ \text{bit/s/Hz}
$$

5. **Why APSK, not QAM? (3 marks):** The satellite's transponder TWTA (or SSPA) operates **near saturation** for power efficiency. In saturation, the amplifier introduces **non-linear AM/AM and AM/PM conversion** (the amplitude and phase distortions). **QAM** (with its densely packed, multi-level amplitude points) is highly sensitive to these non-linearities — the points on the corner of the QAM grid become smeared. **APSK** distributes its points on concentric circles — only 2 (for 16APSK) or 3 (for 32APSK) amplitude levels exist. This makes APSK **inherently tolerant of non-linearity**, preserving a low post-HPA EVM. Additionally, the **outer ring is given more points** (12 of 16 for 16APSK, 16 of 32 for 32APSK) to maximize Euclidean distance against the dominant non-linear distortion axis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q. A part b):**
> Students routinely lose 1–2 marks for **two reasons**:
> 1. They list the four constellations but **forget to give the bit-per-symbol value** $m = \log_2 M$.
> 2. They write that "APSK is better than QAM" **without explaining the non-linearity argument**. A full sentence referencing TWTA saturation, AM/PM conversion, and Euclidean distance is needed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]

**Q. (a)** Derive the spectral efficiency and useful bit rate of a DVB-S2 system using the parameters $B = 30$ MHz, $\alpha = 0.20$, modulation 8PSK, and code rate $R_c = 3/4$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.** We are given: $B = 30\ \text{MHz},\ \alpha = 0.20,\ M = 8\ (\text{so}\ m = \log_2 8 = 3\ \text{bits/symbol}),\ R_c = 3/4 = 0.75$.

**Step 2 — Symbol rate** $R_s$:

$$
R_s = \frac{B}{1+\alpha} = \frac{30 \times 10^6}{1.20} = 25 \times 10^6\ \text{symbols/s} = 25\ \text{MBaud}
$$

**Step 3 — Useful bit rate** $R_b$:

$$
R_b = R_s \cdot m \cdot R_c = 25 \times 10^6 \times 3 \times 0.75
$$

$$
R_b = 25 \times 10^6 \times 2.25 = 56.25\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Spectral efficiency** $\eta$:

$$
\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} = \frac{56.25 \times 10^6}{30 \times 10^6} = 1.875\ \text{bit/s/Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using direct formula:**

$$
\eta = \frac{m \cdot R_c}{1+\alpha} = \frac{3 \cdot 0.75}{1.20} = \frac{2.25}{1.20} = 1.875\ \text{bit/s/Hz} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** Compared to a DVB-S link on the same 30 MHz transponder using QPSK 3/4 ($\eta \approx 1.08$ bit/s/Hz), the DVB-S2 link delivers **$\sim 1.74$ times more bits per Hz**, a 74 % capacity gain — a typical result for moving from DVB-S to DVB-S2.

**Q. (b)** Explain Adaptive Coding and Modulation (ACM) in DVB-S2. How does it improve the overall satellite network throughput? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **What is ACM? (1 mark):** ACM is the **per-terminal, per-frame, real-time** adjustment of the (modulation, code rate) pair, also called **MODCOD**, in the return link (and, in two-way systems, also in the forward link) of a satellite network, based on the current propagation conditions of each user.

2. **Operation (3 marks):**
   - Each VSAT continuously estimates its **received $C/N_0$** (e.g., from a beacon pilot or from the DVB-S2 frame's pilots).
   - It reports this value to the **hub** via a low-rate return channel.
   - The hub consults a **lookup table of (modulation, code rate) pairs** mapped to minimum required $C/N_0$ values.
   - The hub then assigns the **highest-throughput MODCOD** the link can sustain with a target **QEF BER** margin. Typical examples: clear-sky 32APSK 9/10; light rain 16APSK 3/4; heavy rain QPSK 1/4.
   - Re-allocation happens within **tens of milliseconds** to follow rain-cell drift, antenna pointing errors, and interference.

3. **Throughput gain (2 marks):** A fixed-MODCOD satellite must be designed for the **worst-case** user (i.e., the highest rain-fade margin), so all users operate at the corresponding low MODCOD — wasting capacity. With ACM, each user operates at the MODCOD their own link can support, so:
   - Clear-sky users get the highest MODCOD (highest $\eta$).
   - Rain-faded users are protected with low-order MODCOD (high robustness).
   - The **network-wide average throughput** rises by a factor of **2 to 3** compared to fixed-MODCOD operation.

4. **Enabling technology (1 mark):** ACM requires (i) a **return channel** (e.g., DVB-RCS2) so the VSAT can report channel state, and (ii) the **ACM field in the DVB-S2 BBHEADER**, which signals the MODCOD of the immediately following frame to the receiver.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q. B part b):**
> - **Confusion alert:** Many students write that ACM is in the **forward** link of DTH TV. It is **not** — DTH uses **VCM**, not ACM, because there is no return path from the TV to the broadcaster. ACM requires a two-way link (typical of VSAT broadband).
