# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Modeled on KTU ESE Part A — 2-mark + 1-mark elaboration pattern.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**Define the DVB-T Modulator. List the major signal processing blocks inside it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **DVB-T Modulator** is the transmitter subsystem that converts an **MPEG-2 Transport Stream (TS)** into a terrestrial RF signal in the **VHF/UHF** bands, per **ETSI EN 300 744**.
>
> **Major blocks (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **MUX adaptation & sync randomization** (PRBS-based)
> 2. **Outer coding** — Reed–Solomon **RS(204, 188, 8)**
> 3. **Outer interleaving** — convolutional, depth 12
> 4. **Inner coding** — punctured convolutional, rates 1/2 to 7/8
> 5. **Inner interleaving** — bit & symbol
> 6. **Constellation mapping** — QPSK / 16-QAM / 64-QAM (hierarchical option)
> 7. **OFDM frame assembly** with **pilots** and **TPS** carriers
> 8. **IFFT** to generate time-domain OFDM symbols
> 9. **Guard interval insertion** (cyclic prefix)
> 10. **DAC, I/Q modulation, up-conversion** to RF
>
> **Purpose (1.5 marks):** Ensures **robustness against multipath fading, impulse noise, and channel errors** while delivering **5–32 Mbps** per RF channel depending on the chosen constellation and code rate.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**What is a Guard Interval in DVB-T? Why is it needed, and what are the four standardized values of $\Delta / T_u$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Guard Interval (GI)** is a **cyclic prefix (CP)** — a copy of the last $\Delta$ portion of the useful OFDM symbol — prepended to each OFDM symbol in the time domain. (1 mark)
>
> **Why needed (1 mark):** The wireless channel exhibits **multipath propagation**: signals arrive via direct, reflected, and scattered paths. Without GI, the delayed echoes of symbol $n$ would spill into symbol $n+1$, causing **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)**. The GI absorbs these echoes because the cyclic convolution becomes a true circular convolution, and the receiver can still apply a single-tap equalizer per sub-carrier.
>
> **Four standardized fractions (1 mark):**
>
> | $\Delta / T_u$ | Power Loss (dB) | Use Case |
> |---|---|---|
> | **1/4** | 0.97 dB | Long SFN, robust |
> | **1/8** | 0.51 dB | Medium SFN |
> | **1/16** | 0.26 dB | Short SFN, high capacity |
> | **1/32** | 0.13 dB | Single TX, max throughput |

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a DVB-T transmitter modulator. Discuss the role of OFDM, pilots, and TPS carriers in the system.**

#### (a) Functional Architecture of the DVB-T Modulator (7 marks)

**Step-by-step model solution:**

**(i) MPEG-2 TS Input & Sync Randomization (1 mark)**
- The MPEG-2 TS provides packets of **188 bytes** (1 sync byte `0x47` + 187 payload bytes).
- A **PRBS of length $2^{23}-1$** is XORed with the data (sync bytes preserved) to break up long runs of 0s and 1s, ensuring good spectrum shaping and clock recovery at the receiver.

**(ii) Outer Coding — Reed–Solomon RS(204, 188, 8) (1 mark)**
- Adds **16 parity bytes** to every 188-byte packet.
- Corrects up to **8 byte errors** per packet.
- Overhead: **16/188 ≈ 8.5%**.

**(iii) Outer Convolutional Interleaving (0.5 mark)**
- Depth $I = 12$ bytes; distributes burst errors across multiple RS codewords.

**(iv) Inner Coding — Punctured Convolutional Code (1 mark)**
- Mother code: rate **1/2, K=7, generators (171, 133)_octal**.
- Puncturing: **2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8**.
- Soft-decision **Viterbi decoding** at receiver.

**(v) Inner Bit and Symbol Interleaving (0.5 mark)**
- Bit interleaving is required for **non-uniform 16-QAM and 64-QAM** to map more significant bits onto more protected sub-carriers.

**(vi) Constellation Mapping (1 mark)**
- **QPSK** (2 bits/symbol), **16-QAM** (4 bits/symbol), **64-QAM** (6 bits/symbol).
- Optional **hierarchical mode** with $\alpha = 1, 2, 4$.

**(vii) OFDM Frame Assembly, IFFT, and Guard Interval (1 mark)**
- IFFT size: **2048 (2K)** or **8192 (8K)**.
- GI appended: **1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32** of $T_u$.

#### (b) Role of OFDM, Pilots, and TPS (7 marks)

**(i) Why OFDM? (2.5 marks)**
- **Multipath robustness:** Long symbol duration + GI = absorption of echoes up to GI length without ISI.
- **Frequency-selective fading:** Each sub-carrier sees a **flat fade**; the receiver uses **1-tap equalization** per sub-carrier.
- **Spectrum efficiency:** Sub-carriers are **orthogonal** and overlapping in frequency — maximum spectral efficiency.
- **SFN support:** Guard interval longer than the maximum differential delay allows multiple transmitters on the same frequency.

**(ii) Pilots — Continuous Pilots (CP) and Scattered Pilots (SP) (2.5 marks)**
- **Continuous Pilots:** Fixed sub-carrier positions in every symbol — used for **synchronization, frequency-offset estimation, and phase tracking**.
- **Scattered Pilots:** Distributed across time-frequency grid — used for **channel estimation and equalization** (every 12th sub-carrier typically, in a rotating pattern).
- **Boosted amplitude:** Pilots carry **BPSK**-modulated reference data with **2/3 the power of data carriers** (boost of $1.5 = 1.76$ dB).
- **Total pilot count (8K mode):** 701 carriers per OFDM symbol are dedicated to pilots (CP + SP + TPS).

**(iii) TPS — Transmission Parameter Signaling (2 marks)**
- **TPS carriers** convey the **transmission parameters** to the receiver: modulation type, code rate, GI length, 2K/8K mode, frame number, cell ID.
- **Number of TPS carriers (8K):** 68 per OFDM symbol.
- **TPS is differentially BPSK-modulated** — robust against channel errors.
- **Critical role:** Without correct TPS decoding, the receiver cannot configure its demodulator; this is why TPS is **highly protected** and **boosted in power**.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**A DVB-T system operates in 8K mode with 64-QAM, code rate 2/3, guard interval 1/4, and 8 MHz channel bandwidth. Calculate:**
- **(a)** The useful symbol duration, the total OFDM symbol duration, the sub-carrier spacing, and the number of data sub-carriers. (7 marks)
- **(b)** The maximum transmitter separation possible in a Single-Frequency Network. Also compute the net useful bit-rate after accounting for the Reed–Solomon overhead. (7 marks)

#### (a) Part (a) — Duration and Sub-carrier Parameters (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Mode: **8K** → $K_{\text{used}} = 6817$, IFFT size = 8192
- Channel bandwidth $BW = 8 \text{ MHz}$
- Guard interval fraction: $1/4$ → $\Delta = T_u/4$

**Step 1 — Sub-carrier spacing:**

$$
\Delta f = \frac{BW}{K_{\text{used}}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{6}}{6817} \approx 1173.5 \text{ Hz}
$$

(Using exact standard value: $\Delta f = 1116$ Hz for 8K, 8 MHz. Accept both; standard: 1116 Hz.) (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Useful OFDM symbol duration:**

$$
T_u = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{1116} \approx 8.96 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 896 \text{ µs}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 3 — Guard interval:**

$$
\Delta = \frac{T_u}{4} = \frac{896}{4} = 224 \text{ µs}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 4 — Total OFDM symbol duration:**

$$
T_{\text{sym}} = T_u + \Delta = 896 + 224 = 1120 \text{ µs} = 1.12 \text{ ms}
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 5 — Number of data sub-carriers (8K mode):**

Total used = 6817
- Pilots (CP + SP) = 568 (approximation; standard value ≈ 701)
- TPS = 68
- **Data sub-carriers $K_{\text{data}} = 6048$**

(3 marks, with proper subtraction shown)

#### (b) Part (b) — SFN Distance and Net Bit-Rate (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Maximum SFN transmitter separation:**

The maximum differential path delay must be $\le \Delta = 224$ µs. (1 mark)

Distance:

$$
d_{\max} = c \cdot \Delta = 3 \times 10^{8} \cdot 224 \times 10^{-6} = 67200 \text{ m} \approx 67.2 \text{ km}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 2 — Net useful bit-rate (pre-RS):**

$$
R_{\text{useful}} = \frac{K_{\text{data}} \cdot m \cdot R_c \cdot \eta_g}{T_{\text{sym}}}
$$

where $m = 6$ (64-QAM), $R_c = 2/3$, $\eta_g = T_u / T_{\text{sym}} = 4/5 = 0.8$.

(1 mark)

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{useful}} &= \frac{6048 \times 6 \times \tfrac{2}{3} \times \tfrac{4}{5}}{1120 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{6048 \times 4 \times 0.8}{1.12 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{19353.6}{1.12 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&\approx 17.28 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — Apply RS overhead factor $\frac{188}{204}$:**

$$
R_{\text{net}} = 17.28 \times \frac{188}{204} \approx 17.28 \times 0.9216 \approx 15.93 \text{ Mbps}
$$

(1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **forget to multiply by the RS overhead factor 188/204** at the end. This is a 1-mark loss. Always explicitly state: *"The net user bit-rate equals the gross bit-rate multiplied by 188/204 because RS(204,188) introduces 16 parity bytes per 188 data bytes."*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in DVB-T Modulator Problems (Where KTU Students Lose Marks)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the RS overhead 188/204** — Costs 1 mark on every "compute net bit-rate" problem.
> 2. **Confusing 2K and 8K parameters** — 2K has 1705 used sub-carriers, 8K has 6817. Mixing them gives nonsense answers.
> 3. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing µs / kHz / Mbps units.
> 4. **Stating "OFDM removes multipath"** — Technically incorrect. Correct statement: "OFDM **transforms** a frequency-selective channel into $K$ parallel flat-fading sub-channels, and the **guard interval** prevents inter-OFDM-symbol ISI."
> 5. **Forgetting to mention TPS and pilots in long-answer questions** — A common 1–2 mark loss in Q1(b) and Q2(b).
> 6. **Wrong generator polynomials for convolutional code** — The standard specifies **(171, 133)_octal** (or equivalently, **$G_1 = 1+D+D^2+D^3$, $G_2 = 1+D^2+D^3$** in 7-bit shift-register form). Do not invent other polynomials.
> 7. **Mixing up interleaving depths** — Outer = depth 12, inner = bit/symbol interleaving. Conflating them loses marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — DVB-T Modulator**
>
> ✅ **DVB-T** = Terrestrial DVB, defined by **ETSI EN 300 744**, using **VHF/UHF** bands.
>
> ✅ Input is an **MPEG-2 TS** of **188-byte packets**; modulator outputs an **RF signal** at 6/7/8 MHz bandwidth.
>
> ✅ **Channel coding chain:** Reed–Solomon (outer, RS(204,188,8)) → Outer Interleaver (depth 12) → Punctured Convolutional (inner, rates 1/2 to 7/8) → Inner Interleaver → Mapper.
>
> ✅ **Convolutional code:** Constraint length $K=7$, generators **(171, 133)_octal** — a high-yield exam fact.
>
> ✅ **Constellations:** **QPSK** (2 b/sym), **16-QAM** (4 b/sym), **64-QAM** (6 b/sym); **hierarchical** mode allows SD+HD.
>
> ✅ **OFDM** is implemented via **IFFT** — 2048 points for 2K, 8192 points for 8K.
>
> ✅ **Sub-carriers:** 2K → **1705 used**; 8K → **6817 used**. Data sub-carriers ≈ 1512 (2K) and **6048 (8K)**.
>
> ✅ **Sub-carrier spacing:** 2K → **4464 Hz**, 8K → **1116 Hz** (8 MHz channel).
>
> ✅ **Useful symbol duration:** 2K → **224 µs**, 8K → **896 µs**.
>
> ✅ **Guard Interval fractions:** **1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32** of $T_u$.
>
> ✅ **Power loss from GI:** 0.97 / 0.51 / 0.26 / 0.13 dB respectively.
>
> ✅ **SFN distance:** $d_{\max} = c \cdot \Delta$ — for 8K + 1/4 GI → **~67.2 km**.
>
> ✅ **Pilots:** Boosted 16/9 in amplitude (≈1.76 dB); continuous (sync) + scattered (channel estimation).
>
> ✅ **TPS:** 68 carriers per symbol (8K), carries modulation type, code rate, GI, mode, cell ID — **differentially BPSK**.
>
> ✅ **Net bit-rate formula:** $R = \dfrac{K_{\text{data}} \cdot m \cdot R_c \cdot \eta_g \cdot 188/204}{T_{\text{sym}}}$.
>
> ✅ **8K mode → SFN / fixed reception**; **2K mode → mobile / DVB-H**.
>
> ✅ **GI power penalty** $10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{G+1}{G}\right)$ — memorize all four values.
>
> ✅ **Orthogonality condition:** $\Delta f \cdot T_u = 1$ — the foundation of OFDM.
>
> ✅ **DVB-T2** is the successor (LDPC + 256-QAM + rotated constellations + 32K FFT) — *not* in KTU 2024 Module 2 scope.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DVB-T Carriers and System Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** DVB-T Carriers and System Parameters # DVB-T Carriers and System Parameters ## 1. Core Technical Definition > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (ETSI EN 300 744)** > **DVB-T (Digital Video Broadcasting – Terrestrial)** is the European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI) standard for the broadcast transmission of digital terrestrial television. It employs **Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (COFDM)** with either **2048 (2K mode)** or **8192 (8K mode)** sub-carriers, supporting channel bandwidths of **6 MHz, 7 MHz, and 8 MHz**, and constellation schemes of **QPSK, 16-QAM, and 64-QAM** (with optional hierarchical modulation). ### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multi-Lane Cargo Highway" Imagine a single-lane dirt track where one massive truck carries the entire TV signal. If the truck hits a pothole (multipath reflection from a building), the *whole* signal collapses. Now replace that with a **100-lane superhighway** where the cargo is split into **thousands of tiny cars**, each carrying a small slice of the programme. Even if potholes wreck a few lanes, the receiver ignores the broken cars and reassembles the payload perfectly. That is the essence of DVB-T: instead of one fast serial stream, it transmits **thousands of slow, narrow, mutually orthogonal sub-carriers** in parallel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Orthogonal" in OFDM** means adjacent sub-carriers are mathematically guaranteed *not* to interfere with each other, even though their spectra overlap. The receiver can perfectly separate them because each carrier's peak coincides exactly with every other carrier's null. ### 1.2 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Value / Range | Significance | |---|---|---| | Channel bandwidth | **6, 7, or 8 MHz** | Region-dependent allocation | | FFT size (N_FFT) | **2048 (2K)** or **8192 (8K)** | Number of sub-carriers generated | | Used carriers (K_used) | **1705 (2K)** / **6817 (8K)** | Active data + pilot carriers | | Constellation | QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM | Spectral efficiency trade-off | | Inner FEC rate | 1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8 | Punctured convolutional code | | Outer code | RS(204,188) | Reed-Solomon error protection | | Guard interval ratio (Δ/T_u) | 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32 | Multipath robustness | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis ## 2.1 The Operational Logic of DVB-T The DVB-T standard solves the **multipath fading problem** of terrestrial broadcasting through a carefully engineered combination of three pillars: **multi-carrier transmission, time-domain guard intervals, and concatenated forward error correction**. ### Pillar 1 — Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM) - **Splitting the bandwidth**: A wideband serial bit-stream is divided into N parallel low-rate streams. - **Carrier placement**: Each low-rate stream modulates its own sub-carrier at a precise frequency $f_k = k \cdot \Delta f$. - **Orthogonality condition**: $\Delta f = 1/T_u$ ensures that the spectrum of each carrier has its null exactly at the centre frequency of every other carrier. - **Implementation**: The full OFDM symbol is generated in one shot using an **Inverse Fast Fourier Transform (IFFT)** of size N, followed by cyclic prefix insertion. ### Pillar 2 — Guard Interval (Cyclic Prefix) - A copy of the **last part** of each useful OFDM symbol is prepended to its start. - Duration: $\Delta = G \cdot T_u$, where $G \in \{1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32\}$. - **Purpose**: The receiver only "listens" to the useful part $T_u$, discarding the prefix. Any echo arriving within $\Delta$ appears to be part of the same symbol and does not cause Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI). ### Pillar 3 — Channel Coding - **Outer code**: Reed-Solomon RS(204,188,8) corrects burst errors. - **Inner code**: Punctured convolutional code (constraint length 7) provides configurable rates. - **Interleaving**: Time, bit, and symbol interleavers spread errors across many codewords so that the convolutional decoder sees them as random. ## 2.2 The 2K vs 8K Trade-off > [!IMPORTANT] > **2K mode** is used for **single-frequency networks (SFN) with small cells** (regional transmitters). **8K mode** is used for **large-area SFNs** (national networks) because its longer symbol duration absorbs echoes of greater delay spread. | Aspect | 2K Mode | 8K Mode | |---|---|---| | Maximum echo tolerance | ~ 56 µs (Δ/4 in 8 MHz) | ~ 224 µs (Δ/4 in 8 MHz) | | Carrier spacing | Wide (~ 4.46 kHz) | Narrow (~ 1.12 kHz) | | Doppler sensitivity | Robust at high mobile speeds | Fragile — limited to fixed / pedestrian reception | | Mobility use case | Excellent | Limited | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All exams revolve around these seven equations.** Memorise the relationships, not just the numbers. | # | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_u}$ | Carrier spacing = reciprocal of useful symbol duration | | 2 | $T_u = \dfrac{N_{FFT}}{B_w}$ | Useful symbol duration for given FFT size and channel bandwidth | | 3 | $\Delta = G \cdot T_u$ | Guard interval duration, where $G \in \{1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32\}$ | | 4 | $T_s = T_u + \Delta = T_u(1+G)$ | Total OFDM symbol duration | | 5 | $R_{net} = \dfrac{K_{data} \cdot b \cdot r_{cc}}{T_s}$ | Net useful bit-rate, where $b \in \{2, 4, 6\}$, $r_{cc}$ = convolutional code rate | | 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{R_{net}}{B_w}$ | Spectral efficiency in bits/s/Hz | | 7 | $\dfrac{S}{N} \approx \log_2(M)$ | Approximate Shannon capacity for M-ary QAM (with margin) | > Where $B_w$ is the *occupied* channel bandwidth (≈ **7.61 MHz** for an 8 MHz channel), $K_{data}$ is the number of data carriers (1512 or 6048), $b$ is bits per constellation symbol (2 for QPSK, 4 for 16-QAM, 6 for 64-QAM), and $r_{cc}$ is the convolutional code rate. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility DVB-T's COFDM architecture is **field-proven in single-frequency networks**, where every transmitter in a region broadcasts on the **same frequency** and the receiver treats the delayed copies as constructive multipath (as long as the echo falls inside the guard interval). This unlocks **frequency-reuse patterns of 1**, dramatically improving spectrum utilisation compared to analogue TV. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Worked Problems ## 3.1 Derivation of Useful Symbol Duration We begin with the fundamental relationship that defines orthogonal spacing in OFDM. The receiver must be able to sample each sub-carrier at its peak and at the nulls of all others. This requires that the frequency spacing $\Delta f$ be exactly the reciprocal of the time-domain useful symbol duration $T_u$: $$\Delta f \cdot T_u = 1$$ For an FFT of size $N_{FFT}$ producing orthogonal carriers that fit inside the **occupied bandwidth** $B_w$ of the channel, the carrier spacing is also: $$\Delta f = \dfrac{B_w}{N_{FFT}}$$ Equating the two expressions for $\Delta f$ and solving for $T_u$: $$\dfrac{1}{T_u} = \dfrac{B_w}{N_{FFT}}$$ $$T_u = \dfrac{N_{FFT}}{B_w}$$ > **Step-by-step interpretation:** the larger the FFT size, the more carriers we pack into the same bandwidth, but this forces each carrier to be narrower and the symbol to be longer in time. This is the heart of the 2K vs 8K trade-off. ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — 8K Mode in an 8 MHz Channel **Given:** $N_{FFT} = 8192$, occupied bandwidth $B_w = 7.61$ MHz. **Find:** Useful symbol duration $T_u$, carrier spacing $\Delta f$, and the four guard-interval durations. **Step 1 — Useful symbol duration** $$T_u = \dfrac{N_{FFT}}{B_w} = \dfrac{8192}{7.61 \times 10^6}$$ $$T_u = 1.0762 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \approx 1076 \text{ µs}$$ > *Note: The standard defines Tu as the inverse of the *exact* nominal carrier spacing 1116 Hz, giving $T_u = 896$ µs for an 8 MHz channel in 8K mode. The "occupied bandwidth" derivation above is the textbook form; board exams accept either $T_u = 896$ µs (the round ETSI value) or the formula-based value. **KTU convention:** use the ETSI nominal values listed below.* | Mode | $T_u$ (8 MHz) | $\Delta f$ (8 MHz) | $T_u$ (7 MHz) | $T_u$ (6 MHz) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **2K** | **224 µs** | **4464 Hz** | 256 µs | 298.67 µs | | **8K** | **896 µs** | **1116 Hz** | 1024 µs | 1197.33 µs | **Step 2 — Carrier spacing (8K, 8 MHz)** $$\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_u} = \dfrac{1}{896 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1116 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 3 — Guard interval durations** for $G \in \{1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32\}$: $$\Delta_{1/4} = \dfrac{T_u}{4} = \dfrac{896}{4} = 224 \text{ µs}$$ $$\Delta_{1/8} = \dfrac{T_u}{8} = \dfrac{896}{8} = 112 \text{ µs}$$ $$\Delta_{1/16} = \dfrac{T_u}{16} = \dfrac{896}{16} = 56 \text{ µs}$$ $$\Delta_{1/32} = \dfrac{T_u}{32} = \dfrac{896}{32} = 28 \text{ µs}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Net Bit-Rate Calculation **Given:** 8K mode, 8 MHz channel, 64-QAM, convolutional code rate 2/3, guard interval 1/32. **Find:** Net useful bit-rate $R_{net}$. **Step 1 — Data carriers per OFDM symbol** $$K_{data} = 6048$$ **Step 2 — Bits per constellation symbol (64-QAM)** $$b = \log_2(64) = 6 \text{ bits/symbol}$$ **Step 3 — Bits per OFDM symbol before FEC** $$N_{raw} = K_{data} \cdot b = 6048 \times 6 = 36288 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 4 — Apply convolutional code rate** $$N_{coded} = N_{raw} \cdot r_{cc} = 36288 \times \dfrac{2}{3} = 24192 \text{ bits}$$ > *Note: RS(204,188) overhead is applied at the transport-stream layer and is already accounted for in the "useful" rate definition used by ETSI.* **Step 5 — Total OFDM symbol duration** for 8K, 8 MHz, G = 1/32: $$T_s = T_u + \Delta = 896 + 28 = 924 \text{ µs}$$ **Step 6 — Net bit-rate** $$R_{net} = \dfrac{N_{coded}}{T_s} = \dfrac{24192}{924 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$R_{net} \approx 26.18 \text{ Mbit/s}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DVB-T Physical Layer Parameters (8 MHz reference) | Parameter | 2K Mode | 8K Mode | |---|---|---| | FFT size $N_{FFT}$ | 2048 | 8192 | | Used carriers $K_{used}$ | 1705 | 6817 | | Data carriers $K_{data}$ | 1512 | 6048 | | Total pilots (CP + SP + TPS) | 193 (45+131+17) | 769 (177+524+68) | | Useful symbol duration $T_u$ | 224 µs | 896 µs | | Carrier spacing $\Delta f$ | 4464 Hz | 1116 Hz | | Max. guard interval Δ/4 | 56 µs | 224 µs | | Min. guard interval Δ/32 | 7 µs | 28 µs | | Max. supported Doppler spread | ~ 1.3 kHz | ~ 333 Hz | | Net bit-rate (64-QAM, 2/3, G=1/32) | ~ 26.13 Mbit/s | ~ 26.18 Mbit/s | # 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics ## 4.1 DVB-T Transmitter — Block-Level Functional Architecture The diagram below traces the **signal processing chain from MPEG-2 transport stream input to RF OFDM output**, mapping exactly where each system parameter is applied. ```mermaid flowchart LR src[("MPEG-2 Transport
Stream Input")] --> rs["Outer Encoder
RS 204 188"] rs --> oi["Outer Interleaver
Convolutional 12 by 17"] oi --> split{"Hierarchical
Mode Enabled"} split -- Yes --> hpLP["Split into HP and LP streams"] split -- No --> conv["Inner Encoder
Punctured Conv 1/2 2/3 3/4 5/6 7/8"] hpLP --> conv conv --> bi["Bit Interleaver"] bi --> sm["Symbol Interleaver
Native or In Depth"] sm --> map["Constellation Mapper
QPSK 16-QAM 64-QAM"] map --> fr["Frame Adaptor
Pilot and TPS Insertion"] fr --> tps["TPS Signalling
68 carriers 8K 17 carriers 2K"] tps --> ifft["IFFT Modulator
N equals 2048 or 8192"] ifft --> cp["Cyclic Prefix Insert
Guard Interval G equals 1-4 1-8 1-16 1-32"] cp --> dac["DAC and Upconversion"] dac --> rf[("RF Output
VHF or UHF")] cp -. 8K mode .- ifft8k["8192 point IFFT
896 us T_u"] cp -. 2K mode .- ifft2k["2048 point IFFT
224 us T_u"] style src fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style rf fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style ifft fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900 style cp fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 style map fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc style split fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600 ``` ## 4.2 OFDM Sub-Carrier Spectrum — Orthogonality Visualisation The diagram below shows how N=8 orthogonal sub-carriers (illustrative, not the DVB-T full 6817) pack into a single OFDM spectrum, each carrier's peak aligning with every other carrier's zero-crossing. ```mermaid flowchart TB axis["Frequency Axis f"] s1["Sub-carrier k equals 0
Peak at f0"] --> o1[Null at all other centres] s2["Sub-carrier k equals 1
Peak at f0 plus Delta f"] --> o2[Null at all other centres] s3["Sub-carrier k equals 2
Peak at f0 plus 2 Delta f"] --> o3[Null at all other centres] s4["Sub-carrier k equals 3
Peak at f0 plus 3 Delta f"] --> o4[Null at all other centres] s5["Sub-carrier k equals 4
Peak at f0 plus 4 Delta f"] --> o5[Null at all other centres] o1 --- o2 o2 --- o3 o3 --- o4 o4 --- o5 axis --- s1 style s1 fill:#ffe1e1 style s2 fill:#e1ffe1 style s3 fill:#e1e1ff style s4 fill:#fff4cc style s5 fill:#f0e1ff ``` ## 4.3 Carrier Allocation Within One OFDM Symbol (8K Mode) ```mermaid flowchart LR axis2["Carrier index k from 0 to 6816"] d["Data Carriers
k equals scattered positions
6048 carriers"] --> data[Carry MPEG-2 payload bits] cp["Continual Pilots CP
177 fixed positions"] --> cpuse[Channel estimation
frequency sync] sp["Scattered Pilots SP
524 positions every 12th symbol"] --> spuse[Time varying channel tracking] tp["TPS Carriers
68 fixed positions"] --> tpuse[Convey transmission parameters] axis2 --- d axis2 --- cp axis2 --- sp axis2 --- tp style d fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600 style cp fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900 style sp fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style tp fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank ## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Define DVB-T and list the two FFT modes it supports with their respective carrier counts.** **Model Answer:** > **DVB-T (Digital Video Broadcasting – Terrestrial)** is the ETSI standard (EN 300 744) for digital terrestrial TV broadcasting using **Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (COFDM)**. > > The two FFT modes are: > > | Mode | FFT Size | Used Carriers | Data Carriers | > |---|---|---|---| > | **2K** | 2048 | 1705 | 1512 | > | **8K** | 8192 | 6817 | 6048 | > > The choice between them depends on the **single-frequency network cell size** and **expected Doppler spread**. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **What is the purpose of the guard interval in DVB-T? Mention the available guard interval fractions.** **Model Answer:** The **guard interval** (also called cyclic prefix) is a copy of the **last portion** of each useful OFDM symbol, prepended before transmission. Its purposes are: 1. **Eliminates Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** caused by multipath echoes arriving within the guard duration. 2. **Converts the linear convolution** of the channel into a circular convolution, allowing the receiver to apply simple single-tap equalisation per sub-carrier. 3. **Absorbs echoes** from distant transmitters in a Single-Frequency Network (SFN). The four selectable guard interval fractions are: $$\dfrac{\Delta}{T_u} \in \left\{\dfrac{1}{4},\ \dfrac{1}{8},\ \dfrac{1}{16},\ \dfrac{1}{32}\right\}$$ --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` #### (a) Explain the OFDM modulation technique used in DVB-T and discuss its advantages over single-carrier transmission. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Conceptual foundation:** OFDM distributes the high-rate serial data stream across N parallel low-rate sub-carriers. Each sub-carrier carries a small fraction of the total bit-rate, making each symbol duration long and resistant to delay spread. **Working principle:** 1. The input serial bit-stream is converted to N parallel sub-streams. 2. Each sub-stream modulates its own sub-carrier at frequency $f_k = f_0 + k \cdot \Delta f$. 3. Orthogonality is maintained when $\Delta f = 1/T_u$. 4. The transmitter computes one N-point IFFT to generate the entire OFDM symbol in the time domain. 5. A cyclic prefix of duration $\Delta = G \cdot T_u$ is prepended. **Advantages over single-carrier transmission:** | Aspect | Single Carrier | OFDM (DVB-T) | |---|---|---| | Equalisation | Complex adaptive time-domain equaliser needed | Simple single-tap per carrier in frequency domain | | Multipath robustness | Requires long training sequences | Robust via guard interval | | Spectral efficiency | Requires guard bands | Carrier spectra overlap; orthogonality separates them | | SFN compatibility | Poor | Excellent — echoes absorbed by guard interval | | Doppler sensitivity | Better at mobile speeds | 2K mode acceptable up to ~200 km/h; 8K only fixed/pedestrian | **Conclusion:** OFDM is ideal for terrestrial broadcasting where long multipath echoes from buildings and hills would otherwise destroy single-carrier reception. --- #### (b) A DVB-T system operates in 8K mode over an 8 MHz channel with a guard interval ratio of 1/8 and 64-QAM constellation at a convolutional code rate of 3/4. Calculate: (i) useful symbol duration, (ii) carrier spacing, (iii) guard interval duration, (iv) total symbol duration, and (v) net useful bit-rate. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(i) Useful symbol duration** (8K, 8 MHz): $$T_u = 896\ \mu s$$ **(ii) Carrier spacing:** $$\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_u} = \dfrac{1}{896 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1116\ \text{Hz}$$ **(iii) Guard interval duration** for $G = 1/8$: $$\Delta = \dfrac{T_u}{8} = \dfrac{896}{8} = 112\ \mu s$$ **(iv) Total OFDM symbol duration:** $$T_s = T_u + \Delta = 896 + 112 = 1008\ \mu s$$ **(v) Net useful bit-rate** with $K_{data} = 6048$, $b = 6$ bits/symbol (64-QAM), $r_{cc} = 3/4$: $$N_{coded} = K_{data} \cdot b \cdot r_{cc} = 6048 \times 6 \times \dfrac{3}{4}$$ $$N_{coded} = 6048 \times 4.5 = 27216\ \text{bits per OFDM symbol}$$ $$R_{net} = \dfrac{N_{coded}}{T_s} = \dfrac{27216}{1008 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$R_{net} \approx 27.0\ \text{Mbit/s}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` #### (a) Describe the hierarchical modulation concept in DVB-T. Explain the role of the High Priority and Low Priority streams and the alpha factor. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Definition:** Hierarchical modulation is a DVB-T option where two independent MPEG-2 transport streams — **High Priority (HP)** and **Low Priority (LP)** — are mapped onto a single multi-level constellation simultaneously. **Mapping logic:** - The **HP stream** is mapped onto the most significant bits (MSBs) of each constellation symbol → gives robust reception over a wide area. - The **LP stream** is mapped onto the least significant bits (LSBs) → gives high capacity in strong-signal zones. **The α factor (constellation scaling):** For 16-QAM and 64-QAM hierarchical modes, the constellation points are spaced according to: $$d_1 = \alpha \cdot d_2$$ where $d_1$ is the distance between constellation points within the same quadrant (HP decision), and $d_2$ is the distance between quadrants (LP decision). Typical α values are **1, 2, or 4** (α = 1 corresponds to uniform, non-hierarchical mapping). - **α = 1** → uniform constellation, HP and LP equally protected. - **α = 2** → moderate hierarchy, standard recommendation. - **α = 4** → strong hierarchy, very robust HP, fragile LP. **Receiver operation:** - A receiver in a **poor coverage area** decodes only the HP stream → recovers basic picture and sound. - A receiver in a **strong coverage area** decodes both HP and LP → recovers full-quality programme. **Engineering utility:** allows a broadcaster to deliver **two programme qualities from one RF channel**, ideal for handheld vs fixed-reception scenarios in a single-frequency network. --- #### (b) Compare and contrast the 2K and 8K transmission modes of DVB-T in terms of FFT size, carrier spacing, useful symbol duration, mobility tolerance, and SFN cell size. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** | Comparison Parameter | 2K Mode | 8K Mode | |---|---|---| | FFT size $N_{FFT}$ | 2048 | 8192 | | Used carriers $K_{used}$ | 1705 | 6817 | | Useful symbol duration $T_u$ (8 MHz) | 224 µs | 896 µs (4× longer) | | Carrier spacing $\Delta f$ (8 MHz) | 4464 Hz (4× wider) | 1116 Hz (narrower) | | Max guard interval (G = 1/4) | 56 µs | 224 µs (4× longer) | | Echo tolerance | Up to ~56 µs delay spread | Up to ~224 µs delay spread | | Maximum SFN transmitter separation | ~17 km | ~67 km | | Doppler tolerance | High — works at vehicular speeds | Low — only fixed / pedestrian | | Symbol rate at the IFFT output | Higher (shorter $T_s$) | Lower (longer $T_s$) | | Spectral efficiency (data carriers / used carriers) | 1512 / 1705 ≈ 88.7 % | 6048 / 6817 ≈ 88.7 % (identical) | | Pilot overhead fraction | 193 / 1705 ≈ 11.3 % | 769 / 6817 ≈ 11.3 % (identical) | **Critical trade-off interpretation:** The two modes have **identical spectral efficiency** (same data-to-pilot ratio). The trade-off is purely between **mobility and SFN scalability**: - **2K** has wide carriers (4464 Hz) that survive fast channel variations (Doppler), making it suitable for **mobile TV reception** and **small regional SFNs**. - **8K** has narrow carriers that are easily destroyed by fast Doppler shifts but offer **four times the maximum echo tolerance**, enabling **large national SFNs** where distant transmitters must contribute constructively. **Conclusion:** The selection is dictated by the network operator's deployment topology. The 2K/8K switch is signalled to receivers via the **TPS (Transmission Parameter Signalling)** bits embedded in dedicated carriers of every OFDM symbol. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Using the wrong FFT size:** A common mistake is to write $K_{used} = 2048$ for 2K mode. The FFT size *is* 2048, but only 1705 carriers are *used*; the rest are guard-band nulls. > 2. **Confusing $T_u$ and $T_s$:** Many students write $T_s$ when they mean $T_u$. Examiners deduct 1 mark per occurrence. > 3. **Wrong unit on carrier spacing:** Always express carrier spacing in **Hz**, not in microseconds. > 4. **Forgetting the $r_{cc}$ factor** in bit-rate calculations → lose 2 marks silently. > 5. **Mixing up HP and LP streams** in hierarchical modulation → HP is the *robust* stream, not LP. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** - **DVB-T** = ETSI EN 300 744 standard, uses **COFDM**, supports **6/7/8 MHz** channels. - **Two FFT modes:** 2K (2048-point) and 8K (8192-point). Carrier count itself is **not** the FFT size. - **Used carriers:** 1705 (2K) and 6817 (8K). **Data carriers:** 1512 (2K) and 6048 (8K). - **Carrier spacing:** $\Delta f = 1/T_u$. For 8 MHz → 4464 Hz (2K), 1116 Hz (8K). - **Useful symbol duration** for 8 MHz → 224 µs (2K), 896 µs (8K). - **Guard interval fractions:** 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32 of $T_u$. - **Total symbol duration:** $T_s = T_u(1 + G)$. - **Pilots in every OFDM symbol:** Continual Pilots (CP) at fixed positions, Scattered Pilots (SP) at every 12th carrier in selected symbols, and TPS carriers conveying transmission parameters. - **Constellations:** QPSK (2 b/sym), 16-QAM (4 b/sym), 64-QAM (6 b/sym). - **Hierarchical mode:** HP stream on MSBs (robust), LP stream on LSBs (high-capacity). α factor controls hierarchy depth. - **Net bit-rate formula:** $R_{net} = K_{data} \cdot b \cdot r_{cc} / T_s$. - **Inner code:** Punctured convolutional, rates 1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8. - **Outer code:** Reed-Solomon RS(204,188). - **2K for mobility**, **8K for large SFN** — never confuse these two applications. - **Spectral efficiency** is identical for 2K and 8K (same data-to-pilot ratio). - **TPS (Transmission Parameter Signalling)** bits inform the receiver about constellation, code rate, guard interval, hierarchy, and 2K/8K mode.

topics.title: DVB-T Receiver
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DVB-T Receiver

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**DVB-T (Digital Video Broadcasting – Terrestrial) Receiver** is the end-user electronic subsystem designed to capture, demodulate, channel-decode, and source-decode the COFDM (Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing) based terrestrial digital television signal compliant with the **ETSI EN 300 744** standard. It performs the inverse of the DVB-T transmitter chain, reconstructing the original MPEG-2 Transport Stream (TS) packets from the impaired radio-frequency (RF) channel signal, enabling display of SDTV/HDTV services on a television set or set-top box output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – OEEVT722 Module 2**
> The DVB-T Receiver is studied as a *complete receive chain* — from antenna terminals to baseband TS output. Examiners routinely test the **functional block architecture**, **OFDM demodulation principles**, and **channel decoding order** (Viterbi → Reed-Solomon).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **massive multi-lane highway** where the broadcaster wants to deliver a movie to your home. Instead of putting the entire movie on a single fast truck (which would crash if any obstacle hits it), the broadcaster **fragments the movie into thousands of small packets** and places them onto thousands of small cars, each driving at a *very low speed* on a different parallel lane. The cars are arranged so cleverly that even though they are slow, the total bandwidth is huge. The road is bumpy (multipath, noise), so a small number of cars may crash, but the receiver only needs **most** of them to reassemble the movie. The DVB-T receiver is the **traffic controller** that:

1. Collects all cars back into proper order (synchronization).
2. Discards/repairs the crashed ones (channel coding).
3. Reassembles the movie into a watchable stream.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why OFDM?** It is intrinsically robust against **multipath fading**, the dominant impairment in terrestrial UHF broadcasting. OFDM converts a wideband frequency-selective channel into thousands of parallel flat-fading sub-channels.

### Standard Metrics & Constants (Bolded for Recall)

- **Channel bandwidth:** **6 MHz, 7 MHz, or 8 MHz** (region-dependent; India uses **7 MHz** Band IV/V, Europe uses 8 MHz).
- **Number of carriers (K):** **2K mode = 1705 carriers**, **8K mode = 6817 carriers**.
- **Carrier spacing (Δf):** approximately **1116 Hz (8K)** or **4464 Hz (2K)** for 8 MHz channel.
- **Useful symbol duration (Tu):** **224 µs (8K)**, **896 µs (2K)** in 8 MHz raster.
- **Constellation options:** **QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM**.
- **Inner code:** Punctured convolutional coding (rates 1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8).
- **Outer code:** Reed-Solomon **RS(204,188, t=8)** shortener of RS(255,239, t=8).
- **Net bit rate (8 MHz, 64-QAM, 2/3):** ≈ **24.13 Mbit/s** (max payload).
- **Guard Interval (Δ) choices:** **1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32** of Tu.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Quick Recall:** The DVB-T standard supports *three* hierarchical/non-hierarchical modulation formats in *two* FFT sizes — examiners love asking "list the constellation schemes" or "differentiate 2K vs 8K".

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Objectives of a DVB-T Receiver

A DVB-T receiver must perform the following hierarchical tasks, each of which is a syllabus sub-topic:

1. **RF Conditioning & Down-Conversion** – Select the desired channel, reject adjacent interferers, down-convert to a fixed IF, apply AGC.
2. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** – Sample the IF signal at a rate compatible with the symbol rate and the chosen oversampling factor.
3. **Digital Down-Conversion (DDC) & Clock Recovery** – Translate IF to complex baseband (I/Q), and recover symbol timing.
4. **OFDM Synchronization** – Coarse & fine *time synchronization* (using guard-interval correlation), *frequency synchronization* (carrier frequency offset correction).
5. **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** – Demodulate OFDM symbol from time to frequency domain.
6. **Channel Estimation & Equalization** – Use scattered-pilot interpolation to estimate the complex channel transfer function $\mathbf{H}[k]$ and apply a one-tap equalizer per sub-carrier.
7. **Inner De-interleaving** – Reverse the bit and symbol interleaving of the transmitter to disperse burst errors.
8. **Inner Decoding (Viterbi)** – Decode the punctured convolutional code; produce soft-decision input for the outer stage.
9. **Outer De-interleaving (Convolutional De-interleaver)** – Reverse the Forney-style outer interleaving.
10. **Outer Decoding (Reed-Solomon RS(204,188, t=8))** – Correct up to **8 byte errors per packet**.
11. **Energy Dispersal Removal** – Remove the 15-stage PRBS scrambler (MPEG-2 sync byte inversion).
12. **MPEG-2 Transport Stream Demultiplexing** – Filter the desired PID packets.
13. **MPEG-2 A/V Decoding** – Source decode the compressed audio and video for display.

### 2.2 Why the Order Matters (The "Why" Behind the Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Decoding Chain is Strictly Inverse to the Encoding Chain**
> A single ordering error makes the entire stream unreadable because each block re-establishes a *symbolic alignment* required by the next block.

- The **Inner decoder (Viterbi)** produces *soft* bit estimates so that the **Outer decoder (Reed-Solomon)** can apply erasure decoding using the reliability information from the Viterbi output. This is the key reason Viterbi **precedes** Reed-Solomon.
- **De-interleaving** must occur **after** the inner decoder (to disperse burst errors produced by deep fades) and **before** the outer decoder (so RS sees a quasi-random error distribution).
- **Channel estimation** must follow FFT, not precede it, because the equalizer works in the *frequency domain* on a per-sub-carrier basis.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Formulas: "One T symbol equals one over Delta F"** — memorize the inverse relationship between OFDM symbol duration and carrier spacing.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Numerical Value (8 MHz, 8K) | Unit |
|---|-----------|----------------------|----------------------------|------|
| 1 | Useful symbol duration | $T_u = \dfrac{1}{\Delta f}$ | $T_u = \dfrac{1}{1116} \approx 896$ | µs |
| 2 | Total symbol duration | $T_s = T_u + T_g$ | $T_s = 1120$ (with $\Delta = 1/8$) | µs |
| 3 | Guard interval | $T_g = \Delta \cdot T_u$ | $Delta = 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32$ | µs |
| 4 | Carrier spacing | $\Delta f = \dfrac{BW}{K \cdot (1+\Delta)}$ | 1116 | Hz |
| 5 | Max carriers (8K) | $K_{max} = 6817$ | 6817 | sub-carriers |
| 6 | Number of active carriers | $K_{active}$ | 6048 (8K, 8 MHz) | sub-carriers |
| 7 | Symbol rate (per sub-carrier) | $R_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | ≈ 893 | kBaud |
| 8 | Bit rate (gross) | $R_{gross} = R_s \cdot \log_2(M) \cdot K_{active}$ | depends on M | Mbit/s |
| 9 | Net bit rate (MPEG payload) | $R_{net} = \dfrac{188}{204} \cdot R_{c} \cdot \log_2(M) \cdot K_{active} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | ≈ 24.13 (64-QAM, 2/3) | Mbit/s |
| 10 | Reed-Solomon correction | $t = \dfrac{n-k}{2} = \dfrac{204-188}{2}$ | **8** | bytes/packet |
| 11 | Maximum Doppler tolerance | $f_{D,max} < \dfrac{0.0625}{T_s}$ | depends on mode | Hz |
| 12 | Multipath tolerance (max echo) | $\tau_{max} = T_g$ | up to 224 (8K, 1/4) | µs |
| 13 | One-tap equalizer | $\hat{Y}[k] = \dfrac{Y[k]}{H[k]}$ | — | complex |
| 14 | Viterbi path metric (BM) | $BM = \sum \vert r - s \vert^2$ | soft-decision | linear |
| 15 | Frequency offset tolerance | $\dfrac{\Delta f_{max}}{sub-carrier} \le 0.5$ | strict | sub-carrier spacing |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DVB-T receiver is the technological cornerstone enabling:

- **Free-to-air (FTA) terrestrial TV** in India (Doordarshan Free Dish, although DVB-T is being phased out in favor of DVB-T2 in new deployments).
- **Set-Top Box (STB) industry** — a multi-billion dollar global market.
- **Single-Frequency Networks (SFN)** for broadcasters, which require receivers capable of handling strong artificial multipath from synchronized transmitters.
- **Mobile/portable TV reception** in *2K mode* (smaller FFT, larger sub-carrier spacing, robust against Doppler).
- **HDTV distribution** (64-QAM, 2/3 or 3/4 coding) using the same receiver hardware.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: OFDM Symbol Duration from Carrier Spacing

We begin with the orthogonality condition for OFDM: sub-carriers are spaced by exactly the *reciprocal* of the useful symbol duration $T_u$ to maintain zero inter-carrier interference (ICI) at the sampling instants of the FFT.

**Step 1: Orthogonality condition.**
For two adjacent sub-carriers with frequencies $f_k$ and $f_{k+1}$, orthogonality requires:

$$
\int_{0}^{T_u} e^{j2\pi f_k t} \cdot \left(e^{j2\pi f_{k+1} t}\right)^* \, dt = 0
$$

**Step 2: Simplify the integrand.**
The conjugate of the second exponential flips the sign in the exponent. The integrand becomes $e^{j2\pi(f_k - f_{k+1})t}$:

$$
\int_{0}^{T_u} e^{j2\pi(f_k - f_{k+1})t} \, dt = 0
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the integral.**

$$
= \left[ \dfrac{e^{j2\pi(f_k - f_{k+1})t}}{j2\pi(f_k - f_{k+1})} \right]_{0}^{T_u}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute limits.**

$$
= \dfrac{e^{j2\pi(f_k - f_{k+1})T_u} - 1}{j2\pi(f_k - f_{k+1})}
$$

**Step 5: Impose zero condition.**
The numerator is zero if and only if the argument of the exponential is an integer multiple of $2\pi$:

$$
(f_k - f_{k+1}) \cdot T_u = n, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}
$$

**Step 6: Take the smallest non-trivial integer $n=1$** (minimum spacing to maintain orthogonality):

$$
\Delta f = \vert f_{k+1} - f_k \vert = \dfrac{1}{T_u}
$$

**Result:** $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_u}$ ⟹ $T_u = \dfrac{1}{\Delta f}$ ∎

> [!NOTE]
> This result *defines* the **inverse relationship** between symbol duration and sub-carrier spacing, and is the foundation for both 2K and 8K mode design choices in DVB-T.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Net Bit-Rate (8 MHz, 8K, 64-QAM, 2/3 code rate, 1/4 guard)

**Step 1: Compute the carrier spacing for 8K mode, 8 MHz, 1/4 guard interval.**

$$
\Delta f = \dfrac{BW_{channel}}{K_{used}} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{6}}{6817} \approx 1173.5 \text{ Hz}
$$

Wait — the official DVB-T value uses $K = 8192$ for FFT size (zero-padded to 8192), and the *useful* sub-carriers are 6817. The carrier spacing formula in the standard is:

$$
\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_u}
$$

where $T_u = 8192 \cdot T_s$ in the 8K case (FFT size). Numerically, for 8 MHz, **$\Delta f = 1116$ Hz** and $T_u = 896$ µs.

**Step 2: Compute the total symbol duration with guard interval $\Delta = 1/4$.**

$$
T_s = T_u \left(1 + \Delta\right) = 896 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{4}\right) = 896 \cdot 1.25 = 1120 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 3: Compute the gross bit rate (per active carrier × bits per symbol × symbol rate).**

Number of active carriers in 8K, 8 MHz = **6048**.
Bits per symbol for 64-QAM = $\log_2(64) = 6$.

$$
R_{gross} = 6048 \cdot 6 \cdot \dfrac{1}{T_s} = 6048 \cdot 6 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1120 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R_{gross} = 36288 \cdot 892.857 \approx 32.40 \text{ Mbit/s}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the convolutional code rate $R_c = 2/3$.**

$$
R_{after\_Viterbi} = R_{gross} \cdot R_c = 32.40 \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} = 21.60 \text{ Mbit/s}
$$

**Step 5: Apply Reed-Solomon overhead $(188/204) \approx 0.9216$.**

$$
R_{net} = 21.60 \cdot 0.9216 = 19.91 \text{ Mbit/s}
$$

**Result:** Net MPEG-2 payload ≈ **19.9 Mbit/s** for this configuration. ∎

> [!TIP]
> Different sources quote **24.13 Mbit/s** as the maximum net rate — this corresponds to **64-QAM, coding rate 7/8, guard interval 1/32, 8 MHz channel**. Always specify all four parameters in your exam answer.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Reed-Solomon Error Correction Capability

**Step 1: Recall the standard RS parameters.**
DVB-T uses a **shortened RS(204,188)** derived from the original RS(255,239, t=8).

**Step 2: Compute the parity bytes per packet.**

$$
n - k = 204 - 188 = 16 \text{ parity bytes}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the error-correction capability.**

$$
t = \dfrac{n - k}{2} = \dfrac{16}{2} = 8 \text{ bytes per packet}
$$

**Result:** The RS decoder can correct **any combination of up to 8 erroneous bytes per 188-byte MPEG packet** (and can detect up to 16). ∎

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Beyond 8 byte errors, the packet is flagged as *uncorrectable* and the transport stream's continuity counter is used to mask the error at the MPEG decoder.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: One-Tap Frequency-Domain Equalizer (FDE)

**Step 1: Received signal in frequency domain after FFT.**
Let $X[k]$ be the transmitted constellation symbol on sub-carrier $k$, and $H[k]$ be the channel frequency response at sub-carrier $k$. With additive white Gaussian noise $N[k]$:

$$
Y[k] = H[k] \cdot X[k] + N[k]
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $X[k]$ by dividing by $H[k]$.**

$$
\hat{X}[k] = \dfrac{Y[k]}{H[k]} = X[k] + \dfrac{N[k]}{H[k]}
$$

**Step 3: Note that channel estimation $\hat{H}[k]$ is imperfect**, so in practice:

$$
\hat{X}[k] = \dfrac{Y[k]}{\hat{H}[k]}
$$

**Step 4: Pilot-based estimation in DVB-T.**
Continual and scattered pilots are inserted at known positions. The receiver estimates:

$$
\hat{H}[k_p] = \dfrac{Y[k_p]}{X_{ref}[k_p]}
$$

for each pilot sub-carrier $k_p$, and then **interpolates** across the data sub-carriers.

**Result:** The frequency-domain equalizer is reduced to a *single complex division per sub-carrier per OFDM symbol*, which is computationally trivial and ideal for real-time hardware. ∎

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Receiver Block vs Impairment Addressed

| Receiver Block | Primary Impairment Addressed | Typical Implementation | Computational Cost |
|----------------|------------------------------|------------------------|--------------------|
| RF Tuner + SAW filter | Adjacent-channel interference, image | Superheterodyne / direct conversion | Low |
| AGC | Large dynamic range of input | Variable-gain LNA + IF VGA | Low |
| ADC + DDC | Sampling jitter, aliasing | 10–12 bit ADC, NCO mixer | Medium |
| Time/Freq sync | Symbol timing offset, CFO | Guard-interval correlation, SC algorithm | Medium |
| FFT (OFDM demod) | Multipath channel | 2K or 8K radix-2 FFT | High (8K dominant) |
| Channel estimation | Channel distortion | Pilot interpolation (2-D Wiener) | Medium |
| One-tap FDE | Per-subcarrier flat fading | Complex division | Low |
| Inner de-interleaver | Burst errors from deep fades | RAM address permutation | Low |
| Viterbi decoder | Random errors (AWGN) | Trellis search, soft-decision | High |
| Outer de-interleaver | Burst errors from Viterbi | Convolutional (Forney) | Low |
| RS decoder | Byte-error bursts | Berlekamp-Massey / Euclidean | Medium |
| Descrambler | Energy dispersal (spectrum shaping) | 15-stage LFSR XOR | Negligible |
| TS demux | PID filtering | Hardware filter | Low |
| MPEG-2 decoder | Source compression | ASIC / DSP | Very high |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete DVB-T Receiver Block Diagram (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Antenna UHF/VHF"] --> B["RF Tuner with SAW Filter"]
    B --> C["IF Stage 36 13 MHz"]
    C --> D["AGC Automatic Gain Control"]
    D --> E["ADC 10 to 12 bit"]
    E --> F["Digital Down Conversion to Baseband I Q"]
    F --> G["OFDM Sync Time and Frequency"]
    G --> H["FFT 2K 2048 or 8K 8192 point"]
    H --> I["Channel Estimation Pilot Interpolation"]
    I --> J["One Tap Frequency Equalizer"]
    J --> K["Inner De-interleaver Bit and Symbol"]
    K --> L["Viterbi Decoder Soft Output Convolutional"]
    L --> M["Outer De-interleaver Forney"]
    M --> N["Reed Solomon RS 204 188 t=8"]
    N --> O["Energy Dispersal Descrambler"]
    O --> P["MPEG-2 Transport Stream Demux"]
    P --> Q["MPEG-2 Audio Decoder"]
    P --> R["MPEG-2 Video Decoder"]
    Q --> S["DAC and Speaker Output"]
    R --> T["Video DAC and Display"]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Decoding Flow (Inner to Outer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["OFDM Demod Output"] --> B["Inner De-interleaver"]
    B --> C["Viterbi Decoder 1/2 2/3 3/4 5/6 7/8"]
    C --> D["Outer De-interleaver"]
    D --> E["Reed Solomon RS 204 188"]
    E --> F["MPEG-2 Transport Stream 188 byte packets"]
```

### 4.3 OFDM Symbol Timing Structure (Mermaid Gantt-style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Guard Interval 1/4 or 1/8 or 1/16 or 1/32 of Tu"] --> B["Useful Symbol Tu 896 us 8K or 224 us 2K"]
    B --> C["Guard Interval Cyclic Prefix Copy of tail of Tu"]
```

### 4.4 Modulation and Coding Modes Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["QPSK Robustness 1/2 to 7/8"]
    M2["16-QAM Balanced 1/2 to 7/8"]
    M3["64-QAM High Capacity 1/2 to 7/8"]
    M1 --> O1["Low bit rate Long range Mobile"]
    M2 --> O2["Medium bit rate SDTV"]
    M3 --> O3["High bit rate HDTV Fixed reception"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above intentionally use a **block-functional** representation because the underlying DSP dataflow and synchronization loops are too complex for a single physical schematic. Each block represents a hardware module in a typical DVB-T STB chipset (e.g., NXP TDA10048, Broadcom BCM3563, ST Micro STV0900).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. Define the term "DVB-T" and state its governing standard. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**DVB-T (Digital Video Broadcasting – Terrestrial)** is the ETSI (European Telecommunications Standards Institute) standard for digital terrestrial television broadcasting, formally specified under **ETSI EN 300 744**. It uses **Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (COFDM)** to deliver MPEG-2 transport streams over VHF/UHF channels, supporting 2K and 8K FFT modes with QPSK, 16-QAM, or 64-QAM constellations and concatenated Reed-Solomon + convolutional channel coding. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Q2. List any three advantages of OFDM as used in DVB-T. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Robustness against multipath fading:** The cyclic prefix (guard interval) absorbs inter-symbol interference caused by echoes up to $T_g$ in duration.
2. **Efficient equalization:** A frequency-selective channel becomes a set of flat-fading sub-channels, equalized by **one complex division per sub-carrier** (single-tap FDE).
3. **Spectrum efficiency and SFN capability:** Orthogonal sub-carriers allow tightly packed allocation and seamless support of **Single-Frequency Networks** for broadcasters. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Module Internal Choice — Module 2

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, OEEVT722 Entertainment Electronics, Module 2 Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Draw and explain the complete block diagram of a DVB-T receiver from the antenna input to the baseband MPEG-2 Transport Stream output. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the OFDM symbol structure and the role of the **guard interval** in DVB-T. Discuss how the choice of guard interval affects the maximum **bit rate** and **multipath tolerance**. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

The DVB-T receiver chain consists of the following sequential blocks (1 mark for the diagram, 1 mark per major block category, 1 mark for overall explanation):

1. **Antenna & RF Tuner:** Captures the UHF/VHF signal; selects a single channel via a tuned RF filter and SAW filter; down-converts to a fixed intermediate frequency (IF), typically 36 MHz or 13 MHz. *AGC* maintains a constant signal level.
2. **ADC & DDC:** The IF signal is digitized by a 10–12 bit ADC at ≥ 2× the IF; a numerically controlled oscillator (NCO) and digital mixer translate it to complex baseband (I/Q).
3. **OFDM Synchronization:** Time synchronization exploits the cyclic prefix correlation; frequency synchronization corrects the carrier frequency offset (CFO) using the continual pilots.
4. **FFT Block:** Performs 2K (2048-point) or 8K (8192-point) FFT to convert the OFDM time-domain symbol into frequency-domain constellation points.
5. **Channel Estimation & Equalization:** Scattered and continual pilots are used to estimate $\hat{H}[k]$; interpolation gives a per-sub-carrier complex channel response, and a one-tap equalizer corrects the data sub-carriers.
6. **Inner De-interleaver:** Reverses the bit and symbol interleaving, dispersing burst errors before Viterbi decoding.
7. **Viterbi Decoder (Inner):** Decodes the punctured convolutional code (rates 1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8), producing soft-decision bits.
8. **Outer De-interleaver:** Convolutional (Forney) de-interleaver disperses Viterbi-decoded bit errors.
9. **Reed-Solomon Decoder (Outer):** RS(204,188, t=8) corrects up to 8 byte errors per MPEG packet.
10. **Energy Dispersal Removal & TS Demux:** Descrambler restores MPEG sync bytes; the demux filters the relevant PIDs and outputs the MPEG-2 transport stream. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**OFDM Symbol Structure (3 marks):**
An OFDM symbol in DVB-T consists of two parts:

- **Useful part (Tu):** Contains the 2K (1705) or 8K (6817) modulated sub-carriers. For 8 MHz channel, **Tu = 896 µs (8K)** and **Tu = 224 µs (2K)**.
- **Guard Interval (Tg):** A cyclic prefix — a copy of the last $T_g$ portion of the useful symbol, prepended to the symbol. Allowed ratios: **1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32** of $T_u$.

**Role of Guard Interval (2 marks):**
The guard interval acts as a *buffer zone* that absorbs multipath echoes. As long as the maximum echo delay $\tau_{max} \le T_g$, the orthogonality of sub-carriers is preserved, and the receiver FFT sees only the cyclic convolution of the transmitted symbol with the channel impulse response — *no ISI*. The receiver discards the first $T_g$ portion and FFT-processes the remaining $T_u$ cleanly.

**Trade-off (2 marks):**

$$
R_{net} \propto \dfrac{1}{T_u (1 + \Delta)}
$$

- **Larger guard interval** (1/4): Higher multipath tolerance (up to 224 µs echo in 8K) but **lower net bit rate** (≈20% loss).
- **Smaller guard interval** (1/32): Higher net bit rate (≈3% loss) but limited multipath tolerance (≈28 µs in 8K).
- **Trade-off selection:** SFN broadcasters choose 1/4 or 1/8; single-transmitter high-bit-rate HDTV services use 1/32. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **channel decoding hierarchy** in a DVB-T receiver. Why is **Viterbi decoding placed before Reed-Solomon decoding**? Discuss the role of the interleavers. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A DVB-T system uses **8 MHz channel bandwidth**, **8K FFT mode**, **64-QAM modulation**, **convolutional code rate 2/3**, and **guard interval ratio 1/4**. Calculate:
   (i) Useful symbol duration $T_u$
   (ii) Total symbol duration $T_s$
   (iii) Approximate **net MPEG-2 bit rate**, given 6048 active sub-carriers. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Channel Decoding Hierarchy (3 marks):**
The DVB-T receiver uses a *concatenated coding scheme* with two layers:

1. **Inner code — Punctured Convolutional Code (rates 1/2 to 7/8):** Implemented by a **Viterbi decoder** producing soft-decision bit estimates (log-likelihood ratios).
2. **Outer code — Reed-Solomon RS(204,188, t=8):** A block code correcting byte errors in the 188-byte MPEG payload.

**Why Viterbi precedes Reed-Solomon (2 marks):**

- The Viterbi decoder is *naturally* suited to produce **soft-decision outputs** (reliability information) which the **Reed-Solomon decoder can exploit via erasure decoding**, improving effective correction by 2× for the same SNR.
- Convolutional codes are *better* at correcting the **random errors** caused by AWGN on each sub-carrier; Reed-Solomon is *better* at correcting **burst errors** that may still slip through. The sequence "soft random → burst-resistant" is optimal.

**Role of Interleavers (2 marks):**
- The **inner (bit/symbol) interleaver** spreads deep-fade errors across the convolutional code's constraint length, allowing Viterbi to decode them effectively.
- The **outer (Forney) interleaver** disperses Viterbi-decoded burst errors so that RS sees an effectively random distribution of byte errors.
- Together, the two interleavers implement a **time-diversity** strategy without adding redundancy. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** 8 MHz, 8K mode, 64-QAM, code rate 2/3, guard $\Delta = 1/4$, 6048 active carriers.

**(i) Useful symbol duration $T_u$ (2 marks):**
For 8K mode in 8 MHz, the carrier spacing is $\Delta f = 1116$ Hz (standard value).

$$
T_u = \dfrac{1}{\Delta f} = \dfrac{1}{1116} = 896 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 896 \text{ µs}
$$

**(ii) Total symbol duration $T_s$ (2 marks):**

$$
T_s = T_u (1 + \Delta) = 896 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{4}\right) = 896 \times 1.25 = 1120 \text{ µs}
$$

**(iii) Net MPEG-2 bit rate (3 marks):**

$$
R_{net} = \underbrace{6048}_{active} \cdot \underbrace{6}_{64\text{-QAM bits}} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{2}{3}}_{R_c} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{188}{204}}_{RS~overhead} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{T_s}}_{symbol~rate}
$$

$$
R_{net} = 6048 \cdot 6 \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{188}{204} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1120 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

**Compute step by step:**

$$
6048 \cdot 6 = 36288
$$

$$
36288 \cdot \dfrac{2}{3} = 24192
$$

$$
24192 \cdot \dfrac{188}{204} = 24192 \cdot 0.92157 \approx 22295 \text{ bits/symbol} \cdot \text{??}
$$

*(Note: units tracked as "MPEG-2 bits per OFDM symbol per second")*

$$
R_{net} = 22295 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1.120 \times 10^{-3}} = 22295 \cdot 892.857
$$

$$
R_{net} \approx 19.91 \text{ Mbit/s}
$$

**Result:** $T_u = 896$ µs, $T_s = 1120$ µs, $R_{net} \approx 19.91$ Mbit/s. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the RS overhead $(188/204)$** — students commonly quote ~21.6 Mbit/s and lose 1.5 marks. Always mention "**net MPEG-2 payload**" in your final line.
> 2. **Confusing 2K and 8K active carriers** — 2K has 1512 active carriers, 8K has 6048. Using the wrong value yields a 4× error in the bit rate.
> 3. **Mixing up useful and total symbol duration** — examiners expect the guard interval to be explicitly shown in your calculation, not silently absorbed.
> 4. **Writing $\log_2(64) = 8$** instead of **6** — this is a common slip; double-check the base of the logarithm.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — DVB-T Receiver**

- ✅ **DVB-T = COFDM over UHF/VHF**, standard **ETSI EN 300 744**.
- ✅ **Two FFT sizes:** 2K (1705 useful) and 8K (6817 useful) carriers.
- ✅ **Three constellations:** QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM (2, 4, 6 bits/symbol).
- ✅ **Five code rates:** 1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8 (punctured convolutional).
- ✅ **Four guard interval ratios:** 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32.
- ✅ **Inner code = Viterbi decoder** (soft decision); **Outer code = Reed-Solomon RS(204,188, t=8)**.
- ✅ **Two interleavers:** inner (bit + symbol) and outer (Forney convolutional).
- ✅ **One-tap FDE** in frequency domain after FFT — divide by $\hat{H}[k]$.
- ✅ **Net bit rate formula** = carriers × bits × $R_c$ × (188/204) × (1/Ts).
- ✅ **Maximum net bit rate** ≈ 24.13 Mbit/s (8 MHz, 64-QAM, 7/8, 1/32).
- ✅ **MPEG-2 TS packets** = 188 bytes; sync byte = 0x47.
- ✅ **DVB-T → DVB-T2** uses LDPC + BCH inner codes and larger FFTs (1K, 2K, 4K, 8K, 16K, 32K).
- ✅ **Common exam traps:** sign errors in $\log_2(M)$, forgetting RS overhead, mis-stating the cyclic prefix as *postfix*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Broadcasting for Handheld Devices: DVB-H Standard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Broadcasting for Handheld Devices: DVB-H Standard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DVB-H (Digital Video Broadcasting - Handheld)** is a technical specification standardized by the **ETSI (European Telecommunications Standards Institute)** under the reference **EN 302 304**, designed specifically for the delivery of digital television and multimedia services to low-power, battery-operated, mobile handheld devices such as smartphones, PDAs, and portable media players.

The DVB-H standard is fundamentally an **extension of the terrestrial DVB-T (Digital Video Broadcasting - Terrestrial)** standard, but is heavily optimized for the unique challenges of handheld reception, including **mobility, limited battery capacity, small-form-factor antennas, and hostile propagation environments**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** DVB-H was formally adopted by ETSI in **November 2004**. It operates in the **UHF band (470 MHz – 862 MHz)** and **VHF band** with channel bandwidths of **5 MHz, 6 MHz, 7 MHz, or 8 MHz** depending on the regional allocation (e.g., 8 MHz in Europe, 6 MHz in the US).

## 1.2 The Three Foundational Pillars of DVB-H

The DVB-H specification introduces three key technological enhancements over its parent DVB-T standard:

| # | Pillar | Purpose |
|---|--------|---------|
| 1 | **Time Slicing** | Aggressive power-saving mechanism for the receiver front-end |
| 2 | **MPE-FEC (Multi-Protocol Encapsulation – Forward Error Correction)** | Robust error protection for IP datagrams in mobile channels |
| 3 | **4K Mode (Optional)** | Extended OFDM carrier mode for single-antenna portable reception |

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Boy:** Imagine you are waiting for tomorrow's newspaper. A normal delivery (DVB-T) drops the paper every minute all day long, keeping your porch light on continuously. A smart delivery (DVB-H) sends the *entire* newspaper in **one big burst** for a few seconds and then tells the delivery boy (the receiver) to **"go to sleep"** for the rest of the hour. Your porch light is on only 10% of the time → **massive power savings**. This is precisely what Time Slicing does to the receiver.

## 1.3 Why DVB-H Was Needed

> [!NOTE]
> DVB-T was originally designed for **fixed rooftop reception** and **vehicular reception**, not for **pocket-sized devices**. The limitations of DVB-T for handheld usage included:
> - **High power consumption** of the receiver (draining small batteries in under 1 hour).
> - **Insufficient Doppler tolerance** for pedestrian mobility.
> - **Lack of robust error protection** for IP-based interactive services.
> - **No graceful handover mechanism** between cells of the broadcast network.

DVB-H addresses each of these by introducing a **battery-friendly, IP-based, mobile-optimized** broadcast architecture.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture Overview

The DVB-H system is a **one-way downlink broadcast system** with optional **return channel** interaction (typically via GPRS, UMTS, or Wi-Fi). The complete signal chain consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Service Layer:** Encapsulates audio/video (e.g., H.264/AVC, MPEG-4 AAC) and file/data services into **IP datagrams**.
2. **IP Encapsulation Layer:** Uses **MPE (Multi-Protocol Encapsulation)** sections to carry IP packets within MPEG-2 Transport Streams (TS).
3. **MPE-FEC Layer:** Adds Reed-Solomon parity data to the MPE sections for additional robustness.
4. **Time Slicing Layer:** Organizes elementary streams into **bursts** separated by off-periods.
5. **DVB-T Physical Layer:** Performs channel coding (convolutional + Reed-Solomon), interleaving, and **OFDM modulation** (QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM with 2K or 4K FFT).

## 2.2 Time Slicing — The Heart of DVB-H

### 2.2.1 Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Slicing** means that each broadcast service is transmitted in **short, high-bandwidth bursts** rather than as a continuous low-bitrate stream. Between bursts, the receiver front-end (tuner + demodulator) is **switched OFF**, saving up to **~90% of the tuner power**.

### 2.2.2 Operational Logic

- The total **constant bitrate (CBR)** of a service, $R_{\text{CBR}}$, is compressed in time into a burst with peak bitrate $R_{\text{burst}}$.
- The duty cycle $\delta$ is the fraction of time the receiver is active:

$$\delta = \frac{R_{\text{CBR}}}{R_{\text{burst}}} = \frac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$$

- The **power saving factor** is given by:

$$\text{Power Saving} = (1 - \delta) \times 100\%$$

- Typical values in commercial deployments: $R_{\text{burst}} \approx 10 \times R_{\text{CBR}}$, giving $\delta \approx 0.1$ and a **power saving of approximately 90%**.

### 2.2.3 Delta-t (Δt) Signaling

The DVB-H transmitter inserts a special field called **Delta-t** into the **MPE section header** (a 12-bit or 15-bit field). This tells the receiver **exactly when the next burst of the *same* service will arrive**, allowing the receiver to wake up precisely in time.

## 2.3 MPE-FEC — Robust Error Protection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MPE-FEC (Multi-Protocol Encapsulation – Forward Error Correction)** is a *separate* outer error protection layer applied to IP datagrams. It uses a **Reed-Solomon RS(255, 191)** code over **GF(256)** arranged in a virtual matrix.

### 2.3.1 The Virtual Matrix Structure

The MPE-FEC frame is structured as a 2D matrix:

- **Number of rows:** $N_{\text{rows}}$ (typically 256 to 1024).
- **Number of columns:** 191 (data) + 64 (parity) = **255 columns total**.
- The data area (left side) carries the **IP datagrams** packed in MPE sections.
- The parity area (right side) carries the **RS parity bytes** computed row-wise.

The receiver can **partially fill** the matrix with received data and **erase-fill** the rest, then perform RS decoding to recover the original data — a technique called **Virtual Decoding**.

### 2.3.2 The "MPE-FEC Frame" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> An MPE-FEC frame consists of $N_{\text{rows}} \times 255$ bytes, of which $N_{\text{rows}} \times 191$ are application data and $N_{\text{rows}} \times 64$ are RS parity. The code rate is therefore $R_{\text{MPE-FEC}} = \frac{191}{255} \approx 0.749$.

## 2.4 The 4K Mode

> [!NOTE]
> The **4K FFT mode** (4096 subcarriers) was introduced in DVB-H as a *compromise* between the **2K mode** (good for mobile, small cells) and the **8K mode** (good for fixed, large SFN cells). It offers:
> - Symbol duration: $T_{\text{sym, 4K}} = 448 \,\mu\text{s}$ (for 8 MHz channel).
> - Better trade-off between **Doppler tolerance** and **SFN (Single Frequency Network) coverage**.
> - Reduced guard interval overhead compared to 8K.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table summarizes the most important equations, parameters, and boundary conditions for DVB-H. **Memorize these for the KTU ESE.**

| Parameter / Concept | Formula or Value | Units / Notes |
|---------------------|------------------|---------------|
| Time-Slicing Duty Cycle | $\delta = R_{\text{CBR}} / R_{\text{burst}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Power Saving Factor | $(1 - \delta) \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| MPE-FEC Code Rate | $R_{\text{MPE-FEC}} = 191/255$ | $\approx 0.749$ |
| Reed-Solomon Code | RS(255, 191) over GF($2^8$) | 64 parity bytes per row |
| OFDM FFT Sizes | 2K, 4K, 8K | Subcarriers: 2048, 4096, 8192 |
| Channel Bandwidths | 5, 6, 7, 8 | MHz (region-dependent) |
| QAM Constellations | QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM | Spectral efficiency: 2, 4, 6 bits/s/Hz |
| OFDM Symbol Duration (8K, 8 MHz) | $T_{\text{sym}} = 896$ | $\mu$s |
| OFDM Symbol Duration (4K, 8 MHz) | $T_{\text{sym}} = 448$ | $\mu$s |
| Guard Interval | $\Delta = T_g / T_u$ | Ratios: 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32 |
| RF Band (UHF) | 470 – 862 | MHz |
| Net Data Rate (8 MHz, 64-QAM, 2/3, 1/8 GI) | $\approx 24.1$ | Mbit/s |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> DVB-H was commercially deployed by operators such as **3 Italia** (2006), **DVB-H pilot in Finland**, **Hi3G in Sweden**, and others. Although largely superseded by **DVB-T2 Lite, DVB-NGH, and 3GPP eMBMS / 5G Broadcast** for newer networks, DVB-H remains a **pivotal case study** in the design of mobile broadcast systems, especially for the KTU 2024 syllabus which emphasizes the **evolution of broadcast technology for entertainment devices**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Achievable Power Saving

Let us derive the theoretical maximum power saving achievable through Time Slicing under ideal conditions.

### Step 1 — Define the Continuous-Stream Bitrate

For a single DVB-H service delivered as a continuous stream (no time slicing), the bitrate of the service as seen by the receiver is:

$$R_{\text{CBR}} = \text{Constant Bitrate of one elementary stream (Mbit/s)}$$

### Step 2 — Define the Burst Bitrate

In a Time-Sliced system, the same service is delivered in a compressed time window, with peak bitrate:

$$R_{\text{burst}} = \frac{R_{\text{CBR}}}{\delta} \quad \text{where} \quad 0 < \delta \leq 1$$

### Step 3 — Define the Burst Duration and Cycle Period

The **on-time** of the receiver during one cycle is:

$$T_{\text{on}} = \frac{\text{Burst Size (bits)}}{R_{\text{burst}}}$$

The **total cycle time** is:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{T_{\text{on}}}{\delta}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Power Saving

The fraction of time the receiver's tuner is **switched OFF** is:

$$\eta_{\text{saving}} = \frac{T_{\text{cycle}} - T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = 1 - \delta$$

Hence the **percentage power saving** is:

$$\text{Power Saving (\%)} = (1 - \delta) \times 100\%$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example (Board-Exam Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A DVB-H service has $R_{\text{CBR}} = 400 \,\text{kbit/s}$. The transmitter bursts this service at $R_{\text{burst}} = 4 \,\text{Mbit/s}$. Calculate the duty cycle and the tuner power saving.

**Solution:**

$$\delta = \frac{R_{\text{CBR}}}{R_{\text{burst}}} = \frac{400 \times 10^3}{4 \times 10^6} = 0.1$$

$$\text{Power Saving} = (1 - 0.1) \times 100\% = 90\%$$

Hence, the receiver is **off for 90%** of the time — a critical benefit for battery-operated devices. This is the **most frequently asked numerical in KTU exams** on this topic.

## 3.2 Derivation: MPE-FEC Code Rate

### Step 1 — Identify the RS Parameters

The MPE-FEC frame uses **RS(255, 191, t = 32)** over **GF($2^8$)**, where:
- $n = 255$ (total symbols per row)
- $k = 191$ (data symbols per row)
- $2t = n - k = 64$ parity symbols per row
- The error-correcting capability is $t = 32$ symbol errors per row.

### Step 2 — Apply the Code-Rate Formula

The code rate is simply:

$$R_{\text{MPE-FEC}} = \frac{k}{n} = \frac{191}{255} \approx 0.7490$$

### Step 3 — Overhead Calculation

The **FEC overhead** added to the stream is:

$$\text{Overhead} = \frac{n - k}{k} = \frac{64}{191} \approx 33.51\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Insight:** This 33.5% overhead is the *price paid* for dramatically improved mobile-channel robustness. In conjunction with the inner DVB-T convolutional code (e.g., rate 2/3), the **effective total code rate** is:

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{convolutional}} \times R_{\text{MPE-FEC}} = \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{191}{255} \approx 0.4993$$

This means **roughly half** of the transmitted bits are useful payload — a worthwhile trade-off given the hostile mobile environment.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DVB-T vs DVB-H

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is **high-yield for KTU** — questions comparing DVB-T and DVB-H are extremely common.

| Feature | DVB-T (Terrestrial) | DVB-H (Handheld) |
|---------|---------------------|------------------|
| Target Device | Fixed TV / vehicular | Mobile handheld |
| Power Consumption | High | Low (Time Slicing saves ~90%) |
| MPE-FEC | Not present | Present (RS(255,191)) |
| Time Slicing | No | Yes (mandatory) |
| 4K Mode | No (only 2K/8K) | Yes (optional) |
| Bitrate per Service | Continuous | Bursty (with off-periods) |
| Handover Support | Limited | Yes (using Delta-t + cell ID) |
| Typical Constellation | 64-QAM (fixed) | QPSK, 16-QAM (mobile-friendly) |
| Receiver Sensitivity | Standard | Enhanced (DVB-H SFN gain) |
| IP Encapsulation | Optional | Native (MPE/MPE-FEC) |

## 3.4 Derivation: Time Slicing — Burst Size vs Bitrate

> [!NOTE]
> Let the **burst size** (amount of data transmitted in one burst) be $B$ bytes. The burst transmission time is:

$$T_{\text{burst}} = \frac{B \times 8}{R_{\text{burst}}} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

The receiver can buffer this entire burst in RAM and then **play it out at $R_{\text{CBR}}$** while the tuner sleeps. The total playback time of one burst is:

$$T_{\text{playout}} = \frac{B \times 8}{R_{\text{CBR}}}$$

Since $T_{\text{playout}} \gg T_{\text{burst}}$ (because $R_{\text{burst}} \gg R_{\text{CBR}}$), the receiver stays in low-power state for most of the playout duration.

---
