# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: DVB-H Transmitter Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Audio Video Source H.264 or MPEG4] --> B[MPEG4 AAC Audio Encoder]
    A --> C[H.264 or AVC Video Encoder]
    B --> D[RTP Layer Encapsulation]
    C --> D
    D --> E[UDP IP Datagram Generation]
    E --> F[MPE Section Builder]
    F --> G[MPE FEC Frame Builder RS 255 191]
    G --> H[Time Slicing Scheduler with Delta t]
    H --> I[MPEG2 Transport Stream Multiplexer]
    I --> J[DVB-T Channel Coding Convolutional RS Outer]
    J --> K[OFDM Modulation 2K 4K or 8K FFT]
    K --> L[RF Upconversion to UHF 470 to 862 MHz]
    L --> M[Transmit Antenna SFN Network]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: DVB-H Receiver Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RA[Receive Antenna on Handheld Device] --> RB[UHF Tuner and LNA]
    RB --> RC[OFDM Demodulation 2K 4K 8K]
    RC --> RD[Channel Decoding Viterbi plus RS Outer]
    RD --> RE[Transport Stream Demultiplexer]
    RE --> RF[Time Slice Burst Detector reads Delta t]
    RF --> RG{Is this burst for my service?}
    RG -- Yes --> RH[Buffer the Burst in RAM]
    RG -- No --> RI[Power Down Tuner and Demod]
    RH --> RJ[MPE FEC Decoder RS 255 191 Erasure Decoding]
    RJ --> RK[IP Datagram Extraction]
    RK --> RL[UDP and RTP Decapsulation]
    RL --> RM[H.264 Video Decoder]
    RL --> RN[AAC Audio Decoder]
    RM --> RO[Display on Handheld Screen]
    RN --> RP[Audio Output to Headphones]
    RI --> RF
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: MPE-FEC Virtual Matrix Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Matrix["MPE FEC Virtual Matrix 1024 rows by 255 columns"]
        D1["Application Data Area 191 columns left side"]
        D2["Reed Solomon Parity Area 64 columns right side"]
    end
    subgraph DA["Data Area filled by MPE sections"]
        IP1["IP Datagram 1"]
        IP2["IP Datagram 2"]
        IP3["IP Datagram N"]
    end
    subgraph PA["Parity Area computed by RS encoder"]
        P1["Row 1 parity 64 bytes"]
        P2["Row 2 parity 64 bytes"]
        P3["Row M parity 64 bytes"]
    end
    D1 --> DA
    D2 --> PA
    DA --> PA
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: DVB-H Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Studio["Content Provider Studio"]
        S1[Audio Video Sources]
        S2[IP Encapsulation and MPE FEC]
        S3[Time Slicing and Multiplexing]
    end
    subgraph Tx["DVB H Transmitter Site"]
        T1[DVB T Modulator OFDM]
        T2[High Power UHF Amplifier]
        T3[Broadcast Antenna]
    end
    subgraph Net["SFN Broadcast Network"]
        N1[Cell 1 UHF Channel]
        N2[Cell 2 UHF Channel same frequency]
        N3[Cell 3 UHF Channel same frequency]
    end
    subgraph Rx["Handheld Receiver"]
        R1[Antenna and Tuner]
        R2[Time Slice Synchronization]
        R3[MPE FEC Decoding]
        R4[Application Layer Video Audio Output]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Timeline: Time Slicing — ON/OFF Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cycle["One DVB H Burst Cycle T cycle"]
        ON["Burst ON T on = T burst ~ 10 percent"]
        OFF["Power OFF 90 percent time"]
    end
    Cycle --> Next["Next Cycle T cycle seconds later"]
    Next --> Cycle
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Explain the concept of Time Slicing in DVB-H. How does it help in reducing the power consumption of handheld receivers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Time Slicing is a power-saving mechanism in the DVB-H standard where each service is transmitted in **short, high-bitrate bursts** rather than as a continuous stream. Between two consecutive bursts of the same service, the receiver's RF tuner and OFDM demodulator are **switched OFF**. The receiver buffers the burst data in RAM and plays it out at the original constant bitrate (CBR) of the service. Since the receiver is active only for a small fraction ($\delta$) of the time — typically 10% — the **power saving achieved is approximately 90%**. A special field called **Delta-t** in the MPE section header informs the receiver about the exact arrival time of the next burst. *(3 marks: 1 for concept + 1 for mechanism + 1 for numerical/quantification.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**What is MPE-FEC in DVB-H? Briefly explain the role of the Reed-Solomon RS(255, 191) code in it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

MPE-FEC stands for **Multi-Protocol Encapsulation – Forward Error Correction**. It is an **outer error protection layer** applied to the IP datagrams before they are transmitted via DVB-H. The data is organized in a **virtual matrix** of typically 1024 rows × 255 columns. The first 191 columns are filled with IP datagrams (data), and the remaining **64 columns are RS parity bytes** computed by a Reed-Solomon RS(255, 191) encoder operating over **GF(256)**. At the receiver, when the channel causes burst errors, the missing bytes can be *erasure-filled* and corrected by the RS decoder. The code rate is $191/255 \approx 0.749$, providing an overhead of ~33.5% but dramatically improving the **mobile-channel robustness** — essential for Doppler-shifted, multipath-prone handheld reception. *(3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **DVB-H transmitter architecture**. Describe the role of each functional block from the audio/video source to the RF output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DVB-H service has a constant bitrate of 350 kbit/s. The transmitter bursts this service at a peak bitrate of 5 Mbit/s.
   - (i) Calculate the **duty cycle** $\delta$. **(2 marks)**
   - (ii) Calculate the **percentage power saving** at the receiver. **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) If the total cycle time is 1 second, compute the **burst transmission time** $T_{\text{burst}}$. **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — DVB-H Transmitter Architecture (7 marks):**

The DVB-H transmitter consists of the following sequential blocks:

1. **Audio/Video Source:** Provides the raw audio (e.g., MPEG-4 AAC) and video (e.g., H.264/AVC) elementary streams.
2. **Encoders:** H.264 video encoder and AAC audio encoder compress the sources to a low bitrate suitable for handheld viewing (typically 200–500 kbit/s per service).
3. **RTP/UDP/IP Encapsulation:** Each elementary stream is packetized into **Real-time Transport Protocol (RTP)** packets, then into **UDP**, and finally into **IP datagrams**.
4. **MPE Section Builder:** The IP datagrams are encapsulated into **MPEG-2 Multi-Protocol Encapsulation (MPE) sections**, which are the fundamental containers in the DVB transport stream.
5. **MPE-FEC Frame Builder:** A virtual matrix is constructed. IP datagrams populate the **data columns (191)** and a **Reed-Solomon RS(255, 191)** encoder computes the **parity columns (64)** row-wise.
6. **Time Slicing Scheduler:** Organizes the data into **bursts** and inserts the **Delta-t** field in the MPE section header to indicate the start time of the next burst.
7. **MPEG-2 Transport Stream Multiplexer:** Combines multiple DVB-H services (and possibly DVB-T services) into a single **188-byte TS packet** stream.
8. **DVB-T Channel Coding:** Adds an outer **Reed-Solomon RS(204, 188)** code, **convolutional interleaving**, and an inner **punctured convolutional code** (rates 1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8).
9. **OFDM Modulation:** Maps the coded bits to **QPSK, 16-QAM, or 64-QAM** constellations, inserts pilots, and performs an **IFFT** with 2K, 4K, or 8K size. A **guard interval** is prepended to each OFDM symbol to combat multipath.
10. **RF Up-conversion and Amplification:** The OFDM baseband signal is up-converted to the **UHF band (470–862 MHz)**, amplified by a high-power amplifier, and radiated by the transmit antenna.

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram:** Refer to **SECTION 4.1** for the Mermaid block diagram of the DVB-H transmitter chain.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $R_{\text{CBR}} = 350 \,\text{kbit/s} = 0.35 \,\text{Mbit/s}$
- $R_{\text{burst}} = 5 \,\text{Mbit/s}$
- $T_{\text{cycle}} = 1 \,\text{s}$

**(i) Duty Cycle (2 marks):**

$$\delta = \frac{R_{\text{CBR}}}{R_{\text{burst}}} = \frac{0.35}{5} = 0.07$$

**(ii) Power Saving (2 marks):**

$$\text{Power Saving (\%)} = (1 - \delta) \times 100 = (1 - 0.07) \times 100 = 93\%$$

**(iii) Burst Transmission Time (3 marks):**

$$T_{\text{on}} = \delta \times T_{\text{cycle}} = 0.07 \times 1 = 0.07 \,\text{s} = 70 \,\text{ms}$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{burst}} = 70 \,\text{ms}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** A very common mistake is to use $R_{\text{burst}}/R_{\text{CBR}}$ instead of $R_{\text{CBR}}/R_{\text{burst}}$ when calculating $\delta$. **Always remember:** $\delta$ is the **fraction of time ON** (small number), not the OFF time. Power saving = $1 - \delta$, **not** $\delta$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model]`

**Q. (a)** Explain **MPE-FEC** in DVB-H. With a suitable diagram, describe the **virtual matrix structure**, the **RS(255, 191) encoding process**, and the concept of **virtual decoding** at the receiver. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **DVB-T** and **DVB-H** standards under the following heads:
   - (i) Target application and device **(2 marks)**
   - (ii) Power consumption strategy **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) Error protection scheme **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — MPE-FEC Deep Dive (7 marks):**

**Definition:** MPE-FEC (Multi-Protocol Encapsulation – Forward Error Correction) is a **mandatory outer error-correction layer** introduced in DVB-H to provide an additional level of robustness over the inner convolutional code of DVB-T. It is defined in **ETSI EN 302 304** and uses a **Reed-Solomon RS(255, 191)** code over the Galois Field **GF($2^8$)**.

**Virtual Matrix Structure:**

The MPE-FEC frame is organized as a 2D matrix with:
- **Rows:** $N_{\text{rows}}$ — configurable from 1 to 1024 (default typically 256 or 512).
- **Columns:** **255** (191 data + 64 parity).

```
+---------------------+----------------+
|   Data Area         |   Parity Area  |
|   (191 columns)     |   (64 columns) |
|   IP datagrams in   |   RS parity    |
|   MPE sections      |   bytes        |
+---------------------+----------------+
```

**Encoding Process (3 steps):**
1. **IP datagrams** are packed sequentially, left-to-right, top-to-bottom, into the **Data Area** (191 columns × $N_{\text{rows}}$ rows). If padding is needed, the bytes `0xFF` are used.
2. A **Reed-Solomon RS(255, 191) encoder** is applied **row-wise** across the 255-byte row (191 data + 64 parity positions). The encoder outputs 64 parity bytes per row.
3. The resulting **Parity Area** (64 columns × $N_{\text{rows}}$ rows) is transmitted in **MPE-FEC sections** as a separate elementary stream.

**Virtual Decoding at the Receiver:**

At the receiver, the matrix is reconstructed. Due to channel impairments (Doppler, multipath, fading), some columns/rows may be corrupted. The receiver:
1. Fills the **Data Area** with correctly received MPE data.
2. Marks **unknown or corrupted columns** as **erasures**.
3. For each row, applies **RS erasure decoding** — which can correct up to **64 erasures per row** (since $2t = 64$, and erasure decoding can correct up to $n - k = 64$ erasures).
4. The corrected IP datagrams are then handed up to the upper layers.

> [!TIP]
> **Key advantage of Virtual Decoding:** Even if the inner Viterbi decoder fails on a long burst of errors (e.g., 50 ms deep fade), the outer RS code — with its wide 255-byte row — still has a high probability of recovering the data. This makes DVB-H tolerant of the long, deep fades typical in mobile reception.

**Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Head | DVB-T | DVB-H |
|------|-------|-------|
| **(i) Target Application** | Fixed rooftop reception, vehicular TV (cars), set-top boxes | **Low-power mobile handheld devices** (phones, PDAs, PMPs) with small built-in antennas |
| **(ii) Power Consumption** | Continuous reception → tuner ON 100% of the time; **battery dies in < 1 hour** on handheld | **Time Slicing** → tuner ON only ~10% of the time; **battery lasts 4–6 hours**; ~90% power saving |
| **(iii) Error Protection** | Single layer: DVB-T convolutional code (1/2 to 7/8) + outer RS(204,188) | **Two layers:** DVB-T inner code (1/2 to 7/8) + outer RS(204,188) **+ MPE-FEC RS(255,191)** at IP layer; supports **4K OFDM mode** for better Doppler/SFN trade-off |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Many students write "DVB-H has a different modulation scheme than DVB-T" — **this is WRONG**. Both use the *same* OFDM-based DVB-T physical layer. The **only modulation-level difference** is the *optional* addition of the **4K FFT mode** in DVB-H. Students lose 1–2 marks for this misconception. **Memorize:** DVB-H = DVB-T physical layer + Time Slicing + MPE-FEC + 4K mode.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final high-density revision summary — go through this the night before the KTU ESE.**

- ✅ **DVB-H** = **DVB-T + Time Slicing + MPE-FEC + (optional) 4K mode**.
- ✅ Standard: **ETSI EN 302 304**, adopted **2004**.
- ✅ Frequency band: **UHF 470–862 MHz**, channels of 5/6/7/8 MHz.
- ✅ **Time Slicing** saves ~**90% power** by bursting each service at $R_{\text{burst}} \approx 10 \times R_{\text{CBR}}$.
- ✅ **Delta-t** field in MPE header tells the receiver *when* the next burst arrives.
- ✅ **MPE-FEC** uses **RS(255, 191)** over **GF(256)** arranged in a virtual matrix of **191 data + 64 parity columns**.
- ✅ MPE-FEC code rate = $191/255 \approx 0.749$ (overhead ≈ 33.5%).
- ✅ **Virtual decoding** uses RS *erasure* decoding to correct up to **64 erasures/row**.
- ✅ **4K FFT mode** offers 4096 subcarriers — a compromise between 2K (mobile) and 8K (large SFN).
- ✅ OFDM symbol duration (8 MHz channel): **2K = 224 µs, 4K = 448 µs, 8K = 896 µs**.
- ✅ Constellations used: **QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM** (QPSK most common for mobility).
- ✅ DVB-H supports **IP-based services** via MPE encapsulation, enabling convergence with cellular IP networks.
- ✅ **Handover** in DVB-H is possible because the receiver knows *when* and *on which frequency* the next cell's burst will appear (via Delta-t and cell signaling).
- ✅ Successor/related standards: **DVB-T2, DVB-T2 Lite, DVB-NGH, 3GPP eMBMS, 5G Broadcast**.
- ✅ **Common board-exam trap:** DVB-H is *not* a new physical layer — it is an **enhancement of the DVB-T physical layer** with new upper-layer techniques.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DVB Teletext
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DVB Teletext

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**DVB Teletext** is the digital implementation of the legacy EBU (European Broadcasting Union) Teletext system, redefined by the **ETSI EN 300 706** standard, which allows textual, graphical, and subtitle information to be transported within a **DVB (Digital Video Broadcasting) MPEG-2 Transport Stream** as a dedicated **Packetized Elementary Stream (PES)**. It retains the classical magazine–page–row structure of analog teletext but replaces the **VBI (Vertical Blanking Interval)** analog insertion with carriage inside the digital MPEG-2 TS multiplex.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** DVB Teletext is **not** a separate service — it is a *data broadcasting service* piggy-backed on the DVB MPEG-2 transport stream. Each teletext page is a logical entity that the receiver's decoder reassembles from a sequence of PES packets identified by a unique **PID (Packet Identifier)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Newspaper Hidden Inside Your TV Signal"**
> Imagine you are watching a movie in a cinema. The film is the main content (video + audio). Now, hidden inside the same film reel, the projectionist has slipped a thin, transparent, scrolling newspaper. You cannot see it during the movie, but if you pause and turn on a special "reading lamp," the newspaper becomes visible. The cinema manager (broadcaster) decides when each page of the newspaper appears, but you (the viewer) can flip to any page instantly.
> - **DVB Teletext = the digital "newspaper"**
> - **MPEG-2 Transport Stream = the film reel**
> - **PES Packets = the thin transparent sheets**
> - **Receiver Teletext Decoder = the "reading lamp"**

In analog TV, this "newspaper" was physically squeezed into lines **6–22 and 318–335** of the VBI (lines the human eye could not see). In **DVB Teletext**, the same information is repackaged as digital PES packets, allowing **error protection (Hamming codes)**, **faster page access**, and **multilingual subtitle support** without consuming analog bandwidth.

## 1.3 Core Terminology & Constants (Bolded for Emphasis)

| Term | Expansion | Standard / Value |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| **DVB** | Digital Video Broadcasting | ETSI EN 300 744 (DVB-T), EN 300 421 (DVB-S), EN 300 429 (DVB-C) |
| **TS** | Transport Stream | MPEG-2 ISO/IEC 13818-1 |
| **PES** | Packetized Elementary Stream | ISO/IEC 13818-1 |
| **PID** | Packet Identifier | 13-bit field (0–8191) |
| **PMT** | Program Map Table | Identifies PID of teletext stream |
| **VBI** | Vertical Blanking Interval | Lines 6–22 / 318–335 (analog) |
| **ETSI EN 300 706** | DVB Teletext Specification | Enhanced Teletext Standard |
| **EBU** | European Broadcasting Union | Original Teletext specifier |
| **FLOF** | Full Level One Features | Navigation enhancement |
| **TOP** | Table of Pages | Advanced navigation table |
| **Default Cycle Time** | Full magazine refresh | ~25 seconds |
| **Standard Page Size** | 24 rows × 40 columns | 960 characters max |
| **Total Possible Pages** | 8 magazines × 256 pages | **2048 pages per service** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard 8-bit magazine numbering convention reserves magazine number **0** to mean magazine **8** (i.e., magazines are numbered 1–8, with 0 acting as an alias for 8 to preserve hex compatibility).

---

## 1.4 Evolution: From Analog Teletext to DVB Teletext

The journey from analog to digital teletext represents a major leap in efficiency and capability:

| Generation | Era | Medium | Bit Rate per Line | Error Protection |
|------------|-----|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Ceefax / Oracle / Antiope** | 1970s | Analog VBI | ~360 bits/line × 25 lines ≈ **7 Mbit/s effective** | None (Hamming 8/4 on header only) |
| **WST (World System Teletext)** | 1980s | Analog VBI | 360 bits/line | Hamming 8/4 |
| **DVB Teletext (ETSI EN 300 706)** | 1990s–Present | DVB MPEG-2 TS PES | Configurable, often **≈ 6.875 Mbit/s per teletext PES** | Reed–Solomon + Hamming |
| **DVB Subtitling (ETSI EN 300 743)** | 2000s | PES dedicated to subtitles | Variable | Reed–Solomon |
| **TTML / HbbTV** | 2010s+ | IP/Broadband hybrid | Application-driven | IP-level |

> [!NOTE]
> DVB Teletext is therefore best understood as **"Teletext re-engineered for the digital age"** — same data semantics, but a completely new transport layer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a DVB Teletext Service

A DVB Teletext service is logically partitioned into **three architectural planes**, mirroring the OSI-style layering of DVB systems:

1. **Transport Plane (Physical / Link Layer)**
   - MPEG-2 Transport Stream (TS) packetization
   - **188-byte TS packets** with 4-byte header
   - PES layer wraps individual teletext pages
   - Each teletext line (row) typically fills **one TS packet payload** (184 bytes) — but teletext rows are 46 bytes, so multiple teletext rows can share a TS packet
2. **Signaling Plane (Service Description)**
   - **PMT (Program Map Table)** contains a `teletext_descriptor`
   - **SDT (Service Description Table)** names the teletext service
   - **EIT (Event Information Table)** can carry teletext-related event metadata
3. **Application Plane (Content Semantics)**
   - Magazine/Page/Row addressing
   - Character sets (Latin, Cyrillic, Greek, Arabic, Hebrew — per ETSI TS 101 162)
   - Display attributes (colors, flash, conceal, boxing)
   - Navigation aids (FLOF, TOP, packet 26/27/28)

## 2.2 Magazine and Page Numbering Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The addressing system is hierarchical:** Service → Magazine → Page → Sub-page → Row.

**Magazine Number Field** (3 hex digits in 24-bit page address, but displayed as 3-digit decimal):
- Decoded using **Hamming 8/4 code** for the magazine hundreds digit
- Magazines are numbered **1 to 8** in display, but stored as hex where `0` ≡ `8`

**Page Number** is a 3-digit decimal (e.g., page `100`, `225`, `888`):
- Hundreds digit: 1–8 (also Hamming 8/4 protected)
- Tens and units digits: standard BCD

**Sub-page** (a 4-digit hex number from 0000 to FFFF):
- Allows up to **65,536 sub-pages** per page
- Used for rotating information (news headlines, sports scores)

**Row Number**: 1 to 25 (row 0 is the page header)

## 2.3 Page Header (Row 0) Structure

The page header is the most critical 45-byte structure; it tells the decoder **which page** is being broadcast and on which sub-page.

| Bytes | Field | Purpose |
|-------|-------|---------|
| 0–1 | **Clock Run-In (CRI)** | Bit synchronization (8 alternating bits) |
| 2 | **Framing Code** | Byte synchronization (11100100 = 0xE4) |
| 3–4 | **Magazine + Page Address** | Hamming 8/4 protected |
| 5–8 | **Sub-page bits** | Control + Sub-code (S1–S4) |
| 9–10 | **Control bits** (C1–C14) | Update indicator, etc. |
| 11–40 | **Data bytes** | Typically broadcast links, time, network info |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are **KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) ready**. Master these and you can solve any numeric problem the examiner can pose.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Unit | Notes |
|---|---------------------|----------|------|-------|
| 1 | **TS Packet Length** | $L_{TS} = 188$ bytes | bytes | 4-byte header + 184-byte payload |
| 2 | **Sync byte** | $0 \times 47$ | hex | First byte of every TS packet |
| 3 | **PID field width** | $PID \in [0, 8191]$ | — | 13-bit field |
| 4 | **Teletext row data size** | $L_{row} = 45$ bytes | bytes | 2 CRI + 1 framing + 2 addr + 40 data |
| 5 | **Display characters per page** | $N_{char} = 24 \times 40 = 960$ | characters | 24 rows × 40 columns |
| 6 | **Bytes per full page** | $B_{page} = 25 \times 45 = 1125$ | bytes | Including header row |
| 7 | **Magazines per service** | $M = 8$ | — | Numbered 1–8 |
| 8 | **Total pages per service** | $P_{total} = 8 \times 256 = 2048$ | pages | |
| 9 | **Sub-pages per page** | $S = 2^{16} = 65536$ | sub-pages | |
| 10 | **Teletext bit rate (DVB)** | $R_{tt} = 6.875 \text{ Mbit/s}$ (typical PES) | Mbit/s | Depends on PES bandwidth |
| 11 | **Teletext PES packet size** | $L_{PES} \leq 64$ KB | bytes | Per ISO/IEC 13818-1 |
| 12 | **Rows per TS packet** | $N_{rows/pkt} = \lfloor 184 / 45 \rfloor = 4$ | rows | 4 rows + 4 bytes overhead |
| 13 | **Cycle time (default)** | $T_{cycle} \approx 25$ s | seconds | Full magazine refresh |
| 14 | **Acquisition time** | $T_{acq} = T_{cycle} \times \dfrac{P_{requested}}{P_{transmitted}}$ | seconds | Worst case ~25 s |
| 15 | **Parallel transmission factor** | $K = N_{parallel\_transmissions}$ | — | Many broadcasters use K=2 for fast access |
| 16 | **Effective bit rate per row** | $R_{row} = 45 \times 8 \times 25 = 9000$ bps per line basis | bps | Legacy analog WST value |
| 17 | **Hamming 8/4 redundancy** | $4 \text{ data} : 4 \text{ parity}$ | bits | Corrects 1-bit errors per nibble |
| 18 | **Reed-Solomon over TS** | $RS(204, 188, t=8)$ | — | DVB MPE-FEC over TS packets |
| 19 | **PSI/SI table size max** | $L_{PSI} = 1024$ bytes | bytes | Per ISO/IEC 13818-1 |
| 20 | **PCR jitter tolerance** | $\pm 500$ ns | ns | System clock reference |

### 2.4.1 Derived Real-World Formulas

**Time to acquire a single page (worst case):**

$$T_{acq} = \frac{P_{total}}{K \cdot f_{cycle}}$$

where:
- $P_{total}$ = total pages in the service (typically 100–800 in practice, max 2048)
- $K$ = number of parallel page transmissions per cycle
- $f_{cycle}$ = cycle frequency = $1 / T_{cycle}$

**Effective bandwidth consumed by DVB Teletext in the multiplex:**

$$BW_{teletext} = \frac{N_{rows} \cdot 8 \cdot 45}{T_{line}} = \frac{25 \cdot 8 \cdot 45}{64 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 6.875 \text{ Mbit/s}$$

where $T_{line} = 64 \, \mu s$ is the standard PAL line period.

**Bit error rate (BER) reduction by Reed-Solomon:**

$$BER_{out} \approx \left(\frac{BER_{in}}{2}\right)^{t+1}$$

where $t = 8$ for DVB's RS(204,188) — meaning **up to 8 byte errors per TS packet** can be corrected.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

DVB Teletext remains operationally important in the following engineering contexts:

1. **Emergency Warning Systems (EWS)** — broadcasters can wake up receivers and overlay warnings using teletext packets
2. **Closed-Subtitle Delivery for Hearing-Impaired** — sub-pages 0x03 of language-coded teletext
3. **EPG Fallback** — when broadband/IP EPG fails, teletext pages provide schedule data
4. **Low-Literacy / No-Internet Audiences** — rural broadcast receivers still rely on teletext
5. **Regulatory Mandates** — many countries (Germany, UK, Nordic, India DD FreeDish) **legally require** teletext support
6. **Hybrid Networks** — HbbTV applications use teletext-page IDs as interop anchors to legacy receivers

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Teletext Bit Rate in a DVB Multiplex

**Given:**
- Standard PAL line period: $T_{line} = 64 \, \mu s$
- Rows (lines) per field used for teletext (DVB equivalent): $N_{rows} = 25$
- Bytes per teletext row (data portion): $L_{row} = 45$ bytes
- Bits per byte: $b = 8$

**To Find:** Effective bit rate $R_{tt}$ of a single DVB teletext PES occupying its historical VBI bandwidth equivalent.

### Step 1 — Bits per line
Each teletext row carries 45 bytes of structured data.

$$B_{row} = L_{row} \times b = 45 \times 8 = 360 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 2 — Lines per second
A standard 50 Hz PAL field has 312.5 lines per field. But teletext historically uses 25 lines per field, 50 fields per second (interlaced):

$$f_{lines} = 25 \times 50 = 1250 \text{ lines/second}$$

### Step 3 — Total bit rate
Multiply bits per line by lines per second:

$$R_{tt} = B_{row} \times f_{lines}$$

$$R_{tt} = 360 \times 1250$$

$$R_{tt} = 450{,}000 \text{ bits/second per teletext service line-group}$$

### Step 4 — Scaling to 25-row full-magazine transmission
This is the per-line figure. To obtain the aggregate DVB teletext PES rate that mimics full-bandwidth analog WST:

$$R_{PES} = 360 \times 25 \times 50 = 450{,}000 \text{ bps}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction Note:** The widely-quoted 6.875 Mbit/s figure is the **total PES bit rate when a broadcaster chooses to allocate a full PES bandwidth** to teletext, equivalent to all 625 lines being filled. For practical VBI-equivalent operation, the rate is **≈ 0.45 Mbit/s**.

### Step 5 — Bandwidth share of a typical DVB-T 8 MHz channel
A standard DVB-T 8 MHz channel carries about **24–31 Mbit/s** depending on modulation (QPSK → 64-QAM):

$$BW_{share} = \frac{R_{tt}}{R_{DVB-T}} \times 100\%$$

For 64-QAM, $R_{DVB-T} \approx 24.88$ Mbit/s:

$$BW_{share} = \frac{0.45}{24.88} \times 100\% \approx 1.81\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of derivation:** DVB Teletext consumes **less than 2% of an 8 MHz DVB-T multiplex** while providing 2048 addressable pages. This is the engineering reason teletext remains the most bandwidth-efficient metadata system in broadcasting.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Page Acquisition Time

**Given:**
- Service contains $P_{total} = 800$ pages (typical large teletext service)
- Cycle time $T_{cycle} = 25$ s
- $K = 2$ parallel page transmissions per cycle (broadcasters commonly send the popular pages twice)

**To Find:** Worst-case $T_{acq}$ for a random page.

### Step 1 — Define cycle frequency
$$f_{cycle} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{1}{25} = 0.04 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 2 — Apply acquisition formula
$$T_{acq} = \frac{P_{total}}{K \cdot f_{cycle}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute values
$$T_{acq} = \frac{800}{2 \times 0.04}$$

$$T_{acq} = \frac{800}{0.08}$$

$$T_{acq} = 10{,}000 \text{ seconds} \approx 2.78 \text{ hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Wait — that is huge!** In practice, **acquisition is bounded by the magazine cycle, not the full service**: a single magazine is 256 pages, refreshed in ~25 s. So real acquisition time is:
> $$T_{acq}^{practical} \approx \frac{256}{K} \times T_{cycle} = \frac{256}{2} \times 1 \text{ (s per page)} = 128 \text{ s} \approx 2.13 \text{ min}$$
> Examiners test this nuance: *always check whether the question is "magazine acquisition" or "service-wide acquisition."*

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Carriage Mechanisms

| Property | Analog VBI Teletext (WST) | DVB Teletext (PES) | DVB Subtitling (ETSI EN 300 743) | HbbTV / TTML |
|----------|---------------------------|--------------------|----------------------------------|---------------|
| **Standard** | ETSI EN 300 706 (legacy) | ETSI EN 300 706 (DVB mode) | ETSI EN 300 743 | ETSI TS 102 796 |
| **Transport** | Analog VBI lines | MPEG-2 TS PES | MPEG-2 TS PES | IP over broadband |
| **Bandwidth** | 1 VBI line = 45 B | Full PES | Display-only PES | Variable |
| **Error Protection** | Hamming 8/4 | RS + Hamming | RS | TLS / IP layer |
| **Max Pages** | 2048 | 2048 | N/A (subtitle only) | Unlimited (app-driven) |
| **Subtitles** | Yes (sub-page 0x03) | Yes | Yes (display-set optimized) | Yes (TTML) |
| **Graphics** | Limited (Mosaic, DRCS) | Same | Yes (clut-indexed) | Full SVG/HTML |
| **Interactivity** | None | None | Limited (composition) | Full (HTML/JS) |
| **Cycle Time** | 25 s | Configurable | Streamed | Real-time |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 ESE Style)

**Problem:** A DVB-T service carries a teletext PES with bit rate $R_{tt} = 0.45$ Mbit/s inside an 8 MHz channel with $R_{DVB-T} = 24.88$ Mbit/s. A page contains 25 rows of 45 bytes each. Compute:
(a) Total bytes per page
(b) Pages transmitted per second
(c) Time to transmit 100 distinct pages sequentially
(d) Percentage of channel bandwidth used by teletext

**Solution:**

**(a) Bytes per page:**
$$B_{page} = N_{rows} \times L_{row} = 25 \times 45 = 1125 \text{ bytes}$$

**(b) Pages per second:**
$$f_{pages} = \frac{R_{tt}}{B_{page} \times 8} = \frac{450{,}000}{1125 \times 8} = \frac{450{,}000}{9000} = 50 \text{ pages/second}$$

**(c) Time for 100 distinct pages:**
$$T_{100} = \frac{100}{f_{pages}} = \frac{100}{50} = 2 \text{ seconds}$$

**(d) Bandwidth percentage:**
$$P_{tt} = \frac{0.45}{24.88} \times 100\% \approx 1.81\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The above numerical problem is a *classic* KTU-style 7-mark sub-question. Memorize the derivation flow.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define DVB Teletext. How is it different from analog WST teletext?*

**Model Answer:**

**DVB Teletext** is the digital implementation of the European Broadcasting Union (EBU) teletext specification, standardized under **ETSI EN 300 706**, which carries teletext data as a **Packetized Elementary Stream (PES)** inside an MPEG-2 Transport Stream of a DVB broadcast.

**Key differences from analog WST teletext:**

| Aspect | Analog WST | DVB Teletext |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| **Transport** | VBI lines 6–22 / 318–335 | MPEG-2 TS PES packets |
| **Multiplexing** | Single line per field | Multiple pages per PES |
| **Error protection** | Hamming 8/4 only | Reed–Solomon + Hamming |
| **Bandwidth efficiency** | Fixed (1 line = 45 B) | Configurable PES bit rate |
| **Subtitles** | Teletext sub-page 0x03 | Native PES + dedicated subtitle PES (EN 300 743) |
| **Page addressing** | 3-digit decimal | Same, but inside PES framing |
| **Reception quality** | Degrades with signal | Near error-free with RS |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for comparison)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Explain the function of the teletext_descriptor in the PMT of a DVB Transport Stream.*

**Model Answer:**

The **teletext_descriptor** is a metadata tag inside the **Program Map Table (PMT)** of the MPEG-2 Transport Stream. Its functions are:

1. **Identifies** the elementary stream PID that carries teletext data
2. **Specifies the teletext type**:
   - `0x01` = Initial Teletext page
   - `0x02` = Teletext additional page
   - `0x03` = Teletext subtitle page
   - `0x04` = Teletext subtitle for hearing impaired
3. **Indicates the language** using a 24-bit ISO 639-2 language code (e.g., `eng`, `mal`, `hin`)
4. **Provides the magazine number** (3 bits, range 0–7, where 0 = magazine 8)
5. **Provides the page number** (5 bits + 4 bits for tens/units — giving 0–99 in BCD)

**Format (per ETSI EN 300 468):**
```
descriptor_tag = 0x56
descriptor_length = N
for i = 1 to N:
    reserved (3 bits)  | teletext_type (5 bits)
    reserved (3 bits)  | magazine_number (3 bits)
    page_number (2 bits tens, 4 bits units)
    language_code (24 bits)
```

*(3 marks: 1 mark descriptor purpose + 1 mark fields + 1 mark language/type tagging)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**(a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of a DVB Teletext transmission system. Discuss the role of the teletext_descriptor in the PMT. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A DVB-T service carries a teletext PES at 0.45 Mbit/s. Each teletext page has 25 rows of 45 bytes. Compute:
- (i) Bytes per page
- (ii) Pages transmitted per second
- (iii) Acquisition time for 256 pages with K=2 parallel transmission
- (iv) Bandwidth share if total DVB-T capacity is 24.88 Mbit/s *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 marks)

**Block Diagram of DVB Teletext Transmission System:**

```
Content Source → Teletext Encoder (ETSI EN 300 706)
              → Page Builder (Magazine 1–8, Page 000–FF)
              → PES Packetizer (ISO/IEC 13818-1)
              → MPEG-2 TS Multiplexer (188-byte packets)
              → Channel Coding (Reed–Solomon RS 204,188)
              → Interleaver + Modulator (DVB-T COFDM)
              → Transmit Antenna
```

**Role of each block:**

1. **Content Source:** Editorial system producing news, sports, subtitles
2. **Teletext Encoder:** Converts text into rows, applies Hamming 8/4 protection, builds magazine structure
3. **Page Builder:** Assigns page numbers, organizes sub-pages
4. **PES Packetizer:** Wraps pages into PES packets with `stream_id = 0xBD` (private stream 1)
5. **TS Multiplexer:** Inserts PES into 188-byte TS packets, applies PID
6. **Channel Coding:** RS(204,188) corrects up to 8 byte errors per TS packet
7. **Modulator:** COFDM for DVB-T, QPSK for DVB-S

**Role of `teletext_descriptor` in PMT:**

- The PMT entry for the video/audio elementary stream includes a `teletext_descriptor` (tag `0x56`)
- It tells the receiver **which PID** carries teletext
- It declares the **type** (initial/subtitle/HOH) and **language**
- Without it, the receiver cannot locate or interpret the teletext PES

*(7 marks: 3 for diagram + 2 for explanation + 2 for descriptor role)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 marks)

**(i) Bytes per page:**
$$B_{page} = 25 \times 45 = 1125 \text{ bytes}$$

**(ii) Pages per second:**
$$f_{pages} = \frac{R_{tt}}{B_{page} \times 8} = \frac{450{,}000}{9000} = 50 \text{ pages/second}$$

**(iii) Acquisition time for 256 pages with K=2:**
$$T_{acq} = \frac{256}{2 \times 50} = \frac{256}{100} = 2.56 \text{ seconds}$$

**(iv) Bandwidth share:**
$$P_{tt} = \frac{0.45}{24.88} \times 100\% \approx 1.81\%$$

*(7 marks: 1.5 + 2 + 2 + 1.5 distribution)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Describe the magazine and page addressing scheme of DVB Teletext. Why is magazine `0` aliased to magazine `8`? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare DVB Teletext with DVB Subtitling (ETSI EN 300 743). Discuss the role of Hamming 8/4 code in the page header. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 marks)

**Magazine and Page Addressing:**

A DVB Teletext service is divided into **8 magazines**, numbered 1 to 8. Each magazine contains up to **256 pages**, numbered 0x00 to 0xFF (displayed as 3-digit decimal, e.g., 100, 256, 888).

- A page is uniquely identified by `(Magazine, Page)` pair
- The address is encoded in **row 0 (page header)** as 24 bits
- The **magazine hundreds digit** and the **page hundreds digit** are both protected by **Hamming 8/4** codes

**Why magazine 0 = magazine 8?**

The 3-digit page number is represented in **hexadecimal for the hundreds digit only**. Since 3 bits can only encode 0–7, but we need 8 magazines (1–8), the value `0b000` (decimal 0) is **aliased to magazine 8**. This allows the address to remain in 3 bits while supporting all 8 magazines.

**Hierarchy:** Service → Magazine → Page → Sub-page (16 bits, up to 65,536) → Row (0–25)

*(7 marks: 3 for scheme + 2 for hex-aliasing + 2 for hierarchy)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (DVB Teletext vs DVB Subtitling):**

| Feature | DVB Teletext (EN 300 706) | DVB Subtitling (EN 300 743) |
|---------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Purpose** | News, info, subtitles | Subtitles only (no info pages) |
| **Composition** | 24 rows × 40 columns | Display set + region composition |
| **Page count** | 2048 max | Unlimited subtitle tracks |
| **Graphics** | Mosaic, DRCS (low-res) | Clut-indexed full graphics |
| **Ergonomics** | Page-flip navigation | Auto-displayed with audio track |
| **Storage** | Receiver caches pages | Streamed line by line |
| **Use case** | Multi-purpose | Pure accessibility |

**Role of Hamming 8/4 Code in Page Header:**

- The Hamming 8/4 code encodes 4 data bits into 8 transmitted bits (50% redundancy)
- It is applied to the **magazine address** and the **page hundreds digit** in row 0
- **Capability:** corrects any **single-bit error** and detects all **2-bit errors** within the 8-bit block
- This is critical because if the page address is corrupted, the decoder might discard an otherwise valid page

**Mathematical form:**
$$H = [d_3 d_2 d_1 d_0 \mid p_3 p_2 p_1 p_0]$$
where parity bits are computed as:
$$p_0 = d_0 \oplus d_1 \oplus d_2, \quad p_1 = d_1 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_3, \quad p_2 = d_0 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_3, \quad p_3 = d_0 \oplus d_1 \oplus d_3$$

*(7 marks: 3 for comparison + 2 for Hamming principle + 2 for mathematical form)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Student Beware!)**
>
> 1. **Confusing teletext with subtitles** — DVB Teletext is a *general-purpose* data broadcasting service; subtitles are merely *one* use (sub-page 0x03). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you call teletext "a subtitle system."
>
> 2. **Wrong magazine count** — Students often write "16 magazines" — it is **exactly 8** (with 0 aliased to 8).
>
> 3. **Forgetting `teletext_descriptor` tag** — The descriptor tag for teletext is **`0x56`**, not `0x6A` (which is subtitling_descriptor). Mixing these costs 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Wrong cycle time** — Default magazine cycle is **~25 seconds**, not 10 s, not 60 s. Memorize this.
>
> 5. **Row numbering error** — Teletext has **25 rows** (0–24, with 0 being the header), **not 24 rows**. Many textbooks slip on this.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Hamming protection explanation** — Examiners allocate explicit marks for the Hamming 8/4 mechanism on the page header.
>
> 7. **Confusing PID with stream_id** — `stream_id = 0xBD` (PES level); `PID` is in the TS header. These are **two different identifiers** in two different headers.
>
> 8. **Missing the magazine 0 = 8 aliasing** — Direct 2-mark question in past papers. Always explain this when discussing addressing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**
>
> - **Standard:** ETSI EN 300 706 defines DVB Teletext
> - **Transport:** MPEG-2 TS PES (private_stream_1, stream_id = 0xBD)
> - **Descriptor tag:** `0x56` for teletext_descriptor in PMT
> - **Magazines:** **8** (1–8, with 0 aliased to 8)
> - **Pages per magazine:** 256 (00–FF)
> - **Total pages per service:** **2048** (max)
> - **Sub-pages per page:** 65,536 (16-bit field)
> - **Rows per page:** **25** (row 0 = header, rows 1–24 = display)
> - **Columns per row:** 40
> - **Bytes per row:** 45 (2 CRI + 1 framing + 2 address + 40 data)
> - **Bytes per page:** 25 × 45 = **1125 bytes**
> - **Default cycle time:** ~25 seconds
> - **Effective teletext PES rate:** ~0.45 Mbit/s (VBI-equivalent)
> - **Hamming 8/4:** applied to magazine + page hundreds digit
> - **Reed-Solomon:** RS(204,188) at TS layer, corrects 8 byte errors
> - **VBI line range (legacy analog):** 6–22 / 318–335
> - **Line period:** 64 µs (PAL)
> - **Subtitle identifier:** sub-page 0x03 of a teletext service
> - **Hearing-impaired subtitle:** `teletext_type = 0x04`
> - **Teletext PES data_unit_id:** `0x02` (EBU Teletext) or `0x03` (inverted)
> - **Field parity bit:** 0 = first field, 1 = second field
> - **Line offset:** 5 bits indicating original VBI line position
> - **Framing code:** `0xE4` (binary 11100100) — 8 bits
> - **Display characters:** 24 × 40 = 960 max per page
> - **Character sets:** Latin, Cyrillic, Greek, Arabic, Hebrew (per ETSI TS 101 162)
> - **Navigation enhancements:** FLOF (Full Level One Features) and TOP (Table of Pages)
> - **MHP (Multimedia Home Platform):** successor platform; DVB Teletext is **not** interactive
> - **HbbTV:** modern hybrid; uses teletext page numbers as legacy-anchor interop points
> - **Engineering utility:** EWS, EPG fallback, regulatory mandate, low-cost delivery
> - **Bandwidth share:** < 2% of an 8 MHz DVB-T channel
> - **Key formula:** $T_{acq} = \dfrac{P}{K \cdot f_{cycle}}$
> - **Key formula:** $BW_{tt} \approx 6.875 \text{ Mbit/s max}$ per PES, ~0.45 Mbit/s practical
> - **Comparison anchor:** Teletext is general-purpose data; Subtitling (EN 300 743) is display-only
> - **Exam mantra:** *"Teletext is data, not just subtitles; carried as PES, not VBI."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DVB Subtitling System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DVB Subtitling System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **DVB (Digital Video Broadcasting) Subtitling System** is a standardized bitmap-based subtitle transport architecture defined by the **ETSI EN 300 743** specification. It provides a method for delivering multi-language, region-based graphical subtitle data (text rendered as bitmaps, not character codes) for digital television programs carried over **MPEG-2 Transport Streams (TS)**. Each subtitle stream is packetized as a **PES (Packetized Elementary Stream)** and is composed of hierarchical units called **Display Sets**, **Epochs**, and **Segments**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The official standard for DVB Subtitling is **ETSI EN 300 743**, which describes the syntax, semantics, and timing required to deliver subtitles within a DVB MPEG-2 TS. The DVB subtitle decoder is mandatory in all **iDTV (integrated Digital TV)** receivers sold in Europe under the **DVB-MHP (Multimedia Home Platform)** profile.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are watching a stage play. The actors are on stage (the decoded video frame), and the **subtitles are like transparent slides** placed on top of a glass screen, each slide showing one line of dialogue in a specific language with specific colors and font sizes.

- The **stage** = the decoded MPEG-2 video frame.
- Each **transparent slide** = a **Region** (a rectangular area of the screen).
- A **scene in the play** = an **Epoch** (a continuous time during which the subtitle visual state is stable).
- The **director cueing a new slide** = the arrival of a new **Display Set** telling the decoder to update the regions.
- The **reel of slides being delivered to the cinema** = a **PES stream** flowing in the MPEG-2 transport stream.

The system is **bitmap-based**, which means the broadcaster has already pre-rendered the glyphs (characters) at the head-end. The set-top box simply **blits** (bit-block transfers) the received pictures onto the screen — this is why DVB subtitles look **identical on every TV**, regardless of the receiver’s font quality.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

| Constant / Metric | Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **ETSI Standard Reference** | **EN 300 743 (V1.6.1 – latest)** | Governing specification for DVB subtitles |
| **Pixel Resolution (SD)** | up to **720 × 576** | Standard definition composition page |
| **Pixel Resolution (HD)** | up to **1920 × 1080** | High definition composition page |
| **MPEG-2 PCR Clock** | **90 kHz** | Time-base for all display_set_timing fields |
| **Stream Type ID** | **0x06** in PMT | Identifies the PES as a DVB subtitle stream |
| **Maximum Regions per Epoch** | Practically **2–3** for dialogue | Defined in the Composition Descriptor |
| **Supported Pixel Depths** | **2-bit, 4-bit, 8-bit (CLUT indexed)** | CLUT-based indexed color model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why bitmap subtitles (and not text)?** Early DVB-MHP receivers had inconsistent font rasterizers. By sending **pre-rasterized** glyphs, broadcasters guarantee visual fidelity — a critical requirement for the **DVB-T2 / DVB-S2 / DVB-C2** broadcast chains serving **SDT (Subtitling for the Deaf and Hard of Hearing)** audiences.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Layers of a DVB Subtitle Stream

The DVB subtitling system has a **five-level logical hierarchy** that mirrors how subtitles are produced, assembled, and rendered:

1. **Subtitle Service** — Identified in the **PMT (Program Map Table)** via a *Subtitling Descriptor* (tag = 0x59).
2. **Display Set** — The atomic update unit; contains all segments needed to render one new subtitle layout.
3. **Epoch** — A time window during which the visual state is constant. Each Epoch is bounded by a `display_set_timing` field and ends when a *new* Display Set arrives (or after the `page_time_out` if the broadcaster uses page-based timeouts).
4. **Composition Page** — A virtual 2D canvas (up to HD resolution) on which **Regions** are placed.
5. **Region** — A rectangular block of pixels (clipped, colored) containing either **text/character objects** or **bitmaps**.

### 2.2 The Five Segment Types (Mandatory KTU Knowledge)

Every Display Set is composed of five logical segment types. KTU examiners frequently test the **purpose of each segment**:

| Segment | Code (Hex) | Function | KTU Quick Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PCS** (Page Composition Segment) | `0x10` | Defines **regions**, their **position** (X, Y) on the composition page, and their **depth ordering** (foreground/background) | Always the **first** segment in a Display Set |
| **RDS / RCS** (Region Composition Segment) | `0x11` | Defines **width, height, fill colors, and the pixel depth** of a specific region | One RDS per region in the Display Set |
| **CDS** (CLUT Definition Segment) | `0x12` | Maps **CLUT entries** to actual 24-bit RGB colors | Allows broadcaster-defined color palettes |
| **ODS** (Object Data Segment) | `0x13` | Carries the **actual pixel data** (run-length encoded bitmap) of each region | Largest payload segment in the stream |
| **EDS** (End of Display Set) | `0x80` | Sentinel marking the **logical end** of a Display Set | Mandatory; signals decoder to commit update |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RLE (Run-Length Encoding):** The ODS data is not raw pixel data. It is compressed using a **run-length encoded bitmap** scheme where repeated identical pixel values are replaced by a `run_length_2x` or `run_length_4x` token. This is critical for saving bandwidth in **DVB-T2 SFN (Single Frequency Network)** deployments where the TS bit-rate is a constrained resource.

### 2.3 Display Update Lifecycle — Step by Step

The decoder maintains a finite state machine. The flow is:

1. Decoder detects PES stream_id = `0x20`–`0x3F` (private stream 1) with stream_type = `0x06` in PMT.
2. Demux strips off the PES header and feeds the payload to the **subtitle parser**.
3. Parser looks for PCS — this is the **routing map** telling the decoder *which regions exist and where*.
4. Parser matches RCS, CDS, ODS segments to regions declared in PCS.
5. When **EDS** is received, the decoder **atomically swaps** the new regions onto the screen → this is the **composition update**.
6. The previous Epoch's regions are **flushed** (erased) and the new ones remain visible until the **next EDS** arrives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Atomicity Guarantee:** A Display Set is rendered only when the **EDS** marker is seen. This prevents partial / glitchy subtitles from being displayed if any segment in the Display Set is lost — a frequent KTU exam question.

### 2.4 Comparison: Teletext Subtitles vs DVB Subtitles

| Feature | **Teletext (EBU Teletext)** | **DVB Subtitles (EN 300 743)** |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | Embedded in VBI / PES (ETSI EN 300 472) | Native MPEG-2 PES (ETSI EN 300 743) |
| Display Mode | **Character-based** (decoded fonts) | **Bitmap-based** (pre-rasterized) |
| Standard | EBU Tech 3264 | **EN 300 743** |
| Fonts | Receiver-dependent (variable quality) | **Identical on all receivers** |
| Color Depth | 1-bit monochrome or 4-color CLUT | 2-bit, 4-bit, or 8-bit CLUT |
| Anti-aliasing | No (aliasing artifacts) | **Yes** (rendered at broadcast) |
| Languages per Stream | One language per teletext magazine | **Multiple languages** (one PES per language) |
| SDT (Deaf) support | Limited | **Native** (via composition page_id) |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relationship | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **PCR time units → seconds** | $t_{sec} = \dfrac{t_{90kHz}}{90\,000}$ | Converting MPEG-2 90 kHz clock to wall time |
| **Subtitle bandwidth (worst case)** | $BW_{subs} = \dfrac{N_{bytes\_per\_DS} \times f_{DS}}{T_{epoch}}$ | Estimating PES bitrate for stream budgeting |
| **Region pixel count** | $P_{region} = W_{region} \times H_{region} \times 2^{b_{depth}}$ | Sizing the ODS payload |
| **Compressed ODS size (RLE)** | $S_{ODS} \leq P_{region}$ (upper bound; with RLE typically $0.3 P_{region}$ to $0.7 P_{region}$) | Practical bandwidth sizing for HD subtitles |
| **CLUT entries** | $N_{CLUT} = 2^{b_{depth}}$ where $b_{depth} \in \{2, 4, 8\}$ | Validating CLUT payload length |
| **Region position offset (HD)** | $X \in [0, 1919]$, $Y \in [0, 1079]$ | Boundary check for region placement |
| **Epoch duration** | $T_{epoch} = t_{DS2} - t_{DS1}$ in seconds | Measuring subtitle dwell time |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** When asked for the **CLUT size**, students often write $16$ for 4-bit depth and $256$ for 8-bit depth. Both are correct, but the **CDS segment length** is always a **multiple of 4 bytes** because each CLUT entry is exactly **4 bytes** (R, G, B, transparency/full_range flags). So $N_{CLUT} \times 4$ is the byte count you must state.

### 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Broadcast Engineering:** DVB subtitling is the only mechanism in Europe that meets the **EBU R 128** accessibility quota for **SDT (Subtitles for the Deaf and Telly-hearing impaired)**.
- **IPTV & OTT:** The same EN 300 743 PES structure is reused inside **DVB-DASH (ISO/IEC 23009-1)** CMAF containers, making the knowledge directly applicable to **OTT app development**.
- **Set-top Box Firmware:** The PES parser, segment router, and CLUT blender are core sub-modules of any **iDTV middleware** (e.g., ETSI TS 102 812 / HbbTV).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Epoch Duration from Display Set Timestamps

The MPEG-2 PCR clock runs at exactly $90\,000$ ticks/second. Each `display_set_timing` field stores a **horizontal_display_set_time** (the time the display set should be shown). Derive the wall-clock duration:

**Step 1 —** Record the value of `display_set_timing` when Display Set **DS1** arrives: denote it $t_{DS1}$ (units: 90 kHz ticks).

**Step 2 —** Record the value when the next Display Set **DS2** arrives: denote it $t_{DS2}$.

**Step 3 —** The Epoch during which DS1 is visible has duration (in MPEG clock ticks):

$$
\Delta t_{ticks} = t_{DS2} - t_{DS1}
$$

**Step 4 —** Convert to seconds by dividing by the 90 kHz clock frequency:

$$
T_{epoch} = \dfrac{\Delta t_{ticks}}{90\,000}
$$

**Step 5 —** Convert to milliseconds for HMI / UI display:

$$
T_{epoch}^{ms} = \dfrac{\Delta t_{ticks}}{90}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Dividing by 90 yields ms because $90\,000 \text{ Hz} \div 1000 = 90$. This is a **high-frequency KTU short-answer type** calculation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Required PES Bitrate for an SD DVB Subtitle

Suppose a broadcast uses **one Region**, **4-bit pixel depth**, dimensions of $720 \times 60$ (a single-line banner subtitle at SD), updated **twice per second** (i.e., $f_{DS} = 2$ Hz). Compute the required PES bitrate.

**Step 1 —** Compute raw pixel count per region:

$$
P_{region} = W \times H = 720 \times 60 = 43\,200 \text{ pixels}
$$

**Step 2 —** Convert pixel count to bytes (4-bit depth = 2 pixels per byte):

$$
S_{pixels\_bytes} = \dfrac{P_{region}}{2} = \dfrac{43\,200}{2} = 21\,600 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 —** Apply the typical RLE compression ratio for Latin glyphs. Conservative factor: $0.5$:

$$
S_{ODS} \approx 0.5 \times 21\,600 = 10\,800 \text{ bytes per Display Set}
$$

**Step 4 —** Add overheads (PCS, RDS, CDS, EDS, PES headers — typical combined = 64 bytes):

$$
S_{DS\_total} = 10\,800 + 64 = 10\,864 \text{ bytes per DS}
$$

**Step 5 —** Compute the required PES bitrate at $f_{DS} = 2$ Hz:

$$
R_{PES} = S_{DS\_total} \times 8 \text{ bits} \times f_{DS}
$$

$$
R_{PES} = 10\,864 \times 8 \times 2 = 173\,824 \text{ bps} \approx 170 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 6 —** Convert to kbps for the PMT bandwidth allocation:

$$
R_{PES}^{kbps} = \dfrac{R_{PES}}{1000} \approx 173.8 \text{ kbps}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is why broadcast-grade encoders can fit an entire 3-language multilingual SDT bundle (English + Hindi + Malayalam, for example) inside **~500 kbps** of the 4–5 Mbps total DVB-T2 video bitrate.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Region Position Offset Validation (HD Mode)

A region is defined by **X, Y** (top-left origin) and **W, H**. For HD composition, the page is $1920 \times 1080$. The boundary conditions are:

$$
0 \leq X \leq 1920 - W
$$

$$
0 \leq Y \leq 1080 - H
$$

**Worked example:** Subtitle region of size $1600 \times 80$ placed at the bottom of an HD screen.

**Step 1 —** Compute the maximum legal X:

$$
X_{max} = 1920 - 1600 = 320
$$

**Step 2 —** Compute the maximum legal Y (bottom-aligned):

$$
Y_{max} = 1080 - 80 = 1000
$$

**Step 3 —** A typical **bottom-centered** layout uses:

$$
X = 160, \quad Y = 1000
$$

**Step 4 —** Verify:

$$
X + W = 160 + 1600 = 1760 \le 1920 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
Y + H = 1000 + 80 = 1080 \le 1080 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Segment Validation Rules

| Validation Rule | Formula / Condition | Failure Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **ODS referenced region exists in PCS** | $\text{region\_id}_{ODS} \in \text{region\_id}_{PCS}$ | Decoder discards Display Set silently |
| **CLUT entry count** | $N_{CLUT} = 2^{b_{depth}}$ | CDS parse error → region rendered black |
| **RDS horizontal/vertical extent** | $W_{region} \le 1920$, $H_{region} \le 1080$ | Region clipped at decoder side |
| **Display Set atomicity** | EDS must be present | Update ignored until next DS |
| **PES stream_type** | $= 0x06$ | Stream filtered out by demux |
| **PTS validity** | $PTS_{subtitle} \approx PTS_{video}$ | Subtitle out of sync with dialogue |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — DVB Subtitle System Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["MPEG 2 Transport Stream Input"] --> nodeB["Demultiplexer PES Filter Stream Type 0x06"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["PES Header Parser PTS Extraction"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Segment Router"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["PCS Page Composition Segment"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["RCS Region Composition Segment"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["CDS CLUT Definition Segment"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["ODS Object Data Segment RLE Bitmap"]
    nodeD --> nodeI["EDS End of Display Set Marker"]
    nodeE --> nodeJ["Composition Engine"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Frame Buffer Blender"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["SDHD Video Output Frame"]
    nodeC --> nodeM["Epoch Timer 90 kHz PCR Clock"]
    nodeM --> nodeJ
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **Demultiplexer** filters the transport stream for PES packets whose `stream_type = 0x06`. The PES payload is then routed to the **Segment Router** based on the segment type byte. The **Composition Engine** waits for the **EDS** sentinel and then **atomically** pushes the assembled regions into the **Frame Buffer Blender**, which mixes them on top of the decoded video frame.

---

### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Display Set Update State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> ParsingPCS: PCS segment received
    ParsingPCS --> ParsingRCS: RCS segment received
    ParsingRCS --> ParsingCDS: CDS segment received
    ParsingCDS --> ParsingODS: ODS segment received
    ParsingODS --> ParsingMoreODS: additional ODS
    ParsingMoreODS --> ParsingODS: more ODS in DS
    ParsingMoreODS --> CommitEpoch: EDS received
    ParsingODS --> CommitEpoch: EDS received
    CommitEpoch --> Idle: epoch visible
    Idle --> Flush: timeout or next DS
    Flush --> Idle
```

---

### 4.3 Hierarchy Diagram — Service to Region Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Subtitle Service ID in PMT descriptor 0x59"] --> nodeB["PES Stream type 0x06"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Display Set Atomic Update Unit"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Epoch Time Window"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Composition Page Virtual Canvas 1920x1080"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Region A Foreground Text"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Region B Background Logo"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["ODS Bitmap Pixels"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["RDS Geometry"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["CDS Color Palette"]
```

> **Design takeaway:** The hierarchy is **strictly one-to-many downward** — a single Display Set contains exactly one Epoch which owns one Composition Page that hosts multiple Regions. This tree is the structure expected in any **KTU 14-mark question** asking for the DVB subtitle hierarchy.

---

### 4.4 Packetization Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Subtitle Elementary Stream ES at head end"] --> nodeB["PES Packizer 32 byte header"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["MPEG 2 TS Packager 188 byte packets"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["DVB MUX Multiplexer"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["DVB Modulator DVB T2 S2 C2"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Channel Air Cable Satellite"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Receiver Demodulator"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Set Top Box Subtitle Decoder"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the DVB subtitling system. Name the ETSI standard that defines it and state the PES stream_type identifier used to carry it in an MPEG-2 Transport Stream.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **DVB Subtitling System** is a bitmap-based subtitle delivery mechanism that carries pre-rendered text and graphics as PES packets within a **DVB MPEG-2 Transport Stream**, allowing multi-language, region-based, and **SDT (Subtitles for the Deaf and Hard of Hearing)** capable captioning for digital TV services.

- **Governing Standard:** **ETSI EN 300 743** (1 mark)
- **PES stream_type identifier:** **0x06** declared in the **PMT** (1 mark)
- **Bitmap nature + multi-language capability** (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Differentiate between Teletext subtitling and DVB subtitling in terms of (i) transport mechanism, (ii) rendering technique, and (iii) the standard document governing each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Teletext Subtitling | DVB Subtitling |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Transport | **VBI (Vertical Blanking Interval)** of analogue signal or PES in DVB (ETSI EN 300 472) | Native MPEG-2 PES (ETSI EN 300 743) |
| (ii) Rendering | **Character-based** – decoder renders fonts from received character codes | **Bitmap-based** – pre-rasterized pixels sent by broadcaster |
| (iii) Standard | **EBU Tech 3264** (teletext), EN 300 472 (transport) | **ETSI EN 300 743** |

(1 mark per correct row)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Questions (ESE Pattern, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 ESE Style]**
*(a)* Explain the architecture of the DVB Subtitling System with a neat block diagram. Mention the role of the five segment types. (7 marks)
*(b)* Describe the **Page Composition Segment (PCS)** in detail, including its syntax fields and how the decoder uses it to position regions. (7 marks)

---

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

The DVB Subtitling System uses a **hierarchical PES structure** to deliver pre-rasterized bitmap subtitles over MPEG-2 TS.

1. **PMT Signaling (1 mark):** The **PMT entry** for the subtitle service uses `stream_type = 0x06`. A **Subtitling Descriptor (tag 0x59)** indicates the language code, composition page_id, and ancillary page_id.

2. **PES Demux (1 mark):** The receiver's demux filters PES packets with `stream_id` in the private_stream_1 range. The **PES header** carries a **PTS (Presentation Time Stamp)** synchronized to the video PCR.

3. **Five Logical Segments (3 marks):**
   - **PCS (0x10)** — defines the composition page (regions, positions, depths).
   - **RCS (0x11)** — defines region geometry and fill colors.
   - **CDS (0x12)** — defines the CLUT color table.
   - **ODS (0x13)** — carries the actual **RLE-compressed bitmap** pixels.
   - **EDS (0x80)** — marks the end of a Display Set, triggering atomic update.

4. **Composition Engine & Display (2 marks):** Upon receiving the EDS, the decoder commits the new regions to the frame buffer, blended over the decoded video using alpha-blending per CLUT entry.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

The **PCS** is the routing map of a Display Set. Its key fields (per EN 300 743 §7.2.1) are:

1. `page_id` (1 mark) — A 16-bit identifier binding the PCS to a composition page declared in the **Subtitling Descriptor** in the PMT.
2. `page_time_out` (1 mark) — Optional field specifying how long the page should remain visible (in seconds) if no new DS arrives.
3. `page_version_number` (1 mark) — Bumped when the composition (region layout) changes; allows the decoder to detect updates even if PCS data is identical.
4. `region_composition_segment_address` (1 mark) — The byte offset within the PES payload where the first RCS segment can be found.
5. **Region list** (3 marks) — A sequence of region descriptors, each containing:
   - `region_id` — links to RCS and ODS.
   - `region_horizontal_address` (X coordinate on the composition page).
   - `region_vertical_address` (Y coordinate).
   - `region_depth` — the Z-order; defines whether the region is rendered in front of or behind other regions.

The decoder uses PCS data to **allocate region buffers** in memory and **calculate pixel positions** on the screen, before any pixel data from the ODS arrives.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 ESE Style]**
*(a)* With a neat diagram, explain the concept of **Display Sets, Epochs, and the Atomic Update Mechanism** in the DVB subtitling system. (7 marks)
*(b)* Discuss the **PES packetization** of DVB subtitles and explain the function of the **Subtitling Descriptor (tag 0x59)**. (7 marks)

---

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Display Set (2 marks):** The atomic update unit. It is the **complete set of segments** (PCS + N×RCS + N×CDS + N×ODS + EDS) that the decoder needs to render one new subtitle layout. A Display Set is a **logical group**, not a single PES packet — it can span multiple PES packets.

2. **Epoch (2 marks):** The continuous time interval during which the visual state of the subtitle is constant. The Epoch starts when the decoder commits the Display Set (at EDS) and ends either:
   - when the next Display Set's EDS arrives, or
   - when the `page_time_out` expires.

3. **Atomic Update Mechanism (3 marks):** The decoder **buffers** the entire Display Set in memory before any region becomes visible. It commits the update **only when the EDS sentinel is parsed**. This guarantees:
   - No partial / glitchy subtitles appear on screen.
   - If any segment is lost (e.g., due to DVB-T impulse noise), the **previous Epoch** continues to be displayed until a **complete** Display Set is received.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **PES Packetization (4 marks):** The subtitle elementary stream is first packetized into **PES packets** of up to 64 KB payload. Each PES packet has:
   - A **32-byte PES header** containing the **PTS** (Presentation Time Stamp) used to synchronize the subtitle with the corresponding video frame.
   - The **subtitle segment bytes** (PCS, RCS, CDS, ODS, EDS) concatenated.
   - The PES is then sliced into **188-byte TS packets** for transport.

2. **Subtitling Descriptor (0x59) (3 marks):** Defined in **ETSI EN 300 468**, this descriptor is carried in the **PMT** to identify and describe each subtitle stream. Its fields include:
   - ISO 639 language code (3 bytes)
   - `subtitling_type` (e.g., 0x10 = SDT, 0x20 = Open)
   - `composition_page_id` (16 bits)
   - `ancillary_page_id` (16 bits)

It allows the receiver's **EPG / Service Manager** to know **which subtitles are available** in a given program — e.g., a Malayalam-speaking viewer can configure their STB to always select the `mal` language subtitles from this descriptor.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the EDS:** Students often list four segment types and omit the **End of Display Set (EDS)** marker. KTU expects all five. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing `page_id` and `composition_page_id`:** `composition_page_id` is in the **PMT descriptor**, while `page_id` is in the **PCS segment**. Examiners mark this distinction strictly. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Calling DVB subtitles "text-based":** The system is **bitmap-based**. Writing "the decoder renders the fonts" is a hard fail. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Wrong stream_type value:** Writing `0x06` is correct; writing `0x20` (which is a private_stream_1 PES stream_id) is a common confusion — note these are two different fields in two different tables. (–1 mark)
> 5. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-questions:** Omitting a labeled block diagram costs 1–2 marks. Always draw a structured **flow / hierarchy / block** diagram.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Standard:** **ETSI EN 300 743** is the governing document for DVB Subtitling. Always cite this in answers.
- **Stream Type:** DVB subtitle PES uses **`stream_type = 0x06`** in the PMT.
- **Rendering Nature:** **Bitmap-based**, not text-based — pre-rasterized at the head-end.
- **Five Segments:** PCS, RCS, CDS, ODS, EDS — all five are required, in that conceptual order, per Display Set.
- **Atomic Update:** The **EDS** (0x80) sentinel is the **commit trigger**; the decoder does not render until EDS is received.
- **CLUT Model:** 2-bit, 4-bit, or 8-bit indexed color, defined per region by the CDS. Each CLUT entry is **4 bytes** (R, G, B, flags).
- **RLE Compression:** ODS payload uses **Run-Length Encoding** to reduce bandwidth — typical 30–70% reduction on Latin scripts.
- **Time Base:** All `display_set_timing` fields are in the **90 kHz MPEG-2 PCR clock**; convert to seconds by dividing by **90,000**.
- **Hierarchy (5 levels):** Subtitle Service → Display Set → Epoch → Composition Page → Region.
- **SDT Support:** DVB subtitles natively support **SDT** via the **composition_page_id** and the **subtitling_type** field in the Subtitling Descriptor.
- **HD Support:** Composition pages can be up to **1920 × 1080**, enabling HD subtitle rendering.
- **Descriptors:** The **Subtitling Descriptor (0x59)** in the PMT is the metadata link between the service and the subtitle PES.
- **PES stream_id:** DVB subtitles ride on **private_stream_1** (stream_id 0xBD region, 0x20–0x3F with stream_id_extension).
- **PCR Sync:** The PTS in the PES header must match the video PTS ± 1 frame; out-of-sync subtitles are a common broadcast fault.
- **Typical Bandwidth:** An SD multilingual SDT bundle fits in ~500 kbps of TS bitrate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB): Comparison with DVB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB): Comparison with DVB ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB) **Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB)** is a digital radio standard for broadcasting audio services using **Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (COFDM)**. It was developed within the **Eureka 147 Project** (initiated in 1987) and standardized by the European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI) as **EN 300 401**. DAB is engineered to deliver near-CD quality audio, robustly, even to high-speed mobile receivers (up to **300 km/h**), by exploiting frequency diversity, time interleaving, and a single-frequency network (SFN) architecture. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** DAB is the only mandatory digital radio standard tested under the *Entertainment Electronics* course. It must be studied alongside DVB to highlight the architectural similarities and application-level divergences of the two broadcasting families. ### 1.2 Formal Definition of DVB **Digital Video Broadcasting (DVB)** is a suite of internationally accepted open standards for digital television broadcasting. The most relevant terrestrial variant, **DVB-T** (and its successor **DVB-T2**), also uses OFDM-based multicarrier transmission. The acronym covers satellite (**DVB-S/S2**), cable (**DVB-C/C2**), and handheld (**DVB-H**) variants. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Conveyor Belt vs. The Express Train"** > > Imagine two postal systems: > - **DAB** is like a **specialized conveyor belt** designed exclusively to carry *audio parcels* (music, voice, small data). It uses many small parallel belts (the *sub-carriers* of OFDM), each moving slowly and reliably, so even if one belt jams (fades), the package still arrives intact. > - **DVB** is like a **massive express train**, built to haul *video* (huge data parcels). It uses the same kind of parallel-track technology (OFDM) but with **wider tracks, longer trains, and stronger error-correction engines (LDPC)** to carry high-definition content. > > Both systems share the same underlying railway gauge (**OFDM**), but DAB runs a *shorter, narrower, music-only train*, while DVB runs a *long, wide, multimedia train*. ### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **DAB transmission bandwidth per block:** **1.536 MHz** (Mode I, II, III, IV) - **Number of OFDM sub-carriers (Mode I):** **1536** - **DAB useful bit rate:** **0.576 to 1.864 Mbps** - **DVB-T channel bandwidth:** **6 MHz, 7 MHz, or 8 MHz** (region dependent) - **DVB-T sub-carriers (2k mode):** **1705**, (8k mode): **6817** - **DAB audio codec:** **MPEG-1 Audio Layer II (MP2)**; for **DAB+** it is **HE-AAC v2 (MPEG-4)** - **DVB-T video codec:** **MPEG-2 Part 2** (H.262); for **DVB-T2**: **H.264/MPEG-4 AVC** or **H.265/HEVC** - **Operating frequency — DAB:** **Band III (174–240 MHz)** and **L-band (1452–1492 MHz)** - **Operating frequency — DVB-T:** **VHF/UHF (47–862 MHz)** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept of DAB The DAB transmission chain follows a structured pipeline: 1. **Audio Source Coding** — Analog audio (mono or stereo) is compressed using **MPEG-1 Layer II** (or **HE-AAC v2** in DAB+). This reduces the bit rate from ~1.4 Mbps (CD-DA) to roughly **128–192 kbps per stereo program** with near-transparent quality. 2. **Programme Service Multiplex** — Multiple audio programs, plus **Programme Associated Data (PAD)** (e.g., song titles, slideshow images, traffic info) are time-interleaved into a **Main Service Channel (MSC)**. 3. **Convolutional Encoding** — A **punctured convolutional code** (mother code rate 1/4, constraint length 7) is applied with selectable rates: **1/4, 3/8, 1/2, 3/4** depending on the desired robustness. 4. **Time Interleaving** — Interleaving depth of **24 frames (~1.2 s)** distributes burst errors in time, so Rayleigh fades affect only a small fraction of any program. 5. **Frequency Interleaving** — Sub-carriers are mapped using a fixed reordering pattern to disperse frequency-selective fading. 6. **COFDM Modulation** — The encoded data is mapped onto **1536 (or 384/192/768)** orthogonal sub-carriers using **Differential QPSK (D-QPSK)** in classic DAB, or **Differential 16-QAM / 64-QAM** options. 7. **Single Frequency Network (SFN) Insertion** — Identical content is broadcast on the *same frequency* from multiple transmitters; the receiver treats the combined signal as artificial multipath, which COFDM absorbs. 8. **RF Transmission** — A **Transmission Frame** of **96 ms duration (Mode I)** containing a **null symbol, phase-reference symbol, and 76 data symbols** is radiated. > [!NOTE] > **Why COFDM?** It transforms a frequency-selective channel into **1536 flat-fading narrow-band channels**, each experiencing only simple AWGN. A single deep fade therefore destroys only a few sub-carriers — a loss easily recovered by the **Viterbi decoder** with its built-in convolutional error correction. ### 2.2 Operational Concept of DVB-T DVB-T follows a similar OFDM philosophy but is purpose-built for higher bitrates: 1. **Video source coding** using **MPEG-2** (or **H.264/HEVC** for T2). 2. **Transport stream multiplexing** (MPEG-TS, 188-byte packets). 3. **Outer coding** — **Reed–Solomon (RS 204,188)**. 4. **Outer interleaving** — convolutional byte interleaver. 5. **Inner coding** — punctured convolutional code (rates 1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 7/8). 6. **Inner interleaving** — bit + symbol interleaver. 7. **Mapping** — QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM. 8. **OFDM framing** — 2k (1705 carriers) or 8k (6817 carriers) mode. 9. **Guard interval insertion** — 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32 of symbol duration. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Formula | DAB (Eureka 147) | DVB-T (ETS 300 744) | DVB-T2 (EN 302 755) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Channel Bandwidth $B$ | **1.536 MHz** | **6 / 7 / 8 MHz** | **1.7 / 5 / 6 / 7 / 8 / 10 MHz** | | 2 | OFDM Sub-carriers $N$ | 1536 (Mode I) | 1705 (2k) / 6817 (8k) | 853–27841 (1k–32k) | | 3 | Useful Symbol Duration $T_u$ | $T_u = 1 \text{ ms}$ | $T_u = 224 \text{ μs (2k)}, 896 \text{ μs (8k)}$ | $T_u = 112 \text{ μs – 3.584 ms}$ | | 4 | Guard Interval $T_g$ | $T_g = 246 \text{ μs (Mode I)}$ | $T_g = T_u / 4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32$ | $T_g = T_u / 4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32, 1/64, 1/128$ | | 5 | Sub-carrier Spacing $\Delta f$ | $\Delta f = 1 \text{ kHz}$ | $\Delta f \approx 4.46 \text{ kHz (2k)}, 1.12 \text{ kHz (8k)}$ | $\Delta f \approx 279 \text{ Hz – 8.94 kHz}$ | | 6 | Modulation Schemes | D-QPSK, D-16/64-QAM (DAB+ with non-diff) | QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM | QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM, 256-QAM | | 7 | Inner FEC | Convolutional $1/4, 3/8, 1/2, 3/4$ | Convolutional $1/2 \to 7/8$ | **LDPC** + BCH (rates 1/3, 1/2, …, 5/6) | | 8 | Outer FEC | None (time-int. only) | Reed–Solomon (204,188) | BCH outer code | | 9 | Net Bit Rate | **0.576 – 1.864 Mbps** | **4.98 – 31.67 Mbps** | **up to 50+ Mbps** | | 10 | Audio Codec | MPEG-1 Layer II / HE-AAC v2 | MPEG-1/2 Layer II, AC-3 | MPEG-4 HE-AAC, AC-3, xHE-AAC | | 11 | Video Codec | PAD only (slideshow) | MPEG-2 | H.264 / H.265 | | 12 | SFN Support | **Yes (inherent)** | Yes (8k mode preferred) | Yes (long guard interval) | | 13 | Mobility Support | Up to **~300 km/h** | Up to ~120 km/h | Up to ~350 km/h | | 14 | Frequency Bands | VHF Band III, L-band | VHF/UHF | VHF/UHF | **Core General OFDM Formula (applies to both DAB and DVB):** $$ T_u = \frac{1}{\Delta f} $$ $$ B_{\text{channel}} \approx N \cdot \Delta f $$ $$ T_{\text{total}} = T_u + T_g $$ $$ \text{Roll-off factor (guard ratio)} = \frac{T_g}{T_u} $$ ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **DAB** powers national digital radio networks in **Norway (2017), Switzerland, UK, Germany, India (in trial)** — enabling **single-frequency networks** that save spectrum and reduce inter-cell interference in hilly terrain. - **DVB-T/T2** underpins terrestrial **DTT (Digital Terrestrial Television)** in Europe, Australia, India (DD Free Dish uses DVB-S2), and parts of Africa — delivering SD, HD, and 4K UHD broadcasts. - Both systems share **MPEG transport fundamentals** (DAB uses an MPEG-2-like stream, DVB uses MPEG-TS) — explaining why a single SoC (System-on-Chip) receiver can often demodulate both. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: DAB Sub-carrier Spacing and Symbol Duration We derive the fundamental relationship between sub-carrier spacing $\Delta f$, useful symbol duration $T_u$, and the number of sub-carriers $N$ for DAB Mode I. **Step 1: Orthogonality Condition** For OFDM, sub-carriers must be mutually orthogonal over the useful symbol interval $T_u$. The orthogonality condition is: $$ \int_{0}^{T_u} e^{j 2 \pi f_k t} \cdot e^{-j 2 \pi f_m t}\, dt = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad k \neq m $$ This is satisfied when the sub-carrier spacing is: $$ \Delta f = \frac{1}{T_u} $$ **Step 2: Apply Mode I Specification** For DAB Mode I, the useful symbol duration is fixed by the standard at: $$ T_u = 1000 \text{ μs} = 1 \text{ ms} $$ Therefore the sub-carrier spacing becomes: $$ \Delta f = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 1000 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ kHz} $$ **Step 3: Compute the Number of Sub-carriers** The DAB block bandwidth (Mode I) is allocated as: $$ B = 1.536 \text{ MHz} $$ The total number of OFDM sub-carriers $N$ is: $$ N = \frac{B}{\Delta f} = \frac{1.536 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 10^{3}} = 1536 $$ **Step 4: Total Frame Duration** Each DAB
