transmission frame has a duration of: $$ T_{\text{frame}} = 96 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{Fixed by ETSI EN 300 401}) $$ The frame contains 1 null symbol, 1 phase-reference symbol, and 76 data symbols, each of duration $T_u + T_g$ where $T_g = 246 \text{ μs}$. **Step 5: Total Symbol Duration** $$ T_{\text{sym}} = T_u + T_g = 1.000 + 0.246 = 1.246 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 6: Verify Frame Duration** $$ T_{\text{frame}} = 78 \times T_{\text{sym}} = 78 \times 1.246 \times 10^{-3} = 97.188 \text{ ms} $$ The slight difference is due to standard-defined overhead for the Synchronization Channel. ### 3.2 Derivation: DVB-T 2k Mode Sub-carrier Spacing For DVB-T, the useful symbol duration is related to the FFT size: $$ T_u = \frac{N_{\text{FFT}}}{B} \cdot \text{(for an 8 MHz channel, 2k mode)} $$ **Step 1: Known Quantities** $$ N_{\text{FFT}} = 2048 \quad (\text{including 343 virtual carriers}), \quad N_{\text{used}} = 1705 $$ $$ B = 8 \text{ MHz} \quad \text{(typical European channel)} $$ **Step 2: Compute Useful Symbol Duration** $$ T_u = \frac{2048}{8 \times 10^{6}} = 256 \text{ μs} $$ **Step 3: Compute Sub-carrier Spacing** $$ \Delta f = \frac{1}{T_u} = \frac{1}{256 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 3906.25 \text{ Hz} \approx 4.46 \text{ kHz} $$ **Step 4: Equivalent Bandwidth from Used Carriers** $$ B_{\text{used}} = 1705 \times 4.46 \times 10^{3} \approx 7.61 \text{ MHz} $$ The remaining bandwidth is allocated to the **guard band** at channel edges. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (DAB vs. DVB) | **Aspect** | **Why It Matters** | **DAB** | **DVB-T** | **Difference** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Primary Application** | Defines the system goal | **Audio broadcasting** | **Video broadcasting** | DAB optimized for low bitrate + high mobility; DVB-T for high bitrate + image quality. | | **Channel Bandwidth** | Determines capacity | **1.536 MHz** | **6/7/8 MHz** | DVB-T has ~4–5× more bandwidth. | | **Modulation** | Bits per symbol efficiency | D-QPSK (2 bits/sym) | 64-QAM (6 bits/sym) | DVB-T carries 3× more bits per carrier-symbol. | | **Bit Rate** | Total payload | 0.576–1.864 Mbps | 5–32 Mbps | DVB-T carries 10–20× more total data. | | **FEC Strength** | Robustness vs. efficiency | Convolutional $1/4$ to $3/4$ | Convolutional $1/2$ to $7/8$ | DAB allows stronger coding (1/4), better for mobile. | | **Sub-carrier Spacing** | Doppler tolerance | **1 kHz** (large $\Delta f$) | 1.12 kHz – 4.46 kHz | Larger $\Delta f$ in DAB → better Doppler tolerance for fast mobility. | | **Guard Interval** | Echo tolerance | 246 μs (~24% of $T_u$) | 28–224 μs (1/32 – 1/4 of $T_u$) | DAB's *relative* guard interval is similar; absolute value higher in 8k DVB-T. | | **Mobility** | Practical use case | Up to 300 km/h | Up to ~120 km/h | DAB is more robust at high speeds. | | **SFN Operation** | Spectrum efficiency | Native and **mandatory** design feature | Supported (especially 8k) | DAB was *designed for* SFNs from the start. | | **Source Coding** | Compression efficiency | MP2 / HE-AAC v2 (audio only) | MPEG-2 Video + MP2/AAC audio | DVB-T's much higher rate is consumed by video. | | **Multimedia Capability** | Slideshow, EPG, etc. | Limited (PAD only) | Full EPG, teletext, subtitles, MHP | DVB-T is a richer multimedia platform. | | **Receiver Complexity** | Cost, power | Lower (1536-pt FFT) | Higher (8k = 8192-pt FFT) | DAB receivers are cheaper and lower-power. | | **Receiver Power** | Battery life | ~0.5 W typical | ~2–3 W typical | DAB receivers are 4–6× more power efficient. | ### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: DAB Throughput Estimation A DAB ensemble uses: - Modulation: **D-QPSK** → 2 bits per sub-carrier symbol - Code rate: **1/2** convolutional coding - Sub-carriers: **1536** (Mode I) **Step 1: Bits per OFDM symbol** $$ \text{Bits/symbol} = 1536 \times 2 = 3072 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 2: OFDM symbols per second (Mode I)** $$ \text{Symbols/s} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{sym}}} = \frac{1}{1.246 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 802.57 \text{ symbols/s} $$ **Step 3: Gross bit rate** $$ R_{\text{gross}} = 3072 \times 802.57 \approx 2.466 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 4: Apply code rate (1/2)** $$ R_{\text{net}} = 2.466 \times 0.5 = 1.233 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 5: Subtract Fast Information Channel (FIC) overhead (~10%)** $$ R_{\text{MS}} = 1.233 \times 0.9 \approx 1.110 \text{ Mbps} $$ This matches the standard's reported **~1.1 Mbps** for Mode I, code rate 1/2, D-QPSK — confirming the analysis. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 DAB Transmitter — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD node1[Audio Source Left + Right] --> node2[MPEG Layer II Encoder
or HE-AAC v2 Encoder] node3[PAD Data
Slideshow, EPG] --> node2 node2 --> node4[Programme Service Multiplexer
MSC Sub-channel] node5[Other Audio Programmes] --> node4 node6[Convolutional Encoder
Rate 1/4 to 3/4] --> node4 node4 --> node6 node6 --> node7[Time Interleaver
Depth 24 frames] node7 --> node8[Frequency Interleaver
Fixed Permutation] node8 --> node9[OFDM Mapper
D-QPSK Symbols] node9 --> node10[IFFT Block
2048 point] node10 --> node11[Guard Interval Insertion
246 us] node11 --> node12[DAC and RF Up-conversion
174-240 MHz Band III] node12 --> node13[SFN Transmitter Network
Multiple Sites Same Frequency] ``` ### 4.2 DVB-T Transmitter — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD n1[Video Source MPEG-2
or H.264 Encoder] --> n2[MPEG Transport Stream
188 byte packets] n3[Audio MPEG Layer II
or AC-3] --> n2 n4[Service Information SI
and EPG] --> n2 n2 --> n5[Reed Solomon Outer FEC
RS 204,188] n5 --> n6[Outer Convolutional Interleaver] n6 --> n7[Inner Convolutional Encoder
Rate 1/2 to 7/8] n7 --> n8[Inner Bit and Symbol Interleaver] n8 --> n9[Constellation Mapper
QPSK 16QAM 64QAM] n9 --> n10[OFDM Frame Builder
2k or 8k Mode] n10 --> n11[IFFT 2k 8192 point] n11 --> n12[Guard Interval Insertion
1/4 1/8 1/16 1/32] n12 --> n13[RF Front End
VHF UHF 47 to 862 MHz] n13 --> n14[MISO or SFN Transmitters] ``` ### 4.3 Side-by-Side Protocol Stack Comparison ```mermaid graph TB subgraph stackDAB[DAB Protocol Stack] d1[Audio Application
MP2 or HE-AAC v2] --> d2[MPEG Layer II Transport
ETSI EN 300 401] d2 --> d3[Service Information
FIG and PAD] d3 --> d4[Convolutional FEC
and Interleaving] d4 --> d5[COFDM Modulation
D-QPSK 1536 carriers] end subgraph stackDVB[DVB-T Protocol Stack] v1[Video Application
MPEG-2 H.264 H.265] --> v2[MPEG-2 Transport Stream
188 byte packets] v2 --> v3[Service Information
DVB SI and EPG] v3 --> v4[Reed Solomon Outer
and Convolutional Inner] v4 --> v5[OFDM Modulation
QAM 2k or 8k] end style stackDAB fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1 style stackDVB fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 ``` ### 4.4 Receiver Architecture — Unified Concept ```mermaid graph LR a1[Antenna RF Front End
Tuner] --> a2[ADC
Sampling] a2 --> a3[Synchronization
Time and Frequency] a3 --> a4[FFT Demodulator
Remove Guard Interval] a4 --> a5[Frequency De-interleaver] a5 --> a6[Time De-interleaver] a6 --> a7[Viterbi Decoder
Inner FEC] a7 --> a8[MPEG Source Decoder
Audio MP2 or Video MP2] a8 --> a9[Output
Loudspeaker or Display] ``` > [!NOTE] > The architecture is identical for DAB and DVB-T receivers; only the **FFT size**, **FEC parameters**, and **source decoder** differ. This is why modern SDR (Software Defined Radio) chipsets such as the **Realtek RTL2832** can demodulate both with different firmware loads. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** *Define DAB. List any two advantages of DAB over conventional AM/FM broadcasting.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB) is a digital radio broadcasting standard based on **Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (COFDM)**, defined in **ETSI EN 300 401 (Eureka 147)**, that delivers near-CD quality audio along with ancillary data services to fixed, portable, and mobile receivers. **Advantages over AM/FM (2 marks – any two):** 1. **Robustness to multipath and fading** — COFDM's frequency diversity and time interleaving eliminate the flutter and distortion typical of FM in moving cars. 2. **Spectrum efficiency** — DAB supports **Single Frequency Networks (SFN)**, where the entire country can reuse one frequency block, saving spectrum compared to FM's geographically distinct carriers. 3. **Higher audio quality at lower bitrates** — MPEG Layer II / HE-AAC v2 codecs deliver studio quality at ~128–192 kbps. 4. **Value-added services** — Slideshows, Electronic Programme Guides (EPG), traffic updates (TPEG), and Journaline text can be embedded in PAD. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *Mention the modulation technique and audio codec used in standard DAB.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Modulation technique (1.5 marks):** **Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (COFDM)** with **Differential Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (D-QPSK)** on **1536 sub-carriers** (Mode I). DAB+ additionally supports non-differential 16-QAM and 64-QAM. - **Audio codec (1.5 marks):** **MPEG-1 Audio Layer II (MP2)** in classic DAB, supporting bitrates from **32 to 192 kbps** per stereo programme. The successor **DAB+** uses **High-Efficiency AAC v2 (HE-AAC v2, MPEG-4 Part 3)** for better quality at lower bitrates. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice) --- **Part B – Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the **DAB transmitter architecture**. Identify the role of the **Time Interleaver** and the **COFDM modulator** in achieving robustness against mobile-channel impairments. **(b) [7 Marks)** Compare and contrast **DAB and DVB-T** under the following heads: (i) primary application, (ii) channel bandwidth, (iii) modulation scheme, (iv) source coding, (v) mobility support, (vi) receiver complexity. --- ### 5.3 Model Solution — Question A **Solution to (a) — DAB Transmitter Architecture [7 marks]** **Block diagram (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the complete functional block diagram. Key blocks are: 1. **Audio encoder** (MPEG-1 Layer II / HE-AAC v2) — compresses audio to 32–192 kbps. 2. **PAD inserter** — adds Programme Associated Data. 3. **Programme multiplexer** — combines multiple services into the Main Service Channel (MSC). 4. **Convolutional encoder** — adds redundancy for error correction. 5. **Time interleaver** — depth 24 frames (~1.2 s). 6. **Frequency interleaver** — reorders sub-carriers using a fixed permutation. 7. **COFDM modulator** — performs D-QPSK mapping, IFFT, and guard interval insertion. **Role of Time Interleaver (2 marks):** Bursts of errors caused by short-duration fading (e.g., passing under a bridge) would otherwise obliterate a contiguous block of audio. The time interleaver **spreads these errors across 24 transmission frames** so that after de-interleaving at the receiver, the errors are dispersed thinly and the **Viterbi decoder** can correct them using its convolutional redundancy. This provides **time diversity** without consuming extra bandwidth. **Role of COFDM Modulator (2 marks):** COFDM splits the wideband **1.536 MHz** DAB channel into **1536 narrowband sub-carriers of 1 kHz spacing**. Each sub-carrier experiences **flat fading** (no inter-symbol interference) because the symbol duration $T_u = 1 \text{ ms}$ is much longer than the channel's multipath delay spread. The **guard interval of 246 μs** absorbs echoes from distant transmitters in an **SFN**, allowing multiple transmitters to broadcast on the **same frequency** without intra-network interference. --- **Solution to (b) — Comparison Table [7 marks — 1 mark per head with correct detail]** | **Head** | **DAB** | **DVB-T** | |---|---|---| | **(i) Primary Application** | Audio broadcasting (digital radio) | Video broadcasting (digital TV) | | **(ii) Channel Bandwidth** | **1.536 MHz** (fixed) | **6 / 7 / 8 MHz** (region dependent) | | **(iii) Modulation Scheme** | **D-QPSK** (DAB+ allows 16/64-QAM) | **QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM** | | **(iv) Source Coding** | MPEG-1 Layer II / HE-AAC v2 (audio only) | MPEG-2 Video + MPEG Audio | | **(v) Mobility Support** | Excellent — up to **300 km/h** | Moderate — best up to **120 km/h** (8k mode) | | **(vi) Receiver Complexity** | Lower (1536-point FFT, 1 kHz spacing) | Higher (1705/6817 carriers, complex FEC) | --- ### 5.4 Part B – Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a block diagram, explain the **DVB-T transmitter chain**. What is the purpose of the **Reed–Solomon (204, 188) outer code** and the **inner convolutional code**? **(b) [7 Marks]** A DAB ensemble uses **D-QPSK modulation** with a **convolutional code rate of 1/2** in **Mode I**. Calculate the **net audio data rate** available in the Main Service Channel (MSC), assuming the Fast Information Channel (FIC) consumes **10% of the gross data rate**. --- ### 5.5 Model Solution — Question B **Solution to (a) — DVB-T Transmitter Chain [7 marks]** **Block diagram (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes. The key blocks in order are: **Source coding → MPEG-2 TS multiplexer → Reed–Solomon (204,188) outer FEC → Outer interleaver → Inner convolutional encoder (rate 1/2 to 7/8) → Inner interleaver → QAM mapper → OFDM frame builder → IFFT → Guard interval insertion → RF upconversion.** **Purpose of Reed–Solomon (204, 188) outer code (2 marks):** This block code adds **16 parity bytes to every 188-byte transport packet**, allowing the receiver to correct up to **8 erroneous bytes per packet**. It is exceptionally powerful against **burst errors** (e.g., impulsive noise from electrical appliances) and acts as the **second line of defence** after the inner convolutional decoder. **Purpose of inner convolutional code (2 marks):** The punctured convolutional code (constraint length 7, mother rate 1/2) provides **continuous soft-decision error correction** for random errors introduced by the wireless channel. Combined with the **Viterbi decoder** at the receiver, it dramatically lowers the required **carrier-to-noise ratio (C/N)** for a target **Bit Error Rate (BER) of 2 × 10⁻⁴** (the Quasi-Error-Free threshold of the RS code). --- **Solution to (b) — DAB Net Data Rate Calculation [7 marks]** **Given:** - Modulation: **D-QPSK** → 2 bits per sub-carrier symbol - Sub-carriers in Mode I: **$N = 1536$** - Code rate: **$R_c = 1/2$** - Symbol duration: **$T_{\text{sym}} = T_u + T_g = 1000 \text{ μs} + 246 \text{ μs} = 1246 \text{ μs}$** - FIC overhead: **10% of gross rate** **Step 1: Bits per OFDM symbol [1 mark]** $$ \text{Bits per OFDM symbol} = N \times \text{bits per carrier} = 1536 \times 2 = 3072 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 2: OFDM symbols per second [1 mark]** $$ f_{\text{sym}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{sym}}} = \frac{1}{1246 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 802.57 \text{ symbols/s} $$ **Step 3: Gross bit rate [1 mark]** $$ R_{\text{gross}} = 3072 \times 802.57 \approx 2.466 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 4: Apply code rate [1 mark]** $$ R_{\text{net (channel coded)}} = 2.466 \times 0.5 = 1.233 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 5: Subtract FIC overhead (10%) [1 mark]** $$ R_{\text{MSC}} = 1.233 \times (1 - 0.10) = 1.233 \times 0.90 \approx 1.110 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Step 6: Final Answer [2 marks]** $$ \boxed{R_{\text{MSC}} \approx 1.110 \text{ Mbps}} $$ This capacity supports approximately **6–9 high-quality stereo audio programmes** at **128–192 kbps each**, plus ancillary PAD data. --- ### 5.6 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Confusing DAB with DAB+** — Classic DAB uses **D-QPSK + MP2**; **DAB+** uses **non-differential 16/64-QAM + HE-AAC v2**. Examiners deduct 1 mark if the student attributes HE-AAC to classic DAB. > 2. **Wrong FFT / sub-carrier count** — DAB Mode I has **1536** *active* sub-carriers (out of 2048 FFT points, with 512 null carriers for spectral shaping). DVB-T 2k mode has **1705** *active* sub-carriers (out of 2048 FFT). Confusing *FFT size* with *active carriers* is a frequent error. > 3. **Forgetting the Reed–Solomon code in DAB** — DAB has **no outer RS code**; only convolutional + time interleaving. Students often wrongly assume DAB has the same two-tier FEC as DVB-T. > 4. **Mixing up DVB-T and DVB-T2** — DVB-T2 uses **LDPC + BCH** (powerful modern codes), not convolutional. Examiners will *specifically* ask "DVB-T" — do not write T2 parameters unless asked. > 5. **Bit-rate calculation errors** — Always convert μs to seconds correctly: $1246 \text{ μs} = 1.246 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$. A unit-conversion slip costs the entire 7 marks in numerical questions. --- ### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **DAB = Digital Audio Broadcasting**, based on **Eureka 147** / **ETSI EN 300 401**. - [ ] **Modulation = COFDM with D-QPSK**; **Audio codec = MPEG-1 Layer II** (or **HE-AAC v2** in DAB+). - [ ] DAB Mode I uses **1536 sub-carriers**, **1 kHz spacing**, **1 ms useful symbol**, **246 μs guard interval**, **96 ms frame**. - [ ] DAB **net bit rate**: **0.576 – 1.864 Mbps** depending on code rate and modulation. - [ ] **DVB-T = Digital Video Broadcasting – Terrestrial**, ETSI EN 300 744; DVB-T2 = EN 302 755 with LDPC. - [ ] DVB-T uses **2k (1705) or 8k (6817) carriers**, **QPSK/16-QAM/64-QAM**, **Reed–Solomon (204,188) + Convolutional** FEC. - [ ] DVB-T **net bit rate**: **~5 to ~32 Mbps**; DVB-T2: **up to 50+ Mbps**. - [ ] Both systems support **Single Frequency Networks (SFN)** — DAB was *designed for* it natively. - [ ] DAB is **more mobile-friendly** (up to 300 km/h) than DVB-T (best at ~120 km/h). - [ ] DAB has **smaller bandwidth (1.536 MHz)** vs. DVB-T (**6/7/8 MHz**) — DVB-T carries video. - [ ] DAB has **no outer FEC** (only convolutional + interleaving); DVB-T has **two-tier FEC** (RS outer + convolutional inner). - [ ] Receiver FFT size: DAB = **2048-point** (1536 active); DVB-T 2k = **2048-point** (1705 active); DVB-T 8k = **8192-point** (6817 active). - [ ] DAB is **receiver-cheaper and lower-power** — ideal for portable and automotive radios. - [ ] DAB+ is the modern, spectrally efficient upgrade; many countries (e.g., Norway, Australia) have switched off analogue FM in favour of DAB+.

topics.title: Physical Layer of DAB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Physical Layer of DAB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of the Physical Layer of DAB

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **Physical Layer of DAB (Digital Audio Broadcasting)**, standardized in **ETSI EN 300 401**, is the lowest layer of the DAB protocol stack responsible for the **digital transmission, modulation, channel coding, and synchronization** of audio/data signals over the terrestrial radio spectrum. It is built upon the foundation of **COFDM (Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)** technology, which is a multi-carrier transmission scheme specifically engineered to combat **multipath fading**, **Doppler shifts**, and **inter-symbol interference (ISI)** in mobile reception environments such as moving vehicles.

The DAB Physical Layer is unique because it was the **first mass-market consumer broadcast system** to deploy COFDM in the VHF Band III (~174–240 MHz) and L-Band (~1452–1492 MHz). Unlike traditional analog FM or AM radio, where a single carrier is modulated, DAB distributes its data across **hundreds or thousands of closely-spaced orthogonal sub-carriers** transmitted simultaneously.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Marathon Relay Race"** 🏃‍♂️

Imagine sending a long, heavy letter (audio stream) across a stormy sea (the radio channel):
- **In Analog FM (Old Way):** You place the entire letter on a single huge paper plane. If the wind (multipath/echo) blows against it, the whole message is destroyed.
- **In DAB COFDM (New Way):** You tear the letter into **1,536 tiny pieces** and give each piece to one of **1,536 sprinters** (sub-carriers) running in **parallel lanes** on the same track. Even if some sprinters (sub-carriers) get hit by a gust of wind (deep fade), only a few letters are lost, and the convolutional error correction can **reconstruct the original message** perfectly.

## 1.3 Core Specifications of the DAB Physical Layer

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Modulation Scheme** | **DQPSK (Differential Quadrature Phase Shift Keying)** on sub-carriers |
| **Multiplexing** | **COFDM (Coded OFDM)** |
| **Frequency Bands** | VHF Band III (174–240 MHz), L-Band (1452–1492 MHz) |
| **Channel Coding** | **Convolutional Coding** (punctured, code rates 1/4, 3/8, 1/2, 3/4) |
| **Network Topology** | **SFN (Single Frequency Network)** capable |
| **Transmission Modes** | **Four Modes: I, II, III, IV** (adaptive to network geometry) |
| **Service Channels** | **FIC** (Fast Information Channel) + **MSC** (Main Service Channel) |

## 1.4 Why is the Physical Layer "Special" in DAB?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The DAB Physical Layer is engineered around **three foundational pillars** that distinguish it from legacy broadcasting:
> 1. **Multipath Immunity** — Robust against reflections from buildings, mountains, and aircraft.
> 2. **SFN Capability** — All transmitters in a network can use the **exact same frequency**, eliminating the need for frequency planning.
> 3. **Mobile Reception** — Seamless, dropout-free audio reception in vehicles traveling at highway speeds (up to **200 km/h** for Mode II).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The COFDM Architecture — Operational Breakdown

COFDM is the **engine of the DAB physical layer**. It combines two powerful techniques:
- **OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing):** Splits a high-bitrate stream into many low-bitrate parallel sub-streams.
- **Coding (C):** Adds forward error correction (FEC) across the data so that losses can be recovered.

### Operational Logic Steps:

> **Step 1 — Source Encoding:** Audio is compressed using **MPEG-1 Audio Layer II (MP2)** or newer HE-AAC formats.

> **Step 2 — Energy Dispersal Scrambling:** A pseudo-random binary sequence (PRBS) is XORed with the data to prevent long strings of 1s or 0s, ensuring the spectrum remains "DC-balanced" for clock recovery.

> **Step 3 — Convolutional Channel Coding:** Redundancy bits are added using a **convolutional encoder** with constraint length **K = 7** and various punctured rates (1/4, 3/8, 1/2, 3/4) to balance robustness vs. efficiency.

> **Step 4 — Time Interleaving:** Audio bits are deliberately **shuffled across multiple OFDM symbols** (over a span of ~384 ms in Mode I) to disperse burst errors caused by short fades.

> **Step 5 — Frequency Interleaving:** Sub-carriers are **re-mapped to a random order** before transmission, so a localized frequency fade destroys only a *scattered* set of bits rather than a contiguous block.

> **Step 6 — DQPSK Mapping:** Data modulates each sub-carrier using **Differential Quadrature Phase Shift Keying**, where information is carried in the **phase difference** between consecutive symbols (no need for a coherent reference).

> **Step 7 — IFFT Generation:** An **Inverse Fast Fourier Transform (IFFT)** converts the frequency-domain OFDM symbol into a time-domain waveform to be transmitted.

> **Step 8 — Guard Interval Insertion:** A **cyclic prefix (guard interval)** is prepended to each OFDM symbol to absorb intersymbol interference from echoes (multipath).

## 2.2 The Four DAB Transmission Modes

| Mode | Max Carrier Spacing | Number of Carriers (N) | Symbol Duration (T_s) | Guard Interval (Δ) | Max Tx Distance | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode I** | **1 kHz** | **1536** | 1.246 ms | 246 µs | **96 km** | VHF Band III, Terrestrial SFN |
| **Mode II** | **4 kHz** | **384** | 311.5 µs | 62 µs | **24 km** | L-Band, Mobile, local |
| **Mode III** | **8 kHz** | **192** | 155.75 µs | 31 µs | **12 km** | Satellite Hybrid |
| **Mode IV** | **2 kHz** | **768** | 623 µs | 123 µs | **48 km** | L-Band, Hybrid SFN |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$T_s = T_u + \Delta$$ | Total OFDM symbol duration = Useful symbol + Guard interval |
| 2 | $$T_u = \frac{1}{f_{\text{spacing}}}$$ | Useful symbol duration = inverse of carrier spacing |
| 3 | $$N_{\text{cells}} = N + N_{\text{null}}$$ | Total spectrum cells (e.g., 1536 + 0 = 1536 in Mode I) |
| 4 | $$R_{\text{net}} = R_{\text{coder}} \times \eta_{\text{mod}} \times \frac{T_u}{T_s}$$ | Net bitrate calculation (Efficiency) |
| 5 | $$R_{\text{coder}} \in \left\{\frac{1}{4},\ \frac{3}{8},\ \frac{1}{2},\ \frac{3}{4}\right\}$$ | Punctured convolutional code rates |
| 6 | $$\eta_{\text{mod}} = 2 \text{ bits/symbol (DQPSK)}$$ | Spectral efficiency of DQPSK |
| 7 | $$f_{\text{max\_Doppler}} = \frac{v}{c} \times f_c$$ | Maximum Doppler shift (mobile reception) |
| 8 | $$B_{\text{total}} = N \times \Delta f$$ | Total DAB bandwidth (e.g., 1536 × 1 kHz = 1.536 MHz) |
| 9 | $$\text{Delay spread tolerance} = \Delta$$ | Max multipath echo the guard interval can absorb |
| 10 | $$\text{Capacity}_{MSC} \approx 2.4 \text{ Mbit/s (Mode I)}$$ | Practical usable audio bitrate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The DAB physical layer's COFDM scheme is the direct ancestor of **DVB-T (Digital Video Broadcasting – Terrestrial)**, **DVB-H**, **DAB+**, and **4G LTE downlink** — all of which borrow the same OFDM core. Studying DAB's physical layer is essentially studying the historical foundation of modern broadband wireless.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Symbol Duration and Guard Interval Relationship

The total DAB symbol time is the sum of the **useful symbol period (T_u)** and the **guard interval (Δ)**.

> **Starting Principle:** The useful symbol period is the inverse of the carrier spacing ($\Delta f$):

$$ T_u = \frac{1}{\Delta f} $$

The total symbol period extends this by the guard interval:

$$ T_s = T_u + \Delta $$

**Substitution:**

$$ T_s = \frac{1}{\Delta f} + \Delta $$

**For Mode I ($\Delta f = 1000$ Hz, $\Delta = 246$ µs):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_u &= \frac{1}{1000} = 1.000 \text{ ms} \\
T_s &= 1.000 \text{ ms} + 0.246 \text{ ms} \\
T_s &= 1.246 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A receiver only needs to "listen" to the **1.000 ms useful portion** to capture all the data. The 246 µs **cyclic prefix** at the start of every symbol is identical to the tail of that symbol — it absorbs echoes arriving within 246 µs (corresponding to a maximum echo path of ~73.8 km at speed of light).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Transmitter Spacing in an SFN

In a **Single Frequency Network (SFN)**, multiple transmitters broadcast the **same content on the same frequency simultaneously**. To prevent self-interference, the differential path delay between any two transmitters must be **less than the guard interval**.

> **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

The maximum path length $d_{\max}$ that the guard interval can "tolerate" is:

$$ d_{\max} = c \times \Delta $$

**For Mode I:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\max} &= (3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}) \times (246 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}) \\
d_{\max} &= 73,800 \text{ m} \\
d_{\max} &\approx 73.8 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Therefore, the maximum transmitter separation in a Mode I SFN is 73.8 km.** If transmitters are placed farther apart, the echo from the farther transmitter arrives *after* the guard interval has ended, causing inter-symbol interference (ISI). This is exactly why the KTU table lists **96 km** as a *practical engineering limit* (with safety margins).

**For Mode II ($\Delta = 62$ µs):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\max} &= (3 \times 10^8) \times (62 \times 10^{-6}) \\
d_{\max} &= 18,600 \text{ m} = 18.6 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the KTU table value of ~24 km (with margins).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Net Bitrate of the DAB Ensemble

The **ensemble bitrate** depends on the code rate, modulation order, and time-efficiency.

> **Formula:**

$$ R_{\text{net}} = R_{\text{coder}} \times \eta_{\text{mod}} \times \frac{T_u}{T_s} \times B_{\text{total}} \times \text{(spectral use factor)} $$

A simplified, engineering-level calculation for the **Maximum MSC Bitrate (Mode I, code rate 3/8, UEP):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{net}} &= 0.375 \times 2 \times \frac{1000}{1246} \times 1{,}536{,}000 \\
&= 0.375 \times 2 \times 0.8026 \times 1.536 \times 10^6 \\
&\approx 924{,}000 \text{ bit/s (per code path)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> With proper convolutional coding across multiple service components, the total **MSC capacity for Mode I is approximately 1.5–2.4 Mbit/s**, sufficient for **~6–9 CD-quality stereo audio programs** in a single 1.536 MHz DAB ensemble block.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Doppler Shift Limit for Mobile Reception

A vehicle moving at speed $v$ causes a frequency shift in the received signal:

$$ f_d = \frac{v}{c} \times f_c $$

> **For Mode II (L-Band, $f_c = 1.5$ GHz, vehicle at 200 km/h ≈ 55.56 m/s):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_d &= \frac{55.56}{3 \times 10^8} \times 1.5 \times 10^9 \\
f_d &= 0.2778 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

For Mode II, the carrier spacing is 4 kHz, so $f_d < \Delta f$ → **reception remains robust**. This is the **theoretical justification for why Mode II is the preferred mode for L-Band mobile reception**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Note:** Since the DAB Physical Layer is a signal-processing chain (not a physical device), the most pedagogically valuable diagram is a **functional block flow** showing how audio data is transformed from source bits into a transmitted RF waveform.

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: DAB Physical Layer Transmitter Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Audio Source MP2 Encoder] --> B[Energy Dispersal Scrambler PRBS XOR]
    B --> C[Convolutional Encoder K=7]
    C --> D[Time Interleaver 384 ms span]
    D --> E[Frequency Interleaver Random Remap]
    E --> F[DQPSK Mapper Constellation Mapping]
    F --> G[IFFT Inverse Fast Fourier Transform]
    G --> H[Guard Interval Insertion Cyclic Prefix]
    H --> I[Digital to Analog Conversion]
    I --> J[RF Upconversion VHF or L Band]
    J --> K[Power Amplifier and Antenna Transmission]

    subgraph CHANNEL_RF
        K --> L[Multipath Propagation Channel]
    end

    L --> M[Receiver Antenna]
    M --> N[Tuner and ADC]
    N --> O[Synchronization and Channel Estimation]
    O --> P[FFT Demodulator]
    P --> Q[Guard Interval Removal]
    Q --> R[Frequency De Interleaver]
    R --> S[Time De Interleaver]
    S --> T[Viterbi Decoder Convolutional]
    T --> U[Energy Dispersal De Scrambler]
    U --> V[Audio Decoder MP2 to PCM]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: DAB Transmission Frame Structure (Mode I)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FRM[TRANSMISSION FRAME MODE I T_frame = 96 ms]
        SYN[Synchronization Channel NULL Symbol = 1.297 ms]
        FSCI[Fast Information Channel FIC = 3 OFDM symbols]
        MSC[Main Service Channel MSC = 72 OFDM symbols]
    end

    SYN --- FSCI
    FSCI --- MSC

    MSC --> SVC1[Service 1 Audio]
    MSC --> SVC2[Service 2 Audio]
    MSC --> SVC3[Service 3 Audio]
    MSC --> SVC4[Service 4 Data PAD]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: COFDM Sub-Carrier Spacing Logic Across Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MI[Mode I VHF BAND III] -- 1 kHz spacing --> C1536[1536 Carriers]
    MII[Mode II L BAND MOBILE] -- 4 kHz spacing --> C384[384 Carriers]
    MIII[Mode III SATELLITE] -- 8 kHz spacing --> C192[192 Carriers]
    MIV[Mode IV L BAND HYBRID] -- 2 kHz spacing --> C768[768 Carriers]

    C1536 -- T_u = 1 ms --> GI1[Guard 246 us]
    C384 -- T_u = 0.25 ms --> GI2[Guard 62 us]
    C192 -- T_u = 0.125 ms --> GI3[Guard 31 us]
    C768 -- T_u = 0.5 ms --> GI4[Guard 123 us]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

### **Q1. What is COFDM? Why is it used in the DAB physical layer? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **COFDM (Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)** is a multi-carrier modulation technique used in the DAB physical layer. It splits a high-bitrate digital stream into many lower-bitrate parallel streams, each modulating a sub-carrier that is mathematically **orthogonal** to the others. The "Coded" prefix refers to the addition of **forward error correction (convolutional coding)** combined with **time and frequency interleaving**.

> It is used in DAB because it provides:
> 1. **Robustness against multipath fading** in mobile reception.
> 2. **Support for Single Frequency Networks (SFN)** via the guard interval.
> 3. **Efficient spectrum use** through orthogonality (no guard bands between sub-carriers).

---

### **Q2. What is the function of the guard interval in DAB COFDM? (3 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Answer:**

> The **guard interval (cyclic prefix)** is a copy of the end of each OFDM symbol that is prepended to the symbol before transmission. Its primary functions are:
> 1. **To absorb intersymbol interference (ISI)** caused by multipath echoes arriving later than the direct signal.
> 2. **To enable SFN operation** by allowing the differential path delay from multiple transmitters to fall within the guard interval, preventing self-interference.
>
> In DAB Mode I, the guard interval is **246 µs**, equivalent to absorbing echo path lengths up to **~73.8 km**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** *Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically divided into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer the **complete question**.*

---

### **Question A — (14 Marks) [Choose either A or B]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

> **(a)** Explain the DAB physical layer transmitter chain with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of convolutional coding and interleaving. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** Compare the four DAB transmission modes (I, II, III, IV) with respect to carrier spacing, number of carriers, guard interval, and applications. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

The DAB physical layer transmitter chain performs the following operations sequentially:

1. **Source Coding:** Audio is compressed using MPEG-1 Layer II (MP2) to a typical rate of 128–192 kbps per stereo program.
2. **Energy Dispersal:** A 16-bit PRBS is XORed with the data to ensure a balanced bit stream.
3. **Convolutional Coding:** A **rate-compatible punctured convolutional (RCPC) encoder** with constraint length K=7 adds redundancy. Puncturing allows code rates of **1/4, 3/8, 1/2, 3/4**.
4. **Time Interleaving:** Bits are spread across **~16 OFDM symbols** (~384 ms in Mode I) to protect against burst errors from short fades.
5. **Frequency Interleaving:** Sub-carrier index assignments are randomized to scatter any narrowband fade across the whole signal.
6. **DQPSK Mapping:** Each sub-carrier is modulated in Differential Quadrature Phase Shift Keying, encoding 2 bits per symbol.
7. **IFFT + Guard Interval:** The signal is transformed to time-domain and a cyclic prefix is added.

**Role of Convolutional Coding:** Provides forward error correction; puncturing allows unequal error protection (UEP) so that critical audio bits (header, scale factors) get more redundancy than less important bits (fine quantization).

**Role of Interleaving:** Converts burst errors (concentrated in time/frequency) into random errors, which convolutional codes correct much more efficiently.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

| Feature | **Mode I** | **Mode II** | **Mode III** | **Mode IV** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Band** | VHF III | L-Band | Satellite | L-Band |
| **Carrier Spacing** | **1 kHz** | **4 kHz** | **8 kHz** | **2 kHz** |
| **# of Carriers** | **1536** | **384** | **192** | **768** |
| **Guard Interval** | 246 µs | 62 µs | 31 µs | 123 µs |
| **Use Case** | Terrestrial SFN | Mobile L-Band | Satellite | Hybrid SFN |

Mode I is optimized for large-coverage SFNs. Mode II tolerates high Doppler (mobile). Mode III is for satellite-to-car reception. Mode IV is a compromise for terrestrial L-Band SFN.

---

### **Question B — (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

> **(a)** Derive the maximum transmitter spacing in a DAB Mode I SFN. Show the relationship between guard interval and physical path length. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** Explain the structure of the DAB transmission frame. Differentiate between FIC and MSC. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

In a **Single Frequency Network (SFN)**, multiple transmitters operate on the **same frequency simultaneously**. The maximum allowable separation is governed by the guard interval ($\Delta$).

The maximum echo path the guard interval can absorb:

$$ d_{\max} = c \times \Delta $$

For Mode I, $\Delta = 246$ µs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\max} &= (3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}) \times (246 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}) \\
d_{\max} &= 73{,}800 \text{ m} \\
d_{\max} &\approx 73.8 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, **the theoretical maximum transmitter separation is ~73.8 km**. In practice, with safety margins for synchronization jitter, the **engineering limit is ~96 km**.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

A DAB **transmission frame** (96 ms in Mode I) consists of three sections:

1. **Synchronization Channel (SC):** Contains the **NULL symbol** and a **Phase Reference Symbol (PRS)**. The receiver uses these to detect the start of a frame, perform frequency synchronization, and estimate channel response.

2. **Fast Information Channel (FIC):** Carries the **MCI (Multiplex Configuration Information)**, SI (Service Information), and signaling for fast acquisition. It uses **fixed, robust coding (code rate 1/4)** so receivers can decode it quickly during channel switching.

3. **Main Service Channel (MSC):** Carries the actual **audio and data services**. It uses **variable, less-robust coding** (rates up to 3/4) optimized for higher capacity. Subdivided into **Common Interleaved Frames (CIFs)** containing sub-channels.

**Key Difference:** FIC = control/signaling (robust, low latency); MSC = content (higher capacity, more efficient coding).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cyclic prefix** — Students often explain COFDM but omit that the guard interval is what *enables* SFN operation. This is a 2–3 mark loss in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Conflating "COFDM" and "OFDM"** — Always specify the **Coding (C)** part: convolutional coding + interleaving. Writing just "OFDM" loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong units in derivations** — In Mode I derivations, ensure $\Delta f = 1000$ **Hz** (not kHz) and $\Delta = 246$ **µs** (not ms). Unit errors cause full-mark cancellation in numerical problems.
> 4. **Confusing FIC with MSC** — FIC carries **MCI/SI** (signaling), MSC carries **audio/data**. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Skipping the differential nature of DQPSK** — DQPSK encodes data in **phase *differences***, which is why DAB receivers don't need channel-state estimation before demodulation. This is a frequent sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Physical Layer of DAB**
> - ✅ DAB physical layer = **COFDM + Convolutional Coding + DQPSK** modulation.
> - ✅ Core technology: **COFDM** = Orthogonal sub-carriers + Forward Error Correction.
> - ✅ **Four transmission modes** (I, II, III, IV) adapt to network type: terrestrial SFN, mobile L-Band, satellite, hybrid.
> - ✅ **Mode I** = VHF Band III, 1536 carriers, 1 kHz spacing, 246 µs guard interval → **73.8 km SFN range**.
> - ✅ **Mode II** = L-Band, 384 carriers, 4 kHz spacing → optimized for **mobile reception up to 200 km/h**.
> - ✅ **Guard interval (cyclic prefix)** = absorbs ISI + enables SFN.
> - ✅ **Time interleaving (~384 ms) + Frequency interleaving** = scatters burst errors.
> - ✅ **Convolutional coding** uses K=7, with punctured rates (1/4, 3/8, 1/2, 3/4) for UEP.
> - ✅ **DQPSK** modulation = 2 bits/symbol, differential encoding, no coherent reference needed.
> - ✅ **DAB Frame** = Sync Channel + **FIC (signaling)** + **MSC (content/audio)**.
> - ✅ **FIC** uses fixed robust coding (1/4); **MSC** uses higher-rate coding for capacity.
> - ✅ Total DAB bandwidth = **1.536 MHz** per ensemble block.
> - ✅ First commercial COFDM-based broadcast system → ancestor of DVB-T, DVB-H, and 4G LTE.
> - ✅ Net MSC bitrate in Mode I ≈ **1.5–2.4 Mbit/s** → ~6–9 stereo audio programs per ensemble.
> - ✅ **SFN advantage:** Frequency reuse across an entire country; no frequency planning required.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DAB Modulator
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# DAB Modulator — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — DAB Modulator**
> A **Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB) Modulator** is the transmit-side signal-processing chain that converts one (or several) compressed digital audio/data bit-streams into a **Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (COFDM)** waveform, using **Differential Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (D-QPSK)** on thousands of orthogonal sub-carriers, and broadcasts it in the **VHF Band-III (~174–240 MHz)** or **L-Band (~1452–1492 MHz)** per the **Eureka-147 / ETSI EN 300 401** standard.

The DAB modulator implements **four distinct physical-layer functions in cascade**:
1. **Energy Dispersal (Randomisation)** — bit-stream scrambling using a 16-bit Pseudo-Random Binary Sequence (PRBS) to ensure DC-balance and a uniform spectrum.
2. **Convolutional Channel Coding** — mother code rate **R = 1/4**, memory order **m = 6** (constraint length K = 7), followed by puncturing to obtain Unequal Error Protection (UEP) profiles.
3. **Time & Frequency Interleaving** — provides robustness against frequency-selective fading and time-varying impairments.
4. **COFDM Mapping (D-QPSK + IFFT)** — distributes the data across **N sub-carriers** (Mode-I → **1536**, Mode-II → **384**, Mode-III → **192**, Mode-IV → **768**), applies DQPSK, and inserts a **Guard Interval (T_G)** to combat inter-symbol interference.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Multilingual Container Ship"**
> Imagine the DAB modulator as a giant **container ship** (the COFDM frame) preparing to leave port.
> - The **audio programmes** are *parcels* of different sizes (different bit-rates).
> - The **convolutional encoder** is the *quality-control team* that adds redundancy labels (parity bits) on every parcel.
> - The **time interlever** *shuffles* the parcels across many ships, so a single storm (burst of interference) cannot sink one whole programme.
> - The **frequency interleaver** scatters the parcels across many *container slots* on the ship — if a tidal wave hits one side (deep fade), only a few slots are lost, and Forward Error Correction can recover the rest.
> - The **COFDM modulator** is the *loading crane* that places every parcel onto its own designated sub-carrier (frequency slot) and adds a *safety gap (guard interval)* between ships so collisions (ISI) never occur.
> - Finally, the **D-QPSK mapper** labels each slot with one of four *colour codes* (phase shifts) — and instead of measuring absolute colour, the receiver measures the *change* in colour, making detection robust to slow phase drifts.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Standard DAB Transmission Parameters (ETSI EN 300 401)**
> - Carrier Frequency Bands: **VHF Band-III (174–240 MHz)** and **L-Band (1452–1492 MHz)**
> - Sampling Rate (Source Coding, MPEG-1 Layer II): **48 kHz**
> - Gross Bit-Rate per Ensemble: **≈ 2.4 Mbit/s** (Mode-I, II, III) / **≈ 1.2 Mbit/s** (Mode-IV)
> - Modulation on Sub-carriers: **D-QPSK** (π/4-DQPSK differential)
> - Useful Symbol Duration T_U: **1 ms** (Modes I, II, III) / **250 µs** (Mode-IV)
> - Guard Interval Duration T_G: **¼ · T_U** (i.e. 246 µs / 62 µs respectively)
> - Total Symbol Duration T_S = T_U + T_G
> - Sub-carrier Spacing Δf = 1/T_U
> - Total Frame Duration T_F = **96 ms** (Modes I, III) / **48 ms** (Mode-II) / **24 ms** (Mode-IV)
> - OFDM Symbols per Frame: **76** (Symbol 0 = NULL, Symbol 1 = PRS, Symbols 2–75 = Data)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block-by-Block Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The DAB modulator is **not a single block** but a strict **cascaded signal-processing pipeline**. KTU questions routinely test the student on the exact *order* of these blocks.

The canonical processing chain is:

**① Energy Dispersal Scrambler** → **② Outer Convolutional Encoder (R = 1/4, K = 7)** → **③ Puncturing for UEP** → **④ Time Interleaver (depth ≈ 16 OFDM symbols)** → **⑤ Frequency Interleaver (over all used sub-carriers)** → **⑥ D-QPSK Differential Mapper (π/4-shift)** → **⑦ IFFT (produces time-domain COFDM symbol)** → **⑧ Guard-Interval Insertion (cyclic prefix)** → **⑨ DAC + RF Up-conversion**.

### 2.1.1 Energy Dispersal (Randomisation)
- Implemented by a **16-stage Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR)** with generator polynomial
  $$g(x) = x^{16} + x^{14} + x^{13} + x^{11} + 1$$
  Initialised to **all-ones** at the start of every **Logical Frame**.
- XORs the bit-stream with the PRBS to eliminate long runs of 1s or 0s (which would cause DC wander and poor spectral flatness).

### 2.1.2 Convolutional Channel Coding
- **Mother code**: rate **1/4**, constraint length **K = 7**, generators
  $$G_1 = 133_O, \quad G_2 = 171_O, \quad G_3 = 145_O, \quad G_4 = 133_O$$
- Total encoded bits per bit of input = **4**. Puncturing matrices allow extraction of effective rates **1/3, 2/5, 1/2, 3/4, etc.**
- Provides **Unequal Error Protection (UEP)** — audio header bits get stronger protection than audio body bits.

### 2.1.3 Time Interleaving
- Spreads bursts of errors over **≈ 16 OFDM symbols**.
- Critical against short-duration impulse noise (e.g., car-ignition spikes).

### 2.1.4 Frequency Interleaving
- Distributes interleaved bits pseudo-randomly across the entire ensemble bandwidth.
- Frequency interleaving depth in Mode-I = **1536 sub-carriers**.
- Protects against narrowband frequency-selective fades.

### 2.1.5 D-QPSK Differential Mapping
- Every pair of bits (b_0, b_1) produces a **phase change** Δφ relative to the previous sub-carrier symbol.
- Mapping rule (uniform, π/4-DQPSK) is given in the formula sheet below.

### 2.1.6 COFDM Modulation (IFFT)
- The complex sub-carrier symbols feed an **Inverse Fast Fourier Transform (IFFT)** of size **2^k**, where **2^k ≥ N**.
- Mode-I → **2-point IFFT of size 2048** (1024 unused, including the DC bin = "central" sub-carrier).

### 2.1.7 Guard-Interval Insertion (Cyclic Prefix)
- The last **T_G** fraction of each useful OFDM symbol is copied and prepended as a cyclic prefix.
- This makes the **linear convolution with the channel** behave like a **circular convolution**, eliminating ISI provided the channel impulse response fits inside T_G.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sub-carrier spacing | $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_U}$ | Hz |
| 2 | Useful symbol duration (Mode I/II/III) | $T_U = 1 \text{ ms}$ | Time, ms |
| 3 | Useful symbol duration (Mode IV) | $T_U = 250 \text{ µs}$ | Time, µs |
| 4 | Guard interval | $T_G = \dfrac{1}{4}\,T_U$ | 25 % of T_U |
| 5 | Total symbol duration | $T_S = T_U + T_G = \dfrac{5}{4}\,T_U$ | s |
| 6 | Total frame duration (Mode I/III) | $T_F = 76 \times T_S = 96 \text{ ms}$ | ms |
| 7 | Total frame duration (Mode II) | $T_F = 24 \text{ ms}$ | ms |
| 8 | Ensemble gross bit-rate (Mode I/II/III) | $R_b \approx \dfrac{N_{used}}{T_S} \times 2 \text{ bits} \approx 2.432 \text{ Mbit/s}$ | Mbit/s |
| 9 | DQPSK phase change | $\Delta\varphi \in \{\pm\dfrac{\pi}{4},\ \pm\dfrac{3\pi}{4}\}$ | rad |
| 10 | Convolutional code constraint length | $K = m + 1 = 7$ (memory $m=6$) | — |
| 11 | Generator polynomials (octal) | $G = (133,\ 171,\ 145,\ 133)_O$ | Mother rate 1/4 |
| 12 | PRBS polynomial | $g(x) = x^{16} + x^{14} + x^{13} + x^{11} + 1$ | Init: all-1 |
| 13 | OFDM sub-carriers (Mode I) | $N_{used} = 1536$ | of 2048 IFFT bins |
| 14 | Cyclic prefix length (Mode I) | $L_{CP} = \dfrac{N_{FFT}}{4} = 512 \text{ samples}$ | samples |
| 15 | Bandwidth occupied (Mode I) | $BW = N_{used} \cdot \Delta f \approx 1.536 \text{ MHz}$ | MHz |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DAB Modulator design matters in industry**
> - **Automotive Infotainment**: DAB receivers are integrated into dashboards (Bosch, Continental, NXP). Robustness against Doppler spread at 200 km/h relies entirely on the guard-interval and interleaver depth chosen by the modulator.
> - **Single-Frequency Networks (SFN)**: All transmitters in an SFN broadcast **the same ensemble on the same frequency** simultaneously. This is *only* possible because the COFDM guard interval absorbs the artificial multipath caused by multiple transmitters.
> - **Spectrum Efficiency**: A single DAB ensemble (~1.5 MHz) replaces **~9–12 analogue FM channels**, delivering typically 6–9 stereo programmes plus PAD (Programme Associated Data).
> - **Evolution to DAB+ / DRM+**: DAB+ replaces MPEG-1 Layer II with **HE-AAC v2**, giving ~2× the audio capacity. The modulator's COFDM/DQPSK core remains *unchanged*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The Baseband COFDM Signal at the Modulator Output

Let us derive the **continuous-time baseband COFDM signal** $s(t)$ emitted by the DAB modulator for one complete OFDM symbol of useful duration $T_U$.

### Step 1 — Sub-carrier Orthogonality Condition
Two complex exponentials $e^{j2\pi f_k t}$ and $e^{j2\pi f_l t}$ are orthogonal over $[0, T_U]$ **iff** their frequency separation is an integer multiple of $1/T_U$.

$$
\int_{0}^{T_U} e^{\,j2\pi f_k t} \left(e^{\,j2\pi f_l t}\right)^{\ast}\,dt = \int_{0}^{T_U} e^{\,j2\pi (f_k - f_l)t}\,dt = 0 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; (f_k - f_l)\,T_U \in \mathbb{Z}
$$

Choosing the **smallest non-zero separation** that satisfies this gives the sub-carrier spacing $\Delta f$:

$$
\boxed{\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_U}}
$$

### Step 2 — Sub-carrier Frequencies
For Mode-I with $N_{used} = 1536$ used sub-carriers centred around the ensemble centre-frequency, the $k$-th sub-carrier frequency is:

$$
f_k = f_c + k \cdot \Delta f, \quad k = -\frac{N_{used}-1}{2},\ \ldots,\ -1,\ 0,\ +1,\ \ldots,\ +\frac{N_{used}-1}{2}
$$

In Mode-I: $k \in [-768,\,+768]$ excluding DC, yielding exactly **1536 active sub-carriers**.

### Step 3 — DQPSK Modulation: Mapping Bits to Phase Changes
Each pair of input bits $(b_{2n},\ b_{2n+1})$ is mapped to a phase change $\Delta\varphi_n$ relative to the *previous* sub-carrier phase $\varphi_{n-1}$:

$$
\varphi_n = \varphi_{n-1} + \Delta\varphi_n
$$

The D-QPSK mapping (uniform Gray-coded set) is:

| $b_{2n}$ | $b_{2n+1}$ | $\Delta\varphi_n$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | $+\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$ |
| 0 | 1 | $+\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ |
| 1 | 0 | $-\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ |
| 1 | 1 | $-\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$ |

The complex sub-carrier symbol on sub-carrier $k$ during symbol $n$ is therefore:

$$
C_{k,n} = e^{\,j\varphi_{k,n}} \quad \text{with} \quad \varphi_{k,n} = \varphi_{k,n-1} + \Delta\varphi_{k,n}
$$

### Step 4 — Useful OFDM Symbol (Inverse Fourier Synthesis)
By the inverse discrete Fourier transform principle, the time-domain representation of the useful part of OFDM symbol $n$ is:

$$
x_n(t) = \sum_{k=-768}^{+768} C_{k,n}\; e^{\,j2\pi k\Delta f \cdot t}, \quad t \in [0,\ T_U]
$$

### Step 5 — Guard Interval Insertion (Cyclic Prefix)
Define $T_G = T_U / 4$. The cyclic prefix of length $T_G$ is the *last* $T_G$ seconds of $x_n(t)$ repeated at the front:

$$
s_n(t) = \begin{cases} x_n(t + T_U - T_G), & -T_G \le t < 0 \\[4pt] x_n(t), & 0 \le t < T_U \end{cases}
$$

The total transmitted symbol duration becomes:

$$
\boxed{T_S = T_U + T_G = \frac{5}{4}\,T_U}
$$

For Mode-I: $T_U = 1$ ms $\Rightarrow$ $T_G = 0.246$ ms $\Rightarrow$ $T_S = 1.246$ ms.

### Step 6 — Complete Frame Construction
A transmission frame of $T_F = 96$ ms contains exactly **76** OFDM symbols, distributed as:

| Symbol index | Content | Purpose |
|:---:|---|---|
| 0 | **NULL symbol** | No transmission — provides coarse AGC setting and time-reference at receiver |
| 1 | **PRS** — Phase Reference Symbol | Allows channel estimation; $C_{k,1}$ is *fixed by the standard* (so the receiver knows the absolute phase reference for DQPSK) |
| 2 to 75 | **Data symbols** | Carry **FIC** (Fast Information Channel, 3 symbols) + **MSC** (Main Service Channel, 72 symbols) |

### Step 7 — Net Bit-Rate Derivation
Number of useful bits per OFDM symbol in Mode-I:

$$
N_{bits/sym} = N_{used} \times \log_2(4) = 1536 \times 2 = 3072 \text{ bits}
$$

Ignoring the NULL and PRS overhead, the gross payload per frame is:

$$
R_{frame} = 74 \times 3072 = 227{,}328 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

Dividing by the frame duration $T_F = 96$ ms gives:

$$
\boxed{R_b = \frac{227{,}328}{0.096} \approx 2.432 \text{ Mbit/s}}
$$

This matches the ETSI EN 300 401 specification.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — DQPSK Phase Trajectory

**Problem.** Given a DQPSK input bit sequence `[0, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1, 1]` starting from an initial sub-carrier phase $\varphi_0 = 0$, compute the resulting absolute phase trajectory $\varphi_n$ for $n = 0,1,2,3,4$.

**Solution.**

| $n$ | $(b_{2n}, b_{2n+1})$ | $\Delta\varphi_n$ | $\varphi_n = \varphi_{n-1} + \Delta\varphi_n$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | (0, 1) | $+\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ | $0 + \dfrac{\pi}{4} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$ |
| 1 | (1, 0) | $-\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{4} - \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 0$ |
| 2 | (0, 0) | $+\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$ | $0 + \dfrac{3\pi}{4} = \dfrac{3\pi}{4}$ |
| 3 | (1, 1) | $-\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$ | $\dfrac{3\pi}{4} - \dfrac{3\pi}{4} = 0$ |

Final phase trajectory: $[\pi/4,\ 0,\ 3\pi/4,\ 0]$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Frame Timing for Mode-II

For Mode-II, useful symbol duration is $T_U = 250$ µs and the total symbol duration is $T_S = T_U + T_G = 250 + 62.5 = 312.5$ µs. With 76 symbols per frame:

$$
T_F = 76 \times 312.5 \text{ µs} = 23{,}750 \text{ µs} \approx 24 \text{ ms}
$$

This confirms the KTU/ETSI specification for Mode-II.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics — DAB Modulator Block Architecture

## 4.1 Complete DAB Modulator — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **complete DAB modulator pipeline**, from audio input to RF up-conversion, including all functional blocks in their strict cascade order.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Audio and Data Sources] --> A1[MPEG-1 Layer II Audio Encoder or PAD Input]
    A1 --> A2[Service and Ensemble Multiplexer]
    A2 --> A3[Energy Dispersal Scrambler - 16-bit PRBS]
    A3 --> A4[Outer Convolutional Encoder - Rate 1-4 K=7]
    A4 --> A5[Puncturing Stage - UEP Profile Selection]
    A5 --> A6[Time Interleaver - depth 16 OFDM symbols]
    A6 --> A7[Frequency Interleaver - 1536 sub-carriers]
    A7 --> A8[QPSK Mapper - bit pair to complex symbol]
    A8 --> A9[DQPSK Differential Encoder - previous symbol phase reference]
    A9 --> A10[Active Sub-carrier Allocator - exclude DC and edges]
    A10 --> A11[IFFT Processor - 2048 point inverse FFT]
    A11 --> A12[Guard Interval Insertion - cyclic prefix 25 percent TU]
    A12 --> A13[Digital to Analog Converter DAC]
    A13 --> A14[RF Up-conversion to VHF Band III or L Band]
    A14 --> A15[Power Amplifier and Antenna Feed]

    classDef stage1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937
    classDef stage2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937
    classDef stage3 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937
    classDef stage4 fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#86198f,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937
    classDef stage5 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937

    class A0,A1,A2 stage1
    class A3,A4,A5,A6,A7 stage2
    class A8,A9,A10,A11,A12 stage3
    class A13,A14,A15 stage4
```

## 4.2 DAB Transmission Frame — Internal Symbol Mapping

The following diagram visualises how a single 96 ms DAB frame (Mode-I) is internally partitioned into Synchronisation, Fast Information, and Main Service Channels.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F[DAB Transmission Frame - Duration 96 ms - 76 OFDM Symbols]

    F --> S0[Symbol 0 - NULL Symbol - No Transmission - Coarse AGC]
    F --> S1[Symbol 1 - PRS Phase Reference - Fixed known phase per sub-carrier]
    F --> SC[Symbols 2 to 75 - Data Payload - 74 useful OFDM symbols]

    SC --> FIC[FIC - Fast Information Channel - Symbols 2 to 4 - 3 symbols - MCI and SI signalling]
    SC --> MSC[MSC - Main Service Channel - Symbols 5 to 75 - 71 symbols - Audio and data sub-channels]

    FIC --> FIC1[MCI - Multiplex Configuration Information]
    FIC --> FIC2[SI - Service Information and labels]
    FIC --> FIC3[CA - Conditional Access data]

    MSC --> MSC1[Sub-channel 1 - Audio programme A]
    MSC --> MSC2[Sub-channel 2 - Audio programme B]
    MSC --> MSC3[Sub-channel 3 - PAD - Programme Associated Data]
    MSC --> MSC4[Sub-channel N - Additional services]

    classDef frameNode fill:#1f2937,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#f9fafb
    classDef syncNode fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937
    classDef dataNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937
    classDef subNode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937

    class F frameNode
    class S0,S1 syncNode
    class SC,FIC,MSC dataNode
    class FIC1,FIC2,FIC3,MSC1,MSC2,MSC3,MSC4 subNode
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Signal Spectrum — Why COFDM is Robust

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Single Carrier FM - Tall Narrow Spike - Vulnerable to Selective Fade] --> A1[A narrow fade wipes out the entire programme]
    B[COFDM - Thousands of Narrow Sub-carriers - Flat Wide PSD] --> B1[A narrow fade wipes out only a few sub-carriers - FEC recovers the rest]

    classDef bad fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937
    classDef good fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937

    class A,A1 bad
    class B,B1 good
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Define DAB. List the four DAB transmission modes with their corresponding useful symbol durations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **DAB** stands for **Digital Audio Broadcasting**, standardised by **ETSI EN 300 401** under the Eureka-147 project, providing **CD-quality digital audio** plus data services via **COFDM transmission** in VHF Band-III and L-Band.
- **Mode I**: $T_U = 1$ ms, $N = 1536$, for terrestrial VHF Band-III (SFN operation).
- **Mode II**: $T_U = 250$ µs, $N = 384$, for terrestrial L-Band up to ~1.5 GHz (smaller cells, more Doppler-tolerant).
- **Mode III**: $T_U = 1$ ms, $N = 192$, for satellite and hybrid satellite-terrestrial (≤3 GHz).
- **Mode IV**: $T_U = 250$ µs, $N = 768$, for terrestrial L-Band SFN with smaller cells.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is the function of the Phase Reference Symbol (PRS) in a DAB frame?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The PRS is **OFDM Symbol 1** of every DAB transmission frame.
- Its **complex sub-carrier values are fixed by the ETSI standard** and known to every receiver.
- The receiver uses PRS to perform **channel estimation** (estimate the complex channel transfer function $H(f)$ at every sub-carrier) and to **establish an absolute phase reference** for the subsequent differential DQPSK demodulation.
- Without the PRS, the differential receiver could only detect *changes* in phase but would not know which constellation point it started from after a long deep fade.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a DAB modulator**, briefly describing the role of each block.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Derive an expression for the **total DAB frame duration** in Mode-I and the **ensemble gross bit-rate**, clearly stating all parameters.

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The DAB modulator is a cascaded signal-processing chain whose output is a COFDM/D-QPSK waveform conforming to ETSI EN 300 401. The functional blocks are:

1. **Audio Encoder (MPEG-1 Layer II)** [1 mark]: Compresses each stereo programme from ~1.4 Mbit/s PCM down to typically 128–192 kbit/s. Each programme becomes a *Service Component* with a sub-channel bit-rate.
2. **Service & Ensemble Multiplexer** [1 mark]: Time-division multiplexes multiple services (audio + PAD) into the **Main Service Channel (MSC)** and adds the **Fast Information Channel (FIC)** carrying MCI and SI signalling.
3. **Energy Dispersal Scrambler** [½ mark]: XORs the bit-stream with a 16-bit PRBS (polynomial $x^{16}+x^{14}+x^{13}+x^{11}+1$, initialised to all-ones at every Logical Frame) to ensure DC balance.
4. **Outer Convolutional Encoder** [1 mark]: Mother code rate **1/4, K = 7**, generators (133, 171, 145, 133)$_O$. Provides redundancy for FEC.
5. **Puncturing (UEP)** [½ mark]: Selectively removes parity bits to realise different effective code rates for different protection classes.
6. **Time Interleaver** [½ mark]: Spreads bursts of channel errors over **≈ 16 OFDM symbols** so the convolutional decoder sees pseudo-random errors.
7. **Frequency Interleaver** [½ mark]: Distributes interleaved bits pseudo-randomly over all **1536** used sub-carriers.
8. **QPSK Mapper** [½ mark]: Maps each pair of bits to a complex symbol on the unit circle.
9. **D-QPSK Differential Encoder** [½ mark]: Encodes *phase changes* relative to the previous OFDM symbol, providing robustness against slow phase drift.
10. **IFFT (2048-point)** [½ mark]: Synthesises the time-domain useful OFDM symbol from the complex sub-carrier values.
11. **Guard Interval (Cyclic Prefix) Insertion** [½ mark]: Prepends the last 25 % of the useful symbol to absorb multipath echo and enable SFN operation.

The complete block architecture was rendered in **Section 4.1 (Mermaid diagram)** above.

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given (Mode-I parameters):**
- Useful symbol duration $T_U = 1$ ms = $10^{-3}$ s
- Guard interval $T_G = T_U / 4 = 0.246$ ms
- Used sub-carriers $N_{used} = 1536$
- Number of symbols per frame $= 76$
- DQPSK ⇒ 2 bits per sub-carrier per symbol.

**Step 1 — Total symbol duration** [1 mark]:

$$
T_S = T_U + T_G = T_U\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{4}\right) = \tfrac{5}{4}\,T_U = \tfrac{5}{4} \times 1\text{ ms} = 1.246 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Frame duration** [2 marks]:

$$
T_F = 76 \times T_S = 76 \times 1.246 \text{ ms} = 94.696 \text{ ms} \approx 96 \text{ ms}
$$

(Strictly, $T_F = 76 \times 1.246 = 94.696$ ms; the rounded ETSI figure is **96 ms** because exact sub-carrier spacing is $1/T_U$ with the symbol synchronised to a 96 ms frame grid.)

**Step 3 — Bits per OFDM symbol** [1 mark]:

$$
N_{bits/sym} = N_{used} \times 2 = 1536 \times 2 = 3072 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Useful data symbols per frame** [½ mark]: NULL (1) + PRS (1) excluded ⇒ 74 useful symbols.

**Step 5 — Gross bit-rate** [2 marks]:

$$
R_{frame} = 74 \times 3072 = 227{,}328 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

$$
R_b = \frac{227{,}328 \text{ bits}}{0.096 \text{ s}} \approx 2.368 \text{ Mbit/s}
$$

Accounting for FIC overhead the effective payload is ≈ **2.4 Mbit/s**, matching ETSI specification.

---

> ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain **why Differential QPSK (DQPSK) is used in DAB instead of coherent QPSK**, listing the consequences of this choice on receiver complexity and robustness.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A DAB transmitter uses **Mode-II** with $T_U = 250$ µs. Calculate the **guard-interval duration, total symbol duration, frame duration, sub-carrier spacing, and the occupied bandwidth** for a transmission using all $N = 384$ used sub-carriers.

### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **No need for channel estimation at every sub-carrier for the data symbols** [2 marks]: DQPSK encodes information in the *difference* between successive sub-carrier phases. The receiver only needs to compare $\varphi_k[n]$ with $\varphi_k[n-1]$ — no absolute phase reference is required for the data symbols.
2. **Robust against slow phase drift and frequency-offset residual errors** [2 marks]: Crystal-oscillator imperfections in low-cost receivers produce phase rotations common to *all* sub-carriers; DQPSK removes these automatically because the rotation cancels in the difference.
3. **Receiver complexity reduction** [2 marks]: No complex carrier-recovery PLL or per-sub-carrier equaliser needed before symbol detection — only a simple *differential decoder* (one-symbol delay + complex multiply + threshold). This was a critical design decision when DAB was specified in the early 1990s.
4. **Trade-off — ~3 dB SNR penalty vs coherent QPSK** [½ mark]: Because differential decoding effectively doubles the noise variance, DQPSK requires ~3 dB more SNR than coherent QPSK for the same BER. This is the price paid for the simpler receiver.
5. **Trade-off — error propagation** [½ mark]: A single detection error corrupts *two* consecutive bits. Mitigated by convolutional coding + interleaving.

### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given (Mode-II):** $T_U = 250$ µs, $N_{used} = 384$.

**Step 1 — Guard interval** [1 mark]:

$$
T_G = \frac{T_U}{4} = \frac{250\ \mu s}{4} = 62.5\ \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Total symbol duration** [1 mark]:

$$
T_S = T_U + T_G = 250 + 62.5 = 312.5\ \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Frame duration** [1 mark]:

$$
T_F = 76 \times T_S = 76 \times 312.5\ \mu s = 23{,}750\ \mu s = 23.75 \text{ ms} \approx 24 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Sub-carrier spacing** [1 mark]:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_U} = \frac{1}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 4000 \text{ Hz} = 4 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 5 — Occupied bandwidth** [2 marks]:

$$
BW = N_{used} \times \Delta f = 384 \times 4\text{ kHz} = 1536 \text{ kHz} \approx 1.536 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 6 — Gross bit-rate** [1 mark, bonus]:

$$
R_b = \frac{N_{used} \times 2 \times 74}{T_F} = \frac{384 \times 2 \times 74}{0.024} \approx 2.432 \text{ Mbit/s}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Order of blocks matters!** Many students place the convolutional encoder *after* the QPSK mapper. This is **wrong** — coding must be applied in the *bit domain*, before symbol mapping. Loss: up to **3 marks**.
> 2. **Frame duration must include the NULL symbol.** Writing $75 \times T_S$ instead of $76 \times T_S$ costs **1 mark** instantly.
> 3. **Confusing $T_U$ with $T_S$** in DQPSK sub-carrier spacing calculation. The correct formula is $\Delta f = 1/T_U$, NOT $1/T_S$. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to exclude DC and edge sub-carriers** when stating "1536 used sub-carriers in a 2048-point IFFT". Always explain that the remaining 512 bins are reserved for the DC carrier, Nyquist margin, and unused edge carriers.
> 5. **DQPSK phase-change vs absolute-phase confusion.** DQPSK encodes $\Delta\varphi$, not $\varphi$. A common error is to map bit pairs directly to absolute constellation points. Loss: **2–3 marks** on the modulator block diagram question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **DAB = COFDM + DQPSK + MPEG-1 Layer II audio** (Eureka-147, ETSI EN 300 401).
- [x] **Four transmission modes** with different $(T_U, N_{used})$ pairs; Mode-I is most common for VHF Band-III terrestrial broadcasting.
- [x] **Frame structure: 76 symbols** → 1 NULL + 1 PRS + 74 data symbols (3 FIC + 71 MSC).
- [x] **Sub-carrier spacing** $\Delta f = 1/T_U$ — never use $T_S$.
- [x] **Guard interval** is exactly **25 % of $T_U$** in all four modes — cyclic prefix, not zero-padding.
- [x] **Gross bit-rate ≈ 2.4 Mbit/s** in Modes I/II/III; ≈ 1.2 Mbit/s in Mode-IV (due to half $T_F$).
- [x] **Convolutional encoder**: rate **1/4, K = 7**, generators **(133, 171, 145, 133)$_O$**.
- [x] **Energy dispersal polynomial**: $x^{16}+x^{14}+x^{13}+x^{11}+1$, initial state **all-1**.
- [x] **DQPSK mapping**: 4 phase changes $\{\pm\pi/4, \pm 3\pi/4\}$ — Gray-coded.
- [x] **PRS is the absolute-phase anchor** that enables DQPSK detection after a long deep fade.
- [x] **SFN operation** is enabled purely by the cyclic prefix absorbing artificial multipath from co-frequency transmitters.
- [x] **Receiver simplicity** is the *historical* reason for choosing DQPSK over coherent QPSK — accepted a 3 dB SNR penalty.
- [x] **UEP (Unequal Error Protection)** via puncturing is critical — audio header bits are more protected than audio body bits.
- [x] **Time interleaver depth ≈ 16 symbols**, **Frequency interleaver depth = 1536 sub-carriers** (Mode-I).
- [x] **DAB+ upgrades only the source coder to HE-AAC v2** — the modulator chain remains identical to DAB.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DAB Data Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DAB Data Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DAB Data Structure Definition:**
> The **DAB (Digital Audio Broadcasting) Data Structure** is a standardized, hierarchical multiplex frame architecture defined under **ETSI EN 300 401** that organizes digital audio, data services, and signalling information into a single broadcast ensemble. It consists of three primary layers: the **Synchronization Channel**, the **Fast Information Channel (FIC)**, and the **Main Service Channel (MSC)**, transmitted within fixed-duration transmission frames using OFDM modulation.

The DAB transmission system is a **multi-service, multi-receiver, multi-bandwidth** digital broadcasting system. Unlike FM which carries one audio program per carrier, DAB multiplexes **several programs and data services** into a single "ensemble" and broadcasts them together. The data structure is what makes this multiplexing possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standardization Body:** European Telecommunications Standards Institute (**ETSI**) — Document Reference: **ETSI EN 300 401** (Radio Broadcasting Systems; Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB) to mobile, portable and fixed receivers).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Radio Supermarket" Model

Imagine a **large supermarket** that delivers groceries to your home every 96 milliseconds:

| Supermarket Element | DAB Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| The delivery truck | DAB Transmitter | Carries the entire payload |
| The shopping bag | **DAB Transmission Frame** (96 ms) | One complete delivery unit |
| Product labels and aisle signs | **Fast Information Channel (FIC)** | Tells the radio *where* each product is located |
| The actual groceries | **Main Service Channel (MSC)** | Contains the real content (audio, data) |
| Individual product items | **Sub-channels / Service Components** | One radio station or data stream |
| Aisle numbers | **CIFs (Common Interleaved Frames)** | Logical containers for capacity allocation |
| A delivery schedule card | **FIBs and FIGs (Fast Information Blocks / Groups)** | Signalling metadata |

In simple terms, the **FIC is the "TV Guide"** of the DAB signal — it tells the receiver *what services are available, where they are located, and how to decode them*. The **MSC is the "actual content"** — the audio and data streams the listener actually wants to hear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** The FIC is **not** interleaved or convolutionally encoded in the same way as the MSC. It uses a fixed, robust coding (rate 1/3 with 4-FEC) to ensure signalling survives even under poor reception.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Transmission Frame Duration (Mode I, II, IV):** **96 ms**
- **Transmission Frame Duration (Mode III — Satellite):** **24 ms**
- **Total Carriers (Mode I):** **1536** carriers (usable subcarriers per OFDM symbol)
- **OFDM Symbols per Frame (Mode I):** **76** symbols
- **Guard Interval (Mode I):** **246 µs** (cyclic prefix)
- **MSC Net Bit Rate (Mode I, with UEP):** Approximately **2.4 Mbit/s**
- **Total Channel Bandwidth:** **1.536 MHz** per DAB block
- **Capacity Unit (CU):** **64 bits** (smallest addressable MSC resource)

---

### 1.4 The Three Logical Channels of a DAB Frame

> [!NOTE]
> **The 3-Channel DAB Frame Architecture:**
> 1. **Synchronization Channel** — Phase Reference Symbol (PRS) + Automatic Frequency Control (AFC)
> 2. **Fast Information Channel (FIC)** — Signalling and multiplex configuration
> 3. **Main Service Channel (MSC)** — Audio, data, and packet-mode service components

This three-channel split is the **foundational concept** of DAB. Every Module-2 question on DAB data structure will reference this hierarchy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Layer-by-Layer Breakdown

The DAB data structure is best understood as a **5-level hierarchy** from the broadest container down to the smallest data unit:

#### **Level 1: The DAB Ensemble (Multiplex)**
- A collection of **multiple audio services and data services** bundled into one RF transmission
- Identified by a **16-bit Ensemble Identifier (EId)**
- Allocated by the network operator; receivers use the EId to find the desired ensemble

#### **Level 2: Services**
- A **Service** is a user-selectable output (e.g., "BBC Radio 1", "All India Radio News")
- Identified by a **Service Identifier (SId)** of **16 or 32 bits**
- A service may comprise **one or more service components** (e.g., audio + slideshow data + EPG)

#### **Level 3: Service Components**
- The smallest individually selectable stream
- Identified by an **8-bit Service Component Identifier (SCId)**
- Types:
  * **Audio stream** (DAB audio codec: MPEG-1 Layer II, HE-AAC v2 in DAB+)
  * **PAD (Programme Associated Data)** — text, images, dynamic labels
  * **NPAD (Non-Programme Associated Data)** — file casting, TPEG traffic data
  * **Packet-mode data** (e.g., IP datagrams via MOT protocol)

#### **Level 4: Sub-channels within the MSC**
- A **sub-channel** is the physical time-frequency slot in the MSC
- Defined by a **start address** (CIF index) and a **length** (in Capacity Units)
- Identified by an **8-bit Sub-channel Identifier (SubChId)**

#### **Level 5: Capacity Units (CUs)**
- The **smallest allocatable unit** of MSC bandwidth
- Each CU = **64 bits** at the CIF level
- A sub-channel must be a multiple of CUs

---

### 2.2 Detailed Anatomy of the Fast Information Channel (FIC)

The FIC is **non-interleaved** and uses **punctured convolutional coding** with a fixed protection level to ensure reliable signalling reception.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIC Building Blocks (mnemonic: "FIG-FIB-FIC"):**
> - **FIC** = 96 ms worth of signalling (one per transmission frame)
> - **FIB** (Fast Information Block) = **256 bits** each; the FIC contains **12 FIBs** in Mode I
> - **FIG** (Fast Information Group) = Variable length; the meaningful payload inside a FIB
> - **FIG type + FIG length header** = **5 bits** (3-bit type + 2-bit length indicator)
> - **FIG data field** = 0–30 bytes depending on length field

#### **The 8 FIG Types (ETS 300 401 §5.2):**

| FIG Type | Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **FIG 0/0** | MCI — Ensemble Information | Ensemble Identifier, country, local time offset |
| **FIG 0/1** | MCI — Sub-channel Organization | Sub-channel IDs, start CUs, bit rate, protection level |
| **FIG 0/2** | MCI — Service Organization | Service-to-component mapping |
| **FIG 0/3** | MCI — Service Component (packet mode) | Packet address, CA flag, DG flag |
| **FIG 0/4** | MCI — Service Component (stream mode) | Audio codec, sampling rate, mono/stereo |
| **FIG 0/5** | MCI — Service Component language | Language code, programme type |
| **FIG 0/6** | MCI — Application Linking | Application reference, data services |
| **FIG 0/7** | MCI — FEC Sub-channel Organization | FEC for sub-channels |
| **FIG 0/8** | MCI — Service Global Definition | Ensemble-wide service list |
| **FIG 0/9** | MCI — Country / LTO / ECC | Country identification, local time offset |
| **FIG 0/10** | MCI — Date and Time | Real-time clock |
| **FIG 0/11** | MCI — Region definition | Regional ensemble addressing |
| **FIG 0/12** | MCI — User Application | User application type for X-PAD |
| **FIG 0/13** | MCI — Packet data | Data group length, CA organization |
| **FIG 0/14** | MCI — CA messages | Conditional access (subscription) |
| **FIG 0/15–21** | MCI — FIDC, Region, etc. | Extended signalling |
| **FIG 1/x** | **Service Information (SI)** | Programme Type (PTY), Service Label, Programme Number |
| **FIG 2/x** | **Conditional Access (CA)** | Entitlement Management Messages (EMM) |
| **FIG 5/x** | **Fast Information Data Channel (FIDC)** | Unidirectional data services |
| **FIG 6/x** | **CA Application Information** | |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Tip for Exam:** "**M-C-I-S**" — **M**ultiplex Configuration Info, **C**onditional Access, **I**nformation (service), **S**pecial (FIDC). FIGs 0, 1, 2, 5 are the four main families you will be asked about.

---

### 2.3 Detailed Anatomy of the Main Service Channel (MSC)

The MSC carries the **actual content** of the broadcast — audio, PAD, and data.

- The MSC is logically a sequence of **Common Interleaved Frames (CIFs)**
- **One CIF = 55296 bits** in Mode I
- A CIF is divided into **864 Capacity Units (CUs)** of **64 bits each**
- **Time Interleaving** is applied over the full **24 ms** of the MSC (4 CIFs in Mode I)
- **Frequency Interleaving** spreads data across all OFDM carriers

#### **MSC Sub-channel Capacity Calculation:**

Given a sub-channel with $L_{cu}$ capacity units, the gross bit rate of the sub-channel is:

$$R_{sub} = \frac{L_{cu} \times 64 \text{ bits}}{24 \text{ ms}}$$

Since the **CIF repeats every 24 ms** in Mode I, the **net bit rate** after convolutional coding and puncturing depends on the **protection profile** (UEP or EEP) and **protection level**.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — DAB Data Structure Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| DAB frame duration (Mode I) | $T_{f}$ | $T_{f} = 96$ | ms |
| CIF duration (Mode I) | $T_{CIF}$ | $T_{CIF} = 24$ | ms |
| CIF bits | $N_{CIF}$ | $N_{CIF} = 55296$ | bits |
| Capacity Unit size | $N_{CU}$ | $N_{CU} = 64$ | bits |
| Number of CUs per CIF | $N_{CU}^{total}$ | $N_{CU}^{total} = 864$ | CUs |
| Sub-channel bit rate | $R_{sub}$ | $R_{sub} = \frac{L_{cu} \times 64}{24 \times 10^{-3}}$ | bit/s |
