| Number of CUs in FIC | $N_{CU}^{FIC}$ | $N_{CU}^{FIC} = 96$ | CUs |
| Number of FIBs per FIC | $N_{FIB}$ | $N_{FIB} = 12$ | FIBs |
| Bits per FIB | $N_{FIB}^{bits}$ | $N_{FIB}^{bits} = 256$ | bits |
| Total number of subcarriers (Mode I) | $N_{c}$ | $N_{c} = 1536$ | carriers |
| Null carriers (DC + edges) | $N_{null}$ | $N_{null} = 0$ (centre DC excluded) | — |
| OFDM symbol duration (useful) | $T_{u}$ | $T_{u} = 1$ | ms |
| Guard interval (Mode I) | $\Delta$ | $\Delta = 246$ | µs |
| Total OFDM symbol duration | $T_{s}$ | $T_{s} = T_{u} + \Delta = 1.246$ | ms |
| OFDM symbols per frame | $N_{sym}$ | $N_{sym} = 76$ | symbols |
| Channel bandwidth | $BW$ | $BW = 1.536$ | MHz |
| Carrier spacing | $\Delta f$ | $\Delta f = 1$ | kHz |
| Convolutional code rates | $r$ | $r \in \{1/4, 1/3, 1/2, 2/3, 3/4\}$ | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** Students often confuse the *CIF* (24 ms, 55296 bits) with the *Transmission Frame* (96 ms, containing 4 CIFs). The **CIF is the logical data unit of the MSC**; the **transmission frame is the physical OFDM structure**.

---

### 2.5 Why This Structure? — Engineering Justification

The DAB data structure is engineered to satisfy three simultaneous goals:

1. **Robustness under multipath / Doppler** → The 96 ms frame and 1 ms useful OFDM symbol allow reception at vehicle speeds up to 200 km/h (Mode I). The deep time interleaving (24 ms) spreads burst errors caused by shadowing.
2. **Spectrum efficiency** → OFDM with 1536 subcarriers in a 1.536 MHz block achieves spectral efficiency of ~1.5 bit/s/Hz with QPSK.
3. **Flexible multiplexing** → Capacity Units and sub-channels allow the network operator to dynamically re-allocate bandwidth between audio and data services, supporting one ~1.5 Mbps audio service or 6–8 lower-rate services per ensemble.

---

### 2.6 The Complete DAB Transmitter Data Path

The data structure is populated through a specific signal-processing chain:

> [!NOTE]
> **DAB Transmitter Chain (per logical channel):**
> **Source (audio/data) → Energy Dispersal Scrambling (PRBS) → Outer Convolutional Encoder (rate 1/3, memory 6) → Puncturing (to UEP/EEP rate) → Time Interleaving (only MSC) → Frequency Interleaving (only MSC) → QPSK Mapping → OFDM Modulation → DAC → RF Upconversion.**

For the **FIC**: same chain but **no time interleaving, no frequency interleaving**, and **fixed protection level 1/3 with strong puncture pattern**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sub-Channel Capacity to Net Bit Rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Show the complete derivation from sub-channel CU allocation to final audio bit rate after convolutional coding and puncturing.

**Step 1 — Define input parameters:**

Let a sub-channel be allocated $L_{cu}$ capacity units within the CIF.

**Step 2 — Calculate the raw CIF payload bits contributed by the sub-channel:**

The CIF contains 864 CUs of 64 bits each. The sub-channel contributes:

$$B_{CIF}^{sub} = L_{cu} \times N_{CU} = L_{cu} \times 64 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — The CIF repeats every $T_{CIF} = 24$ ms in Mode I:**

Therefore, the **input bit rate to the convolutional encoder** is:

$$R_{in} = \frac{L_{cu} \times 64}{24 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ bit/s}$$

$$R_{in} = \frac{L_{cu} \times 64}{0.024}$$

$$R_{in} = 2666.67 \times L_{cu} \text{ bit/s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply convolutional coding of rate $r$:**

The convolutional encoder of constraint length 7 produces output at rate $R_{out}$:

$$R_{out} = \frac{R_{in}}{r}$$

For example, with $r = 1/2$:

$$R_{out} = 2 \times R_{in} = 5333.33 \times L_{cu} \text{ bit/s}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the net audio bit rate for a typical DAB configuration:**

Suppose a sub-channel is allocated **48 CUs** with **EEP protection level 3-A** (code rate = 1/2).

**Net bit rate calculation:**

$$R_{in} = 2666.67 \times 48$$

$$R_{in} = 128{,}000 \text{ bit/s} = 128 \text{ kbit/s}$$

After rate-1/2 convolutional coding:

$$R_{out} = 2 \times 128 = 256 \text{ kbit/s (channel bit rate)}$$

The **net useful audio rate** available to the MPEG Layer II decoder is therefore **128 kbit/s**, which corresponds to **stereo audio at 48 kHz sampling** (a typical DAB stereo broadcast).

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical DAB audio rates:** 32, 48, 56, 64, 80, 96, 112, 128, 160, 192, 224, 256, 320, 384 kbit/s. The 128 kbit/s stereo is the most common broadcast configuration worldwide.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Available MSC Capacity in Mode I

**Step 1 — Total bits per CIF:**

$$N_{CIF}^{total} = 864 \text{ CUs} \times 64 \text{ bits/CU} = 55{,}296 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — The FIC consumes the equivalent of 96 CUs (this is reserved capacity, not interleaved in the MSC, but the FIC occupies a fixed portion of the frame):**

$$N_{FIC}^{CUs} = 96 \text{ CUs}$$

**Step 3 — Available MSC CUs:**

$$N_{MSC}^{CUs} = 864 - 96 = 768 \text{ CUs}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum MSC bit rate (at the input of convolutional encoder):**

$$R_{MSC}^{max} = \frac{768 \times 64}{24 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$R_{MSC}^{max} = \frac{49{,}152}{0.024}$$

$$R_{MSC}^{max} = 2{,}048{,}000 \text{ bit/s} \approx 2.048 \text{ Mbit/s}$$

**Step 5 — Verify against the ETSI standard:**

The standard states the MSC supports approximately **2.3 Mbit/s** gross at protection level 1/4, or **2.4 Mbit/s** with UEP. The discrepancy is due to guard-band null carriers and signalling overhead. The calculation confirms the order of magnitude.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Number of FIBs and FIG Packing Efficiency

**Step 1 — The FIC is 96 ms long, equivalent to 4 CIFs in Mode I.**

In Mode I, the FIC occupies 12 FIBs per transmission frame:

$$N_{FIB} = 12 \text{ FIBs/frame}$$

**Step 2 — Each FIB contains:**

- **Header:** 16 bits (FIB type indicator, cyclic redundancy, etc.)
- **Data field:** 240 bits (variable-length FIGs)

$$N_{FIB}^{header} = 16 \text{ bits}, \quad N_{FIB}^{data} = 240 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Each FIG starts with a 5-bit header (3-bit type + 2-bit length).**

A FIG of type 0 with the "long form" length indicator can carry up to 30 bytes (240 bits) of payload.

**Step 4 — Maximum FIG packing per FIB:**

$$\text{FIGs per FIB} = \left\lfloor \frac{240 \text{ bits}}{8 \text{ bits (min FIG) + 5 bits header}} \right\rfloor \approx 18 \text{ short FIGs}$$

In practice, **5–10 FIGs per FIB** is typical because real FIGs are 4–20 bytes long.

**Step 5 — Total FIGs per transmission frame:**

$$N_{FIG}^{frame} \approx 12 \times 7 = 84 \text{ FIGs/frame}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing FIC and MSC

| Property | FIC (Fast Information Channel) | MSC (Main Service Channel) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Signalling, multiplex config | Audio, data, PAD |
| **Time interleaving** | **NO** | **YES** (24 ms depth) |
| **Frequency interleaving** | **NO** | **YES** |
| **Convolutional coding** | Fixed rate 1/3, strong puncture | Variable 1/4 to 3/4, UEP/EEP |
| **Logical unit** | FIB (256 bits) | CIF (55,296 bits) |
| **Number per frame (Mode I)** | 12 FIBs | 4 CIFs |
| **CUs allocated (Mode I)** | 96 CUs | 768 CUs |
| **Protection level** | Always maximum (most robust) | Configurable (UEP/EEP) |
| **Carries PAD?** | No | Yes (in audio sub-channels) |
| **Can carry conditional access?** | Yes (FIG 2/x) | No |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam memory aid:** "**FIC is for the brain (signalling), MSC is for the ears (content)**." FIC is never time-interleaved because loss of signalling data would cause the receiver to lose lock on the entire multiplex.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — DAB Transmission Frame Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DAB Transmission Frame 96 ms Mode I"] --> B["Synchronization Channel PRS + AFC"]
    A --> C["Fast Information Channel FIC 12 FIBs"]
    A --> D["Main Service Channel MSC 4 CIFs 864 CUs"]

    C --> C1["FIB 1 256 bits"]
    C --> C2["FIB 2 256 bits"]
    C --> C3["FIB N 12th FIB"]

    C1 --> D1["FIG 0 MCI Multiplex Config"]
    C1 --> D2["FIG 1 SI Service Info"]
    C1 --> D3["FIG 2 CA Conditional Access"]
    C1 --> D4["FIG 5 FIDC Fast Data"]

    D --> E1["CIF 1 55296 bits 24 ms"]
    D --> E2["CIF 2 55296 bits 24 ms"]
    D --> E3["CIF 3 55296 bits 24 ms"]
    D --> E4["CIF 4 55296 bits 24 ms"]

    E1 --> F1["Sub-channel 1 Audio Service A 64 CU"]
    E1 --> F2["Sub-channel 2 Audio Service B 48 CU"]
    E1 --> F3["Sub-channel 3 Data PAD 8 CU"]
    E1 --> F4["Unused CUs 744 CU"]

    F1 --> G1["MPEG Layer II Audio 128 kbps"]
    F1 --> G2["Programme Associated Data PAD"]

    F2 --> H1["MPEG Layer II Audio 96 kbps"]

    F3 --> I1["MOT Slideshow / DLS Text"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — DAB Transmitter Data Processing Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Audio Source 1"] --> MUX["Multiplexer MUX"]
    A2["Audio Source 2"] --> MUX
    D1["Data Service PAD NPAD"] --> MUX
    SIG["Signalling Generator FIGs"] --> MUX

    MUX --> ED["Energy Dispersal Scrambler PRBS X9 + X5 + 1"]
    ED --> CC["Convolutional Encoder Rate 1/3 Memory 6"]
    CC --> PUN["Puncturing Unit UEP EEP"]
    PUN --> TI["Time Interleaver 24 ms depth MSC only"]
    TI --> FI["Frequency Interleaver MSC only"]
    FI --> MAP["QPSK Symbol Mapper"]
    SIG --> SIGED["FIC Path No time/freq interleaving"]
    SIGED --> MAP

    MAP --> OFDM["OFDM Modulator IFFT 1536 carriers Mode I"]
    OFDM --> DAC["Digital to Analog Converter"]
    DAC --> RF["RF Upconversion VHF Band III or L Band"]
    RF --> ANT["Transmit Antenna"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Hierarchy — Service-to-Container Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ENS["DAB Ensemble 16 bit EId"] --> S1["Service 1 BBC Radio 1"]
    ENS --> S2["Service 2 Classic FM"]
    ENS --> S3["Service 3 TPEG Traffic Data"]

    S1 --> SC1a["Service Component 1a Primary Audio 128 kbps"]
    S1 --> SC1b["Service Component 1b MOT Slideshow"]

    S2 --> SC2a["Service Component 2a Primary Audio 192 kbps"]
    S2 --> SC2b["Service Component 2b DLS Text PAD"]

    S3 --> SC3a["Service Component 3a TPEG Packets 32 kbps"]

    SC1a --> SUB1["Sub-channel ID 1 Start CU 0 Length 48 CUs"]
    SC1b --> SUB1
    SC2a --> SUB2["Sub-channel ID 2 Start CU 48 Length 64 CUs"]
    SC2b --> SUB2
    SC3a --> SUB3["Sub-channel ID 3 Start CU 112 Length 12 CUs"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — FIC Multiplex Configuration Decoding Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RF["FIC Received"] --> FIBD["FIB Demodulator 12 FIBs"]
    FIBD --> FIGP["FIG Parser 3 bit Type + 2 bit Length"]

    FIGP --> F0["FIG 0 MCI Type"]
    FIGP --> F1["FIG 1 SI Type"]
    FIGP --> F2["FIG 2 CA Type"]
    FIGP --> F5["FIG 5 FIDC Type"]

    F0 --> F00["FIG 0 0 Ensemble ID Country"]
    F0 --> F01["FIG 0 1 Sub-channel Organization"]
    F0 --> F02["FIG 0 2 Service Organization"]
    F0 --> F04["FIG 0 4 Stream Mode Audio Codec"]

    F00 --> DEC["Receiver Builds Service Database"]
    F01 --> DEC
    F02 --> DEC
    F04 --> DEC
    F1 --> DEC
    F2 --> DEC
    F5 --> DEC

    DEC --> TUNE["Tune to Selected Service Sub-channel"]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DAB Receiver Demultiplexer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN["RF Front End Tuner VHF or L Band"] --> ADC["ADC 2.048 MHz sampling"]
    ADC --> FFT["2048 point FFT OFDM Demodulator"]
    FFT --> SYNC["Frame Synchronization PRS Detection"]
    FFT --> FICX["FIC Demodulator QPSK De-map Punctured Viterbi"]
    FFT --> MSCD["MSC Demodulator QPSK De-map Punctured Viterbi"]

    FICX --> FIGD["FIG Decoder Service List"]
    FIGD --> CTRL["Receiver Controller CPU"]

    MSCD --> FID["Frequency De-interleaver"]
    FID --> TID["Time De-interleaver 24 ms"]
    TID --> VC["Viterbi Decoder Rate 1/3"]
    VC --> DES["Energy Dispersal De-scrambler"]
    DES --> DMX["Service De-multiplexer by CU addresses from FIG 0 1"]

    CTRL --> DMX
    DMX --> AUD["MPEG Layer II Audio Decoder"]
    DMX --> PAD["PAD Extractor DLS Slideshow MOT"]
    DMX --> DAT["Packet Data TPEG IP Datagrams"]

    AUD --> SPK["Loudspeaker Output"]
    PAD --> DISP["Display Screen"]
    DAT --> APP["Data Application"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each of the four diagrams above represents a different "view" of the same DAB system. The first shows the *physical frame layout*; the second shows the *transmitter processing chain*; the third shows the *logical service-to-subchannel mapping*; the fourth shows the *receiver demultiplexer*. KTU exam questions often ask you to draw one or more of these views.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

#### **Question 1** (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> *List the three logical channels of a DAB transmission frame and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer:**

The DAB transmission frame consists of three logical channels:

1. **Synchronization Channel** — Provides the Phase Reference Symbol (PRS) and Automatic Frequency Control (AFC) reference to allow the receiver to achieve frame, time, and frequency synchronization.
2. **Fast Information Channel (FIC)** — Carries multiplex configuration and signalling information (FIGs) such as ensemble ID, sub-channel organization, service list, and programme-type codes. It uses a fixed robust code rate and no time/frequency interleaving.
3. **Main Service Channel (MSC)** — Carries the actual service components (audio, PAD, and data) of all services in the ensemble. It is time- and frequency-interleaved and supports variable UEP/EEP protection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark per channel + 1 mark for correct functional description. Award full marks if the student lists all three and gives a correct one-line function for each.

---

#### **Question 2** (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> *What is a FIG in the DAB Fast Information Channel? Name the four main FIG type families.*

**Model Answer:**

A **FIG (Fast Information Group)** is the smallest meaningful signalling unit inside an FIB of the DAB FIC. Each FIG begins with a 5-bit header (3-bit type field + 2-bit length indicator) and is followed by a variable-length data field.

The four main FIG type families are:

1. **FIG type 0** — Multiplex Configuration Information (MCI): ensemble ID, sub-channel organization, service-component-to-subchannel mapping, country codes.
2. **FIG type 1** — Service Information (SI): programme type (PTY), service label, programme number.
3. **FIG type 2** — Conditional Access (CA): entitlement messages for pay-radio services.
4. **FIG type 5** — Fast Information Data Channel (FIDC): unidirectional data services carried directly in the FIC.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (2 × 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]**
> **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the hierarchical data structure of a DAB transmission frame. Discuss the role of the Fast Information Channel (FIC) and the Main Service Channel (MSC). **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Describe in detail the structure of a Common Interleaved Frame (CIF) in Mode I. How are sub-channels defined within the CIF? A DAB ensemble in Mode I allocates 96 capacity units to a sub-channel with EEP protection level 3-A (convolutional code rate = 1/2). Calculate the net audio bit rate available at the input of the MPEG audio decoder. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The DAB transmission frame in Mode I has a total duration of 96 ms and is logically divided into three channels:

- **Synchronization Channel:** Provides the Phase Reference Symbol (PRS) at the start of each frame. The PRS consists of a known OFDM symbol pattern that allows the receiver to perform channel estimation (for equalization), frame timing recovery, and AFC.

- **Fast Information Channel (FIC):** Carries 12 Fast Information Blocks (FIBs) of 256 bits each (total 3072 bits per frame). Each FIB is divided into Fast Information Groups (FIGs) which are 5-bit-header-prefixed signalling data units. The FIC is **not time-interleaved** but is **convolutionally encoded at fixed rate 1/3 with strong puncturing** for maximum robustness. Its contents (FIGs) tell the receiver *how* the ensemble is organized.

- **Main Service Channel (MSC):** Carries 4 Common Interleaved Frames (CIFs) per transmission frame (one CIF every 24 ms). The MSC is both time-interleaved (24 ms depth) and frequency-interleaved for robustness against burst and selective-fading errors. It contains the actual audio service components, PAD (Programme Associated Data), and data services of the ensemble.

The role of the FIC is essentially **multiplex configuration signalling** — it provides the receiver with a "service table" mapping each service ID to its sub-channel location, codec, and protection level. The role of the MSC is to **deliver content** — the actual audio, video, or data the listener has selected.

**Block diagram: See Section 4.1 (Mermaid Diagram 1).**

> **Marking Distribution for (a):** 2 marks for the three-channel split; 2 marks for FIC structure; 2 marks for MSC structure; 1 mark for the diagram.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Structure of the CIF (Mode I):**

- A **Common Interleaved Frame (CIF)** is the logical data unit of the MSC.
- In Mode I, one CIF has a duration of **24 ms** and contains **55,296 bits**.
- The CIF is divided into **864 Capacity Units (CUs)**, each of **64 bits**.
- 96 CUs of the CIF are reserved for the FIC; the remaining **768 CUs** are available for MSC sub-channels.
- A **sub-channel** is a contiguous or non-contiguous allocation of CUs (always a multiple of CUs) that carries one logical stream (audio or data).
- Each sub-channel is identified by an 8-bit **SubChId** and defined by a **start CU address** and a **length in CUs** (both carried in FIG 0/1).
- Sub-channels may use **UEP (Unequal Error Protection)** or **EEP (Equal Error Protection)** profiles, with protection levels 1–5.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $L_{cu} = 96$ CUs, $r = 1/2$, $T_{CIF} = 24$ ms, $N_{CU} = 64$ bits.

**Step 1 — Input bit rate to the convolutional encoder (i.e., the net bit rate available to the audio codec):**

$$R_{in} = \frac{L_{cu} \times N_{CU}}{T_{CIF}}$$

$$R_{in} = \frac{96 \times 64}{24 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$R_{in} = \frac{6144}{0.024}$$

$$R_{in} = 256{,}000 \text{ bit/s}$$

$$R_{in} = 256 \text{ kbit/s}$$

**Step 2 — Channel bit rate after rate-1/2 convolutional coding:**

$$R_{channel} = \frac{R_{in}}{r} = \frac{256}{1/2} = 512 \text{ kbit/s}$$

**Answer:** The net audio bit rate available to the MPEG Layer II audio decoder is **256 kbit/s**. This corresponds to high-quality stereo audio at 48 kHz sampling rate.

> **Marking Distribution for (b):** 2 marks for CIF structure; 1 mark for sub-channel definition; 1 mark for formula; 2 marks for substitution; 1 mark for correct final answer with units.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2 — OR]**
> **(a)** Explain the Fast Information Channel (FIC) of DAB in detail. Describe the structure of a Fast Information Block (FIB) and list at least six different FIG types from the Multiplex Configuration Information (MCI) family, explaining the purpose of each. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A DAB receiver must decode the FIC before it can tune to a service. Draw a flowchart showing the receiver's demultiplexing process from RF input to audio output, clearly indicating where the FIC is processed and used. Also explain the difference between the FIC and MSC in terms of interleaving, protection, and content. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The **Fast Information Channel (FIC)** is the signalling channel of a DAB ensemble. Its purpose is to allow the receiver to determine, without having to scan the content, what services are available, how they are organized, and how to decode them.

**FIB Structure:**

- One transmission frame (96 ms) contains **12 FIBs** in Mode I.
- Each **FIB is 256 bits** long and consists of:
  - A **16-bit header** (FIB data flag, cyclic redundancy check, and other control bits)
  - A **240-bit data field** containing one or more **FIGs**
- A FIG begins with a **5-bit header** (3 bits for FIG type, 2 bits for the length indicator which tells the receiver how many bytes of FIG follow)
- Multiple FIGs can be packed into a single FIB until the 240-bit data field is filled

**Six FIG types from the MCI family (FIG 0/x):**

| FIG | Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **FIG 0/0** | Ensemble Information | 16-bit EId, country code, ensemble label |
| **FIG 0/1** | Sub-channel Organization | SubChId, start CU, length in CU, bit rate, protection level |
| **FIG 0/2** | Service Organization | Maps SId to SubChId; defines primary component |
| **FIG 0/3** | Service Component in Packet Mode | DG flag, CA flag, packet address |
| **FIG 0/4** | Service Component in Stream Mode | Audio codec type (MPEG Layer II), sampling rate, mono/stereo flag |
| **FIG 0/5** | Service Component Language | Language code (8-bit) and programme type (PTY) |
| **FIG 0/9** | Country / Local Time Offset | Country code, local time offset, daylight-saving flag |
| **FIG 0/10** | Date and Time | Real-time-clock setting for receiver display |

> **Marking Distribution for (a):** 2 marks for FIC purpose; 2 marks for FIB/FIG structure; 3 marks for the six FIGs (0.5 each).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**DAB Receiver Demultiplexing Flowchart:** (See Section 4.5 — Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture for the complete diagram.) The key steps are:

1. **RF front-end tuner** selects the DAB block frequency (e.g., 225.648 MHz — Block 12, Band III)
2. **ADC** samples at 2.048 MHz
3. **FFT (2048-point)** performs OFDM demodulation, extracting all 1536 subcarriers
4. **Frame synchronization** using the PRS
5. **FIC demodulation** — QPSK de-mapping, Viterbi decoding (rate 1/3), FIG parsing → **builds the service database in receiver memory**
6. **MSC demodulation** — QPSK de-mapping, Viterbi decoding
7. **Frequency de-interleaving** and **time de-interleaving** of the MSC
8. **Service de-multiplexer** uses the FIC database to extract the requested sub-channel's CUs
9. **Energy dispersal de-scrambling**
10. **Audio decoder** (MPEG Layer II) → loudspeaker; **PAD extraction** → display

**Differences between FIC and MSC:**

| Property | FIC | MSC |
|---|---|---|
| **Interleaving** | None (time and frequency) | Both time (24 ms) and frequency |
| **Protection** | Fixed 1/3, robust puncture | Variable UEP/EEP, rates 1/4 to 3/4 |
| **Content** | Signalling (FIGs) | Audio, data, PAD |
| **Logical unit** | FIB (256 bits) | CIF (55,296 bits) |
| **Repetition** | 12 per frame | 4 CIFs per frame |
| **Receiver dependency** | Must be decoded first | Tuned only after FIC is known |

> **Marking Distribution for (b):** 3 marks for the flowchart; 4 marks for the comparison table with correct distinctions.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in DAB Data Structure Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CIF and Transmission Frame** — CIF is 24 ms (one logical MSC unit); Transmission Frame is 96 ms (4 CIFs + 1 PRS + 1 FIC slot). Examiners *will* deduct marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting that the FIC is NOT time-interleaved** — This is a favourite short-answer trap. Always mention the no-time-interleaving property of the FIC explicitly.
> 3. **Wrong CU count** — Mode I CIF has **864 CUs total** (not 768). 96 CUs are reserved for the FIC; 768 CUs are available for MSC sub-channels. Don't mix these up.
> 4. **Forgetting the unit in numerical answers** — "256" is wrong. "256 kbit/s" is right. **Always include units.**
> 5. **Confusing FIG 0/0 with FIG 0/1** — FIG 0/0 carries the *ensemble* info; FIG 0/1 carries *sub-channel* organization. Many students swap these.
> 6. **Writing "FIG = FIB"** — FIG is a sub-unit of an FIB. The hierarchy is **FIC → FIB → FIG**.
> 7. **Wrong convolutional code rate** — The convolutional encoder is rate 1/3 (mother code), with *puncturing* applied to obtain higher effective rates (1/2, 2/3, 3/4).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist (Print-and-Carry):**
>
> - [ ] DAB standard is **ETSI EN 300 401** (not ISO, not IEEE)
> - [ ] Frame duration: **96 ms** (Mode I, II, IV); **24 ms** (Mode III)
> - [ ] **Three logical channels:** Synchronization, **FIC**, **MSC**
> - [ ] **FIC:** 12 FIBs × 256 bits = 3072 bits/frame; **NOT** time/frequency interleaved
> - [ ] **MSC:** 4 CIFs × 55,296 bits = 221,184 bits/frame
> - [ ] **CIF:** 864 CUs × 64 bits = 55,296 bits; duration 24 ms
> - [ ] **96 CUs** of CIF reserved for FIC; **768 CUs** for MSC sub-channels
> - [ ] **FIG hierarchy:** FIG type (3 bits) + length (2 bits) = 5-bit header
> - [ ] **FIG families:** Type 0 (MCI), Type 1 (SI), Type 2 (CA), Type 5 (FIDC)
> - [ ] **OFDM:** 1536 carriers in Mode I; 1 kHz carrier spacing; 1 ms useful symbol
> - [ ] **Guard interval:** 246 µs in Mode I
> - [ ] **Modulation:** π/4-DQPSK on all data subcarriers
> - [ ] **Convolutional code:** Rate 1/3, memory 6, with puncturing
> - [ ] **Service Identifiers:** Ensemble (16 bits), Service (16/32 bits), Component (8 bits), Sub-channel (8 bits)
> - [ ] **Audio codec:** MPEG-1 Layer II (DAB) or HE-AAC v2 (DAB+)
> - [ ] **Time interleaving depth:** 24 ms in MSC (4 CIFs in Mode I)
> - [ ] **Typical audio bit rate:** 128 kbit/s stereo at 48 kHz sampling
> - [ ] **Energy dispersal:** PRBS polynomial $X^9 + X^5 + 1$ per ETSI EN 300 401
> - [ ] **Frequency bands:** Band III (~174–240 MHz) and L-band (~1452–1492 MHz)
> - [ ] **Bandwidth per DAB block:** 1.536 MHz
> - [ ] **DAB+ uses HE-AAC v2** and adds an extra Reed-Solomon outer FEC layer
> - [ ] **T-DAB** = Terrestrial DAB (the main broadcast mode); **DMB** = Digital Multimedia Broadcasting (extension for video)

---

**End of Notes — DAB Data Structure (Module 2, OEEVT722 Entertainment Electronics, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: DAB Single Frequency Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** DAB Single Frequency Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DAB Single Frequency Network (SFN):**
> A *Single Frequency Network* in Digital Audio Broadcasting (DAB) is a **broadcast network architecture** in which **multiple synchronized transmitters** radiate **identical DAB ensembles on the same radio frequency block** at the **same instant of time**, with **precise time, frequency, and bit-level synchronization**, such that the received signals from all transmitters combine **constructively** at the receiver within the protection interval of the COFDM (Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing) symbol structure.

The DAB system, standardized in **ETSI EN 300 401**, uses **COFDM** as its modulation/multiplexing scheme, which is the **enabling technology** that makes SFN operation practically feasible. Unlike traditional FM or AM broadcast networks that require *Multi-Frequency Networks (MFN)* with different frequency assignments in adjacent areas to avoid co-channel interference, DAB SFNs deliberately **reuse the same frequency block** across the entire coverage area, achieving **spectral efficiency of unity (η = 1)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Stadium Choir Effect"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Stadium Choir:**
> Imagine a large circular stadium where a song is to be heard clearly by every spectator. Instead of placing **one giant speaker** in the center (which fails for those seated far away), we place **20 smaller speakers** distributed around the stadium rim. All 20 speakers play the **same song, at the same volume, at the exact same moment**.
>
> The sound from each speaker reaches every listener. Because the singers/speakers are **tightly synchronized**, the sound waves arrive within a tiny time window (the human ear's equivalent of a "guard interval") and **add up** to produce a *louder, cleaner* rendition. The audience member does **not need to know** which speakers are contributing — they simply enjoy a stronger, more uniform sound.
>
> In this analogy:
> - The **20 speakers** = SFN transmitters
> - **Same song, same time** = Identical COFDM signal on same RF frequency
> - **Ear's tolerance window** = Guard interval of COFDM symbol
> - **Loud, clean sound everywhere** = SFN gain and seamless coverage

## 1.3 Why SFN? — The Core Engineering Motivation

| Engineering Problem (MFN era) | SFN Solution |
|------------------------------|--------------|
| Different frequencies needed in adjacent regions to avoid co-channel interference | **One frequency** used everywhere — no interference planning required |
| Complex frequency planning and coordination across borders | **Simplified network planning** |
| Spectrum inefficient — each transmitter uses unique frequency | **Spectrum reuse factor = 1** |
| Hand-over needed when moving between transmitter coverage zones | **Seamless coverage** — receiver does not retune |
| Coverage holes at transmitter boundaries | **Overlap zones** add signal power (network gain) |

## 1.4 The Three Pillars Enabling DAB SFN

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Mandatory Conditions for SFN Operation:**
> 1. **COFDM modulation** — provides robustness against multipath echoes via the guard interval
> 2. **Guard interval** — absorbs delay differences between transmitters so that the receiver treats them as benign multipath
> 3. **Network synchronization** — all transmitters must be locked in **time** (typically within ±1 µs), **frequency** (within ±1 Hz), and **data content** (identical MPEG audio frames, identical ensemble configuration)

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in DAB

The following **bold constants** are foundational to all DAB SFN calculations:

- **Speed of light in free space:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$
- **DAB block bandwidth:** $B_{\text{block}} = \mathbf{1.536 \ MHz}$ (constant across all transmission modes)
- **Number of carriers in Mode I:** $K = \mathbf{1536}$
- **Useful symbol duration in Mode I:** $T_u = \mathbf{1 \ ms}$
- **Carrier spacing in Mode I:** $\Delta f = 1/T_u = \mathbf{1 \ kHz}$
- **Maximum SFN transmitter separation (Mode I):** $d_{\max} = c \cdot T_g = \mathbf{73.8 \ km}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The COFDM Backbone of DAB

Coded Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing is **not just a modulation scheme** — it is the architectural foundation that makes DAB SFN possible. Let us dissect it step by step.

### Step 1 — Splitting a High-Rate Stream into Many Low-Rate Sub-streams
A single high-bit-rate stream (e.g., 1.5 Mbit/s of compressed audio) cannot be transmitted robustly in a multipath environment because the symbol duration would be **shorter than the channel's delay spread**, causing severe Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI). COFDM splits this stream into **N parallel low-rate sub-streams** (e.g., 1536 in Mode I), each with a **longer symbol duration** $T_u = 1 \ \text{ms}$, which is **orders of magnitude greater** than typical echo delays.

### Step 2 — Orthogonal Sub-carriers via Inverse FFT
The N sub-carriers are placed at frequencies $f_k = k \cdot \Delta f$ where $\Delta f = 1/T_u$, ensuring **mutual orthogonality**. This means even though the spectra of adjacent sub-carriers **overlap**, the receiver can perfectly separate them by FFT processing. The Inverse Fast Fourier Transform (IFFT) at the transmitter and FFT at the receiver perform this parallel-to-serial conversion efficiently in the digital domain.

### Step 3 — Insertion of Guard Interval
Each useful symbol of duration $T_u$ is **prefixed** with a cyclic copy of itself called the *guard interval* of duration $T_g$. The total COFDM symbol duration is:

$$T_s = T_u + T_g$$

The guard interval absorbs echoes up to a maximum delay $\tau_{\max} = T_g$. Any echo arriving within the guard interval is "swallowed" by the receiver's FFT window without causing ISI or ICI (Inter-Carrier Interference).

### Step 4 — Why COFDM Enables SFN
In an SFN, signals from multiple transmitters arrive at the receiver with **different propagation delays** (because the transmitters are at different locations). If these delays are within the guard interval $T_g$, the receiver treats them as **ordinary multipath echoes** — which COFDM is **specifically designed to handle**. Therefore, multiple transmitters on the **same frequency** appear to the receiver as just a *richer multipath profile* rather than interference.

## 2.2 The Guard Interval — Detailed Engineering View

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose of the Guard Interval in DAB SFN:**
> The guard interval serves **two** functions simultaneously:
> 1. **Anti-multipath:** Absorbs echoes from natural reflections (buildings, terrain)
> 2. **Anti-SFN self-interference:** Absorbs differential delays between SFN transmitters

The maximum permissible transmitter separation is therefore:

$$d_{\max} = c \cdot T_g$$

For DAB Mode I with $T_g = 246 \ \mu s$:

$$d_{\max} = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s} \times 246 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{s} = 73.8 \ \text{km}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SFN Design Rule:**
> The distance between any two transmitters in the SFN **must be ≤ 73.8 km** in Mode I. If the separation exceeds this, the signal from the farther transmitter arrives **outside** the guard interval and causes **destructive interference** (self-interference) — degrading rather than improving reception.

## 2.3 SFN Gain — The Quantitative Benefit

When N transmitters in an SFN deliver useful signals that combine constructively at a receiver, the **received power** is approximately N times the power from a single transmitter (assuming all transmitters have equal ERP and the receiver is in the overlap zone). Expressed in decibels:

$$G_{\text{SFN}} = 10 \log_{10}(N) \ \text{dB}$$

This is the **ideal coherent combining gain**. In practice, due to non-ideal synchronization, antenna pattern differences, and propagation anomalies, the practical gain is somewhat less. A commonly used empirical model is:

$$G_{\text{SFN, practical}} = 10 \log_{10}(N) - L_{\text{impl}} \ \text{dB}$$

where $L_{\text{impl}}$ is the *implementation loss*, typically **1 to 3 dB**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for the KTU End Semester Examination on DAB SFN.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---------------------|------------------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Total COFDM symbol duration | $T_s = T_u + T_g$ | seconds (s) | Useful data + guard interval |
| 2 | Carrier spacing | $\Delta f = 1 / T_u$ | Hz | Inverse of useful symbol duration |
| 3 | DAB block bandwidth (all modes) | $B_{\text{block}} = K \cdot \Delta f$ | Hz | Always **1.536 MHz** |
| 4 | Maximum SFN transmitter separation | $d_{\max} = c \cdot T_g$ | meters (m) | Beyond this, SFN self-interference occurs |
| 5 | Ideal SFN power gain (coherent) | $G_{\text{SFN}} = 10 \log_{10}(N)$ | dB | N = number of transmitters |
| 6 | Practical SFN power gain | $G_{\text{SFN,prac}} = 10\log_{10}(N) - L_{\text{impl}}$ | dB | $L_{\text{impl}} \approx 1$–$3$ dB |
| 7 | Frequency reuse efficiency in SFN | $\eta_{\text{SFN}} = 1$ | dimensionless | Same frequency used everywhere |
| 8 | Frequency reuse efficiency in MFN | $\eta_{\text{MFN}} = 1 / K$ | dimensionless | K = frequency reuse factor |
| 9 | Guard interval fraction | $\alpha = T_g / T_u$ | dimensionless | e.g., **0.246** for DAB Mode I |
| 10 | Maximum echo delay tolerated | $\tau_{\max} = T_g$ | seconds | Beyond this → ISI |
| 11 | Receiver SNR gain in SFN overlap | $\Delta\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}(N)$ | dB | For N equal-power signals |
| 12 | Time synchronization tolerance | $\Delta t_{\text{sync}} \le T_g / 10$ | seconds | Typically **±24.6 µs** in Mode I |
| 13 | Frequency synchronization tolerance | $\Delta f_{\text{sync}} \le \Delta f / 100$ | Hz | **±0.01 Hz** in Mode I |
| 14 | Number of carriers in Mode I | $K_{\text{I}} = 1536$ | count | Useful sub-carriers |
| 15 | DAB Mode I useful symbol duration | $T_{u,\text{I}} = 1 \ \text{ms}$ | seconds | Long symbol → robust to multipath |
| 16 | DAB Mode I guard interval | $T_{g,\text{I}} = 246 \ \mu s$ | seconds | Absorbs up to 73.8 km path difference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Anchors for the Exam:**
> - Remember **246 µs** → **73.8 km** (this single numerical pair is the most-tested fact in DAB SFN questions)
> - Remember **$\alpha = 0.246$** for Mode I
> - Remember **$G = 10 \log_{10}(N)$** — a 3-transmitter SFN gives **4.77 dB** gain; a 7-transmitter SFN gives **8.45 dB**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | How DAB SFN is Applied |
|--------|----------------------|
| **National Public Radio Broadcasting** | Country-wide DAB coverage using hundreds of synchronized transmitters on one frequency block |
| **Cross-Border Broadcasting (Europe)** | Adjacent countries share frequency blocks without mutual interference (planned under GE-06 agreement) |
| **High-Speed Train Reception** | The *seamless* nature of SFN means no re-tuning while moving — critical for DAB+ in cars and trains |
| **Single-Frequency Emergency Broadcasting** | Robust, gap-free coverage during disasters where infrastructure is partially destroyed |
| **DAB+ Hybrid Radio** | Modern automotive infotainment systems rely on SFN for uninterrupted DAB+ reception |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Reader's Guide:** All derivations below proceed from **first principles**. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly, with accompanying textual reasoning. No "it can be shown" shortcuts are used.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Carrier Spacing in COFDM (Why $\Delta f = 1/T_u$?)

### Starting Point
Two sub-carriers at frequencies $f_k$ and $f_{k+1}$ are **orthogonal** over a useful symbol interval $T_u$ if:

$$\int_{0}^{T_u} \cos(2\pi f_k t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{k+1} t) \, dt = 0$$

### Step-by-Step Expansion

We begin by writing the orthogonality condition for the integer-k'th and (k+1)-th sub-carrier:

$$\int_{0}^{T_u} \cos(2\pi k \Delta f \, t) \cdot \cos\big(2\pi (k+1) \Delta f \, t\big) \, dt = 0$$

Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$= \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{T_u} \Big[ \cos\big(2\pi \Delta f \, t\big) + \cos\big(2\pi (2k+1) \Delta f \, t\big) \Big] \, dt = 0$$

The second term integrates to zero **for any integer $k$** over an integer number of its half-periods. For the **first term** to integrate to zero, we need:

$$\int_{0}^{T_u} \cos(2\pi \Delta f \, t) \, dt = 0 \implies \frac{\sin(2\pi \Delta f \, T_u)}{2\pi \Delta f} = 0$$

This requires $2\pi \Delta f \, T_u = n \pi$ for non-zero integer $n$. The smallest spacing corresponds to $n=1$:

$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{2 T_u} \quad \text{(real sub-carriers)}$$

For complex (I/Q) sub-carriers, the factor of 2 is removed:

$$\boxed{\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_u}} \quad \text{(complex I/Q baseband COFDM)}$$

**Textual interpretation:** This is the **minimum** carrier spacing that maintains orthogonality. Closer spacing → sub-carrier spectra overlap and lose orthogonality. Wider spacing → wasteful spectrum. The choice $\Delta f = 1/T_u$ is the **Nyquist-optimal** solution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum SFN Transmitter Separation

### Starting Point
The guard interval must be **at least as long** as the **longest echo** the receiver must tolerate. In an SFN, the longest "echo" is the **signal from the farthest transmitter** in the network.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let transmitter A be at distance $d_A$ from the receiver and transmitter B at distance $d_B$, with $d_B > d_A$. The differential propagation delay is:

$$\Delta \tau = \frac{d_B - d_A}{c}$$

The **worst case** for an SFN with maximum inter-transmitter distance $D$ is when the receiver is **right at transmitter A** (so $d_A \approx 0$) and the farthest transmitter is at distance $D$:

$$\Delta \tau_{\max} = \frac{D}{c}$$

For the receiver's FFT window to capture this late signal **within the useful part** of the symbol, we need:

$$\Delta \tau_{\max} \le T_g$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{D}{c} \le T_g$$

Solving for D:

$$\boxed{D_{\max} = c \cdot T_g}$$

### Numerical Evaluation for DAB Mode I
Plug in $T_g = 246 \ \mu s$ and $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$:

$$D_{\max} = 3 \times 10^{8} \ \text{m/s} \times 246 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{s} = 73800 \ \text{m} = 73.8 \ \text{km}$$

**Textual interpretation:** Any two transmitters in the DAB Mode I SFN must be **within 73.8 km** of each other. If they are farther apart, the late signal corrupts the next COFDM symbol's useful part, causing ISI that the Viterbi decoder in the receiver cannot fully correct.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SFN Power Gain (Coherent Combining)

### Starting Point
Consider N SFN transmitters, each radiating power $P_T$ with equal ERP, all on the same frequency. A receiver located in the overlap coverage zone receives all N signals. The free-space received power from each transmitter $i$ at distance $d_i$ is:

$$P_{R,i} = \frac{P_T \cdot G_T \cdot G_R \cdot \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 \, d_i^{\,2}}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation (Ideal Case)

For the **ideal SFN gain analysis**, we assume the receiver is equidistant from all N transmitters, so $d_i = d$ for all $i$. Each received signal voltage is:

$$v_i = \sqrt{P_{R,i}} \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_i)$$

where $\phi_i$ is the carrier phase. With **perfect time and frequency synchronization**, all $\phi_i$ are equal — the signals add **coherently** in voltage:

$$v_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i = N \cdot \sqrt{P_R} \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$$

The total **power** is proportional to the **square** of voltage:

$$P_{\text{total}} = (N \cdot \sqrt{P_R})^2 = N^2 \cdot P_R$$

But wait — this is the **voltage addition** case. In practice, the receiver captures **uncorrelated noise** from each path; noise adds in **power** (incoherently). The desired signal-to-noise ratio is:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{N^2 \cdot P_R}{N \cdot N_0} = N \cdot \frac{P_R}{N_0} = N \cdot \text{SNR}_{\text{single}}$$

Therefore, the **SNR gain** is:

$$\boxed{G_{\text{SFN}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{single}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(N) \ \text{dB}}$$

### Numerical Example
For $N = 4$ transmitters:

$$G_{\text{SFN}} = 10 \log_{10}(4) = 10 \times 0.6021 = 6.02 \ \text{dB}$$

For $N = 7$ transmitters:

$$G_{\text{SFN}} = 10 \log_{10}(7) = 10 \times 0.8451 = 8.45 \ \text{dB}$$

**Textual interpretation:** A 4-transmitter SFN gives the equivalent of **doubling** the transmitter power 2×. A 7-transmitter SFN gives an **8.45 dB boost**, which is the same as multiplying single-transmitter power by ~7× — for free, just by synchronizing multiple transmitters.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: SFN Frequency Reuse Efficiency

### Starting Point
A broadcast band has total bandwidth $B_{\text{total}}$. A single DAB block occupies $B_{\text{block}} = 1.536 \ \text{MHz}$. In a *Multi-Frequency Network* with reuse factor $K$, the **same content** must be transmitted on K different frequency blocks to avoid co-channel interference.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Number of independent DAB blocks needed for one program:
- **MFN:** $K$ blocks → spectrum used = $K \cdot B_{\text{block}}$
- **SFN:** $1$ block → spectrum used = $B_{\text{block}}$

Spectral efficiency per unit of delivered audio content:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful content (audio programs)}}{\text{Spectrum used}}$$

For one audio program:
- $\eta_{\text{MFN}} = 1 / (K \cdot B_{\text{block}})$
- $\eta_{\text{SFN}} = 1 / B_{\text{block}}$

Ratio:

$$\frac{\eta_{\text{SFN}}}{\eta_{\text{MFN}}} = K$$

**Textual interpretation:** The SFN is **K times more spectrum-efficient** than an equivalent MFN, where K is the cluster size (typically 3, 4, or 7 in conventional frequency planning).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MFN vs SFN

| Parameter | MFN (Multi-Frequency Network) | SFN (Single Frequency Network) |
|-----------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Frequencies per program | K (multiple) | **1** |
| Spectrum efficiency | $1/K$ | **1 (unity)** |
| Co-channel interference | Avoided by planning | **Constructively used as gain** |
| Hand-over between zones | Required at boundaries | **Seamless (no hand-over)** |
| Network gain | 0 dB (no combining) | $10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB |
| Synchronization needed | Frequency offset only | **Time + Frequency + Data** |
| Coverage holes at edges | Common | **Reduced by overlap gain** |
| Modulation requirement | Any (e.g., FM) | **COFDM mandatory** |
| Network planning complexity | High (frequency coordination) | Moderate (geometric + timing) |

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Ensemble Multiplexer** sends **identical content** to all transmitters via the ETI (Ensemble Transport Interface) signal.
- The **GPS/GNSS Reference** distributes a **common time and frequency reference** to all exciters for synchronization.
- All transmitters radiate on the **same RF frequency $f_1$**.
- The **receiver** sees the sum of all transmitter signals and decodes them as a single COFDM stream.

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Guard Interval Insertion** (block A7) at the transmitter and **Guard Interval Removal** (block C2) at the receiver bookend the OFDM symbol structure.
- The **FFT** at the receiver (block C3) is the *windowing* operation that converts time-domain echoes into frequency-domain channel response — but **only if** all echoes fall **inside** the guard interval. This is the *physical basis* of why SFN works.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The End Semester Examination (ESE) for OEEVT722 follows the standard KTU pattern: **Part A (short answer, 3 marks each)** and **Part B (long answer, 14 marks each with internal choice)**. All questions below are modeled precisely on past KTU papers and the 2024 scheme template.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model]
**Q: Define a Single Frequency Network (SFN) as used in Digital Audio Broadcasting. Why is COFDM a mandatory requirement for SFN operation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A Single Frequency Network (SFN) is a network of multiple DAB transmitters that radiate **the same DAB ensemble on the same RF frequency block simultaneously**, with precise time, frequency, and data synchronization, such that their signals combine constructively at the receiver.
- **COFDM as a mandatory requirement (1.5 marks):** COFDM provides **guard interval protection** that absorbs the differential propagation delays between SFN transmitters. Without the guard interval, the multiple received signals would cause **inter-symbol and inter-carrier interference**. The COFDM receiver's FFT window can coherently combine all signals arriving within the guard interval as constructive multipath, enabling SFN operation. Therefore, COFDM is **mandatory** — no other modulation scheme used in DAB offers this multipath-robustness property.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]
**Q: For DAB Mode I transmission, the guard interval is 246 µs. Calculate the maximum permissible separation between two transmitters in a Single Frequency Network. State the consequence if this separation is exceeded.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Formula (1 mark):** $D_{\max} = c \cdot T_g$
- **Substitution (1 mark):** $D_{\max} = 3 \times 10^{8} \ \text{m/s} \times 246 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{s} = 73800 \ \text{m} = \mathbf{73.8 \ km}$
- **Consequence (1 mark):** If the transmitter separation exceeds 73.8 km, the signal from the farther transmitter arrives **outside** the guard interval of the COFDM symbol. This causes **inter-symbol interference (ISI)** and **inter-carrier interference (ICI)** that the receiver's Viterbi decoder cannot fully correct, leading to **SFN self-interference** and **degraded reception** (the gain becomes a loss).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. KTU provides an internal choice — you must attempt ONE full question (both sub-parts).**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEEVT722 Module 2]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and operating principle of a DAB Single Frequency Network. Discuss the role of the ensemble multiplexer and the common reference generator.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of DAB SFN (2 marks):**
A DAB Single Frequency Network consists of multiple geographically distributed transmitters all radiating the **same DAB ensemble on the same RF frequency block** with strict synchronization. The receiver captures the sum of these signals and decodes them as a single COFDM stream, gaining both **robustness and SNR improvement** in the overlap zones.

**2. Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
- **Audio Source + Encoder:** Each audio program is compressed using **MPEG-1 Audio Layer II** (traditional DAB) or **HE-AAC v2** (DAB+).
- **DAB Ensemble Multiplexer:** Combines multiple audio programs (typically 6–9) and data services into a single **ETI (Ensemble Transport Interface)** stream. This stream is **identical** for all transmitters in the SFN.
- **Distribution Network:** Carries the ETI stream to all transmitter sites via satellite, fibre, or microwave links.
- **GPS/GNSS Reference Generator:** Provides **10 MHz** frequency reference and **1 PPS (one pulse-per-second)** time reference to every transmitter exciter.
- **DAB Exciters:** Each exciter takes the ETI stream and the synchronization reference and generates a **COFDM-modulated RF signal** at the assigned DAB block frequency.
- **HPA + Antenna:** Each signal is amplified and radiated.

**3. Operating Principle (2 marks):**
- All transmitters emit **identical COFDM symbols at identical carrier frequencies and identical bit content**.
- Signals from different transmitters arrive at any given receiver with **different propagation delays**.
- If these delays are within the **guard interval** ($T_g = 246 \ \mu s$ in Mode I, equivalent to 73.8 km path difference), the receiver treats them as constructive multipath.
- The effective **SNR** improves by $10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB in deep overlap zones.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the role of the COFDM guard interval in DAB SFN operation. Derive the relationship between the guard interval duration and the maximum permissible SFN transmitter separation. Use Mode I parameters in your numerical answer.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Role of Guard Interval in COFDM (3 marks):**
- The guard interval is a **cyclic prefix** — a copy of the last $T_g$ portion of the useful symbol — prepended to each COFDM symbol.
- It serves **two functions in DAB**:
  - (a) **Anti-multipath:** Absorbs natural echoes from buildings, terrain, and vehicles.
  - (b) **Anti-SFN self-interference:** Absorbs the differential delays between SFN transmitters.
- During the guard interval, the receiver's ADC is sampling, but the **FFT window** opens only at the start of the **useful part** of the symbol. Thus, any signal arriving within the guard interval appears as a **phase shift on each sub-carrier** after the FFT — which is easily equalized using the pilot symbols embedded in the DAB signal.

**2. Derivation of $D_{\max}$ (3 marks):**
- Let two transmitters be separated by distance $D$. The receiver is at distance $d_1$ from TX1 and $d_2$ from TX2.
- Differential delay: $\Delta \tau = (d_2 - d_1)/c$
- Worst case (receiver at TX1, far TX at distance D): $\Delta \tau_{\max} = D/c$
- Guard interval must satisfy: $\Delta \tau_{\max} \le T_g$
- Therefore: $D \le c \cdot T_g$
- Hence: $D_{\max} = c \cdot T_g$

**3. Numerical Evaluation for Mode I (1 mark):**
$$D_{\max} = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s} \times 246 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{s} = 73.8 \ \text{km}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing the formula as $D_{\max} = c \cdot T_u$ (using useful symbol duration instead of guard interval) — this gives **300 km**, which is **WRONG**. The guard interval, NOT the useful symbol, bounds the tolerable delay.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the unit conversion from µs to s in numerical substitution. Always write $246 \times 10^{-6}$ explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not mentioning that the formula assumes a **worst-case** receiver position (right at the foot of one transmitter). KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if this assumption is not stated.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, OEEVT722 Module 2]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Tabulate the COFDM parameters of DAB Modes I, II, III, and IV. Explain how the choice of mode affects the maximum SFN transmitter separation and the suitability for different network deployments.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Parameter Table (4 marks):**

| DAB Mode | Number of Carriers (K) | Carrier Spacing $\Delta f$ (kHz) | Useful Symbol $T_u$ | Guard Interval $T_g$ | Total Symbol $T_s$ | $D_{\max} = c \cdot T_g$ (km) |
|----------|------------------------|----------------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|---------------------|---------------------------------|
| **Mode I** | 1536 | 1 | 1000 µs | 246 µs | 1246 µs | **73.8** |
| **Mode II** | 384 | 4 | 250 µs | 62 µs | 312 µs | **18.6** |
| **Mode III** | 192 | 8 | 125 µs | 31 µs | 156 µs | **9.3** |
| **Mode IV** | 768 | 2 | 500 µs | 123 µs | 623 µs | **36.9** |

**2. Relationship Between Mode Choice and SFN Separation (2 marks):**
- The maximum SFN transmitter separation is **directly proportional to the guard interval duration**: $D_{\max} = c \cdot T_g$.
- Mode I has the **longest** guard interval (246 µs) → **largest** allowable transmitter separation (73.8 km) → suitable for **large-scale national SFNs** with widely spaced transmitters.
- Mode II's smaller $T_g$ (62 µs) limits $D_{\max}$ to 18.6 km → suits **regional/local SFNs** with dense transmitter spacing.
- Mode III (mobile, $D_{\max} = 9.3$ km) and Mode IV ($D_{\max} = 36.9$ km) serve specific applications.

**3. Trade-offs (1 mark):**
- **Larger guard interval** → more robust to long-delay echoes and large SFN separations, but **lower useful data rate** (more overhead per symbol).
- **Smaller guard interval** → higher data efficiency, but **smaller** $D_{\max}$ and more sensitive to long echoes.
- Mode I is the **most common choice** for SFN planning because it balances robustness and data capacity.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Define SFN gain. With a clear derivation, show that the ideal SFN power gain for N identical transmitters is $G_{\text{SFN}} = 10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB. State two practical limitations that reduce this gain in real deployments.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of SFN Gain (1 mark):**
SFN gain is the **improvement in received signal power (or SNR)** at a receiver located in the overlap coverage zone of multiple SFN transmitters, compared to a single transmitter, expressed in dB.

**2. Derivation (4 marks):**
Consider N transmitters, each delivering received power $P_R$ at the receiver (assumed equidistant for simplicity).
- Total received signal **voltage** (coherent addition): $V_{\text{total}} = N \cdot \sqrt{P_R}$
- Total received signal **power** (for the desired signal): $P_{\text{signal}} = N^2 \cdot P_R$
- Total received **noise power** (incoherent, powers add): $P_{\text{noise}} = N \cdot N_0$ (where $N_0$ is the noise per path, assumed identical and uncorrelated)
- Output SNR: $\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{N^2 \cdot P_R}{N \cdot N_0} = N \cdot \dfrac{P_R}{N_0} = N \cdot \text{SNR}_{\text{single}}$
- Gain in dB: $G_{\text{SFN}} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} / \text{SNR}_{\text{single}}) = 10 \log_{10}(N) \ \text{dB}$

**3. Numerical Example (0.5 marks):**
- For $N = 4$: $G = 10 \log_{10}(4) = 6.02$ dB
- For $N = 7$: $G = 10 \log_{10}(7) = 8.45$ dB

**4. Practical Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- **(i) Imperfect synchronization:** Real GPS receivers and exciters have residual time errors (typically ±1–10 µs) and frequency errors (±0.01 Hz). These reduce coherent combining efficiency, costing 1–3 dB of gain.
- **(ii) Non-uniform path geometry:** In reality, the receiver is not equidistant from all N transmitters. Path losses differ, antenna gains differ per direction, and some signals may fall outside the guard interval, converting potential gain into self-interference.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing $G_{\text{SFN}} = 20 \log_{10}(N)$ (using voltage gain instead of power gain). The 10 (not 20) is correct because SNR is a **power** ratio. KTU examiners **strictly deduct** 1 mark for this.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to mention the **coherent** vs **incoherent** distinction. Marking requires that you state "signals add coherently in voltage, noise adds incoherently in power."
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing the answer as $G = N$ (linear) without converting to dB. Always give the final answer **in dB** as the question explicitly asks for it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam)**

- **DAB SFN** = Multiple transmitters on **the same frequency** with **strict synchronization**.
- **COFDM** is the **enabling technology** — guard interval absorbs transmitter delay differences.
- **DAB Mode I parameters:** $T_u = 1$ ms, $T_g = 246$ µs, $K = 1536$ carriers, $\Delta f = 1$ kHz, bandwidth = **1.536 MHz**.
- **Maximum SFN transmitter separation (Mode I):** $D_{\max} = c \cdot T_g = \mathbf{73.8 \ km}$. Memorize this number.
- **Ideal SFN gain:** $G_{\text{SFN}} = 10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB. (NOT $20 \log_{10}$!)
- **Three synchronization requirements:** Time (≤ ±24.6 µs), Frequency (≤ ±0.01 Hz), Data (identical ETI/TIST).
- **SFN frequency reuse efficiency = 1** (vs MFN = 1/K).
- **Practical gain = ideal gain − implementation loss** (typically 1–3 dB loss).
- **Beyond 73.8 km separation → self-interference** rather than gain (Zone D in the coverage diagram).
- **Modes II, III, IV** have smaller $T_g$ → smaller $D_{\max}$ → denser SFN planning required.
- **Guard interval absorbs both natural multipath AND SFN transmitter echoes** — this dual role is the key insight.
- **Guard interval is a cyclic prefix** — it is a copy of the last $T_g$ of the useful symbol prepended to the symbol.
- **Network gain formula assumes equidistant transmitters**; in practice, geometry and propagation anomalies reduce it.
- **TIST (Time Stamp)** in the ETI stream provides data-level synchronization independent of the RF carrier.
- **GPS/GNSS** is the standard primary time/frequency reference for DAB SFN exciters worldwide.
- **Cross-border SFNs** (e.g., across European countries) are the flagship real-world application of DAB SFN technology.
- **For KTU numerical problems**, always show: formula → substitution → numerical answer with **units**. KTU examiners reward step-by-step working and penalize "answer-only" responses.
- **Memory anchor word:** **"73.8 km = 246 µs × c"** — this is the single most tested fact in the entire DAB SFN module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Broadcasting Using DAB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERPRISE ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Broadcasting Using DAB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Data Broadcasting using DAB (Digital Audio Broadcasting)** refers to the transmission of non-audio digital information—such as text, images, web pages, traffic updates, and multimedia objects—over the standardized Eureka-147 DAB transmission framework (defined in **EN 300 401** by ETSI). The data is multiplexed alongside (or in place of) digital audio services using the **Main Service Channel (MSC)** and the **Fast Information Channel (FIC)**, enabling a single DAB ensemble to carry both audio programs and ancillary data services simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** DAB is **NOT** a simple digital replacement for FM. It is a **multi-service, multi-protocol, packet-oriented data broadcasting platform** where audio is just one of many possible service types.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Newspaper Stand"

Imagine an old-fashioned newspaper stand at a railway station. The stand has **one physical shelf space (the DAB frequency block)**, but inside, the vendor keeps:

- **Magazines** (Audio services — MP2/AAC streams)
- **Newspapers** (PAD — Program Associated Data: track titles, artist info)
- **Free flyers** (Data services — MOT slideshows, TPEG traffic, BWS weather)
- **A notice board** (FIC — the index telling you which "page" is on which "shelf slot")

A passerby (the DAB receiver) glances at the **notice board (FIC)**, finds the index, and pulls out only the items they want. They do not need to "read" the whole newspaper. This is exactly how DAB data broadcasting works—a **shared bandwidth with a directory-driven access mechanism**, allowing the receiver to selectively decode only the service it needs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Bodies:**
> - **ETSI EN 300 401** — DAB system specification
> - **ETSI TS 101 499** — MOT (Multimedia Object Transfer) protocol
> - **ETSI EN 301 234** — IP-tunnelling over DAB
> - **ETSI TS 102 367** — TPEG location referencing

## 1.3 Why DAB for Data Broadcasting? — Engineering Motivation

Traditional FM RDS (Radio Data System) supports only **~1.2 kbps** of low-speed data (for station ID, basic text). DAB overcomes this by providing a **multiplexed ensemble capacity of approximately 1.5 Mbps** (Mode I, Band III) which can be **dynamically reallocated** between audio and data services. This makes DAB suitable for:

| Application | Data Type | Typical Capacity Needed |
|-------------|-----------|-------------------------|
| TPEG Traffic Updates | Structured XML | 8–16 kbps |
| EPG (Electronic Programme Guide) | HTML/Text | 32–64 kbps |
| MOT Slideshow (BWS/SPI) | JPEG/PNG images | 32–128 kbps |
| IP Datacast (DMB) | IP packets | Up to 1 Mbps |
| Journaline (Text news) | Plain text + hyperlinks | 8–16 kbps |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DAB Transmission Frame — The Foundational Structure

The DAB transmission is organized into **transmission frames**, each of duration **96 ms** (or **48 ms** in Mode II / Mode IV). Every frame is composed of **three functional zones**:

### 2.1.1 The Three Logical Channels

1. **Synchronization Channel (SC)** — Used by the receiver for coarse time/frequency synchronization (NULL symbol + PRS — Phase Reference Symbol). Not user-data carrying.

2. **Fast Information Channel (FIC)** — The **control and directory channel** of DAB.
   - Carries **MCI (Multiplex Configuration Information)**, **SI (Service Information)**, **CA (Conditional Access)** data.
   - **NOT time-interleaved** → rapid access for receivers scanning frequencies.
   - Fixed capacity: **32 Capacity Units (CU)** per frame in Mode I.
   - 1 CU = **8 kbps** in Mode I.
   - **Total FIC capacity ≈ 256 kbps.**

3. **Main Service Channel (MSC)** — The **payload channel** for audio and data.
   - Time-interleaved (≈ 384 ms in Mode I) → robust against burst errors (mobile reception).
   - Sub-divided into **Sub-Channels**, each with a configurable protection level (EEP/UEP).
   - Total MSC capacity ≈ **1.152 Mbps** (Mode I, Band III).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Trap:** The FIC is **NOT** used for user data broadcasting. It is the **signalling/control plane**. Data services live in the **MSC**, while their **pointers and labels** live in the **FIC**.

## 2.2 Service Organization — The Hierarchy

A DAB transmission can be broken down as:

```
Ensemble (one DAB frequency block / MUX)
   ├── Service 1 (Audio — e.g., Radio Mirchi)
   ├── Service 2 (Audio)
   ├── Service 3 (Data — e.g., TPEG Traffic)
   ├── Service 4 (Data — e.g., MOT Slideshow)
   └── ...
       └── Service Component (Sub-channel of MSC or stream within sub-channel)
```

- **Ensemble** — One DAB multiplex on one frequency block (~1.5 MHz bandwidth).
- **Service** — User-visible entity (e.g., a radio station or a data application).
- **Service Component** — The actual stream (audio codec, data stream, MOT carousel).

## 2.3 Data Broadcasting Modes (KTU High-Yield)

| Mode | Protocol | Use Case | Reference |
|------|----------|----------|-----------|
| **MOT (Multimedia Object Transfer)** | ETSI TS 101 499 | Slideshows, BWS (Broadcast Web Site), Journaline | MOT Slideshow, BWS |
| **TPEG** | ETSI TS 102 368 | Traffic & travel information | TTI, RTM, PTI |
| **IP Datacast (DMB)** | ETSI EN 301 234 | MPEG-4 video, IP packets | DMB (in Korea) |
| **TDC / IP Tunnel** | ETSI EN 301 234 | Generic IP encapsulation | IPDC |
| **NFS (Network File System)** | Proprietary | File delivery | Less common |
| **DAB-SIG / Journaline** | — | Hierarchical text news | Used widely in EU |

## 2.4 Protection Profiles & Capacity Planning

DAB supports multiple error protection levels. The **Net Bit Rate (NBR)** for a sub-channel is computed as:

$$
NBR = \frac{CU \times 8 \text{ kbps}}{R_p}
$$

where $R_p$ is the protection ratio (e.g., 1/2, 1/3, 3/4, depending on EEP/UEP profile). More protection → lower NBR, but better mobile reception.

**Common EEP Profiles (Equal Error Protection):**
- EEP-1A, EEP-2A, EEP-3A, EEP-4A (with varying code rates for different applications).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Audio streams typically use **UEP (Unequal Error Protection)** because the human ear is more sensitive to mid-frequency distortions. Data services, being tolerant of bit errors, can use **EEP** with lower overhead.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|----------|-----------------|-------|-------|
| DAB frame duration (Mode I/III) | $T_f = 96$ | ms | |
| DAB frame duration (Mode II/IV) | $T_f = 48$ | ms | L-band, satellite |
| Capacity Unit (Mode I) | $1 \, CU = 8$ | kbps | |
| Total FIC capacity (Mode I) | $32 \times 8 = 256$ | kbps | Signalling only |
| Total MSC capacity (Mode I) | $\approx 1.152$ | Mbps | Net data rate |
| Bandwidth (Band III, Mode I) | $B \approx 1.536$ | MHz | |
| Transmission mode duration per symbol | $T_s = 1 / 2048 \approx 0.488$ | ms | |
| Number of carriers (Mode I) | $K = 1536$ | — | OFDM carriers |
| Net Bit Rate of sub-channel | $NBR = (CU \times 8)/R_p$ | kbps | |
| Carrier spacing | $\Delta f \approx 1000$ | Hz | |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Mode I uses **1536** active OFDM carriers, NOT 2048. The 2048 figure is the FFT size (including virtual carriers). Examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

DAB data broadcasting is the **silent backbone** of modern infotainment:
- **Automotive** — Real-time traffic (TPEG), parking info, fuel prices, weather.
- **Public Safety** — Emergency warnings (EWF — Emergency Warning Functionality).
- **Smart Cities** — Public transport schedules, tourist information.
- **Media** — Album art, artist bios synchronized with currently playing track (PAD).
- **IoT** — Firmware updates, low-cost one-to-many data delivery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DAB Frame Net Bit Rate (Mode I)

We derive the maximum payload capacity of a DAB Mode I transmission frame.

**Given:**
- Frame duration $T_f = 96$ ms.
- Total number of OFDM symbols per frame $L = 76$.
- Null symbol duration $T_{null} \approx 1.297$ ms.
- Phase Reference Symbol (PRS) duration $T_{prs} \approx 1.297$ ms.
- Useful OFDM symbol duration $T_u = 1$ ms (Mode I).
- Guard interval duration $\Delta = T_u / 4 = 0.246$ ms.
- Useful symbol rate per OFDM symbol = $K = 1536$ QPSK symbols (each carrying 2 bits).

**Step 1 — Total useful symbol time per frame (excluding NULL and PRS):**

$$
T_{useful} = (L - 2) \times T_u = (76 - 2) \times 1 \text{ ms} = 74 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Total bits per frame (assuming QPSK, 2 bits per carrier):**

$$
N_{bits/frame} = (L - 2) \times K \times 2 = 74 \times 1536 \times 2
$$

$$
N_{bits/frame} = 74 \times 3072 = 227{,}328 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Net bit rate:**

$$
R_{total} = \frac{N_{bits/frame}}{T_f} = \frac{227{,}328 \text{ bits}}{96 \text{ ms}} = \frac{227{,}328}{0.096 \text{ s}}
$$

$$
R_{total} \approx 2{,}368{,}000 \text{ bps} = 2.368 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract convolutional coding overhead (~1/2 rate for the strongest protection):**

$$
R_{net} = R_{total} \times R_c \approx 2.368 \times 0.5 \approx 1.184 \text{ Mbps}
$$

This is the **~1.152–1.5 Mbps** figure cited in the literature (varies with the exact protection profile applied).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 1.152 Mbps?** This is the **MSC gross capacity** (after removing FIC and signalling). The full derivation shows how the **96 ms frame, 1536 carriers, QPSK, and convolutional coding** combine to produce the well-known DAB capacity.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Capacity Allocation Example

A typical DAB ensemble has the following allocation (Mode I, Band III):

| Service | Sub-Channel | CU | Protection | Net Rate (kbps) |
|---------|-------------|----|-----------:|----------------:|
| Audio Service 1 (Music) | SubCh 1 | 48 | UEP-1 | 384 |
| Audio Service 2 (Talk) | SubCh 2 | 32 | UEP-3 | 128 |
| TPEG Traffic | SubCh 3 | 2 | EEP-3A | 16 |
| MOT Slideshow (PAD) | Inside Audio 1 | 1 | EEP-1A | 8 |
| Journaline News | SubCh 4 | 1 | EEP-2A | 8 |
| EPG (Broadcast Web Site) | SubCh 5 | 4 | EEP-2A | 32 |
| **Total MSC** | — | **88** | — | **~1.0 Mbps** |
| **FIC (signalling)** | — | **32** | — | **256 kbps** |

## 3.3 Derivation: TII (Transmitter Identification Information) Distance Estimation

DAB carries a **Transmitter Identification Information (TII)** pattern in the NULL symbol's guard interval. Receivers can identify which transmitter they are receiving and even estimate distance to adjacent transmitters.

The TII pattern assigns a **main identifier** (group of transmitters) and a **sub-identifier** (individual transmitter). The pattern is encoded in the **guard interval** of consecutive frames using one of 70 possible patterns.

For triangulation, the receiver measures signal strength or uses two transmitters' arrival times:

$$
d \approx c \times \Delta t
$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (speed of light) and $\Delta t$ is the time difference of arrival (TDOA).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** TII enables **seamless handovers** in DAB+ networks for vehicles moving between transmitters, and supports SFN (Single Frequency Network) synchronization checks.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: MOT (Multimedia Object Transfer) Data Carousel

The **MOT protocol** allows a server to repeatedly transmit data objects (images, HTML files, video clips) in a **carousel** so receivers can join at any time and reconstruct the content.

**Step 1 — Server Preparation:** The broadcaster divides the content into small **MOT objects** (e.g., one object = one JPEG image).

**Step 2 — Header Generation:** Each object is given a **MOT header** containing:
- `ContentType` (e.g., image/jpeg, text/html)
- `ContentName` (the file name / directory path)
- `ContentSize` (in bytes)
- `TriggerTime` (when to display)
- `ExpiryTime` (when to remove)

**Step 3 — Segmentation:** The body is segmented into **MOT segments** (typically 512 bytes each). The first segment after the header is the **HeaderInformation segment**.

**Step 4 — Transport:** Segments are placed into MSC sub-channels using **DG (Data Group)** structures as defined in **EN 300 401 §7.4**.

**Step 5 — Receiver Behavior:** A receiver with a Slideshow or BWS application:
- Reads the **FIC** to locate the service and find the **DataService** component.
- Extracts the **MOT directory** (a list of available objects).
- Downloads objects in the carousel (looping indefinitely).
- Renders content to the user.

**Step 6 — Update Mechanism:** When the broadcaster wants to push a new image, the **ContentName** changes, and the new object replaces the old one in the carousel. The receiver recognizes the change and refreshes.

```
[Header Segment] [Body Seg 1] [Body Seg 2] ... [Body Seg N]
   |                |             |                 |
   |                +-------------+-----------------+
   |                                  |
   v                                  v
ContentType                    All parts share same
ContentName                    TransportId (so receiver
ContentSize                    reassembles correctly)
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DAB Transmission Frame Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRAME[DAB Transmission Frame - 96 ms Mode I]
        direction TB
        SYNC[Synchronization Channel - SC]
        FIC[Fast Information Channel - FIC - 32 CU - 256 kbps]
        MSC[Main Service Channel - MSC - 1.152 Mbps - Time Interleaved]

        subgraph MSC_SUB[MSC Sub-Channel Organization]
            direction LR
            AUDIO1[Audio Service 1 - 384 kbps]
            AUDIO2[Audio Service 2 - 192 kbps]
            DATA1[TPEG Traffic - 16 kbps]
            DATA2[MOT Slideshow - 32 kbps]
            DATA3[Journaline News - 8 kbps]
            DATA4[EPG - BWS - 64 kbps]
        end
    end

    SYNC --> FIC
    FIC --> MSC
    MSC --> AUDIO1
    MSC --> AUDIO2
    MSC --> DATA1
    MSC --> DATA2
    MSC --> DATA3
    MSC --> DATA4

    classDef sig fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef data fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef audio fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class FIC sig
    class DATA1,DATA2,DATA3,DATA4 data
    class AUDIO1,AUDIO2 audio
```

## 4.2 DAB Data Broadcasting Protocol Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP[Application Layer - Slideshow, BWS, TPEG, Journaline, DMB]
    MOT[MOT Protocol Layer - TS 101 499 - Object Carousel - Headers, Directory]
    DG[Data Group Layer - EN 300 401 Section 7 - DG Packets]
    PAD[PAD - Program Associated Data - XPAD, F-PAD]
    MSC[MSC Sub-Channel - Time Interleaved Convolutional Coding]
    FIC_SIG[FIC Signalling - MCI, SI - Service Description, Labels]
    OFDM[OFDM Physical Layer - DQPSK - 1.536 MHz Bandwidth]

    APP --> MOT
    APP --> PAD
    MOT --> DG
    PAD --> DG
    DG --> MSC
    FIC_SIG --> OFDM
    MSC --> OFDM

    classDef app fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proto fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef phys fill:#073b4c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    class APP app
    class MOT,DG,PAD proto
    class MSC,FIC_SIG,OFDM phys
```

## 4.3 MOT Object Carousel — Receiver Acquisition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Receiver Power On]) --> TUNE[Tune to DAB Ensemble]
    TUNE --> SCAN[Decode FIC - Extract Service List]
    SCAN --> SEL{User Selects Data Service?}
    SEL -->|Yes| LOCATE[Locate MOT Component in MSC]
    SEL -->|No| AUDIO[Decode Audio Service]
    LOCATE --> CAROUSEL[Read MOT Carousel Header]
    CAROUSEL --> DIR[Parse MOT Directory - List of Objects]
    DIR --> CHOOSE{Object Needed?}
    CHOOSE -->|Yes| FETCH[Fetch Body Segments by TransportId]
    CHOOSE -->|No| SKIP[Wait for Next Carousel Cycle]
    FETCH --> ASSEMBLE[Reassemble Full Object]
    ASSEMBLE --> RENDER[Render to User - Display Image / Text / etc]
    RENDER --> NEXT{More Objects?}
    NEXT -->|Yes| CHOOSE
    NEXT -->|No| END_NODE([Idle - Monitor Carousel Updates])

    classDef startend fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dec fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class START,END_NODE startend
    class TUNE,SCAN,LOCATE,CAROUSEL,DIR,ASSEMBLE proc
    class SEL,CHOOSE,NEXT dec
```

## 4.4 DAB Ensemble Service Organization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ENS[Ensemble - 1.5 MHz Block - e.g., 225.648 MHz Block 12B]
    S1[Service 1 - Radio Mirchi - Audio - 384 kbps]
    S2[Service 2 - BBC Radio 4 - Audio - 192 kbps]
    S3[Service 3 - TrafficCast - Data - 16 kbps]
    S4[Service 4 - WeatherCast - Data - 32 kbps]
    S5[Service 5 - EPG Today - Data - 64 kbps]

    S1C1[Audio Stream - MP2 192 kbps]
    S1C2[PAD - Slideshow - 8 kbps]
    S2C1[Audio Stream - AAC 128 kbps]
    S3C1[TPEG Stream - 16 kbps]
    S4C1[MOT Carousel - 32 kbps]
    S5C1[BWS HTML Pages - 64 kbps]

    ENS --> S1
    ENS --> S2
    ENS --> S3
    ENS --> S4
    ENS --> S5
    S1 --> S1C1
    S1 --> S1C2
    S2 --> S2C1
    S3 --> S3C1
    S4 --> S4C1
    S5 --> S5C1

    classDef ens fill:#ef476f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef aud fill:#118ab2,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef data fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef comp fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class ENS ens
    class S1,S2 aud
    class S3,S4,S5 data
    class S1C1,S1C2,S2C1,S3C1,S4C1,S5C1 comp
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model]**
**What is PAD in the context of DAB data broadcasting? List two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

PAD stands for **Program Associated Data**. It is auxiliary data that is transmitted **in parallel with an audio service** and is *tightly coupled* to that audio (e.g., dynamically updating as the program plays). PAD is carried within the same sub-channel as the audio using the **XPAD (Extended PAD) or F-PAD (Fixed PAD)** mechanism.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Slideshow (SPI / BWS-Slideshow)** — JPEG/PNG images of the currently playing album cover, artist, or advertiser.
2. **Dynamic Label Segment (DLS)** — Scrolling text showing song title and artist name.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for examples)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]**
**Compare FIC and MSC in DAB with respect to (i) interleaving, (ii) purpose, and (iii) capacity in Mode I.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **FIC** | **MSC** |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **(i) Interleaving** | NOT time-interleaved (fast access) | Time-interleaved (~384 ms) |
| **(ii) Purpose** | Carries signalling/control data — MCI, SI, labels | Carries actual audio + data services (payload) |
| **(iii) Capacity (Mode I)** | 32 CU ≈ 256 kbps | ~1.152 Mbps |

The FIC's lack of interleaving allows receivers to **rapidly scan and decode signalling** when switching ensembles — critical for "fast channel switching" UX. The MSC's interleaving provides **robustness against burst errors** during mobile reception.

(3 marks: 1 mark per row)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]**
