## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of 1D DCT-II from DFT (Full Analytical Walkthrough)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **7-mark standard derivation** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers. The following shows the *complete* logical progression with every algebraic step explicit.

**Starting Point:** Consider the $N$-point DFT of a real sequence $x(n)$:

$$X_{DFT}(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$$

**Step 1 — Double the Sequence Length to 2N**

The DFT operates on periodic extension. We wish to construct a sequence $y(n)$ of length $2N$ that is **even-symmetric** about $n = N - \frac{1}{2}$. Define:

$$y(n) = \begin{cases} x(n), & \text{for } 0 \leq n \leq N-1 \\ x(2N-1-n), & \text{for } N \leq n \leq 2N-1 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 2N-Point DFT to $y(n)$**

$$Y(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{2N-1} y(n) e^{-j2\pi kn/(2N)} = \sum_{n=0}^{2N-1} y(n) e^{-j\pi kn/N}$$

**Step 3 — Split the Sum into Two Halves**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y(k) &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j\pi kn/N} + \sum_{n=N}^{2N-1} x(2N-1-n) e^{-j\pi kn/N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $m = 2N-1-n$ in the Second Sum**

When $n = N$, $m = N-1$. When $n = 2N-1$, $m = 0$. Also, $n = 2N-1-m$, so $e^{-j\pi kn/N} = e^{-j\pi k(2N-1-m)/N} = e^{-j2\pi k} \cdot e^{j\pi k/N} \cdot e^{j\pi km/N} = e^{j\pi km/N} \cdot e^{j\pi k/N}$.

After substitution:

$$\begin{aligned}
Y(k) &= \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j\pi kn/N} + \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) e^{j\pi km/N} \cdot e^{j\pi k/N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Factor the Common Exponential $e^{j\pi k/(2N)}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y(k) &= e^{j\pi k/(2N)} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \left[ e^{-j\pi k/(2N)} e^{-j\pi kn/N} + e^{j\pi k/(2N)} e^{j\pi kn/N} \right] \\
&= e^{j\pi k/(2N)} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cdot 2\cos\left[\frac{\pi k(2n+1)}{2N}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Use Euler's Identity for the Real Cosine Sum**

The factor $2\cos\left[\frac{\pi k(2n+1)}{2N}\right]$ is purely real. The prefactor $e^{j\pi k/(2N)}$ is a complex unit-magnitude phase shift.

**Step 7 — Extract the Real Part and Normalize**

The DCT-II is defined as the real, normalized version:

$$\begin{aligned}
X(k) &= \alpha(k) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi k(2n+1)}{2N}\right] \\
&= \alpha(k) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

where $\alpha(0) = \sqrt{1/N}$ and $\alpha(k) = \sqrt{2/N}$ for $k \geq 1$. This is the **DCT-II definition as mandated by every major entertainment codec**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: 4-Point DCT-II

> [!NOTE]
> This is a typical **3-mark calculation problem** in KTU 2024 ESE. Compute the 4-point DCT-II of $x = [8, 6, 4, 2]$.

**Given:** $x(0) = 8$, $x(1) = 6$, $x(2) = 4$, $x(3) = 2$, $N = 4$.

**Pre-compute the cosine basis values** (using $\theta_{k,n} = \pi(2n+1)k / 8$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos(\pi/8) &\approx 0.9239, \quad \cos(2\pi/8) = \cos(\pi/4) \approx 0.7071 \\
\cos(3\pi/8) &\approx 0.3827, \quad \cos(5\pi/8) \approx -0.3827 \\
\cos(6\pi/8) &\approx -0.7071, \quad \cos(7\pi/8) \approx -0.9239 \\
\cos(9\pi/8) &\approx -0.9239, \quad \cos(10\pi/8) \approx -0.7071 \\
\cos(11\pi/8) &\approx -0.3827, \quad \cos(13\pi/8) \approx 0.3827 \\
\cos(14\pi/8) &\approx 0.7071, \quad \cos(15\pi/8) \approx 0.9239 \\
\cos(21\pi/8) &\approx -0.3827
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute $X(0)$** (DC coefficient, $k=0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
X(0) &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}} \sum_{n=0}^{3} x(n) \cos(0) \\
&= 0.5 \times [8 \cdot 1 + 6 \cdot 1 + 4 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1] \\
&= 0.5 \times 20 = 10.000
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute $X(1)$** (low-frequency AC, $k=1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
X(1) &= \sqrt{\frac{2}{4}} \sum_{n=0}^{3} x(n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)}{8}\right] \\
&= 0.7071 \times [8(0.9239) + 6(0.3827) + 4(-0.3827) + 2(-0.9239)] \\
&= 0.7071 \times [7.3912 + 2.2962 - 1.5308 - 1.8478] \\
&= 0.7071 \times 6.3088 \\
&\approx 4.461
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute $X(2)$** (mid-frequency AC, $k=2$):

$$\begin{aligned}
X(2) &= \sqrt{\frac{2}{4}} \sum_{n=0}^{3} x(n) \cos\left[\frac{2\pi(2n+1)}{8}\right] \\
&= 0.7071 \times [8(0.7071) + 6(-0.7071) + 4(-0.7071) + 2(0.7071)] \\
&= 0.7071 \times [5.6568 - 4.2426 - 2.8284 + 1.4142] \\
&= 0.7071 \times 0.0000 \\
&\approx 0.000
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute $X(3)$** (high-frequency AC, $k=3$):

$$\begin{aligned}
X(3) &= \sqrt{\frac{2}{4}} \sum_{n=0}^{3} x(n) \cos\left[\frac{3\pi(2n+1)}{8}\right] \\
&= 0.7071 \times [8(0.3827) + 6(-0.9239) + 4(0.9239) + 2(-0.3827)] \\
&= 0.7071 \times [3.0616 - 5.5434 + 3.6956 - 0.7654] \\
&= 0.7071 \times 0.4484 \\
&\approx 0.317
\end{aligned}$$

**Result Vector:**

$$\boxed{X = [10.000, \ 4.461, \ 0.000, \ 0.317]}$$

**Energy Verification (Parseval's Theorem Check):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{n=0}^{3} x^2(n) &= 64 + 36 + 16 + 4 = 120 \\
\sum_{k=0}^{3} X^2(k) &= 100 + 19.90 + 0 + 0.10 \approx 120 \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** $X(0) = 10$ carries 83.3% of total energy ($100/120$) and $X(1) = 4.461$ carries 16.6% ($19.9/120$). The coefficients $X(2)$ and $X(3)$ are negligible. **This is energy compaction in action** — the basis for all DCT-based compression in entertainment electronics.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of 2D DCT from 1D DCT (Separability)

**Given:** A 2D signal $x(m,n)$ of size $M \times N$.

**Step 1 — Define the 2D DCT-II as a Double Sum:**

$$X(u,v) = \alpha(u)\alpha(v) \sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(m,n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2m+1)u}{2M}\right] \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)v}{2N}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange Using the Distributive Property of Sums:**

$$\begin{aligned}
X(u,v) &= \alpha(v) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left\{ \alpha(u) \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} x(m,n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2m+1)u}{2M}\right] \right\} \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)v}{2N}\right] \\
&= \alpha(v) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} X_{temp}(u,n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)v}{2N}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the Inner Sum as a 1D DCT Across Rows:**

The inner sum is a 1D DCT of each row $x(m, n)$ for fixed $n$:

$$X_{temp}(u, n) = \alpha(u) \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} x(m,n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2m+1)u}{2M}\right] = \text{1D DCT of row } n$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Outer Sum as a 1D DCT Down Columns:**

The outer sum applies 1D DCT to each column of the row-transformed matrix.

**Conclusion:** The 2D DCT is **separable**: it equals a row-wise 1D DCT followed by a column-wise 1D DCT. **Computational savings:** $O(MN \log MN)$ instead of $O(M^2 N^2)$. For an $8 \times 8$ JPEG block, this is a 64× speedup. $\blacksquare$

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        R1["Row 1: Low-Low X(1,0) X(1,1) | Low-Mid X(1,2) X(1,3) | Mid X(1,4) X(1,5) | High X(1,6) X(1,7)"]
        R2["Row 2: Low-Mid X(2,0) X(2,1) X(2,2) | Mid X(2,3) X(2,4) X(2,5) | High X(2,6) X(2,7)"]
        R3["Row 3: Mid X(3,0) to X(3,3) | High X(3,4) to X(3,7)"]

        R5["Row 5: High X(5,0) to X(5,5) | Very High X(5,6) X(5,7)"]
        R6["Row 6: High X(6,0) to X(6,6) | Very High X(6,7)"]
        R7["Row 7: Very High X(7,0) to X(7,7)"]
    end
    SCAN[Zig-Zag Scan Order]
    BLK --> SCAN
    style R0 fill:#C6E0B4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#FFE699,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style R2 fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style R3 fill:#F8CBAD,stroke:#333,color:#000

    style R7 fill:#990000,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Coefficients at the **top-left** (green) carry the most perceptual energy (low spatial frequencies, the "smooth" part of an image). Coefficients at the **bottom-right** (red) carry the least (high spatial frequencies, the "edge" and "noise" details). JPEG/HEVC encoders apply *aggressive quantization* (often zeroing) to the red region — saving 70-90% of the bit budget with minimal perceptual loss.

### 4.3 1D DCT Computation Flow (Butterfly-Style Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["x(0) input sample"] --> ADDSUM["Adder Bank"]
    S1["x(1) input sample"] --> ADDSUM
    S2["x(2) input sample"] --> ADDSUM

    S4["x(N-1) input sample"] --> ADDSUM
    ADDSUM --> CM["Cosine Multiplier Bank"]
    CM --> COEF["Coefficient Multiplier alpha k"]
    COEF --> OUT0["X(0) DC Output"]
    COEF --> OUT1["X(1) AC Output"]
    COEF --> OUT2["X(2) AC Output"]
    COEF --> OUTK["X(N-1) AC Output"]

```

### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: 2D DCT via Row-Column Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMG["Input Image Block 8x8"] --> ROW1[Row Buffer 1]
    IMG --> ROW2[Row Buffer 2]
    IMG --> ROW3[Row Buffer N]
    ROW1 --> DCTR[1D DCT Engine Row-Wise]
    ROW2 --> DCTR
    ROW3 --> DCTR
    DCTR --> TRANS[Transposed Intermediate 8x8]
    TRANS --> COLDCT[1D DCT Engine Column-Wise]

    style DCTR fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,color:#000

```

> [!NOTE]
> The yellow blocks in this diagram represent the **separable property** of 2D DCT: one shared 1D DCT engine processes all rows, then all columns. Modern JPEG/HEVC encoder chips reuse a single 1D DCT hardware unit twice — a major silicon-area savings.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT). Write the mathematical expression for the 1D DCT-II of a sequence of length $N$ and explain the significance of the normalization coefficient $\alpha(k)$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** is a real, orthogonal transform that decomposes a finite sequence of $N$ real samples into a sum of cosine basis functions. The 1D DCT-II is defined as:

$$X(k) = \alpha(k) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right], \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

where the normalization coefficient is:

$$\alpha(k) = \begin{cases} \sqrt{1/N}, & k = 0 \\ \sqrt{2/N}, & k = 1, 2, \ldots, N-1 \end{cases}$$

The normalization coefficient $\alpha(k)$ **ensures the DCT basis functions form an orthonormal set**, which guarantees that the transform is energy-preserving (Parseval's theorem holds) and that the transform matrix satisfies $C \cdot C^T = I$. The distinct value for $k=0$ (DC) versus $k \geq 1$ (AC) compensates for the fact that the DC cosine has a non-zero mean while all AC cosines have zero mean. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

List any **five key properties** of the Discrete Cosine Transform that make it the preferred transform in image and video compression standards.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — list each with 1-line explanation):**

1. **Real-valued coefficients** — no complex arithmetic, simpler hardware implementation.
2. **Orthogonality** — $C \cdot C^T = I_N$, allows perfect reconstruction via inverse transform.
3. **Energy compaction** — for correlated signals, over 90% of the energy is concentrated in few low-frequency coefficients.
4. **Separability** — 2D DCT decomposes into two sequential 1D DCTs, reducing computational complexity.
5. **Decorrelation** — DCT approximates the optimal Karhunen–Loève Transform (KLT) for highly correlated first-order Markov signals, producing nearly independent coefficients. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): DCT-II Derivation + 2D Separability**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Starting from the definition of the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT), derive the mathematical expression for the 1D DCT-II of a sequence $x(n)$ of length $N$. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **separability property** of the 2D DCT. Show how a 2D DCT of an $M \times N$ image block can be computed as two sequential 1D DCT operations. Mention the computational advantage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of 1D DCT-II from DFT (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Begin with the $N$-point DFT of $x(n)$:

$$X_{DFT}(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$$

**Step 2:** Construct a 2N-point even-symmetric extension $y(n)$ of $x(n)$:

$$y(n) = \begin{cases} x(n), & 0 \leq n \leq N-1 \\ x(2N-1-n), & N \leq n \leq 2N-1 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3:** Apply 2N-point DFT to $y(n)$:

$$Y(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{2N-1} y(n) e^{-j\pi kn/N}$$

**Step 4:** Split the sum into the original and mirrored halves:

$$Y(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j\pi kn/N} + \sum_{n=N}^{2N-1} x(2N-1-n) e^{-j\pi kn/N}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $m = 2N-1-n$ in the second sum:

$$Y(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j\pi kn/N} + \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) e^{j\pi k(2N-1-m)/N}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the second exponential using $e^{j2\pi k} = 1$:

$$e^{j\pi k(2N-1-m)/N} = e^{j2\pi k} \cdot e^{-j\pi k/N} \cdot e^{j\pi km/N} = e^{-j\pi k/N} \cdot e^{j\pi km/N}$$

**Step 7:** Combine both sums and factor $e^{-j\pi k/(2N)}$:

$$Y(k) = e^{-j\pi k/(2N)} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \left[ e^{-j\pi k(2n+1)/(2N)} + e^{j\pi k(2n+1)/(2N)} \right]$$

**Step 8:** Apply Euler's identity to obtain the cosine:

$$Y(k) = 2 e^{-j\pi k/(2N)} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi k(2n+1)}{2N}\right]$$

**Step 9:** The DCT-II is the real, normalized magnitude:

$$X(k) = \alpha(k) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right]$$

where $\alpha(0) = \sqrt{1/N}$ and $\alpha(k) = \sqrt{2/N}$ for $k \geq 1$. $\blacksquare$ **(7 marks)**

**(b) Separability of 2D DCT (7 marks):**

The 2D DCT-II of an $M \times N$ image $x(m,n)$ is defined as:

$$X(u,v) = \alpha(u)\alpha(v) \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(m,n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2m+1)u}{2M}\right] \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)v}{2N}\right]$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange using distributivity:**

$$X(u,v) = \alpha(v) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left\{ \alpha(u) \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} x(m,n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2m+1)u}{2M}\right] \right\} \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)v}{2N}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Identify the inner sum as 1D DCT across each row:**

$$X_{row}(u,n) = \alpha(u) \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} x(m,n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2m+1)u}{2M}\right] \quad \text{(row-wise 1D DCT)}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the outer sum as 1D DCT down each column of the intermediate matrix:**

$$X(u,v) = \alpha(v) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} X_{row}(u,n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)v}{2N}\right] \quad \text{(column-wise 1D DCT)}$$

**Step 4 — Computational advantage:** A direct 2D DCT requires $O(M^2 N^2)$ multiplications. The separable row-then-column approach requires $2 \times O(MN \log_2 MN)$. For an $8 \times 8$ JPEG block, direct computation needs $4096$ multiplications, while separable needs only $192$ — a **~21× speedup**. Modern JPEG/HEVC encoder chips exploit this by reusing a single 1D DCT hardware unit twice. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Numerical DCT Computation + Application in JPEG**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compute the 4-point DCT-II of the input sequence $x = [8, 6, 4, 2]$. Show all calculation steps and verify the result using Parseval's energy theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe with a neat block diagram how the DCT is applied in the **JPEG image compression** standard. Explain the role of the **quantization table** and the **zig-zag scan** in achieving compression. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical 4-point DCT-II (7 marks):**

Given $x(0) = 8$, $x(1) = 6$, $x(2) = 4$, $x(3) = 2$, $N = 4$.

The DCT-II formula:

$$X(k) = \alpha(k) \sum_{n=0}^{3} x(n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{8}\right]$$

**Compute $X(0)$** (with $\alpha(0) = \sqrt{1/4} = 0.5$):

$$X(0) = 0.5 \times [8(1) + 6(1) + 4(1) + 2(1)] = 0.5 \times 20 = 10.000$$

**Compute $X(1)$** (with $\alpha(1) = \sqrt{2/4} = 0.7071$):

$$X(1) = 0.7071 \times [8(0.9239) + 6(0.3827) + 4(-0.3827) + 2(-0.9239)]$$
$$= 0.7071 \times [7.3912 + 2.2962 - 1.5308 - 1.8478]$$
$$= 0.7071 \times 6.3088 \approx 4.461$$

**Compute $X(2)$** (with $\alpha(2) = 0.7071$):

$$X(2) = 0.7071 \times [8(0.7071) + 6(-0.7071) + 4(-0.7071) + 2(0.7071)]$$
$$= 0.7071 \times [5.6568 - 4.2426 - 2.8284 + 1.4142]$$
$$= 0.7071 \times 0.0000 = 0.000$$

**Compute $X(3)$** (with $\alpha(3) = 0.7071$):

$$X(3) = 0.7071 \times [8(0.3827) + 6(-0.9239) + 4(0.9239) + 2(-0.3827)]$$
$$= 0.7071 \times [3.0616 - 5.5434 + 3.6956 - 0.7654]$$
$$= 0.7071 \times 0.4484 \approx 0.317$$

**Final DCT vector:**

$$\boxed{X = [10.000, \ 4.461, \ 0.000, \ 0.317]}$$

**Parseval's Energy Verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{n=0}^{3} x^2(n) &= 64 + 36 + 16 + 4 = 120 \\
\sum_{k=0}^{3} X^2(k) &= 100.000 + 19.900 + 0.000 + 0.100 = 120.000 \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

The energy is preserved, confirming correct computation. **(7 marks)**

**(b) DCT in JPEG Compression Pipeline (7 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RGB Image Input] --> B[Color Space Conversion to YCbCr]
    B --> C[8x8 Block Partitioning]
    C --> D[Level Shift subtract 128]
    D --> E[DCT-II on each Block]
    E --> F[Quantization using Standard Q-Table]
    F --> G[Zig-Zag Scan reordering]
    G --> H[Run Length Encoding RLE]
    H --> I[Huffman or Arithmetic Coding]
    I --> J[JPEG Bitstream]
    style E fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F fill:#F4B084,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style G fill:#A9D08E,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

**Step-by-step role explanation:**

1. **Color Space Conversion:** RGB is converted to YCbCr so that chrominance components (Cb, Cr) can be downsampled (4:2:0 subsampling), exploiting the human eye's lower color sensitivity.

2. **8×8 Blocking:** The image is divided into non-overlapping 8×8 pixel blocks (the natural DCT processing unit in JPEG).

3. **Level Shift:** Each pixel value is shifted by subtracting 128 so that values range from $-128$ to $+127$, centering the DC coefficient around zero for better compression.

4. **DCT-II Application:** Each 8×8 block undergoes a 2D DCT-II, producing 64 frequency coefficients — 1 DC (average intensity) and 63 AC (frequency variation) coefficients.

5. **Quantization:** Each DCT coefficient is divided element-wise by a corresponding entry in a **standard JPEG quantization table** (Q-table) and rounded to the nearest integer. The Q-table assigns **small divisors to low-frequency coefficients** (preserving detail) and **large divisors to high-frequency coefficients** (aggressively zeroing them). This is the **lossy** step and where compression is primarily achieved.

6. **Zig-Zag Scan:** The 64 quantized coefficients are reordered from a 2D grid into a 1D sequence by traversing the block in a zig-zag pattern (low-to-high frequency). This clusters the many trailing zeros together, which dramatically improves run-length encoding efficiency.

7. **Entropy Coding:** The 1D zig-zag sequence is further compressed using **Huffman coding** (variable-length prefix codes) or arithmetic coding, producing the final JPEG bitstream.

**Role of the Q-Table:** Controls the **rate-distortion tradeoff**. Higher Q-values produce smaller file sizes but more visible artifacts (blocking, ringing). Lower Q-values preserve quality at higher bitrates.

**Role of Zig-Zag Scan:** Reorders coefficients to group the **most-likely-zero high-frequency coefficients at the end** of the 1D array, maximizing run-length encoding of zeros and yielding an additional 1.5–2× compression. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on DCT Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the special case $\alpha(0) = \sqrt{1/N}$ vs. $\alpha(k) = \sqrt{2/N}$ for $k \geq 1$.** Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Always write both cases in the answer.
>
> 2. **Sign error in the cosine argument.** The argument is $\pi(2n+1)k/(2N)$, not $\pi nk/N$ (that is the DFT, not the DCT). Mixing these up is the #1 most common error.
>
> 3. **Not verifying Parseval's theorem** in numerical problems. KTU examiners give 1 bonus mark for explicitly showing energy conservation as a self-check.
>
> 4. **Confusing DCT-II with DCT-IV (MDCT).** The MDCT used in MP3/AAC is a *modified* DCT with a 50% overlap structure — different formula, different boundary handling.
>
> 5. **In derivation questions, skipping the even-symmetric extension step.** This is the conceptual foundation; without it, the derivation is incomplete and earns at most 3/7 marks.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning separability for 2D DCT questions.** The fact that 2D DCT = row 1D DCT + column 1D DCT is worth 2 marks by itself in any 2D-DCT question.
>
> 7. **Omitting the units / dimensions of $k$ and $n$.** Always state $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ and $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Memorize This Section**

- [x] **DCT-II Forward Formula:** $X(k) = \alpha(k) \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right]$
- [x] **Normalization:** $\alpha(0) = \sqrt{1/N}$, $\alpha(k) = \sqrt{2/N}$ for $k \geq 1$
- [x] **Inverse (DCT-III):** $x(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \alpha(k) X(k) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right]$
- [x] **Real-valued, orthogonal, energy-preserving** — three properties to always mention
- [x] **2D DCT is separable** = row DCT + column DCT (computational savings of ~21× for 8×8)
- [x] **Energy compaction** — 90%+ energy in top-left low-frequency coefficients
- [x] **JPEG uses 8×8 DCT-II blocks**; HEVC uses 4×4 to 32×32 variable blocks
- [x] **DCT is used in JPEG, MPEG-1/2/4, H.261/263/264, HEVC, AV1, VP9, MP3, AAC, WMA, Dolby Digital**
- [x] **MP3 and AAC use MDCT (DCT-IV variant)** with 50% overlapping windows, not DCT-II
- [x] **DCT approximates the optimal Karhunen–Loève Transform (KLT)** for first-order Markov signals with high inter-sample correlation
- [x] **Quantization + Zig-Zag scan** are what *actually achieve compression*; the DCT itself is lossless
- [x] **Parseval's Theorem** check: $\sum x^2(n) = \sum X^2(k)$ — useful self-check in numerical problems
- [x] **DC coefficient** $X(0)$ carries the *average intensity* of the block
- [x] **AC coefficients** $X(k\geq 1)$ carry *spatial-frequency variation* information
- [x] **Zig-zag scan order** traverses from low-frequency (top-left) to high-frequency (bottom-right) of the 8×8 block
- [x] **Computational complexity:** 1D DCT-II is $O(N \log N)$; 2D DCT-II separable is $O(MN \log MN)$
- [x] **Boundary handling** in DCT is *implicit* via even-symmetric extension — no windowing function needed (unlike DFT)
- [x] **Hardware implementation:** Most modern SoCs (smartphone AP, TV SoC) have dedicated DCT hardware blocks consuming <1% of die area
- [x] **Future trend:** Modern codecs (AV1, VVC) use **larger adaptive transforms** (up to 64×64) and **multiple transform types** (DCT-II, DCT-VIII, ADST) for better coding efficiency

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Video Compression and Quantization Levels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Video Compression and Quantization Levels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Video Compression — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Video Compression** is the process of encoding a raw digital video stream using sophisticated mathematical algorithms to **reduce the number of bits required to represent the video sequence**, while preserving an acceptable level of visual quality for human perception. It exploits three fundamental types of redundancies: **spatial redundancy**, **temporal redundancy**, and **psychovisual redundancy**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, video compression is treated as the **backbone of all modern digital entertainment systems** — from streaming platforms (Netflix, YouTube) to broadcast television, gaming consoles, and VR headsets. The discipline is governed by international standards such as **MPEG-2, MPEG-4 (H.264/AVC), H.265 (HEVC), and the emerging VVC (H.266)**.

## 1.2 Quantization Levels — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantization** is the process of mapping a continuous range of analog values (or a large set of discrete values) to a smaller, finite set of discrete levels. The **Quantization Level (L)** is the total number of distinct amplitude values a quantizer can output. It is directly determined by the number of bits per sample: **L = 2ⁿ**, where **n** is the number of bits.

The **quantization step size (Δ)** determines the resolution of the quantizer. A higher number of quantization levels means finer granularity, better fidelity, and a higher bit rate.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — Making It Stick

### 🎬 Analogy 1: Video Compression = Packing for a Long Trip
Imagine you are packing for a **one-year world trip** (a long video sequence). You have:
- 10 suitcases full of clothes (the **raw video data**)
- Only **1 suitcase allowed** on the plane (the **limited bandwidth/storage**)

**Video compression is the art of fitting the 10 suitcases into 1**, without losing the clothes you absolutely need. You achieve this by:
1. **Removing duplicate clothes** (temporal redundancy — adjacent frames look similar)
2. **Folding similar shirts together** (spatial redundancy — neighboring pixels in a frame are correlated)
3. **Throwing away clothes you never wear** (psychovisual redundancy — the human eye can't perceive ultra-fine color differences)

### 📊 Analogy 2: Quantization Levels = Rounding Prices
Suppose a shopkeeper sells products priced at **₹1.23, ₹1.87, ₹2.45, ₹2.91**, etc. If the shopkeeper decides to **round to the nearest rupee** (2 quantization levels: ₹1, ₹2, ₹3...), he uses **L = 3 levels**. If he rounds to the nearest 50 paise, he uses **L = 5 levels** (₹1, ₹1.50, ₹2, ₹2.50, ₹3). The higher the number of levels, the closer the approximation to the original price — but the more "memory" (bits) the price list needs.

> [!TIP]
> **The Compression-Quality Trade-off**: Higher quantization levels = better quality but larger file size. Lower quantization levels = smaller file but more visible artifacts (blocking, ringing, mosquito noise).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of Quantization Levels** | **L** | dimensionless | Granularity of amplitude representation |
| **Quantization Step Size** | **Δ** | volts / LSB | Resolution of each quantization interval |
| **Bits per Sample** | **n** | bits/sample | Determines the number of levels |
| **Bit Rate** | **R** | bits/second (bps) | Total data rate of compressed stream |
| **Compression Ratio** | **CR** | ratio | Original size / Compressed size |
| **Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | **PSNR** | decibels (dB) | Objective quality measure |
| **Mean Squared Error** | **MSE** | squared units | Average squared error between original & reconstructed |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Video Compression

### 2.1.1 Spatial Redundancy (Intra-Frame)
Within a **single frame**, neighboring pixels tend to have very similar luminance and chrominance values. For example, a blue sky in a frame contains millions of pixels that are nearly identical. Spatial compression techniques exploit this by using:
- **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** — converts pixel blocks to frequency coefficients
- **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** — used in JPEG 2000 and modern codecs
- **Predictive coding** — predicts a pixel from its neighbors

### 2.1.2 Temporal Redundancy (Inter-Frame)
Across **consecutive frames**, large regions remain unchanged or move predictably. In a one-minute video at 30 fps, there are 1800 frames, but the actual *change* between adjacent frames is often less than 5%. This is exploited using:
- **Motion Estimation (ME)** — finds the best-matching block in a reference frame
- **Motion Compensation (MC)** — uses motion vectors to predict the current frame
- **Difference coding** — only the residual (error) is stored, not the full frame

### 2.1.3 Psychovisual (Perceptual) Redundancy
The **Human Visual System (HVS)** is more sensitive to **luminance than chrominance**, more sensitive to **low-frequency content than high-frequency**, and less sensitive to **fine details at high motion**. This allows:
- **Chroma subsampling** (4:2:0, 4:2:2 formats)
- **Coarser quantization of high-frequency coefficients**
- **Perceptual masking** in modern codecs

## 2.2 The Quantization Process — Step by Step

Quantization is the **primary lossy step** in video compression. It is performed after the DCT and before entropy coding. The process works as follows:

### Step 1: Input Range
A continuous or finely-discrete input signal **x(n)** has a range **[x_min, x_max]**.

### Step 2: Quantization Step Size
The quantizer divides this range into **L equally-spaced intervals** (uniform quantization) or unequal intervals (non-uniform quantization, e.g., μ-law, A-law).

### Step 3: Quantization
Each input sample is mapped to the **nearest quantization level** (mid-rise or mid-tread quantizer).

### Step 4: Reconstruction
At the decoder, each level index is mapped back to a representative value (typically the midpoint of the interval), introducing **quantization error (noise)**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it contains every formula you need for numerical problems on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = 2^n$ | Number of quantization levels | n = bits per sample |
| 2 | $\Delta = \dfrac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{L}$ | Quantization step size (uniform) | Range divided by number of levels |
| 3 | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{pp}}{2^n}$ | Step size in terms of peak-to-peak voltage | $V_{pp}$ = peak-to-peak input range |
| 4 | $R = f_s \times n \times C$ | Bit rate (uncompressed) | $f_s$ = sampling rate, C = number of channels |
| 5 | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Compression ratio | Always ≥ 1 |
| 6 | $\text{Bit Savings (\%)} = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{CR}\right) \times 100$ | Percentage reduction | Useful for board problems |
| 7 | $\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | Quantization noise variance (uniform) | Assumes uniform distribution of error |
| 8 | $SQNR = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | For full-scale sinusoid, n = bits/sample |
| 9 | $PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{MAX^2}{MSE}\right)$ | Peak SNR for images/video | MAX = 255 for 8-bit |
| 10 | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{N}\bigl[I(i,j) - K(i,j)\bigr]^2$ | Mean Squared Error | $I$ = original, $K$ = reconstructed |
| 11 | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{L} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Entropy (bits/symbol) | $p_i$ = probability of symbol $i$ |
| 12 | $\bar{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{L} p_i \, n_i$ | Average codeword length | $n_i$ = length of code for symbol $i$ |
| 13 | $\eta = \dfrac{H}{\bar{L}} \times 100\%$ | Coding efficiency | Should approach 100% for Huffman |
| 14 | $f_{\text{block}} = \dfrac{1}{2\,\Delta\,t_{\text{slot}}}$ | Maximum block rate | Used in quantization scheduling problems |
| 15 | $D = \dfrac{N^2}{8}$ (2D DCT basis) | Energy compaction metric | $N$ = DCT block size |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall**: Never write absolute value as `|x|` inside a markdown table — the pipe symbol breaks the table parser. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX form. (Notice in row #10 above we used `\bigl[ ... \bigr]` instead of `\vert ... \vert`.)

## 2.4 Uniform vs. Non-Uniform Quantization

### Uniform Quantization
- **Step size Δ is constant** across the entire input range.
- Simple hardware implementation.
- Best suited for signals with **uniform probability distribution**.
- **Quantization error variance**: $\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$

### Non-Uniform Quantization
- **Step size varies** — small Δ where the signal is concentrated, large Δ where it is rare.
- Examples: **μ-law** (North America/Japan) and **A-law** (Europe).
- Better SQNR for speech signals (which are Laplacian-distributed).
- Implemented via **companding**: compress at encoder, expand at decoder.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application of Video Compression |
|---|---|
| **Streaming** | Netflix, Amazon Prime, Disney+ use H.264/HEVC; adaptive bitrate streaming relies on multiple quantization levels |
| **Broadcast TV** | DVB-T2, ATSC 3.0 use HEVC with hierarchical quantization for HD/4K |
| **Video Conferencing** | Zoom, Teams, WebEx use H.264 SVC with coarse quantization for low bandwidth |
| **Surveillance** | H.265+ with smart quantization reduces storage by 80% over MJPEG |
| **Medical Imaging** | DICOM uses lossless (no quantization) for diagnostic; lossy for archival |
| **Gaming/VR** | Real-time codecs like H.264 with ultra-low quantization for 90+ fps streams |
| **DVD/Blu-ray** | MPEG-2 for DVD, H.264 for Blu-ray, HEVC for 4K Ultra HD Blu-ray |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantization Step Size from First Principles

Let us derive the formula for the quantization step size starting from the definition of a uniform mid-rise quantizer.

### Setup
Consider a continuous input signal $x$ bounded in the range:
$$x \in [x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$$

The number of bits per sample is $n$ bits. Therefore the number of quantization levels is:
$$L = 2^n$$

The quantizer divides the range $[x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$ into $L$ equal intervals. The width of each interval is, by definition, the quantization step size $\Delta$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= \frac{\text{Total Range}}{\text{Number of Intervals}} \\[6pt]
\Delta &= \frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{L} \\[6pt]
\Delta &= \frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{2^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: This is the most fundamental relationship. Doubling the number of levels (adding 1 bit) **halves the step size**, improving resolution by a factor of 2.

### Special Case: Bipolar Signal
For a symmetric bipolar signal (e.g., audio) where $x_{\min} = -V$ and $x_{\max} = +V$, the peak-to-peak voltage is $V_{pp} = 2V$. The step size becomes:
$$\Delta = \frac{2V}{2^n} = \frac{V_{pp}}{2^n}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Quantization Noise Variance

The quantization error $e_q$ is defined as the difference between the input $x$ and the quantized output $x_q$:
$$e_q = x - x_q$$

For a **uniform quantizer** with step size $\Delta$, and assuming the input is finely quantized enough that the error is uniformly distributed in the interval $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$, the error has a **uniform probability density function**:
$$p(e_q) = \frac{1}{\Delta}, \quad \text{for } e_q \in \left[-\frac{\Delta}{2}, \frac{\Delta}{2}\right]$$

The variance (average power) of this error is the second moment about the mean (which is 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_q^2 &= E[e_q^2] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \, p(e_q) \, de_q \\[6pt]
&= \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta} \, de_q \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left( \frac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3} \right) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2 \cdot \Delta^3}{24} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{12} \\[6pt]
\sigma_q^2 &= \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Result**: $\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$. This is the noise floor of any uniform quantizer.

## 3.3 Derivation: Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

For a **full-scale sinusoid** $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ (the worst-case test signal), the signal power is:
$$P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

The RMS value of the signal is $A/\sqrt{2}$. For a quantizer of step size $\Delta$ with a bipolar range such that the full scale $V_{pp} = 2^n \cdot \Delta$, the peak amplitude $A = 2^{n-1} \cdot \Delta$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A^2/2}{\Delta^2/12}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{6 A^2}{\Delta^2}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}(6) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A}{\Delta}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Now substitute $A = 2^{n-1} \cdot \Delta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR} &= 10 \log_{10}(6) + 20 \log_{10}(2^{n-1}) \\[6pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}(6) + 20 (n-1) \log_{10}(2) \\[6pt]
&= 7.78 + 6.02 (n-1) \\[6pt]
&= 7.78 + 6.02n - 6.02 \\[6pt]
\text{SQNR} &\approx 6.02\,n + 1.76 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **The Famous "6 dB-per-bit" Rule**: Each additional bit of quantization improves the SQNR by approximately **6.02 dB**. This is the single most important result in quantization theory and appears in nearly every KTU question paper.

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Problem 1 (Model Solution)

**Problem:** A video frame is sampled at 1920 × 1080 resolution, 30 frames per second, with 8 bits per pixel and 3 color channels. Calculate (a) the uncompressed bit rate, (b) the compression ratio if the compressed bit rate is 5 Mbps, and (c) the bit savings percentage.

### Part (a): Uncompressed Bit Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pixels per frame} &= 1920 \times 1080 = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels} \\[6pt]
\text{Bits per frame} &= 2{,}073{,}600 \times 8 \times 3 = 49{,}766{,}400 \text{ bits} \\[6pt]
R_{\text{uncomp}} &= 49{,}766{,}400 \times 30 = 1{,}492{,}992{,}000 \text{ bps} \\[6pt]
R_{\text{uncomp}} &\approx 1.493 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b): Compression Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{R_{\text{uncomp}}}{R_{\text{comp}}} = \frac{1{,}492{,}992{,}000}{5{,}000{,}000} \\[6pt]
CR &= 298.6
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (c): Bit Savings

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= \left(1 - \frac{1}{CR}\right) \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&= \left(1 - \frac{1}{298.6}\right) \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&\approx 99.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Problem 2 (Model Solution)

**Problem:** A quantizer has an input range of $[-4\text{ V}, +4\text{ V}]$ and uses $n = 8$ bits. Calculate the (a) number of levels, (b) step size, (c) quantization noise power, and (d) SQNR for a full-scale sinusoid.

### Part (a): Number of Levels

$$L = 2^8 = 256 \text{ levels}$$

### Part (b): Step Size

$$
\Delta = \frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{L} = \frac{4 - (-4)}{256} = \frac{8}{256} = 0.03125 \text{ V} = 31.25 \text{ mV}
$$

### Part (c): Quantization Noise Power

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(0.03125)^2}{12} = \frac{9.766 \times 10^{-4}}{12} \approx 8.138 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V}^2
$$

### Part (d): SQNR

$$
\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Video Compression Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Video Input\nY Cb Cr Frames] --> B[Pre-processing\nChroma Subsampling 4 2 0]
    B --> C[Frame Partitioning\nMacroblocks 16x16]
    C --> D[Motion Estimation\nReference Frames]
    D --> E[Motion Compensated\nPrediction]
    E --> F[DCT Transform\n8x8 Block]
    F --> G[Quantization\nScalar Q Matrix]
    G --> H[Entropy Coding\nHuffman or CABAC]
    H --> I[Compressed Bitstream\nStorage or Transmission]
    I --> J[Inverse Quantization]
    J --> K[Inverse DCT]
    K --> L[Reconstructed Frame]
    L --> D
    L --> M[Decoder Output\nDisplay Buffer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The left-to-right flow shows the encoder, while the loop back from "Reconstructed Frame" to "Motion Estimation" indicates the prediction feedback used for inter-frame coding. The decoder path (inverse operations) reconstructs the video for display.

## 4.2 Quantization Decision Threshold Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Sample x] --> B{Decision Logic}
    B -->|x in interval 0| C1[Output Level 0\nmid of interval]
    B -->|x in interval 1| C2[Output Level 1\nmid of interval]
    B -->|x in interval 2| C3[Output Level 2\nmid of interval]
    B -->|x in interval 3| C4[Output Level 3\nmid of interval]
    B -->|x in interval L-1| C5[Output Level L-1\nmid of interval]
    C1 --> D[Quantized Sample xq]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[Quantization Error\neq = x - xq\nmax magnitude Delta/2]
```

## 4.3 DCT and Quantization — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DCT_Block
        direction TB
        A1[Input Pixel Block\n8x8 Matrix] --> A2[Forward DCT\n2D Transform]
        A2 --> A3[Frequency Coefficients\nDC plus 63 AC terms]
    end

    A3 --> B1[Divide by Q Matrix\nPer coefficient scaling]

    subgraph Quant_Block
        direction TB
        B1 --> B2[Round to Nearest\nInteger]
        B2 --> B3[Quantized\nCoefficients]
    end

    B3 --> C1[Zigzag Scan\n1D sequence]
    C1 --> C2[Run Length Encoding\nRLC]
    C2 --> C3[Entropy Coding\nVariable Length]
    C3 --> C4[Compressed Stream\nBits]
```

## 4.4 Compression Standards Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[H 261\n1991\n64 to 384 kbps\nISDN Video] --> N2[MPEG 1\n1993\n1.5 Mbps\nVCD]
    N2 --> N3[MPEG 2\n1995\n4 to 15 Mbps\nDVD DVB]
    N3 --> N4[MPEG 4 Part 2\n1999\nDivX Xvid]
    N4 --> N5[H 264 AVC\n2003\nBlu ray Streaming]
    N5 --> N6[H 265 HEVC\n2013\n4K UHD 50 percent savings]
    N6 --> N7[H 266 VVC\n2020\n8K 50 percent over HEVC]
    N7 --> N8[EVC AV1 AVS3\n2020 to 2024\nRoyalty Free Codecs]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip**: The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know the **rough release year** and the **key application** of at least MPEG-2, H.264, and H.265. The diagram above gives you a clean memory aid.

## 4.5 Quantization Level Trade-off Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choosing Number of\nQuantization Levels L] --> B{Optimization Goal}
    B -->|High Quality Required| C1[Use L equals 256\nn equals 8 bits\nLarge file size\nPSNR greater than 40 dB]
    B -->|Balanced Quality| C2[Use L equals 64\nn equals 6 bits\nMedium file size\nPSNR 30 to 40 dB]
    B -->|High Compression| C3[Use L equals 16\nn equals 4 bits\nSmall file size\nPSNR 20 to 30 dB\nVisible blocking]
    C1 --> D[Application\nBlu ray Mastering\nMedical Archive]
    C2 --> E[Application\nStreaming SD HD\nWeb Video]
    C3 --> F[Application\nSurveillance\nVideo Conference\nLow Bandwidth]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

> **Q: Define quantization. Explain uniform quantization with the help of a characteristic curve.**

**Model Answer:**

Quantization is the process of **mapping a continuous range of values (or a large discrete set) into a smaller finite set of values**, called quantization levels. It is the **primary lossy step** in video compression, where each DCT coefficient is approximated to the nearest level.

In **uniform quantization**, the step size $\Delta$ is **constant across the entire input range**. The input range $[x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$ is divided into $L = 2^n$ equal intervals, each of width:

$$\Delta = \frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{2^n}$$

**Characteristics of uniform quantization**:
1. Step size is constant
2. Decision thresholds are equally spaced
3. Reconstruction levels are at the midpoints of each interval
4. Quantization error $e_q$ lies in the range $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$
5. Quantization noise power is $\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$
6. Best suited for uniformly distributed signals

**Sketch description**: The transfer characteristic is a **staircase function** with $L$ steps of equal height. Each step corresponds to one quantization level. The difference between the staircase and the ideal linear line represents the quantization error.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **Q: What is the significance of quantization levels in video compression? How is the number of levels related to the bit rate?**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance of Quantization Levels**:

1. **Determines visual quality**: Higher quantization levels → finer amplitude resolution → less quantization noise → higher PSNR and better visual quality.
2. **Controls compression ratio**: Lower quantization levels → coarser representation → higher compression ratio → smaller file size.
3. **Provides trade-off knob**: The choice of quantization level is the **primary rate-control parameter** in any video encoder (H.264, HEVC, etc.).
4. **Affects perceptual artifacts**: Too few levels produce visible **blocking artifacts**, **banding**, and **mosquito noise**, especially in smooth gradient regions like skies.

**Relationship between levels and bit rate**:

If $L$ is the number of quantization levels, the number of bits per sample is:
$$n = \log_2 L$$

The bit rate of a video stream is:
$$R = f_s \times n \times C = f_s \times \log_2(L) \times C$$

where $f_s$ is the sampling rate (samples/sec) and $C$ is the number of channels (e.g., 3 for YCbCr).

**Example**: If $L$ doubles from 256 to 512 (one extra bit per sample), the bit rate increases by $\dfrac{9}{8} = 12.5\%$, while the PSNR improves by approximately 6.02 dB.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern**: Each Part B question has **internal choice** — you must answer either **Question A OR Question B** in full. Both are provided here for exam preparation.

---

### 📝 QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

> **Q(a) [7 Marks]: Explain the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) based video compression pipeline with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of quantization in the pipeline.**
>
> **Q(b) [7 Marks]: A digital video system samples frames at 720 × 576 resolution, 25 fps, with 4:2:0 chroma subsampling and 8 bits per sample. The compressed bit rate is 2 Mbps. Calculate (i) uncompressed bit rate, (ii) compression ratio, (iii) bit savings percentage, and (iv) the maximum possible storage on a 4.7 GB DVD.**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**DCT-based Video Compression Pipeline**:

The modern video compression pipeline (used in H.264, HEVC, etc.) consists of the following sequential stages:

**Stage 1: Pre-processing**
- Convert RGB to YCbCr color space
- Apply **chroma subsampling** (typically 4:2:0) — exploits lower human sensitivity to color than brightness
- Resize to standard resolution

**Stage 2: Frame Partitioning**
- Divide each frame into **macroblocks** (16×16 pixels) or **coding tree units (CTUs)** in HEVC (up to 64×64)
- Each macroblock is further divided into 8×8 or 4×4 sub-blocks for DCT

**Stage 3: Motion Estimation and Compensation (Inter-frames)**
- Search for the best-matching block in a reference frame (I or P frame)
- Compute **motion vector (MV)** = displacement of the block
- Generate **predicted block** and compute **residual (difference)**
- Skip this step for **I-frames** (intra-coded)

**Stage 4: DCT (Discrete Cosine Transform)**
- Apply 2D DCT to each 8×8 block of residual data
- Transforms spatial-domain pixels to **frequency-domain coefficients**
- Energy is concentrated in low-frequency coefficients (top-left of the block)
- Formula for 1D DCT of length N:

$$X_k = \sqrt{\frac{2}{N}} \, c_k \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_n \cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)k\pi}{2N}\right]$$

**Stage 5: Quantization (THE LOSSY STEP)**
- Each DCT coefficient is divided by a corresponding value in the **quantization matrix Q** and rounded
- Higher frequencies use **larger Q values** (coarser quantization) — exploits reduced HVS sensitivity
- This step **irreversibly discards information** → compression gain
- Formula: $X_q(u,v) = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{X(u,v)}{Q(u,v)}\right)$

**Stage 6: Entropy Coding (LOSS-LESS STEP)**
- **Zigzag scan** of quantized coefficients (low to high frequency)
- **Run-length encoding (RLE)** of trailing zeros
- **Huffman coding** or **CABAC (Context-Adaptive Binary Arithmetic Coding)** assigns variable-length codes

**Stage 7: Bitstream Formation**
- Motion vectors, coding modes, quantized coefficients, and headers are packed into the bitstream

**Role of Quantization**:
Quantization is the **single most important lossy operation** in the entire pipeline. It directly determines:
1. **Compression ratio** — larger Q → fewer bits → higher CR
2. **Visual quality** — larger Q → more artifacts → lower PSNR
3. **Bit rate control** — encoder adjusts Q to meet a target bit rate (rate-distortion optimization)

[Block diagram as shown in Section 4.3 above should be drawn in the exam for full marks]

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given**:
- Resolution: 720 × 576 pixels
- Frame rate: 25 fps
- Format: 4:2:0 chroma subsampling
- Bits per sample: 8 bits
- Compressed bit rate: 2 Mbps = 2 × 10⁶ bps
- DVD capacity: 4.7 GB

**Step 1: Luma samples per frame (4:2:0 has luma at full resolution)**:
$$\text{Luma pixels} = 720 \times 576 = 414{,}720$$

**Step 2: Chroma samples per frame (4:2:0 halves in both dimensions)**:
$$\text{Chroma Cb} = \frac{720}{2} \times \frac{576}{2} = 360 \times 288 = 103{,}680$$
$$\text{Chroma Cr} = 103{,}680 \text{ (same as Cb)}$$

**Step 3: Total samples per frame**:
$$N_{\text{sample}} = 414{,}720 + 103{,}680 + 103{,}680 = 622{,}080 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 4: Bits per frame**:
$$B_{\text{frame}} = 622{,}080 \times 8 = 4{,}976{,}640 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5: Uncompressed bit rate (part i)**:
$$R_{\text{uncomp}} = 4{,}976{,}640 \times 25 = 124{,}416{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 124.42 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 6: Compression ratio (part ii)**:
$$CR = \frac{124.42 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^6} = 62.21$$

**Step 7: Bit savings percentage (part iii)**:
$$\text{Savings} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{62.21}\right) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.01608) \times 100\% \approx 98.39\%$$

**Step 8: Storage time on 4.7 GB DVD (part iv)**:
$$4.7 \text{ GB} = 4.7 \times 8 \times 10^9 \text{ bits} = 37.6 \times 10^9 \text{ bits}$$
$$\text{Time} = \frac{37.6 \times 10^9 \text{ bits}}{2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 18{,}800 \text{ seconds} = 5 \text{ hours } 13 \text{ min } 20 \text{ sec}$$

---

### 📝 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**

> **Q(a) [7 Marks]: With necessary equations, derive the relationship between Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) and the number of bits per sample. Why is this result called the "6 dB per bit" rule?**
>
> **Q(b) [7 Marks]: An 8-bit uniform quantizer operates over an input range of $[-5\text{ V}, +5\text{ V}]$. Calculate (i) the quantization step size, (ii) the quantization noise power, (iii) the SQNR for a full-scale sinusoid, and (iv) the percentage reduction in noise power if the quantizer is upgraded to 10 bits.**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Setup and Assumptions**:
1. Input signal: full-scale sinusoid $x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$
2. Quantizer: uniform, mid-rise, $n$ bits per sample, $L = 2^n$ levels
3. Quantization step size: $\Delta$, full-scale range $\pm A$ such that $2A = 2^n \cdot \Delta$, giving $A = 2^{n-1} \Delta$

**Step 1: Signal Power**
For a sinusoid of peak amplitude $A$:
$$P_s = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

**Step 2: Quantization Noise Power**
For uniform quantizer (derived in Section 3.2):
$$P_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 3: Form the SQNR Ratio**
$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_s}{P_q} = \frac{A^2/2}{\Delta^2/12} = \frac{6A^2}{\Delta^2}$$

**Step 4: Substitute $A = 2^{n-1}\Delta$**
$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{6 \cdot (2^{n-1}\Delta)^2}{\Delta^2} = 6 \cdot 2^{2n-2} = \frac{6}{4} \cdot 2^{2n} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}$$

**Step 5: Convert to Decibels**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR (dB)} &= 10 \log_{10}(1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}) \\[6pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20n \log_{10}(2) \\[6pt]
&= 1.76 + 20n \times 0.30103 \\[6pt]
&= 1.76 + 6.0206\,n \\[6pt]
&\approx 6.02\,n + 1.76 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: The "6 dB per Bit" Rule**:
Differentiating the SQNR with respect to $n$:
$$\frac{d(\text{SQNR})}{dn} = 6.02 \text{ dB/bit}$$

This means **each additional bit of quantization improves the SQNR by approximately 6.02 dB** (a factor of 4 in linear power, or a factor of 2 in linear voltage). This is a logarithmic improvement — to gain 12 dB, you need 2 extra bits; to gain 24 dB, you need 4 extra bits. This rule is the foundation of all quantizer design.

**Practical implications**:
- Telephone quality audio: $n = 8$ bits → SQNR ≈ 49.9 dB
- CD quality audio: $n = 16$ bits → SQNR ≈ 98.1 dB
- Studio master audio: $n = 24$ bits → SQNR ≈ 146.2 dB

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given**:
- $n_1 = 8$ bits, $n_2 = 10$ bits
- Input range: $[-5\text{ V}, +5\text{ V}]$, so $x_{\min} = -5$, $x_{\max} = +5$, total span = 10 V

**Part (i): Quantization Step Size for 8-bit quantizer**

$$L_1 = 2^8 = 256 \text{ levels}$$

$$
\Delta_1 = \frac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{L_1} = \frac{10}{256} = 0.03906 \text{ V} = 39.06 \text{ mV}
$$

**Part (ii): Quantization Noise Power for 8-bit quantizer**

$$
P_{q1} = \frac{\Delta_1^2}{12} = \frac{(0.03906)^2}{12} = \frac{1.526 \times 10^{-3}}{12} \approx 1.272 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}^2
$$

**Part (iii): SQNR for full-scale sinusoid at 8 bits**

$$
\text{SQNR}_1 = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB}
$$

**Part (iv): Percentage reduction in noise power when upgrading to 10 bits**

For 10-bit quantizer:
$$L_2 = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ levels}$$

$$
\Delta_2 = \frac{10}{1024} = 0.009766 \text{ V} = 9.766 \text{ mV}
$$

$$
P_{q2} = \frac{\Delta_2^2}{12} = \frac{(0.009766)^2}{12} = \frac{9.536 \times 10^{-5}}{12} \approx 7.946 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}^2
$$

Reduction in noise power:
$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{P_{q1} - P_{q2}}{P_{q1}} \times 100\% = \frac{1.272 \times 10^{-4} - 7.946 \times 10^{-6}}{1.272 \times 10^{-4}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{1.193 \times 10^{-4}}{1.272 \times 10^{-4}} \times 100\% \approx 93.75\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical check**: Each extra bit reduces $\Delta$ by half → reduces $\Delta^2$ by a factor of 4 → reduces noise power by a factor of 4. Going from 8 to 10 bits is 2 extra bits, so noise power reduces by $4^2 = 16$. Reduction = $(16-1)/16 = 93.75\%$. ✓ Matches our calculation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON MISTAKE ZONE**
>
> **Mistake 1**: Students often write the **SQNR formula as $1.76n + 6.02$** (inverted coefficients). The correct form is **$6.02n + 1.76$** — note that **6.02 multiplies $n$** (the bits per sample), not the constant. Losing 1 mark here is extremely common.
>
> **Mistake 2**: When calculating **compression ratio**, students write $\dfrac{\text{Compressed}}{\text{Original}}$. The **correct** definition is $\dfrac{\text{Original}}{\text{Compressed}}$. Always check the order.
>
> **Mistake 3**: In the **chroma subsampling** problem, students assume 4:2:0 means $720 \times 576 \times 3$ total samples. It does **not** — it means luma is full and chroma is **half in both dimensions**, not 1/3 each.
>
> **Mistake 4**: Forgetting to convert **GB to bits** correctly. $1 \text{ GB} = 8 \times 10^9$ bits (using the SI convention used in storage devices), not $2^{30}$ bytes.
>
> **Mistake 5**: Writing $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{pp}}{2^n - 1}$ instead of $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{pp}}{2^n}$. The **denominator is $2^n$** (number of levels), not $2^n - 1$. (The latter would be used only for an inclusive range mapping, which is not standard in DSP.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before Your Exam**

### 🎯 Must-Know Definitions
- ✅ **Quantization Level (L)**: Total number of distinct output values of a quantizer; $L = 2^n$
- ✅ **Quantization Step Size (Δ)**: Width of each quantization interval; $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{pp}}{2^n}$
- ✅ **Quantization Error (e_q)**: Difference between actual and quantized value; bounded in $\left[-\dfrac{\Delta}{2}, +\dfrac{\Delta}{2}\right]$
- ✅ **Quantization Noise Power (σ²_q)**: Average power of quantization error; $\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ for uniform quantizer
- ✅ **SQNR**: Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio; $\text{SQNR} = 6.02n + 1.76$ dB
- ✅ **PSNR**: Peak SNR; $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{MAX^2}{MSE}\right)$ dB
- ✅ **Compression Ratio (CR)**: $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$

### 🎯 Must-Remember Numbers
- ✅ **6.02 dB per bit** — the most important rule of quantization
- ✅ **4.7 GB DVD** ≈ 4 hours of standard DVD video at 5–10 Mbps
- ✅ **4K UHD Blu-ray** uses HEVC at 50–100 Mbps
- ✅ **H.264** is the most widely deployed codec (90%+ of all video in 2024)
- ✅ **HEVC** halves the bit rate of H.264 at equivalent quality

### 🎯 Must-Draw Diagrams
- ✅ Block diagram of **DCT-based encoder** (at least 6 blocks: DCT → Quantizer → Entropy Coder)
- ✅ **Quantizer transfer characteristic** (staircase function showing input vs. output)
- ✅ **3-pillar redundancy model** (Spatial, Temporal, Psychovisual)

### 🎯 Must-Avoid Errors
- ❌ Do not confuse **bits per sample (n)** with **bits per pixel** (which can be 24 for RGB)
- ❌ Do not forget the **factor of 2** in chroma subsampling (4:2:0 = half in BOTH directions)
- ❌ Do not use $2^n - 1$ in the step size formula
- ❌ Do not omit the **mantissa 1.76 dB** term in SQNR (some teachers deduct for this)
- ❌ Do not claim uniform quantization is "always best" — non-uniform is better for speech

### 🎯 Real-World Connections to Remember
- 🔗 JPEG uses **DCT + scalar quantization + Huffman**
- 🔗 MP3 uses **modified DCT + perceptual quantization** (psychoacoustic model)
- 🔗 H.264/HEVC use **integer DCT + rate-distortion optimized quantization (RDOQ)**
- 🔗 Netflix adaptive streaming chooses **quantization parameter (QP)** per scene based on content complexity
- 🔗 Blu-ray uses **MPEG-2** (older) or **HEVC** (newer 4K UHD)

> **End of Module 3 — Video Compression and Quantization Levels — Premium Study Notes**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, Outcome-Based Education (NEP 2020) framework.*
