topics.title: Horizontal/Vertical Blanking Interval
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Horizontal/Vertical Blanking Interval

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Blanking Interval** in a video/television signal is the portion of the scan cycle during which the display's writing mechanism (electron beam in a CRT, or pixel-addressing circuitry in LCD/OLED panels) is intentionally suppressed (blanked) to permit the beam/pointer to retrace from the end of one line/scan to the start of the next without producing a visible trace on the screen.

- The **Horizontal Blanking Interval (HBI)** suppresses the beam during the **horizontal retrace** (right edge of screen → left edge of screen).
- The **Vertical Blanking Interval (VBI)** suppresses the beam during the **vertical retrace** (bottom-right corner → top-left corner).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The blanking interval is NOT "wasted" time. In modern broadcasting, the VBI is a critical data carrier for **Teletext, Closed Captions, V-Chip ratings, Copy-Generation Management System (CGMS), and Active Format Description (AFD)** signals.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are writing on a blackboard with chalk, moving strictly from **left-to-right**, and when you reach the end of the line you:
1. **Lift the chalk** (this is the *blanking* – the chalk is touching the board but not leaving a mark),
2. **Move your hand quickly back to the start** of the next line (this is the *retrace*),
3. **Put the chalk down** and begin writing again.

If you forgot to lift the chalk, you would scribble a messy diagonal line all the way back to the start. The **blanking interval is the "lifted chalk" period** during the retrace. The same logic applies to vertical movement (end of page → start of new page).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Standard broadcast TV field rate (NTSC M) | **59.94 Hz** |
| Standard broadcast TV field rate (PAL B/G) | **50 Hz** |
| Human visual flicker fusion threshold (CRT) | **~50–60 Hz** |
| Color subcarrier frequency (NTSC M) | **3.579545 MHz** |
| Color subcarrier frequency (PAL B/G) | **4.433618 MHz** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **odd field/even field** interlaced structure was specifically designed to fit within the 50/60 Hz mains frequency standard to avoid mains-frequency hum bars on the screen. The **vertical blanking interval straddles the boundary** between the two fields.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Horizontal Blanking Interval (HBI)

The HBI is a small time slice at the right edge of every scan line. It contains **three sub-intervals**, separated by the **DC level transitions** of the composite video signal:

1. **Front Porch (FP):** A short, black-level (0% brightness) interval immediately following the end of active video. Duration ≈ **1.5 µs (NTSC)** / **1.5 µs (PAL)**.
2. **Horizontal Sync Pulse (H-Sync):** A pulse driven *below* the black level (the "blacker than black" tip), used by the receiver's PLL to lock the horizontal oscillator. Duration ≈ **4.7 µs (NTSC M)** / **4.7 µs (PAL)**.
3. **Back Porch (BP):** The interval between the trailing edge of H-Sync and the start of the next active line. The **color burst** (8–10 cycles of subcarrier) is positioned on the back porch in NTSC/PAL systems for chroma demodulation reference.

$$T_{HBI} = T_{FP} + T_{H\text{-}Sync} + T_{BP}$$

## 2.2 Anatomy of the Vertical Blanking Interval (VBI)

The VBI is much longer than the HBI because the beam must travel the entire vertical extent of the raster. It typically spans **9 to 25 horizontal line-periods** depending on standard. Its internal structure is more complex due to interlace requirements:

1. **Pre-Equalizing Pulses (5 pulses):** Short double-frequency pulses to maintain interlace synchronization at the field boundary.
2. **Vertical Sync Pulse Block (5 serrated pulses):** Long pulses with notches (serrations) so that horizontal sync information is preserved even during the vertical sync.
3. **Post-Equalizing Pulses (5 pulses):** Mirror of pre-equalizing pulses to ensure clean field handoff.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Why:** The "equalizing" and "serrated" pulses are NOT legacy relics — they are a *clever engineering trick* allowing the horizontal sync PLL to remain locked even while the vertical sync is being processed. This guarantees **constant horizontal pull-in** throughout the VBI.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> **Critical LaTeX Rule:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol is replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` inside all table cells to prevent markdown table breakage.

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Standard | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| $f_L$ | $\dfrac{1}{T_L}$ | — | Hz | Line (horizontal) frequency |
| $f_F$ | $\dfrac{f_L}{N_{total}/2}$ | — | Hz | Field (vertical) frequency |
| $T_L$ | $\dfrac{1}{f_L}$ | NTSC: 63.556; PAL: 64 | µs | Total line period |
| $T_{HBI}$ | $T_L - T_{active}$ | NTSC: 10.9; PAL: 12.05 | µs | Horizontal blanking interval |
| $T_{VBI}$ | $(N_{total} - N_{active}) \times T_L$ | NTSC: 1334.7; PAL: 1568 | µs | Vertical blanking interval |
| $N_{total}$ | Total lines per frame | NTSC: 525; PAL: 625 | lines | Per complete frame (2 fields) |
| $N_{active}$ | Visually displayed lines | NTSC: 480; PAL: 576 | lines | Excludes VBI lines |
| $BW_{video}$ | $\approx \dfrac{0.7}{T_{active\_line}}$ | NTSC: $\approx 4.2$; PAL: $\approx 5.0$ | MHz | Luminance baseband bandwidth |
| $D_{duty}$ | $\dfrac{N_{active}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | NTSC: 91.4%; PAL: 92.2% | % | Active scan duty cycle |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Broadcast Engineering:** VBI lines 10–21 of NTSC carry **Closed Captioning (CC)** EIA-608 data. VBI line 21 also carries **XDS (Extended Data Services)** for V-Chip ratings.
- **Test & Measurement:** Line 17 and Line 330 of NTSC (and equivalent PAL lines) carry **VITS (Vertical Interval Test Signals)** used by broadcasters to monitor signal quality end-to-end without disrupting picture.
- **Forensic / Industrial:** Timecode, teletext, and ancillary data hidden in VBI are still extracted by professional equipment for archival, sports analysis (frame-accurate triggering), and broadcast automation.
- **Modern Digital TV:** The legacy VBI concept is preserved as the **Vertical Ancillary (VANC) / Horizontal Ancillary (HANC) data space** in SDI (Serial Digital Interface) and SMPTE ST 291 streams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Line Period and Blanking Interval Duration (NTSC M)

The NTSC M system is defined by:
- Color subcarrier $f_{sc} = 3.579545\,\text{MHz}$ (more precisely $\frac{315}{88}$ MHz).
- Frame rate (exact) $f_{frame} = \dfrac{f_{sc}}{2 \times 227.5} = \dfrac{30}{1.001}$ Hz.
- 2 fields per frame → Field rate $f_{field} = 60$ Hz (exact) or $59.94$ Hz (color).
- 525 total lines per frame → 262.5 lines per field.

### Step 1 — Field Period

$$T_{field} = \dfrac{1}{f_{field}} = \dfrac{1}{59.94} \approx 16.6833\,\text{ms}$$

### Step 2 — Line Period (Time for One Full Horizontal Scan Including HBI)

Because there are 262.5 lines in one field:

$$T_L = \dfrac{T_{field}}{262.5} = \dfrac{16.6833\,\text{ms}}{262.5}$$

$$T_L \approx 63.556\,\mu\text{s}$$

### Step 3 — Active Line Duration

Of the 525 lines per frame, only 480 carry visible picture. Therefore active line time:

$$T_{active} = \dfrac{480}{525} \times T_L = 0.9143 \times 63.556\,\mu\text{s}$$

$$T_{active} \approx 58.115\,\mu\text{s}$$

### Step 4 — Horizontal Blanking Interval

$$T_{HBI} = T_L - T_{active} = 63.556 - 58.115$$

$$\boxed{T_{HBI} \approx 10.9\,\mu\text{s} \text{ (NTSC M)}}$$

### Step 5 — Vertical Blanking Interval in Microseconds

The VBI consumes the remaining 45 lines per frame:

$$T_{VBI} = 45 \times T_L = 45 \times 63.556\,\mu\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{T_{VBI} \approx 2860\,\mu\text{s} \approx 2.86\,\text{ms (NTSC M)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: PAL B/G System

PAL B/G uses a simpler integer-locked timing structure:
- Field rate $f_{field} = 50$ Hz (locked to mains).
- Frame rate $f_{frame} = 25$ Hz.
- Total lines per frame $N_{total} = 625$.
- Active lines per frame $N_{active} = 576$.

### Step 1 — Line Period

$$T_L = \dfrac{1}{f_L} = \dfrac{1}{15625} = 64\,\mu\text{s}$$

(where $f_L = 625 \times 25 = 15625$ Hz).

### Step 2 — Active Line Duration

$$T_{active} = \dfrac{576}{625} \times 64\,\mu\text{s} = 0.9216 \times 64\,\mu\text{s}$$

$$T_{active} \approx 58.98\,\mu\text{s}$$

### Step 3 — Horizontal Blanking Interval

$$T_{HBI} = 64 - 58.98$$

$$\boxed{T_{HBI} \approx 5.02\,\mu\text{s} \text{ nominal, often quoted as 12.05 µs for full BLANK including setup region (PAL B/G)}}$$

### Step 4 — Vertical Blanking Interval

$$T_{VBI} = 49 \times 64\,\mu\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{T_{VBI} \approx 3136\,\mu\text{s} \approx 3.14\,\text{ms (PAL B/G)}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | NTSC M | PAL B/G | SECAM | HD 1080i/50 | HD 1080i/60 |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Total lines / frame | **525** | **625** | **625** | 1125 | 1125 |
| Active lines | 480 | 576 | 576 | 1080 | 1080 |
| Field rate (Hz) | 59.94 | 50 | 50 | 50 | 59.94 |
| $T_L$ (µs) | 63.556 | 64.000 | 64.000 | 35.556 | 29.970 |
| $T_{HBI}$ (µs) | 10.9 | 12.05 | 12.05 | 5.33 | 4.50 |
| $T_{VBI}$ (ms) | 1.27 – 2.86 | 3.14 | 3.14 | ~0.6 | ~0.5 |
| HBI sub-pulses | FP+HS+BP+CB | FP+HS+BP+CB | FP+HS+BP | Tri-level sync (SMPTE) | Tri-level sync (SMPTE) |

> **Note on HD:** Modern HDTV uses a **bi-level or tri-level sync** standard (SMPTE 170M / SMPTE 274M), with HANC/VANC digital data packets replacing the legacy analog VBI.

## 3.4 Sub-Component Duration Check (NTSC M)

For verification:

$$T_{FP} \approx 1.5\,\mu\text{s}$$
$$T_{H\text{-}Sync} \approx 4.7\,\mu\text{s}$$
$$T_{BP} \approx 4.7\,\mu\text{s (includes color burst at 0.6 µs)}$$

$$T_{HBI} = 1.5 + 4.7 + 4.7 = 10.9\,\mu\text{s} \;\;\checkmark$$

This matches our derived value, confirming the internal consistency of the SMPTE 170M / RS-170A specification.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Composite Video Line Structure

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the timing structure of **one horizontal line** and the relationship of the **Vertical Blanking Interval** to the active video region. This is a *block-level functional architecture flow* as required for non-physical topics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Line t=0] --> B[Active Video Luma + Chroma 0 to 52.5 us]
    B --> C[Front Porch 0 IRE Black Level 1.5 us]
    C --> D[Horizontal Sync Pulse 40 IRE 4.7 us]
    D --> E[Back Porch 0 IRE Black Level 3.6 us]
    E --> F[Color Burst 8 to 10 cycles of 3.58 MHz subcarrier 1.1 us]
    F --> G[Active Video Region of Next Line]
    G --> H{End of Frame Field?}
    H -- No --> B
    H -- Yes --> I[Vertical Blanking Interval VBI]
    I --> J[Pre-Equalizing Pulses 5 pulses]
    J --> K[Serrated Vertical Sync 5 pulses]
    K --> L[Post-Equalizing Pulses 5 pulses]
    L --> M[Lines Carrying VBI Data Teletext CC VITS]
    M --> N[Start of Next Field Active Video]
    N --> B
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Blanking Intervals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Composite Video Signal] --> Q[Active Picture Region]
    P --> R[Blanking Region]
    R --> S[Horizontal Blanking HBI 10.9 us per line]
    R --> T[Vertical Blanking VBI 21 to 45 lines per frame]
    S --> S1[Front Porch]
    S --> S2[Horizontal Sync]
    S --> S3[Back Porch with Color Burst]
    T --> T1[Equalizing Pulses]
    T --> T2[Serrated V-Sync Pulses]
    T --> T3[Post-Equalizing Pulses]
    T --> T4[VBI Data Lines Captioning Teletext VITS]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Flow: Receiver Synchronization Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Composite Video Input] --> SPL[Sync Separator]
    SPL --> HPLL[Horizontal PLL 15.734 kHz]
    SPL --> VINT[Vertical Integrator Field Detect]
    HPLL --> HDEF[Horizontal Deflection Driver]
    VINT --> VDEF[Vertical Deflection Driver]
    HDEF --> CRTSCAN[CRT Scan or LCD Pixel Clock Recovery]
    VDEF --> CRTSCAN
    IN --> VBIEXT[VBI Data Extractor Line 21 CC Teletext]
    VBIEXT --> DISP[Display Overlay Subtitles etc]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Aid for Students:** The diagram shows that the *same* sync-separator output drives **two independent recovery loops** — a fast horizontal PLL and a slow vertical integrator. The blanking intervals contain the *control energy* (sync tips) needed to keep these loops locked.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define Horizontal Blanking Interval. List its three sub-components and state the typical duration of each for the NTSC M system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Horizontal Blanking Interval (HBI) is the portion of the horizontal scan period during which the electron beam is blanked (suppressed) while it retraces from the end of one line to the start of the next.

| Sub-component | Function | Duration (NTSC M) |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| Front Porch | Black-level settling before sync | **1.5 µs** |
| Horizontal Sync Pulse | PLL reference, beam retrace trigger | **4.7 µs** |
| Back Porch (with color burst) | Black level + chroma reference | **4.7 µs (1.1 µs burst)** |
| **Total HBI** | — | **10.9 µs** |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for table with durations.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the Vertical Blanking Interval? Mention any three services/data carried in the VBI in modern broadcasting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The VBI is the time interval during which the scanning beam retraces from the bottom of the picture to the top. It comprises the lines not used for active picture display (NTSC: 45 lines, PAL: 49 lines).

**Three services carried in the VBI:**
1. **Closed Captioning (EIA-608/708)** — line 21 of NTSC for hearing-impaired viewers.
2. **Teletext** — European data service on multiple VBI lines.
3. **V-Chip / XDS (Extended Data Services)** — parental-rating metadata on line 21.
4. *(Bonus)* VITS (Vertical Interval Test Signals) for broadcast quality monitoring.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing services.)*

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of the composite video signal during one horizontal line, clearly indicating the HBI sub-components and their typical durations. Show the IRE levels for the sync tip, blanking level, and peak white. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the duration of the Horizontal Blanking Interval and the Vertical Blanking Interval for the NTSC M system, given $f_{field} = 59.94$ Hz and total lines per frame = 525 with 480 active lines. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Structure of composite video signal (7 marks)**

The composite video waveform for one horizontal line (total 63.556 µs) contains:

- **Active video region (0 to 52.5 µs):** Contains luminance (0–100 IRE) and modulated chroma on top of the luma. 100 IRE = peak white (0.714 V), 0 IRE = black level (0 V).
- **Front porch (52.5 to 54.0 µs):** Sits at **0 IRE (black)**, allowing the video amplifier to settle before sync.
- **Horizontal sync tip (54.0 to 58.7 µs):** Dips to **−40 IRE (0.286 V below black)**, providing the reference edge for the receiver's horizontal PLL.
- **Back porch (58.7 to 62.4 µs):** Returns to **0 IRE (black)** and contains the **color burst** (8–10 cycles of 3.579545 MHz subcarrier at 0 IRE ± 20 IRE).
- **Active video of next line begins at 63.556 µs.**

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
     100 IRE
       ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
       │       ACTIVE VIDEO LINE         │
 0 IRE │├──┤Front  ├──┤Sync ├──┤Back  ├─── │── Active of next line
       │   │Porch │   │Tip  │  │Porch │    │
 -40IRE│   │      │   │     │  │  CB  │    │
       └───┴──────┴───┴─────┴──┴──────┴────┘
       52.5   54.0    58.7    62.4      63.556  µs
       |1.5 |   4.7  |   3.7  |  1.1  |        (approx)
```

*(3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for IRE levels + 2 marks for durations.)*

**(b) Derivations (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Field period

$$T_{field} = \frac{1}{f_{field}} = \frac{1}{59.94} \approx 16.6833\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 2:** Total lines per field = 525/2 = 262.5 lines. Line period:

$$T_L = \frac{16.6833\,\text{ms}}{262.5} = 63.556\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Active line time (480 of 525 lines carry picture):

$$T_{active} = \frac{480}{525} \times 63.556 = 58.115\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 4:** Horizontal Blanking Interval:

$$T_{HBI} = 63.556 - 58.115 = \boxed{10.9\,\mu\text{s}}$$

**Step 5:** Vertical Blanking Interval (45 unused lines per frame):

$$T_{VBI} = 45 \times 63.556\,\mu\text{s} = \boxed{2.86\,\text{ms}}$$

*(1 mark per step × 5 = 5 marks; 2 marks for final boxed answers with units.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Compare the Horizontal and Vertical Blanking Intervals in terms of purpose, duration, structural complexity, and the data/services they carry in NTSC and PAL systems. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram the function of the sync separator circuit in a television receiver. Show how it extracts horizontal and vertical timing information from the composite video. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Horizontal Blanking (HBI) | Vertical Blanking (VBI) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Purpose** | Allow beam retrace from right to left at end of line | Allow beam retrace from bottom to top at end of field |
| **Duration (NTSC)** | 10.9 µs per line (≈ 171 lines equivalent) | 45 lines per frame = 2.86 ms |
| **Duration (PAL)** | 12.05 µs per line | 49 lines per frame = 3.14 ms |
| **Structural Complexity** | Simple: FP + HS + BP (+ color burst) | Complex: pre-equalizing + serrated V-sync + post-equalizing + data lines |
| **Frequency** | 15,734 Hz (NTSC), 15,625 Hz (PAL) | 59.94 Hz / 50 Hz (mains-locked) |
| **Data Carried** | Color burst (chroma reference) | Teletext, Closed Captions, VITS, V-Chip, XDS, timecode |
| **Sync Type** | Single short pulse | Serrated pulse block + equalizing pulses |

*(7 marks — 1 mark per row with at least 4 rows required for full marks.)*

**(b) Sync Separator Block Diagram (7 marks)**

```
┌────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────┐    ┌────────────────────┐
│  Composite     │───▶│  Sync Separator │───▶│  Horizontal PLL    │──▶ H-Drive (15.7 kHz)
│  Video Input   │    │  (Clamp + Slice │    │  (AFC Loop)        │
│  (1 Vpp)       │    │   at -40 IRE)   │    └────────────────────┘
└────────────────┘    │                 │
                      │                 │    ┌────────────────────┐
                      │                 │───▶│  Vertical          │──▶ V-Drive (59.94 Hz)
                      │                 │    │  Integrator        │
                      │                 │    │  (RC low-pass,     │
                      │                 │    │   threshold detect)│
                      └─────────────────┘    └────────────────────┘
                               │
                               ▼
                       ┌───────────────┐
                       │ VBI Data      │──▶ Teletext/CC decoder
                       │ Line Gate     │
                       └───────────────┘
```

**Working Principle:**
- The sync separator **clamps** the video signal to a fixed DC level using a back-porch clamp.
- A **slicer** circuit then slices off the portion of the waveform that lies **below the blanking level** (i.e., everything below ~−20 IRE is the sync region).
- The extracted composite sync is fed simultaneously to:
  - A **horizontal PLL/AFC** that locks the 15.734 kHz horizontal oscillator using the H-sync edges.
  - A **vertical integrator** (low-pass RC + Schmitt trigger) that integrates the V-sync serrated pulse block to produce a 59.94 Hz vertical trigger.
- A **line gate** selects the VBI lines to extract ancillary data (teletext, captions).

*(3 marks diagram + 2 marks working + 2 marks outputs.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Field vs Frame rate** — A common blunder is using 29.97 Hz instead of 59.94 Hz for $f_{field}$. Remember: *1 frame = 2 fields* in interlaced systems.
> 2. **Mixing up HBI sub-components** — Students frequently invert the order (Back Porch → Sync → Front Porch). Memorize: **F**ront, **S**ync, **B**ack = "FSB" — the sync is *sandwiched* between the porches.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in the final answer for derivations. **Always write µs or ms explicitly** — KTU examiners frequently deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 4. **Wrong total lines per field** — NTSC has 525 lines/frame but **262.5 lines/field**. Using 525 in $T_L$ calculation will halve your line period.
> 5. **Drawing VBI and HBI on the same scale** — HBI is ~11 µs; VBI is ~2.86 ms. They differ by **~260×**. Do not draw them proportionally without labels.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🔹 **HBI = Front Porch + H-Sync + Back Porch** ≈ **10.9 µs (NTSC M)** / **12.05 µs (PAL B)**.
- 🔹 **VBI ≈ 21–45 lines per frame** depending on standard; carries Teletext, CC, VITS, V-Chip data.
- 🔹 **HBI happens 15,734 (NTSC) / 15,625 (PAL) times per second** — once per line.
- 🔹 **VBI happens 59.94 (NTSC) / 50 (PAL) times per second** — once per field.
- 🔹 **Sync tip is at −40 IRE (0 V in NTSC)**, black level is 0 IRE (0.286 V), peak white is 100 IRE (1.0 V).
- 🔹 **Color burst sits on the back porch** — 8–10 cycles of subcarrier for chroma demodulation reference.
- 🔹 **Interlacing mandates equalizing + serrated pulses** in VBI to maintain horizontal sync lock during field handoff.
- 🔹 **Modern HDTV replaces analog sync** with **bi-level/tri-level digital sync** (SMPTE 274M/296M).
- 🔹 **VBI → VANC/HANC** in digital SDI streams; the concept persists in SMPTE ST 291 ancillary data space.
- 🔹 **Key equation:** $T_L = 1/f_L$; $T_{HBI} = T_L - T_{active}$; $T_{VBI} = (N_{total} - N_{active}) \times T_L$.
- 🔹 **Duty cycle of active scan ≈ 91–92%** for both NTSC and PAL.
- 🔹 **Engineering "why" of blanking:** Prevents visible retrace artifacts AND provides a clean time-multiplexed channel for sync and ancillary data.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vertical Color Resolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vertical Color Resolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Vertical Color Resolution** is defined as the maximum number of distinct vertical color transitions (alternations) that a colour television system can faithfully reproduce per picture height. It is a measure of the finest vertical detail that the *chrominance* (colour) channel of a video system can resolve, expressed in **TV lines per picture height (TVL/PH)**.

Unlike vertical *luminance* resolution, which is fundamentally limited by the total number of active scanning lines (480 for NTSC, 576 for PAL), the vertical colour resolution is **further restricted** by:

1. The reduced **chrominance bandwidth** allocated in composite video systems (~1.3 MHz for NTSC, ~1.0 MHz for PAL I-band).
2. The relationship between the **colour subcarrier frequency (f_sc)** and the **horizontal line frequency (f_h)**.
3. The **interlaced scanning** structure, which inherently halves the vertical chroma sampling rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is a direct continuation of Module 3 (Colour Television Systems). Students must be able to derive vertical colour resolution values for both **NTSC** and **PAL** systems and explain *why* colour vertical detail is intrinsically poorer than luminance vertical detail.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Stained Glass Window" View

Imagine a stained glass window. The **black leading (came)** represents the **scanning lines** — these are the structural grid that limits how fine the picture can be. Now imagine the window is backlit by:

- A **bright white floodlight** (luminance) — this reveals *every* tiny glass piece clearly.
- A **soft coloured spotlight** (chrominance) — this is much "grainier" and can only identify large coloured regions, not tiny ones.

The **vertical colour resolution** is like asking: *"How many separate horizontal bands of different colours, stacked vertically, can you still distinguish?"* The answer is always **less** than the number of physical window panes (scanning lines), because the colour spotlight is dimmer and blurrier than the white one.

### Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | NTSC (M) | PAL (B/G) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Colour subcarrier f_sc | **3.579545** | **4.433619** | MHz |
| Line frequency f_h | **15.734** | **15.625** | kHz |
| f_sc / f_h ratio | **227.5** (455/2) | **283.75** (1135/4) | dimensionless |
| Active video lines | 480 | 576 | lines |
| Chroma bandwidth | ~1.3 | ~1.0 | MHz |

> [!NOTE]
> The exact value **f_sc / f_h = 227.5** for NTSC is *not* arbitrary. It is deliberately chosen as a **half-integer** to make the colour subcarrier **invert phase on alternate lines**, achieving *colour subcarrier interlace* — a brilliant trick that improves compatibility with monochrome receivers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Theory — Why Colour Has Lower Vertical Resolution

In a composite colour TV system (NTSC/PAL/SECAM), the **chrominance signal C(t)** modulates a high-frequency subcarrier placed at the upper end of the video spectrum. The luminance signal Y(t) occupies the **full video bandwidth** (up to ~4.2 MHz for NTSC, ~5.0 MHz for PAL). This spectral separation is the root cause of the resolution disparity:

- **Luminance** is wideband ⇒ can resolve small features ⇒ high vertical resolution.
- **Chrominance** is narrowband ⇒ cannot resolve small features ⇒ low vertical resolution.

The **vertical resolution** of any video channel is given by the classical **Kell factor** relationship:

$$N_{vertical} = k \cdot N_{active}$$

where $k$ is the **Kell factor** (typically 0.7 for interlaced systems, accounting for the fact that not all theoretical lines are resolvable). For the *luminance* channel this yields ~480 × 0.7 ≈ 336 TVL/PH in NTSC.

For the *colour* channel, however, the binding constraint is the **ratio of chroma bandwidth to line rate**, which leads to a separate, stricter formula.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Deduction

1. **The colour subcarrier completes (f_sc / f_h) cycles per line.** For NTSC: 3.579545 MHz ÷ 15.734 kHz = 227.5 cycles per line.
2. **Across two consecutive lines (one frame period of line-interlace), the subcarrier completes 2 × (f_sc / f_h) cycles.** For NTSC: 455 cycles.
3. **The maximum vertical frequency the chroma channel can represent is half this count** (Nyquist limit on vertical axis), giving the **theoretical maximum vertical colour resolution**:

$$N_{c(max)} = 2 \cdot \frac{f_{sc}}{f_{h}}$$

4. **However**, because chroma occupies only ~1.3 MHz of the 4.2 MHz luminance bandwidth, the *effective* vertical colour resolution is scaled down by the **bandwidth ratio**:

$$N_{c(eff)} = N_{c(max)} \cdot \frac{BW_{chroma}}{BW_{luma}}$$

5. **Furthermore**, vertical chroma is typically averaged across adjacent lines (vertical low-pass filtering by the eye and the decoder), introducing a **practical derating factor** of ~0.4 to 0.5.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **canonical formulas** examiners expect. Memorize them in the exact form shown.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical NTSC | Typical PAL |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{sc} = \frac{455}{2} \cdot f_{h}$ | NTSC subcarrier–line relation | 3.579545 MHz | — |
| 2 | $f_{sc} = \left(284 - \frac{1}{4}\right) \cdot f_{h}$ | PAL subcarrier–line relation (with 1/4 line offset) | — | 4.433619 MHz |
| 3 | $N_{c(max)} = 2 \cdot \frac{f_{sc}}{f_{h}}$ | Theoretical max vertical colour resolution (TVL/PH) | 455 | 567.5 |
| 4 | $N_{c(eff)} \approx 0.4 \cdot N_{active}$ | Practical effective vertical colour resolution | ~192 | ~230 |
| 5 | $N_{Y(vert)} = k \cdot N_{active}$ | Luminance vertical resolution ($k \approx 0.7$) | ~336 | ~403 |
| 6 | $R = \frac{N_{c(eff)}}{N_{Y(vert)}}$ | Resolution ratio (chroma/luma), a KTU favourite | ~0.57 | ~0.57 |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse $N_{c(max)}$ (a theoretical bound derived from the subcarrier) with $N_{c(eff)}$ (the actual usable resolution in a real picture). Examiners will deduct marks if you quote 455 TVL/PH as the *practical* NTSC vertical colour resolution.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding vertical colour resolution is essential in:

- **MPEG/H.264/H.265 video codec design:** The 4:2:0 chroma subsampling scheme used in digital video (Blu-ray, streaming, broadcast) is a *direct descendant* of this analog limitation — colour is sampled at half the vertical rate of luminance.
- **Camera sensor design:** Single-chip colour cameras use **Bayer patterns** that exploit the fact that human vision tolerates lower chroma spatial resolution.
- **Display calibration:** Understanding the ~2:1 luma-to-chroma bandwidth ratio explains why text remains sharp on TV but coloured edges appear softer.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: NTSC Vertical Colour Resolution (Full Working)

**Given data for NTSC M-system:**

$$f_{h} = 15.73426 \text{ kHz}, \quad f_{sc} = 3.579545 \text{ MHz}, \quad N_{active} = 480$$

**Step 1 — Compute the cycles-per-line ratio:**

$$\frac{f_{sc}}{f_{h}} = \frac{3.579545 \times 10^{6}}{15.73426 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$\frac{f_{sc}}{f_{h}} = 227.5$$

**Conversion logic:** We are converting MHz/kHz to a dimensionless ratio. $1 \text{ MHz} / 1 \text{ kHz} = 1000$, so we divide by 1000 to normalize.

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum vertical colour resolution:**

$$N_{c(max)} = 2 \cdot \frac{f_{sc}}{f_{h}} = 2 \times 227.5$$

$$N_{c(max)} = 455 \text{ TVL/PH}$$

**Conversion logic:** A "TV line" represents one half-cycle of a vertical spatial frequency. The full cycle count over two adjacent lines is $2 \times 227.5 = 455$, hence the count of resolvable TV lines equals this number.

**Step 3 — Compute the effective vertical colour resolution using the bandwidth ratio:**

The chroma bandwidth in NTSC is approximately 1.3 MHz (one half of the 2.6 MHz QAM sidebands), and luminance bandwidth is 4.2 MHz.

$$N_{c(eff)} = N_{c(max)} \cdot \frac{BW_{chroma}}{BW_{luma}}$$

$$N_{c(eff)} = 455 \times \frac{1.3}{4.2}$$

$$N_{c(eff)} = 455 \times 0.3095$$

$$N_{c(eff)} \approx 141 \text{ TVL/PH}$$

**Conversion logic:** This scales the theoretical bound by the fraction of spectrum actually available to chroma, yielding the *true* usable vertical chroma detail.

**Step 4 — Apply the practical Kell-style derating (chroma line interlace averaging):**

$$N_{c(practical)} \approx 0.4 \times N_{active} = 0.4 \times 480 = 192 \text{ TVL/PH}$$

**Conversion logic:** Empirically, the eye and the decoder average chroma over 2–3 lines. Hence a rough but widely-cited rule of thumb gives ~192 TVL/PH for the *practical* NTSC vertical colour resolution.

### 3.2 Derivation: PAL Vertical Colour Resolution (Full Working)

**Given data for PAL B/G system:**

$$f_{h} = 15.625 \text{ kHz}, \quad f_{sc} = 4.433619 \text{ MHz}, \quad N_{active} = 576$$

**Step 1 — Compute the cycles-per-line ratio:**

$$\frac{f_{sc}}{f_{h}} = \frac{4.433619 \times 10^{6}}{15.625 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$\frac{f_{sc}}{f_{h}} = 283.7516 \approx 283.75$$

**Conversion logic:** Same MHz-to-kHz normalization as before. The value 283.75 corresponds to the canonical PAL value of $\left(284 - \frac{1}{4}\right) = 1135/4$.

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum vertical colour resolution:**

$$N_{c(max)} = 2 \times 283.75 = 567.5 \text{ TVL/PH}$$

**Conversion logic:** Same as NTSC. PAL's higher f_sc yields a higher theoretical bound.

**Step 3 — Compute the effective vertical colour resolution:**

PAL chroma bandwidth ≈ 1.0 MHz, luminance bandwidth ≈ 5.0 MHz.

$$N_{c(eff)} = 567.5 \times \frac{1.0}{5.0} = 113.5 \text{ TVL/PH}$$

**Step 4 — Practical value (rule of thumb):**

$$N_{c(practical)} \approx 0.4 \times 576 = 230 \text{ TVL/PH}$$

**Conversion logic:** Despite having more lines than NTSC, PAL's relative chroma bandwidth is similar, so the *practical* vertical colour resolution scales roughly with $N_{active}$.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | NTSC | PAL | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Theoretical max $N_{c(max)}$ | 455 TVL/PH | 567.5 TVL/PH | PAL |
| Practical $N_{c(practical)}$ | ~192 TVL/PH | ~230 TVL/PH | PAL |
| Active lines | 480 | 576 | PAL |
| f_sc / f_h | 227.5 | 283.75 | PAL |
| Chroma bandwidth | 1.3 MHz | 1.0 MHz | NTSC (narrower) |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question)

> **Problem:** A colour TV system has 625 scanning lines, line frequency 15.625 kHz, and colour subcarrier 4.433619 MHz. Calculate (a) the maximum vertical colour resolution, and (b) the practical effective vertical colour resolution if the chroma bandwidth is 1 MHz and luminance bandwidth is 5 MHz.

**Solution (a):**

$$\frac{f_{sc}}{f_{h}} = \frac{4.433619 \times 10^{6}}{15.625 \times 10^{3}} = 283.75$$

$$N_{c(max)} = 2 \times 283.75 = 567.5 \text{ TVL/PH}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$N_{c(eff)} = 567.5 \times \frac{1}{5} = 113.5 \text{ TVL/PH}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map — Factors Governing Vertical Colour Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> C[2. Chroma Bandwidth]
    A --> D[3. Interlaced Scanning]
    A --> E[4. Kell Derating Factor]

    B --> B1["f_sc / f_h ratio\n(NTSC: 227.5, PAL: 283.75)"]
    B1 --> F["N_c(max) = 2 * f_sc / f_h\nNTSC: 455  |  PAL: 567.5 TVL/PH"]

    C --> C1["BW_chroma / BW_luma\n(NTSC: 0.31, PAL: 0.20)"]
    C1 --> G["N_c(eff) = N_c(max) * BW_ratio\nNTSC: ~141  |  PAL: ~113 TVL/PH"]

    D --> D1["Odd half-integer offset\ncauses phase inversion\nper line"]
    D1 --> H["Color subcarrier interlace\nreduces visible dot pattern"]

    E --> E1["Practical 0.4 factor\nNTSC: ~192  |  PAL: ~230 TVL/PH"]

    F --> I[Final Usable Resolution]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    E1 --> I
```

    end

    L1 --> L2[Chrominance Vertical Resolution\nNTSC: ~192 TVL/PH\nPAL: ~230 TVL/PH\npractical 0.4 derating]
