The MPEG-4 Part 2 encoder pipeline is built around the concept of **Video Object Planes (VOPs)**. A *video object* is an instance of a video clip in time, and a *VOP* is a snapshot of that object at a particular instant. The architecture consists of the following sub-modules:

### Step-by-Step Functional Flow:

1. **Video Segmentation:** The input frame is segmented into meaningful objects (foreground vs. background). This step is largely **semi-automatic** in practice and is the most difficult component.
2. **Shape Coding:** The binary or grey-scale alpha mask of each VOP is encoded. This is unique to MPEG-4.
3. **Motion Estimation & Compensation (ME/MC):** Block-based motion vectors (MVs) are estimated between consecutive VOPs.
4. **Texture Coding:** The luminance (Y) and chrominance (Cb, Cr) pixel values of the VOP are encoded using **8×8 DCT**, quantization, and VLC (Variable Length Coding).
5. **Sprite Coding:** A long-term static background (e.g., a panorama) can be encoded once as a "sprite" and re-used.
6. **Multiplexing & Packaging:** All coded VOPs are packetized into a **flexible bitstream syntax** along with timing, scene description, and Object Descriptors (ODs).

> [!NOTE]
> **VOP Types in MPEG-4 (memorize this!):**
> - **I-VOP:** Intra-coded VOP (independent, like MPEG-2 I-frame)
> - **P-VOP:** Predictive VOP (uses one reference, like MPEG-2 P-frame)
> - **B-VOP:** Bidirectionally predictive VOP (uses two references, like MPEG-2 B-frame)
> - **S-VOP:** Sprite VOP (encoded against a static global sprite)
> - **S(GMC)-VOP:** Sprite VOP with Global Motion Compensation

## 2.2 Bitstream Syntax Hierarchy (MPEG-4 Visual)

The MPEG-4 bitstream follows a strict **hierarchical structure** (top-down):

```
Visual Object Sequence (VS)
   └── Visual Object (VO)         ← contains audio + video
        └── Video Object Layer (VOL)   ← scalability layer
             └── Group of Video Object Planes (GOV)
                  └── Video Object Plane (VOP)
                       ├── Shape coding
                       ├── Motion vectors
                       └── Texture (DCT coefficients)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VOL (Video Object Layer)** is the critical scalability enabler. Multiple VOLs can exist for the same VO, each offering different spatial resolution, temporal resolution (frame rate), or SNR quality. The decoder can switch between them — this is **scalable coding**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Quantity | Equation | Units / Notes |
|--------------------|----------|---------------|
| Compression Ratio (CR) | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Uncompressed size}}{\text{Compressed size}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Uncompressed Frame Size | $S_{raw} = W \times H \times 3 \text{ bytes (Y Cb Cr 4:2:0)}$ | Bytes |
| Bit Rate | $R = f \times \dfrac{S_{raw}}{CR} \times 8$ | bits per second (bps) |
| PSNR (Luminance) | $PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255^{2}}{MSE}\right)$ | dB |
| Mean Squared Error | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{W \cdot H} \sum_{i=1}^{W}\sum_{j=1}^{H}\!\left[I(i,j) - \hat{I}(i,j)\right]^{2}$ | Squared intensity units |
| DCT Forward (8×8) | $F(u,v) = \dfrac{C(u)\,C(v)}{4}\sum_{x=0}^{7}\sum_{y=0}^{7} f(x,y)\cos\!\left[\dfrac{(2x+1)u\pi}{16}\right]\cos\!\left[\dfrac{(2y+1)v\pi}{16}\right]$ | $C(k) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ if $k=0$, else 1 |
| Quantization | $F_{q}(u,v) = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{F(u,v)}{Q(u,v)}\right)$ | $Q$ is the quantizer step |
| Motion Vector Sum | $MV_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{blocks}} \vert MV_{x_i} \vert + \vert MV_{y_i} \vert$ | Pixels |
| Block Distortion (SAD) | $SAD = \sum_{x,y} \vert f_t(x,y) - f_{t-1}(x+dx, y+dy) \vert$ | Intensity sum |
| Shape Coding Bits | $B_{shape} = b_{CAE} \times N_{BABs}$ | $b_{CAE}$ = bits per BAB, $N_{BABs}$ = Binary Alpha Blocks |
| SNR Scalability Layers | $R_{total} = R_{base} + \sum_{k=1}^{L-1} R_{enhancement,k}$ | $L$ = total layers |
| Temporal Scalability | $f_{total} = f_{base} + \sum_{k} f_{enhancement,k}$ | Hz |
| Storage per minute | $T_{MB} = \dfrac{R \times 60}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024}$ | MB/minute |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Note for Tables:** In the markdown table above, all absolute-value and division notations use $\vert$ and $\dfrac$ to avoid breaking the table syntax with raw `|` characters. Always replicate this in your own answer scripts.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

MPEG-4 is the **workhorse codec of the streaming era**:

- **H.264/AVC (Part 10):** Powers YouTube, Netflix SD/HD, Blu-ray, FaceTime, iTunes Store, video conferencing, drone video, dashcams, CCTV.
- **HEVC/H.265 (Part 15):** Used for 4K UHD Blu-ray, Netflix 4K HDR, Apple ProRes workflows, modern streaming.
- **Part 2 (Simple Profile):** Found in legacy 3GP mobile video, low-bit-rate surveillance.
- **Object-based features:** Underpin **MPEG-4 BIFS (Binary Format for Scenes)**, used in interactive ads, AR/VR, and digital signage.
- **MP4 container (Part 14):** The most widely used multimedia container on the planet.

In the **KTU 2024 scheme** context, understanding the **block diagram of an MPEG-4 encoder** and the **bitstream hierarchy** carries the highest mark weightage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bit Rate from Compression Ratio

**Given:** A CIF video sequence (352 × 288), 4:2:0 chroma subsampling, 30 fps, target compression ratio CR = 50.

**Step 1:** Compute the raw (uncompressed) size of a single frame.
For 4:2:0 subsampling, each pixel corresponds to **1 Y + 0.5 Cb + 0.5 Cr = 1.5 bytes** (or equivalently, **12 bits**).

$$S_{raw} = 352 \times 288 \times 1.5 \text{ bytes}$$

Evaluating:

$$S_{raw} = 101376 \times 1.5 = 152064 \text{ bytes/frame}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the raw bit rate (uncompressed).

$$R_{raw} = S_{raw} \times f \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}$$

$$R_{raw} = 152064 \times 30 \times 8 = 36495360 \text{ bps} \approx 36.5 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the compression ratio to obtain the compressed bit rate.

$$R = \dfrac{R_{raw}}{CR} = \dfrac{36.5 \text{ Mbps}}{50} = 0.73 \text{ Mbps} = 730 \text{ kbps}$$

**Interpretation:** This is precisely the order of magnitude used for **MPEG-4 Part 2 Simple Profile CIF streaming** at good VCD-replacement quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid:** For 4:2:0 video, $S_{raw} = 1.5 \times W \times H$ bytes. For 4:2:2 it is $2 \times W \times H$. For 4:4:4 it is $3 \times W \times H$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PSNR Computation

**Given:** An original 8-bit luminance frame of size 256×256, and a reconstructed frame with MSE = 78.5 (computed pixel-wise).

**Step 1:** The peak signal value for 8-bit representation is:

$$MAX_{I} = 2^{8} - 1 = 255$$

**Step 2:** Apply the PSNR formula.

$$PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{MAX_{I}^{2}}{MSE}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255^{2}}{78.5}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Numerator evaluation.

$$255^{2} = 65025$$

**Step 4:** Ratio evaluation.

$$\dfrac{65025}{78.5} \approx 828.34$$

**Step 5:** Apply $\log_{10}$ and multiply by 10.

$$10 \times \log_{10}(828.34) = 10 \times 2.9184 = 29.18 \text{ dB}$$

**Conclusion:** $PSNR \approx 29.18$ dB — just at the threshold of perceptually acceptable quality (>30 dB is good, >40 dB is excellent).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — DCT Coefficient Calculation (Single 8×8 Block)

**Given:** A constant grey block where $f(x,y) = 128$ for all $x, y \in \{0,1,\ldots,7\}$.

**Step 1:** The DCT formula at position $(u,v)$ is:

$$F(u,v) = \dfrac{C(u)\,C(v)}{4}\sum_{x=0}^{7}\sum_{y=0}^{7} f(x,y)\cos\!\left[\dfrac{(2x+1)u\pi}{16}\right]\cos\!\left[\dfrac{(2y+1)v\pi}{16}\right]$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the DC coefficient $F(0,0)$. Here $C(0) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

$$F(0,0) = \dfrac{(1/\sqrt{2})(1/\sqrt{2})}{4} \sum_{x=0}^{7}\sum_{y=0}^{7} 128 \cdot 1 \cdot 1$$

$$F(0,0) = \dfrac{1/2}{4} \times 128 \times 64 = \dfrac{1}{8} \times 8192 = 1024$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate any AC coefficient, say $F(1,0)$. Here $C(1) = 1$ and $C(0) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

$$F(1,0) = \dfrac{1 \cdot (1/\sqrt{2})}{4} \sum_{x=0}^{7}\sum_{y=0}^{7} 128 \cdot \cos\!\left[\dfrac{(2x+1)\pi}{16}\right] \cdot 1$$

The inner sum $\sum_{x=0}^{7} \cos\!\left[\tfrac{(2x+1)\pi}{16}\right] = 0$ (orthogonality of cosine basis).

$$F(1,0) = 0$$

**Step 4:** The result is a single DC value of 1024 and all 63 AC values equal to 0. After quantization (with step Q = 8), only one non-zero coefficient remains at $F_q(0,0) = 128$. This explains why **flat regions compress extremely well** — the entire 8×8 block is encoded as a single small number.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Shape Coding (CAE – Context-based Arithmetic Encoding)

**Given:** A Binary Alpha Block (BAB) of size 16×16 with the following properties:
- Number of opaque pixels: $N_{opaque} = 96$
- Total pixels: $N_{total} = 256$
- Average BAB size in the VOP: $\overline{N}_{BAB} = 5$ bits
- Total BABs in the VOP: $N_{BABs} = 80$

**Step 1:** Compute the shape complexity ratio.

$$\rho = \dfrac{N_{opaque}}{N_{total}} = \dfrac{96}{256} = 0.375$$

**Step 2:** Compute the total shape bits.

$$B_{shape} = \overline{N}_{BAB} \times N_{BABs} = 5 \times 80 = 400 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3:** Add BAB header overhead (8 bits per BAB for mode/position).

$$B_{total} = B_{shape} + 8 \times N_{BABs} = 400 + 640 = 1040 \text{ bits} \approx 130 \text{ bytes}$$

**Conclusion:** The shape information for a moderately complex VOP costs only ~130 bytes — a tiny fraction of a typical texture-encoded frame. This is why **CAE was chosen** in MPEG-4: it exploits the spatial redundancy within and between alpha blocks.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Key Trade-offs in MPEG-4 Coding

| Parameter | Increase Effect on Quality | Increase Effect on Bit Rate | Increase Effect on Complexity |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Quantizer step $Q$ | ↓ (more loss) | ↓ (lower rate) | = |
| Frame rate $f$ | ↑ (smoother motion) | ↑ (proportional) | ↑ (more ME work) |
| GOP length | Variable | ↓ (more I-frames compress less) | ↓ (less temporal redundancy exploited) |
| Search range for ME | ↑ (better MV accuracy) | ↓ (slightly better residuals) | ↑↑ (exponential cost) |
| Sprite size | ↑ (better long-term coding) | ↓ (saves repeated background) | ↑ (large memory) |
| Number of VOLs | ↑ (more scalable) | ↑ (overhead) | ↑ (decoder complexity) |
| B-VOP usage | ↑ (best quality for given rate) | ↓ (lowest rate for given quality) | ↑ (two references needed) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MPEG-4 Part 2 — Object-Based Encoder Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Video Frame] --> B[Video Segmentation\nForeground vs Background]
    B --> C1[Shape Coding Module\nCAE BABs]
    B --> C2[Motion Estimation\n16x16 and 8x8 blocks]
    B --> C3[Texture Coding\n8x8 DCT + Quant + VLC]
    C1 --> D[Multiplexer and Bitstream Packager]
    C2 --> E[Motion Compensation]
    C3 --> F[Inverse Quant and IDCT]
    F --> G[Reference VOP Store]
    G --> E
    E --> C3
    D --> H[Encoded MPEG-4 Bitstream]
    D --> I[Object Descriptors and BIFS Scene Graph]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The right-hand loop (Inverse Quant → IDCT → Reference Store → Motion Compensation → Texture Coding) is the **decoder-in-the-encoder loop** that ensures the encoder and decoder reference the same reconstructed VOPs — preventing drift.

## 4.2 MPEG-4 Bitstream Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Visual Object Sequence] --> S2[Visual Object VO]
    S2 --> S3[Video Object Layer VOL]
    S3 --> S4[Group of VOPs GOV]
    S4 --> S5[Video Object Plane VOP]
    S5 --> S6a[Shape Information]
    S5 --> S6b[Motion Vectors]
    S5 --> S6c[Texture DCT Coefficients]
    S5 --> S6d[Stuffing and Resync Markers]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The `GOV` (Group of VOPs) layer is where **video packets** start in MPEG-4. Each GOV is independently decodable, providing the basis for **random access** and **error resilience** — a feature absent in MPEG-1.

## 4.3 VOP Type Dependency Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IVOP[IVOP Intra] --> PVOP1[PVOP]
    IVOP --> PVOP2[PVOP]
    PVOP1 --> BVOP[BVOP]
    PVOP2 --> BVOP
    PVOP1 --> PVOP3[PVOP]
    PVOP2 --> PVOP3
    BVOP --> PVOP3
    PVOP3 --> IVOP2[IVOP]
```

This shows that **B-VOPs require two surrounding anchor VOPs** (I or P), forming the classic **IPPP…IPP…B…B** display-order vs. coding-order relationship that students often confuse.

## 4.4 MPEG-4 Scalability Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Base[Base Layer VOL\nLow Resolution Low Frame Rate] --> En1
    En1[Enhancement Layer 1\nSpatial upsample]
    Base --> En2
    En2[Enhancement Layer 2\nTemporal add frames]
    Base --> En3
    En3[Enhancement Layer 3\nSNR Finer Quantization]
    En1 --> Final[Decoded High Quality Stream]
    En2 --> Final
    En3 --> Final
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** A single MPEG-4 bitstream with multiple VOLs can be received by **low-power mobile decoders** (base layer only) and **high-end HDTVs** (all layers), all from the *same transmission*. This is the foundation of modern **HTTP Adaptive Bitrate Streaming (HLS, DASH)** even though they use H.264/HEVC today.

## 4.5 MPEG-4 Family Evolution (Timeline View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[MPEG-1\n1993\nVCD] --> A2[MPEG-2\n1995\nDVD DVB]
    A2 --> A3[MPEG-4 Visual\nPart 2\n1999]
    A2 --> A4[MPEG-4 AVC\nPart 10 H.264\n2003]
    A3 --> A5[MPEG-4 HEVC\nPart 15 H.265\n2013]
    A4 --> A6[VVC H.266\n2020]
    A5 --> A6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between MPEG-2 and MPEG-4 video coding in terms of structure and features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MPEG-2 | MPEG-4 |
|--------|--------|--------|
| Fundamental unit | Frame (rectangular) | Video Object (arbitrary shape) |
| Standard target | SDTV/DVD | Streaming, mobile, interactive |
| Object-based coding | Not supported | Native (shape + texture + motion per object) |
| Scalability | Limited (SNR, spatial, temporal — Part 2 of MPEG-2) | Native (multi-VOL per VO) |
| Bit rate | 4–15 Mbps typical | 64 kbps – 40 Mbps adaptive |
| Error resilience | Limited | Resync markers, data partitioning, RVLC |
| Interactivity | Passive | Scene graph (BIFS) allows user interaction |

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a VOP? List the different VOP types used in MPEG-4.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Video Object Plane (VOP)** is a snapshot of a video object at a given instant $t$. It encapsulates shape, motion, and texture information for that object.

**Types of VOPs:**
1. **I-VOP** — Intra-coded; uses only intra-coding, no references.
2. **P-VOP** — Predictive; uses one past I- or P-VOP for ME/MC.
3. **B-VOP** — Bidirectional; uses both past and future references.
4. **S-VOP** — Sprite VOP; predicted from a static sprite (e.g., background).
5. **S(GMC)-VOP** — Sprite VOP using Global Motion Compensation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an MPEG-4 Part 2 video encoder. Discuss the role of **shape coding** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **VOPs** and the **bitstream syntax hierarchy** from Visual Object Sequence down to the VOP level. How does GOV enable random access? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) MPEG-4 Part 2 Encoder Architecture — 7 marks**

The MPEG-4 encoder consists of the following sub-modules (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Input & Segmentation** (1 mark): The input frame is segmented into multiple Video Objects. The simplest case is two objects: a foreground VOP and a background VOP. Real-world systems often use chroma-keying or motion-based segmentation.

2. **Shape Coding Module** (2 marks): The binary (or grey-scale) alpha mask of each VOP is encoded using **CAE (Context-based Arithmetic Encoding)**. The bounding box of the VOP is divided into 16×16 Binary Alpha Blocks (BABs). Each BAB is classified into one of three modes:
   - All-opaque → no shape bits needed
   - All-transparent → no shape bits needed
   - Mixed → CAE mode, context-based arithmetic coding of the boundary pixels using 10 context templates.
   This produces the **shape bitstream** (separate from texture).

3. **Motion Estimation and Compensation** (1 mark): Standard 16×16 and 8×8 block-based motion estimation (full-search or fast algorithm). Motion vectors are encoded differentially.

4. **Texture Coding** (1 mark): 8×8 DCT, quantization, run-level VLC. The reconstruction loop (inverse quant + IDCT) maintains the reference VOP store.

5. **Multiplexer & Object Descriptors** (1 mark): Combines shape, motion, and texture streams into a single flexible bitstream with header information and timing.

6. **Sprite coder** (1 mark): Special path for static-panorama backgrounds; uses global motion compensation (GMC) with 4 or 8 warping parameters.

**Role of Shape Coding (key innovation):**
- Enables **object-based representation** — a VOP is not restricted to a rectangle.
- Allows **content-based interaction** (select, scale, or replace a single object).
- Achieves high compression by not encoding transparent regions of the alpha mask.
- Compressed shape bits (BAB) become the **"mask" for the texture coder** so that DCT is only applied to opaque pixels.

**(b) VOPs and Bitstream Syntax Hierarchy — 7 marks**

A **VOP (Video Object Plane)** is a 2-D snapshot of a Video Object (VO) at a particular time instant. Each VOP is independently decodable and has three components: **shape, motion, texture**.

The **bitstream hierarchy** (top-down) is (refer to Section 4.2):

1. **Visual Object Sequence (VS)** (0.5 marks): Top-level container.
2. **Visual Object (VO)** (0.5 marks): Combines one or more layers (e.g., natural video + synthetic objects).
3. **Video Object Layer (VOL)** (1 mark): Represents a single scalability layer (spatial / temporal / SNR / object).
4. **Group of Video Object Planes (GOV)** (1 mark): A group of consecutive VOPs forming an independently decodable chunk.
5. **Video Object Plane (VOP)** (1 mark): The actual coded picture.

**How GOV enables random access (2 marks):**
- The GOV header contains a **time code** and a flag indicating the **closed-GOV** boundary.
- A decoder can jump to the **start of any GOV** and resume playback because GOV is independently decodable (anchored by an I-VOP or resync marker).
- This is critical for **video editing, trick-play (fast forward / rewind), and error recovery** on lossy networks.
- MPEG-2 required I-frame access only; MPEG-4's GOV concept allows finer-grained random access at the **object** level, not just the frame level.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the various **scalability modes** supported by MPEG-4. Why is scalability important for streaming and mobile applications? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **MPEG-4 Part 2 (Visual)**, **MPEG-4 Part 10 (H.264/AVC)**, and **MPEG-4 Part 15 (H.265/HEVC)** in terms of compression efficiency, computational complexity, and typical use cases. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) MPEG-4 Scalability Modes — 7 marks**

MPEG-4 supports **four primary scalability modes** (each implemented as separate VOLs):

1. **Spatial Scalability** (2 marks): Base layer is at low resolution; enhancement layer carries the difference needed to reconstruct a higher-resolution version (e.g., QCIF → CIF → 4CIF). The enhancement VOP is upsampled and added to the base VOP after motion compensation.
2. **Temporal Scalability** (2 marks): Base layer contains frames at a lower temporal rate (e.g., 15 fps); enhancement layer adds the *intermediate* frames to reach a higher frame rate (e.g., 30 fps). Often used with B-VOPs in the enhancement layer.
3. **SNR / Quality Scalability** (2 marks): Base layer uses coarse quantization; enhancement layer carries the **residual quantization error** (finer quantized DCT coefficients). Same spatial and temporal resolution in both layers; only quality improves.
4. **Object Scalability / Fine-Granular Scalability (FGS)** (1 mark): Some MPEG-4 profiles (Streaming) support bitstream truncation — the enhancement bitstream can be cut at any point to match available bandwidth, trading quality smoothly.

**Why scalability matters (1 mark):**
- **Heterogeneous receivers:** A single transmission can serve a feature phone (low bandwidth, low CPU) and a smart TV (high bandwidth, high CPU) without re-encoding.
- **Adaptive streaming:** The server can throttle transmission based on real-time network conditions.
- **Mobile/wireless channels:** Bandwidth fluctuates; scalability enables graceful degradation.
- **Storage and archival:** A single master file supports multiple consumption profiles.

**(b) Comparison of MPEG-4 Visual, AVC, and HEVC — 7 marks**

| Feature | MPEG-4 Part 2 (Visual) | H.264 / AVC (Part 10) | H.265 / HEVC (Part 15) |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Year of standardization | 1999 | 2003 | 2013 |
| Block size | Fixed 8×8 | 4×4 to 16×16 (macroblock) | 4×4 to 64×64 (CTU) |
| Intra prediction modes | Limited (DC, AC) | 9 modes (4×4) + 4 (16×16) | 35 angular + DC + Planar |
| Motion compensation | Block-based, 16×16 | Variable block sizes, multiple refs | Large CTU, merge mode, AMVP, affine |
| In-loop filter | None | Deblocking filter | Deblocking + SAO |
| Compression efficiency | 1× (baseline) | ~2× vs Part 2 at same quality | ~2× vs H.264 at same quality |
| Computational complexity | Low (~30 MOPS decode) | Medium (~100 MOPS decode) | High (~500–1000 MOPS decode) |
| Entropy coding | VLC | CAVLC + CABAC | CABAC only |
| Typical use cases | Legacy 3GP mobile, surveillance | Blu-ray, YouTube, streaming, video calls | 4K UHD Blu-ray, Netflix 4K, modern streaming, AR/VR |
| Object-based coding | Yes (native) | No (block-based) | No (CTU-based) |
| Bit rate for SD (≈ DVD quality) | 1.5–2 Mbps | 0.7–1 Mbps | 0.4–0.6 Mbps |

**Summary (1 mark):** H.264/AVC and H.265/HEVC sacrifice the object-based paradigm of MPEG-4 Part 2 in favor of more sophisticated **block/transform prediction**, delivering roughly 2× compression efficiency per generation. The object-based features of Part 2 are still used in niche interactive multimedia (BIFS, DIMS) and surveillance, while AVC/HEVC dominate consumer streaming.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write "MPEG-4 is the same as H.264" — they are different parts of the same family. This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Do NOT** omit the *shape coding* sub-module when drawing the MPEG-4 encoder. It is the **signature feature** of Part 2 and examiners explicitly look for it.
> 3. **Do NOT** confuse the acronym *GOV* (Group of VOPs) with *GOP* (Group of Pictures in MPEG-2). They are related concepts but GOV operates at the *object* level.
> 4. For **PSNR problems**, always state the assumption: 8-bit pixel depth → $MAX_I = 255$. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
> 5. For **DCT problems**, remember the orthonormal basis coefficient $C(k) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ for $k=0$ and $C(k) = 1$ otherwise. Skipping this loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 6. When drawing the encoder block diagram, **always include the feedback loop** (Inverse Quant → IDCT → Reference Store → MC). Forgetting it costs 1 mark.
> 7. In scalability questions, students often describe only *one* scalability mode. Aim to mention **all four** (spatial, temporal, SNR, object/FGS) for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam):**

- **MPEG-4 = "Coding of Audio-Visual Objects"** (ISO/IEC 14496). Object-based, not frame-based.
- **Core unit:** Video Object Plane (**VOP**) = shape + motion + texture.
- **Five VOP types:** I, P, B, S (Sprite), S-GMC (Sprite with Global Motion Compensation).
- **Shape coding** uses **CAE (Context-based Arithmetic Encoding)** on 16×16 Binary Alpha Blocks (BABs).
- **Texture coding** uses **8×8 DCT**, scalar quantization, and VLC.
- **Motion compensation** uses **16×16** blocks (and 8×8 refinement) with integer-pixel MVs.
- **Bitstream hierarchy:** VS → VO → VOL → GOV → VOP.
- **VOL** = scalability layer. **GOV** = random-access unit.
- **Four scalability modes:** Spatial, Temporal, SNR, Object/FGS.
- **H.264 = MPEG-4 Part 10**; **H.265 = MPEG-4 Part 15**. Both are *block-based*, not object-based.
- **Error resilience tools:** Resync markers, data partitioning, reversible VLC (RVLC), NEWPRED.
- **Sprite coding** is unique to MPEG-4 Part 2 — encode a static background once, reuse forever.
- **Key formulas:** $CR = S_{raw}/S_{comp}$, $R = f \cdot S_{raw} \cdot 8 / CR$, $PSNR = 10 \log_{10}(255^2/MSE)$.
- **For 4:2:0 video:** $S_{raw} = 1.5 \cdot W \cdot H$ bytes per frame.
- **MPEG-4 = 1×**, **H.264 = 2×**, **H.265 = 4×** the compression of MPEG-2 for equivalent quality.
- **PSNR thresholds:** < 20 dB poor, 20–30 dB acceptable, 30–40 dB good, > 40 dB excellent (perceptually lossless).
- **Container:** Part 14 = **MP4** format (`.mp4`), the most widely used multimedia file format.
- **MPEG-4 powers:** YouTube, Netflix, video calls, mobile video, CCTV, Blu-ray (H.264/HEVC), AR/VR.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram of Video Reproduction System in a TV
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Block Diagram of Video Reproduction System in a TV

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Video Reproduction System** in a television receiver is the complete electronic signal-processing chain that recovers the original picture information from a received RF (Radio Frequency) television signal and converts it into a visible image on the display device (CRT, LCD, LED, or OLED panel). It encompasses the RF tuner, IF (Intermediate Frequency) stages, video detector, video amplifiers, synchronizing (sync) circuits, deflection systems, and the display device itself.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is often referred to as the **Composite Video Signal Recovery and Reproduction Subsystem** of a TV receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board Exam Standard):**
> "A video reproduction system in a TV is the receiver-side circuitry that takes the modulated RF television signal from the antenna, demodulates it to retrieve the baseband video signal, separates the synchronizing information, deflects the electron beam in a raster pattern, and modulates the beam intensity to reconstruct the transmitted picture on the screen."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a TV's video reproduction system like a **"Mail Delivery and Reading Assistant"**:

1. **The Mailroom (Tuner + IF Amplifier)**: Just as a mailroom receives hundreds of letters (RF signals from all channels) and picks out only the one addressed to you (your desired channel) and amplifies it for clarity, the tuner selects the correct channel and the IF amplifier boosts the signal to a usable level.

2. **The Translator (Video Detector/Demodulator)**: The translator takes the "coded letter" (modulated carrier wave containing the picture) and decodes it back into a "readable language" (the baseband video signal).

3. **The Sorting Clerk (Sync Separator)**: Imagine a letter containing a story — the clerk separates the "instructions on how to read it" (sync pulses) from the "actual story content" (video/luminance information).

4. **The Pen and Paper (Deflection + Display)**: The deflection circuits act like a hand moving a pen across a blank paper in a precise pattern (left to right, top to bottom), while the video signal tells the pen exactly how dark or light to make each dot — together recreating the original picture.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Standard Values for Indian/PAL Television Systems (B/G standard used in India):**
> - **RF Channel Bandwidth:** **7 MHz** (Vision carrier + Sound carrier within VHF/UHF band)
> - **Vision IF Carrier Frequency:** **38.9 MHz**
> - **Sound IF Carrier Frequency:** **33.4 MHz** (Inter-carrier spacing of 5.5 MHz)
> - **Video Baseband Bandwidth:** **5 MHz** (approx 5.5 MHz including sound)
> - **Total Number of Lines per Frame:** **625 lines**
> - **Field Frequency:** **50 Hz** (interlaced, 2 fields per frame)
> - **Frame Frequency:** **25 Hz**
> - **Line (Horizontal) Scanning Frequency:** **15,625 Hz** = 625 × 25
> - **Aspect Ratio:** **4:3** (traditional) or **16:9** (widescreen HD)
> - **Interlacing Ratio:** **2:1**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-by-Block Functional Analysis

The video reproduction system in a monochrome or color TV receiver is divided into the following major functional blocks. Each block performs a specific signal-processing task that, when cascaded, recreates the transmitted image.

#### Block 1: RF Tuner (VHF/UHF Tuner)
- **Function:** Selects the desired RF television channel from the antenna signal and converts it to a fixed Intermediate Frequency (IF).
- **Key Concepts:**
  - Uses a **varactor-tuned** or mechanical tuner with local oscillator.
  - Heterodyning principle: $f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_{RF}$ (for high-side injection)
  - Provides RF amplification (low-noise) before mixing.
- **Why it matters:** The tuner determines the sensitivity and selectivity of the entire receiver.

#### Block 2: IF Amplifier (Intermediate Frequency Amplifier)
- **Function:** Provides the bulk of the receiver's gain and selectivity at a fixed frequency (38.9 MHz for vision, 33.4 MHz for sound in PAL B/G).
- **Key Concepts:**
  - Typically **3 to 4 stages** of amplification.
  - Uses **SAW (Surface Acoustic Wave) filter** in modern TVs for precise bandpass shaping.
  - Adjacent channel rejection (>30 dB) is achieved here.
- **Why it matters:** AGC (Automatic Gain Control) voltage is derived from IF stages to maintain constant video output.

#### Block 3: Video Detector (Demodulator)
- **Function:** Demodulates the AM-modulated vision IF carrier to recover the baseband composite video signal (CVBS).
- **Key Concepts:**
  - Uses a **diode envelope detector** or **synchronous detector** in modern designs.
  - Produces the composite video signal containing: luminance (Y), chrominance (C), blanking pulses, and sync pulses.
  - Also produces the inter-carrier beat (5.5 MHz) containing the FM sound signal.

#### Block 4: Video Amplifier
- **Function:** Amplifies the detected video signal to a level suitable for driving the display device (typically 1–5 V peak-to-peak).
- **Key Concepts:**
  - DC restoration (clamping) to restore true black level.
  - Contrast and brightness controls are applied here.
  - In color TV, separate Y, U, V paths are processed.

#### Block 5: Sync Separator
- **Function:** Separates the **horizontal sync pulses** (line sync) and **vertical sync pulses** (field sync) from the composite video signal.
- **Key Concepts:**
  - Uses **amplitude clipping** (sync tips are at 75–100% of peak carrier, beyond black level).
  - Differentiator → horizontal sync (15,625 Hz).
  - Integrator → vertical sync (50 Hz).

#### Block 6: Horizontal Deflection System
- **Function:** Generates the **15,625 Hz sawtooth current** through the horizontal deflection yoke coils to sweep the electron beam from left to right.
- **Key Concepts:**
  - Includes horizontal oscillator, AFC (Automatic Frequency Control), driver, and output stage.
  - Produces the **EHT (Extra High Tension) voltage (~25 kV for CRT)** via flyback transformer.

#### Block 7: Vertical Deflection System
- **Function:** Generates the **50 Hz sawtooth current** through the vertical deflection yoke coils to sweep the beam from top to bottom.
- **Key Concepts:**
  - Uses an oscillator (or divider in digital TVs) synchronized to vertical sync.
  - Vertical output stage drives the yoke with high current at low frequency.

#### Block 8: Display Device (CRT/Flat Panel)
- **Function:** Converts the electrical video signal into a visible image.
- **Key Concepts:**
  - **CRT:** Electron beam intensity modulated by video signal; position controlled by yoke.
  - **LCD/LED/OLED:** Matrix addressing with row/column drivers; backlight (LED) or self-emissive pixels.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Board Exam Success**

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Relation** | **Standard Value (PAL B/G)** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Line (Horizontal) Frequency $f_H$ | $f_H = N \times f_{frame}$ | $625 \times 25 = 15{,}625$ | Hz |
| Frame Frequency $f_{frame}$ | $f_{frame} = f_{field} / 2$ | $50 / 2 = 25$ | Hz |
| Line Period $T_H$ | $T_H = 1/f_H$ | $64$ | $\mu s$ |
| Active Line Period | $T_{active} \approx 0.82 \times T_H$ | $\approx 52$ | $\mu s$ |
| Horizontal Blanking Interval | $T_{HBI} \approx 0.18 \times T_H$ | $\approx 12$ | $\mu s$ |
| Vertical Blanking Interval | $T_{VBI}$ | $\approx 1.6$ | ms |
| Interlacing Factor | Odd-line offset between fields | 2:1 | — |
| Inter-carrier Sound Frequency | $f_{IF,vision} - f_{IF,sound}$ | $38.9 - 33.4 = 5.5$ | MHz |
| Aspect Ratio (4:3) | Width : Height | $4:3$ | — |
| Video Bandwidth (approx) | $B \approx f_H \times (\text{active lines per frame}) \times (\text{aspect correction})/2$ | $\approx 5$ | MHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Every modern smartphone, digital TV, and broadcast monitoring system uses a derivative of this block architecture. The tuner + IF + detector + sync + deflection chain is the **universal template** for all television receivers (analog and digital). Understanding this block diagram is foundational to designing **software-defined radios (SDR)**, **digital TV demodulators (DVB-T2)**, and **video processing ASICs**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Line, Frame, and Field Frequency

In an interlaced TV system, the picture is divided into **two fields** (odd and even lines). Each field contains half the total lines, and when interleaved they form a complete **frame**.

**Step 1:** Define the total number of lines in one complete frame.

Let $N$ = number of lines per frame. For PAL system, $N = 625$.

**Step 2:** Relate lines to fields.

Since interlacing uses 2 fields per frame:

$$
\text{Number of lines per field} = \frac{N}{2} = \frac{625}{2} = 312.5 \text{ lines}
$$

The half-line offset is what makes interlacing work — field 1 ends on a half-line, field 2 begins on a half-line, creating the **odd-line interlace**.

**Step 3:** Define field frequency.

To avoid flicker, fields are presented at the local mains frequency to take advantage of the mains-supplied lighting's persistence:

$$
f_{field} = 50 \text{ Hz (India/PAL)}
$$

**Step 4:** Derive frame frequency.

$$
f_{frame} = \frac{f_{field}}{2} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 5:** Derive line (horizontal) frequency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_H &= N \times f_{frame} \\
f_H &= 625 \times 25 \\
f_H &= 15{,}625 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Derive line period.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_H &= \frac{1}{f_H} = \frac{1}{15{,}625} \\
T_H &= 64 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 64 \text{ }\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Signal Flow Characteristics

| **Block** | **Input Signal Type** | **Output Signal Type** | **Frequency / Bandwidth** | **Typical Gain** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RF Tuner | RF modulated signal (~47–862 MHz) | RF at IF (38.9 MHz) | 7–8 MHz | 20–30 dB |
| IF Amplifier | IF modulated signal | Amplified IF | 38.9 MHz (vision) | 60–70 dB total |
| Video Detector | AM-IF at 38.9 MHz | Composite Video Baseband (CVBS) | 0–5 MHz | Conversion loss ~6 dB |
| Video Amplifier | CVBS (~1 Vpp) | High-level video (~3–5 Vpp) | 0–5 MHz | 10–14 dB |
| Sync Separator | CVBS with sync tips | H-sync + V-sync pulses | 15,625 Hz / 50 Hz | Comparator output |
| Horizontal Deflection | H-sync | Sawtooth current in yoke + EHT | 15,625 Hz | Power stage |
| Vertical Deflection | V-sync | Sawtooth current in yoke | 50 Hz | Power stage |
| Display (CRT) | Video + deflection | Visible image | 0–5 MHz video | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the **frequency keeps decreasing** as we move down the chain — from MHz (RF) to kHz (horizontal) to Hz (vertical). This is a classic receiver design pattern: process high-frequency signals first, then progressively extract lower-frequency information.

### 3.3 Detailed Explanation of Sync Separation Logic

The composite video signal has a specific structure:

- **Sync tip level:** 75% of peak carrier (most negative for negative modulation)
- **Blanking level:** 75% of peak carrier
- **Black level:** 70% of peak carrier
- **White level:** 10–20% of peak carrier

Sync separation uses **DC restoration (clamping)** to fix the sync tips at a known reference voltage, then a **comparator (clipper)** slices off everything above (or below) this level, isolating only the sync pulses.

- **Differentiation** (high-pass): Produces sharp pulses at the leading edges of horizontal sync → recovers 15,625 Hz.
- **Integration** (low-pass): Accumulates the equalizing and vertical serration pulses → recovers 50 Hz vertical sync.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Video Reproduction System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[antenna] --> B[rftuner]
    B --> C[ifamplifier]
    C --> D[videodetector]
    D --> E[videoamplifier]
    E --> F[syncseparator]
    F --> G[horizdefl]
    F --> H[vertdefl]
    G --> I[yokeh]
    H --> J[yokev]
    E --> K[displaydevice]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    G --> L[ehtgen]
    L --> K
    D --> M[audiosection]
    M --> N[speaker]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Annotated Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[rfinput] --> B[vhfuhftuner]
    B --> C[mixerstage]
    C --> D[ifamp1]
    D --> E[ifamp2]
    E --> F[sawfilter]
    F --> G[ifamp3]
    G --> H[videodetector]
    H --> I1[videoamp]
    H --> I2[agcdetector]
    I2 --> B
    I1 --> J[clamper]
    J --> K[contraststage]
    K --> L[outputtoCRT]
    H --> M[syncclipper]
    M --> N[hsyncout]
    M --> O[vsyncout]
    N --> P[hafc]
    P --> Q[hoscillator]
    Q --> R[hdrive]
    R --> S[houtput]
    S --> T[hyoke]
    S --> U[flybackxformer]
    U --> V[eht25kV]
    O --> W[vsyncin]
    W --> X[voscillator]
    X --> Y[voutput]
    Y --> Z[vyoke]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Description Table

| **Node ID** | **Block Name** | **Primary Function** | **Key Output** |
|---|---|---|---|
| antenna | Receiving antenna | Captures broadcast RF energy | Multiple RF channels |
| rftuner | RF tuner | Channel selection & frequency conversion | Selected RF at IF |
| ifamplifier | IF amplifier | Gain & selectivity at fixed IF | Strong IF signal at 38.9 MHz |
| videodetector | Video detector | AM demodulation | Baseband composite video |
| videoamplifier | Video amplifier | Signal amplification & DC restoration | High-level CVBS |
| syncseparator | Sync separator | Extract H & V sync from CVBS | H-sync + V-sync pulses |
| horizdefl | Horizontal deflection | Generate 15.625 kHz sawtooth | Deflection current + EHT |
| vertdefl | Vertical deflection | Generate 50 Hz sawtooth | Deflection current |
| displaydevice | CRT/Flat panel | Convert electrical signals to image | Visible picture |
| yokeh | Horizontal yoke | Magnetic field for horizontal sweep | Beam horizontal position |
| yokev | Vertical yoke | Magnetic field for vertical sweep | Beam vertical position |
| ehtgen | EHT generator | High voltage for CRT anode | ~25 kV DC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above use purely alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `rftuner`, `videodetector`) to comply with the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule**. All node labels are kept clean (no markdown bold/italics/tables inside label strings) for proper rendering.

### 4.4 Signal Flow Description (Rastar Formation)

The picture on the screen is built **line by line** in a **raster scan pattern**:

1. Beam starts at top-left corner.
2. Sweeps left-to-right along one line, modulated in intensity by the video signal.
3. Reaches right edge — horizontal retrace (blanked) to next line.
4. Continues for 312.5 lines (first field — odd lines).
5. Beam returns to top (vertical retrace, blanked).
6. Begins second field — even lines, offset by half-line.
7. Two fields interlace to form one complete 625-line frame.
8. Process repeats 25 times per second (frame rate).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define the term "video reproduction system" in a television receiver. List its main functional blocks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A video reproduction system in a TV receiver is the complete signal-processing chain that recovers the original picture information from the received RF television signal and displays it on the screen. The main functional blocks are:
1. RF Tuner (VHF/UHF)
2. IF Amplifier
3. Video Detector (Demodulator)
4. Video Amplifier
5. Sync Separator
6. Horizontal Deflection System
7. Vertical Deflection System
8. Display Device (CRT/Flat panel)

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing all 8 blocks — ½ mark per block, minimum 6 required for full 2 marks)*

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **What is the function of the sync separator in a TV receiver? Mention the frequencies of horizontal and vertical sync in the PAL-B system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The sync separator extracts the horizontal and vertical synchronizing pulses from the composite video signal. These pulses are essential to keep the receiver's deflection oscillators locked in step with the camera's scanning at the transmitter.
- Horizontal sync frequency (PAL-B): **15,625 Hz**
- Vertical sync frequency (PAL-B): **50 Hz**

*(1 mark function + 1 mark each for the two frequencies)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **(a)** Draw the complete block diagram of a video reproduction system in a television receiver and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the interlace scanning method used in TV systems. Derive the relationship between line frequency, frame frequency, and field frequency for the PAL-B system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Functions (7 marks):**

Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1. Explanation of each block:

- **RF Tuner (1 mark):** Selects the desired channel from the VHF/UHF band and converts it to a fixed IF (38.9 MHz) using a local oscillator and mixer. Provides RF amplification with low noise figure.
- **IF Amplifier (1 mark):** Provides most of the receiver's gain and selectivity at the fixed IF. Uses SAW filter for precise bandpass shaping. AGC voltage is derived here.
- **Video Detector (1 mark):** Demodulates the AM vision IF to recover the baseband composite video signal (CVBS) containing luminance, chrominance, blanking, and sync information.
- **Video Amplifier (1 mark):** Amplifies CVBS to a level (3–5 Vpp) sufficient to drive the display. Includes DC restoration, contrast, and brightness controls.
- **Sync Separator (1 mark):** Uses amplitude clipping to separate sync pulses from video. Differentiator extracts horizontal sync (15,625 Hz); integrator extracts vertical sync (50 Hz).
- **Horizontal & Vertical Deflection + Display (2 marks):** Generate sawtooth currents in the yoke to sweep the electron beam in a raster pattern. The video signal modulates beam intensity to recreate the picture on the CRT/display.

**(b) Interlaced Scanning & Frequency Relationships (7 marks):**

**Interlaced Scanning (4 marks):**
In interlaced scanning, each frame is divided into two fields. The first field scans the odd-numbered lines (1, 3, 5, …, 623) from top to bottom. The beam then retraces to the top and the second field scans the even-numbered lines (2, 4, 6, …, 624), interleaved between the odd lines. The total number of lines is odd (625) so that the two fields interlace perfectly. This reduces flicker (since fields refresh at 50 Hz) while maintaining vertical resolution (since frames are 25 Hz complete pictures).

**Frequency Relationship Derivation (3 marks):**

For the PAL-B system:
- Total lines per frame: $N = 625$
- Fields per frame = 2 (interlaced)
- Field frequency: $f_{field} = 50$ Hz
- Frame frequency: $f_{frame} = f_{field}/2 = 50/2 = 25$ Hz
- Line (horizontal) frequency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_H &= N \times f_{frame} \\
&= 625 \times 25 \\
&= 15{,}625 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

Line period:

$$
T_H = \frac{1}{f_H} = \frac{1}{15{,}625} = 64 \text{ }\mu\text{s}
$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **(a)** Explain the function of the RF tuner and IF amplifier in a TV receiver. State the standard vision and sound IF frequencies for the PAL-B system. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the horizontal and vertical deflection circuits used in a TV receiver. Why is interlacing used in television broadcasting? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RF Tuner, IF Amplifier, and IF Frequencies (7 marks):**

**RF Tuner (3 marks):**
The RF tuner selects the desired channel from the broadband antenna signal covering VHF (47–230 MHz) and UHF (470–862 MHz) bands. It consists of an RF amplifier, a local oscillator, and a mixer. The local oscillator frequency is tuned to be **38.9 MHz higher** than the desired channel's vision carrier (high-side injection), so that the mixer output produces a fixed IF of 38.9 MHz. This fixed-IF approach simplifies amplifier design since all channels are processed at the same frequency.

**IF Amplifier (2 marks):**
The IF amplifier provides most of the receiver's gain (60–70 dB) and adjacent-channel selectivity. Modern receivers use a **SAW (Surface Acoustic Wave) filter** to achieve a precise bandpass response with sharp roll-off at adjacent channel boundaries. AGC (Automatic Gain Control) is also applied at the IF stage to maintain constant video output despite signal strength variations.

**Standard IF Frequencies (2 marks):**
- **Vision IF (PAL-B):** **38.9 MHz**
- **Sound IF (PAL-B):** **33.4 MHz**
- **Inter-carrier spacing:** $38.9 - 33.4 = 5.5$ MHz

**(b) Deflection Circuits and Need for Interlacing (7 marks):**

**Horizontal Deflection (2 marks):**
Generates a **15,625 Hz sawtooth current** through the horizontal yoke coils. The circuit includes an AFC loop (comparing incoming H-sync with a local oscillator), an oscillator, a driver, and a power output stage (typically using a high-voltage transistor or thyristor in older designs). The rapid collapse of current at the end of each line produces a high-voltage flyback pulse, which is stepped up by the **flyback transformer** to generate the **EHT (Extra High Tension, ~25 kV)** needed for the CRT anode.

**Vertical Deflection (2 marks):**
Generates a **50 Hz sawtooth current** through the vertical yoke coils. Uses a 50 Hz oscillator (or a divider from horizontal in digital sets) locked to the V-sync, followed by a vertical output amplifier. The lower frequency and smaller deflection power make this circuit simpler than horizontal.

**Need for Interlacing (3 marks):**
Interlacing transmits two fields (odd and even lines) per frame, refreshing the screen at 50 Hz while sending a complete picture only at 25 Hz. This:
1. **Reduces flicker** (the eye perceives 50 Hz refresh, not 25 Hz).
2. **Halves the video bandwidth** required for a given vertical resolution (since only 312.5 lines are transmitted per field).
3. **Compatibility with mains frequency** (50 Hz India, 60 Hz US) — fields are synchronized to mains to avoid mains-induced hum patterns.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Confusing "field" and "frame" frequencies:** Many students write 25 Hz as the line frequency or 15,625 Hz as the vertical frequency. Memorize: **Field = 50 Hz, Frame = 25 Hz, Line = 15,625 Hz.**
> 2. **Wrong IF values:** Always specify **38.9 MHz (vision)** and **33.4 MHz (sound)** for PAL-B. Do NOT write 45.75 MHz (that is for NTSC/M, used in USA).
> 3. **Skipping the retrace/blanking concept:** When explaining interlacing, you MUST mention that the beam is **blanked** during horizontal and vertical retraces — the receiver must include blanking circuits.
> 4. **Forgetting the EHT block:** The EHT (Extra High Tension) generator is a critical part of the horizontal deflection system in CRT TVs. Omitting it loses 1–2 marks in deflection-related questions.
> 5. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows:** KTU examiners explicitly check for proper signal flow arrows. Always show direction with `→` arrows between blocks.
> 6. **Not labeling frequencies:** Every block in a TV block diagram should ideally have its operating frequency mentioned. Failure to do so loses marks.
> 7. **Confusing aspect ratio:** Indian PAL broadcasts use **4:3** traditionally; HD broadcasts use **16:9**. State the ratio explicitly when discussing display geometry.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Block Diagram of Video Reproduction System**

- [ ] **Main blocks (8 total):** RF Tuner → IF Amp → Video Detector → Video Amp → Sync Separator → H-Deflection → V-Deflection → Display
- [ ] **Vision IF (PAL-B):** **38.9 MHz**
- [ ] **Sound IF (PAL-B):** **33.4 MHz**
- [ ] **Inter-carrier sound:** **5.5 MHz**
- [ ] **Total lines per frame:** **625**
- [ ] **Field frequency:** **50 Hz** (2 fields per frame)
- [ ] **Frame frequency:** **25 Hz**
- [ ] **Line frequency:** **15,625 Hz**
- [ ] **Line period:** **64 µs**
- [ ] **Video bandwidth:** **~5 MHz**
- [ ] **Aspect ratio:** **4:3** (or 16:9 for HD)
- [ ] **Sync separator uses:** Differentiator (H-sync) + Integrator (V-sync)
- [ ] **EHT for CRT:** **~25 kV** (generated from horizontal flyback)
- [ ] **Interlacing:** Odd/even field interleave; 2:1 ratio
- [ ] **SAW filter:** Used in IF stage for sharp selectivity
- [ ] **AGC (Automatic Gain Control):** Derived from IF/video stage, applied to tuner + IF
- [ ] **Composite Video Signal (CVBS):** Contains video + blanking + sync information
- [ ] **Blanking:** Beam is turned OFF during horizontal and vertical retraces

> **🎯 KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw and explain," always provide a **labeled block diagram with arrows** AND a **function description for each block**. This fetches full marks. Memorize the IF frequencies and scanning frequencies — they appear in almost every TV-related question paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cathode Ray Tubes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cathode Ray Tubes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT)** is an evacuated (vacuum) glass envelope device that produces visible images through the controlled bombardment of a phosphor-coated screen by a focused, high-velocity beam of electrons. The "cathode ray" is a historical misnomer for a stream of electrons emitted from a heated cathode, first identified by Sir **J.J. Thomson (1897)** and practically engineered into a display device by **Karl Ferdinand Braun (1897)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (Course Code: OEEVT722 – Entertainment Electronics), the CRT forms the historical and pedagogical baseline of display engineering, because it introduces the foundational physics of *electron beam steering*, *phosphor luminescence*, and *raster/vector scanning* that all modern displays (LCD, LED, OLED, plasma) implicitly inherit or replace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** CRTs must be studied in terms of (i) electron gun optics, (ii) electrostatic and magnetic deflection, (iii) screen phosphors, and (iv) the differences between monochrome and color (shadow-mask) architectures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Light Pen on a Dark Wall"** 🎯
>
> Imagine you are standing in a pitch-dark room holding a laser pointer. The wall is painted with a special paint that *glows brightly* for a fraction of a second wherever the laser dot touches it. If you flick your wrist very fast, the bright dot "draws" patterns and shapes on the wall — even though the wall itself is dark.
>
> A CRT works almost identically:
> - The **laser pointer** = a beam of electrons fired by the **electron gun**.
> - The **special glowing paint** = the **phosphor screen** on the inside of the front glass.
> - The **flicking wrist** = the **deflection system** (electric plates or magnetic coils) that sweeps the electron beam across the screen.
> - The **dark room** = the **high vacuum** inside the tube, which lets electrons travel freely without colliding with air molecules.
>
> Your brain perceives a *complete, steady image* only because the beam redraws (scans) the entire screen 25 to 100 times per second, exploiting **persistence of vision**.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

- **Operating Accelerating Voltage (EHT):** Typically **20 kV – 30 kV** for color TV CRTs; up to **50 kV** in projection CRTs.
- **Electron Beam Current:** **100 μA – 1 mA** (per gun, in color CRTs).
- **Phosphor Persistence:** **1 ms – 100 ms** depending on phosphor type (P4, P22, P31, etc.).
- **Vacuum Pressure:** Approximately **10⁻⁶ Torr** (≈ **1.33 × 10⁻⁴ Pa**).
- **Screen Refresh Rate:** **50 Hz / 60 Hz / 100 Hz** interlaced or progressive.
- **Dot Pitch (color CRTs):** **0.21 mm – 0.41 mm** — directly determines perceived sharpness.

---

### 1.4 Historical Significance in Entertainment Electronics

| Decade | Milestone | Engineering Impact |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| 1897 | Braun's first oscilloscope CRT | Birth of electronic visualization |
| 1920s | Zworykin's iconoscope | TV signal → electronic image conversion |
| 1938 | First commercial TV (USA) | CRT enters consumer entertainment |
| 1950s | Shadow-mask color CRT | Full-color broadcast era begins |
| 1980s | Trinitron (Sony) — aperture grille | Higher brightness, vertical-stripe phosphors |
| 2000s+ | LCD/LED displacement | CRT becomes archival/educational technology |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although CRTs are commercially obsolete since the early 2010s, the KTU 2024 syllabus retains them because they form the **didactic backbone** of display-system teaching: scanning, deflection physics, colorimetry via shadow masking, persistence, and gamma correction are all concepts *inherited* by modern flat-panel displays.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a CRT — The Five Subsystems

A CRT is composed of five clearly identifiable subsystems, each with a distinct engineering function. Understanding the role of each is the single most important prerequisite for KTU Part-B (14-mark) questions.

#### (A) The Electron Gun Assembly
This subsystem *generates, accelerates, and focuses* the electron beam.

1. **Cathode (Indirectly Heated):** A nickel cylinder coated with barium/strontium oxides. A heater filament (≈ 6.3 V, 0.3 A) raises it to ≈ **1000 K**, triggering **thermionic emission** described by the **Richardson–Dushman equation**.
2. **Control Grid (Wehnelt Cylinder):** Held at a small *negative* potential relative to the cathode. It controls beam intensity — increasing the negative bias reduces beam current; reducing it brightens the spot. This is the **brightness/z-modulation** input.
3. **Pre-Accelerating Anode (A1):** Held at ≈ **+400 V to +1000 V**. Accelerates electrons out of the cathode region.
4. **Focusing Anode (A2 / A3):** A bipotential or unipotential lens system that focuses the diverging electrons into a tight beam of diameter ≈ **0.2 mm** at the screen. The geometry is analogous to an optical convex lens, but acts on charged particles via the **Buneman–Pierce lens principle**.
5. **Final Accelerating Anode (EHT Anode):** A conductive graphite coating (aquadag) on the inside of the funnel wall, connected to **+20 kV to +30 kV**. It gives electrons their final kinetic energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the aquadag coating?** It forms the anode physically *along* the path of the beam. The accelerating electric field thus stretches into the tube, which is essential for the beam to remain focused over the long distance (≈ **40 cm**) from gun to screen.

#### (B) The Deflection System
Once a focused beam is produced, it must be *steered* in two orthogonal directions to trace a raster (or vector path).

- **Electrostatic Deflection (E):** Used in oscilloscope CRTs. Two *parallel-plate capacitors* are placed inside the tube neck. A voltage $V_d$ applied between the plates creates a transverse field $E_y = V_d / d$ that bends the electron trajectory.
- **Magnetic Deflection (M):** Used in TV/monitor CRTs. Two pairs of *saddle-shaped coils* on the yoke around the tube neck create a transverse magnetic field $B$ that exerts the **Lorentz force** $\vec{F} = -e(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ on the moving electrons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Trade-off:** Magnetic deflection allows a shorter tube neck and higher deflection angles (≈ 90°–110°), which is why all consumer TV CRTs use it. Electrostatic deflection is faster (higher bandwidth), which is why oscilloscope CRTs use it. *This is a guaranteed 3-mark KTU question every cycle.*

#### (C) The Phosphor Screen
The inside of the front glass is coated with a thin layer of phosphor crystals. Common phosphors:

| Phosphor | Color | Persistence | Application |
|----------|-------|-------------|-------------|
| **P4** | White | Medium | Black & white TV |
| **P22** | R, G, B triads | Medium | Color TV (shadow mask) |
| **P31** | Green | Short | Oscilloscopes |
| **P39** | Yellow-green | Long | Radar |

**Luminescence Mechanism:** When a high-energy electron strikes a phosphor atom, it raises bound electrons to higher energy states. As the electrons fall back, photons are emitted. For zinc sulfide (ZnS) doped with silver (P22 green), the emission peak is at ≈ **530 nm**.

**Persistence** is the time the phosphor continues to glow after excitation. Short persistence (< 1 ms) is needed for fast signals; long persistence (> 50 ms) is used in radar to leave a "trail" of moving targets.

#### (D) The Shadow Mask (Color CRTs Only)
A thin **iron sheet** perforated with ≈ **200,000 to 400,000** precisely aligned holes, placed ≈ **10–15 mm** behind the phosphor screen. It ensures that the **red gun** can only hit **red phosphors**, the **green gun** only **green**, and the **blue gun** only **blue**.

Three separate electron guns (RGB) are arranged in a **delta (triangular)** or **in-line** configuration, and the shadow mask is what enforces the color purity. Misalignment produces a *convergence error*, visible as colored fringes at the screen edges.

> [!WARNING]
> The shadow mask absorbs ≈ **80%** of the electron beam energy! This is the principal reason CRTs are power-inefficient and run hot — a critical disadvantage relative to LCD/LED displays.

#### (E) The Vacuum Envelope
The glass envelope maintains a vacuum of ≈ **10⁻⁶ Torr**. This is essential because:
- Electrons must travel **~40 cm** without scattering off gas molecules.
- Any residual gas would ionize, producing ions that bombard the cathode ("**ion burn**") and destroy the oxide coating.
- It provides a rigid mechanical support for the high-voltage anode.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE.** It contains every equation that has appeared in KTU previous-year papers for this module.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Final velocity of accelerated electron | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2eV_a}{m_e}}$ | $V_a$: anode voltage (V); $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg; $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C | Non-relativistic ($V_a \lesssim 10^5$ V) |
| 2 | Kinetic energy of electron | $KE = eV_a$ | Joules (J) | Classical regime |
| 3 | Electrostatic deflection on screen | $Y = \dfrac{L \,\ell \, V_d}{2 \, d \, V_a}$ | $L$: plates→screen distance (m); $\ell$: plate length (m); $d$: plate separation (m); $V_d$: plate voltage (V) | Parallel plates, uniform field |
| 4 | Electrostatic deflection sensitivity | $S_e = \dfrac{\ell}{2 d V_a}$ (per plate volt) | m/V | Higher sensitivity ⟶ lower $V_a$ |
| 5 | Time of flight through deflection plates | $t_p = \dfrac{\ell}{v_x} = \ell \sqrt{\dfrac{m_e}{2eV_a}}$ | s | Used in transient analysis |
| 6 | Magnetic deflection on screen | $Y = \dfrac{B \, \ell \, L}{\sqrt{2eV_a / m_e}}$ | $B$: flux density (T); $\ell$: coil length (m) | Transverse $\vec{B}$, $L$ as above |
| 7 | Magnetic deflection sensitivity | $S_m = \dfrac{\ell \, L \, \sqrt{e}}{\sqrt{2 m_e \, V_a}}$ | m/T or m/(Wb/m²) | Decreases as $V_a$ rises |
| 8 | Radius of circular electron path in $\vec{B}$ | $r = \dfrac{m_e v}{e B} = \dfrac{1}{B}\sqrt{\dfrac{2 m_e V_a}{e}}$ | m | Cyclotron motion |
| 9 | Lorentz force magnitude | $F = e v B \sin\theta$ | N | $\theta$ = angle between $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$ |
| 10 | Thermionic emission current density (Richardson–Dushman) | $J = A_R T^2 e^{-W/(kT)}$ | A/m²; $A_R \approx 6 \times 10^5$ A·m⁻²·K⁻² for oxide cathodes; $W$: work function (eV); $k$: Boltzmann const. | $T$ in Kelvin |
| 11 | Persistence / decay of phosphor brightness | $I(t) = I_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\tau$: time constant of phosphor | Empirical; $\tau$ depends on activator |
| 12 | Phosphor luminous efficacy | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{P_{beam}}$ | lm/W | Typical P22 ≈ 25–35 lm/W |
| 13 | Convergence error (color CRT) | $\Delta = \dfrac{s \, L}{p}$ | $s$: gun offset (m); $p$: gun-to-mask distance (m) | Defines mask alignment tolerance |
| 14 | Spot size (diameter) | $d_s \approx \dfrac{2}{3} \, D_o$ | $D_o$: object-side lens diameter | Approximation of demagnified gun crossover |
| 15 | Line frequency (TV) | $f_H = f_V \times N_{lines} \times K$ | $f_V$: vertical refresh; $N_{lines}$: 625/525; $K$: interlace factor (≈ 1) | Used in video timing |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mathematical framework above is not purely academic. The same equations govern:
- **Oscilloscope design** (real-time bandwidth = 1 / $t_p$).
- **Particle accelerators** (electrons deflected in a magnetic field to follow a circular path).
- **Cathode Ray Oscillators (CROs)** in medical electronics (EKG/EEG displays).
- **Electron beam lithography** for semiconductor nanofabrication.
- **Electron microscopes** (TEM, SEM) — descendant technology of CRT optics.

A KTU examiner can award full marks only when a student can connect the math to *both* the entertainment display *and* a non-display application.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Final Velocity of an Electron in the EHT Field

**Given:** An electron of mass $m_e$ and charge $-e$ is released from rest at the cathode and accelerated through a potential difference $V_a$.

**Find:** Velocity $v$ as it strikes the phosphor screen.

**Step 1 — Energy Conservation.** All electrical potential energy converts to kinetic energy:

$$W = qV_a = eV_a$$

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

**Step 2 — Equate the two energies:**

$$\frac{1}{2} m_e v^2 = eV_a$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $v$:**

$$v^2 = \frac{2 e V_a}{m_e}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 e V_a}{m_e}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical sanity check.** For $V_a = 25\,\text{kV} = 2.5 \times 10^4$ V:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (2.5 \times 10^4})}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{8.0 \times 10^{-15}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{8.78 \times 10^{15}}$$

$$v \approx 9.37 \times 10^7 \,\text{m/s} \approx 0.31\,c$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** This velocity is about **31% of the speed of light**. At higher anode voltages (> 100 kV), relativistic corrections become necessary, and the simple $\frac{1}{2} m v^2 = eV$ formula no longer holds. CRTs operating above ≈ 30 kV are *mildly relativistic*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electrostatic Deflection of an Electron Beam

**Setup:** An electron enters a parallel-plate region of length $\ell$, plate separation $d$, with a uniform transverse electric field $E_y = V_d/d$ between the plates. The plates are followed by a field-free drift region of length $L$ to the screen.

**Step 1 — Initial axial velocity (from Derivation 1):**

$$v_x = \sqrt{\frac{2 e V_a}{m_e}}$$

**Step 2 — Transverse acceleration in the field region:**

$$a_y = \frac{F_y}{m_e} = \frac{e E_y}{m_e} = \frac{e V_d}{m_e d}$$

**Step 3 — Time spent inside the plates (axial motion is undisturbed):**

$$t_p = \frac{\ell}{v_x} = \ell \sqrt{\frac{m_e}{2 e V_a}}$$

**Step 4 — Transverse velocity acquired inside the plates:**

$$v_y = a_y \, t_p = \frac{e V_d}{m_e d} \cdot \ell \sqrt{\frac{m_e}{2 e V_a}}$$

$$v_y = \frac{V_d \, \ell}{d} \sqrt{\frac{e}{2 m_e V_a}}$$

**Step 5 — Transverse displacement at the exit of the plates:**

$$y_1 = \frac{1}{2} a_y t_p^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{e V_d}{m_e d} \cdot \frac{\ell^2 m_e}{2 e V_a}$$

$$\boxed{y_1 = \frac{V_d \, \ell^2}{4 d \, V_a}}$$

**Step 6 — Additional drift in the field-free region.** After exiting the plates, the electron travels straight with transverse velocity $v_y$ for time $t_d = (L - \ell/2)/v_x$ (the centroid of the deflection occurs at $\ell/2$ inside the plates, an approximation valid for $\ell \ll L$):

$$y_2 = v_y \cdot \frac{L}{v_x} = \frac{V_d \, \ell \, L}{2 d \, V_a}$$

**Step 7 — Total deflection at the screen:**

$$\boxed{Y = y_1 + y_2 = \frac{V_d \, \ell^2}{4 d V_a} + \frac{V_d \, \ell \, L}{2 d V_a} = \frac{V_d \, \ell}{2 d V_a}\left(\frac{\ell}{2} + L\right)}$$

**Step 8 — Practical approximation** (since $L \gg \ell$, typically $L \approx 30$ cm and $\ell \approx 2$ cm):

$$\boxed{Y \approx \frac{V_d \, \ell \, L}{2 d \, V_a}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master equation** for KTU 14-mark derivations. You must derive it step-by-step; partial credit depends on showing Steps 1–6 explicitly.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Magnetic Deflection of an Electron Beam

**Setup:** A uniform magnetic field $B$ exists over a length $\ell$ in a region of the tube neck. The beam enters axially with velocity $v_x$. The Lorentz force on the electron is:

$$\vec{F} = -e(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

**Step 1 — Magnitude of transverse force** (assuming $\vec{v} \perp \vec{B}$):

$$F_y = e v_x B$$

**Step 2 — Transverse acceleration:**

$$a_y = \frac{e v_x B}{m_e}$$

**Step 3 — Time in the field region:**

$$t_p = \frac{\ell}{v_x}$$

**Step 4 — Transverse velocity acquired:**

$$v_y = a_y \, t_p = \frac{e v_x B}{m_e} \cdot \frac{\ell}{v_x} = \frac{e B \ell}{m_e}$$

**Step 5 — Transverse exit displacement:**

$$y_1 = \frac{1}{2} a_y t_p^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{e v_x B}{m_e} \cdot \frac{\ell^2}{v_x^2} = \frac{e B \ell^2}{2 m_e v_x}$$

**Step 6 — Drift-region contribution (similar to electrostatic case):**

$$y_2 = v_y \cdot \frac{L}{v_x} = \frac{e B \ell L}{m_e v_x}$$

**Step 7 — Total magnetic deflection:**

$$Y = y_1 + y_2 = \frac{e B \ell}{m_e v_x}\left(\frac{\ell}{2} + L\right) \approx \frac{e B \ell L}{m_e v_x}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $v_x = \sqrt{2 e V_a / m_e}$:**

$$Y = \frac{e B \ell L}{m_e} \sqrt{\frac{m_e}{2 e V_a}} = \frac{B \ell L}{\sqrt{2 m_e V_a / e}}$$

$$\boxed{Y = B \, \ell \, L \sqrt{\frac{e}{2 m_e V_a}}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Electrostatic vs. Magnetic Deflection

| Parameter | Electrostatic ($E$) | Magnetic ($B$) | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------|-------------------------|
| Deflection $Y$ | $\propto V_d / V_a$ | $\propto B / \sqrt{V_a}$ | $B$ deflection falls *slower* with $V_a$ than $E$ |
| Sensitivity to $V_a$ | $S_e \propto 1/V_a$ | $S_m \propto 1/\sqrt{V_a}$ | Magnetic better for high EHT |
| Frequency response | Excellent (plates) | Limited (coil inductance) | $E$ used in oscilloscopes |
| Tube length required | Long (deep neck) | Short (shallow neck) | $B$ used in TVs |
| Power consumption | Low | High (yoke current) | $E$ is more efficient |
| Deflection angle | < 30° practical | up to 110° practical | $B$ enables flat front panels |
| Spot quality (astigmatism) | Better | Slightly poorer | $E$ better for measurement |
| Sweep linearity | Good with sawtooth ramp | Needs pincushion correction | $B$ needs dynamic correction |
| Effect of EHT change | $Y \propto 1/V_a$ — large | $Y \propto 1/\sqrt{V_a}$ — small | $B$ insensitive to EHT ripple |
| Use case | CRO, lab displays | TV, computer monitor | Application-specific |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A CRT has an electrostatic deflection system with $\ell = 2\,\text{cm}$, $d = 1\,\text{cm}$, and $L = 30\,\text{cm}$. The accelerating voltage is $V_a = 4\,\text{kV}$. A sinusoidal signal of peak value $V_d = 200\,\text{V}$ is applied. Calculate (a) the deflection sensitivity, and (b) the peak-to-peak screen deflection.

**Solution:**

**(a) Deflection sensitivity:**

$$S_e = \frac{\ell \, L}{2 d V_a} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-2})(30 \times 10^{-2})}{2 (1 \times 10^{-2})(4 \times 10^3)}$$

$$S_e = \frac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{80} = 7.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m/V}$$

$$\boxed{S_e = 7.5 \,\mu\text{m/V}}$$

**(b) Peak-to-peak screen deflection:**

$$V_{pp} = 2 V_d = 400\,\text{V}$$

$$Y_{pp} = S_e \times V_{pp} = 7.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 400 = 3 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{Y_{pp} = 3\,\text{mm}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Monochrome CRT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heater Filament 6.3V AC] --> B[Oxide Coated Cathode]
    B --> C[Thermionic Emission of Electrons]
    C --> D[Control Grid G1 - Brightness Modulation]
    D --> E[Pre-accelerating Anode A1]
    E --> F[Focusing Anode A2 - Electron Lens]
    F --> G[EHT Anode Aquadag 25kV]
    G --> H[High Velocity Electron Beam]
    H --> I[Deflection Plates Y axis]
    H --> J[Deflection Plates X axis]
    I --> K[Phosphor Screen ZnS doped]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Visible Light Emission]
    L --> M[Vacuum Envelope 10^-6 Torr]
    M --> N[External Glass Faceplate]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture of a Color CRT (Shadow Mask Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GUN[Electron Gun Assembly - 3 in delta config]
        G1R[Red Gun R]
        G1G[Green Gun G]
        G1B[Blue Gun B]
    end
    GUN --> CONV[Convergence Yoke Assembly]
    CONV --> MASK[Shadow Mask 200000 holes]
    MASK --> SCREEN[Phosphor Dot Triad RGB]
    SCREEN --> FACE[Glass Faceplate]
    subgraph COILS[Deflection Yoke Coils]
        YOKEY[Horizontal Coils X]
        YOKEX[Vertical Coils Y]
    end
    COILS --> CONV
    subgraph BASE[Base Pin Connections]
        PIN1[Heater Pins 1 and 14]
        PIN2[Grid 1 Pin 2]
        PIN3[Cathode Pin 3]
        PIN4[Focus Anode Pin 9]
    end
    BASE --> GUN
```

### 4.3 Detailed Functional Block Diagram of the Electron Gun

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Heater] --> CATH[Cathode with Oxide Coating]
    CATH -->|thermionic emission| GRID[Control Grid G1]
    GRID -->|variable negative bias| A1[Pre-accelerating Anode]
    A1 --> A2[Focusing Anode Lens 1]
    A2 --> A3[Focusing Anode Lens 2]
    A3 --> EHT[Final EHT Anode 25kV]
    EHT --> BEAM[Collimated Electron Beam]
    BEAM --> FOCUS[Beam Crossover approx 0.2 mm]
    FOCUS --> SCREEN[Phosphor Screen]
    SCREEN --> LIGHT[Light Pulse]
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow and Control Loops in a CRT Television

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VIDEO[Composite Video Input] --> SYNC[Sync Separator]
    SYNC --> HSYNC[Horizontal Sync 15625 Hz]
    SYNC --> VSYNC[Vertical Sync 50 Hz]
    HSYNC --> HOSC[H Drive Oscillator]
    VSYNC --> VOSC[V Drive Oscillator]
    HOSC --> HOUT[Horizontal Output Stage]
    VOSC --> VOUT[Vertical Output Stage]
    HOUT --> YOKEH[Horizontal Yoke Coils]
    VOUT --> YOKEV[Vertical Yoke Coils]
    YOKEH --> BEAM[Deflected Electron Beam]
    YOKEV --> BEAM
    VIDEO --> VIDEOAMP[Video Amplifier]
    VIDEOAMP --> CATHODE[CRT Cathode]
    CATHODE --> BEAM
    BEAM --> SCREEN[Phosphor Screen]
    SCREEN --> PICTURE[Displayed Image]
    EHT[High Voltage EHT Supply 25kV] --> ANODE[Final Anode]
    ANODE --> BEAM
```

### 4.5 Energy Flow Diagram in a CRT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAT[Electrical Heater Energy ~ 2W] --> EMIT[Electron Emission]
    ELEC[Anode Supply 25kV] --> ACCEL[Electron Kinetic Energy]
    ACCEL --> BEAM[Beam at Screen]
    BEAM --> PHOS[Phosphor Excitation]
    PHOS --> VIS[Visible Light approx 5 percent of beam energy]
    PHOS --> HEAT2[Heat Loss approx 95 percent]
    BEAM --> MASK[Shadow Mask Absorption approx 80 percent of beam in color CRT]
    MASK --> HEAT3[Heat]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight from the Energy Diagram:** Only about **5%** of the electron beam's kinetic energy is converted to visible light. The remaining 95% becomes heat, most of which is absorbed by the shadow mask in color CRTs. This explains why CRTs ran hot, needed large heat sinks, and consumed significant power (typically 80–150 W for a 21-inch TV) compared to modern LED displays (30–60 W for the same screen size).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These model the KTU 2024 Scheme Part-A format: 2-mark weightage per question with a 1-mark for any sub-clause. Each question below carries 3 marks and demands a *precise, textbook-grade* answer.

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define "persistence" of a phosphor. How does persistence choice differ between oscilloscope CRTs and radar CRTs? Give one example phosphor for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Persistence** is defined as the time interval during which the luminance of a phosphor decays to **10% (or sometimes 1%)** of its peak brightness immediately after the electron beam excitation has ceased. It is a direct measure of how long the phosphor "remembers" the excitation event. Mathematically, for a single exponential decay:

$$I(t) = I_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$$

where $\tau$ is the **time constant** of the phosphor, related to the persistence $T_{10}$ by $T_{10} = \tau \ln 10 \approx 2.303 \tau$.

**Difference in applications:**

- **Oscilloscope CRTs** require **short persistence** phosphors, typically in the range of **10 μs to 1 ms**. This is because the screen must faithfully follow fast-changing signals (often > 100 MHz equivalent bandwidth); a long persistence would "smear" successive traces, obscuring transient details. **Example: P31 phosphor** (green, persistence ≈ 40 μs).

- **Radar CRTs** require **long persistence** phosphors, typically in the range of **1 s to 60 s**. A radar antenna rotates slowly (e.g., 6–24 RPM), and a long persistence phosphor allows a fading "trail" of past target positions, so that moving targets appear as bright streaks and stationary clutter fades. **Example: P39 phosphor** (yellow-green, persistence ≈ 1 s).

**(3 marks: definition 1, application contrast 1, example 1)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Why is a vacuum maintained inside a CRT? What is the typical pressure maintained and what happens if the vacuum fails?**

**Model Answer:**

A **high vacuum** is maintained inside a CRT for three critical reasons:

1. **Unimpeded electron travel:** Electrons accelerated to velocities of ≈ $10^8$ m/s must traverse ≈ **40 cm** from the electron gun to the phosphor screen without undergoing collisions with gas molecules. Any collision would scatter the beam, blur the spot, and reduce image sharpness.

2. **Prevention of gas ionization and ion damage:** At ≈ 10⁻⁶ Torr, the mean free path of an electron is far greater than the tube length, so the probability of ionizing a residual gas molecule is negligible. If vacuum fails, residual gas ions would be accelerated *backwards* toward the cathode by the high EHT field, bombarding and destroying the delicate barium/strontium oxide coating — a phenomenon called **"ion burn"** or **"cathode poisoning."**

3. **High-voltage insulation:** The 25 kV anode potential would arc through any gas at near-atmospheric pressure. A vacuum provides the highest possible dielectric strength.

**Typical pressure maintained:** ≈ **10⁻⁶ Torr** (which equals **1.33 × 10⁻⁴ Pa** or **1.33 × 10⁻⁶ mbar**).

**Consequence of vacuum failure:** A "cracked tube" loses its vacuum. Atmospheric pressure (≈ 10⁵ Pa) acting on the evacuated glass envelope (which has an area of ≈ 0.5 m² for a 21-inch tube) exerts a net inward force of ≈ **5 × 10⁴ N** (≈ 5 tons!). The glass implodes violently, producing an explosive inward collapse that scatters glass shards at high velocity. Additionally, the cathode is destroyed by ion bombardment within seconds, and the high-voltage EHT supply arcs internally.

**(3 marks: three reasons 2, pressure value 0.5, consequence 0.5)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part-B question offers *internal choice* — typically (a) is 7 marks and (b) is 7 marks. We provide **TWO completely independent alternative questions** below, both with full model solutions.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.A.(a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(7 marks)**
**Derive the expression for the electrostatic deflection of an electron beam in a CRT. Hence, define deflection sensitivity and state how it varies with accelerating anode voltage.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Consider a CRT with an electron beam accelerated through a potential $V_a$, entering a region of length $\ell$ between two parallel deflection plates separated by distance $d$. A transverse potential $V_d$ applied across the plates produces a uniform transverse electric field:

$$E_y = \frac{V_d}{d}$$

The electron enters the plate region with axial velocity:

$$v_x = \sqrt{\frac{2 e V_a}{m_e}} \quad \text{(from energy conservation)}$$

**Inside the plates**, the transverse force on the electron is:

$$F_y = e E_y = \frac{e V_d}{d}$$

This produces a transverse acceleration:

$$a_y = \frac{F_y}{m_e} = \frac{e V_d}{m_e d}$$

The time the electron spends inside the plates is:

$$t_p = \frac{\ell}{v_x}$$

**Transverse displacement at plate exit:**

$$y_1 = \frac{1}{2} a_y t_p^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{e V_d}{m_e d} \cdot \frac{\ell^2 m_e}{2 e V_a}$$

$$y_1 = \frac{V_d \, \ell^2}{4 d V_a}$$

**Transverse velocity at plate exit:**

$$v_y = a_y t_p = \frac{V_d \, \ell}{d} \sqrt{\frac{e}{2 m_e V_a}}$$

**Drift through field-free region of length $L$:**

$$y_2 = v_y \cdot \frac{L}{v_x} = \frac{V_d \, \ell \, L}{2 d V_a}$$

**Total deflection at screen:**
