$$Y = y_1 + y_2 = \frac{V_d \, \ell}{2 d V_a}\left(\frac{\ell}{2} + L\right)$$

Since $L \gg \ell$ in practical CRTs:

$$\boxed{Y \approx \frac{V_d \, \ell \, L}{2 d V_a}}$$

**Deflection Sensitivity** is defined as the screen deflection produced per unit deflection-plate voltage:

$$S_e = \frac{Y}{V_d} = \frac{\ell \, L}{2 d V_a} \quad \text{(units: m/V)}$$

**Variation with $V_a$:** From the expression, $S_e \propto 1/V_a$. Therefore, **increasing the accelerating voltage decreases the deflection sensitivity**. This is the fundamental reason oscilloscope designers trade off brightness (which requires higher $V_a$) against deflection sensitivity — and the reason external *post-deflection acceleration* schemes were developed.

**(7 marks: derivation 5, sensitivity definition 1, $V_a$ variation 1)**

---

#### **Q.A.(b) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(7 marks)**
**Explain the construction and working of a shadow-mask color CRT with a neat block diagram. State the role of the convergence yoke and the reason for the dot-triad arrangement.**

**Model Solution:**

A **shadow-mask color CRT** produces full-color images by spatially combining red, green, and blue phosphor emissions, exploiting the trichromatic theory of human color vision (Young–Helmholtz).

**Construction:**

1. **Three electron guns (R, G, B):** Arranged either in a **delta (triangular)** configuration (most common in older CRTs) or in an **in-line** configuration (used in modern tubes). Each gun is identical in structure to a monochrome gun, with its own cathode, control grid, and focusing electrodes.

2. **Convergence yoke assembly:** A set of magnetic deflection coils and adjustable permanent magnets placed around the tube neck, *before* the main deflection yoke. It independently tilts the three beams so that they all pass through the same hole in the shadow mask — a process called **convergence**.

3. **Main deflection yoke:** Two pairs of saddle coils that sweep the converged three-beam bundle horizontally and vertically in synchronism.

4. **Shadow mask:** A thin iron sheet, curved to match the screen curvature, perforated with ≈ **200,000–400,000** precisely photo-etched holes, placed ≈ **10–15 mm** behind the phosphor screen. The mask is thermally bi-metallic so it "breathes" outward when the tube heats up, keeping it in focus.

5. **Phosphor dot-triad screen:** The inside of the faceplate is coated with ≈ **600,000 phosphor dots** arranged in triangular clusters, with **red (Y₂O₃:Eu or Y₂O₂S:Eu)**, **green (ZnS:Cu,Al)**, and **blue (ZnS:Ag)** dots at the three vertices of each triangle. Behind each cluster is one hole in the shadow mask.

**Working Principle:**

The three electron beams (R, G, B) are converged to cross at a single point in the plane of the shadow mask, then diverge slightly so each beam passes through a *different* hole. Because the mask geometry is precisely aligned with the phosphor triads, the red beam can only strike red phosphors, the green beam only green phosphors, and the blue beam only blue phosphors. By modulating the intensity of each gun independently (using the three color video signals), any visible color is produced at each pixel by the principle of **spatial color addition**.

**Role of the Convergence Yoke:**

- Ensures that all three beams intersect at the *same* hole in the shadow mask.
- Compensates for the geometric separation of the three guns (≈ 5 mm apart in the neck).
- Provides dynamic convergence correction (more current at edges) because the screen is flat but the beam origin is offset.

**Reason for Dot-Triad Arrangement:**

- The triangular cluster shape allows three phosphors to be packed tightly (≈ 0.21–0.41 mm dot pitch) while preserving a near-circular "white" pixel when all three are excited.
- A *line* arrangement would produce an elongated pixel and reduce horizontal resolution.
- The arrangement, combined with the shadow mask, makes **mis-convergence highly visible** as colored fringes — a built-in self-check on alignment.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 above — the shadow-mask color CRT block diagram illustrates the full signal path.)

**(7 marks: construction 3, working 2, convergence yoke 1, dot-triad 1)**

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

#### **Q.B.(a) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(7 marks)**
**Derive the expression for the magnetic deflection of an electron beam in a CRT. Compare the deflection sensitivity of magnetic and electrostatic deflection systems.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Consider an electron beam of axial velocity $v_x$ entering a region of uniform magnetic flux density $B$ over a length $\ell$. The magnetic field is transverse (perpendicular) to the beam axis.

The Lorentz force on the electron (charge $-e$) is:

$$F = e v_x B$$

This force is transverse to the velocity, producing a transverse acceleration:

$$a_y = \frac{e v_x B}{m_e}$$

Time spent in the magnetic field region:

$$t_p = \frac{\ell}{v_x}$$

Transverse velocity acquired at exit:

$$v_y = a_y \, t_p = \frac{e v_x B}{m_e} \cdot \frac{\ell}{v_x} = \frac{e B \ell}{m_e}$$

Transverse displacement at exit:

$$y_1 = \frac{1}{2} a_y t_p^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{e v_x B}{m_e} \cdot \frac{\ell^2}{v_x^2} = \frac{e B \ell^2}{2 m_e v_x}$$

Drift-region contribution over length $L$:

$$y_2 = v_y \cdot \frac{L}{v_x} = \frac{e B \ell L}{m_e v_x}$$

Total deflection at the screen:

$$Y = y_1 + y_2 = \frac{e B \ell}{m_e v_x}\left(\frac{\ell}{2} + L\right) \approx \frac{e B \ell L}{m_e v_x}$$

Substituting $v_x = \sqrt{2 e V_a / m_e}$:

$$Y = \frac{e B \ell L}{m_e} \sqrt{\frac{m_e}{2 e V_a}} = B \ell L \sqrt{\frac{e}{2 m_e V_a}}$$

$$\boxed{Y = B \, \ell \, L \sqrt{\frac{e}{2 m_e V_a}}}$$

**Magnetic Deflection Sensitivity:**

$$S_m = \frac{Y}{B} = \ell L \sqrt{\frac{e}{2 m_e V_a}}$$

**Comparison of Sensitivities:**

| Aspect | Electrostatic $S_e$ | Magnetic $S_m$ |
|--------|---------------------|------------------|
| Formula | $S_e = \ell L / (2 d V_a)$ | $S_m = \ell L \sqrt{e / (2 m_e V_a)}$ |
| $V_a$ dependence | $S_e \propto 1/V_a$ (linear) | $S_m \propto 1/\sqrt{V_a}$ (square-root) |
| At $V_a = 25$ kV | Drops sharply | Drops slowly |
| Frequency response | Excellent (capacitive plates) | Limited (coil inductance ≈ 1–10 mH) |
| Power consumption | Negligible (reactive) | High (resistive yoke current) |

**Key Engineering Conclusion:** Because $S_m$ decreases more slowly with $V_a$ than $S_e$, magnetic deflection is preferred for high-brightness, high-resolution TV tubes that require EHT > 20 kV. Electrostatic deflection is preferred for low-EHT, high-bandwidth applications (oscilloscopes).

**(7 marks: derivation 5, comparison table 2)**

---

#### **Q.B.(b) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(7 marks)**
**Explain the construction and working of the electron gun in a CRT. With the help of a neat sketch, show the various electrodes and their typical potentials.**

**Model Solution:**

The **electron gun** is the heart of a CRT. It produces a narrow, high-velocity, intensity-modulated beam of electrons. A typical electron gun (the **bipotential type**, most common in color CRTs) consists of the following electrodes in sequence:

**Construction and Typical Potentials:**

| Electrode | Function | Typical Potential (relative to cathode) |
|-----------|----------|----------------------------------------|
| **Heater (H)** | Raises cathode temperature to ≈ 1000 K via thermionic emission | 6.3 V AC, 0.3 A |
| **Cathode (K)** | Emits electrons from barium/strontium oxide coating | 0 V (reference) |
| **Control Grid (G1)** | Modulates beam current; biases negative relative to cathode | -30 V to -100 V (variable) |
| **Screen Grid (G2)** | Pre-accelerates electrons; limits grid-current effects | +400 V to +800 V |
| **Focusing Anode (G3 / A2)** | First lens of the bipotential electron lens | +5 kV to +8 kV (≈ 25–35% of EHT) |
| **Final Anode (G4 / A3)** | Final acceleration to full beam energy; forms second half of lens | +25 kV to +30 kV (EHT) |
| **Aquadag coating** | Conductive inner coating of the funnel — extends the accelerating field | Same as G4 |

**Working Principle — Step by Step:**

1. **Thermionic Emission:** The heater raises the oxide-coated cathode to ≈ 1000 K, releasing electrons via the Richardson–Dushman effect:

$$J = A_R T^2 e^{-W/(kT)}$$

For typical oxide cathodes, $W \approx 1.0$ eV, and at 1000 K this gives a current density of several A/cm².

2. **Beam Current Modulation:** The control grid G1, biased negatively, repels electrons back to the cathode. The more negative G1 is, the fewer electrons escape — the beam current (and therefore screen brightness) is controlled by the *video signal* applied to either G1 or the cathode (with G1 held at a fixed cutoff bias).

3. **Pre-Acceleration:** Electrons passing through G1 are accelerated by the positive G2 and converge toward the optical axis due to the electrostatic field geometry.

4. **Electron-Optical Focusing (Bipotential Lens):** The G3 and G4 electrodes form a **bipotential electrostatic lens**, analogous to a thick optical convex lens. The equipotential lines in the gap between G3 and G4 bend the electron trajectories, focusing the diverging electron cone into a near-parallel beam with a *crossover* (a sharp waist) at the screen.

5. **Final Acceleration:** Beyond G4, the beam coasts at constant velocity under the influence of the aquadag-coated funnel, eventually striking the phosphor screen at ≈ 30% of the speed of light.

**Why Bipotential?** A single-lens design cannot focus electrons at such high velocity without severe spherical aberration. The two-lens (bipotential) design reduces aberration and produces a smaller, sharper spot (≈ 0.2 mm diameter) at the screen.

**Sketch Description (refer to Section 4.3 of the notes for the complete Mermaid block diagram):**

The electron gun is a sequence of coaxial cylinders, with progressively higher potentials. The cathode is the leftmost (smallest diameter) cylinder; G2 is slightly larger; G3 is larger still; and G4 (the aquadag) is the entire inside of the funnel. The optical axis is the central horizontal line; electron trajectories originate from the cathode, cross the axis at the lens center, and emerge as a near-parallel beam moving rightward.

**(7 marks: electrode table 3, working 3, sketch description 1)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in CRT Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the relativistic limit.** Students frequently apply $v = \sqrt{2eV_a/m}$ at $V_a = 100$ kV (projection CRTs) without comment. The correct velocity should use relativistic mass, and the classical formula over-estimates $v$ by up to 30%. **Always state the assumption.**
>
> 2. **Confusing "deflection sensitivity" units.** It is **m/V** for electrostatic and **m/T** (or **m/(Wb·m⁻²)**) for magnetic, NOT m per V in both cases. Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this.
>
> 3. **Missing the $L \gg \ell$ approximation.** The complete formula $Y = (V_d \ell/2dV_a)(\ell/2 + L)$ should be reduced to $Y \approx V_d \ell L/(2dV_a)$ *with justification*. Writing the full formula without simplification loses 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the shadow mask's energy absorption.** When asked why CRTs are inefficient, students typically mention "heat loss in phosphor." They **must also mention** the shadow mask absorbing 80% of the beam energy in color CRTs. This is worth a full mark.
>
> 5. **Not identifying the specific phosphor.** P4, P22, P31, P39 are not interchangeable. Examiners expect the *exact* phosphor designation for the given application.
>
> 6. **Mixing up the deflection equations for $B$ and $E$.** A common error is writing $Y = eB\ell L/(m v_x)$ for magnetic but then forgetting to substitute $v_x$ in terms of $V_a$. The final answer must explicitly contain $V_a$, not $v_x$.
>
> 7. **Convergence vs. deflection confusion.** In color CRTs, the *convergence* yoke adjusts beam alignment (static + dynamic), while the *main deflection* yoke sweeps the converged beam. Students often describe both as "deflection" — examiners deduct 0.5 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

#### 🔑 Core Components
- ☐ **Electron gun** = cathode (oxide-coated) + grid G1 + G2/G3/G4 anodes
- ☐ **Cathode** emits electrons by **thermionic emission** (Richardson–Dushman)
- ☐ **G1 is negative** relative to cathode → controls brightness
- ☐ **Aquadag coating** on inner funnel wall = final anode
- ☐ **EHT = 20–30 kV** for color TV CRTs

#### ⚡ Key Physics
- ☐ $v = \sqrt{2eV_a/m_e}$ (non-relativistic)
- ☐ Electrostatic deflection: $Y = V_d \ell L / (2dV_a)$
- ☐ Magnetic deflection: $Y = B \ell L \sqrt{e/(2mV_a)}$
- ☐ $S_e \propto 1/V_a$; $S_m \propto 1/\sqrt{V_a}$
- ☐ Lorentz force $F = evB \sin\theta$ is always perpendicular to $\vec{v}$ — no work done, no change in speed

#### 🎨 Color CRT Specifics
- ☐ Three electron guns (R, G, B)
- ☐ **Shadow mask** has 200,000–400,000 holes
- ☐ Phosphor **dot triads** (not stripes in delta-gun designs)
- ☐ Shadow mask absorbs ≈ **80%** of beam energy → major heat source
- ☐ **Convergence** = aligning 3 beams to pass through one mask hole
- ☐ **Purity** = ensuring each beam hits only its own phosphor color
- ☐ **Trinitron** uses an **aperture grille** (vertical wires) instead of a mask — no vertical absorption

#### 📺 Phosphors to Memorize
- ☐ **P4** → White, monochrome TV
- ☐ **P22** → RGB color triad
- ☐ **P31** → Green, oscilloscopes (short persistence)
- ☐ **P39** → Yellow-green, radar (long persistence)

#### 🧮 Engineering Trade-offs
- ☐ **Electrostatic**: oscilloscopes (high bandwidth)
- ☐ **Magnetic**: TVs/monitors (short neck, wide deflection angle)
- ☐ Higher EHT → sharper spot but **lower deflection sensitivity**
- ☐ Higher beam current → brighter picture but reduced phosphor life

#### 🏛️ Historical / Contextual
- ☐ Invented by **Karl Ferdinand Braun**, 1897
- ☐ Replaced by LCD (TFT) starting ≈ 2000, fully obsolete ≈ 2010
- ☐ Still used in some **oscilloscopes** and **aviation cockpit displays** (high reliability, no pixelation, true blacks)
- ☐ Same electron-optics physics underlies **TEM, SEM, e-beam lithography**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Principle of Plasma Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Principle of Plasma Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Plasma Display Panel (PDP)** is a flat-panel emissive display technology in which each picture element (pixel) is essentially a microscopic **gas-discharge cell** (also called a Plasma Cell) containing a low-pressure noble-gas mixture (typically **Neon + Xenon** or **Helium + Xenon**). When a sufficient **alternating voltage** is applied across a pair of electrodes, the gas ionizes into a **plasma state** (the 4th state of matter), emitting **ultraviolet (UV) photons**. These UV photons strike a **phosphor coating** on the cell walls, causing the phosphor to fluoresce and emit **visible light** (Red, Green, or Blue) corresponding to the color of that sub-pixel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A Plasma Display is fundamentally an array of millions of tiny, individually controlled **neon-style fluorescent lamps**, fabricated between two glass plates and addressed using a matrix-electrode scheme similar to that of LCDs, but where the light is **emitted** (not modulated like LCD).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine an entire wall of **millions of tiny neon-sign tubes**, each one smaller than a grain of sand, sandwiched between two glass plates. Now imagine a circuit that can switch on or off any single one of those tubes in a fraction of a millisecond, and even adjust its brightness 256 times within a single TV frame.

- The **neon gas** in the tube is what makes real neon signs glow orange.
- In a Plasma TV, the gas is **Ne + Xe** (or He + Xe), and the glow is in the **ultraviolet** range, which is invisible to our eyes.
- A **phosphor coating** (similar to the white powder inside a fluorescent tube light) absorbs this invisible UV light and re-emits it as the **visible Red, Green, or Blue** light we actually see on the screen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Clarification:** A plasma display is **not** a "plasma" like in a star (super-hot fusion plasma). It is a **cold plasma** — a partially ionized gas at near-room temperature, where free electrons are accelerated by an electric field and collide with neutral atoms, exciting them and producing light.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following constants and metrics are standard for KTU 2024 numerical/analytical questions:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| Plasma gas mixture | **Ne + Xe (1–10% Xe)** or He + Xe | Determines UV emission wavelength |
| Xe UV emission lines | **147 nm** and **173 nm** | Vacuum-UV; excite phosphors |
| Cell pressure | **200–500 Torr** (~0.3–0.7 atm) | Below atmospheric for easier ionization |
| Drive voltage (sustain) | **170–200 V (rms)** | AC sustain pulses |
| Address voltage | **60–100 V** | DC write pulse |
| Operating temperature | **−20 °C to +40 °C** | Wide thermal range |
| Typical response time | **< 1 µs** | Much faster than LCD |
| Pixel pitch (HD TV) | **0.48 mm – 0.90 mm** | 42"–65" screens |
| Discharge current | **0.1 – 1 mA per cell** | Low per cell, but millions add up |
| Phosphor | Zn₂SiO₄:Mn (green), Y₂O₃:Eu (red), BaMgAl₁₀O₁₇:Eu (blue) | Visible emission |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of a Plasma Display Panel

A PDP is a **sandwich structure** with two parallel glass plates, separated by a rib (barrier) structure that isolates individual cells.

**Top Plate (Front/Viewing Side):**
- Front glass substrate
- Transparent **Indium Tin Oxide (ITO)** electrodes (sustain electrodes – X electrodes)
- Bus electrodes (Cr-Cu-Cr or Ag) for conductivity
- Transparent dielectric layer (PbO-based glass, ~30 µm)
- **MgO protective layer** (~0.5 µm) — critical for low-voltage operation and long life

**Bottom Plate (Rear):**
- Rear glass substrate
- **Address electrodes (Y electrodes)** running perpendicular to the sustain electrodes
- White reflective dielectric layer
- **Barrier ribs** (typically 0.1–0.2 mm height) — partition the panel into individual cells
- **Phosphor coatings** (R, G, B) on the sidewalls and bottom of each cell

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "From Light → To Light":** UV photon → strikes phosphor → phosphor emits visible light. The plasma does NOT produce visible light directly.

### 2.2 Operational Phases (Address-Display Separation – ADS)

KTU 2024 frequently tests the **ADS sub-field driving scheme**, which divides one TV frame (16.7 ms for 60 Hz) into **8 sub-fields**, each having an **initialization, addressing, and sustain period**.

**Phase 1 — Initialization (Reset):** All cells are brought to a uniform state using a weak discharge, ensuring each cell has the same starting wall-charge condition. This eliminates history effects.

**Phase 2 — Addressing (Write):** A data pulse is applied selectively to the cells that must be ON. Wall charges accumulate only in selected cells.

**Phase 3 — Sustain (Display):** A continuous train of AC sustain pulses (~200 V, ~50 kHz) is applied to ALL cells. Only cells that received address wall charges will discharge; unaddressed cells remain dark.

**Brightness Control:** Each sub-field has a different number of sustain pulses, weighted in binary:
- Sub-field 1 → 1 pulse
- Sub-field 2 → 2 pulses
- Sub-field 3 → 4 pulses
- …
- Sub-field 8 → 128 pulses
- Total = 255 combinations → **8-bit gray scale (256 levels per color)**

### 2.3 Why "Plasma"? — The Physics of the Discharge

1. **Voltage application** across electrodes creates an electric field $\vec{E} = V/d$ (where $d$ is electrode gap, typically 0.1 mm).
2. **Seed electrons** (from cosmic rays or MgO secondary emission) accelerate under $\vec{E}$.
3. **Electron-impact ionization:** $e^- + \text{Xe} \rightarrow 2e^- + \text{Xe}^+$ (Townsend avalanche).
4. **Excimer formation:** $\text{Xe}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{Xe}^*$
5. **Excimer radiation:** $\text{Xe}^* \rightarrow \text{Xe} + h\nu_{UV}$ (147 nm or 173 nm photon).
6. **Phosphor absorption:** $h\nu_{UV} + \text{Phosphor} \rightarrow \text{Phosphor}^*$
7. **Visible emission:** $\text{Phosphor}^* \rightarrow \text{Phosphor} + h\nu_{visible}$ (R, G, or B).

### 2.4 Types of Plasma Displays

| Feature | **DC-PDP** | **AC-PDP** |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Electrode coupling | Direct contact with gas | Dielectric-covered |
| Discharge type | DC arc | AC surface discharge |
| Memory function | No (no wall charges) | Yes (wall charges persist) |
| Drive scheme | Simpler | ADS sub-field |
| Lifespan | Lower | **Higher (commercial standard)** |
| Efficiency | Lower | **Higher** |
| Used in | Early prototypes | **All modern Plasma TVs** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All commercial Plasma TVs (Panasonic, LG, Samsung) use the **AC-PDP** architecture with MgO cathode layer and sub-field driving.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles every formula a KTU 2024 student must know for numerical/analytical questions on Plasma Displays. Use `\vert` for absolute values.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_b = \dfrac{B \cdot p \cdot d}{\ln(A \cdot p \cdot d) - \ln\!\left(\ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)\right)}$ | **Paschen's Law** — gas breakdown voltage | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V_b \approx f(p \cdot d)$ — minimum at $p \cdot d \approx 1 \text{ Torr·cm}$ | Paschen minimum | V |
| 3 | $E_{photon} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | UV photon energy (Xe: $\lambda = 147 \text{ nm}$) | Joules (J) |
| 4 | $E_{photon} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ | Same formula in eV·nm units | eV |
| 5 | $\tau_{response} \approx \dfrac{1}{\alpha \cdot v_d}$ | Discharge response time | seconds (s) |
| 6 | $L_{gray} = 2^{N_{sf}} - 1$ | Gray levels with $N_{sf}$ sub-fields | integer |
| 7 | $N_{sustain} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{sf}} b_i \cdot 2^{i-1}$ | Total sustain pulses in one frame | integer |
| 8 | $P_{panel} = N_{cells} \cdot V_s \cdot I_{dis} \cdot \eta_{duty}$ | Power consumption estimate | Watts |
| 9 | $\eta_{Xe} = \dfrac{N_{UV}}{N_{e^-}}$ | Xenon UV conversion efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_{cell} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_{gap}}$ | Cell capacitance (AC-PDP) | Farads (F) |
| 11 | $Q_{wall} = C_{cell} \cdot V_{wall}$ | Wall charge stored per discharge | Coulombs (C) |
| 12 | $f_{sustain} = \dfrac{1}{T_{frame}}}{2 \cdot N_{pulses,max}}$ | Sustain pulse frequency | Hz |

**Real-world Engineering Utility:** Paschen's Law directly determines the **sustain voltage** for a given cell geometry. PDP manufacturers tune the **Xe partial pressure** and **electrode gap** to operate near the Paschen minimum, minimizing drive electronics cost and power consumption.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: UV Photon Energy from Xenon Emission

The dominant UV emission line in Ne+Xe plasma is at **$\lambda = 147 \text{ nm}$** (vacuum-UV). Calculate the energy of one such photon and confirm it can excite the green phosphor (bandgap ≈ 6.5 eV).

**Step 1 — Recall the Planck-Einstein relation:**

$$
E_{photon} = h \cdot \nu = \dfrac{h \cdot c}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert standard constants in SI units:**

- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
- $c = 3.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$
- $\lambda = 147 \text{ nm} = 147 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$

**Step 3 — Substitute and evaluate:**

$$
E_{photon} = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \cdot (3.0 \times 10^{8})}{147 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator calculation:**

$$
h \cdot c = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \cdot 3.0 \times 10^{8} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Final division:**

$$
E_{photon} = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{147 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.352 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to electron-volts** (1 eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J):

$$
E_{photon} = \dfrac{1.352 \times 10^{-18}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 8.44 \text{ eV}
$$

**Step 7 — Verification:** Since $8.44 \text{ eV} > 6.5 \text{ eV}$ (phosphor bandgap), the UV photon has **sufficient energy** to excite the green phosphor. $\checkmark$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Higher Xe content raises UV intensity but increases breakdown voltage. Commercial PDPs optimize at **4–8% Xe** for the best luminous efficiency.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Paschen's Law for PDP Cell Design

A PDP cell has electrode gap $d = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 0.01 \text{ cm}$ and uses Ne+Xe (5% Xe) at total pressure $p = 400 \text{ Torr}$. For pure Ne, the Paschen constants are $A = 4 \text{ (Torr·cm)}^{-1}$, $B = 100 \text{ V/(Torr·cm)}$, and secondary emission coefficient $\gamma_{se} = 0.1$. Find the breakdown voltage.

**Step 1 — Compute $p \cdot d$:**

$$
p \cdot d = 400 \text{ Torr} \times 0.01 \text{ cm} = 4 \text{ Torr·cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A \cdot p \cdot d$:**

$$
A \cdot p \cdot d = 4 \times 4 = 16
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inner logarithmic term:**

$$
\ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right) = \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{0.1}\right) = \ln(11) = 2.398
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\ln(2.398)$:**

$$
\ln(2.398) = 0.8744
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator of Paschen's Law:**

$$
\ln(16) - 0.8744 = 2.773 - 0.8744 = 1.898
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
B \cdot p \cdot d = 100 \times 4 = 400 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 7 — Final breakdown voltage:**

$$
V_b = \dfrac{400}{1.898} = 210.7 \text{ V}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This calculated value (~210 V) **matches the experimentally observed sustain voltage** of commercial AC-PDPs (170–200 V). The slight difference is due to MgO's high secondary emission coefficient (γ ≈ 0.5), which is why MgO coating is essential.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Gray-Scale Calculation Using ADS Sub-fields

A 50-inch 1080p Plasma TV uses **8 sub-fields** in ADS driving. Calculate:
(a) The number of gray levels per color.
(b) The maximum number of sustain pulses per frame.
(c) The frame time at 60 Hz.

**Part (a) — Gray levels:**

$$
L_{gray} = 2^{N_{sf}} - 1 = 2^{8} - 1 = 255 \text{ levels}
$$

So **8-bit color depth** per primary → 16.7 million colors total.

**Part (b) — Maximum sustain pulses:**

$$
N_{sustain,max} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} 2^{i-1} = 1 + 2 + 4 + 8 + 16 + 32 + 64 + 128 = 255 \text{ pulses}
$$

**Part (c) — Frame time at 60 Hz:**

$$
T_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{60} = 16.67 \text{ ms}
$$

Time per sustain pulse (idealized, equal allocation):

$$
T_{pulse} = \dfrac{16.67 \text{ ms}}{255} \approx 65.4 \text{ µs}
$$

In practice, the **address period** consumes additional time (~30–40% of frame), so sustain pulse time is reduced to ~30–40 µs.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: PDP vs Other Display Technologies

| Parameter | **PDP** | **LCD (IPS/VA)** | **OLED** |
|-----------|---------|------------------|----------|
| Light generation | Emissive (gas discharge) | Backlight + modulator | Emissive (organic) |
| Response time | **< 1 µs** | 5–10 ms | < 0.1 ms |
| Contrast ratio | 30,000 : 1 (native) | 1,000 : 1 (IPS) | 1,000,000 : 1 |
| Viewing angle | **180° (perfect)** | 178° (IPS) | 180° |
| Peak luminance | 100–150 cd/m² | 400–1000 cd/m² | 600–1000 cd/m² |
| Power @ 50" | **High** (~300 W) | Low (~80 W) | Medium |
| Panel size capability | Up to **150"** | Practical up to 100" | Up to 88" |
| Burn-in risk | Moderate | None | High |
| Manufacturing cost | High (50–65") | Low | High |
| Status (2024) | **Discontinued** | Dominant | Premium segment |

> [!NOTE]
> Although **discontinued for consumer TVs** (last Panasonic plant closed 2021), PDPs remain conceptually important for KTU 2024 syllabus and as a reference for understanding **emissive flat-panel displays**.
