## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Define a Plasma Display Panel. Why is the gas mixture predominantly Neon with a small percentage of Xenon?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Plasma Display Panel (PDP)** is a flat-panel emissive display where each pixel is a **gas-discharge cell** containing a low-pressure noble gas mixture, sandwiched between two glass plates with orthogonal electrodes. UV photons from gas excitation cause phosphor coatings to emit visible R, G, B light.

Neon is used as the **majority gas** because:
- It has a **low ionization potential** (~21.6 eV) — easy to ionize at moderate voltages.
- It produces stable, reproducible discharges and provides Penning ionization enhancement.
- Xenon is added in small % (4–8%) because it produces the desired **resonant UV emission at 147 nm** that efficiently excites the phosphors.
- Pure Xe is too easily ionized, leading to unstable discharges.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** What is the function of the MgO layer in a Plasma Display Panel?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **MgO (Magnesium Oxide)** layer, deposited (~0.5 µm) over the dielectric on the front plate, serves three critical functions:
1. **High secondary electron emission coefficient** ($\gamma_{se} \approx 0.5$), which dramatically **reduces the sustain voltage** from ~300 V to ~200 V.
2. **Protection of the dielectric layer** from ion bombardment sputtering, extending panel lifespan (>100,000 hours).
3. **Lower work function** than glass dielectric, ensuring consistent discharge initiation and reducing **jitter** (statistical delay) during addressing.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the construction of an AC-type Plasma Display Panel with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of barrier ribs and phosphors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a timing diagram, explain the **Address-Display Separation (ADS)** sub-field driving method used in Plasma Displays. How are 256 gray levels achieved? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Construction of AC-PDP (7 marks):**

An AC-PDP consists of two glass plates sealed together with a **gas-filled gap** of ~0.1–0.2 mm between them.

**Front plate** (viewer's side) contains:
- **Sustain electrodes (X):** Transparent **ITO (Indium Tin Oxide)** strips running horizontally.
- **Bus electrodes:** Narrow metallic strips (Cr/Cu/Cr or Ag) overlaid on ITO to reduce resistance.
- **Dielectric layer:** Transparent lead-oxide-based glass (~30 µm) covers electrodes — it allows AC coupling and stores **wall charges**.
- **MgO protective layer** (~0.5 µm) for low-voltage operation.

**Rear plate** contains:
- **Address electrodes (Y):** Running perpendicular to the X electrodes, forming an **X-Y matrix**.
- **White dielectric** with high reflectivity.
- **Barrier ribs:** Parallel walls (typically striped) of height 100–200 µm made of frit glass or photosensitive glass. They serve to:
  1. **Isolate** adjacent cells optically and electrically (prevent cross-talk).
  2. Provide **mechanical support** between the two plates.
  3. Increase the surface area for phosphor coating.
- **Phosphor coatings:** Three phosphors for R, G, B sub-pixels are deposited by screen-printing or photo-lithography on the **sidewalls and bottom** of each cell. The most common phosphors are:
  - **Red:** Y₂O₃:Eu³⁺
  - **Green:** Zn₂SiO₄:Mn²⁺
  - **Blue:** BaMgAl₁₀O₁₇:Eu²⁺

The two plates are sealed with **low-melting-point frit glass** at ~450 °C, then evacuated and back-filled with **Ne + Xe (or He + Xe)** mixture at 200–500 Torr.

**Part (b) — ADS Sub-field Driving (7 marks):**

In the ADS method, the single TV frame ($T_f$ = 16.67 ms at 60 Hz) is divided into **8 sub-fields (SF1 to SF8)**. Each sub-field has three phases:

1. **Reset (Initialization):** A small ramped voltage is applied to all cells, creating a **weak discharge** that equalizes the wall-charge state of every cell. This ensures uniform starting conditions regardless of previous frame data.

2. **Address (Write):** A data pulse is applied to the **Y (address) electrode** of selected cells, in synchronization with a **scan pulse** on the X electrodes. This selective write creates a wall-charge pattern only in the cells to be turned ON.

3. **Sustain (Display):** A continuous train of **AC sustain pulses** (~200 V, square wave) is applied between X and Y of all cells. Cells with stored wall charges undergo discharge (emit UV → phosphor → visible light). Cells without wall charges remain OFF. The number of sustain pulses per sub-field is binary-weighted:

$$
N_i = 2^{i-1} \quad (i = 1 \text{ to } 8)
$$

So sustain pulse counts: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128 (total = 255).

**256 gray levels** are produced by selecting which combination of sub-fields are turned ON for each pixel. For example:
- Gray level 0 = 00000000 (no SF ON)
- Gray level 100 = 01100100 (SF3 + SF6 + SF7 = 4+32+64 = 100)
- Gray level 255 = 11111111 (all SF ON)

This binary-weighted pulse-count modulation produces **8-bit color depth** per primary, i.e., 16.7 million colors overall.

---

> **Question B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternate Choice]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **basic principle of operation of a Plasma Display Panel**. Discuss the role of gas ionization, UV generation, and phosphor fluorescence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Paschen's Law**? A PDP cell has an electrode gap of 0.08 cm filled with Ne at 500 Torr. Using Paschen constants A = 4 cm⁻¹Torr⁻¹, B = 100 V/(cm·Torr), and γₛₑ = 0.05, calculate the **breakdown voltage**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Basic Principle of Operation (7 marks):**

The Plasma Display Panel operates on the principle of **gas discharge and photoluminescence**, where the light output is produced in two stages:

**Stage 1 — Gas Discharge and UV Photon Generation:**

When a sustain voltage (typically 170–200 V) is applied between the X and Y electrodes of a PDP cell, an electric field is established in the gas gap:
$$
E = \dfrac{V_s}{d_{gap}} \approx \dfrac{200 \text{ V}}{0.01 \text{ cm}} = 20 \text{ kV/cm}
$$
This strong field accelerates **seed electrons** (originating from cosmic rays, MgO secondary emission, or residual ionization) to high kinetic energies. These energetic electrons undergo **inelastic collisions** with Ne and Xe atoms, causing:
- **Excitation:** $e^- + \text{Xe} \rightarrow e^- + \text{Xe}^*$ (Xe raised to an excited state)
- **Ionization:** $e^- + \text{Xe} \rightarrow 2e^- + \text{Xe}^+$

Through a series of three-body collisions, **excimers** (excited dimers) are formed:
$$
\text{Xe}^* + 2\text{Xe} \rightarrow \text{Xe}_2^* + \text{Xe}
$$

The excimer is unstable and quickly dissociates, releasing its bond energy as a **vacuum-ultraviolet (VUV) photon**:
$$
\text{Xe}_2^* \rightarrow 2\text{Xe} + h\nu \quad (\lambda = 147 \text{ nm or } 173 \text{ nm})
$$

**Stage 2 — Phosphor Fluorescence (Visible Light Emission):**

The VUV photons (8.4 eV energy) strike the **phosphor coatings** on the cell walls. The phosphor absorbs this high-energy photon, exciting its electrons to a higher energy state. When the electrons relax back to the ground state, they emit a photon of **lower energy** (Stokes shift), which falls in the visible spectrum:
$$
\text{Phosphor} + h\nu_{UV} \rightarrow \text{Phosphor}^* \rightarrow \text{Phosphor} + h\nu_{visible}
$$
- Red phosphor Y₂O₃:Eu³⁺ emits at **611 nm**
- Green phosphor Zn₂SiO₄:Mn²⁺ emits at **530 nm**
- Blue phosphor BaMgAl₁₀O₁₇:Eu²⁺ emits at **450 nm**

By independently controlling the brightness of the R, G, B sub-pixels, the PDP produces **full-color images** through additive color mixing.

**Part (b) — Paschen's Law and Breakdown Voltage (7 marks):**

**Paschen's Law Statement:** The breakdown voltage $V_b$ of a gas between two parallel electrodes is a function only of the product $p \cdot d$ (gas pressure × electrode gap distance) and the gas composition. It is given by:

$$
V_b = \dfrac{B \cdot p \cdot d}{\ln(A \cdot p \cdot d) - \ln\!\left(\ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\gamma_{se}}\right)\right)}
$$

The law exhibits a **minimum** at $p \cdot d \approx 1 \text{ Torr·cm}$ for most gases; below or above this, $V_b$ increases sharply. This is the famous **Paschen minimum**.

**Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Electrode gap: $d = 0.08 \text{ cm}$
- Pressure: $p = 500 \text{ Torr}$
- $A = 4 \text{ cm}^{-1}\text{Torr}^{-1}$, $B = 100 \text{ V/(cm·Torr)}$, $\gamma_{se} = 0.05$

**Step 1:** $p \cdot d = 500 \times 0.08 = 40 \text{ Torr·cm}$

**Step 2:** $A \cdot p \cdot d = 4 \times 40 = 160$

**Step 3:** $1/\gamma_{se} = 1/0.05 = 20$; $1 + 1/\gamma_{se} = 21$

**Step 4:** $\ln(21) = 3.045$

**Step 5:** $\ln(3.045) = 1.114$

**Step 6:** Denominator $= \ln(160) - 1.114 = 5.075 - 1.114 = 3.961$

**Step 7:** Numerator $= B \cdot p \cdot d = 100 \times 40 = 4000 \text{ V}$

**Step 8:** 
$$
V_b = \dfrac{4000}{3.961} = 1009.8 \text{ V} \approx 1010 \text{ V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A breakdown voltage of ~1010 V for pure Ne is significantly higher than that of the commercial Ne+Xe mixture (~200 V) due to **Penning ionization** in Ne+Xe, where Ne metastables ionize Xe (Penning effect), drastically lowering the effective ionization threshold. The MgO layer further reduces $V_b$ through secondary electron emission.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "plasma" with fusion plasma:** Many students write "high temperature plasma like the sun." This loses 1 mark. Always specify **"cold, low-pressure gas discharge plasma."**
> 2. **Saying "plasma produces visible light":** Wrong! Plasma produces **UV light**; **phosphor** produces visible light. Losing this distinction costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the role of MgO:** This is a **favorite 2–3 mark question** in KTU. Mention all three functions: secondary emission, protection, low-voltage operation.
> 4. **Numerical errors in Paschen's Law:** Most students forget the **inner logarithm** term $\ln(\ln(1+1/\gamma_{se}))$ and get wrong answers. Memorize the full formula.
> 5. **Confusing AC-PDP and DC-PDP:** Students often write "DC-PDP has memory function." This is wrong — only **AC-PDP has memory function** (via wall charges).
> 6. **Skipping sub-field weighting:** In ADS questions, always list the binary weights (1, 2, 4, ..., 128) and show the total = 255.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plasma Displays**

- ✅ Plasma Display = array of millions of **microscopic gas-discharge cells** with phosphor coatings.
- ✅ Plasma = **cold, partially ionized gas** (NOT fusion plasma); 4th state of matter.
- ✅ Gas mixture: **Ne + Xe (4–8%)** at 200–500 Torr; sometimes He + Xe.
- ✅ Light path: **Gas → UV photon (147/173 nm) → Phosphor → Visible light (R/G/B)**.
- ✅ Phosphors: Y₂O₃:Eu (R), Zn₂SiO₄:Mn (G), BaMgAl₁₀O₁₇:Eu (B).
- ✅ **AC-PDP** uses dielectric-covered electrodes and has **memory function**; commercial standard.
- ✅ **MgO layer** reduces sustain voltage to ~200 V and extends panel life.
- ✅ **Barrier ribs** isolate cells, prevent cross-talk, support glass plates.
- ✅ **ADS sub-field driving** with 8 sub-fields gives **256 gray levels** (8-bit color).
- ✅ Sustain pulse weighting: **1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128** = 255 total pulses per frame.
- ✅ **Paschen's Law:** $V_b = f(p \cdot d)$; minimum at $p \cdot d \approx 1$ Torr·cm.
- ✅ Advantages: **Perfect viewing angle (180°), very fast response (<1 µs), high contrast, large sizes.**
- ✅ Disadvantages: **High power consumption, phosphor aging, screen burn-in, heavy panel, lower brightness than LCD.**
- ✅ Discontinued for consumer TVs (2021) but **principles remain essential for KTU syllabus** and form the basis for understanding other emissive displays (OLED, micro-LED).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LC Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** LC Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)** is a flat-panel display technology that utilizes the unique electro-optical properties of **liquid crystal (LC) molecules** — which exhibit intermediate phases of matter between conventional liquids and solid crystals — to modulate light transmission or reflection. By selectively controlling the orientation of these rod-shaped organic molecules through applied electric fields, LCDs regulate the passage of polarized light through a matrix of picture elements (**pixels**), thereby forming visible images without emitting light intrinsically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> An LCD is a **passive (non-emissive) electro-optic modulator** in which an array of pixels, each containing a thin layer (typically **3 µm to 10 µm**) of nematic liquid crystal sandwiched between two transparent electrodes and alignment layers, is used to spatially modulate an external illumination source (backlight or ambient light) to produce images for entertainment and information display.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Venetian Blind" of Light:**
> 
> Imagine a window fitted with **two sets of controllable venetian blinds** placed back-to-back. Each set can rotate its slats independently. When both sets are aligned in a particular configuration, light from outside passes through and lights the room. When their slats are twisted by 90° relative to each other, no light passes. Now, imagine an electrical knob that can rotate one set of slats at will — the moment you turn the knob, the room either brightens or darkens. This is *exactly* how a single LCD pixel works: the **liquid crystal layer** acts as the rotatable "blinds," and the **two polarizers** act as the fixed entrance/exit gates for light.

A more relatable analogy: Think of LC molecules as **thousands of tiny transparent pencils** lying flat in a long box. They can be made to **"stand up" or "lie down"** by applying a small voltage. When standing, they block polarized light; when lying, they twist/rotate it. The polarizers act as **security gates** that only allow light in a specific orientation to pass.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Engineering Constants (Bolded)

- **Refractive index of LC material (ordinary axis):** $n_o \approx 1.5$ to $1.6$
- **Refractive index of LC material (extraordinary axis):** $n_e \approx 1.7$ to $1.8$
- **Birefringence ($\Delta n$):** $\Delta n = n_e - n_o \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$
- **Cell gap (d):** typically **3 µm – 10 µm**
- **Optical anisotropy:** the LC is a **uniaxial birefringent** medium
- **Operating voltage (RMS):** typically **3 V – 5 V** per pixel
- **Response time (modern TFT-LCD):** **1 ms – 16 ms** (gray-to-gray or black-to-white)
- **Standard refresh rate for entertainment LCDs:** **60 Hz, 120 Hz, 144 Hz, 240 Hz**

## 1.4 Classification of LCDs (KTU Module 4 Scope)

| Family | Full Form | Application in Entertainment |
|---|---|---|
| **TN-LCD** | Twisted Nematic | Low-cost monitors, calculator displays |
| **STN-LCD** | Super-Twisted Nematic | Early laptops, monochrome TVs |
| **IPS-LCD** | In-Plane Switching | Smartphones, premium TVs, professional monitors |
| **VA-LCD** | Vertical Alignment | High-contrast TVs, curved displays |
| **FFS/PLS** | Fringe-Field Switching / Plane-to-Line Switching | Touchscreen displays |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Mesophases of Liquid Crystals

Liquid crystals are organic compounds exhibiting **mesophases** — phases between the solid (crystalline) and liquid (isotropic) states:

1. **Smectic (Sm):** Molecules arranged in well-defined layers; quasi-solid order.
2. **Nematic (N):** Molecules aligned along a common **director ($\hat{n}$)** but with no positional order. *Most widely used in displays.*
3. **Cholesteric / Chiral Nematic (N*):** Nematic phase with a helical twist of the director; used in reflective displays.
4. **Discotic:** Disc-shaped molecules stacked in columns; emerging for OLED-like applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Nematic LCs Dominate Displays:**
> Nematic LCs have **low viscosity (fast response)** and **strong dielectric anisotropy ($\Delta \varepsilon$)**, which means an applied voltage can reorient the molecules predictably. Smectic phases are too viscous, and cholesteric phases have memory effects unsuitable for fast video-rate modulation.

## 2.2 Working Principle — The Twisted Nematic (TN) Cell

The **TN-LCD** is the foundational architecture. Its operation is described in five sequential stages:

**Step 1 — Polarized Light Entry**
- The **front polarizer** (P1) admits only the component of backlight oscillating along its transmission axis (say, the **x-direction**).

**Step 2 — Liquid Crystal Twist (90°)**
- The LC molecules are aligned by **mechanically buffed polyimide layers** such that the director $\hat{n}$ twists by exactly **90°** between the front and rear substrates.
- The incident **x-polarized** light follows the rotating director (via **waveguiding / adiabatic following**), emerging as **y-polarized** light at the rear substrate.

**Step 3 — Analyzer (Rear Polarizer P2)**
- The rear polarizer (P2) is oriented with its axis at **90° to P1** (i.e., along **y**). Since the light is now y-polarized, it **passes through P2 unimpeded**.
- **Result:** Bright pixel — **"ON" state"**.

**Step 4 — Voltage Application (OFF State)**
- When a voltage $V > V_{th}$ (Freedericksz threshold) is applied, the LC molecules **stand up** along the field (homeotropic alignment). They no longer rotate the polarization.
- The x-polarized light reaches P2 unchanged and is **absorbed by the analyzer**.
- **Result:** Dark pixel — **"OFF" state**.

**Step 5 — Brightness Modulation (Gray Levels)**
- By varying the **RMS voltage** between $V_{th}$ and $V_{sat}$, the LC director tilt angle is partially set, yielding intermediate polarization rotation and **continuous gray levels**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning / Use** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta n = n_e - n_o$ | Optical birefringence of LC; sets retardation |
| 2 | $\delta = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot \Delta n \cdot d}{\lambda}$ | Phase retardation in LC cell ($\lambda$ = wavelength) |
| 3 | $\tau_{on} \propto \dfrac{\gamma_1 \cdot d^2}{\Delta \varepsilon \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot V^2 - K \pi^2}$ | Rise time (flow-based switching) |
| 4 | $\tau_{off} \propto \dfrac{\gamma_1 \cdot d^2}{K \pi^2}$ | Decay time (elastic relaxation) |
| 5 | $V_{th} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \Delta \varepsilon}}$ | **Freedericksz threshold voltage** |
| 6 | $C_{pixel} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_{LC} \cdot A}{d}$ | Pixel capacitance (important for charge retention) |
| 7 | $T_{transmission} = \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi \cdot \Delta n \cdot d}{\lambda}\right)$ | Gooch-Tarry transmission curve (TN cell) |
| 8 | $R_{c} = \dfrac{\tau_{on} + \tau_{off}}{2}$ | Average LC response time |
| 9 | $I_{transmitted} = I_0 \cos^2(\chi)$ | Malus's law ($\chi$ = angle between polarizer & analyzer) |
| 10 | $\Delta \varepsilon = \varepsilon_{\parallel} - \varepsilon_{\perp}$ | Dielectric anisotropy — drives molecular reorientation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where to Use These in Real Engineering:**
> - **Formula 5 ($V_{th}$):** Determines **driver IC voltage rails** (e.g., a 5 V logic driver must overcome $V_{th} \approx 1.5$–$2$ V).
> - **Formula 2 ($\delta$):** Used to **design retarder films** for wide-viewing-angle compensation in IPS/VA panels.
> - **Formula 7:** Used in **optical modeling** to determine the optimal cell gap for maximum transmittance in TN cells — known as the **Gooch-Tarry minima**.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of LCDs

LCDs are the **workhorse of the entertainment industry** because they offer:
- **High spatial resolution** (4K = 3840×2160, 8K = 7680×4320 achievable).
- **Scalability** from **0.5-inch smartwatches** to **85-inch TVs**.
- **Low power consumption** (40–200 W for 55" TV vs. 300+ W for plasma equivalents).
- **Long operational lifetime** (50,000–100,000 hours).
- **Compatibility with TFT backplanes** enabling active-matrix addressing.

However, they require a **backlight**, which limits true black levels (improved with **local dimming** and **mini-LED** / **dual-cell** architectures).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Freedericksz Threshold Voltage

The **Freedericksz transition** is the foundational threshold for LC reorientation. We derive $V_{th}$ below.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The free energy density of a nematic LC in an applied electric field is given by:

$$
f = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[K_{11}(\nabla \cdot \hat{n})^2 + K_{22}(\hat{n} \cdot \nabla \times \hat{n})^2 + K_{33}(\hat{n} \times \nabla \times \hat{n})^2\right] - \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \Delta \varepsilon (\hat{n} \cdot \vec{E})^2
$$

**Step 1 — Simplify for the Standard Geometry**
Assume a planar cell with strong anchoring, the LC director tilts only in the x-z plane. Define the tilt angle as $\theta(z)$. The elastic energy reduces to a single Frank constant $K$ (assuming equal splay, twist, and bend for simplicity):

$$
f_{elastic} = \tfrac{1}{2} K \left(\dfrac{d\theta}{dz}\right)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Add the Dielectric Energy**
With field $\vec{E} = (0, 0, E)$ and director $\hat{n} = (\sin\theta, 0, \cos\theta)$:

$$
f_{dielectric} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \Delta \varepsilon E^2 \cos^2\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Euler-Lagrange Equation**
Minimization of total free energy with boundary conditions $\theta(0) = \theta(d) = 0$:

$$
K \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dz^2} + \varepsilon_0 \Delta \varepsilon E^2 \sin\theta \cos\theta = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Linearize Near Threshold**
For small $\theta$, $\sin\theta\cos\theta \approx \theta$:

$$
K \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dz^2} + \varepsilon_0 \Delta \varepsilon E^2 \theta = 0
$$

The solution is $\theta(z) = \theta_0 \sin(\pi z / d)$ to satisfy boundary conditions. Substituting:

$$
-K \dfrac{\pi^2}{d^2} \theta_0 + \varepsilon_0 \Delta \varepsilon E^2 \theta_0 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Critical Field**

$$
E_{th}^2 = \dfrac{K \pi^2}{\varepsilon_0 \Delta \varepsilon d^2}
$$

Converting to voltage: $V_{th} = E_{th} \cdot d$:

$$
\boxed{V_{th} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \Delta \varepsilon}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** $V_{th}$ is **independent of cell gap** $d$ — a remarkable and useful result. This is why LC manufacturers focus on tuning $K$ and $\Delta\varepsilon$ of the LC mixture rather than cell thickness when setting threshold voltages.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Gooch-Tarry Transmission Curve

For a TN cell under applied voltage, the transmittance through crossed polarizers is given by:

$$
T = \dfrac{1}{2}\sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{2\Delta n \cdot d}{\lambda}\right)^2}\right)
$$

For the case of no applied voltage (fully twisted), the expression becomes:

$$
T = \sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi \cdot \Delta n \cdot d}{\lambda}\right) \quad \text{(first-order approximation)}
$$

**Step-by-Step Application:**

For a TN cell with $\Delta n = 0.1$, $\lambda = 550$ nm, find optimal $d$ for maximum transmittance:

Set $T_{max}$: $\sin^2(\pi \Delta n d / \lambda) = 1 \Rightarrow \pi \Delta n d / \lambda = \pi/2$

$$
\dfrac{\Delta n \cdot d}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{2}
$$

$$
d_{opt} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \Delta n} = \dfrac{550 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 0.1} = 2.75\ \mu m
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Practical Cell Gap:** Real TN cells use $d \approx 4$–$5\ \mu m$ (corresponding to the **first Gooch-Tarry minimum**) which provides a wide spectral transmission profile (white appearance) and **insensitivity to cell-gap variations** during manufacturing.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of LCD Modes

| **Parameter** | **TN** | **STN** | **IPS** | **VA** | **FFS** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Twist angle** | 90° | 180°–270° | 0° (in-plane) | 0° (vertical) | 0° (fringe-field) |
| **Viewing angle** | Poor (90°) | Poor | Excellent (178°) | Good (160°–178°) | Excellent (178°) |
| **Contrast ratio** | 500:1 | 60:1 | 1000:1 | 3000:1–5000:1 | 1500:1 |
| **Response time** | Fast (2–5 ms) | Slow (50–200 ms) | Moderate (5–16 ms) | Moderate (8–20 ms) | Fast (3–8 ms) |
| **Color shift at angles** | Severe | Severe | Minimal | Mild | Minimal |
| **Drive voltage** | Low | Higher | Higher | Higher | Moderate |
| **Application** | Monitors | Old laptops | Phones, TVs | Premium TVs | Touch screens |
| **Pixel electrode** | On top | On top | On TFT substrate | On top | Common electrode above |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Confusing **IPS** with **VA**. IPS electrodes are **on the same plane** (interdigitated comb), causing in-plane rotation. VA electrodes are on **opposite substrates**, causing tilt along the field direction. The contrast ratio differences (VA is higher) stem from the near-perfect vertical alignment in the OFF state.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a TFT-LCD

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Video Source HDMI DP] --> B[Timing Controller TCON]
    B --> C1[Source Driver Column]
    B --> C2[Gate Driver Row]
    C1 --> D[Pixel TFT Array 1920 x 1080]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Liquid Crystal Layer 3 to 10 um]
    E --> F[Color Filter Substrate RGB Stripes]
    F --> G[Front Polarizer]
    D --> H[Rear Polarizer]
    H --> I[Backlight Unit LED Edge or Direct]
    I --> J[Diffuser and Brightness Enhancement Films BEF]
    J --> H
    G --> K[Viewer Eye]
    F --> K
```

## 4.2 TN-LCD Cross-Sectional Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Top Polarizer 0 deg] --> L2[Top Glass Substrate with ITO Electrode]
    L2 --> L3[Top Alignment Layer Rubbed Polyimide]
    L3 --> L4[Liquid Crystal Molecules Twisted 90 deg]
    L4 --> L5[Bottom Alignment Layer Rubbed 90 deg to Top]
    L5 --> L6[Bottom Glass with ITO Electrode]
    L6 --> L7[Bottom Polarizer 90 deg to Top]
    L7 --> L8[Backlight Reflector and Diffuser]
```

## 4.3 Active Matrix (TFT) Pixel Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GateLine[Gate Line Scan Row] --> TFTnodeA[TFT Gate Terminal]
    DataLine[Data Line Source Column] --> TFTnodeB[TFT Source Terminal]
    TFTnodeA --> TFTdev[TFT Thin Film Transistor a-Si or LTPS]
    TFTnodeB --> TFTdev
    TFTdev --> DrainNode[Drain Connected to Pixel Electrode]
    DrainNode --> Cap[Storage Capacitor Cs]
    Cap --> CommonLine[Common Voltage Line Vcom]
    DrainNode --> LCcell[Liquid Crystal Cell]
    LCcell --> CommonLine
```

## 4.4 Voltage Waveform & LC Response State Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OffState[OFF State V = 0 V] -->|Molecules lie flat 90 deg twist| LightPass[Polarized light rotates passes analyzer]
    LightPass --> BrightPixel[BRIGHT pixel ON]
    OnState[ON State V greater than Vth] -->|Molecules stand up vertically| NoRotate[No polarization rotation]
    NoRotate --> Blocked[Light absorbed by analyzer]
    Blocked --> DarkPixel[DARK pixel OFF]
    BrightPixel -->|Adjust RMS voltage| GrayScale[Intermediate Gray Levels via Pulse Width Modulation]
    DarkPixel -->|Adjust RMS voltage| GrayScale
```

## 4.5 IPS Mode Electrode Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Plane1[Common Electrode Plane Vcom] --> Spacer1[Insulating Layer SiNx]
    Spacer1 --> Comb1[Interdigitated Comb Electrode A on TFT Substrate]
    Spacer1 --> Comb2[Interdigitated Comb Electrode B on TFT Substrate]
    Comb1 -->|Lateral E field| LCRot[LC molecules rotate in plane of substrate]
    Comb2 -->|Lateral E field| LCRot
    LCRot --> NoSubstrateTilt[No vertical tilt; uniform viewing angle]
```

## 4.6 Color Sub-Pixel Arrangement (RGB Stripe)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Red Sub-Pixel] --> G[Green Sub-Pixel] --> B[Blue Sub-Pixel]
    R --> R2[Red Sub-Pixel of next column]
    G --> G2[Green Sub-Pixel of next column]
    B --> B2[Blue Sub-Pixel of next column]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Backlight Technology Variants (Important for Entertainment Electronics):**
> - **CCFL (Cold Cathode Fluorescent Lamp):** Legacy, used in early LCD TVs.
> - **Edge-Lit LED:** LEDs at the edges of the panel; thin, but limited local dimming.
> - **Direct-Lit LED (Full-Array Local Dimming, FALD):** Hundreds of LED zones behind the panel; superior contrast.
> - **Mini-LED:** Thousands of small LEDs; high-zone-count FALD.
> - **Quantum Dot Enhancement Film (QDEF):** Converts blue LED to pure red/green for wider color gamut (Samsung QLED, Sony Triluminos).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Freedericksz transition" in the context of liquid crystal displays. Why is it important in LCD design?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Freedericksz transition** is the critical phenomenon in nematic liquid crystals where, above a threshold voltage $V_{th}$, the LC molecules begin to reorient away from their equilibrium alignment due to the torque exerted by an applied electric field on their dielectric anisotropy. It is governed by the equation:

$$
V_{th} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \Delta \varepsilon}}
$$

**Importance:** It defines the **minimum operating voltage** of an LCD pixel. Below $V_{th}$, no reorientation occurs and the pixel remains in its default state. Therefore, all display driving schemes must ensure that the data voltage crosses this threshold to achieve a defined ON/OFF state, making it the **fundamental design parameter** for driver ICs.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Passive Matrix LCD (PMLCD) and Active Matrix LCD (AMLCD).**

**Model Answer:**

| **Feature** | **PMLCD** | **AMLCD (TFT-LCD)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Switching element** | None (direct row/column addressing) | One TFT per pixel |
| **Contrast ratio** | Low (~30:1) | High (>1000:1) |
| **Response time** | Slow (~300 ms) | Fast (<16 ms) |
| **Multiplexing limit** | Max ~64 rows | 1080p, 4K, 8K feasible |
| **Crosstalk** | Severe at high resolution | Minimal |
| **Application** | Calculators, watches, low-end displays | TVs, monitors, phones |
| **Power consumption** | Lower | Moderate |

## Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the construction and working of a **Twisted Nematic (TN) LCD** with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of polarizers and alignment layers.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction of TN-LCD:**

1. **Two glass substrates** with transparent **Indium Tin Oxide (ITO)** electrodes patterned as row-column matrix.
2. **Polyimide alignment layers** are coated and **mechanically buffed** at 90° to each other on the inner surfaces of the two substrates.
3. **Spacers** of diameter **3–10 µm** are sprayed to maintain uniform cell gap.
4. **Nematic liquid crystal** (e.g., 5CB, E7) is injected via vacuum filling or ODF (One Drop Fill).
5. **Two outer polarizers** are attached, with transmission axes at 90° (crossed configuration).

**Working:**

- **OFF state (V=0):** The 90° twisted LC layer rotates the polarization of incoming linearly polarized light by 90°. The light now matches the analyzer axis and is transmitted — pixel is **BRIGHT**.
- **ON state (V > Vth):** The LC molecules align vertically with the electric field. The polarization is **not rotated**, and the light is blocked by the analyzer — pixel is **DARK**.
- **Intermediate gray levels** are obtained by controlling the RMS voltage (or pulse-width modulation of the data signal).

**Role of Polarizers:** They convert unpolarized backlight into linearly polarized light and act as the analyzer at the exit. Without them, the LC's polarization rotation would have no visual effect.

**Role of Alignment Layers:** The buffed polyimide creates **anisotropic anchoring** that forces LC molecules to lie along the buffing direction with a small **pretilt angle (~2°–5°)** to ensure uniform switching and prevent reverse tilt defects.

---

**(b) (7 Marks)** With relevant equations, explain the **Freedericksz transition** and derive the expression for the **threshold voltage** of a nematic LC cell.

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** The Freedericksz transition is the voltage-induced reorientation of LC molecules in a uniformly aligned cell, occurring when the dielectric torque exceeds the elastic restoring torque.

**Setup:** Consider a planar cell with the director initially along the x-axis. Apply an electric field E along the z-axis. The director tilts by an angle $\theta(z)$.

**Total free energy density:**

$$
f = \tfrac{1}{2} K \left(\dfrac{d\theta}{dz}\right)^2 - \tfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 \Delta \varepsilon E^2 \sin^2\theta
$$

**Euler-Lagrange equation:**

$$
K \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dz^2} + \varepsilon_0 \Delta \varepsilon E^2 \sin\theta \cos\theta = 0
$$

**Linearization near threshold** ($\theta$ small): $\sin\theta \approx \theta$, $\cos\theta \approx 1$

$$
K \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dz^2} + \varepsilon_0 \Delta \varepsilon E^2 \theta = 0
$$

**Boundary conditions:** $\theta(0) = \theta(d) = 0$. Trial solution $\theta(z) = \theta_0 \sin(\pi z / d)$.

Substitution yields:

$$
-K \left(\dfrac{\pi}{d}\right)^2 + \varepsilon_0 \Delta \varepsilon E^2 = 0
$$

**Threshold field:**

$$
E_{th} = \dfrac{\pi}{d} \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\varepsilon_0 \Delta \varepsilon}}
$$

**Threshold voltage:** $V_{th} = E_{th} \cdot d$:

$$
\boxed{V_{th} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\varepsilon_0 \Delta \varepsilon}}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Do not** confuse $V_{th}$ with $V_{sat}$ (saturation voltage). $V_{th}$ is the **onset** of reorientation; $V_{sat}$ is the voltage at which molecules are **fully aligned** vertically.
> - **Do not** forget the **pretilt angle** assumption — strictly, the buffed alignment creates a small pretilt, and the full derivation includes it. However, for KTU answers, the idealized form above is sufficient.
> - Students often write $V_{th} = \sqrt{K/\varepsilon_0 \Delta\varepsilon}$ **without the factor of $\pi$** — this is a **1-mark deduction**.

### **Question B: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) (7 Marks)** Compare the **IPS (In-Plane Switching)** and **VA (Vertical Alignment)** LCD modes. Discuss their respective advantages and disadvantages for large-screen entertainment displays.

**Model Solution:**

**IPS Mode:**

- **Geometry:** Both the pixel and common electrodes are **interdigitated combs on the same (TFT) substrate**. No electrode on the top substrate.
- **OFF state:** LC molecules are **horizontally aligned** (parallel to substrate) along the comb direction, orthogonal to the polarizer axis → light blocked → **DARK**.
- **ON state:** Lateral electric field rotates the molecules **in the plane of the substrate** by 90° → polarization rotates → light passes → **BRIGHT**.
- **Advantages:**
  - Extremely **wide viewing angle** (178°/178°) with **minimal color shift**.
  - Excellent **color accuracy** at off-axis angles → preferred for **professional monitors, smartphones**.
- **Disadvantages:**
  - Lower **contrast ratio** (~1000:1) because the OFF state has some residual light leakage.
  - Slightly higher **power consumption** (transmittance ~70%–80%).
  - **IPS glow / IPS bleed** in dark scenes.

**VA Mode:**

- **Geometry:** Pixel electrode on TFT substrate, common electrode on the color-filter substrate. LC molecules are **vertically aligned** (homeotropic) in the OFF state.
- **OFF state:** Vertically aligned molecules **do not rotate light** → blocked by crossed polarizers → **truly DARK** (very high contrast).
- **ON state:** Molecules tilt along the field direction → rotate polarization → light passes.
- **Advantages:**
  - **Highest contrast ratio** among LCD modes (3000:1 – 5000:1, even 6000:1 with local dimming).
  - **Best black levels** → preferred for **premium home theater TVs**.
- **Disadvantages:**
  - **Color and gamma shift** at oblique viewing angles.
  - Slower response than TN/IPS (slight "VA smear" in fast motion).

**Conclusion for Entertainment Displays:**
- **IPS** dominates in **mid-range TVs, monitors, and smartphones** where viewing angle and color stability are prioritized.
- **VA** dominates in **premium home-theater LCD TVs** where black level and contrast are paramount, especially when paired with **full-array local dimming (FALD)** or **mini-LED backlights**.

---

**(b) (7 Marks)** Describe the structure of an **active-matrix TFT-LCD pixel**. Explain the role of the **storage capacitor** and the **liquid crystal capacitance** in pixel voltage retention.

**Model Solution:**

**TFT-LCD Pixel Circuit:**

Each pixel consists of:
- One **Thin-Film Transistor (TFT)** — usually **a-Si:H**, **LTPS**, or **IGZO** — with its gate connected to the **gate line** (row scan), source to the **data line** (column), and drain to the **pixel electrode**.
- A **pixel electrode** (ITO) forming a capacitor with the **common electrode (Vcom)** on the opposite substrate, with the LC layer as the dielectric — this is the **liquid crystal capacitor $C_{LC}$**.
- A **storage capacitor $C_{s}$** in parallel with $C_{LC}$, typically formed between the pixel electrode and a separate storage line (or the previous gate line).

**Operation:**

1. **Addressing Phase:** When the gate line is activated (Vg high), the TFT turns ON. The data voltage from the source line charges $C_{LC}$ and $C_{s}$ in parallel.
2. **Hold Phase:** When the gate line goes LOW, the TFT turns OFF. The charge on the **parallel combination $C_{LC} + C_{s}$** is **trapped**, holding the voltage across the LC for the entire frame period (typically 16.67 ms for 60 Hz).
3. **Modulation:** The voltage across $C_{LC}$ reorients the LC molecules, controlling the transmittance and hence the pixel brightness.

**Role of $C_s$ (Storage Capacitor):**
- Increases the **total charge storage** at the pixel.
- Reduces **voltage droop** caused by:
  - **LC capacitance variation** with applied voltage (a non-linear parasitic effect that distorts the voltage).
  - **Leakage current** through the TFT in OFF state.
- Typically $C_s \approx 0.5 \cdot C_{LC}$ to $2 \cdot C_{LC}$. Total capacitance:

$$
C_{total} = C_{LC} + C_s
$$

**Voltage Retention:** The percentage of voltage retained during one frame is:

$$
V_{retained}(\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{frame}}{C_{total} \cdot V_{pixel}}\right) \times 100
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do not** forget to mention that the **TFT leakage current** is the primary cause of pixel voltage droop. Modern **IGZO TFTs** have leakage currents 100× lower than **a-Si:H**, enabling **smaller pixels (8K) and higher refresh rates (120 Hz/240 Hz)**.
> - A common error: stating that $C_s$ is in **series** with $C_{LC}$. In reality, they are **in parallel** between the pixel electrode and Vcom.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LCDs are non-emissive modulators** — they need a **backlight** (LED, CCFL, or quantum-dot enhanced).
- **The TN cell is the workhorse architecture**, with a 90° twisted nematic layer between crossed polarizers.
- **Freedericksz threshold voltage:** $V_{th} = \pi\sqrt{K / (\varepsilon_0 \Delta\varepsilon)}$ — independent of cell gap.
- **Phase retardation** $\delta = 2\pi \Delta n d / \lambda$ determines the Gooch-Tarry transmittance and is the key optical design parameter.
- **TFT-AM LCD** uses one transistor per pixel for active addressing — essential for high-resolution, video-rate displays.
- **IPS** offers the best viewing angle; **VA** offers the best contrast; **TN** is cheapest and fastest; **FFS/PLS** is dominant in touchscreens.
- **Storage capacitor $C_s$** in parallel with $C_{LC}$ prevents voltage droop and ensures flicker-free display.
- **Modern entertainment LCDs** integrate **mini-LED backlights**, **quantum-dot color enhancement**, and **120/240 Hz refresh** to remain competitive with OLED.
- **Comparison with OLED:** LCDs are brighter, longer-lasting, and cheaper; OLEDs offer perfect blacks and per-pixel dimming.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick-Recall Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **"T-N-F-I-V"** — TN, Nematic, Freedericksz, IPS, VA — the five pillars of LCD technology for Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light-Emitting Diode Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Light-Emitting Diode Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

A **Light-Emitting Diode (LED) Display** is an optoelectronic display technology that uses a two-dimensional matrix (or pre-patterned arrangement) of light-emitting diodes as the individual pixel elements to render alphanumeric characters, graphics, and full-motion video. Each LED is a semiconductor p-n junction diode that converts **electrical energy directly into light** through the physical process of *electroluminescence* — the spontaneous radiative recombination of electrons and holes at the depletion region, releasing a photon whose energy (and hence wavelength/color) is governed by the band gap of the active semiconductor material.

$$ E_{photon} = h \nu = \frac{h c}{\lambda} \approx E_g $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** LED displays form the backbone of modern consumer entertainment electronics — from TV panels and stadium scoreboards to gaming peripherals and wearable device indicators. They are classified under *active (self-emissive) flat-panel displays*, distinguishing them fundamentally from LCD (which is a passive light-modulating technology).

## Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **honeycomb of tiny controllable fireflies** glued onto a flat board. Each firefly (LED) can be switched ON or OFF (or dimmed) independently by an electrical control signal. By turning on the right pattern of fireflies, you can spell out a letter, draw a picture, or paint a moving image. Unlike LCD pixels (which are mere shutters blocking a backlight), each LED is itself a *light source*, meaning a switched-off LED is genuinely **black** — giving the display its famous infinite contrast ratio.

- **Forward Voltage (Vf):** Typically **1.8 V – 3.5 V** depending on color
- **Drive Current (If):** Standard indicator LEDs: **20 mA**; High-brightness LEDs: **20 mA – 350 mA**
- **Luminous Efficacy:** **80 – 200 lm/W** (white LED)
- **Viewing Angle:** **100° – 160°** depending on package epoxy shape
- **Response Time:** **< 100 ns** (orders of magnitude faster than LCD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The color of the emitted light is *not* determined by the plastic housing (as in a filtered incandescent bulb). The color is a **direct quantum-mechanical consequence of the semiconductor's band gap energy (Eg)** — for example, GaAs (Eg ≈ 1.42 eV) → infrared, AlGaInP (Eg ≈ 2.0 eV) → red/orange, InGaN (Eg ≈ 2.7 eV) → green/blue.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How"

### Step 1: Forward Bias Injection
When the p-n junction is forward biased above the cut-in voltage, electrons are injected from the n-region into the active (depletion) region, and holes are injected from the p-region. The external applied voltage must overcome the **built-in potential (Vbi)**.

### Step 2: Carrier Recombination
Inside the active junction, an electron occupying a higher-energy conduction-band state falls down into a lower-energy valence-band state occupied by a hole. The energy difference equals the **band gap (Eg)** of the semiconductor.

### Step 3: Photon Emission (Radiative Recombination)
In a *direct band gap* semiconductor (e.g., GaAs, InGaN), the conduction band minimum and valence band maximum lie at the same crystal momentum (k-vector), so the recombination does not require a phonon to conserve momentum. The energy is therefore released almost entirely as a **photon** of light, making the process efficient.

### Step 4: Extraction & Collimation
The emitted photon travels through the semiconductor and exits through the dome-shaped epoxy lens. The lens shape increases the **escape cone** (Snell's law refraction at the semiconductor-air interface), significantly boosting external quantum efficiency (EQE).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only ~2–4% of photons escape a flat-junction LED?** Total internal reflection traps most light inside the high-index semiconductor. A hemispherical epoxy dome, an index-matching layer, and sophisticated *photonic crystal* structures are used to mitigate this.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks / KTU Exam Tip |
|---|--------------------|----------------------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Photon Energy | $E = h \nu = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$ | eV or J | Planck's constant $h = 4.136 \times 10^{-15}$ eV·s |
| 2 | Wavelength vs Band Gap | $\lambda(\mu m) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g (eV)}$ | μm | Quick inverse relation for KTU numericals |
| 3 | LED Forward Current | $I_F = \dfrac{V_S - V_F}{R_S}$ | A | Series current-limiting resistor design |
| 4 | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta_{lum} = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{P_{elec}}$ | lm/W | Higher is better; white LED $\approx$ **150 lm/W** |
| 5 | Luminous Intensity | $I_v = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{\Omega}$ | cd (candela) | $\Omega$ is solid angle in steradians |
| 6 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON}+T_{OFF}}$ | dimensionless | Perceived brightness $\propto D$ (for $f > 100$ Hz) |
| 7 | Multiplex Duty Cycle | $D_{mux} = \dfrac{1}{N_{rows}}$ | dimensionless | Average LED current $= I_{peak} \times D_{mux}$ |
| 8 | RGB to Luminance (Rec.709) | $Y = 0.2126 R + 0.7152 G + 0.0722 B$ | — | Used in full-color video displays |
| 9 | LED Display Power | $P = N \cdot V_F \cdot I_F \cdot D$ | W | $N$ = number of simultaneously active LEDs |
| 10 | Refresh Rate | $f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{N_{rows} \cdot t_{row}}$ | Hz | Must be $\geq$ **60 Hz** to avoid flicker |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** In all numerical problems, **always distinguish between *luminous flux* (lumens, total light) and *luminous intensity* (candelas, light per solid angle)**. They are *not* the same quantity, and confusing them is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

LED displays dominate the entertainment electronics industry because they:
- Deliver **true blacks** (self-emissive pixels can be fully turned off)
- Offer **wide color gamuts** (Rec.2020 coverage with quantum-dot enhanced LEDs)
- Provide **fast response times** essential for gaming and VR
- Are **mechanically rugged** (no glass vacuum envelope like CRTs)
- Enable **flexible and curved form factors** (using organic/inorganic hybrid structures on plastic substrates)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wavelength of Emitted Light from Band Gap

**Problem:** A red LED is fabricated using an AlGaInP active region with a direct band gap of **2.05 eV**. Determine the dominant wavelength of the emitted light in nanometers and identify its spectral color.

**Step 1:** Recall the fundamental photon-energy relation. Each emitted photon's energy equals the band gap energy of the recombination event (first-order approximation neglecting thermal broadening).

$$ E_{photon} = E_g = \frac{h c}{\lambda} $$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for wavelength. Insert the standard product of Planck's constant and the speed of light, using the convenient identity $hc = 1240$ eV·nm.

$$ \lambda = \frac{h c}{E_g} $$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$ \lambda = \frac{1240 \; eV \cdot nm}{2.05 \; eV} $$

**Step 4:** Perform the division.

$$ \lambda = 604.88 \; nm $$

**Step 5:** Round and identify the spectral region. From the visible spectrum reference table, **620 nm – 750 nm** corresponds to **RED** light. The computed value of 604.88 nm sits at the orange-red boundary, consistent with an AlGaInP red-orange LED.

$$ \boxed{\lambda \approx 605 \; nm \; (\text{orange-red}) } $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Current-Limiting Resistor Design

**Problem:** A blue indicator LED ($V_F = 3.2$ V, $I_F = 20$ mA) is driven from a 5 V logic supply of a microcontroller. Calculate the required series current-limiting resistor and the power dissipated in the LED.

**Step 1:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the series loop (Supply → Resistor → LED → Ground).

$$ V_S = V_R + V_F $$

**Step 2:** Solve for the voltage drop across the resistor.

$$ V_R = V_S - V_F = 5.0 \; V - 3.2 \; V = 1.8 \; V $$

**Step 3:** Apply Ohm's Law to find the resistor value. Note that $20 \; mA = 0.020 \; A$.

$$ R = \frac{V_R}{I_F} = \frac{1.8 \; V}{0.020 \; A} $$

**Step 4:** Compute.

$$ R = 90 \; \Omega $$

**Step 5:** Select the nearest **standard E12 value** to ensure safe current limiting. The closest standard is **100 Ω** (a slightly lower current of 18 mA results, which is well within the LED's safe operating range).

**Step 6:** Calculate power dissipation in the LED.

$$ P_{LED} = V_F \times I_F = 3.2 \; V \times 0.020 \; A = 0.064 \; W = 64 \; mW $$

$$ \boxed{R = 100 \; \Omega \; (1/8 \; W), \quad P_{LED} = 64 \; mW } $$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of LED Display Architectures

| Architecture | Pixel Density | Drive Method | Best Use Case | Pros | Cons |
|--------------|---------------|--------------|---------------|------|------|
| **7-Segment** | 7 + DP per digit | Static / Multiplexed | Clocks, calculators, instrument readouts | Simple driver, low cost, high brightness | Limited to numerals & few letters |
| **Dot Matrix (5×7)** | 35 LEDs per char | Row/Column Multiplex | Scrolling text displays, airport boards | Flexible alphanumeric | Lower brightness due to 1/N duty |
| **Dot Matrix (8×8)** | 64 LEDs per block | Charlieplex / Mux | Pixel art, small graphics | True bitmap | Complex driver (e.g., MAX7219) |
| **Full-Color Matrix (P2.5)** | > 100,000 px/m² | PWM + Scan driver | Indoor video walls | High color depth | Expensive driver ICs |
| **SMD LED Panel** | Variable | Constant current sink | Stadium / outdoor screens | Weatherproof, very bright (>5000 nits) | Power hungry, high heat |
| **Micro-LED** | > 1,000,000 px/m² | Active-matrix TFT | Next-gen TVs, AR/VR | Best contrast, longevity | Mass-transfer manufacturing is hard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight — Multiplexing Math:** In an 8-row multiplexed dot-matrix display, each row is active for only 1/8th of the time. To maintain the same *average* brightness as a statically driven LED, the *peak* current must be increased by approximately a factor of **8×**. This is why driver ICs like the **MAX7219** allow setting the segment current (typically 10–40 mA) far higher than the static-drive rating.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: PWM-Based Perceived Brightness

**Problem:** An LED is driven with a 1 kHz PWM signal. The ON-time is 250 µs and the OFF-time is 750 µs. Calculate (a) the duty cycle, and (b) the average current if the peak current is 80 mA.

**Step 1:** Compute the total period.

$$ T = T_{ON} + T_{OFF} = 250 + 750 = 1000 \; \mu s $$

**Step 2:** Calculate the duty cycle.

$$ D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T} = \frac{250}{1000} = 0.25 = 25\% $$

**Step 3:** Compute the average current (perceived brightness is proportional to average current for $f > 100$ Hz due to retinal persistence).

$$ I_{avg} = I_{peak} \times D = 80 \; mA \times 0.25 = 20 \; mA $$

$$ \boxed{D = 25\%, \quad I_{avg} = 20 \; mA } $$

This is the foundational principle behind **16-level (4-bit) grayscale** generation in video LED walls: 256 different ON-time fractions per frame produce 256 intensity levels per primary color, giving **16.7 million colors** ($256^3$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: LED Display System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Video Source / MCU / FPGA] --> B[Graphics Controller / Processor]
    B --> C[Frame Buffer Memory]
    C --> D[LED Driver IC - Constant Current Sink]
    D --> E[Row Decoder - Multiplexer]
    D --> F[Column Shift Register - Latch]
    E --> G[LED Matrix Panel - Anode Rows]
    F --> H[LED Matrix Panel - Cathode Columns]
    G --> I[Visible Image Output]
    H --> I
    J[Power Supply - Constant Current Driver] --> D
    K[Thermal Management - Heatsink / Fan] --> G
    K --> H
    L[Control Signals - Brightness / Refresh / Gamma] --> B
```

## 4.2 Schematic: 7-Segment LED Display with Common-Cathode Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[5V DC Supply] --> B[220 Ohm Current Limiting Resistor Network]
    B --> C[7-Segment LED Display - Common Cathode]
    D[Microcontroller GPIO - 7 Segments a-g] --> E[Transistor Driver Array - ULN2003]
    E --> C
    C --> F[GND via Common Cathode Pin]
    G[Decimal Point DP Pin] --> B
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: 4-to-1 Row Multiplexing for an 8x8 LED Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Frame Start - Refresh Cycle] --> B0[Row Selector = Row 0 ON]
    B0 --> C0[Shift Out Column Data for Row 0]
    C0 --> D0[Latch and Illuminate Row 0 for t_row]
    D0 --> E0[Blank Display - Brief Dead Time]
    E0 --> F0[Row Selector = Row 1 ON]
    F0 --> G0[Shift Out Column Data for Row 1]
    G0 --> H0[Latch and Illuminate Row 1 for t_row]
    H0 --> I0[Blank Display]
    I0 --> J0[Continue for Rows 2 through 7]
    J0 --> K0[Return to Row 0 - Loop]
    K0 --> A0
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: PWM Color Mixing Subsystem (RGB LED)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[8-bit Red Data] --> B[Red PWM Generator - 256 Levels]
    C[8-bit Green Data] --> D[Green PWM Generator - 256 Levels]
    E[8-bit Blue Data] --> F[Blue PWM Generator - 256 Levels]
    B --> G[Red LED Driver Channel]
    D --> H[Green LED Driver Channel]
    F --> I[Blue LED Driver Channel]
    G --> J[RGB LED Package - Common Anode]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Optical Output - Additive Color Mix]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The above diagrams abstract away physical reality (e.g., the dome epoxy lens, the wire bonds, the heat slug of the LED package) and instead focus on the *signal-flow architecture* — which is what KTU examiners expect you to draw and label in your answer sheets. A clean, labeled block diagram scores higher than a poorly-rendered physical drawing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "electroluminescence" and explain how it is produced in a Light-Emitting Diode. Mention the role of band gap energy.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Electroluminescence** is the phenomenon of non-thermal generation of light resulting from the application of an electric field to a material (or, equivalently, from the passage of an electric current through it).

**Production in an LED:** When a p-n junction diode is forward biased, electrons from the n-region and holes from the p-region are injected into the depletion (active) region. They recombine radiatively across the band gap. The energy difference (E_c − E_v = E_g) is released as a photon.

**Role of band gap:** The band gap energy E_g of the active semiconductor *directly determines* the wavelength (and therefore color) of the emitted photon, as governed by $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$. Higher E_g → shorter wavelength (blue/UV); Lower E_g → longer wavelength (red/IR).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a 7-segment LED display. How are the segments interconnected in a common-cathode configuration?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A 7-segment LED display consists of **seven individually addressable LEDs** arranged in the shape of the numeral "8" — segments labelled **a, b, c, d, e, f, g** — plus an optional eighth LED for the decimal point (DP). Each LED is a separate semiconductor die with its own anode and cathode lead-outs.

**Common-Cathode Configuration:** All seven (plus DP) cathodes are tied together and brought out to a single common ground pin. The anode of each segment is brought out to a separate pin. To illuminate a segment, the corresponding anode is driven HIGH through a current-limiting resistor while the common cathode is grounded. This configuration is preferred when the display is driven from logic-level HIGH outputs (e.g., CMOS/TTL microcontrollers).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the working principle of an LED with the help of a labeled diagram. Discuss the relationship between the semiconductor band gap and the color of the emitted light. State the materials typically used for red, green, and blue LEDs.

**(b) (7 Marks)** With a suitable block diagram, describe the working of a multiplexed 8×8 LED dot-matrix display system. How is brightness controlled using the PWM technique? Show the mathematical relationship between duty cycle and perceived luminance.

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Compare the three principal LED packaging types: **DIP, SMD, and COB**. Discuss their respective construction, thermal performance, and typical application areas in entertainment electronics.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Design the current-limiting resistor for a green LED ($V_F = 2.2$ V, $I_F = 15$ mA) driven from a 9 V supply. If 20 such LEDs are wired in parallel (each with its own 680 Ω resistor), calculate the total power drawn from the supply and the total power dissipated as useful light (assume luminous efficacy = 50 lm/W). State two reasons why LED displays outperform LCD displays in entertainment applications.

---

## Model Solutions for Question A

### (a) Model Solution

**Working Principle:** An LED is a forward-biased p-n junction diode fabricated from a **direct band gap** semiconductor (e.g., GaAs, AlGaInP, InGaN). Under forward bias, electrons from the n-side and holes from the p-side are injected into the depletion region. Recombination of these minority carriers releases energy equal to the band gap. In direct band gap materials, this energy is emitted predominantly as a photon (radiative recombination), unlike in silicon where the recombination is non-radiative (phonon-mediated).

**Band Gap vs Color:** $\lambda = \dfrac{1240 \; eV \cdot nm}{E_g}$

| LED Color | Band Gap (eV) | Wavelength (nm) | Material System |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Red | 1.9 – 2.0 | 620 – 650 | AlGaInP / GaAsP |
| Green | 2.2 – 2.4 | 520 – 570 | AlGaInP / InGaN |
| Blue | 2.6 – 3.0 | 450 – 470 | InGaN / SiC |

**Diagram Description:** Labeled cross-section showing p-layer, n-layer, active region, anode contact, cathode contact, substrate, and epoxy dome lens with light emission cone.

### (b) Model Solution

**Block Diagram:** As shown in SECTION 4.3 — a microcontroller/FPGA drives a row decoder (e.g., 3-to-8 decoder) to sequentially enable one row at a time, while a column shift register (e.g., 74HC595) latches the pixel data for that row. The cycle completes in $8 \times t_{row}$. For a refresh rate of 60 Hz, $t_{row} = 1/(8 \times 60) \approx 2.08$ ms.

**PWM Brightness Control:** Each LED is driven with a PWM waveform whose duty cycle $D = T_{ON}/T$ determines the average current and hence the perceived brightness.

$$ I_{avg} = I_{peak} \cdot D $$

$$ L_{perceived} \propto I_{avg} = I_{peak} \cdot D $$

For multi-bit grayscale (e.g., 8-bit), 256 discrete duty cycles from 0/255 to 255/255 produce 256 brightness levels per color channel.

---

## Model Solutions for Question B

### (a) Model Solution

| Feature | DIP (Dual In-line Package) | SMD (Surface Mount Device) | COB (Chip-on-Board) |
|---------|---------------------------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| Construction | LEDs in molded plastic body with leads through PCB holes | LEDs soldered directly onto PCB surface | Bare LED dies mounted directly on PCB substrate |
| Size | Large (e.g., 5 mm) | Small (e.g., 3528, 5050) | Very small, densely packed |
| Thermal | Poor (limited heat dissipation) | Moderate (heat pad on bottom) | Excellent (direct thermal path) |
| Brightness | Low–Medium | Medium–High | Very High |
| Application | Indicators, toys, 7-seg | TV backlights, signage, phones | High-power stage lights, automotive |

### (b) Model Solution

**Resistor Design:**
$$ R = \frac{V_S - V_F}{I_F} = \frac{9 - 2.2}{0.015} = \frac{6.8}{0.015} = 453.3 \; \Omega $$

Nearest standard value: **470 Ω** (giving $I_F \approx 14.5$ mA, safe).

**Total Power from Supply:**
$$ P_{supply} = V_S \times I_{total} = 9 \; V \times (20 \times 0.015) = 9 \times 0.3 = 2.7 \; W $$

**Total Useful Light (Radiant):**
$$ P_{radiant} = P_{electrical} \times \eta = 2.7 \times 0.5 = 1.35 \; W $$

**Why LED > LCD for entertainment:**
1. **True black & infinite contrast** (self-emissive, can fully switch off)
2. **Faster response time** (<100 ns vs ~5 ms), eliminating motion blur in fast action scenes
3. **Wider color gamut** and **better viewing angles**
4. **Lower power consumption** for the same perceived brightness (no backlight always-on)
5. **Longer operational lifetime** (50,000 – 100,000 hours)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse luminous flux (lumens) with radiant flux (watts).** They are different physical quantities. Luminous flux is *photometric* (weighted by the eye's response curve), while radiant flux is *radiometric* (raw optical power). Luminous efficacy has units of lm/W.
> 2. **Never say "LEDs use a filament."** This will cost you a mark. LEDs are solid-state devices.
> 3. **For multiplexing questions, always state the refresh rate** (≥ 60 Hz) to show that flicker is avoided.
> 4. **Always label units** in numerical answers — particularly nm, eV, lm, mA, and Ω.
> 5. **In block diagrams, label every block and arrow** — unlabeled diagrams fetch only 1–2 marks out of 7.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LED = Light Emitting Diode**, a forward-biased p-n junction that emits light via **electroluminescence** when electrons and holes recombine radiatively across the band gap.
- **Wavelength–Band Gap relationship:** $\lambda(\text{nm}) = 1240 / E_g(\text{eV})$ — the most-tested formula in KTU exams.
- **Three primary materials:** AlGaInP (red/amber/yellow), InGaN (green/blue/UV), AlGaAs (red/IR).
- **Key color rule:** Red (low E_g) → Green (mid E_g) → Blue (high E_g) → UV (very high E_g).
- **Forward voltage** depends on color: Red ≈ 1.8–2.2 V; Green ≈ 2.0–2.4 V; Blue/White ≈ 3.0–3.5 V.
- **Standard drive current** for indicator LEDs = **20 mA**; high-power LEDs = **350 mA – 3 A**.
- **Always use a current-limiting resistor** $R = (V_S - V_F)/I_F$ to prevent thermal runaway.
- **Multiplexing** is used to reduce pin count: duty cycle = 1/N, peak current scales by N.
- **PWM** (Pulse Width Modulation) controls perceived brightness: $L \propto D$ (for $f > 100$ Hz).
- **RGB color mixing** is *additive*; 8-bit per channel = 16.7 million colors ($256^3$).
- **Luminous efficacy** of modern white LEDs: **150 – 200 lm/W** (vs 15 lm/W for incandescent).
- **Response time** of LEDs: **< 100 ns** — orders of magnitude faster than LCD.
- **Display types to remember:** 7-segment, 14-segment, dot matrix (5×7, 8×8), full-color video panels, Micro-LED.
- **Key driver ICs:** MAX7219 (8×8 matrix), TLC5940 (16-channel PWM), MBI5024 (16-channel constant current).
- **KTU 2024 specific:** Focus on *block diagrams* and *signal-flow* — labeled diagrams always score.
- **Always end an LED numerical with the "nearest standard resistor value"** — this is a KTU board-exam convention.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field Emission Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Field Emission Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Field Emission Display (FED)** is a flat-panel display technology that combines the high image quality and fast response of a Cathode Ray Tube (CRT) with the thin, lightweight form factor of a Liquid Crystal Display (LCD). It operates by employing a large matrix-addressed array of **cold cathodes (field emitters)** that release electrons into a high-vacuum environment via **quantum-mechanical tunnelling** under the influence of a strong local electric field. These emitted electrons are accelerated across a small vacuum gap to strike phosphor sub-pixels, generating visible light through cathodoluminescence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Field Emission Displays fall under the advanced emissive display family. Unlike CRTs (hot cathodes) or LCDs (light modulation), FEDs use **field emission** as the electron source — this is the foundational distinction students MUST remember in the exam.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Imagine a digital billboard made of millions of tiny water cannons.**
- Each water cannon (field emitter) is pointed at a coloured paint spot (phosphor dot — red, green, or blue).
- A high electric field acts as the "trigger button" that releases a single burst of electrons from each cannon.
- A matrix-addressed grid system acts like a traffic controller, deciding which cannons fire and at what intensity.
- The electrons travel through empty space (vacuum) and slam into the paint, causing it to glow.

The brilliance of the design: you get **CRT-quality colour and viewing angles** from a panel as thin as an LCD, because each sub-pixel has its own electron gun.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Cold" Cathode?** Unlike CRT filaments that are heated (~2500 K) to boil off electrons (thermionic emission), FED emitters work at **room temperature** using a strong electric field (~10⁹–10¹⁰ V/m) to pull electrons out via tunnelling. This is more energy-efficient and faster.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bold)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| **Required local electric field (E)** | **10⁹ – 10¹⁰ V/m** | Threshold for field emission |
| **Typical work function (φ) of Mo tips** | **~4.5 eV** | Determines tunnelling probability |
| **Operating voltage (anode)** | **1 kV – 10 kV** | Accelerates electrons to phosphors |
| **Operating voltage (gate)** | **5 V – 100 V** | Triggers field emission |
| **Vacuum gap** | **0.2 mm – 2 mm** | Between cathode plate and anode plate |
| **Pixel pitch** | **0.2 mm – 0.5 mm** | High-definition capability |
| **Tip radius (Spindt)** | **~30 nm – 50 nm** | Sharpens field to trigger emission |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle — Quantum Tunnelling

In field emission, electrons are not "boiled off" the metal surface (as in thermionic emission). Instead, when a very high external electric field is applied, the surface potential barrier of the metal becomes **thin enough** that electrons in the conduction band can **quantum-mechanically tunnel** through it into the vacuum, even at room temperature.

The width of the triangular potential barrier becomes:

$$w \approx \frac{\phi}{eE}$$

Where:
- $w$ = barrier width (m)
- $\phi$ = work function (eV)
- $e$ = electron charge (1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C)
- $E$ = external electric field (V/m)

> [!TIP]
> **For a strong emission current**, $w$ must be small (typically < 1–2 nm), which is why the **local field at the tip apex must be enormous** — even a modest voltage applied across an ultra-sharp tip produces a massive local field.

## 2.2 The Fowler–Nordheim Equation (★ KTU High-Yield ★)

The current density due to field emission is given by the **Fowler–Nordheim (F-N) equation**:

$$J = \frac{A E^2}{\phi \, t^2(y)} \, \exp\left(-\frac{B \, \phi^{3/2} \, v(y)}{E}\right)$$

In simplified form (often used in engineering analysis):

$$J \approx \frac{A E^2}{\phi} \exp\left(-\frac{B \, \phi^{3/2}}{E}\right)$$

**Where:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Value/Unit |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| $J$ | Emission current density | A/m² |
| $E$ | Local electric field at emitter tip | V/m |
| $\phi$ | Work function of emitter material | eV |
| $A$ | First Fowler–Nordheim constant | **1.541 × 10⁻⁶ A·eV/V²** |
| $B$ | Second Fowler–Nordheim constant | **6.831 × 10⁹ V/(eV^(3/2)·m)** |
| $t(y), v(y)$ | Elliptic correction functions (Nordheim functions) | dimensionless, ~1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The F-N equation is **the most important formula** for FED analysis. KTU examiners love to ask: *"Explain the Fowler–Nordheim equation and identify how the parameters influence emission."* Memorize the exponential dependence on $1/E$ and the $E^2$ prefactor.

## 2.3 Engineering Interpretation of the F-N Equation

1. **Quadratic prefactor ($E^2$):** Doubling the field increases the prefactor by 4× — emission rises rapidly with field.
2. **Exponential decay ($\exp(-B\phi^{3/2}/E)$):** The work function $\phi$ has an *enormous* effect. Reducing $\phi$ from 4.5 eV (Mo) to 2.5 eV (CNT-coated tip) increases emission by **orders of magnitude**.
3. **Tip geometry effect:** The local field $E$ is related to the applied voltage $V$ by the field enhancement factor $\beta$:

$$E = \beta \cdot \frac{V}{d}$$

   Where $d$ is the cathode-gate gap. A sharper tip (smaller tip radius) → higher $\beta$ → higher local $E$ → stronger emission at the same applied voltage.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | KTU Use |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| $E = V/d$ | Electric field (parallel plate) | Voltage-to-field conversion |
| $E = \beta V/d$ | Local field with tip enhancement | FED emitter analysis |
| $J = (A E^2 / \phi) \exp(-B \phi^{3/2}/E)$ | Fowler–Nordheim current density | ★ Most important |
| $\ln(J/E^2) = \ln(A/\phi) - (B\phi^{3/2})(1/E)$ | Linearized F-N plot | Experimental verification |
| $w \approx \phi / eE$ | Tunnelling barrier width | Conceptual derivation |
| $\eta = L_{out}/P_{in}$ | Luminous efficiency (cd/W or lm/W) | FED vs LCD/Plasma comparison |
| $t_r = RC$ | RC-limited response time | FED speed advantage (microseconds) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The F-N equation is the cornerstone of designing micro-emitters, scanning tunnelling microscopes (STM), and modern vacuum nano-electronics. The linearized F-N plot is the standard diagnostic — a straight line on a $\ln(J/E^2)$ vs $1/E$ graph confirms field emission.

## 2.5 Comparison: FED vs Other Display Technologies

| Parameter | CRT | LCD | Plasma | OLED | **FED** |
|-----------|-----|-----|--------|------|---------|
| Luminous efficiency | 5–10 lm/W | 3–5 lm/W | 1.5–3 lm/W | 5–10 lm/W | **5–15 lm/W** |
| Response time | µs | ms | µs | µs | **µs** |
| Viewing angle | > 170° | 120–160° | > 170° | > 170° | **> 170°** |
| Operating temperature | Cold cathode | Cold | Heated gas | Cold | **Cold cathode** |
| Backlight needed | No | Yes | No | No | **No** |
| Vacuum required | Yes | No | Yes (low) | No | **Yes (high)** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Local Electric Field at a Spindt Microtip

The Spindt microtip is a sharp molybdenum cone with a tip radius $r$ in the range of **30–50 nm**, surrounded by a gate aperture of diameter $d_g$ and a cathode-to-gate vertical gap $d_{cg}$.

The **field enhancement factor** $\beta$ for a sharp conical tip is approximated as:

$$\beta \approx \frac{h}{r}$$

Where $h$ is the tip height above the gate plane. For typical Spindt geometry ($h \approx 0.5\,\mu\text{m}$, $r \approx 30\,\text{nm}$):

$$\beta \approx \frac{5 \times 10^{-7}}{3 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 16.7$$

The local field at the apex is therefore:

$$E_{local} = \beta \cdot \frac{V_g}{d_{cg}}$$

**Substituting numerical values** ($V_g = 50\,\text{V}$, $d_{cg} = 0.5\,\mu\text{m}$):

$$E_{local} = 16.7 \times \frac{50}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 16.7 \times 10^{8} = 1.67 \times 10^{9}\,\text{V/m}$$

This confirms the field is in the **10⁹ V/m range** — sufficient to drive field emission through quantum tunnelling.

## 3.2 Derivation: Emission Current from a Single Spindt Tip

Starting from the simplified Fowler–Nordheim equation:

$$J = \frac{A E^2}{\phi} \exp\left(-\frac{B \phi^{3/2}}{E}\right)$$

The total current from a single tip is:

$$I_{tip} = J \cdot A_{eff}$$

Where $A_{eff}$ is the effective emission area of the tip (~10⁻¹²–10⁻¹⁴ m² for Spindt tips).

**Step 1 — Substitute the local field:**

$$E = \beta \cdot \frac{V_g}{d_{cg}}$$

$$I_{tip} = \frac{A \, A_{eff}}{\phi} \left(\frac{\beta V_g}{d_{cg}}\right)^2 \exp\left(-\frac{B \phi^{3/2} d_{cg}}{\beta V_g}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Insert numerical values for Mo tip** ($\phi = 4.5\,\text{eV}$, $A_{eff} = 10^{-13}\,\text{m}^2$, $V_g = 50\,\text{V}$, $d_{cg} = 0.5\,\mu\text{m}$, $\beta = 17$):

Exponent term:

$$-\frac{B \phi^{3/2} d_{cg}}{\beta V_g} = -\frac{6.831 \times 10^9 \times (4.5)^{1.5} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{17 \times 50}$$

$$= -\frac{6.831 \times 10^9 \times 9.545 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{850}$$

$$= -\frac{3.26 \times 10^{4}}{850} \approx -38.35$$

Prefactor term:

$$\frac{A \, A_{eff}}{\phi} \left(\frac{\beta V_g}{d_{cg}}\right)^2 = \frac{1.541 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-13}}{4.5} \times \left(\frac{17 \times 50}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}\right)^2$$

$$= 3.42 \times 10^{-20} \times (1.7 \times 10^{9})^2 = 3.42 \times 10^{-20} \times 2.89 \times 10^{18} \approx 0.099\,\text{A}$$

$$I_{tip} = 0.099 \times e^{-38.35} = 0.099 \times 2.05 \times 10^{-17} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-18}\,\text{A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Single tip current is tiny (~pA range).** This is why FEDs need **millions of tips per pixel** (e.g., 1000+ tips/sub-pixel) and the macroscopic current per pixel is summed across the entire array — yielding the visible emission.

## 3.3 Derivation: Linearized F-N Plot (For Experimental Validation)

Take the natural logarithm of both sides of the F-N equation:

$$\ln\left(\frac{J}{E^2}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{A}{\phi}\right) - \frac{B \phi^{3/2}}{E}$$

Let:
- $y = \ln(J/E^2)$
- $x = 1/E$

This gives the equation of a straight line:

$$y = \ln\left(\frac{A}{\phi}\right) - B \phi^{3/2} \cdot x$$

**Slope:**

$$m = -B \phi^{3/2}$$

**Intercept:**

$$c = \ln\left(\frac{A}{\phi}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab / Exam Tip:** A plot of $\ln(J/E^2)$ versus $1/E$ should be a **straight line with negative slope** if the emission mechanism is true field emission (not thermionic or Schottky). From the slope, the effective work function can be extracted. This is the **standard verification method**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — FED Emitter Material Comparison

| Property | Spindt (Mo) Tips | Carbon Nanotubes (CNT) | Silicon Tips | Diamond-like Carbon (DLC) |
|----------|------------------|------------------------|--------------|----------------------------|
| Work function (eV) | 4.5 | 2.5 – 5.0 | 4.1 | 0.2 – 0.4 (NEA) |
| Tip radius | 30 nm | 5 – 20 nm | 50 nm | N/A (planar) |
| Turn-on voltage | 30 – 80 V | 1 – 5 V | 50 – 100 V | 1 – 10 V |
| Current stability | Moderate | High | Moderate | Very high |
| Manufacturing complexity | High (lithography) | Moderate (printing) | High | Low |
| Vacuum tolerance | Excellent | Good | Good | Good |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
