PPD = \frac{\text{horizontal resolution (pixels)}}{\text{horizontal FOV (degrees)}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PPD &= \frac{2064 \text{ pixels}}{110^{\circ}} \\
    &= 18.76 \text{ pixels per degree}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to Human Acuity**

The human eye with 20/20 vision can resolve approximately **1 arc-minute** $= 1/60^{\circ}$. To make individual pixels invisible at the fovea, we need:

$$
PPD_{req} = 60 \text{ pixels per degree}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the "Retina" Horizontal Resolution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{req} &= 60 \cdot FOV_h \\
        &= 60 \cdot 110 \\
        &= 6600 \text{ pixels per eye}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Implication**

The current Quest 3 (PPD $\approx$ 18.76) is about **3.3×** away from "retina" resolution. This is why foveated rendering — concentrating pixels only where the eye is looking — is the dominant research direction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget

**The 20 ms Rule:** A total system latency greater than 20 ms causes the user's vestibular system to detect the mismatch between expected and actual motion, leading to **VR sickness (cybersickness)**.

**Given Standard Latency Components for a Modern HMD:**

| Stage | Symbol | Typical Value (ms) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensor sample time | $T_{sensor}$ | 1.0 |
| Tracking & pose fusion | $T_{track}$ | 2.0 |
| Scene update + GPU render | $T_{render}$ | 8.0 |
| Display scan-out + persistence | $T_{display}$ | 5.5 |
| **Total** | $T_{total}$ | **16.5 ms** |

**Step 1 — Sum the Stages**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= T_{sensor} + T_{track} + T_{render} + T_{display} \\
          &= 1.0 + 2.0 + 8.0 + 5.5 \\
          &= 16.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Check the 90 Hz Frame Budget**

At a refresh rate of 90 Hz, one frame is allotted:

$$
T_{frame} = \frac{1000 \text{ ms}}{90 \text{ Hz}} = 11.11 \text{ ms}
$$

The 16.5 ms total is **1.48× the frame budget**, which means the system uses **Asynchronous Timewarp (ATW)** and **Asynchronous Spacewarp (ASW)** to interpolate and reproject the previous frame to the latest head pose, hiding the latency.

**Step 3 — The At-Worst-Case Bound**

The worst case is two full frame periods:

$$
T_{worst} = 2 \cdot T_{frame} = 22.22 \text{ ms}
$$

This is *just* above the 20 ms cybersickness threshold, which is why 120 Hz panels are increasingly preferred for next-generation HMDs:

$$
T_{frame}^{120Hz} = \frac{1000}{120} = 8.33 \text{ ms} \Rightarrow T_{worst} = 16.66 \text{ ms}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stereoscopic Depth from Disparity

**Given:** Two cameras (or eyes) separated by baseline $B$ (the IPD), focal length $f$ (in pixels), and a feature appearing at horizontal pixel coordinates $X_L$ in the left image and $X_R$ in the right image.

**Step 1 — Disparity Definition**

$$
\Delta X = X_L - X_R
$$

**Step 2 — Similar-Triangle Geometry**

Using the classic stereo-vision geometry, the depth $Z$ of the point in the scene is:

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{X_L - X_R} = \frac{f \cdot B}{\Delta X}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example**

For a user with $B = 63$ mm and a virtual camera focal length $f = 800$ pixels (typical VR rendering setting), the depth of an object producing a disparity of $\Delta X = 4$ pixels is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{800 \cdot 63}{4} \\
  &= \frac{50400}{4} \\
  &= 12600 \text{ mm} \\
  &= 12.6 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convergence to Vergence-Accommodation Limit**

When disparity is small (distant object), depth is large. As disparity approaches 0, depth $\rightarrow \infty$ (parallel optical axes). This is the basis for the **vergence-accommodation conflict** that limits comfortable VR viewing distances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Equation (8) above is the foundation of *all* depth-from-stereo algorithms and is **commonly asked** as a 7-mark derivation in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The VR System Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the end-to-end functional flow of a complete VR system, from user intent to sensory feedback.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[USER INPUT DOMAIN]
        A1[Head Rotation and Position]
        A2[Hand and Controller Motion]
        A3[Eye Gaze and Pupil Dilation]
        A4[Voice and Biometrics]
    end

    subgraph B[SENSING AND TRACKING SUBSYSTEM]
        B1[Inertial Measurement Unit IMU]
        B2[Outside-In IR Cameras]
        B3[Inside-Out Visible Light Cameras]
        B4[Eye Tracking IR Cameras]
    end

    subgraph C[COMPUTE SUBSYSTEM]
        C1[Kalman or Complementary Filter]
        C2[6-DOF Pose Estimator]
        C3[Game Engine and Physics Solver]
        C4[Stereoscopic Renderer with Foveation]
    end

    subgraph D[OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM]
        D1[Head Mounted Display with Pancake Lenses]
        D2[Binaural 3D Audio Processor HRTF]
        D3[Haptic Gloves and Suit Actuators]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B4
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    A4 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C2 --> D3
    D1 -.-> A1
    D3 -.-> A2
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows represent **forward data flow** (sensing → display).
- Dotted arrows represent the **closed-loop feedback** from the display back to the user's perception, which then triggers the next sensor sample.

---

## 4.2 Pipeline Diagram — The Sense–Render–Display Latency Budget

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[USER PHYSICAL HEAD MOVEMENT]
    P1[S1: IMU SAMPLE 1.0 ms]
    P2[S2: TRACKING FUSION 2.0 ms]
    P3[S3: GAME LOGIC UPDATE 1.0 ms]
    P4[S4: GPU RENDER 8.0 ms]
    P5[S5: DISPLAY SCANOUT 5.5 ms]
    P6[USER PERCEIVES NEW IMAGE]
    P7[FRAME BUDGET AT 90 Hz = 11.11 ms]
    P8[TOTAL PIPELINE = 16.5 ms]

    P0 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6
    P7 -. BENCHMARK .-> P4
    P8 -. TOTAL BUDGET .-> P5
```

**Interpretation:** The pipeline operates with a cumulative latency of **16.5 ms**, comfortably under the 20 ms cybersickness threshold. The frame budget annotation shows the GPU render step is the **largest single contributor**, which is why VR-specific GPUs allocate dedicated silicon for reprojection and foveation.

---

## 4.3 Classification Tree — Types of Reality Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q0[REALITY CONTINUUM]
    Q1[REAL ENVIRONMENT]
    Q2[AUGMENTED REALITY AR]
    Q3[AUGMENTED VIRTUALITY AV]
    Q4[FULL VIRTUAL REALITY VR]
    Q5[MIXED REALITY MR]

    Q0 --> Q1
    Q0 --> Q2
    Q0 --> Q3
    Q0 --> Q4
    Q2 --> Q5
    Q3 --> Q5
```

**Reading:** The Milgram–Kishino **Reality–Virtuality Continuum** spans from the purely real world to the fully virtual world, with mixed experiences (AR / AV / MR) occupying intermediate positions.

> [!NOTE]
> The Reality–Virtuality Continuum is **directly referenced in the KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus** and is a frequent 3-mark short-answer topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Virtual Reality. List and briefly explain the three defining characteristics (3 I's) of a VR system.**

**Model Answer:**

Virtual Reality is a computer-generated, three-dimensional, interactive, and immersive simulated environment that can be experienced in real time through electronic display and input devices such as a Head-Mounted Display (HMD) and motion controllers.

The three defining characteristics of VR, popularly called the **"3 I's"**, are:

1. **Immersion:** The technological capability to make the user feel *physically enclosed* within the virtual environment. It is enhanced by wide FOV ($\ge 110^{\circ}$), stereoscopic rendering, and binaural audio.

2. **Interaction:** The ability of the user to *manipulate* virtual objects and receive feedback in real time. Interaction is enabled by 6-DOF tracking, hand controllers, and gesture recognition, with end-to-end latency below 20 ms.

3. **Imagination:** The *cognitive* property where the user's mind accepts the synthetic world as plausible. This is driven by the rendering quality, narrative design, and multi-sensory coherence (visual + audio + haptic).

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing, 1 mark for brief explanation of each I.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR). Give one example application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Virtual Reality (VR) | Augmented Reality (AR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Environment** | Fully synthetic, computer-generated 3D world. | Real world overlaid with virtual 2D/3D elements. |
| **Immersion** | High — user is cut off from the real world. | Low to medium — user remains in the real world. |
| **Primary Hardware** | HMD with opaque displays (e.g., Meta Quest 3). | Transparent optical combiner or camera passthrough (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens, smartphone AR). |
| **Sensory Substitution** | Replaces real-world visual + auditory input. | Augments (adds to) real-world input. |
| **Example Application** | Immersive gaming (Beat Saber) or pilot training. | Navigation overlays on Google Maps Live View or surgical guidance in operation theatres. |

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for the comparison table, 1 mark for the example application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Variant]`

#### (a) Explain the architecture of a typical Virtual Reality system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the Head-Mounted Display, tracking subsystem, and rendering engine. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A complete VR system is structured as a **closed-loop pipeline** consisting of five major functional blocks:

**Block 1 — User Input Devices:**
The user issues commands by moving the head, hands, eyes, and body. Handheld controllers (with trigger, grip, and thumb-stick) and natural hand-tracking via cameras form the primary input modalities.

**Block 2 — Tracking Subsystem:**
This is the "sensory nervous system" of the HMD. It comprises:
- An **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** with a 3-axis gyroscope, accelerometer, and magnetometer that measures angular velocity and linear acceleration.
- **Outside-In cameras** mounted in the room that emit and detect infrared pulses reflected from HMD sensors (e.g., SteamVR Lighthouse).
- **Inside-Out cameras** mounted on the HMD itself that perform visual SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) using feature points in the room.

The tracking subsystem fuses these streams using a **Kalman Filter** to output a stable 6-DOF pose (position $\mathbf{p}$ and orientation quaternion $\mathbf{q}$) at 90–1000 Hz.

**Block 3 — Computing Subsystem (Rendering Engine):**
A host PC or an onboard mobile SoC runs a game engine (Unity or Unreal). The engine:
- Maintains the 3D scene graph.
- Runs the physics solver at a fixed time step.
- Performs **stereoscopic rendering**, generating two perspective views offset by the user's IPD.
- Applies **foveated rendering** when an eye-tracker is available.

**Block 4 — Display Subsystem (HMD):**
The HMD contains two micro-displays (LCD or OLED) and one lens per eye. Lenses focus the image to optical infinity and map the display onto a wide FOV. The display must operate at 90 Hz or higher, with low-persistence backlighting to reduce motion blur.

**Block 5 — Audio and Haptic Output:**
- **Binaural audio** is synthesised using HRTF filtering for accurate 3D localisation.
- **Haptic actuators** in controllers and suits provide tactile feedback for in-hand interactions.

*(Suggested Diagram: Draw the same Mermaid flowchart from Section 4.1 as a labelled block diagram. 1 mark is reserved for the diagram.)*

**Block Diagram of the architecture:**

```
[User] → [Input Devices: HMD + Controllers]
       → [Tracking: IMU + Cameras + SLAM]
       → [Compute: Game Engine + GPU Render]
       → [Display: HMD + Lenses + Audio + Haptics]
       → back to [User]   (closed loop)
```

---

#### (b) Discuss the key performance parameters of a VR display system, including Field of View, Pixel Per Degree, refresh rate, and motion-to-photon latency. Explain their significance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four most critical performance parameters of a VR display are:

**1. Field of View (FOV):**
The horizontal FOV of a human is approximately $200^{\circ}$ (with both eyes). A wider FOV improves immersion because more of the visual periphery is covered. Consumer VR HMDs in 2024–2025 target $110^{\circ}$ to $120^{\circ}$. The FOV is set by the relationship $FOV = 2\arctan(d/2f)$, where $d$ is the display dimension and $f$ is the lens focal length. Going beyond $\approx 120^{\circ}$ requires larger, heavier, and more expensive optics.

**2. Pixel Per Degree (PPD):**
PPD measures display sharpness and is given by $PPD = N_h / FOV_h$. The human eye resolves about 60 PPD (20/20 vision). Current HMDs deliver only 18–25 PPD, which is why individual pixels are still visible — the so-called "screen door effect." A 4K-per-eye display at 110° FOV yields approximately 19 PPD, still short of retina-level 60 PPD.

**3. Refresh Rate:**
The display must refresh at a frequency high enough that flicker is imperceptible and motion is smooth. The standard minimum is **90 Hz**, with modern devices targeting 120 Hz. The frame budget at 90 Hz is $11.11$ ms. Lower refresh rates cause flicker discomfort and exacerbate cybersickness.

**4. Motion-to-Photon Latency:**
This is the time from the user's physical head movement to the updated photon emission from the display. It includes sensor, tracking, render, and display times. The threshold for comfort is **20 ms**; above this, the brain detects visual–vestibular mismatch and cybersickness occurs. Techniques such as **Asynchronous Timewarp (ATW)** and **Asynchronous Spacewarp (ASW)** reproject the previous frame to the latest pose to mask the latency.

**Significance Summary:**
- High FOV → strong sense of *immersion*.
- High PPD → eliminates the *screen-door artefact* and improves realism.
- High refresh rate → reduces *flicker* and motion blur.
- Low latency → prevents *cybersickness* and ensures believable interaction.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Variant]`

#### (a) With the help of a diagram, explain the Reality–Virtuality Continuum. Classify the major types of reality systems and give one example of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Reality–Virtuality (RV) Continuum**, proposed by Milgram and Kishino (1994), is a conceptual axis that classifies all visual displays based on the proportion of real-world versus computer-generated content they present to the user.

**Classification of Reality Systems:**

| Position on Continuum | System Type | Description | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Far Left — Pure Real | **Real Environment** | The unmodified physical world. | A person walking in a park. |
| Middle-Left | **Augmented Reality (AR)** | Real world with computer-generated overlays. | Pokémon GO, Google Maps Live View. |
| Middle | **Augmented Virtuality (AV)** | Mostly virtual world with real elements (e.g., real person or object inserted). | A virtual meeting room with a live video feed of a remote participant. |
| Middle-Right | **Mixed Reality (MR)** | Real and virtual objects coexist and interact in real time. | Microsoft HoloLens 2 with spatial anchors. |
| Far Right — Pure Virtual | **Virtual Reality (VR)** | Fully synthetic, immersive 3D environment. | Meta Quest 3 running *Beat Saber*. |

**Diagram (to be drawn in the answer script):**

```
[Real]  [AR]  [MR / AV]  [VR]
  |------|------|----------|
  Real ◄──────────────────► Virtual
```

**Key Distinction:**
- In **AR**, the user *sees through* a transparent combiner into the real world.
- In **AV/MR**, the user *looks at* a display that already contains real-world camera passthrough.
- In **VR**, the user is *fully enclosed* in a synthetic world with no passthrough.

**Significance:** The continuum guides the choice of hardware and software based on the application — entertainment favours VR, while industrial training increasingly favours MR for context preservation.

---

#### (b) Describe the major applications of Virtual Reality in entertainment, education, training, and healthcare. Discuss at least two challenges that limit mainstream adoption. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Applications:**

**1. Entertainment and Gaming:**
- Fully immersive 3D games (e.g., *Half-Life: Alyx*, *Beat Saber*).
- Cinematic VR experiences and 360° video.
- Location-based VR arcades with motion-platform rigs.

**2. Education and Training:**
- **Medical training** — surgical simulators (e.g., Osso VR) for practising procedures without risk to patients.
- **Industrial training** — Walmart and Boeing use VR to train employees on equipment and safety protocols.
- **Classroom education** — virtual field trips to ancient Rome or inside the human body.

**3. Healthcare:**
- **Pain management** — VR distractions reduce perceived pain during wound dressing or burn treatment.
- **Exposure Therapy (PTSD)** — controlled virtual environments help patients confront trauma safely.
- **Motor rehabilitation** — gamified VR exercises improve stroke recovery outcomes.
- **Surgical planning** — patient-specific VR reconstructions from CT/MRI scans aid pre-operative planning.

**4. Architecture and Real Estate:**
- Walkthroughs of unbuilt buildings.
- Interior design visualisation at 1:1 scale.

**Challenges Limiting Adoption:**

**Challenge 1 — Cybersickness and the Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC):**
The human eye converges and accommodates (focuses) to the same real-world distance. In current VR, the eyes converge to the *stereoscopic depth* of a virtual object but accommodate to the *flat display* behind the lens. This conflict causes eye strain and nausea. Solutions such as varifocal displays and light-field displays are still under research.

**Challenge 2 — Hardware Cost, Weight, and Battery Life:**
High-resolution, high-refresh-rate HMDs with powerful GPUs are expensive (USD 1000+ for a premium PC-tethered setup). Standalone headsets trade graphical fidelity for portability. Heat dissipation, battery life (typically 2–3 hours), and weight (over 500 g for many HMDs) remain significant comfort barriers for multi-hour use.

**Other notable challenges** include content scarcity, hygiene concerns in shared devices, motion-sickness for some users, and unresolved long-term effects on children's visual development.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Don't confuse "refresh rate" with "frame rate."** Refresh rate is the display panel's hardware frequency; frame rate is what the GPU actually produces. The effective motion smoothness depends on the *lower* of the two.
> 2. **Always quote latency components in milliseconds and add them up** — students frequently write the latency budget as a list without summing it, missing the key analytical step.
> 3. **Do not interchange "PPD" with "PPI."** PPD (pixels per degree) is an angular measure of sharpness; PPI (pixels per inch) is a physical display density. The KTU ESE frequently tests this distinction.
> 4. **Distinguish "presence" from "immersion."** Immersion is a *technological* property; presence is a *psychological* state induced by immersion.
> 5. **For derivation questions, always show the trigonometric setup before the formula** — half the marks are awarded for the geometric reasoning, not just the final equation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Virtual Reality (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** VR is a real-time, interactive, computer-simulated environment with three core properties — **Immersion, Interaction, Imagination**.
- **3 I's of VR:** Immersion, Interaction, Imagination.
- **Key Components of a VR System:** HMD, Tracking Subsystem (IMU + cameras), Rendering Engine, Audio (HRTF), Haptics.
- **Three Immersion Levels:** Non-immersive, Semi-immersive, Fully immersive.
- **FOV Formula:** $FOV = 2\arctan(d/2f)$.
- **PPD Formula:** $PPD = N_h / FOV_h$. Target for "retina" VR is 60 PPD.
- **Latency Budget Equation:** $T_{total} = T_{sensor} + T_{track} + T_{render} + T_{display} \le 20$ ms.
- **Frame Budget at 90 Hz:** $11.11$ ms; at 120 Hz: $8.33$ ms.
- **Stereo Depth Formula:** $Z = f \cdot B / \Delta X$ where $B$ is the IPD baseline.
- **Standard IPD:** $\approx 63$ mm (range $54$–$74$ mm).
- **Reality–Virtuality Continuum:** Real → AR → AV/MR → VR (Milgram–Kishino).
- **6-DOF:** 3 translational ($x, y, z$) + 3 rotational (roll, pitch, yaw).
- **Display Target Specs (2024–2026):** 90–120 Hz refresh, 110°–120° FOV, 18–25 PPD current, 60 PPD retina target, $\le 20$ ms MTP latency.
- **Cybersickness Cause:** Mismatch between visual motion and vestibular input, exacerbated by the **Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC)**.
- **Mitigation Techniques:** High refresh rate, low-persistence displays, ATW, ASW, foveated rendering, varifocal/light-field displays.
- **Major Applications:** Gaming, surgical training, exposure therapy, industrial training, architectural walkthroughs.
- **Major Challenges:** VAC, cost, weight, battery life, content scarcity, hygiene.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Virtual Reality**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Augmented Reality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEVT722 - ENTERTAINMENT ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Augmented Reality # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Augmented Reality (AR)** is a technology paradigm that superimposes computer-generated virtual content (2D or 3D sensory information such as sound, video, graphics, or GPS data) onto a user's view of the real-world environment in real time, while allowing the user to continue interacting with the physical surroundings. According to **Ronald Azuma's (1997) seminal definition**, an AR system must simultaneously satisfy three defining properties: 1. **Combines Real and Virtual Content** — Real-world and virtual objects coexist in the same display space. 2. **Interactive in Real Time** — The system must respond to user actions with **low latency (< 20 ms)** to be perceptually seamless. 3. **Registered in 3D** — Virtual objects must be spatially aligned (geometrically registered) with the real world from the user's perspective. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Augmented Reality is positioned within Entertainment Electronics as a **disruptive immersive media technology** that bridges traditional viewing (TV, cinema) and fully synthetic experiences (VR). The examiner expects you to know AR's *position on the Reality–Virtuality Continuum*, its *display taxonomy*, and *entertainment-grade applications*. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are watching a football match on TV. A graphics overlay shows the player's name, speed (e.g., 32.4 km/h), and a yellow first-down line on the field. The pitch is **real**, but the line and statistics are **virtual**, perfectly **registered** on top of the live feed, and they update in **real time** as the player runs. This is *Augmented Reality* — the camera captures reality, and the computer "augments" it. Another everyday analogy: think of a **transparent sticker placed on a window**. The window still shows the street outside (real world), but now you can also see the sticker (virtual overlay). If the sticker stays glued at a fixed point on the window while you turn your head, the illusion of *spatial registration* is achieved. > [!NOTE] > **Reality Check:** AR is fundamentally different from **Virtual Reality (VR)**. In VR, the user is *fully immersed* in a synthetic world and cannot see the real environment. In AR, the real world remains visible and is *enhanced*, not replaced. ## 1.3 Classification within the Mixed Reality Spectrum Paul Milgram and Fumio Kishino defined the **Reality–Virtuality Continuum** (1994), which positions AR between the real world and virtual reality: $$ \text{Real Environment} \longleftrightarrow \text{Augmented Reality} \longleftrightarrow \text{Augmented Virtuality} \longleftrightarrow \text{Virtual Environment} $$ The **Mixed Reality (MR)** umbrella term covers all points on this continuum except the two endpoints. ## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics in AR Systems | Metric | Typical Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **End-to-End Latency** | $\leq 20 \text{ ms}$ | Prevents motion sickness, ensures registration | | **Frame Rate** | $\geq 60 \text{ fps}$, target **90 fps** | Smooth visual rendering | | **Tracking Accuracy** | $\leq 1 \text{ mm}$ (positional), $\leq 0.1°$ (rotational) | Spatial registration fidelity | | **Field of View (FoV)** | **30°–110°** | Determines immersion | | **Display Brightness** | $\geq 2000 \text{ nits}$ (outdoor AR) | Sunlight readability | | **Refresh Rate (Display)** | **75 Hz / 90 Hz / 120 Hz** | Reduces flicker | > [!TIP] > For KTU exams, always cite Azuma's three properties and Milgram's continuum when asked to "define" or "classify" AR. These are the most commonly tested foundational concepts. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Block View of an AR System An AR pipeline executes four sequential functional stages. Each stage has a measurable engineering specification. ### Stage 1 — Scene Capture (Sensing Reality) - **Sensors:** RGB cameras, depth sensors (e.g., **LiDAR**, structured light, time-of-flight), IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit — accelerometer, gyroscope, magnetometer), GPS. - **Function:** Acquire a digital representation of the user's viewpoint and surrounding environment. ### Stage 2 — Tracking & Registration (Knowing Where You Are) - **Marker-based tracking:** Uses fiducial markers (e.g., ARToolKit, ARTag, QR codes) with known patterns. - **Markerless tracking:** Uses **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** to detect feature points in the environment and estimate camera pose. - **Sensor fusion:** Combines visual odometry (from camera) with inertial odometry (from IMU) to robustly track 6-DoF pose. ### Stage 3 — Rendering (Drawing the Virtual) - **Rendering Engine:** Combines the camera feed, registered virtual 3D models, and lighting estimation to produce a composite frame. - **Lighting Estimation:** Modern engines (ARCore, ARKit) estimate *environmental HDR light probes* so virtual objects cast realistic shadows and reflections. ### Stage 4 — Display (Showing the User) - **Optical See-Through (OST):** Transparent glasses/hud; user sees reality directly, virtual projected onto lens. - **Video See-Through (VST):** Camera captures reality, composites with virtual, displays on opaque screen (smartphone, HMD). - **Projection-based:** Virtual content is *physically projected* onto real surfaces (no eyewear needed). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Display Taxonomy — a KTU favourite:** OST vs VST trade-off: OST has *zero added latency* for the real world (no camera pass-through delay) but suffers from *limited brightness* and registration errors. VST allows *perfect pixel-level registration* but introduces *motion-to-photon latency* of typically **~15–40 ms** which can cause VR sickness. ## 2.2 The Pinhole Camera & Projection Model (Foundation for Tracking) AR tracking relies on the **pinhole camera model**. A 3D world point $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, Z)^T$ projects to a 2D image point $\mathbf{x} = (u, v)^T$ via: $$ s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \,[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Where: - $s$ = scalar (depth-related) - $\mathbf{K}$ = $3 \times 3$ **intrinsic camera matrix** (focal length $f_x, f_y$, principal point $c_x, c_y$) - $\mathbf{R}$, $\mathbf{t}$ = rotation and translation (extrinsics — the **6-DoF pose** the tracker must estimate) - $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ = $3 \times 4$ extrinsic matrix $$ \mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ ## 2.3 Field of View (FoV) Geometry The Field of View of a display determines how much of the augmented world is visible. For a rectangular display of width $W$, height $H$, and viewing distance $d$ to the eye: $$ \text{Horizontal FoV} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{W}{2d}\right) $$ $$ \text{Vertical FoV} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{H}{2d}\right) $$ $$ \text{Diagonal FoV} = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{W^2 + H^2}}{2d}\right) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Industry benchmark:** Microsoft HoloLens 2 has a diagonal FoV of **~52°**; Magic Leap 2 reaches **~70°**; Meta Quest 3 (VST pass-through) achieves a virtual FoV of **~110°**. Wider FoV generally improves immersion but increases optical complexity and cost. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Camera Projection | $s\,\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R}\vert\mathbf{t}]\mathbf{X}$ | pixels | 6-DoF pose estimation | | 2 | Intrinsic Matrix | $\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | pixels | Lens calibration | | 3 | Horizontal FoV | $2\arctan\!\left(\frac{W}{2d}\right)$ | radians / degrees | Display engineering | | 4 | Vertical FoV | $2\arctan\!\left(\frac{H}{2d}\right)$ | radians / degrees | Display engineering | | 5 | Diagonal FoV | $2\arctan\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{W^2+H^2}}{2d}\right)$ | radians / degrees | Display engineering | | 6 | Latency Budget (perception threshold) | $L_{\text{total}} \leq 20 \text{ ms}$ | milliseconds | Motion-to-photon design | | 7 | Frame Time | $T_f = \frac{1}{\text{FPS}}$ | seconds | Rendering pipeline | | 8 | Tracking Error (combined) | $E_{\text{combined}} = \sqrt{E_{\text{pos}}^2 + (d \cdot E_{\text{rot}})^2}$ | meters / radians | Registration quality | | 9 | Parallax Cue Distance | $d_p = \frac{b \cdot f}{d_\text{disparity}}$ | meters | Stereoscopic depth | | 10 | Reality–Virtuality Continuum | $\text{RE} \leftrightarrow \text{AR} \leftrightarrow \text{AV} \leftrightarrow \text{VE}$ | qualitative | Classification | > [!WARNING] > **Table cell pipe rule:** Notice the use of `\vert` (and the matrix form $[\mathbf{R}\vert\mathbf{t}]$) inside the table cells. Never write literal `|` characters in formula tables — they break Markdown column parsing. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Engineering Utility of AR | |---|---| | **Gaming & Entertainment** | *Pokémon GO*, *Ingress*, AR face filters on Instagram/Snapchat | | **Broadcast / Sports** | Down-line markers, score overlays, virtual advertisements (e.g., 2022 FIFA World Cup stadium-side boards) | | **Cinematography** | Virtual production (LED volumes — *The Mandalorian*, ILM StageCraft) | | **Live Events & Concerts** | Holographic stage performances (e.g., ABBA Voyage, Tupac at Coachella 2012) | | **Theme Parks** | Location-anchored AR overlays (Disney parks) | | **Edutainment** | Interactive 3D textbooks, museum AR guides | | **Broadcast Cameras** | Vizrt, Brainstorm, ARRI ARRI-Trackless Studio systems | ## 2.6 Tracking Taxonomy (Detailed) ### A. Marker-Based (Fiducial) Tracking - Uses **square black-and-white patterns** with known geometry. - Algorithm: detect quadrilateral → decode pattern → solve **Perspective-n-Point (PnP)** to recover pose. - Pros: high accuracy, computationally cheap. - Cons: requires pre-placed markers; not user-friendly in entertainment. ### B. Markerless / Natural Feature Tracking - Uses **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**. - **Mono-SLAM, ORB-SLAM, LSD-SLAM, Visual-Inertial SLAM** are popular variants. - Pros: works anywhere; user-friendly. - Cons: heavy computation, scale ambiguity in monocular systems. ### C. Depth-Sensor-Based Tracking - Uses **LiDAR** (Apple iPad Pro, iPhone Pro) for direct metric depth measurement. - Eliminates scale ambiguity and improves occlusion handling. ### D. Sensor-Fusion Tracking - Combines **Visual Odometry + IMU + GPS** using a **Kalman Filter** or **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**. - Robust to lighting changes and fast motion blur. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Modern AR SDKs (Google ARCore, Apple ARKit, Meta Spark, Niantic Lightship, 8th Wall)** all implement **Visual-Inertial SLAM** with depth sensing and light estimation, making them "all-in-one" entertainment-grade platforms. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Pinhole Projection of a World Point We derive how a real 3D point in the world ends up as a 2D pixel on the AR display — the mathematical heart of AR tracking. ### Step 1 — World to Camera Transformation A 3D world point $\mathbf{X}_w$ is transformed into the camera's coordinate frame via the rigid-body transformation: $$ \mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{X}_w + \mathbf{t} $$ Here, $\mathbf{R}$ is a $3\times3$ rotation matrix (3 rotational DoF) and $\mathbf{t}$ is a $3\times1$ translation vector (3 translational DoF). Together they encode the **6-DoF pose** of the camera relative to the world. In homogeneous form: $$ \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{X}_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{X}_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 2 — Camera to Image Plane (Perspective Projection) Under a pinhole model, a 3D camera point $\mathbf{X}_c = (X_c, Y_c, Z_c)^T$ projects to a 2D image-plane point $(x, y)$ using similar triangles: $$ x = f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \qquad y = f \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} $$ where $f$ is the focal length (distance between the pinhole and the image plane). This is the canonical **perspective divide**. ### Step 3 — Image Plane to Pixel Coordinates Real image sensors have discrete pixels and an offset principal point. Convert meters to pixels: $$ u = f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x, \qquad v = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y $$ with $f_x = f / s_x$ and $f_y = f / s_y$ where $s_x, s_y$ are pixel dimensions in meters. ### Step 4 — Full Homogeneous Pipeline Combining Steps 1–3 into a single matrix equation (with $s = Z_c$): $$ s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x & 0 \\ 0 & f_y & c_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ This is the full **camera projection equation** — the foundation of every AR tracker. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in AR:** The 6-DoF pose $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ is precisely what the AR tracking algorithm (PnP, SLAM, EKF) must estimate *in real time* to know where to render the virtual object. The intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ is known a-priori from a one-time **camera calibration** step (e.g., Zhang's checkerboard method). ## 3.2 Derivation: Field of View from Display Geometry ### Given - Display width $W$, height $H$, eye-to-display distance $d$. ### Step 1 — Half-Angle in Horizontal Direction By the tangent of half-angle: $$ \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta_h}{2}\right) = \frac{W/2}{d} $$ ### Step 2 — Solve for the Full Angle $$ \frac{\theta_h}{2} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{W}{2d}\right) $$ $$ \theta_h = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{W}{2d}\right) $$ ### Step 3 — Generalize to Vertical and Diagonal By the same geometric argument, replacing $W$ with $H$ for vertical, and with $\sqrt{W^2+H^2}$ for diagonal: $$ \theta_v = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{H}{2d}\right) $$ $$ \theta_d = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{W^2+H^2}}{2d}\right) $$ ### Numerical Example A HoloLens 2 display has $W \approx 43°$ equivalent, $H \approx 29°$ equivalent, with $d \approx 2.0 \text{ cm}$ to the eye. Suppose $W = 1.4 \text{ cm}$ and $H = 0.9 \text{ cm}$ physically: $$ \theta_h = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{0.7}{2.0}\right) = 2\arctan(0.35) \approx 2 \times 19.3° \approx 38.6° $$ (Actual marketed horizontal FoV of HoloLens 2 is **~43°**, reflecting the use of waveguide optics.) ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Display Technologies Compared | Property | Optical See-Through (OST) | Video See-Through (VST) | Projection-Based | |---|---|---|---| | **Real-world perception** | Direct, natural | Camera-mediated (may distort colour/depth) | Direct | | **Latency of real content** | 0 ms | 15–40 ms | 0 ms | | **Registration accuracy** | Lower (calibration drift) | High (pixel-perfect) | Surface-dependent | | **Brightness / Sunlight** | Limited | Display-bound | Projection-bound | | **Occlusion (real hides virtual)** | Difficult | Easy (depth mask) | Easy | | **Form factor** | Glasses / HMD | Phone / HMD | Room installation | | **Examples** | HoloLens 2, Magic Leap 2, Epson Moverio | Meta Quest 3 (pass-through), ARKit apps, Pokémon GO | Sony Spatial Reality, Lightform | ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: AR SDKs (Entertainment-Grade) | SDK | Platform | Tracking Tech | Notable Feature | |---|---|---|---| | **Apple ARKit** | iOS / iPadOS | VIO + LiDAR | People Occlusion, Motion Capture | | **Google ARCore** | Android | VIO + Depth API | Light Estimation, Geospatial API | | **Meta Spark** | Facebook / Instagram | Face mesh + VIO | AR face filters (mass-market entertainment) | | **Niantic Lightship** | Cross-platform | VPS (Visual Positioning Service) | Pokémon GO AR+, shared AR | | **8th Wall** | Web (WebXR) | VIO + SLAM | Browser-based AR, no app install | | **Vuforia** | Cross-platform | Marker + Model Targets | Industrial + gaming crossover | | **Snap Lens Studio** | Snapchat | Face + Body mesh | Mass-market social AR | > [!TIP] > For KTU Part B answers, mention at least *one* SDK and its tracking paradigm. This signals awareness of industry practice and earns full content marks. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Tracking Error Budget An AR system has **positional error** $E_{\text{pos}} = 0.5 \text{ mm}$ and **rotational error** $E_{\text{rot}} = 0.05°$ at a viewing distance of $d = 1.0 \text{ m}$. Convert $E_{\text{rot}}$ to radians: $0.05° \times \frac{\pi}{180} \approx 8.73 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$. Translational impact at distance $d$: $$ E_{\text{rot\_proj}} = d \cdot E_{\text{rot}} = 1.0 \times 8.73 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.873 \text{ mm} $$ Combined RMS error: $$ E_{\text{combined}} = \sqrt{(0.5)^2 + (0.873)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.762} = \sqrt{1.012} \approx 1.006 \text{ mm} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Even with very small individual errors, combined rotational + translational drift can exceed 1 mm at 1 m, which is *perceivable* to a trained observer in high-precision AR. This is why consumer AR still has visible "wobble" on stationary virtual objects. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 AR System Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Scene Capture

The fusion block uses a Kalman/EKF filter to combine noisy visual features with high-rate inertial data, producing a stable 6-DoF pose used by the AR renderer. ## 4.5 AR Entertainment Application Domains ```mermaid mindmap root((AR Entertainment)) Gaming Pokemon GO Ingress Prime AR Mario Broadcast Sports Overlays Virtual Ads AR Set Design Music Hologram Concerts Lyric AR Filters Cinema Virtual Production LED Volumes Social Snap Lenses Instagram Filters TikTok Effects Live Events Theme Parks Stadium AR ``` > **Figure 5.** A mind-map of entertainment sub-domains where AR is currently commercialized, useful for application-based exam answers. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks) ### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)] **Define Augmented Reality. List any two key differences between AR and VR.** **Model Answer:** Augmented Reality (AR) is a technology that overlays computer-generated virtual content onto a user's real-world view in real time, with virtual objects spatially registered in 3D. Per Azuma (1997), AR must satisfy three properties: combination of real and virtual, real-time interactivity, and 3D registration. **Two key differences from VR:** 1. **Reality Channel:** AR retains the user's view of the real environment (transparency or pass-through), while VR completely replaces reality with a synthetic world. 2. **Use Case & Hardware:** AR is typically mobile/lightweight (phones, smart glasses) for situational augmentation, whereas VR uses opaque, fully enclosed HMDs (e.g., Meta Quest 3, Valve Index) for full immersion. ### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)] **What is the Reality–Virtuality Continuum? Where does AR lie on it?** **Model Answer:** The Reality–Virtuality Continuum, proposed by Milgram and Kishino (1994), is a conceptual axis that classifies displays based on how much of the presented content is real versus virtual. At one extreme lies the **Real Environment (RE)** (entirely real), and at the other lies the **Virtual Environment (VE)** (entirely synthetic). Between them sit **Augmented Reality (AR)** (mostly real, augmented with virtual overlays) and **Augmented Virtuality (AV)** (mostly virtual, with real elements added). AR lies closer to the Real Environment end of the continuum. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** Students commonly lose marks by (a) confusing AR with VR in definitions, (b) omitting **Azuma's three properties** or **Milgram's continuum** when defining AR, and (c) describing tracking technologies without naming the **specific algorithms** (PnP, SLAM, Kalman filter). Always anchor answers to the standard textbook framework first, then add engineering detail. ### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 (Model)] **(a)** Explain the three defining properties of an Augmented Reality system as per Azuma's definition. Why is **6-DoF tracking** fundamental to satisfying the third property? (7 Marks) **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional pipeline of an AR system** from scene capture to display. Discuss the role of **sensor fusion** in modern tracking. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Azuma's Three Properties & 6-DoF Tracking** **Property 1: Combination of Real and Virtual Content** - The displayed scene must contain *both* real-world elements and computer-generated virtual content, coexisting in a shared visual or sensory space. - Example: A smartphone camera shows a real table; an AR app overlays a 3D virtual teapot sitting on it. **Property 2: Interactive in Real Time** - The system must update the augmentation in response to user actions (head motion, gesture, tap) with **end-to-end latency ≤ 20 ms** (perceptual threshold for motion-to-photon delay). - Beyond this, users experience registration lag, "swimming" of virtual objects, or motion sickness. **Property 3: Registered in 3D** - Virtual objects must appear *locked* to real-world positions across all six degrees of freedom of user movement: - **3 translational DoF:** $X, Y, Z$ (forward/back, left/right, up/down). - **3 rotational DoF:** pitch, yaw, roll. - This requires continuous **6-DoF pose estimation** — recovering the camera's $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ relative to the world. **Why 6-DoF is Fundamental to Property 3:** The third property demands that the augmentation remains geometrically consistent with the real scene as the user moves. If only 3-DoF (orientation only, Google Cardboard-style) is supported, virtual objects cannot remain anchored in 3D space — they appear to "float" with the head rather than rest on real surfaces. Accurate 6-DoF tracking using **PnP (for marker-based)** or **SLAM (for markerless)** solves the equation $s\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R}\vert\mathbf{t}]\mathbf{X}$ in real time, satisfying 3D registration. **Part (b) — AR Pipeline and Sensor Fusion** **Block Diagram (Narrative):** 1. **Scene Capture Module:** RGB camera captures the live environment; IMU provides high-rate motion data; GPS provides coarse location. 2. **Tracking Engine:** Uses **Visual Odometry** (feature extraction + matching across frames) and **Inertial Odometry** (IMU integration). Output: 6-DoF pose. 3. **Sensor Fusion Layer:** Combines noisy visual estimates (drifty but absolute) with noisy inertial estimates (drifty long-term but precise short-term) using a **Kalman Filter** or **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**. 4. **Pose Output:** $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ at each frame. 5. **Rendering Engine:** Loads 3D models, applies lighting estimation (HDR probes), performs occlusion tests, composites virtual content. 6. **Display Subsystem:** OST (glasses) or VST (screen) presents the augmented frame to the user. **Role of Sensor Fusion:** - Cameras suffer from motion blur at high speed and feature-poor scenes (white walls). IMUs drift over time but are immune to visual degradation. - **Sensor fusion** exploits the complementary strengths: visual odometry corrects IMU drift over the long term, while IMU fills in during fast motion or textureless regions. - Modern AR (ARKit, ARCore) uses **Visual-Inertial SLAM** precisely to achieve robust, drift-free 6-DoF pose estimation at **≥ 60 Hz**. --- ### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023 (Model)] **(a)** Compare **Optical See-Through (OST)** and **Video See-Through (VST)** display technologies. Which is more suitable for outdoor entertainment applications and why? (7 Marks) **(b)** Describe the working of **marker-based tracking** in AR. How does it differ from **markerless tracking using SLAM**? (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — OST vs VST Comparison** | Parameter | Optical See-Through (OST) | Video See-Through (VST) | |---|---|---| | Real-world channel | Direct optical path | Camera capture + screen | | Added latency for reality | 0 ms (direct) | 15–40 ms | | Registration accuracy | Calibration-dependent, lower | Pixel-perfect, higher | | Brightness / sunlight | Limited (projector/waveguide) | Display-bound (high) | | Occlusion handling | Hard (real cannot hide virtual) | Easy (depth mask) | | Form factor | Lightweight glasses | Phone or enclosed HMD | | Examples | HoloLens 2, Magic Leap 2 | Meta Quest 3 (pass-through), Pokémon GO | **Suitability for Outdoor Entertainment:** **VST is more suitable for outdoor entertainment.** Reasons: 1. **Brightness:** OST waveguides wash out in direct sunlight (< 2000 nits typical), while VST can drive LCD/OLED displays at high brightness. 2. **Robust registration:** VST allows pixel-level compositing with depth-correct occlusion (e.g., real trees correctly hiding virtual characters in an AR game). 3. **Cost-effective at scale:** A smartphone with a camera acts as a VST display, making it the de-facto platform for mass-market outdoor AR games like *Pokémon GO*. **OST is preferred in industrial / surgical applications** where zero added reality-latency is non-negotiable. **Part (b) — Marker-Based vs Markerless (SLAM) Tracking** **Marker-Based Tracking:** - A **fiducial marker** (e.g., black-and-white square with unique pattern) is placed in the real world. - Algorithm steps: 1. **Detect** the marker: find quadrilateral contours in the image. 2. **Decode** the inner pattern to identify the marker ID. 3. **Estimate pose** by solving the **Perspective-n-Point (PnP)** problem: given the known 3D coordinates of the marker's corners and their 2D projections, recover $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$. - **Pros:** High accuracy, low computation, deterministic. - **Cons:** Requires pre-placed markers; intrusive for entertainment. **Markerless Tracking (SLAM):** - **SLAM = Simultaneous Localization and Mapping.** - Algorithm steps: 1. **Feature extraction:** Detect interest points (ORB, SIFT, FAST) in successive frames. 2. **Feature matching / tracking:** Match points between frames to compute motion. 3. **Pose estimation:** Use **epipolar geometry** or **PnP** to recover camera pose. 4. **Map building:** Triangulate 3D positions of features. 5. **Loop closure:** Recognize previously visited locations to correct drift. - **Variants:** Mono-SLAM, Stereo-SLAM, RGB-D SLAM, Visual-Inertial SLAM. - **Pros:** No markers needed; works in arbitrary environments. - **Cons:** Heavier computation, scale ambiguity in monocular setups. **Key Difference:** Marker-based tracking relies on *known patterns* in the environment (controlled); SLAM builds and uses a *map of unknown features* (uncontrolled). SLAM is what powers modern entertainment AR (ARCore, ARKit, Niantic Lightship). ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Augmented Reality (Module 4)** - **Azuma's 3 AR properties:** (1) Real + Virtual combination, (2) Real-time interactivity, (3) 3D registration. - **Milgram's Continuum:** RE → AR → AV → VE. AR is closer to the real end. - **AR ≠ VR:** AR augments reality; VR replaces it. - **6-DoF pose** = 3 translation + 3 rotation; essential for 3D registration. - **Core projection equation:** $s\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R}\vert\mathbf{t}]\mathbf{X}$. - **Intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$** contains focal length $(f_x, f_y)$ and principal point $(c_x, c_y)$. - **OST vs VST:** OST = 0 ms real latency, lower registration; VST = pixel-perfect, adds 15–40 ms. - **Latency budget:** Motion-to-photon ≤ **20 ms**; Frame rate ≥ **60 fps** (target 90 fps). - **Tracking methods:** Marker-based (PnP), Markerless (SLAM), Depth (LiDAR), Sensor Fusion (EKF). - **Field of View:** $\theta = 2\arctan(W/2d)$ for horizontal; diagonal uses $\sqrt{W^2+H^2}$. - **Popular AR SDKs:** ARKit (iOS), ARCore (Android), Meta Spark, Niantic Lightship, 8th Wall. - **Entertainment applications:** Gaming (*Pokémon GO*), Broadcast (sports overlays), Cinema (LED volumes), Live concerts (holograms), Social AR (Snap/Insta filters). - **Display examples:** HoloLens 2 (OST, ~52° FoV), Magic Leap 2 (OST, ~70° FoV), Quest 3 (VST, ~110° FoV). - **Key SLAM concepts:** Feature extraction, Visual Odometry, Loop Closure, Drift Correction. - **Tracking error formula:** $E = \sqrt{E_{\text{pos}}^2 + (d \cdot E_{\text{rot}})^2}$ — combines positional and rotational drift. - **Frequently tested pairing:** Always be ready to write the **camera projection equation** and the **FoV formula** for 7-mark derivations. - **Examiner tip:** Anchor every Part B answer to Azuma + Milgram first, then add engineering detail. Definition precision = 2–3 marks secured immediately.
